case_name: Arrington v. MZ 2640 OWNER LLP
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiffs mention need for walk-in shower as disability accommodation but did not plead that such request was made to defendants
key_holding: Complaint dismissed with leave to amend because plaintiffs failed to state plausible claims for relief under the ADA and False Claims Act, and while the court grants leave to amend to assert Fair Housing Act disability discrimination claims under § 3604(f)(1), plaintiffs' mere mention of a walk-in shower accommodation without alleging they requested it from defendants is insufficient to state a reasonable accommodation claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs brought ADA Title III and False Claims Act claims related to a subsidized housing eviction proceeding, with the court dismissing both claims because the apartment is not a public accommodation under ADA Title III and plaintiffs cannot bring a qui tam FCA action pro se. The court granted leave to amend to assert Fair Housing Act disability discrimination claims, noting that plaintiffs' mention of a walk-in shower accommodation was insufficient without pleading that a request was made to defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 025 - MATTER OF HART v New York City Hous Auth 2
case_name: Matter of Hart v. New York City Hous. Auth.
accommodation_description: Tenant with PTSD sought reasonable accommodation to avoid tenancy termination based on alleged aggressive behavior toward NYCHA employees
key_holding: The court upheld NYCHA's tenancy termination finding substantial evidence supported the charges, and concluded the hearing officer gave sufficient consideration to the tenant's reasonable accommodation request for PTSD, determining no accommodation would have offered a solution to the conduct issues.
brief_summary: Vincenia Lee Hart, a public housing tenant with PTSD, petitioned for review of NYCHA's decision terminating her tenancy for nondesirability based on aggressive behavior toward employees, arguing NYCHA failed to reasonably accommodate her disability. The court confirmed NYCHA's determination, finding substantial evidence supported the termination and that the hearing officer gave sufficient consideration to the accommodation request.
key_cases_cited: Matter of Washington v. Olatoye, 173 A.D.3d 467 (2019)
Matter of Prospect Union Assoc. v. DeJesus, 167 A.D.3d 540 (2018)
Handy v. City of New Rochelle, 198 F. Supp. 3d 298 (2016)
Matter of Pell v. Board of Educ., 34 N.Y.2d 222 (1974)

source_file: 025 - MATTER OF HART v New York City Housing Authority
case_name: Matter of Hart v New York City Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Petitioner requested reasonable accommodation for her PTSD to mitigate the penalty for tenancy termination based on alleged aggressive behavior toward NYCHA employees.
key_holding: The hearing officer gave sufficient consideration to the petitioner's reasonable accommodation request, and the penalty of tenancy termination was not disproportionate to the offenses supported by substantial evidence.
brief_summary: Petitioner Vincenia Lee Hart, a NYCHA public housing tenant, challenged the termination of her tenancy as a reasonable accommodation for her PTSD after NYCHA charged her with nondesirability based on aggressive behavior toward employees. The Appellate Division confirmed NYCHA's determination, finding substantial evidence supported the termination and that the hearing officer gave sufficient consideration to the accommodation request.
key_cases_cited: Handy v. City of New Rochelle, 198 F. Supp. 3d 298 (2016)
Matter of Prospect Union Assoc. v. DeJesus, 167 A.D.3d 540 (2018)
Matter of Washington v. Olatoye, 173 A.D.3d 467 (2019)
Matter of Burton v. New York City Hous. Auth., 177 A.D.3d 874 (2019)

source_file: 025 - United States v FOX RUN APARTMENTS LLC
case_name: United States v. Fox Run Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep a 70-pound Doberman Pinscher emotional support animal as a reasonable accommodation to the property's 25-pound pet weight restriction
key_holding: Although the government's reasonable accommodation claim had a reasonable basis in fact and law and was not frivolous, the court nevertheless denied defendants' motion for fees and costs under the EAJA because the government's position was substantially justified and special circumstances made an award unjust given defendants' own conduct.
brief_summary: The United States sued Fox Run Apartments under the FHA alleging failure to accommodate a veteran's request to keep an emotional support animal, but voluntarily dismissed its claims after which defendants sought fees and costs under the EAJA. The court denied the fee motion, finding the government's position was substantially justified because there was record evidence supporting each element of a plausible failure-to-accommodate claim, and special circumstances made a fee award unjust given defendants' own litigation conduct.
key_cases_cited: Christiansburg Garment Co. v. EEOC, 434 U.S. 412 (1978)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Hadden v. Bowen, 851 F.2d 1266 (10th Cir. 1988)
Pierce v. Underwood, 487 U.S. 552 (1988)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)

source_file: 034 - CLOVER COMMUNITIES BEAVERCREEK LLC v MUSSACHIO ARCHITECTS PC
case_name: CLOVER COMMUNITIES BEAVERCREEK, LLC v. MUSSACHIO ARCHITECTS PC
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: UNDETERMINED
brief_summary: UNDETERMINED

case_name: Collins v. TEAM MANAGEMENT LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff raised reasonable accommodation claims under § 3604(f), but the specific accommodation requested is not detailed in the available text.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on all claims, including plaintiff's late-raised reasonable accommodation claims under § 3604(f), noting that plaintiff had withdrawn her own housing application.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Collins sued TEAM MANAGEMENT LLC and related defendants alleging housing discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, including claims under § 3604(a) for discrimination, § 3617 for retaliation, and late-raised reasonable accommodation claims under § 3604(f). The court granted summary judgment for defendants on all claims, noting that plaintiff had withdrawn her own housing application.

case_name: Robinson v. District of Columbia
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to remain at or return to Kia's Place IV homeless shelter as an accommodation for his mental health disabilities (ADHD, PTSD, depressive disorder), arguing he should not have been terminated from the program as a reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed Robinson's FHA reasonable accommodation claim as futile because his proposed amended complaint failed to specify what accommodation he requested, making it impossible to assess whether any such accommodation was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: Javonte Robinson, a homeless individual with mental health disabilities, sued the District of Columbia, a transitional youth shelter operator, and employees after being terminated from Kia's Place IV shelter, claiming they failed to make reasonable accommodations for his disabilities. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss the federal claims as futile, finding Robinson's complaint failed to allege what specific accommodation he requested and why it would be necessary to afford him equal use and enjoyment of the shelter.
key_cases_cited: United States v. District of Columbia, 538 F. Supp. 2d 211 (D.D.C. 2008)
Hunt v. Aimco Properties, L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Douglas v. Kriegsfeld Corp., 884 A.2d 1109 (D.C. 2005)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Dadian v. Village of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 10005587_2024_Allentown Victory Church v. City of Allentown_ca3
case_name: Allentown Victory Church v. City of Allentown
accommodation_description: The church requested permission to house eighteen (later reduced to fifteen) rather than twelve residents in its transitional recovery home as a reasonable accommodation under the zoning ordinance's accommodation provision.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding that the church failed to demonstrate that housing more than twelve residents was 'necessary' rather than merely 'preferable' for the Home's operation, as required under the reasonable accommodation burden-shifting framework.
brief_summary: Allentown Victory Church, which operates a transitional recovery home for men exiting substance abuse treatment and incarceration, sued the City of Allentown and its Zoning Hearing Board after the Board denied its requests to classify the Home as either a Large Group Home or an Institution and to house more than twelve residents as a reasonable accommodation. The Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, finding that while the Church invoked the FHA's reasonable accommodation provision, it failed to produce evidence showing that housing eighteen (later fifteen) residents was 'necessary' rather than merely economically preferable.
key_cases_cited: Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Vorchheimer v. Phila. Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Est., LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Lakeside Resort Enters., LP v. Bd. of Supervisors of Palmyra Twp., 455 F.3d 154 (3d Cir. 2006)
Washington v. Klem, 497 F.3d 272 (3d Cir. 2007)

source_file: 10034090_2024_Jeremy Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Add._ca9
case_name: Jeremy Ray Morris; Kristy Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves religious discrimination in HOA enforcement and interference claims.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed JMOL for the HOA on the § 3604(b) disparate treatment claim because the Morrises did not show sufficient adverse effects, reversed JMOL on the § 3617 interference claim finding sufficient evidence of religious motivation, affirmed the new trial order, and vacated the permanent injunction.
brief_summary: The Morrises sued their HOA under the Fair Housing Act alleging religious discrimination after the HOA attempted to prevent them from hosting a large-scale Christmas program at their home, citing CC&R violations. A jury found for the Morrises on § 3604(b), § 3604(c), and § 3617 claims, awarding $60,000 compensatory and $15,000 punitive damages, but the district court granted judgment as a matter of law for the HOA post-trial. The Ninth Circuit affirmed in part and reversed in part, upholding the § 3604(b) dismissal but reinstating the § 3617 interference claim and vacating the injunction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc.
Barnard v. Theobald
Pacific Shores Apartments v. City of Oakland

source_file: 10038254_2024_Gilead Community Services, Inc. v. Town of Cromwell_ca2
case_name: Gilead Community Services, Inc. v. Town of Cromwell
accommodation_description: Gilead sought to operate a group home for individuals with mental health disabilities in Cromwell, and the town's campaign of discrimination and retaliation was designed to prevent the group home from opening.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed the jury's liability findings under the FHA, including that municipalities can be held vicariously liable for discriminatory acts of officials and that punitive damages are available against municipalities under the FHA, but vacated the $5 million punitive damages award as unconstitutionally excessive and remanded for a new trial on damages unless plaintiffs accepted remittitur to $2 million.
brief_summary: Gilead Community Services and Connecticut Fair Housing Center sued the Town of Cromwell after the town engaged in a sustained campaign of discrimination, including discriminatory public statements, zoning threats, and police targeting, to prevent Gilead's group home for individuals with mental health disabilities from opening, forcing the home to close. The Second Circuit affirmed the jury's liability findings and $181,000 compensatory damages award but vacated the $5 million punitive damages award as unconstitutionally excessive, remanding for reduction to $2 million.
key_cases_cited: Cabrera v. Jakabovitz, 24 F.3d 372 (2d Cir. 1994)
Robinson v. 12 Lofts Realty, Inc., 610 F.2d 1032 (2d Cir. 1979)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
State Farm Mut. Auto Ins. Co. v. Campbell, 538 U.S. 408 (2003)

source_file: 10082236_2024_Joshua Debity v. Vintage Village Homeowners Ass'n_ca6
case_name: Joshua A. Debity, et al. v. Vintage Village Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Request for a six-foot wooden privacy fence in the backyard instead of the HOA-approved wrought-iron fence to prevent the disabled child from eloping and to provide privacy when the child disrobes due to sensory processing disorder.
key_holding: The appellate court reversed summary judgment for the HOA, finding the district court improperly ignored evidence related to the privacy concern and erred in excluding the treating provider's testimony about the child's need for a privacy fence, creating genuine issues of material fact as to whether a wooden privacy fence was necessary to afford the disabled child equal opportunity to enjoy the property.
brief_summary: Joshua and Leah Debity sued their HOA after it refused to approve a wooden privacy fence for their autistic son G.D., instead requiring a wrought-iron fence; the district court excluded the child's medical provider's testimony and granted summary judgment for the HOA. The Sixth Circuit reversed, holding that genuine issues of material fact existed as to whether the wooden privacy fence was necessary to afford the child equal opportunity to use and enjoy the property, and the district court improperly limited the treating provider's testimony about the child's need for privacy accommodations.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Gass v. Marriott Hotel Servs., Inc., 558 F.3d 419 (6th Cir. 2009)
Fielden v. CSX Transp., Inc., 482 F.3d 866 (6th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Katalin Bloodgood-Loper v. Lenard Loper; LVL Investments, LLC; Michelle L. Mercer
accommodation_description: The opinion does not identify any specific accommodation request; the FHA claims were dismissed for failure to plead facts showing defendants acted 'because of' plaintiff's protected class membership.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal because Bloodgood-Loper failed to allege facts sufficient to show defendants engaged in conduct made unlawful by the Fair Housing Act 'because of' her membership in a protected class under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a), (f).
brief_summary: Katalin Bloodgood-Loper, proceeding pro se, appealed the district court's dismissal of her federal claims including Fair Housing Act claims against Lenard Loper, LVL Investments, LLC, and Judge Michelle Mercer. The Ninth Circuit affirmed, holding that Bloodgood-Loper failed to plead sufficient facts showing the defendants' conduct was 'because of' her membership in a protected class under § 3604(a) and (f).
key_cases_cited: Hebbe v. Pliler, 627 F.3d 338 (9th Cir. 2010)
Schucker v. Rockwood, 846 F.2d 1202 (9th Cir. 1988)
Chudacoff v. Univ. Med. Ctr. of S. Nev., 649 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2011)
Price v. State of Hawaii, 939 F.2d 702 (9th Cir. 1991)
Karim-Panahi v. L.A. Police Dep't, 839 F.2d 621 (9th Cir. 1988)

case_name: Neway Mengistu v. Forestview Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an apartment with accessibility modifications, but defendants declined to rent due to the high cost of renovations.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants because plaintiff's FHA claims were barred by the two-year statute of limitations, having accrued when defendants informed him of their decision not to rent on May 11, 2017, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to raise a triable dispute that his requested accommodations were reasonable.
brief_summary: Neway Mengistu sued Forestview Apartments and its manager alleging Fair Housing Act violations after defendants decided not to rent him an apartment due to the high cost of accessibility renovations. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, holding that the claims were time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations and alternatively failed on the merits because Mengistu did not raise a triable dispute that his accommodation requests were reasonable.
key_cases_cited: Soto v. Sweetman, 882 F.3d 865 (9th Cir. 2018)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Comm. Concerning Cmty. Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Nora Dunn v. Carter Manuel, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a temporary substitute apartment and time to gather assistance to move and separate her personal items while her unit was being repaired due to her hoarding disorder.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because hoarding disorder does not qualify as a protected disability under the FHA since plaintiff failed to allege facts showing it substantially limits major life activities, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to plead that her requested accommodation was reasonable, proportional, or necessary to ameliorate her disability's effects.
brief_summary: Nora Dunn sued her landlord Sharon Hydinger alleging Fair Housing Act violations, including failure to accommodate her hoarding disorder, intentional discrimination, retaliation, and hostile environment harassment. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that hoarding disorder is not a protected disability under the FHA because it does not substantially limit major life activities, and alternatively that Dunn failed to plead the remaining elements of her accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Bonasera v. City of Norcross, 342 F. App'x 581 (11th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 10166836_2024_Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated_azd
case_name: Wayne Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated
accommodation_description: Disabled veteran requested permission to keep his garage door open while engaged in therapeutic woodworking inside his garage, as an accommodation from the HOA's rule requiring garage doors to remain closed at all times.
key_holding: The court reopened discovery to allow plaintiff to depose board members regarding emails about his accommodation request, finding that an email in which the board president called the garage door rule 'stupid' is relevant to whether the accommodation request was reasonable under § 3604(f)(3).
brief_summary: Wayne Hiatt, a disabled veteran, sued Sun City Festival Community Association under the Fair Housing Act alleging discrimination based on disability after the HOA denied his accommodation request to keep his garage door open while engaged in therapeutic woodworking. The court's order addressed discovery disputes, denying plaintiff's sanctions request, reopening discovery for limited depositions of board members regarding emails about the accommodation request, and denying defendant's motion to disqualify plaintiff's counsel.

source_file: 10166885_2024_Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated_azd
case_name: Wayne Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled veteran with PTSD and claustrophobia, requested to keep his garage door open while woodworking/engaging in activities in his garage as a therapeutic accommodation.
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff established four of five elements for a reasonable accommodation claim as undisputed facts, but denied summary judgment because a genuine dispute of material fact exists regarding whether keeping the garage door open was necessary to afford Plaintiff equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling, given evidence that Plaintiff could use a community woodworking facility and engaged in other activities without the door open.
brief_summary: Wayne Hiatt, a disabled veteran with PTSD and claustrophobia, sued Sun City Festival Community Association seeking partial summary judgment on his reasonable accommodation claim under the FHA, alleging the HOA's CC&Rs requiring garage doors to remain closed discriminated against his disability-related need to keep his garage door open while woodworking. The Court denied the motion, finding that while four of five elements were undisputed, a genuine dispute of material fact remains regarding whether the accommodation was necessary to afford Plaintiff equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Canady v. Prescott Canyon Ests. Homeowners Ass'n, 60 P.3d 231 (Ariz. Ct. App. 2002)

source_file: 10167251_2024_Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated_azd
case_name: Wayne Hiatt v. Sun City Festival Community Association Incorporated
accommodation_description: Request to waive the HOA's closed-garage-door rule to keep garage door open while engaging in therapeutic woodworking and other activities to manage PTSD symptoms
key_holding: Plaintiff raised serious questions going to the merits of his FHA reasonable accommodation claim but failed to establish irreparable harm due to his 17-month delay in seeking preliminary injunctive relief and his ability to endure the alleged harm during that period.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Wayne Hiatt, a disabled veteran with PTSD, sued the Sun City Festival Community Association seeking a preliminary injunction to allow him to keep his garage door open while woodworking and engaging in other therapeutic activities as a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act. The court denied the motion, finding that while Hiatt raised serious questions on the merits of his reasonable accommodation claim, he failed to demonstrate immediate irreparable harm given his 17-month delay in seeking injunctive relief.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)
Norman v. Rancho del Lago Cmty. Ass'n, No. 19-CV-00486-TUC-JAS-LCK, 2021 WL 5167305 (D. Ariz. Aug. 5, 2021)

source_file: 10167516_2024_Timothy Neilson v. KC Hotels Group, Inc._cacd
10167686_2024_The People of the State of California v. Rish Investments, Inc._cacd

case_name: Deepa Patel et al. v. City of Stanton et al.
accommodation_description: Motel owner and business entity allege City revoked motel permit and issued citations because City sought to drive out minority guests and Asian-Indian owner.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claim under § 3604(b) because the complaint failed to specify how plaintiffs themselves were injured by a discriminatory housing practice, what constituted the discriminatory practice, and which defendant was responsible, while also noting uncertainty whether the FHA applies to motels as temporary shelters.
brief_summary: Deepa Patel and California One Hospitality LLC, owners of Dixie Orange County Motel, sued the City of Stanton and unknown officials alleging the City revoked their motel permit and issued administrative citations because City officials sought to drive out low-income minority guests and the Asian-Indian owner. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to comply with Rule 8(a) pleading requirements, finding the FHA claim vague as to the discriminatory practice, injury, and responsible defendant, while granting leave to amend the federal claims and declining supplemental jurisdiction over state writ claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Cmty. House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)

case_name: United States of America v. Javier Salazar, Jr., et al.
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves sex discrimination and sexual harassment, not disability accommodation
key_holding: The court recommended granting default judgment against defendants for sexual harassment and retaliation in violation of the Fair Housing Act, awarding $45,000 in damages and permanent injunctive relief.
brief_summary: The United States brought this action against landlords and property managers alleging sex discrimination and sexual harassment of tenant Angela McGinnis, including unwelcome sexual comments, unwanted touching, and quid pro quo suggestions about rent. The court recommended granting default judgment, finding defendants liable for violations of 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604(a)-(c) and 3617, and awarded $45,000 in damages with permanent injunctive relief.
key_cases_cited: Eitel v. McCool, 782 F.2d 1470 (9th Cir. 1986)
Fair Hous. v. Combs, 285 F.3d 899 (9th Cir. 2002)
Kolstad v. Am. Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526 (1999)
Silver Sage Partners, Ltd. v. City of Desert Hot Springs, 251 F.3d 814 (9th Cir. 2001)
City of Chicago v. Matchmaker Real Est. Sales Ctr., Inc., 982 F.2d 1086 (7th Cir. 1992)

source_file: 10172026_2024_Filho v. Mercy Housing California XVII, LP_cand
case_name: Filho v. Mercy Housing California XVII, LP
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant prevent his ex-boyfriend from entering the property and provide a safe environment, citing a domestic violence restraining order and his disability-related need for a safe environment to enjoy his dwelling.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff's allegations sufficient to proceed against Mercy Housing on his FHA reasonable accommodation claim, but ordered Plaintiff to show cause why the case should not be dismissed as to defendant Tiscareno because Plaintiff failed to allege facts establishing individual liability under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Roberto Filho, who is HIV positive and has traumatic brain injury, sued Mercy Housing California alleging the landlord failed to reasonably accommodate his disability by refusing to prevent his ex-boyfriend from entering the property despite a domestic violence restraining order. The court found his FHA reasonable accommodation claim sufficient as to Mercy Housing (the property owner) at the screening stage, but ordered Plaintiff to show cause why the claim should not be dismissed as to defendant Tiscareno (property manager) for failure to allege individual liability under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)

case_name: Prado v. City of Berkeley
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, unhoused disabled individuals, request reasonable accommodations to the City's shelter program policies, including visitor policies, emotional support animal policies, and flexible rule enforcement for residents with mental health disabilities.
key_holding: The Court denied dismissal of the FHAA claim as plaintiffs adequately alleged they were denied reasonable accommodations in the City's shelter programs, though some individual accommodation requests (visitors, mental health workers) were dismissed with leave to amend.
brief_summary: Seven unhoused disabled individuals and advocacy organization WDWG sued the City of Berkeley alleging violations of the ADA, FHA, Fourth Amendment, and due process related to the City's homeless encampment abatement and shelter programs. The Court denied the City's motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged they were denied accommodations including emotional support animals and flexible shelter rules, while dismissing certain individual accommodation requests with leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Grants Pass v. Johnson, 603 U.S. ___, 144 S. Ct. 2202 (2024)
Martinez v. City of Clovis, 943 F.3d 1260 (9th Cir. 2019)
Sanchez v. City of Fresno, 914 F. Supp. 2d 1079 (E.D. Cal. 2012)

case_name: Gilead Community Services, Inc. v. Town of Cromwell
accommodation_description: Group home operator challenged municipality's discriminatory opposition campaign including petitions to state health officials, cease-and-desist letters, tax exemption denial, and alleged harassment of disabled residents.
key_holding: The court denied the Town of Cromwell's post-trial motions for judgment as a matter of law and new trial, affirming the jury's verdict that the Town violated the FHA and ADA through discriminatory conduct designed to force the group home to close.
brief_summary: Gilead Community Services, a group home operator, and the Connecticut Fair Housing Center sued the Town of Cromwell alleging the municipality violated the Fair Housing Act by orchestrating a campaign of discriminatory opposition to force closure of a group home for people with mental health disabilities through petitions to state officials, cease-and-desist letters, tax exemption denial, and harassment. Following a six-day trial, the jury found for plaintiffs and awarded $181,000 in compensatory damages and $5,000,000 in punitive damages; the court denied the Town's post-trial motions challenging the verdict.
key_cases_cited: Gilead Cmty. Servs. v. Cromwell
Cobb v. Pozzi, 363 F.3d 89 (2d Cir. 2004)
Nassau County Strip Search Cases, 783 F.3d 414 (2d Cir. 2015)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)

case_name: Edgar Willis v. Nelson Ng
accommodation_description: Willis alleged landlord harassment and lease non-renewal due to race, with a passing reference to denial of unspecified reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA claim because Willis's conclusory allegations of race-based harassment, retaliatory rent increases, and lease non-renewal lack sufficient factual detail to give the defendant fair notice of the claims and the grounds upon which they rest.
brief_summary: Edgar Willis, proceeding pro se, sued his landlord Nelson Ng asserting Fair Housing Act and Section 1981 race discrimination claims, alleging harassment, retaliatory rent increases, and lease non-renewal. The court dismissed the complaint as frivolous under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e) because the complaint failed to allege sufficient factual matter to state plausible claims for relief.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Jackson v. Okaloosa Cnty. Fla., 21 F.3d 1531 (11th Cir. 1994)
Bonasera v. City of Norcross, 342 F. App'x 581 (11th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Sunshine H. Hauanio v. The Michaels Organization
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged that defendant placed two towing stickers on her vehicle while placing only one on others' vehicles, claiming discriminatory treatment in housing matters
key_holding: The FHA housing discrimination claim was dismissed because plaintiff failed to provide sufficient factual allegations to show that defendant's placement of two towing stickers on her vehicle (versus one on others' vehicles) was discriminatory or retaliatory rather than merely conclusory.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sunshine H. Hauanio, proceeding pro se, sued her former employer and landlord The Michaels Organization alleging employment discrimination under Title VII, the ADA, and the Pregnancy Discrimination Act, along with housing discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted TMO's motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim, finding that while the housing discrimination claim invoked the FHA, the SAC provided insufficient factual matter to support any plausible discriminatory motive regarding the placement of towing stickers on plaintiff's vehicle compared to others.
key_cases_cited: Peterson v. Hewlett-Packard Co., 358 F.3d 599 (9th Cir. 2004)
Young v. UPS, Inc., 575 U.S. 206 (2015)
Groff v. DeJoy, 600 U.S. 447 (2023)
Villiarimo v. Aloha Island Air, Inc., 281 F.3d 1054 (9th Cir. 2002)
Craig v. M & O Agencies, Inc., 496 F.3d 1047 (9th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Les Bock and Dunnia E. Aplicano v. Brentwood Homeowners' Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested permission to relandscape their property with low-maintenance materials to accommodate mobility limitations from rheumatoid arthritis and complications from a childhood accident.
key_holding: Although plaintiffs adequately alleged injury-in-fact for standing purposes based on emotional distress damages, they failed to state a reasonable modification claim because they did not adequately allege that the Association knew or should have known of their disabilities, nor that the modification request was made because of disability.
brief_summary: Les Bock and Dunnia Aplicano, homeowners with mobility disabilities, sued the Brentwood Homeowners' Association after it rejected their architectural application to relandscape their property with low-maintenance materials. The district court dismissed their Fair Housing Act reasonable modification claim for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiffs did not adequately allege the Association knew of their disabilities or that the modification request was disability-related, though the court recognized plaintiffs had standing based on alleged emotional distress damages.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Jane Doe v. University of Idaho
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request analyzed; plaintiffs' FHA claims focus on alleged race, national origin, and sex discrimination affecting their ability to remain in the Moscow, Idaho community.
key_holding: The FHA does not abrogate Eleventh Amendment immunity, so claims against the state university are barred; additionally, plaintiffs failed to adequately plead housing discrimination claims because they did not allege any defendant committed discriminatory conduct related to actual rentals, negotiations, terms, or advertising of dwellings.
brief_summary: Jane Doe, Kelly Doe, and John Doe, former law students at the University of Idaho College of Law, sued the University, University officials, and a fellow student under the Fair Housing Act and other statutes, alleging discrimination based on race, national origin, and sex that made living in Moscow, Idaho unsafe and caused them to leave. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, ruling the Eleventh Amendment bars claims against the state university and that plaintiffs failed to state plausible FHA claims because they did not allege discriminatory conduct related to actual housing transactions.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Bd. of Trs. of Univ. of Ala. v. Garrett, 531 U.S. 356 (2001)
Tennessee v. Lane, 541 U.S. 509 (2004)

case_name: Valencia v. City of Springfield, Illinois
accommodation_description: The underlying case involved a group home for persons with disabilities seeking reasonable accommodation from the City's 600-foot spacing rule and its definition of family, which prohibited disabled individuals from living together in the same manner as non-disabled individuals.
key_holding: The court granted in part Private Plaintiffs' motion for attorneys' fees and litigation expenses, awarding $380,350 in attorneys' fees and $33,072.19 in costs, finding the hours expended on zoning matters and discovery were reasonably necessary to advance the successful FHA claims.
brief_summary: Private Plaintiffs Mary B. Valencia, B.A., and Individual Advocacy Group, Inc. moved for reasonable attorneys' fees and litigation expenses following their successful FHA and ADA action against the City of Springfield, Illinois, which had discriminated against a group home for persons with disabilities through its 600-foot spacing rule. The court granted the motion in part, awarding $380,350 in attorneys' fees and $33,072.19 in costs after finding all billed hours were reasonably expended.
key_cases_cited: Valencia II, 446 F. Supp. 3d 369
Valencia I, 883 F.3d 959
Hensley v. Eckerhart, 461 U.S. 424
Buckhannon Bd. & Care Home, Inc. v. W. Va. Dep't of Health & Hum. Res., 532 U.S. 598
Tsombanidis v. West Haven Fire Department, 352 F.3d 565

source_file: 10178561_2019_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Deutsche Bank National Trust_ilnd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Deutsche Bank National Trust
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; this case concerns alleged racial discrimination in the maintenance and marketing of bank-owned foreclosed properties in communities of color.
key_holding: The Court held that fair housing organizations adequately pleaded proximate cause for injuries from diversion of resources and frustration of mission, and sufficiently stated disparate impact and disparate treatment claims based on racial disparities in REO property maintenance, but dismissed claims for lost economic value of community investments and harms to minority neighborhoods as damages.
brief_summary: Fair housing organizations sued Deutsche Bank entities and loan servicers alleging that defendants' discriminatory maintenance and marketing of bank-owned REO properties in communities of color violated the FHA. The court largely denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pleaded proximate cause and stated plausible disparate impact and disparate treatment claims, but dismissed damages claims for lost community investment value.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Kemper v. Deutsche Bank AG, 911 F.3d 383 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 10178987_2024_Connectors Realty Group Corporation, The v. State Farm Fire _ Casualty Company_ilnd
case_name: Connectors Realty Group Corporation v. State Farm Fire & Casualty Company
accommodation_description: Not applicable - this case involves alleged racial discrimination in insurance claim denials based on zip code demographics, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on the race discrimination claims (Counts I-II) because genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether State Farm's investigation and denial of insurance claims was motivated by race, but denied class certification because individual issues predominate and plaintiff Williams presented serious credibility problems making him inadequate as a class representative.
brief_summary: The Connectors Realty Group Corporation and Darryl Williams sued State Farm Fire & Casualty Company alleging the insurer violated the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. §§ 1981-1982 by discriminatorily investigating and denying insurance claims for properties located in majority African-American zip codes. The court denied State Farm's motion for summary judgment on the discrimination claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist based on evidence including a former manager's testimony about a discriminatory policy and statistical analysis showing correlation between zip code demographics and claim denials, but denied the plaintiffs' motion for class certification because individual causation questions predominate and Williams' credibility issues made him an inadequate class representative.
key_cases_cited: N.A.A.C.P. v. American Family Mutual Insurance Co., 978 F.2d 287 (7th Cir. 1992)
United Farm Bureau Mutual Insurance Co. v. Metropolitan Human Relations Commission, 24 F.3d 1008 (7th Cir. 1994)
Thompson v. North American Stainless, LP, 562 U.S. 170 (2011)
Shaikh v. City of Chicago, 2001 WL 123784 (N.D. Ill. 2001)
CE Design Ltd. v. King Architectural Metals, Inc., 637 F.3d 721 (7th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 10179253_2024_Legal Aid Chicago v. Hunter Properties, Inc._ilnd
case_name: Legal Aid Chicago v. Hunter Properties, Inc.
accommodation_description: Fair housing organization challenges landlord's blanket no-evictions screening policy as having disparate impact on Black renters
key_holding: The court granted dismissal for lack of standing, finding Legal Aid Chicago failed to allege a concrete injury beyond ordinary program costs because the alleged injuries (sealing eviction records, litigating eviction cases, education) were precisely the organization's core mission activities rather than new burdens imposed by the defendant.
brief_summary: Legal Aid Chicago sued Hunter Properties, a Chicago property management company, alleging that its blanket no-evictions screening policy violated the Fair Housing Act by having a disparate impact on Black renters. The district court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that Legal Aid Chicago lacked organizational standing because it failed to allege a concrete injury beyond ordinary program costs—the activities it claimed as injuries (litigating eviction cases, sealing records, providing education) were precisely the organization's core mission, not new burdens imposed by the defendant's policy.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Common Cause Indiana v. Lawson, 937 F.3d 944 (7th Cir. 2019)
Democratic Party of Wisconsin v. Vos, 966 F.3d 581 (7th Cir. 2020)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 10179261_2024_Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago v. City Of Chicago_ilnd
case_name: Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago, Inc. v. City of Chicago
subsidy_program: HUD federal funding, HOME Investment Partnerships Program
accommodation_description: Access Living alleges the City of Chicago's affordable housing funding and oversight program fails to ensure compliance with federal accessibility requirements, effectively excluding people with disabilities from affordable housing.
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment for the City, finding genuine issues of fact on whether Chicago's affordable housing scheme is a 'program or activity' subject to ADA, Section 504, and FHA liability, and whether the City's role in funding, approving construction, and contracting for compliance makes it responsible for design-and-construction accessibility failures.
brief_summary: Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago sued the City of Chicago alleging that the City's affordable housing funding program discriminates against people with disabilities by failing to ensure housing developments comply with federal accessibility laws under the ADA, Section 504, and Fair Housing Act. The court denied the City's motion for summary judgment, finding triable issues of fact on whether Chicago's substantial involvement in funding, approving, and contracting for accessibility compliance renders the City liable for design-and-construction accessibility failures under the FHA and for program accessibility under the ADA and Section 504.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Oconomowoc Res. Programs, Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Ashby v. Warrick Cnty. Sch. Corp., 908 F.3d 225 (7th Cir. 2018)
Alexander v. Choate, 469 U.S. 287 (1985)
N.A.A.C.P. v. Am. Fam. Mut. Ins. Co., 978 F.2d 287 (7th Cir. 1992)

source_file: 10180547_2024_FAIR HOUSING CENTER OF CENTRAL INDIANA, INC. v. M_J MANAGEMENT COMPANY, LLC, d_b_a THE SEXTON COMPANIES_insd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana, Inc. v. M&J Management Company, LLC, d/b/a The Sexton Companies
accommodation_description: Two-person-per-bedroom occupancy policy that excludes families with three or more minor children from renting two-bedroom units
key_holding: The court found that plaintiff established a prima facie case of disparate impact based on familial status, but denied both parties' summary judgment motions because genuine disputes exist regarding whether defendants' occupancy policy is necessary to achieve substantial, legitimate, nondiscriminatory interests.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana sued M&J Management (Sexton Companies) alleging that defendants' two-person-per-bedroom occupancy policy has a disparate impact on families with minor children in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding that while plaintiff established a prima facie case, genuine disputes remain about whether defendants' occupancy standard is necessary to achieve substantial, legitimate, nondiscriminatory interests.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Comm. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 560 (1992)
City of Joliet v. New W., L.P., 825 F.3d 827 (7th Cir. 2016)
Louisiana Fair Hous. Action Cntr., Inc. v. Azalea Garden Properties, LLC, 82 F.4th 345 (5th Cir. 2023)

source_file: 10180724_2024_United States v. Fox Run Apartments, LLC_ksd
case_name: United States of America v. Fox Run Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Veteran with PTSD/ADHD/depression requested to keep his 70-pound Doberman Pinscher emotional support animal despite a 25-pound pet weight limit.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion for attorney's fees and costs under the EAJA, finding the government's FHA reasonable accommodation claim was neither frivolous nor lacking substantial justification, as there was a reasonable basis for the factual allegations and legal theories supporting the failure-to-accommodate claim.
brief_summary: The United States sued Fox Run Apartments and related defendants under the FHA alleging failure to accommodate a veteran's ESA request for a 70-pound Doberman Pinscher, but voluntarily dismissed the claims; the court then denied defendants' motion for attorney's fees and costs under the EAJA, finding the government's position was substantially justified because the record supported that the veteran had a disability, requested a reasonable accommodation, and defendants failed to offer alternatives before he withdrew his application.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Christiansburg Garment Co. v. EEOC, 434 U.S. 412 (1978)
Hadden v. Bowen, 851 F.2d 1266 (10th Cir. 1988)
Pierce v. Underwood, 487 U.S. 552 (1988)
Gutierrez v. Sullivan, 953 F.2d 579 (10th Cir. 1992)

source_file: 10181821_2024_Military Road Revitalization Company, LLC v. St. Tammany Parish Government_laed
case_name: Military Road Revitalization Company, LLC et al. v. St. Tammany Parish Government et al.
accommodation_description: Developer sued municipality over moratorium ordinance allegedly blocking affordable housing development serving protected classes including persons with disabilities
key_holding: The court granted Safety National's motion to dismiss the intentional discrimination FHA claims as excluded from insurance coverage but denied the motion as to the disparate-impact FHA claim because it does not require a showing of intent and therefore does not fall within the policy's exclusion for intentional violations of law.
brief_summary: Military Road Revitalization Company sued St. Tammany Parish Government and its insurer Safety National, alleging that a municipal moratorium ordinance blocking an affordable housing development violated the Fair Housing Act by having a disparate impact on protected classes including African Americans, Hispanics, and persons with disabilities. The court granted Safety National's motion to dismiss the intentional discrimination claims as excluded from insurance coverage but denied the motion as to the disparate-impact claim, finding it does not require a showing of intent and therefore may be covered.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10181920_2024_Stennis v. The Moorings of Oak Harbor Property Owners' Association LLC_laed
case_name: Shalmon J. Stennis et al. v. The Moorings of Oak Harbor Property Owners' Association Inc. et al.
accommodation_description: Black homeowners allege HOA defendants selectively enforced community restrictions, issued fines, deactivated entry decal, and engaged in coordinated harassment against plaintiffs because of their race.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims under §§ 3604(a) and (b), finding plaintiffs adequately alleged discriminatory enforcement of HOA restrictions based on race and a hostile environment claim, but granted dismissal of plaintiffs' intentional infliction of emotional distress claims as insufficiently pleaded.
brief_summary: Black homeowners sued their homeowners association, property management company, and board president alleging race-based discrimination and harassment including selective enforcement of community restrictions, excessive fines, and deactivation of entry access. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims under §§ 3604(a) and (b), finding sufficient factual allegations of discriminatory treatment and hostile environment, but dismissed plaintiffs' state law intentional infliction of emotional distress claims as insufficiently pleaded.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Maldonado v. FirstService Residential, Inc., No. CV H-20-1484 (S.D. Tex. 2021)

source_file: 10182858_2022_Estate of DaMon R. Fisher v. City of Annapolis_mdd
case_name: Estate of DaMon R. Fisher v. City of Annapolis
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request at issue; this case concerns HACA's policy of not inspecting and licensing public housing, which allegedly disproportionately harmed African American residents and contributed to tenant DaMon Fisher's death from mold-related respiratory illness.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of Fisher's FHA disparate impact claim, finding that the non-inspection policy bore more heavily on African Americans, historical background supported an inference of discriminatory intent, and Fisher adequately alleged injury from discriminatory housing conditions; however, the § 1986 claim was dismissed as untimely.
brief_summary: DaMon Fisher's estate sued the City of Annapolis and the Housing Authority of the City of Annapolis (HACA) alleging that the City's longstanding policy of exempting HACA public housing from inspection and licensing requirements violated the Fair Housing Act and civil rights laws, and that resulting mold conditions in Fisher's apartment caused his death. The court denied dismissal of Fisher's FHA disparate impact claim, finding sufficient allegations that the non-inspection policy disproportionately harmed African American public housing residents and supported an inference of discriminatory intent, while dismissing the § 1986 claim as untimely.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Develop. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
White v. City of Annapolis, 439 F. Supp. 3d 527 (D. Md. 2020)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10182963_2024_Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc._mdd
case_name: Edward Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to install storage sheds on his front porch and at the back of his house as a reasonable modification/accommodation for his disability.
key_holding: The court held that the BFMA Defendants failed to establish clear error warranting reconsideration, as their arguments merely reasserted disagreements with the court's prior ruling that their role in making recommendations to deny the storage shed requests was sufficient to state an FHA violation.
brief_summary: Edward Siguel sued King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc. and related defendants after they denied his requests for storage sheds as a reasonable accommodation/modification under the Fair Housing Act. The court denied the BFMA Defendants' motion for reconsideration of the court's prior order refusing to dismiss Count VI (storage sheds), finding the defendants merely reargued their position without meeting the high bar for reconsideration.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
U.S. Tobacco Coop., Inc. v. Big South Wholesale of Va., LLC, 899 F.3d 236 (4th Cir. 2018)
Carlson v. Bos. Sci. Corp, 856 F.3d 320 (4th Cir. 2017)
Fair Hous. Ctr. of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Cmty. Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 141 F. Supp. 3d 1321 (S.D. Fla. 2015)

source_file: 10183030_2024_Bailey v. The Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners, Inc._mdd
case_name: Rosanna Bailey v. The Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners, Inc.
accommodation_description: Dr. Bailey alleges she was denied repairs, maintenance, and services (including remediation for mold, door replacements, video cameras) because of her disability and race, while similarly situated non-disabled, white neighbors received these services.
key_holding: The court dismissed the sex discrimination claim for failure to state a claim, but denied the motion to dismiss on the race discrimination, disability discrimination, and retaliation claims, finding sufficient timely allegations that the Council treated Dr. Bailey differently because of her race and disability.
brief_summary: Dr. Rosanna Bailey, a Black woman with disabilities, sued her condominium association alleging race and disability discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, claiming she received inferior repair services and maintenance compared to white, non-disabled neighbors. The court granted the motion to dismiss the sex discrimination claim as conclusory, but denied dismissal on the race and disability discrimination and retaliation claims, finding she alleged sufficient facts to proceed past the pleadings stage.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Tamara Johnson, et al. v. City of Annapolis
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; this is a disparate impact challenge to the City's non-enforcement of rental licensing and inspection requirements for HACA public housing properties
key_holding: The plaintiffs' renewed motion for summary judgment on FHA disparate impact liability was denied because, although the City's non-enforcement policy and the poor conditions at HACA properties are undisputed, the plaintiffs failed to establish the necessary causal link between the City's illegal conduct and the plaintiffs' injury given that the causal inference is not ineluctable and additional discovery is warranted.
brief_summary: Black public housing tenants and the Estate of DaMon R. Fisher sued the City of Annapolis alleging that the City's decades-long non-enforcement of rental licensing and inspection requirements for Housing Authority of the City of Annapolis (HACA) properties violated the Fair Housing Act by disparately impacting almost exclusively Black renters. The court denied the plaintiffs' renewed motion for summary judgment on disparate impact liability because while the City's discriminatory policy and poor housing conditions were undisputed, the plaintiffs failed to establish proximate causation given the debatable causal inference and the need for additional discovery.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo & Co., 14 F.4th 1030 (9th Cir. 2021) (en banc)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 10183095_2024_Estate of DaMon R. Fisher v. City of Annapolis_mdd
case_name: Estate of DaMon R. Fisher v. City of Annapolis
accommodation_description: Challenge to City's exemption of public housing from inspection/licensing requirements, alleged to have disparate impact on Black renters
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment on disparate impact claim because, although the City's non-enforcement policy and poor conditions at HACA properties were largely undisputed, the causal connection between the policy and the alleged harm remained genuinely disputed, precluding summary judgment before adequate discovery.
brief_summary: The Estate of DaMon R. Fisher and other plaintiffs sued the City of Annapolis alleging the City's decades-long policy of exempting Housing Authority of the City of Annapolis (HACA) public housing properties from its rental property inspection/licensing regime violated the Fair Housing Act by having a disparate impact on Black renters. The court denied plaintiffs' renewed motion for summary judgment on disparate impact liability because causation remained genuinely disputed—while evidence showed the non-enforcement policy and poor conditions at HACA properties, plaintiffs failed to establish as a matter of law that the policy directly caused the alleged harm.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo & Co., 14 F.4th 1030 (9th Cir. 2021)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Spann v. Colonial Vill., Inc., 899 F.2d 24 (D.C. Cir. 1990)
Md. Shall Issue, Inc. v. Anne Arundel Cnty., 662 F. Supp. 3d 557 (D. Md. 2023)

case_name: Doll v. Trellis Walnut Towers LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that the elevator work consistently or that Defendants otherwise secure reliable access to their apartments as an accommodation for their mobility disabilities.
key_holding: The reasonable accommodation claim failed because Plaintiffs' request that the elevator 'work consistently' was not reasonably specific and the complaint did not plausibly allege that the elevator functioned worse than it should for a building of its age.
brief_summary: Shannon Doll and Brittney Mandery, wheelchair-bound tenants in a Mankato, Minnesota apartment building, sued their landlord and related entities alleging the building's repeated elevator outages over thirteen months constituted disability discrimination under the FHAA, Section 504, and Minnesota state law. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that while the elevator outages affected Plaintiffs more severely, the complaint failed to state a plausible disparate treatment, disparate impact, or failure-to-accommodate claim because the accommodation request was not reasonably specific.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Housing & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Congdon v. Strine, 854 F. Supp. 355 (E.D. Pa. 1994)

source_file: 10186174_2024_Baptist Homes, Inc. v. City of Madison, Mississippi_mssd
case_name: Baptist Homes, Inc. v. City of Madison, Mississippi
accommodation_description: Baptist Homes requested a reasonable accommodation from the City's R-1 single-family zoning to house four unrelated adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities who would receive residential support services.
key_holding: Baptist Homes adequately stated a FHA reasonable accommodation claim where the nonprofit alleged it requested a zoning accommodation for a home serving disabled adults but the City refused to process its rental license application and failed to respond to multiple accommodation requests.
brief_summary: Baptist Homes, a nonprofit providing residential support services to adults with disabilities, sued the City of Madison, Mississippi after the City refused to process its rental license application for a home intended to house four unrelated disabled adults in an R-1 residential zone. The court denied the City's motion to dismiss, holding that Baptist Homes adequately alleged standing and stated plausible FHA and ADA reasonable accommodation claims where the City's zoning ordinance provisions specifically target the type of zoning regulations at issue in disability housing cases.
key_cases_cited: UNDETERMINED

source_file: 10187994_2023_Brookline Opportunities, LLC v. Brookline, NH, Town of_nhd
case_name: Brookline Opportunities, LLC et al. v. Town of Brookline et al.
accommodation_description: Developers seek to build workforce housing and challenge municipal growth moratorium and growth management ordinance as discriminatory barriers to housing for families with children, Black, and Latino households.
key_holding: The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the Developers' disparate treatment claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the Town's moratorium and growth management ordinance were enacted with discriminatory intent, while granting summary judgment only to the Selectboard on grounds of ministerial action.
brief_summary: Two real estate development companies sued the Town of Brookline, New Hampshire alleging that its moratorium on building permits and growth management ordinance violated the Fair Housing Act by discriminating against families with children, Black, and Latino households who would likely occupy the proposed workforce housing development. The court denied cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact on the Developers' disparate treatment claims regarding discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10188107_2019_BLAKE GARDENS, LLC v. STATE OF NEW JERSEY_njd
case_name: Blake Gardens, LLC v. State of New Jersey
accommodation_description: Developer Blake Gardens challenged New Jersey's 2015 Act requiring use variances for Alzheimer's residences as facially discriminatory against persons with disabilities under the FHA.
key_holding: The 2015 Act facially violates the Fair Housing Act by treating Alzheimer's residences differently from other community residences without justification, as the State failed to demonstrate no alternative less discriminatory course of action was available.
brief_summary: Blake Gardens, LLC, a developer of Alzheimer's residences, sued the State of New Jersey challenging P.L. 2015 c.125, which reclassified dementia care homes as health care facilities requiring use variances in residential zones while allowing similar community residences for other disabilities without such requirements. The court granted Blake Gardens' motion for summary judgment, finding the statute facially discriminates against persons with Alzheimer's disease under the Fair Housing Act because the State failed to justify treating these residences differently from other community residences for disabled persons.
key_cases_cited: Arc of New Jersey, Inc. v. State of N.J., 950 F. Supp. 637 (D.N.J. 1996)
Association for Advancement of the Mentally Handicapped v. City of Elizabeth, 876 F. Supp. 614 (D.N.J. 1994)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10189170_2023_431 E PALISADE AVENUE REAL ESTATE, LLC v. CITY OF ENGLEWOOD_njd
case_name: 431 E Palisade Avenue Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood
accommodation_description: Zoning ordinance restricts assisted-living facilities to industrial zones, restricting elderly and handicapped persons' housing choices; no specific individual accommodation request is made.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of Plaintiffs' disparate impact claims under the FHA, ADA, and RA, finding that restricting assisted-living facilities to industrial zones adequately pleaded greater adverse impact on elderly handicapped persons, including Orthodox Jews who cannot walk to synagogues from industrial districts on Shabbat.
brief_summary: LLC developers proposing to establish an assisted-living facility in Englewood, NJ sued the City, alleging that zoning restrictions confining assisted-living facilities to industrial zones while permitting other residential uses in residential zones constitute disparate impact discrimination against elderly and handicapped persons under the FHA, ADA, and RA. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss the federal disability claims, finding Plaintiffs adequately pleaded disparate impact, but dismissed the Equal Protection Act claim for lack of sufficient allegations of discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: 431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Lapid-Laurel v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of the Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10189406_2024_CAREONE AT BIRCHWOOD, LLC v. TOWNSHIP OF EDISON_njd
case_name: CareOne at Birchwood, LLC v. Township of Edison
accommodation_description: Developer sought a use variance and bulk variances to construct a 106-bed, 3-story assisted living facility in a residential zoning district as a reasonable accommodation for elderly and disabled individuals.
key_holding: The court found that the Township's zoning code has a discriminatory disparate impact on disabled individuals by excluding assisted living facilities from all residential zones, but also found genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the specific 106-bed, 3-story design of the proposed facility was 'necessary' to provide disabled individuals an equal opportunity to housing under the reasonable accommodation analysis.
brief_summary: CareOne at Birchwood, LLC sued Edison Township and its Zoning Board alleging the municipal zoning code, which excludes assisted living facilities from all residential districts, violates the Fair Housing Act by discriminating against elderly and disabled individuals who require congregate care housing. The court granted partial summary judgment to plaintiffs on the disparate impact claim, finding the zoning ordinance disproportionately affects disabled persons, but denied cross-motions on the reasonable accommodation claim because disputed facts exist regarding whether the specific size and design of the proposed 106-bed facility was necessary to provide disabled individuals an equal opportunity to housing.
key_cases_cited: Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Tower Oaks, Inc., 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Yates Real Est., Inc. v. Plainfield Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 404 F. Supp. 3d 889 (D.N.J. 2019)

case_name: Nelson v. Claussen
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege FHA discrimination based on race, religion, and citizenship status in connection with HOA services, but fail to articulate a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Act claim was dismissed with prejudice because Plaintiffs' SAC contained only conclusory statements about discrimination based on race, religion, and citizenship with no factual matter to plausibly state a claim, and amendment would be futile and inequitable.
brief_summary: Three homeowners sued over forty defendants including their HOA (Monmouth Hills, Inc.), property management, and individual residents for alleged Fair Housing Act violations based on race, religion, and citizenship status. The Court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding the SAC contained only conclusory allegations devoid of factual support and that amendment would be futile and inequitable.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Jerry Smith v. Supportive Housing Coalition
accommodation_description: Vague allegations of failure to move plaintiff to safe living conditions due to toxic mold in dwelling unit, with complaint failing to identify any specific disability-related accommodation request.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a plausible FHA claim because it contains no facts establishing plaintiff's membership in a protected class or a causal connection between protected status and any discriminatory conduct, but plaintiff is granted leave to amend.
brief_summary: Jerry Smith sued Supportive Housing Coalition, the City of Albuquerque, and Laura Chavez alleging Fair Housing Act violations and failure to provide safe living conditions free from toxic mold. The federal court found the complaint deficient for failing to allege facts establishing plaintiff's protected class membership and a causal connection between that status and discriminatory conduct, granting plaintiff fourteen days to amend the complaint or face dismissal and remand to state court.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly

case_name: Jerry Smith v. Supportive Housing Coalition
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be transferred to a safer unit as an accommodation for his mental health condition.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice because plaintiff failed to establish a causal connection between his protected status (disability or familial status) and the adverse actions (unsafe living conditions, termination of rental assistance), and his claim addresses general housing conditions that would affect anyone regardless of disability.
brief_summary: Jerry Smith sued Supportive Housing Coalition, the City of Albuquerque, and an employee claiming violations of the Fair Housing Act, alleging defendants failed to accommodate his disability by refusing to transfer him to safer housing and retaliated by terminating his rental assistance. The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice, finding plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing a causal connection between his protected status and the alleged adverse actions.
key_cases_cited: Morgan v. Carrington Mortg. Servs., 719 F. App'x 735 (10th Cir. 2017)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Knight v. Mooring Cap. Fund, LLC, 749 F.3d 1180 (10th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Terrance Dixon v. New York City Housing Development Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discrimination in the housing application process based on race and familial status through community preferences and occupancy policies.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state FHA claims because he did not plausibly allege discriminatory intent to discriminate based on race, familial status, or national origin, and failed to adequately plead protected activity for his retaliation claim under § 3617.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Terrance Dixon sued the New York City Housing Development Corporation and multiple related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations, § 1982 and § 1983 claims, and RICO violations arising from the denial of his application for affordable housing. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent, failed to adequately plead protected activity for his retaliation claim, and failed to state any viable federal claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Sykes v. Mel S. Harris & Assocs. LLC, 780 F.3d 70 (2d Cir. 2015)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10193270_2024_Chestnut Hill NY, Inc. v. City of Kingston_nynd
case_name: Chestnut Hill NY, Inc. v. City of Kingston
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that the Planning Board waive fire safety requirements and accept the structural engineer's reports regarding the fire escape as a reasonable accommodation to continue operating the group home for persons with disabilities.
key_holding: The reasonable accommodation claim was properly dismissed because plaintiffs failed to allege facts showing how the requested waiver of fire safety requirements was related to their disabilities, as required to demonstrate the accommodation was necessary to afford them an equal opportunity to use and enjoy housing; however, the disparate treatment claim survived because plaintiffs adequately alleged facts supporting a plausible inference of disability-based animus influencing the permit denial.
brief_summary: Chestnut Hill NY, Inc. and individual residents sued the City of Kingston challenging the denial of renewal of a special use permit for a group home serving persons with mental health disabilities and recovering from substance use. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim (brought under the ADA) and the Fourteenth Amendment due process claim, but denied dismissal of the Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claim finding plaintiffs adequately alleged disability-based animus influenced the permit denial.
key_cases_cited: Tsombanidis v. West Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. Cty. of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Innovative Health Sys., Inc. v. City of White Plains, 117 F.3d 37 (2d Cir. 1997)
Laflamme v. New Horizons, Inc., 605 F. Supp. 2d 378 (D. Conn. 2009)

case_name: Amanda David and Rootwork Herbals, LLC v. Robert Whittaker, Jr.
accommodation_description: Neighbor harassment claims based on race and sexuality (not a reasonable accommodation case)
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged hostile housing environment and retaliation claims under § 3617, disparate treatment under § 3604(c), and claims under §§ 1981 and 1982.
brief_summary: Amanda David and her business Rootwork Herbals sued neighbor Robert Whittaker alleging nearly three years of racial and sexual harassment including epithets, threats, property damage, and vandalism that forced Rootwork to cancel workshops. The court denied Whittaker's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged hostile housing environment and retaliation claims under § 3617, disparate treatment under § 3604(c), and claims under §§ 1981 and 1982.
key_cases_cited: Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 144 S. Ct. 2244 (2024)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Mohamed v. McLaurin, 390 F. Supp. 3d 520 (D. Vt. 2019)

case_name: Michael O. Williams v. Settle Homes
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was refused an apartment because of his income and choice of occupation as a student and stock market trader.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims were properly dismissed because income and source of income are not protected classes under the FHA, and Plaintiff failed to allege membership in any enumerated protected class (race, color, religion, sex, familial status, national origin, or disability).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Michael Williams sued Settle Homes and two individuals alleging they refused to rent him an apartment because of his income and occupation as a student and stock market trader. The Court dismissed the FHA claims for failure to state a claim because income and source of income are not protected classes under the Fair Housing Act, and the Court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over state law source-of-income discrimination claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Short v. Manhattan Apartments, Inc., 916 F. Supp. 2d 375 (S.D.N.Y. 2012)

case_name: Loren Leon v. Mary Kearney, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case concerns alleged race-based discriminatory treatment including false accusations of subletting, required escort of cleaning staff, and posting of ID at doorman's desk.
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss plaintiff's FHA race discrimination claims because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient factual matter to support even a minimal inference of discriminatory intent, offering only conclusory assertions without comparator facts or evidence that similarly situated tenants outside her protected class were treated differently.
brief_summary: Loren Leon, a Black female tenant, sued Mary Kearney, Melissa Hines, Joni Hughes, Landing Living LLC, and Related Rentals Corporate alleging Fair Housing Act race discrimination, Fourth Amendment, and state law claims after being accused of subletting her apartment and subjected to what she characterized as discriminatory treatment. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff's FHA claims consisted of conclusory allegations of racial profiling without factual support, failed to identify similarly situated comparators treated differently, and did not allege conduct sufficiently severe or pervasive to create a hostile housing environment.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
