A.L.M. ex rel. Moore v. Bd. of Managers of Vireum Schoolhouse Condo., 2021 WL 5121137 (2d Cir. 2021)

case_name: Goldstein v. The Bowery Defendants
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claims exclusion from Bowery Mission shelter due to antisemitism (religious discrimination), not a request for disability accommodation.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims against Bowery Mission defendants were dismissed because his allegations do not support an inference of discriminatory motivation based on his Jewish religion, and district courts have concluded the FHA does not apply to shelters.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jesse Goldstein sued the Bowery Mission and numerous other defendants claiming religious discrimination (antisemitism) when he was excluded from a homeless shelter, invoking the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the FHA claims for failure to state a claim, finding no plausible inference of discriminatory intent and noting that courts have held the FHA does not apply to shelters, while also severing unrelated claims against other defendants.

source_file: 10195352_2024_Hall v. Crestwood Lake Section 8 Holding Corporation_nysd
case_name: Lisa Hall v. Crestwood Lake Section 8 Holding Corporation
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; complaint alleges retaliation for race-based claims and participation in state court proceedings.
key_holding: The complaint failed to comply with Rule 8 and did not state a viable FHA claim because plaintiff alleged no facts supporting discrimination based on race, and nothing in the complaint suggested she suffered an adverse housing action because of her race; the court granted leave to amend.
brief_summary: Lisa Hall, proceeding pro se, sued Crestwood Lake Section 8 Holding Corporation and related defendants alleging violations of her civil and housing rights, claiming she was retaliated against because she is Black and because she countersued defendants in state court. The Court dismissed the complaint for failure to comply with Rule 8, finding no plausible FHA claim because plaintiff alleged no facts supporting race-based discrimination, and granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

case_name: Samuel Indig, Leah Indig, Meir Kahana, Robert Klein, and Naftali Klein v. The Village of Pomona, Brett Yagel, Louis Zummo, Leon Harris, Doris Ulman, and Ian Banks
accommodation_description: This is not a disability accommodation case; plaintiffs allege religious discrimination in building permit approvals and code enforcement against Orthodox Jewish property owners
key_holding: Summary judgment is granted for defendants on Kahana's claims and against Ulman and Banks, but denied on Indigs' and Kleins' claims against Yagel, Harris, and Zummo because genuine disputes exist regarding whether village officials' permit delays, stop work orders, and code enforcement actions were motivated by anti-Semitic animus rather than legitimate zoning enforcement.
brief_summary: Five Orthodox Jewish homeowners sued the Village of Pomona and individual village officials alleging religious discrimination in building permit approvals and selective enforcement of municipal codes against them. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on plaintiff Kahana's claims and dismissed claims against individual defendants Ulman and Banks, but denied summary judgment on the Indigs' and Kleins' claims against Mayor Yagel, Trustee Harris, and Building Inspector Zummo, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding discriminatory intent based on recordings and communications revealing anti-Semitic animus.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Reynolds v. Barrett, 685 F.3d 193 (2d Cir. 2012)
Gilead Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Town of Brookfield, 432 F. Supp. 3d 77 (S.D.N.Y. 2020)

case_name: Charlier v. 21 Astor Place Condominium
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case involves sexual harassment by building doorman resulting in hostile housing environment claims
key_holding: The Court denied dismissal of sex-based hostile housing environment claims under the FHA, NYSHRL, and NYCHRL against the employer-defendants (Douglas Elliman and 21 Astor Place Condominium), finding the plaintiff plausibly alleged Mr. Proverbs' conduct was sufficiently severe and pervasive, and that employer knowledge could be imputed through the building manager supervisor who witnessed the harassment.
brief_summary: Annabelle Charlier sued 21 Astor Place Condominium, its management company Douglas Elliman, and others alleging that building doorman Ricardo Proverbs sexually harassed her for weeks, including entering her apartment uninvited and making sexually explicit comments and threats. The Court granted in part and denied in part the Condo Defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing claims against the Board of Managers and its President for lack of vicarious liability, but allowing the sex discrimination and hostile housing environment claims to proceed against the employer-defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Ellerth, 524 U.S. 742 (1998)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
A.L.M. ex rel. Moore v. Bd. of Managers of Vireum Schoolhouse Condo., 2021 WL 5121137 (2d Cir. 2021)

case_name: Ronald Cavallo v. HPD Section 8; Goddard Riverside
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff appears to seek transfer or improved housing conditions due to disability-related health concerns (tremors, heart disease, hypertension), but no specific accommodation request is clearly articulated.
key_holding: The court dismissed the complaint because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing that defendants discriminated against him because of his disability, as required to state a plausible FHA claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ronald Cavallo sued HPD Section 8 and Goddard Riverside Community Center alleging Fair Housing Act violations for inadequate housing conditions (roach infestation) and disability-related harm, seeking money damages. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing defendants discriminated against him because of his disability, granting him 60 days leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Mazzocchi v. Windsor Owners Corp., 2012 WL 3288240 (S.D.N.Y. 2012)
Connick v. Thompson, 563 U.S. 51 (2011)

case_name: Williams v. 647 E. 229th St. Bronx LLC
accommodation_description: No disability-related accommodation request; plaintiff alleges race-based discrimination and retaliation in housing terms, conditions, and tenancy termination proceedings.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA claim because while plaintiff alleges he is Black and the owner is White, he provides only conclusory allegations about discriminatory motive without factual allegations explaining what defendants did or said that evidenced racial discrimination.
brief_summary: Robert Williams, a pro se Black plaintiff, sued his landlord 647 E. 229th Street Bronx LLC and Eli Weissman alleging race-based housing discrimination under the FHA, claiming defendants disputed his legal tenancy and commenced holdover proceedings against him. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding Williams provided only conclusory allegations of discriminatory motive without factual support, and granted him 30 days leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Fair Hous. in Huntingon Comm. v. Town of Huntington
Palmer v. Fannie Mae

source_file: 10197106_2024_Avila v. New York City Housing Authority_nysd
case_name: Jimmy Avila v. New York City Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff vaguely requests unspecified accommodations to prevent severe mental and physical health crises due to disability, but court did not analyze any specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claims because he failed to allege facts showing he suffered adverse treatment or that NYCHA discriminated against him because of his disability, race, or gender, as required to state a claim of intentional discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Jimmy Avila sued NYCHA under the Fair Housing Act, Rehabilitation Act, ADA Title II, and Section 1983, alleging discrimination based on disability, race, and gender in housing application processing. The Court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because Plaintiff failed to allege any facts showing adverse treatment or discriminatory intent based on his protected classifications, though the Court granted leave to amend within 30 days.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr.

case_name: Annalicia Deandra Pownall El v. S. Realty; Lawrence Spitz; Manny; Mark
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability discrimination related to rent payment and eviction proceedings, but does not articulate a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to allege facts sufficient to show she has a covered disability or that she requested a reasonable accommodation shaped by her disability that the landlord was required to make.
brief_summary: Annalicia Pownall El, a pro se plaintiff, sued S. Realty and related defendants alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act related to her eviction from a Mount Vernon apartment for nonpayment of rent. The Court dismissed her federal claims, finding that while she invoked the FHA, she failed to allege facts sufficient to show she has a covered disability or that she requested a reasonable accommodation shaped by her disability that the landlord was required to make.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)
US Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Hevner v. Village East Towers, Inc., 2011 WL 666340 (S.D.N.Y. 2011)

case_name: Chelsea Culver, et al. v. Melissa Churchill, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation was requested; plaintiffs alleged general FHA violations without identifying any protected class or specific accommodation need.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' Rule 60(b) motion because plaintiffs failed to state a cognizable FHA claim, having not identified any protected class or alleged intentional discrimination in their original complaint.
brief_summary: Pro se tenants Chelsea Culver and Ben Smith sued their former landlords under the Fair Housing Act seeking damages for an eviction dispute; the court originally dismissed for lack of jurisdiction and now denies plaintiffs' motion to reopen under Rule 60(b) because plaintiffs failed to identify any protected class or state a cognizable FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Tasha Chapman v. Justin Davis, et al.
race_if_mentioned: African American or Black, and Native American
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep her emotional support animal, a dog named Nemo, despite a no-pet policy in her lease.
key_holding: The housing provider's request for the ESA's vaccination records during the week following notice did not constitute a refusal to make a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tasha Chapman, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued her landlord and property manager for failing to reasonably accommodate her disability by allowing her to keep an emotional support animal despite a no-pet policy. The court dismissed her complaint on initial screening, finding that the property manager's request for vaccination records did not constitute a refusal to accommodate.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Connor Grp., 805 F. Supp. 2d 396 (S.D. Ohio 2011)

case_name: Ronald D. Leonard v. Hocking Metropolitan Housing Authority, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for his disability, which Defendants allegedly approved in 2020 but later demanded he sign a pet agreement to retain.
key_holding: The court found that requesting plaintiff sign a pet agreement for his ESA, without evidence that Defendants refused to allow the ESA or questioned its legitimacy, did not constitute a failure to accommodate or discriminatory interference under the FHAA.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Ronald Leonard sued Hocking Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging it violated the Fair Housing Act by demanding he sign a pet agreement for his previously approved emotional support animal, which he claimed constituted constructive eviction. The court granted leave to proceed in forma pauperis but recommended dismissal for failure to state a claim, finding that requesting documentation and a pet agreement does not constitute a refusal to accommodate or discriminatory animus when there was no evidence Defendants refused to allow the ESA to remain.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 10199087_2024_Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma, Inc v. Feiock_oked
case_name: Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma, Inc. v. Feiock
accommodation_description: The McNamees requested a one-year lease renewal as a reasonable accommodation for James McNamee's disability; the current order analyzes whether Frank Feiock's conduct (approaching James McNamee about his disability and photographing him during move-out) constituted unlawful harassment.
key_holding: The harassment claim fails because Frank Feiock's conduct—asking about James McNamee's disability and photographing him while moving out—was not sufficiently severe or pervasive to constitute unlawful harassment, and the fair housing organization MFH lacks standing to assert harassment claims where it failed to demonstrate concrete injury to its organizational mission.
brief_summary: Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma and James and Marta McNamee sued landlords Frank and Glenna Feiock alleging discriminatory housing practices under the FHA, including denial of a reasonable accommodation request for lease renewal and harassment based on disability. The Court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that the harassment claim fails because Feiock's alleged conduct (asking about McNamee's disability and photographing him during move-out) was not severe or pervasive enough to constitute unlawful harassment, and MFH lacked standing to bring harassment claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Jackson v. Park Place Condominiums Ass'n, Inc., 619 Fed. Appx. 699 (10th Cir. 2015)
Zhu v. Fisher, Cavanaugh, Smith & Lemon, P.A., 151 F. Supp. 2d 1254 (D. Kan. 2001)

case_name: Rosemary McVicker v. Muskogee Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff appeared to argue that MHA should have adopted a written medical marijuana policy as a reasonable accommodation for her disability, but the court did not analyze this as a formal reasonable accommodation claim.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for MHA on Plaintiff's FHA claims because MHA had a policy prohibiting marijuana use, Plaintiff had actual notice of this policy through newsletters and her lease, and Plaintiff failed to demonstrate that MHA intentionally discriminated against her based on disability or sex.
brief_summary: Rosemary McVicker, a tenant with a disability residing at Heritage Park Apartments (a Section 811 supportive housing property operated by Muskogee Housing Authority), sued MHA for Fair Housing Act violations, claiming sex discrimination as a domestic violence victim and disability discrimination based on her medical marijuana use. The court granted summary judgment for MHA on all federal claims, finding no evidence of intentional sex discrimination and that MHA had provided adequate notice of its no-drug policy despite not having a formal written medical marijuana policy.

case_name: King Anthony Alvarez v. City of Philadelphia, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges termination from subsidized housing program in retaliation for filing fair housing complaints; no specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim as barred by claim preclusion because the same termination from TURN-funded housing was already litigated in a prior case, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to allege membership in a protected class as required to state an FHA retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff King Anthony Alvarez sued the City of Philadelphia, HUD, TURN, and CLS alleging FHA retaliation claims based on his termination from a subsidized housing program in November 2022 in retaliation for filing fair housing complaints. The court dismissed the complaint on two grounds: first, the claim was barred by claim preclusion because Alvarez had already litigated the same termination in a prior case (Alvarez v. HUD, No. 22-3631), and second, even on the merits, the claim failed because Alvarez did not adequately allege membership in a protected class.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Beasley v. Howard, 14 F.4th 226 (3d Cir. 2021)
Gimenez v. Morgan Stanley DW, Inc., 202 F. App'x 583 (3d Cir. 2006)
Haefner v. N. Cornwall Twp., 40 F. App'x 656 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Parker v. Lee
accommodation_description: Parker alleges defendants issued a notice to vacate in retaliation for filing a complaint with the Philadelphia Commission on Human Relations about alleged race, gender, and disability discrimination
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of Parker's FHA retaliation claim because he adequately pled protected activity (filing PCHR complaints about discrimination) and causation (the notice to vacate was issued eight days after his PCHR intake form), while dismissing his race/sex discrimination claims for lack of comparator evidence and his disability accommodation claim because he failed to allege any specific accommodation request.
brief_summary: Gordon Parker sued Ronald and Teresa Lee, their trust, Kenneth Baritz, Jeffrey Mai, and Canaan Realty Investment Group, alleging they attempted to illegally evict him and his brother from their Chinatown apartment in retaliation for filing discrimination complaints with the Philadelphia Commission on Human Relations. The court largely granted defendants' motions to dismiss but denied them as to Parker's FHA retaliation claim under § 3617, finding he adequately alleged protected activity and causation based on temporal proximity between his PCHR intake form and the notice to vacate, while dismissing his race/sex discrimination and failure to accommodate claims for failure to plead comparator evidence and failure to request any specific accommodation, respectively.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wind Gap Mun. Auth. v. Community Servs., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Qing Qin v. Vertex, Inc., 100 F.4th 458 (3d Cir. 2024)

case_name: Miriam Millan-Heffner, et al. v. Gemcraft Homes Group, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request was made; this case involves racial discrimination in the home purchase process, including steering away from a white neighborhood, false information about lot availability, differential treatment, and failure to complete construction repairs.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the FHA and § 1981 claims, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged intentional race discrimination, including steering away from a white neighborhood and differential treatment of Ms. Millan-Heffner based on her Puerto Rican ethnicity, while granting dismissal of the breach of contract claim for failure to plead specific contract terms.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Gary Heffner and Miriam Millan-Heffner sued Gemcraft Homes Group, Inc. and its employees alleging race discrimination during their home purchase in Winslett, a housing development in Harrisburg, PA, including steering them away from a primarily white section of the neighborhood based on Ms. Millan-Heffner's Puerto Rican ethnicity. The court denied the motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Act and § 1981 claims, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged intentional race discrimination and that Gemcraft could be held vicariously liable under the non-delegable duty doctrine, while dismissing the breach of contract claim for failing to allege specific contract terms.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
White v. Barbe
Alexander v. Riga
Castleberry v. STI Group

source_file: 10201075_2024_Ewideh v. Homesite Insurance Company of the Midwest_pamd
case_name: Ewideh v. Homesite Insurance Company of the Midwest
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege discrimination by homeowners insurance company in adjusting property damage claims based on disability and sexual orientation, without specifying a particular accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' FHA claim fails because they have not explained how homeowners insurance claim adjustments fall within the scope of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a)-(f), and their vague, conclusory allegations of discriminatory motive are insufficient to state a plausible claim; dismissal is also warranted under Rule 41(b) due to plaintiffs' history of dilatoriness and failure to comply with court orders.
brief_summary: Omar Ewideh and Nivertiti Geaith, proceeding pro se, sued their homeowners insurance company, its counsel, and claims adjusters under the Fair Housing Act, alleging discrimination based on disability and sexual orientation in the adjustment of their property damage claims. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the amended complaint failed to state a plausible FHA claim because plaintiffs did not explain how insurance claim adjustments fall within the FHA's provisions, and alternatively dismissed the case under Rule 41(b) due to plaintiffs' extensive dilatory conduct and failure to prosecute.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wartluft v. Milton Hershey Sch. & Sch. Tr., 400 F. Supp. 3d 91 (M.D. Pa. 2019)
Wilson v. Hillsborough Twp. Constr. Dep't, 779 F. App'x 969 (3d Cir. 2019)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)

source_file: 10201923_2024_Newbury v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development_rid
case_name: Nancy Newbury, et al. v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged that HUD's approval of easements allowing 55+ residents of West House II to use common areas and parking of West House I (restricted to 62+ residents) constituted impermissible age-group intermingling without Congressional authorization.
key_holding: The court recommended denying plaintiffs' motion for preliminary/permanent injunction because HUD afforded adequate procedural notice and opportunity to comment, and HUD's determination approving the easements was neither arbitrary nor capricious under the APA; plaintiffs' claims that allowing 55+ residents to use WH I common areas required new legislation failed because existing statutory framework permits age-group intermingling.
brief_summary: Three elderly residents of West House I sued HUD and other defendants seeking to enjoin easements permitting residents of an adjacent West House II (restricted to ages 55+) to use WH I's common areas and parking lot, alleging HUD failed to comply with procedural requirements and that such intermingling requires new legislation. The Magistrate Judge recommended denying plaintiffs' motion for preliminary/permanent injunction and entering judgment for Federal Defendants because HUD afforded proper notice and opportunity to comment, and HUD's decision was neither arbitrary nor capricious under the APA.
key_cases_cited: Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 144 S. Ct. 2244 (2024)
Ohio v. Environmental Protection Agency, 144 S. Ct. 2040 (2024)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
DeSuze v. Sununu, 442 F. Supp. 3d 531 (D.R.I. 2020)

case_name: Larocque v. Spring Green Corporation
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; case involves claims of discriminatory enforcement of community rules, denial of maintenance services, and failure to respond to third-party harassment based on religion (Judaism) and sexual orientation (lesbian)
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the Spring Green Defendants because Larocque failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment—she offered no evidence of adverse action, no comparison to similarly situated tenants, and no evidence that any differential treatment was motivated by discriminatory intent based on her religion or sexual orientation; additionally, third-party liability for the landlord's failure to respond to harassment by a non-resident nephew was rejected under the Wetzel framework because the landlord lacked sufficient control over the land tenant's nephew.
brief_summary: Lynn Larocque, a Jewish lesbian tenant at a manufactured housing community in Warwick, Rhode Island, sued Spring Green Corporation and related entities alleging they discriminated against her based on religion and sexual orientation by stringently enforcing community rules, ignoring maintenance requests, and failing to respond to harassment by a neighbor's nephew. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding Larocque failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment because she offered no evidence of adverse action, no comparison to similarly situated tenants, and no evidence of discriminatory motive; the court also rejected her third-party liability theory for failing to respond to harassment.
key_cases_cited: Gros v. Spring Green Corp., 776 F.3d 38 (1st Cir. 2015)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Bostock v. Clayton County, 590 U.S. 644 (2020)
Wetzel v. St. Andrew's Resources for People, Animals, & the Environment, Inc., 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Hughes v. Magic Sky Homes, LLC, 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)

case_name: Murphy v. Brixworth Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; plaintiffs generally alleged FHA protection from discrimination based on political views
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claim because the Fair Housing Act prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin, not political expression, and dismissed the ADA claim because the private residential pool is not a place of public accommodation.
brief_summary: Sean Murphy and his family sued Brixworth Homeowners Association for enforcing sign restrictions and revoking pool/clubhouse access; the court granted BHOA's motion for judgment on the pleadings, dismissing the breach-of-contract, First Amendment, FHA, and ADA claims because the HOA validly enforced its CCRs, the FHA does not protect political expression, and the ADA does not apply to private residential facilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Cynthia George v. Overall Creek Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: The underlying case involves claims that defendants failed to design and construct apartments with accessible features for disabled individuals under § 3604(f)(3)(C); this opinion addresses third-party claims for equitable indemnification, contribution, and breach of contract against the architect.
key_holding: The court held that state-law claims for equitable contribution against an architect are not preempted by the FHA, but claims for equitable indemnification are preempted; the breach-of-contract claim survived to the extent it constitutes a de facto claim for contribution rather than indemnification.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Cynthia George, a wheelchair user, sued Overall Creek Apartments and related entities alleging they failed to design and construct the property with accessible features in violation of the FHA and ADA. The current opinion addresses a motion to dismiss by third-party defendant Niles Bolton Associates, Inc., the architect, challenging whether third-party plaintiffs' state-law claims for equitable indemnification, contribution, and breach of contract are preempted by the FHA. The court granted NBA's motion to dismiss the equitable indemnification claim as preempted, denied dismissal of the equitable contribution claim as not preempted, and granted in part/denied in part the breach-of-contract claim based on whether it constitutes a de facto claim for indemnification (preempted) or contribution (permitted).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
United States v. Murphy Dev., LLC, No. 3:08-0960, 2008 WL 3614829 (M.D. Tenn. Oct. 27, 2009)
Clover Cmty. Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects, P.C., 676 F. Supp. 3d 82 (N.D. N.Y. 2023)

case_name: George v. Overall Creek Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user plaintiff visited an apartment complex and alleged design and construction accessibility barriers that would deter disabled persons from renting units
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA design-and-construction claim is time-barred because the statute of limitations began running when the last rental unit was first rented on March 19, 2021, more than two years before she filed suit on March 31, 2023, and her argument that an unrented model unit should toll the limitations period lacks merit.
brief_summary: Cynthia George, a wheelchair user, sued the designers, developers, and owners of Overall Creek Apartments alleging the complex was not designed and constructed in compliance with FHA accessibility requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C). The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding the claim time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations because the last available rental unit was first occupied on March 19, 2021, more than two years before suit was filed.
key_cases_cited: Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Vill. of Olde St. Andrews, Inc., 210 F. App'x 469 (6th Cir. 2006)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Preferred Living Real Est. Invs., LLC, 2018 WL 4690790 (S.D. Ohio 2018)
Housing Rsch. & Advoc. Ctr. v. WXZ Residential Grp./Circle Lofts 118 LLC, 2017 WL 1078956 (N.D. Ohio 2017)
Housing Advocs., Inc. v. Berardi & Partners, Inc., 2010 WL 4905547 (N.D. Ohio 2010)

case_name: Gamane v. Diamond
accommodation_description: Request for an emotional support cat as a reasonable accommodation for Mrs. Gamane's disabilities including stroke effects, depression, and anxiety
key_holding: The court held that Plaintiffs were unlikely to succeed on the merits because they failed to establish that an ESA cat was necessary to afford equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, as the medical documentation did not establish a causal connection between the disability and the requested accommodation, and the accommodation was unreasonable due to potential burdens on staff and risks to allergic residents.
brief_summary: Mrs. Gamane and her husband sued Bells Retirement Village and its management seeking a preliminary injunction to allow Mrs. Gamane to keep an emotional support cat as a reasonable accommodation for her disabilities. The court denied the motion, finding Plaintiffs unlikely to succeed on the merits because the medical evidence failed to establish that a cat was necessary to afford equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, and the accommodation was unreasonable due to potential burdens on staff and risks to allergic residents.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Phillips v. Acacia on the Green Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 2022 WL 1692948 (6th Cir. May 26, 2022)

source_file: 10204105_2024_Brady v. The Evangelical Lutheran Good Samaritan Society_txed
case_name: Mary Brady v. The Evangelical Lutheran Good Samaritan Society
accommodation_description: Brady requested a priority waitlist position, carpet installation, security system installation, mold testing, and a residence free from environmental triggers as accommodations for her autoimmune disease and allergies.
key_holding: The court dismissed Brady's FHA claim with prejudice because she failed to plausibly allege a qualifying disability or that Good Samaritan Society knew of her alleged handicaps and their consequential limitations, and she failed to establish a nexus between her claimed conditions and her requested accommodations.
brief_summary: Mary Brady sued The Evangelical Lutheran Good Samaritan Society, operator of Denton Village senior living community, alleging the defendant denied her reasonable accommodations for her alleged autoimmune disease and allergies when it rejected her rental application and subsequent accommodation requests. The court granted the motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding Brady failed to plausibly allege a qualifying disability under the FHA or that she communicated her disability and its limitations to the defendant with sufficient specificity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Mueck v. La Grange Acquisitions, L.P., 75 F.4th 469 (5th Cir. 2023)
Windham v. Harris Cnty., 875 F.3d 229 (5th Cir. 2017)
Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)

source_file: 10204970_2024_Haire v. 5445 Caruth Haven Lane Apartments Owner LLC_txnd
case_name: Tiffany Haire v. 5445 Caruth Haven Lane Apartments Owner LLC
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on FHA claims under § 3604 (dismissed as time-barred) and § 3617 (insufficient evidence of interference with HUD investigation), as well as state law claims for intentional infliction of emotional distress and negligence.
brief_summary: Tiffany Haire, an individual tenant, sued apartment owners, property management company, and related individuals alleging Fair Housing Act violations and state law claims. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, dismissing the § 3604 claim as time-barred and the § 3617 interference claim for failure to provide competent summary judgment evidence supporting allegations of sabotage and fraud in the HUD investigation.
key_cases_cited: Zhu v. Countrywide Realty Co., 165 F. Supp. 2d 1181 (D. Kan. 2001)
Stelly v. Duriso, 982 F.3d 403 (5th Cir. 2020)
Acara v. Banks, 470 F.3d 569 (5th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 10205156_2024_Bowman v. SWBC Real Estate Services LLC_txnd
case_name: Bowman v. SWBC Real Estates Services, LLC
accommodation_description: Disabled veteran alleging architectural barriers at 590-unit apartment complex prevented wheelchair access to common areas and unit features.
key_holding: The court found Bowman lacked standing to sue under § 3604(f)(1) for rental discrimination because he did not engage in rental activity, but found he sufficiently alleged standing under § 3604(f)(2) by claiming inability to access privileges of the rental due to architectural barriers; the court granted leave to amend complaint to specify which barriers Bowman encountered.
brief_summary: Dana Bowman, a disabled veteran who lost both legs, sued SWBC Real Estates Services and related entities alleging architectural barriers at a 590-unit apartment complex in Rockwall, Texas prevented wheelchair access to common areas and unit features. The court granted partial dismissal, finding Bowman lacked standing under § 3604(f)(1) because he did not allege sufficient rental activity, but allowed claims under § 3604(f)(2) to proceed, giving Bowman 28 days to amend his complaint to specify which barriers he encountered.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Laufer v. Mann Hosp., L.L.C., 996 F.3d 269 (5th Cir. 2021)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

source_file: 10208849_2022_Parada v. Sandhill Shores Property Owners Association, Inc._txsd
case_name: Cecilia Parada v. Sandhill Shores Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that the HOA not construct a beach-access path near her home, which would force her to leave because her Alzheimer's disease leaves her unable to cope with encounters with strangers outside her residence.
key_holding: The court denied the HOA's motion to dismiss, finding that Parada adequately pleaded she is handicapped under the FHA (Alzheimer's disease substantially limits major life activities including caring for herself, learning, communicating, and interacting with others) and that her requested accommodation—refraining from constructing the beach-access path near her home—is facially reasonable and plausibly necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Cecilia Parada, who suffers from late-onset Alzheimer's disease, sued Sandhill Shores Property Owners Association alleging the HOA violated the Fair Housing Act and ADA by refusing her request to not construct a beach-access path near her home, which construction would force her to leave because her disability prevents her from coping with encounters with strangers. The court denied the HOA's motion to dismiss, finding Parada adequately pleaded Article III standing, survived abstention arguments, and stated a plausible reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) by alleging she is handicapped (Alzheimer's substantially limits major life activities) and her requested accommodation is facially reasonable.
key_cases_cited: Providence Behav. Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)

case_name: Prinyah El-Bey v. Berkshire Hathaway Home Services
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair-bound tenant with asthma, sought injunctive relief to stop a neighbor's marijuana smoking that triggered her asthma, but did not request a specific reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: El-Bey failed to state a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim because she did not plead facts showing she requested a particular reasonable, necessary accommodation or that any defendant refused such a request.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Prinyah El-Bey, a wheelchair-bound tenant with asthma, sued Berkshire Hathaway Home Services and related entities alleging discrimination under the ADA and FHA, claiming the landlord failed to address marijuana smoke from a neighbor that triggered her asthma. The court dismissed her federal claims with prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) for failure to state a plausible claim, finding she failed to allege that she requested a specific reasonable accommodation or that any defendant refused such a request.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Parada v. Sandhill Shores Property Owners Association, Inc., 604 F. Supp. 3d 567 (S.D. Tex. 2022)
Prim v. Stein, 6 F.4th 584 (5th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Shayla Anderson, et al. v. SunRidge Management Group, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation was requested; the case concerns alleged intentional race and sex discrimination rather than a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs' FHA claims under §§ 3604(a)-(b) failed as a matter of law since the alleged discrimination related to habitability rather than housing availability, and plaintiffs failed to establish prima facie cases of disparate treatment or retaliation.
brief_summary: African American tenants Anderson and Edwards sued SunRidge Management Group and Grand Fountain Apartments alleging race and sex discrimination, Title VII violations, § 1983 claims, and retaliation under the FHA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on all claims, finding the FHA claims failed because plaintiffs' alleged harms concerned habitability rather than housing availability, and plaintiffs presented no evidence establishing prima facie cases of intentional discrimination or retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)
Mandawala v. Struga Mgmt., 2023 WL 2712500 (5th Cir. 2023)
Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Brown v. Harris Cty. Hous. Auth., 2018 WL 3080880 (S.D. Tex. 2018)

source_file: 10211893_2019_Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas_txwd
case_name: Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of fees for covered parking as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing the reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs failed to adequately plead the nature of Mr. Payne's disability, stating there were no facts by which the court could determine whether he suffered from a qualifying disability under the FHA, despite noting the accommodation requested concerned a waiver of fees for covered parking.
brief_summary: Don Albert Payne and Gloria Jean Payne, proceeding pro se, sued the City of San Antonio, San Antonio Housing Authority (SAHA), SAHA employee Amy Carrillo, and various police officers alleging wrongful eviction, disability discrimination, and failure to accommodate under the FHA after Mr. Payne requested a waiver of covered parking fees. The magistrate judge recommended granting SAHA's motion to dismiss all claims against it for failure to state a claim, including dismissing the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs failed to adequately plead Mr. Payne's qualifying disability beyond referencing mental health.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Providence Behavioral Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)
Bd. of Comm'rs of Bryan County v. Brown, 520 U.S. 397 (1997)

source_file: 10212330_2020_Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Property Owners Association, Inc._txwd
case_name: Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for a waiver of the twelve-hour street-parking restriction during the coronavirus pandemic shelter-in-place order.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim because the requested parking restriction waiver was not necessary to ameliorate the disability (it was merely inconvenient) and was not reasonable (a blanket waiver was overbroad compared to the defendant's counter-accommodation offer of four cars on the street), while denying the motion to dismiss the retaliation claim based on the close temporal proximity between the HOA's new enforcement scheme and the plaintiffs' protected FHA activities.
brief_summary: Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC (a transitional sober living residence operator) and the property owners sued Parkstone Property Owners Association seeking reasonable accommodation from parking restrictions during the COVID-19 pandemic and challenging new HOA enforcement schemes as discriminatory and retaliatory. The court dismissed the reasonable accommodation, intentional discrimination, and disparate treatment claims but allowed the retaliation claim to proceed based on the temporal proximity between the HOA's new enforcement actions and the plaintiffs' protected FHA activities.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Groome Res., Ltd., L.L.C. v. Par. of Jefferson, 234 F.3d 192 (5th Cir. 2000)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty. Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)

case_name: Mandawala v. Struga Management
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlord's refusal to recognize his occupancy arrangement with other tenants and subsequent eviction was based on racial discrimination.
key_holding: Plaintiff's motion for summary judgment on FHA racial discrimination claim was denied because he failed to present sufficient evidence to prove discriminatory animus or meet his burden of proof on the elements of the claim.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Symon Mandawala sued Struga Management, his former property management company, alleging racial discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act when the manager allegedly refused to recognize his occupancy arrangement with other tenants and forced him to vacate. The court denied Mandawala's motion for summary judgment on his FHA claim because he failed to present sufficient evidence demonstrating discriminatory animus or establishing each element of his claim as a matter of law.
key_cases_cited: Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)
Scott v. Harris, 550 U.S. 372 (2007)

case_name: Mandawala v. Struga Management
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was forced to move after defendant property management initiated eviction proceedings based on his unauthorized occupancy with the Smith family.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiff's racial discrimination claims under the FHA, finding plaintiff pleaded enough facts to qualify as an 'aggrieved person' under 42 U.S.C. § 3602(i) who can pursue discriminatory housing practice claims, while dismissing claims under Texas Property Code § 92.004, § 1981(c)/§ 1983, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and § 1985/§ 1986 conspiracy claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Symon Mandawala sued Struga Management alleging racial discrimination after he was forced to move from an apartment where he had paid a $40 joining fee to reside with the Smith family, and defendants subsequently initiated eviction proceedings based on his unauthorized occupancy. The court granted in part and denied in part defendant's motion to dismiss, dismissing claims for fraudulent practices under Texas Property Code § 92.004, deprivation of rights under color of law, intentional infliction of emotional distress, and conspiracy under §§ 1985-1986, while allowing plaintiff's racial discrimination claims under the Fair Housing Act to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Domino's Pizza, Inc. v. McDonald

source_file: 10212904_2021_Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas_txwd
case_name: Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Mr. Payne requested a reasonable accommodation for his schizoaffective disorder, but the specific nature of the accommodation is not identified in the opinion.
key_holding: The disability discrimination and failure to accommodate claims fail because plaintiffs did not sufficiently identify the disability or the specific accommodation requested, and plaintiffs improperly pursued FHA claims through Section 1983 rather than direct FHA enforcement mechanisms.
brief_summary: Tenants Don and Gloria Payne sued the City of San Antonio, SAPD officers, SAHA, and others alleging disability discrimination, failure to accommodate, constitutional violations, and state law claims arising from their Section 8-subsidized tenancy termination. The Magistrate Judge recommended granting all dispositive motions, finding that plaintiffs' failure to accommodate claim failed because they did not identify the specific accommodation requested, and their constitutional claims failed due to defendants' qualified immunity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lollar v. Baker, 196 F.3d 603 (5th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Symon Mandawala v. Struga Management, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case involves claims that Plaintiff was denied housing and subjected to eviction proceedings because of his race.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act because he did not demonstrate that any defendant intended to discriminate based on race, nor did he prove that defendants' legitimate nondiscriminatory reason (failure to complete co-tenant application requirements) was pretextual.
brief_summary: Symon Mandawala, a Black tenant at Vineyard Gardens Apartments in San Antonio, sued Struga Management and its employees alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after he was forced to vacate an apartment where he had moved in with existing tenants without completing formal co-tenant application requirements. After a bench trial, the court found that plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of discrimination and alternatively failed to prove that defendants' nondiscriminatory reason for the eviction proceedings was pretextual.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Crain v. City of Selma, 952 F.3d 634 (5th Cir. 2020)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of African Am.-Owned Media, 140 S. Ct. 1009 (2020)

source_file: 10213380_2021_Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas_txwd
case_name: Payne v. City of San Antonio, Texas
accommodation_description: Mr. Payne requested free covered parking closer to his apartment building as a reasonable accommodation for his schizoaffective disorder-related paranoia.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to state a plausible FHA disability accommodation claim because Mr. Payne did not allege facts showing his schizoaffective disorder substantially limits a major life activity, and his retaliation claim failed because Midcrown proffered a legitimate non-retaliatory reason (threatening Facebook post) for the eviction.
brief_summary: Don Albert Payne and Gloria Jean Payne sued Midcrown Pavilion Apartments and SAHA employee Amy V. Carrillo alleging disability discrimination, failure to accommodate, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act after being evicted following a request for covered parking near their apartment. The magistrate judge recommended granting defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to plausibly plead Mr. Payne's schizoaffective disorder substantially limited a major life activity to establish a qualifying disability, and failed to rebut Midcrown's legitimate non-retaliatory reason for the eviction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lollar v. Baker, 196 F.3d 603 (5th Cir. 2012)
Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)

case_name: Stephan A. Ricks v. DMA Companies, Diana McIver, Nichole Guptill, Roxanne Marroquin, and OPC Services
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of a Ring Camera with Ring Chime and Speaker in his apartment for his visual disability; also requested communications in Braille or accessible format.
key_holding: OPC was correctly dismissed because Ricks did not request an accommodation from OPC and OPC lacked authority to make modifications to his dwelling; DMA and individual defendants remain pending.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ricks, who has a visual disability, sued DMA Companies (his housing provider), its employees, and Overland Pacific & Cutler LLC (OPC), a relocation contractor, alleging violations of the ADA and FHA for failing to accommodate his requests including a Ring Camera installation and Braille-format communications. The Magistrate Judge recommended granting OPC's Rule 12(c) motion because Ricks failed to allege he requested an accommodation from OPC and OPC lacked authority to grant modifications to his dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)
Neff v. Am. Dairy Queen Corp., 58 F.3d 1063 (5th Cir. 1995)
Georgia State Conf. of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)

source_file: 10215742_2023_Lopez Carpintero v. Del Valle 969 Apartments, LTD_txwd
case_name: Yvette Lopez Carpintero v. Del Valle 969 Apartments, LTD
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request described; plaintiff broadly alleges 'discrimination' and 'retaliation and harassment' including proposed evictions and property assaults.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal of plaintiff's FHA claim as frivolous because she failed to allege discrimination on the basis of a protected class, stating only that defendant used 'intimidation tactics' and that she was assaulted on the property.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Yvette Lopez Carpintero sued her apartment operator alleging violations of ADA, VAWA, and FHA based on 'intimidation tactics' such as proposed evictions and assaults on the property. The Magistrate Judge recommended dismissing all claims as frivolous under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) for failure to state plausible claims, finding the FHA claim failed because plaintiff did not allege discrimination based on a protected characteristic.

case_name: William Holliday d/b/a Not Just T-Shirt v. City of San Antonio
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged racial discrimination in denial of rental assistance applications submitted on behalf of tenants, and in business contracting for promotional items.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City because Plaintiff provided no evidence to support his racial discrimination claims, and the undisputed evidence showed no discriminatory conduct by the City.
brief_summary: William Holliday, proceeding pro se, sued the City of San Antonio alleging racial discrimination in business contracting and denial of rental assistance applications as a landlord. The court granted summary judgment for the City because Plaintiff failed to present any evidence creating a genuine dispute of material fact, and the undisputed evidence showed no discriminatory conduct.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Scott v. Harris, 550 U.S. 372 (2007)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)
Monell v. Department of Social Services of New York City, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

case_name: Harper v. Lindon City
accommodation_description: Operator of an inpatient addiction recovery treatment center requested a reasonable accommodation to waive the City's eight-person occupancy limit for residential facilities for persons with disabilities to allow up to sixteen residents.
key_holding: The court denied the preliminary injunction because Harper failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on his reasonable accommodation claim, as he did not prove his request for sixteen residents was necessary (his own expert evidence indicated eight was sufficient) or reasonable (he failed to show the expansion would not affect the neighborhood character and provided no evidence of economic necessity).
brief_summary: George E. Harper, operator of a substance abuse recovery center, sued Lindon City after the City denied his request for a reasonable accommodation to exceed the eight-person occupancy limit for residential facilities for persons with disabilities, seeking to house up to sixteen residents. The court denied Harper's motion for preliminary injunction, finding he failed to show a substantial likelihood of success on the merits because his own expert evidence acknowledged eight residents was therapeutically sufficient, he provided no evidence that sixteen residents would not affect the neighborhood, and he failed to demonstrate economic necessity.
key_cases_cited: Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Keys Youth Servs., Inc. v. City of Olathe, 248 F.3d 1267 (10th Cir. 2001)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Vill. of Belle Terre v. Boraas, 416 U.S. 1 (1974)

case_name: Conlin v. RU Cliff, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to keep her companion dog Buckley as a reasonable accommodation for anxiety.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the Cullimore Defendants on the reasonable accommodation claim because the six-day response period did not constitute an indeterminate delay sufficient for constructive denial, and the attorneys had no authority to decide accommodation requests; however, the court denied summary judgment on the disparate treatment claim, finding the plaintiff had standing and raised genuine issues of fact on duty, harm, and causation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Kaylee Conlin sued her landlord's attorneys (the Cullimore Defendants) for Fair Housing Act violations arising from burdensome forms they drafted requiring intrusive information from disabled tenants seeking to keep emotional support animals. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the reasonable accommodation claim, finding no constructive denial where approval was granted within six days, but denied summary judgment on the disparate treatment claim, allowing it to proceed on the theory that the attorneys' form-drafting activities constituted unlawful services in connection with housing.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
United States v. Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)
Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Harper v. Lindon City
accommodation_description: Harper requested a reasonable accommodation under the FHA to waive Lindon City's eight-person limit on unrelated people living together in a residential facility for disabled individuals, seeking to house sixteen patients at a time at a residential inpatient treatment facility.
key_holding: Fair Care's concerns about future prejudice from Harper's potential use of the property are too speculative to merit reopening the case, especially since Harper has not reapplied for a permit, asserted no claims against Fair Care, and asserted no counterclaims.
brief_summary: George Harper sued Lindon City after the City denied his request for a reasonable accommodation to operate a residential inpatient treatment facility for sixteen disabled patients despite an eight-person zoning limit for unrelated individuals. The court had previously granted Harper's motion for voluntary dismissal without prejudice, and Fair Care Lindon, LLC (a neighboring homeowners group that had intervened as a defendant) moved for reconsideration. The court denied Fair Care's motion, concluding that Fair Care's concerns about potential future prejudice were too speculative to warrant reopening the case.
key_cases_cited: Nelson v. City of Albuquerque, 921 F.3d 925 (10th Cir. 2019)
United States v. Christy, 739 F.3d 534 (10th Cir. 2014)
Brown v. Baeke, 413 F.3d 1121 (10th Cir. 2005)
County of Santa Fe, N.M. v. Pub. Serv. Co. of New Mexico, 311 F.3d 1031 (10th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Etna Properties, LLC v. Holladay City
accommodation_description: Square One Recovery requested permission to house twelve unrelated substance abuse treatment residents instead of the six residents permitted for disabled residential facilities under the City Code.
key_holding: The requested accommodation was not 'necessary' under the FHA because disabled persons already received preferential treatment under the City Code (six residents vs. four for non-disabled), and allowing twelve residents would grant disabled residents a housing opportunity not available to anyone else in that zone, which exceeds the FHA's mandate to create a level playing field.
brief_summary: Etna Properties and Square One Recovery sued Holladay City alleging the eleven-month delay in granting their reasonable accommodation request to house twelve substance abuse treatment residents (instead of six permitted for disabled facilities) constituted a constructive denial of reasonable accommodation under the FHA. The court granted summary judgment for the City, holding the accommodation was not necessary under the FHA because disabled persons already received preferential treatment (six vs. four residents) compared to non-disabled persons, and allowing twelve residents would give disabled residents an opportunity unavailable to anyone else in that zone.
key_cases_cited: Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Recovery Landholdings, LLC v. City of S. Ogden, No. 1:17-CV-152 TS, 2019 WL 1489003 (D. Utah Apr. 4, 2019)
Harper v. Lindon City, No. 2:18-CV-00772-DAK, 2019 WL 2188910 (D. Utah May 21, 2019)
Haws v. Norman, No. 2:15-CV-00422-EJF, 2017 WL 4221064 (D. Utah Sept. 20, 2017)

case_name: Harrell v. South Jordan Care Group, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Ronald Harrell, an African American man with Alzheimer's dementia, was evicted from an assisted living facility and allegedly denied the option to be transported by family/friend rather than police and ambulance.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that the Utah Health Care Malpractice Act does not apply to federal Fair Housing Act claims and that Mrs. Harrell has standing to pursue claims as an aggrieved person under the FHA's broad standing requirements.
brief_summary: Ronald J. Harrell, an African American man with Alzheimer's dementia, and his wife Christine Harrell sued South Jordan Care Group, LLC d/b/a Pheasant Run Alzheimer's Special Care Center, alleging the assisted living facility discriminated against Mr. Harrell based on race and disability, including using racial slurs, segregating him, and ultimately evicting him after a racially-charged incident. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that the Utah Health Care Malpractice Act does not apply to Fair Housing Act claims and that Mrs. Harrell has standing as an aggrieved person under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Gladstone Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Dowling v. Bullen, 94 P.3d 915 (Utah 2004)

case_name: Conlin v. The Law Offices of Kirk A. Cullimore, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep a Great Dane emotional support animal named Buckley in her apartment as a reasonable accommodation for anxiety disability
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's § 3604(c) and § 3604(f)(2) claims but denied both parties' summary judgment motions on plaintiff's § 3617 claims concerning the overburdensome accommodation Forms and the national training campaign, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding causation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Kaylee Conlin sued The Law Offices of Kirk A. Cullimore, LLC and attorney Kirk Cullimore (who drafted legal forms and training materials used by her landlord) alleging they violated the FHA by creating overburdensome emotional support animal verification forms and conducting a national campaign to discourage legitimate accommodation requests. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on claims under § 3604(c), § 3604(f)(2), and two § 3617 claims (Recovery clause and call to healthcare provider), but denied both parties' motions on the § 3617 claims concerning the burdensome Forms and the national campaign, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding causation.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Hidden Vill. LLC v. City of Lakewood, 734 F.3d 519 (6th Cir. 2013)
Morgan v. Secretary of HUD, 985 F.2d 1451 (10th Cir. 1993)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)

source_file: 10217836_2021_Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Community Association, The_utd
case_name: Wendi Hatfield v. The Cottages on 78th Community Association, Drew Keddington, Dave Ruprecht, Matthew Medford, Miller Harrison LLC, Douglas Shumway, Michelle Pohlman, and PMI of Utah
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; case involves retaliation claim under § 3617 for filing FHA religious discrimination complaint
key_holding: The HOA defendants' September 6 letter to plaintiff's employer, September 10 letter and assessment notice to homeowners, and statements at the annual meeting did not constitute adverse actions under § 3617 because they were not severe enough to deter a person of ordinary fortitude from exercising FHA rights and were not causally connected to plaintiff's protected activity.
brief_summary: Wendi Hatfield, a homeowner in a Utah planned unit development, sued her HOA and related defendants alleging retaliation under the Fair Housing Act for sending letters to her employer and homeowners and making statements at an annual meeting after she filed a religious discrimination complaint with the Utah Labor Commission. The court granted judgment on the pleadings for defendants, finding that none of the challenged communications constituted an adverse action under § 3617 because they were not materially severe enough to deter exercise of FHA rights and were not causally linked to her protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)
Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. Co. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)

case_name: Banks v. Gates Hudson & Associates, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for accommodation to allow an emotional support dog over the 30-pound pet weight limit to reside in the plaintiff's condominium unit.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because plaintiffs failed to state an FHA claim: the accommodation was granted, plaintiffs failed to adequately allege Banks' disability, and the alleged mistreatment was based on the dog's size and waste issues rather than disability.
brief_summary: Matthew Banks and Jessica Britton, proceeding pro se, sued Gates Hudson & Associates, Inc. and the Fountains Condominiums Board of Directors alleging Fair Housing Act violations after receiving approval to keep an emotional support dog despite a 30-pound pet weight limit. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to state an FHA claim because defendants granted the accommodation, plaintiffs inadequately alleged Banks' disability, and the alleged mistreatment by neighbors concerned the dog's size and waste rather than disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Thomas v. Salvation Army Southern Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs. LP, 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
Stevens v. Hollywood Towers & Condominium Ass'n, 836 F. Supp. 2d 800 (N.D. Ill. 2011)

case_name: Abdelhalim v. Lewis
race_if_mentioned: Egyptian (Middle Eastern/Arab), though plaintiff is described as a member of multiple protected classes including race, color, religion, and national origin
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; the case involves alleged interference with housing rights based on plaintiff's race, religion, and national origin stemming from neighbor disputes over short-term rentals.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiff failed to establish discriminatory animus under the Fair Housing Act, as the neighbors' conduct was motivated by concerns about short-term rental activity rather than plaintiff's protected class status (Egyptian national origin, race, religion), and no genuine issue of material fact existed regarding race-based interference or property rights violations.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Nagla Abdelhalim, an Egyptian Muslim immigrant, sued her neighbors Aaron Lewis, Dinara Lewis, Andrew McDevitt, and Roxana McDevitt alleging Fair Housing Act violations under § 3617, 42 U.S.C. § 1982, and § 1985 conspiracy claims based on alleged harassment stemming from disputes over her short-term rental (Airbnb) activity. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiff failed to establish discriminatory animus because the neighbors' conduct concerned rental activity and property use rather than plaintiff's protected characteristics, with no evidence of slurs or race-based motivation.
key_cases_cited: Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Frazier v. Cooke, No. 4:17-CV-54, 2017 WL 5560864 (E.D. Va. Nov. 17, 2017)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)
Simmons v. Poe, 47 F.3d 1370 (4th Cir. 1995)

case_name: Fedynich v. Boulder Housing Partners
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested expedited inspection and extended time on their Housing Choice Voucher to transfer to a new residence due to disability-related safety concerns and mental health needs.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA reasonable accommodation claims because they failed to plausibly allege specific disabilities and failed to establish a nexus between their alleged disabilities and the requested accommodations, merely labeling themselves as disabled without providing factual enhancement.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Elizabeth and Nicole Fedynich, holders of Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers, sued multiple public housing authorities and HUD defendants alleging they violated the FHA by failing to provide reasonable accommodations, including expedited HCV extensions and transfer assistance, for their mental health needs. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege qualifying disabilities or establish any nexus between their alleged disabilities and the requested accommodations.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Roberson v. Graziano
Bryant Woods Inn v. Howard Cty.
Constantine v. Rectors & Visitors of George Mason Univ.

case_name: Nicole Fedynich, et al. v. Juana Lozano, Assistant Manager, Valor Apartments, et al.
accommodation_description: Request for air purifiers due to severe asthma triggered by secondhand smoke in the apartment complex
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA and Rehabilitation Act claims because plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege they suffer from a qualifying disability or that defendants failed to provide reasonable accommodations, and dismissed all other claims except the retaliation claim which survived.
brief_summary: Nicole and Elizabeth Fedynich, Section 8 voucher holders with severe asthma, sued their landlord Valor Apartments and staff alleging disability discrimination under the FHA for failing to provide reasonable accommodations (air purifiers) and creating a hostile housing environment due to secondhand smoke. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss all claims except the retaliation claim, finding plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege a qualifying disability or failure to accommodate, but allowed the retaliation claim to proceed because plaintiffs adequately alleged that defendants cited their letter to other tenants about housing rights as grounds for eviction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Evonne Bryant, et al. v. City of Norfolk, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; this case involves interference and retaliation claims under § 3617 arising from a letter criticizing residents who sued over a public housing redevelopment plan.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiffs leave to amend their complaint to add a § 3617 interference/retaliation claim because the amendment was timely, not made in bad faith, not prejudicial to defendants, and plausibly stated a claim that a letter sent by NRHA's Executive Director to public housing residents criticizing the plaintiffs' FHA lawsuit constituted coercive, intimidating, or interfering conduct.
brief_summary: Public housing residents and community organizations sued the City of Norfolk and NRHA challenging a redevelopment plan to demolish the Tidewater Gardens public housing community, alleging Fair Housing Act violations. The court granted plaintiffs leave to amend their complaint to add a claim under § 3617 that NRHA's Executive Director sent a letter to residents criticizing the lawsuit in a manner designed to coerce, intimidate, or interfere with plaintiffs' FHA rights.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Greystar Management Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. Co. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Joyce A. Beverly v. GatesHudson, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested reassignment to a handicapped parking space closer to an elevator/entrance due to respiratory illness affecting her ambulation.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding that a disabled tenant need not violate parking rules or risk being towed to establish a viable accommodation claim, and that the landlord's failure to address pet waste aggravating the tenant's respiratory disability was actionable.
brief_summary: A retired 100% disabled veteran sued GatesHudson property management, alleging they failed to reasonably accommodate her respiratory disability by refusing to reassign her parking space closer to the building entrance and by failing to address pet waste in common areas that aggravated her condition. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss the FCRA and state law claims but denied dismissal of the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding both accommodation requests plausible under the FHA's reasonable accommodation standard.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 566 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, Maryland, 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Doran v. Prince William County, 2009 WL 10687839 (E.D. Va. 2009)
Costello v. Johnson, 2011 WL 3820890 (E.D. Va. 2011)

case_name: William Whittman v. Associa, Associations, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that Defendants repair the loud, malfunctioning front door of his rented unit, which his mentally disabled son opened and closed repeatedly throughout the day and night due to his disability, causing noise complaints.
key_holding: The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that while the requested door repair would not significantly burden Defendants, the accommodation was not 'necessary' to afford Plaintiff's son equal opportunity to use and enjoy housing because the noise complaints driving the eviction action had no nexus to the door's malfunction and instead stemmed from unrelated conduct by Plaintiff's family members.
brief_summary: William Whittman, proceeding pro se, sued Associa, Community Management Corporation, and Woodburn Village Condominium Unit Owners Association alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate his mentally disabled son, who suffers from severe depression, anxiety, and PTSD, by refusing to repair a malfunctioning front door that made excessive noise when opened and closed. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that while the door repair would not burden Defendants, the accommodation was not 'necessary' to afford equal housing opportunity because the complaints driving the eviction proceedings stemmed from unrelated conduct including loud music, yelling, and other disturbances.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Matarese v. Archstone Pentagon City, 761 F. Supp. 2d 346 (E.D. Va. 2011)
Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Courtney Graves v. Foulger-Pratt Companies, LLC
accommodation_description: Graves requested a transfer to a different unit within the apartment complex due to her breathing difficulties, which she claimed constituted a disability requiring accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed Graves's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to sufficiently allege she qualified as disabled under the FHA and failed to plead facts showing a sufficient nexus between her alleged disability and the denial of any accommodation.
brief_summary: Courtney Graves sued Foulger-Pratt Companies and others alleging, inter alia, that defendants violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate her disability (inability to breathe) by denying her request to transfer to a different unit. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding Graves failed to adequately plead she was disabled under the FHA and failed to establish a nexus between her alleged disability and defendants' conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty.
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory
Fedynich v. Lozano

case_name: Dan Chacko v. Melissa Preston
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges HOA discriminatorily denied his plans for fencing and a second driveway based on his South-Asian origin.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA disparate treatment claim fails because he failed to allege sufficient factual detail showing his property was similarly situated to comparator properties whose owners were permitted to have similar fencing and driveways.
