brief_summary: Dan Chacko, a South Asian homeowner, sued the Waterford Heights HOA and individual defendants alleging they discriminatorily denied his plans for fencing and a second driveway based on his race, in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b). The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Chacko failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent because he did not provide sufficient factual details showing his property was similarly situated to comparator properties.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Claire Payton and Jonathan Katz v. Liana Arias de Velasco Guallart and Christopher Tschapatt
accommodation_description: This case involves familial status discrimination claims (refusal to renew lease after landlord learned tenant was pregnant), not a disability-related reasonable accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B)
key_holding: The court granted Defendants' motion to vacate entry of default after finding five of six factors weighed in Defendants' favor, including that they acted with reasonable promptness upon learning of the default, showed personal responsibility, had no history of dilatory conduct, caused no prejudice to Plaintiffs, and less drastic sanctions were available.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs, a married couple with a child, sued their former landlords alleging discrimination based on familial status and retaliation under the FHA after Defendants refused to renew their lease upon learning Plaintiff Payton was pregnant. The court denied Plaintiffs' motion for default judgment and granted Defendants' motion to vacate the entry of default, finding Defendants acted promptly upon learning of the default and no factors weighed against vacatur.
key_cases_cited: Colleton Prep. Acad., Inc. v. Hoover Univ., Inc., 616 F.3d 413 (4th Cir. 2010)
Mavila v. Absolute Collection Service, Inc., 539 F. App'x 202 (4th Cir. 2013)
Saunders v. Metro. Prop. Mgmt., Inc., 806 F. App'x 165 (4th Cir. 2020)

case_name: Claire Payton and Jonathan Katz v. Liana Arias de Velasco Guallart and Christopher Tschappatt
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation requested; case involves refusal to renew lease based on familial status (pregnancy) and retaliation for asserting fair housing rights
key_holding: The court denied defendant Tschappatt's motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiffs adequately alleged facts to support their fair housing claims against the co-owner landlord, as the FHA's non-discrimination duty is non-delegable and both spouses can be liable when one engages in discriminatory conduct while renting jointly owned property.
brief_summary: A married couple with a child sued their landlords for refusing to renew their lease after learning the wife was pregnant, alleging familial status discrimination under the FHA and VFHL. The court denied the co-owner landlord's motion to dismiss, finding sufficient factual allegations to support claims that both defendants—joint property owners—could be held liable for discriminatory conduct and retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Equal Rights Ctr. v. Niles Bolton Associates, 602 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 2010)
Walker v. Crigler, 976 F.2d 900 (4th Cir. 1992)
King v. Rubenstein, 825 F.3d 206 (4th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Lattimore v. Brahmbhatt
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged wrongful eviction when asked to relocate from manager's apartment at a motel so new owners could occupy the premises; not a reasonable accommodation claim as defined under FHA § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The Brahmbhatts are entitled to summary judgment because they were not Plaintiffs' landlords under the FHA (Plaintiffs abandoned this argument by failing to respond), and even on the merits, Plaintiffs admitted they were asked to move so the new owner could occupy the premises, negating any discriminatory motive.
brief_summary: Patrick and Shirley Lattimore sued their former employers (the Brahmbhatts, motel owners) and Truist Bank, alleging employment discrimination, wrongful eviction under the FHA, and negligence related to wage payments and bank account deductions. The court granted summary judgment for defendants because Plaintiffs failed to exhaust EEOC remedies for race/age discrimination claims and abandoned their FHA claims by failing to respond to defendants' argument that they were not landlords, with the court further noting on the merits that Plaintiffs admitted the move was to accommodate the new owner, not discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Walton v. Harker, 33 F.4th 165 (4th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Jacquelyn A. Allen v. Virgin Islands Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be transferred from Apartment 63 at Lucinda Millin to another apartment away from a busy street because road noise causes her anxiety.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to adequately allege that VIHA knew or should have known of her disability (anxiety) other than through her mere assertion, and she failed to adequately allege that VIHA refused the accommodation since she had not provided the requested medical documentation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jacquelyn A. Allen, a resident of Virgin Islands Housing Authority's Lucinda Millin public housing community, sued VIHA alleging it violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing her reasonable accommodation request to be transferred to an apartment away from road noise that exacerbates her anxiety. The court dismissed her FHA failure-to-accommodate claim because she failed to adequately plead that VIHA knew or should have known of her disability absent medical documentation, and dismissed related claims under the Rehabilitation Act and ADA; dismissal was without prejudice to allow amendment.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Funyak v. Pennrose Mgmt. Co., 2021 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 135617 (W.D. Pa. July 21, 2021)

source_file: 10228909_2024_Nelson v. Long Reef Condominium Homeowners Association_vid
case_name: Sandra Nelson v. Long Reef Condominium Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support dog (chihuahua named Pawla) despite the condominium's no-pets policy, supported by a letter from a licensed clinical social worker.
key_holding: The Court granted in part Nelson's attorneys' fees petition, awarding $106,900 for underlying litigation, $6,345 for fee petition work, and $6,382.44 in costs (total $119,627.44), after reducing requested rates to community standards and applying a 50% reduction for excessive billing, dual representation, and attorney turnover.
brief_summary: Sandra Nelson sued Long Reef Condominium Homeowners Association for refusing to accommodate her emotional support animal under the Fair Housing Act, and after a bench trial won $12,000 in actual damages and $45,000 in punitive damages for the HOA's failure to engage in an interactive process regarding her ESA request. The Court now granted in part Nelson's attorneys' fees petition, reducing the requested $271,508.77 to $119,627.44 after finding excessive billing rates and hours, dual representation with non-prevailing co-plaintiff, and attorney turnover costs.
key_cases_cited: Hensley v. Eckerhart, 461 U.S. 424 (1983)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Carey v. City of Wilkes-Barre, 496 F. App'x 234 (3d Cir. 2012)
Kromenhoek v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 2015 WL 201406 (D.V.I. July 15, 2015)

source_file: 10229836_2019_State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside_waed
case_name: State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case involves challenge to City's Crime Free Rental Housing Program as discriminatory based on national origin, familial status, and sex
key_holding: The State lacks parens patriae standing because it failed to allege injury to a sufficiently substantial segment of its population beyond identifiable individual residents, as only three specific incidents in one city of 16,000 residents were alleged.
brief_summary: The State of Washington sued the City of Sunnyside and police officers challenging the City's Crime Free Rental Housing Program as discriminatory under the Fair Housing Act (national origin, familial status, sex) and unconstitutional under due process. The federal court dismissed the case for lack of standing, finding the State failed to establish parens patriae standing because it did not sufficiently allege injury to a statewide or substantial segment of its population beyond three specific incidents in one municipality.
key_cases_cited: Alfred L. Snapp & Son, Inc. v. Puerto Rico, ex rel., Barez, 458 U.S. 592 (1982)
Mo ex rel. Koster v. Harris, 847 F.3d 646 (9th Cir. 2017)
Town of Chester v. Laroe Estates, Inc., 137 S. Ct. 1645 (2017)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
Carnegie-Mellon University v. Cohill, 484 U.S. 343 (1988)

source_file: 10230268_2020_State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside_waed
case_name: State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside
accommodation_description: The State alleges that Sunnyside's Crime Free Rental Housing Program results in discriminatory extrajudicial evictions based on national origin, sex, and familial status; no disability accommodation is at issue.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the State has parens patriae standing to sue and has stated plausible disparate treatment and disparate impact claims under the Fair Housing Act based on allegations that Sunnyside's Crime Free Rental Housing Program disproportionately affects Latino/as, women, and families with children.
brief_summary: The State of Washington sued the City of Sunnyside and individual police officers alleging that Sunnyside's Crime Free Rental Housing Program results in discriminatory extrajudicial evictions that disproportionately harm Latino/as, women, and families with children in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the State has parens patriae standing and has stated plausible housing discrimination claims under disparate treatment and disparate impact theories.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of N.Y.
Alfred L. Snapp & Son, Inc. v. Puerto Rico, ex rel. Barez

source_file: 10230744_2021_State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside_waed
case_name: State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it challenges the City's Crime Free Rental Housing Program as having a discriminatory impact on Latino/as, women, and families with children.
key_holding: The State of Washington lacks parens patriae standing to pursue FHA and constitutional claims because it failed to produce evidence showing a concrete, particularized injury fairly traceable to Defendants' enforcement of the Crime Free Rental Housing Program, and failed to demonstrate a quasi-sovereign interest beyond the interests of identifiable individual residents.
brief_summary: The State of Washington sued the City of Sunnyside and individual police officers, alleging that the City's Crime Free Rental Housing Program resulted in unconstitutional and discriminatory housing practices against Latino/as, women, and families with children in violation of the Fair Housing Act and constitutional due process. The Court granted Defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding that Washington failed to establish parens patriae standing because the evidence of eight isolated incidents did not demonstrate a concrete, particularized injury traceable to the CFRHP or a quasi-sovereign interest beyond identifiable individuals, and declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Alfred L. Snapp & Son, Inc. v. Puerto Rico ex rel. Barez, 458 U.S. 592 (1982)
Missouri ex rel. Koster v. Harris, 847 F.3d 646 (9th Cir. 2017)
Carnegie-Mellon University v. Cohill, 484 U.S. 343 (1988)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10230879_2021_State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside_waed
case_name: State of Washington v. City of Sunnyside
accommodation_description: Systemic challenge by the State of Washington to Sunnyside's Crime Free Rental Housing Program (CFRHP), alleging discriminatory enforcement based on national origin, sex, and familial status.
key_holding: The Court granted Plaintiff's motion to amend judgment and remanded the entire case to state court because under 28 U.S.C. § 1447(c), a removed case in which the plaintiff lacks Article III standing must be remanded rather than dismissed.
brief_summary: The State of Washington sued the City of Sunnyside and individual officials, alleging that Sunnyside's Crime Free Rental Housing Program violated the Fair Housing Act and constitutional rights by discriminatorily enforcing eviction procedures against tenants based on national origin, sex, and familial status. The Court granted Plaintiff's motion to amend its prior summary judgment order, ruling that the entire case must be remanded to state court under 28 U.S.C. § 1447(c) because the district court lacked Article III standing to adjudicate the claims.
key_cases_cited: Polo v. Innoventions Int'l, LLC, 833 F.3d 1193 (9th Cir. 2016)
Int'l Primate Prot. League v. Administrators of Tulane Educ. Fund, 500 U.S. 72 (1991)
Marlyn Nutraceuticals, Inc. v. Mucos Pharma GmbH & Co., 571 F.3d 873 (9th Cir. 2009)
Bell v. City of Kellogg, 922 F.2d 1418 (9th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Rebecca Lavelle v. CL West Management LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges she was evicted from General Manager's suite based on protected characteristics (sex/religion) in violation of Title VIII.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment on plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because there was no genuine issue of material fact as to whether plaintiff was evicted from the General Manager's suite based on a protected characteristic—she was not the General Manager and was simply told she could not stay for free in the suite while the actual GM was at training.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rebecca Lavelle, proceeding pro se, sued her former employer CL West Management LLC and related entities alleging employment discrimination (ADEA, Title VII, FLSA, EPA) and a Fair Housing Act claim arising from being told she could not stay in the General Manager's suite. The court granted in part and denied in part summary judgment, allowing plaintiff's age discrimination and FLSA overtime claims to proceed while granting summary judgment on the FHA claim because plaintiff failed to demonstrate the eviction from the suite was based on a protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Wood v. City of San Diego, 678 F.3d 1075 (9th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 10234237_2020_Firs Home Owners Association v. City of SeaTac_wawd
case_name: Firs Home Owners Association v. City of SeaTac
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case concerns mobile home park closure approval allegedly driven by discriminatory animus toward Latino/Hispanic residents.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff raised a plausible inference of discriminatory intent based on national origin, but failed to allege a robust causal connection between the City's approval of the relocation plan and the disparate impact on Latino/Hispanic residents as required by Inclusive Communities.
brief_summary: The Firs Home Owners Association, representing predominantly Latino/Hispanic mobile home park residents, sued the City of SeaTac alleging the City discriminated against its members based on national origin when it approved a property owner's plan to close the mobile home park and relocate residents. The court granted in part defendant's motion to dismiss, finding that while plaintiff stated a plausible disparate treatment claim, the disparate impact claim failed because plaintiff did not allege a robust causal connection between the City's actions and the statistical disparity.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Gilbert v. Martinson
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability discrimination related to an eviction judgment, though no specific accommodation request is articulated.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA or ADA claim because it lacks factual allegations establishing a causal connection between defendant's conduct and plaintiff's disability.
brief_summary: Michelle Gilbert, proceeding pro se, sued Jamie Martinson (a property manager at Reach Community Development, Inc.) alleging disability discrimination in connection with an eviction judgment and retaliation for filing a prior lawsuit. The court found the complaint failed to state a claim because it lacked factual allegations establishing a causal connection between defendant's conduct and plaintiff's disability, and ordered plaintiff to show cause why the case should not be dismissed or file an amended complaint by October 19, 2020.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
San Pedro Hotel Co. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)

case_name: Hicks v. Skaar
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this is a disparate treatment claim alleging racial discrimination in lease non-renewal under § 3604(b), not a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiff failed to establish pretext in the non-renewal decision, as defendants articulated legitimate, nondiscriminatory reasons (documented pattern of unsubstantiated complaints and escalating conduct) and the demographic evidence showed no discriminatory pattern.
brief_summary: Alice Hicks, a Black American tenant, sued her former landlord Princeton Property Management and Evergreen Vancouver Apartments, LLC, alleging racial discrimination when they declined to renew her lease. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding Hicks failed to demonstrate pretext—defendants provided legitimate, nondiscriminatory reasons including her documented pattern of unsubstantiated complaints against neighbors, and evidence showed no discriminatory pattern in lease terminations.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Yuzyuk v. Our Place Manor, LLP
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support dog in the apartment as part of treatment plan for minor child M.Y. diagnosed with autism
key_holding: The parties stipulated to a consent order where defendants agreed to allow the emotional support animal, pay $45,000 in damages, implement written reasonable accommodation policies, and provide fair housing training to employees.
brief_summary: Luidmyla and Vadim Yuzyuk, on behalf of their minor son M.Y. who has autism, sued Our Place Manor, LLP and T&S Development Properties LLP alleging defendants violated the Fair Housing Act by initially denying their request to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for M.Y.'s disability under the property's no-pet policy. The parties settled by consent order wherein defendants agreed to permit the emotional support animal, pay $45,000 in damages, adopt written reasonable accommodation procedures, and require employees to undergo fair housing training.
key_cases_cited: United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Overlook Mutual Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 666 F. Supp. 2d 850 (S.D. Ohio 2009)

case_name: Hosseinzadeh v. Bellevue Park Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; case involves claims of intentional discrimination based on national origin and religion (foreclosure, board service denial/removal, records access restriction).
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for BPHOA on the FHA claim because while plaintiff established a prima facie case regarding differential treatment in accessing personal records, plaintiff failed to present evidence that BPHOA's nondiscriminatory justification (litigation concerns) was pretextual.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Hosseinzadeh, who is Iranian and Muslim, sued BPHOA and its director Gonzalez alleging discrimination based on national origin and religion under the Fair Housing Act, Section 1982, WLAD, and CPA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that while plaintiff established a prima facie case regarding differential treatment in accessing personal records, plaintiff failed to rebut BPHOA's legitimate nondiscriminatory reason that litigation concerns required special handling of his records requests.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Gamble v. City of Escondido
Ward v. Caulk
Shaare Tefile Congregation v. Cobb
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc.

case_name: Taylor v. Harbaugh
accommodation_description: Request to allow grandson Ethan Farrington to serve as Plaintiff's live-in caregiver for her multiple sclerosis without being subject to the 55+ community's age restrictions
key_holding: Plaintiff produced sufficient evidence that Defendants departed from normal procedures and established an indication of discriminatory motive regarding the reasonable accommodation claim, and Defendants failed to demonstrate that allowing the grandson to serve as caregiver would have imposed an undue burden.
brief_summary: Amy Taylor, a mobile home park resident with multiple sclerosis, sued the Carriage Estates landlords alleging they violated the FHA by refusing to allow her grandson to serve as her caregiver and by threatening eviction after she requested this accommodation. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the requested accommodation was reasonable and whether Defendants acted with discriminatory motive.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
McGinest v. GTE Serv. Corp., 360 F.3d 1103 (9th Cir. 2004)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)

case_name: Amy Taylor v. Harvey C. Harbaugh, Patricia M. Harbaugh, and Carriage Estates MH 55+ LLC
accommodation_description: Request to have Ethan Farrington serve as plaintiff's caregiver in a mobile home park that allegedly denied him access
key_holding: This pretrial order schedules the case for jury trial and identifies the issues of law to be determined, including whether defendant denied plaintiff's reasonable accommodation request by refusing to allow Ethan Farrington to serve as her caregiver and whether defendant breached its duty to engage in an interactive dialogue.
brief_summary: Amy Taylor, a former tenant with a disability, sued the owners and manager of Carriage Estates Mobile Home Park alleging they discriminated against her by refusing to allow her designated caregiver to assist her. This pretrial order sets the case for jury trial and identifies the core issues as whether having Ethan Farrington serve as a caregiver was a reasonable accommodation, whether defendants refused to make it, and whether defendants breached their duty to engage in an interactive dialogue.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

source_file: 10239844_2022_Firs Home Owners Association v. City of SeaTac_wawd
case_name: Firs Home Owners Association v. City of SeaTac
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case concerns national origin discrimination in municipal approval of a mobile home park relocation plan.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City because plaintiff failed to present evidence from which a reasonable jury could conclude that the City's decision to approve the mobile home park relocation plan was motivated by discriminatory animus against the predominantly Spanish-speaking residents.
brief_summary: The Firs Home Owners Association, representing former residents of a mobile home park, sued the City of SeaTac alleging national origin discrimination when the City approved a relocation plan that led to closure of the park and displacement of its predominantly Spanish-speaking residents. The court granted summary judgment for the City, finding insufficient evidence that the CED Director, Planning Manager, or Hearing Examiner who approved the relocation plan were motivated by discriminatory animus or influenced by the City Council's anti-Hispanic statements.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Antionette Slaughter v. Valley View I LLP
accommodation_description: Ms. Slaughter alleges Valley View denied her reasonable accommodation request, though the specific accommodation is not detailed in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed Ms. Slaughter's FHA retaliation claim because she failed to provide specific factual allegations regarding when she filed her HUD complaints, the subject matter of those complaints, and a causal link between her protected activity and any adverse action by Valley View.
brief_summary: Antionette Slaughter, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Valley View I LLP (her apartment management company), King County Housing Authority, and other defendants alleging retaliation and discrimination under the Fair Housing Act for filing HUD complaints and requesting reasonable accommodations. The court dismissed her complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding she failed to plead sufficient factual detail to support her retaliation and discrimination claims.
key_cases_cited: Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

case_name: Sang W. Mendy v. Tracy L. Larson et al.
accommodation_description: N/A - This is a racial discrimination case, not a reasonable accommodation case
key_holding: The Court found that Mendy's complaint adequately pleaded a Fair Housing Act claim under § 3604(b) against ASP (via successor liability for Northwest), denied ASP's motion for summary judgment because material factual disputes exist regarding whether the Bargers assumed or merely agreed to pay Retained Liabilities, and granted Mendy leave to amend his complaint to add additional factual allegations.
brief_summary: Sang W. Mendy, a Black tenant at the Modera Jackson apartments, sued Tracy Larson (a security guard), Mill Creek Residential Services LLC (the property manager), Northwest Security Services, Inc., and American Security Programs, Inc. (ASP) alleging that Larson repeatedly harassed Black residents based on race and made a false assault accusation that resulted in Mendy's arrest, constituting discrimination in the provision of housing services under § 3604(b) of the Fair Housing Act. The Court denied ASP's motion for summary judgment, finding that genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether ASP (as Northwest's successor) may be held liable for the alleged discriminatory conduct, and granted Mendy leave to amend his complaint to add additional factual allegations supporting his claims.
key_cases_cited: Ohana v. Marriott, 2016 WL 11760169 (C.D. Cal. 2016)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Housing Rights Ctr. v. Sterling, 404 F. Supp. 2d 1179 (C.D. Cal. 2004)

case_name: Ross v. Tucker
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges eviction and sexual harassment based on sexual orientation
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiff failed to allege he was evicted based on his heterosexuality or that the sexual advances were severe or pervasive enough to constitute actionable harassment.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Amontre Ross, detained at a mental health institute, sued his former landlord alleging eviction and sexual harassment based on his heterosexuality in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because Ross did not adequately allege his eviction was based on sexual orientation or that the alleged harassment was severe or pervasive.
key_cases_cited: Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Comm., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Eddie L. Hatch, Jr. and Michelle Davis-Hatch v. Barrett
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged race-based refusal to sell a commercial building they sought to purchase for a food service training facility; no disability accommodation was requested.
key_holding: The FHA does not apply because the property is a commercial building, not a dwelling as defined by 42 U.S.C. § 3602(b), and the moving defendants were not sellers in the transaction.
brief_summary: Eddie and Michelle Hatch sued the City of Milwaukee and various development entities alleging racial discrimination in the sale of a commercial building, claiming the DCD favored a white developer over Black entrepreneurs in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice, holding that the FHA does not apply because the property is a commercial building rather than a dwelling as defined by the Act, and the moving defendants were not sellers in the transaction.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co.
Phillips v. Hunter Trails Cmty. Ass'n

case_name: Eddie L. Hatch, Jr. and Michelle Davis-Hatch v. City of Milwaukee, Tom Barrett, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged racial discrimination in the sale of a commercial building, claiming the City favored a white developer over Black entrepreneurs.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because the commercial building at issue does not constitute a 'dwelling' under the FHA, as plaintiffs intended to use it as a food service training facility for commercial purposes rather than for residential occupancy.
brief_summary: Eddie and Michelle Davis-Hatch, proceeding pro se, sued the City of Milwaukee and various city officials alleging racial discrimination in connection with the City's refusal to sell them a commercial building for use as a food service training facility. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that the commercial property did not constitute a 'dwelling' under the FHA and therefore fell outside the statute's coverage.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Debra Lee Huzjak v. Kim Ellis, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges neighbors harassed her and her mother based on age, disability, race, and religion through break-ins, theft, bank fraud, and reports to the Department of Aging.
key_holding: The court recommends dismissal because Huzjak has not alleged that she personally experienced discrimination rising to the level of actual or constructive eviction, and any claims belonging to her mother cannot be pursued by Huzjak without authorization from the estate.
brief_summary: Debra Lee Huzjak sued her neighbors, the corporate owner of her apartment building, and the manager of the building, alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on discriminatory harassment by neighbors motivated by age, disability, race, and religious animus toward her mother. The court recommends dismissing all claims because Huzjak failed to allege personal discrimination that rose to the level of constructive eviction and cannot assert claims belonging to her mother's estate.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)
Scully v. Goldenson, 751 F. App'x 905 (7th Cir. 2018)
Struck v. Cook Cty. Pub. Guardian, 508 F.3d 858 (7th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Shyanne Tubbs v. PMI of Greater Milwaukee, Monica Steller, David Grisamore, and Peter Hintz
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; plaintiff alleges mistreatment following apartment fire and speculates about racial discrimination.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal because plaintiff's amended complaint failed to allege sufficient facts to state a plausible Fair Housing Act claim, containing only speculative assertions about racial motivation without factual support.
brief_summary: Shyanne Tubbs, proceeding pro se, filed suit against her property management company and related defendants alleging mistreatment after a fire at her apartment and claiming Fair Housing Act violations based on race. The court screened the amended complaint and recommended dismissal, finding that Tubbs failed to allege sufficient facts to state a plausible claim for relief, offering only speculative assertions about racial motivation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal

source_file: 10250032_2022_McFarland v. Housing Authority of Racine County_wied
case_name: McFarland v. Housing Authority of Racine County
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, mother of two disabled children including a physically-disabled son requiring a wheelchair-accessible home, sought an increase in Section 8 Vouchers and an extension of time to find suitable housing, which HARC denied.
key_holding: The court denied Schattner's motion to dismiss, finding plausible Plaintiff's state law retaliation claim under Wis. Stat. § 704.45 for terminating her tenancy after she complained about foundation issues; however, Plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim under § 3617 failed because she did not allege retaliation based on a protected class.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Nakreisha McFarland sued her landlord Michael Schattner and the Housing Authority of Racine County alleging, inter alia, that Schattner evicted her in retaliation for complaining about dangerous foundation conditions, and that HARC denied her request for an extension of time on her Section 8 Voucher without considering her disabled children's needs. The court denied Schattner's motion to dismiss, finding plausible her state law retaliation claim but rejecting her FHA retaliation claim for failure to allege retaliation based on a protected class.
key_cases_cited: Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Johnson v. Flannery, 2022 WL 103710 (N.D. Ill. Jan. 11, 2022)
Romero v. Pinnacle Equities, LLC, 283 F. App'x 429 (8th Cir. 2008)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10250516_2022_Newman v. Nazcr Trac Property Owners Association Inc_wied
case_name: Newman v. Nazcr Trac Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request to build a backyard fence to prevent autistic children from eloping, as a reasonable accommodation under the FHAA.
key_holding: The Association's blanket policy of refusing to consider reasonable accommodation requests and automatically denying them violates § 3604(f)(3)(B), and private covenants and by-laws cannot shield the Association from FHAA obligations.
brief_summary: Lisa and Michael Newman sued the Nazcr Trac Property Owners Association seeking to build a backyard fence as a reasonable accommodation for their three autistic children who have elopement issues. The court granted the preliminary injunction, finding the Association's policy of automatically denying accommodation requests and refusing to engage in the interactive process likely violates the FHAA, and that private covenants cannot override federal fair housing law.
key_cases_cited: Valencia v. City of Springfield, Ill., 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Wis. Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Cassell v. Snyders, 990 F.3d 539 (7th Cir. 2021)

case_name: United States of America v. Chad Eilman, Jeffrey Eilman, and Christine Neigum
accommodation_description: Request to keep a five-pound chihuahua named Roxy as an assistance animal prescribed by a psychologist for anxiety, depression, schizoaffective disorder, and PTSD
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motions to compel a Rule 35 psychological examination of McLean and a Rule 34 examination of her assistance animal, finding both requests relevant and proportional to the needs of the Fair Housing Act case and that good cause exists for the examinations.
brief_summary: The United States sued landlords Chad Eilman, Jeffrey Eilman, and Christine Neigum on behalf of Angela McLean, alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to rent her an apartment after learning she had an assistance animal. The court granted defendants' motions to compel a psychological examination of McLean and an examination of her chihuahua Roxy, finding the discovery requests relevant to whether Roxy was a reasonable and necessary accommodation and proportional to the needs of the case.
key_cases_cited: Balistreri v. United States
Bronk v. Ineichen
Doe v. Oberweis Dairy
Laudicina v. City of Crystal Lake
Rose v. Cahee

case_name: Debrra Lee Huzjak v. Kim Ellis, Caitlin Sexton, Aurora St. Lukes Southshore, Aurora Zilber Family Hospice, Aurora Health, Eastshore Properties, Brenda Matheson, Steven Peters, Kathy Berlin, William Berlin and Chai Franciscan Villa Nursing Home
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; case involves neighbor harassment, nursing home care challenges, and guardianship disputes
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint for failure to state a plausible federal claim, finding the FHA does not apply to disputes between neighbors and any claims belonging to the mother must be pursued by her estate, not the plaintiff.
brief_summary: Plaintiff filed a complaint relating to her mother's death and care, alleging neighbor harassment in her apartment building and challenges to guardianship and nursing home care decisions. The court adopted the magistrate judge's recommendation and dismissed the case for failure to state a plausible federal claim, finding no jurisdiction and no cognizable FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 586 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Preserve at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Badgerow v. Walters, 142 S. Ct. 1310 (2022)
Scully v. Goldenson, 751 F. App'x 905 (7th Cir. 2018)
West v. Atkins, 487 U.S. 42 (1988)

case_name: Suan D. Kendrick v. Oakbrook Corporation et al.
accommodation_description: Criminal background screening denial based on felony conviction over 20 years old
key_holding: The plaintiff stated sufficient facts to proceed with a racial discrimination claim under the Fair Housing Act by identifying the type of discrimination (racial), the defendants (Oakbrook Corporation, Lindley, Gerensky, and Resident Check LLC), and when the discrimination occurred (February 4, 2019).
brief_summary: Suan D. Kendrick, an African American plaintiff, sued Oakbrook Corporation, Rebecca Lindley, Heather Gerensky, and Resident Check LLC alleging that they denied his rental application at Beerline B in Milwaukee because of his race and a criminal conviction over 20 years old, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court screened the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915A and found that the plaintiff stated sufficient factual allegations to proceed with a racial discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41 (1957)

case_name: Shawanda V. Collins v. Team Management LLC et al.
subsidy_program: Section 42 Low Income Housing Tax Credits
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's claims center on a criminal history screening policy alleged to have disparate impact on African American applicants, and an income policy allegedly deterring disabled applicants—not a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The court allowed Collins's racial disparate impact claim to proceed based on statistics showing the criminal history policy disproportionately affects African Americans, but dismissed her federal disability discrimination claim because she failed to allege causation—her criminal history, not her disability, caused the denial.
brief_summary: Shawanda Collins, an African American woman with disabilities, sued Team Management LLC and the owners of Eighteen87 on Water property, alleging that their criminal history screening policy disproportionately excluded African American applicants and that their income policy deterred disabled applicants. The court screened the complaint and allowed the racial disparate impact claim to proceed while dismissing the federal disability discrimination claim for failure to allege causation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Metropolitan Housing Development Corp. v. Village of Arlington Heights, 558 F.2d 1283 (7th Cir. 1977)

case_name: Sara Manzke v. Jefferson County and Town of Ixonia
accommodation_description: Request to keep emotional support animals (miniature goats and geese) on residential property as a reasonable accommodation for disabilities including recurrent depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder
key_holding: The Town of Ixonia cannot be held liable for failing to reasonably accommodate the plaintiff's emotional support animals because the town lacks authority to waive or modify Jefferson County's zoning ordinance, which controls all zoning decisions in the town.
brief_summary: Sara Manzke sued Jefferson County and Town of Ixonia under the Fair Housing Amendments Act after both governmental entities denied her requests for zoning variances and conditional use permits to keep emotional support animals on her residential property. The court granted the Town of Ixonia's motion to dismiss, finding the town lacked authority to grant the requested accommodation because Jefferson County holds final decision-making authority over zoning in the town.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, Illinois, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Good Shepherd Manor Foundation, Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Andrew Kummerow v. Ohawcha.org, Wendy Fromm, and Her Supervisors
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleged discrimination based on sexual orientation and retaliation for complaints about housing conditions.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint failed to state a viable FHA claim because his allegations of discrimination based on sexual orientation did not give rise to an inference of discriminatory intent, and his retaliation claim failed because his complaints about habitability were not protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Andrew Kummerow sued the Oshkosh/Winnebago County Housing Authority and its property manager alleging discrimination based on sexual orientation, substandard housing conditions, and retaliation for complaints. The court dismissed his amended complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding that his allegations did not support an inference of discriminatory animus and that his habitability complaints did not constitute protected activity under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Community, LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Thomas v. Nationstar Mortgage, LLC
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request described; the complaint concerns mortgage payment processing and credit reporting disputes rather than a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim because plaintiffs failed to allege that the challenged actions were motivated by a discriminatory purpose or had a discriminatory impact, failing to state a prima facie case under the FHA.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Anthony and Erica Thomas sued Nationstar Mortgage, LLC and Fortress Investment Group alleging violations of the FDCPA, HMDA, ECOA, FCRA, and FHA arising from a dispute over mortgage payoff procedures and credit reporting after the sale of their home. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss all claims, dismissing the FHA claim without prejudice because plaintiffs failed to allege facts establishing a prima facie case of discriminatory purpose or impact.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., Md.

case_name: United States v. Walden
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request is at issue; this opinion addresses civil contempt for violating a consent decree in a sex discrimination case.
key_holding: The court held defendants in civil contempt for failing to pay $400,000 into a settlement fund and $100,000 in civil penalties as required by a consent decree resolving sexual harassment claims, finding defendants failed to meet their burden of proving inability to comply.
brief_summary: The United States moved to hold landlords Gary Walden and related entities in civil contempt for failing to comply with a 2017 consent decree requiring payment of $500,000 into a settlement fund for sexual harassment victims and $100,000 in civil penalties. The court granted the motion, finding the defendants knowingly violated the decree and failed to prove their defense of inability to pay, despite owning over $700,000 in property.
key_cases_cited: Ashcraft v. Conoco, Inc., 218 F.3d 288 (4th Cir. 2000)
United States v. Rylander, 460 U.S. 752 (1983)
United States v. Butler, 211 F.3d 826 (4th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Deandre Bradley v. Ronald Hain, individually and in his official capacity as the Sheriff of Kane County; Kane County, Illinois; Rochelle Stockman; A. Keaty; S. McKanna; M. Azemi; J. Directo; L. Aguirre; S. Flowers; P. Osmani; Dr. Kul Sood; Wellpath LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a pretrial detainee with paralysis who uses a wheelchair, requested an accessible cell with grab bars, accessible toilet and shower, and an emergency alert button near those fixtures.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Act does not apply to a jail because a jail cell is not a 'dwelling' under 42 U.S.C. § 3602(b), as the FHA's purpose of promoting freedom of choice in housing has no application in the prison context where inmates are subject to strict regulation of where and how they live.
brief_summary: Deandre Bradley, a pretrial detainee with paralysis who uses a wheelchair, sued the Sheriff of Kane County, Kane County, correctional officers, and Wellpath LLC (healthcare contractor) alleging they failed to provide disability accommodations and proper medical care at the Kane County Adult Justice Center. The court granted Defendants' motions to dismiss, dismissing Bradley's FHA claims with prejudice because the court found that a jail cell does not constitute a 'dwelling' under the FHA, and therefore the FHA does not apply to detention facilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Garcia v. Condarco

case_name: Larry L. Henderson v. Cornelius Bryant, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested modifications to bathroom doors, bedrooms, entry doors, and other areas of his apartment unit to accommodate his mobility disability requiring a powered wheelchair, which defendants failed to complete.
key_holding: The Court found plaintiff stated plausible FHA claims against the property owner, management company, and several individual defendants for failing to make reasonable accommodations and modifications, while dismissing claims against one defendant and dismissing ADA claims for failure to establish defendants were public entities.
brief_summary: Larry L. Henderson, a disabled tenant in St. Louis, Missouri who uses a powered wheelchair due to a neurological disorder, sued his apartment complex, property management company, and individual employees alleging they failed to make reasonable accommodations and modifications to his unit and restricted his caregivers' access. The Court granted his in forma pauperis application and allowed his FHA claims to proceed against all defendants except one leasing associate, while dismissing his ADA claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89 (2007)

case_name: Sydney Ellerbe v. Tuscan Highlands Apartments
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges he was wrongfully evicted from Tuscan Highlands Apartments in retaliation for or to cover up racial discrimination incidents at an on-site restaurant.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff sufficiently alleged a disparate treatment claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) because he provided circumstantial evidence of discriminatory intent, including timing of eviction following racial incidents, false proffered reason for eviction, and comparison to similarly situated tenants treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Sydney Ellerbe, an African American man, sued Tuscan Highlands Apartments alleging the landlord wrongfully evicted him three days after racially discriminatory incidents at an on-site restaurant, claiming the eviction was pretextual cover-up for discrimination. The court dismissed negligence and defamation claims without prejudice but allowed the Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claim under § 3604(b) and wrongful eviction claim to proceed, finding plaintiff sufficiently alleged circumstantial evidence of discriminatory intent including false proffered reason, suspicious timing, and disparate treatment compared to similarly situated tenants.
key_cases_cited: Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Pac. Shores Properties, LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Jose Antonio Ramos v. Breaking Ground and City of New York
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's primary claims concern racial discrimination in housing placement (disparate treatment), with passing references to unspecified handicaps affecting use of showers/bathrooms
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss because plaintiff failed to plead a qualifying disability substantially limiting major life activities and failed to allege similarly situated comparators for his racial discrimination claim.
brief_summary: Jose Antonio Ramos, a pro se plaintiff and formerly homeless Hispanic man residing in temporary supportive housing at 123 East 15th Street, sued Breaking Ground and the City of New York alleging racial discrimination in housing placement and disability-related claims under the FHA. The court granted both motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff's disability allegations insufficient because he failed to plead a qualifying disability substantially limiting major life activities, and his racial discrimination claim failed because he did not allege he was similarly situated to the non-Hispanic individuals who allegedly received housing before him.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Kesha Lennon v. NVR Mortgage, et al.
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in mortgage lending and housing transactions.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because her complaint contained only vague, conclusory allegations of racial discrimination without factual matter sufficient to plausibly suggest she suffered adverse action for a discriminatory reason.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kesha Lennon sued NVR Mortgage Finance, Ryan Homes, and Legacy Title Agency alleging fraud, RICO violations, and discrimination claims including Fair Housing Act claims related to her failed attempt to purchase a townhouse in Sicklerville, New Jersey. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts to support any of her federal claims, including vague allegations of racial discrimination under the FHA and ECOA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Township of Mount Holly
Shahin v. PNC Bank
White v. Barbe

case_name: Brooklynn C. Clark v. GSL Properties, Inc., CBRE GWS Real Estate Services, Inc., One & Three Centerpointe Drive, Monica Ulrig, Anthony Doe, John Doe, Jon/Jane Does 1-20, Bryce Siebenthall, and Kimberly Carpenter
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an accommodation related to her anxiety, which defendants requested documentation for but never responded to or honored.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's sex, race, and disability discrimination claims but found she pleaded sufficient facts to survive dismissal on her failure to make reasonable accommodations claim based on anxiety, as she requested an accommodation, provided supporting documentation, and defendants never responded.
brief_summary: Brooklynn C. Clark, a Black transgender woman with anxiety, sued GSL Properties and related defendants alleging housing discrimination based on race, sex, gender, and disability including disparate rent increases, false reports to law enforcement, and security deposit retention. The court dismissed most claims but held that plaintiff pleaded sufficient facts to proceed on her failure to make reasonable accommodations claim, finding she requested an accommodation for her anxiety, provided supporting documentation from her doctor, and defendants failed to respond.
key_cases_cited: McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Gilligan v. Jamco Dev. Corp., 108 F.3d 246 (9th Cir. 1997)
Gibson v. Cmty. Dev. Partners, 2022 WL 10481324 (D. Or. 2022)
Myers v. Highlands at Vista Ridge Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 2022 WL 4452414 (D. Or. 2022)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

source_file: 10286804_2024_Dogbe v. The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC_nvd
case_name: John Dogbe and Alberta Dogbe v. The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege they were subjected to discriminatory treatment based on race when asked to cease a clubhouse party and subsequently served with an eviction notice.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claims under § 3604(b) and § 3617 because the complaint lacked sufficient factual allegations to plausibly establish that Defendant's actions were motivated by racial discrimination rather than legitimate concerns about the party size and conduct.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs John and Alberta Dogbe, who are of African descent, sued their landlord The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC alleging racial discrimination after being required to end a clubhouse party and receiving an eviction notice. The court granted Defendant's motion to dismiss the FHA claims, finding the complaint lacked sufficient factual allegations to plausibly allege discrimination because of race, though the court granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Ave. 6E Investments, LLC v. City of Yuma, Ariz., 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)
Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)

source_file: 10287865_2024_The Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa_ca9
case_name: The Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa
accommodation_description: Ohio House requested a waiver of the 650-foot separation requirement that would allow it to operate its sober-living facility in the multi-family residential zone despite being within 650 feet of other group homes.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed that Ohio House's requested accommodation—a waiver of the City's separation requirement—was unreasonable because the jury had an evidentiary basis to find that granting such a waiver would cause a fundamental alteration of the City's zoning scheme designed to prevent overconcentration of sober living facilities.
brief_summary: The Ohio House, LLC, a sober-living facility operator, sued the City of Costa Mesa challenging zoning ordinances requiring conditional-use permits and a 650-foot separation requirement for group homes serving disabled individuals in recovery from substance abuse. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment for the City, holding that Ohio House failed to prove disparate treatment and disparate impact claims, and that the jury had sufficient evidence to find Ohio House's requested waiver of the separation requirement was unreasonable as it would fundamentally alter the City's zoning scheme.
key_cases_cited: Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Socal Recovery, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 56 F.4th 802 (9th Cir. 2023)
Community House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Giebeler v. M & B Properties, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 10289147_2024_HUMPHRIES v. U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING _ URBAN DEVELOPEMENT_paed
case_name: Jacqueline R. Humphries v. U.S. Department of Housing & Urban Development, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to respond in writing or via electronic communication instead of telephone interviews as an accommodation for disabilities including memory loss, anxiety, and depression during HUD's investigation of her discrimination complaints.
key_holding: The court dismissed all claims because the ADA does not apply to the federal government, the Rehabilitation Act does not waive sovereign immunity for money damages against HUD, the FHA provides no private right of action against HUD for failure to investigate, and plaintiff failed to state plausible constitutional claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jacqueline Humphries sued HUD and its employees alleging they denied her reasonable accommodation requests (to communicate in writing/electronically instead of by phone) during the investigation of her housing discrimination complaints against multiple housing authorities. The court dismissed all claims, finding the ADA and Rehabilitation Act inapplicable to the federal government due to sovereign immunity, the FHA provides no private right of action against HUD, and plaintiff failed to state viable constitutional claims under Bivens.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lane v. Pena, 518 U.S. 187 (1996)
Turner v. Sec'y of U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 449 F.3d 536 (3d Cir. 2006)
Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents of Federal Bureau of Narcotics, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)
Marinoff v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 892 F. Supp. 493 (S.D.N.Y. 1995)

case_name: McGinn v. Broadmead, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request that Broadmead retirement community provide safe, varied gluten-free food options to accommodate celiac disease
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to Broadmead on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case that Broadmead denied her a reasonable accommodation for her celiac disease, and the court also found many claims time-barred under the two-year FHA statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Eleanor McGinn, an 83-year-old resident with celiac disease at Broadmead, a retirement community in Hunt Valley, Maryland, sued Broadmead under the FHA, Rehabilitation Act, and ADA alleging the community failed to accommodate her need for safe gluten-free food, having experienced multiple gluten exposures from meals served at the community. The court granted Broadmead's motion for summary judgment, finding McGinn failed to establish a prima facie failure-to-accommodate claim and that many of her claims were time-barred under the applicable statutes of limitations.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Ricco v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

case_name: Shaniqua Newkirk v. Douglas Elliman, Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; plaintiff alleges racial discrimination through refusal to rent based on housing voucher status (source of income) with disparate impact on African Americans
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss plaintiff's FHA claim as time-barred because discriminatory acts occurred in February and June 2021, beyond the two-year limitations period, and neither the discovery rule nor continuing violation doctrine applies; discriminatory advertisements published within the limitations period did not injure plaintiff who merely viewed them.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Shaniqua Newkirk, an African American housing voucher holder, sued Douglas Elliman and individual brokers alleging racial discrimination under the FHA, claiming that defendants' refusal to rent to voucher holders and publication of income-restrictive advertisements disparately impacted Black and Hispanic renters. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss the FHA claim as time-barred, finding the alleged discriminatory acts occurred in 2021, beyond the two-year statute of limitations, and dismissed the remaining state law claims without prejudice for lack of supplemental jurisdiction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McDermott v. New York Metro LLC, 664 F. Supp. 2d 294 (S.D.N.Y. 2009)
Ragin v. Harry Macklowe Real Estate Company, 6 F.3d 898 (2d Cir. 1993)
Jordan v. Chase Manhattan Bank, 91 F. Supp. 3d 491 (S.D.N.Y. 2015)

case_name: Andrea Ortiz v. Beverly Valasek, Donald E. Loudner, Douglas County Housing Authority, Gretna Crown, L.L.C.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of a handicap ramp at the rental property for her mother who could not use stairs due to disability.
key_holding: The federal district court dismissed plaintiff's complaint without prejudice, finding it lacked subject matter jurisdiction under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine because plaintiff's claims arose from and sought to reverse a state court eviction judgment, and alternatively would abstain under Younger given the ongoing state court proceedings.
brief_summary: Andrea Ortiz, proceeding pro se, sued Douglas County Housing Authority, its Section 8 manager, an attorney, and a property owner alleging disability discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act and due process violations under § 1983, arising from her eviction from public housing for unpaid rent; the court dismissed the case without prejudice on jurisdictional grounds, holding that Rooker-Feldman barred review of claims seeking to reverse the state court eviction judgment and that Younger abstention applied given ongoing state proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Rooker v. Fid. Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
D.C. Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971)

case_name: Michael John Ryall, Sr. v. Normandy Village Apartments, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; plaintiff claims general discrimination from lease non-renewal under the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: The magistrate recommended dismissal because the plaintiff, a white male with no disabilities, failed to plead that he is a member of a protected class under the Fair Housing Act, and the lease dispute is a state contract matter lacking federal jurisdiction.
brief_summary: Michael John Ryall, Sr. sued Normandy Village Apartments, Inc. claiming the landlord discriminated against him under the Fair Housing Act by failing to properly notify him of lease non-renewal. The magistrate recommended dismissal, finding the plaintiff is a white male with no disabilities and failed to allege membership in any protected class required to state an FHA claim, and the underlying lease dispute is a state contract matter.
key_cases_cited: Roberson v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., No. CV 23-4022, 2023 WL 10406019 (E.D. La. Dec. 12, 2023)
Treece v. Perrier Condo. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 593 F. Supp. 3d 422 (E.D. La. 2022)
L & F Homes & Dev., LLC v. City of Gulfport, Miss., 2012 WL 2863481 (S.D. Miss. July 11, 2012)

source_file: 10299281_2024_Semper v. Hawaii Life Real Estate Services, LLC_hid
case_name: Jehan Semper v. Hawaii Life Real Estate Services, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: N/A - plaintiff alleges religious discrimination (based on Judaism) in real estate transactions, not an accommodation request
key_holding: The First Amended Complaint failed to state plausible claims for religious discrimination under the FHA because plaintiff's conclusory allegations of discriminatory intent were insufficient without factual allegations supporting an inference that defendants' conduct was motivated by her Jewish faith.
brief_summary: Jehan Semper, who is Jewish, sued multiple real estate agencies and agents alleging they conspired to prevent her from purchasing vacant land in Hawaii County because of her religion, and later retaliated by interfering with her HUD complaint. The court recommended dismissing the First Amended Complaint with prejudice because plaintiff failed to plead sufficient factual allegations to support a plausible inference of discriminatory intent, offering only conclusory assertions that defendants discriminated against her because she is Jewish.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

Sanders v. SWS Hilltop, LLC, 309 F. Supp. 3d 877 (D. Or. 2018)

case_name: Brooklynn C. Clark v. GSL Properties, Inc. et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant not post or stick any information on her door and not place a trash can in front of her entrance, both as accommodations for anxiety disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff fails to state an FHA claim because she did not allege the race, sex, or gender identity of her neighbors to establish that similarly situated individuals outside her protected class were treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Brooklynn Clark, a Black transgender woman with anxiety, sued GSL Properties Inc. and related defendants alleging discrimination during her tenancy including disproportionate rent increases, police contact, and eviction. The court recommends dismissing her FHA claims because she failed to adequately plead that similarly situated individuals outside her protected classes were treated more favorably, and recommends dismissal of her ADA and Title VII claims on separate grounds.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Carlos A. Alonso Cano, et al. v. 245 C and C, LLC and CFH GROUP, LLC
accommodation_description: Request for assigned handicapped parking space for disabled son Angie
key_holding: Magistrate Judge recommended denying Defendants' motion for attorneys' fees because Plaintiffs' claims were not frivolous under Christiansburg standard, and Defendants failed to demonstrate that fees incurred defending non-frivolous claims were separable from frivolous ones.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Carlos Cano and family sued their former landlords 245 C and C, LLC and CFH GROUP, LLC alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to provide a handicapped parking space and bathroom modification for Cano's disabled son Angie. After prevailing on all claims at trial and through summary judgment, Defendants moved for attorneys' fees under 42 U.S.C. § 3613(c)(2); the Magistrate Judge recommended denying the motion because Plaintiffs' claims, while unsuccessful, were not frivolous under Christiansburg and Defendants failed to apportion fees between frivolous and non-frivolous claims.
key_cases_cited: Buckhannon Bd. & Care Home, Inc. v. W. Va. Dep't of Health & Hum. Res., 532 U.S. 598 (2001)
Christiansburg Garment Co. v. EEOC, 434 U.S. 412 (1978)
Sullivan v. Sch. Bd. of Pinellas Cnty., 773 F.2d 1182 (11th Cir. 1985)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Gonzalez v. Lexington Police Department
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who has asthma, requested that the landlord enforce the non-smoking lease provision against other tenants who were smoking and affecting her respiratory condition.
key_holding: The FHAA reasonable accommodation claim fails because the complaint does not identify what specific accommodation Plaintiff requested, whether that request was reasonable, or what accommodation (if any) the landlord provided.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Tania A. Gonzalez sued the Lexington Police Department and Beacon Point Development LLC in Massachusetts state court, alleging disability discrimination under the ADA and FHAA, constitutional violations under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, and other claims related to alleged failures to accommodate her asthma and complaints about police conduct. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the case because the complaint failed to state viable claims—the FHA reasonable accommodation claim failed because Plaintiff did not identify what specific accommodation was requested, and the § 1983 claims failed because the complaint did not allege violations of clearly established constitutional rights.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Sinatra Young v. Ben Schwadel
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint is too vague to identify a specific accommodation request; references harassment, eviction threats, refusal to make repairs, and a lower-floor apartment request from DHCR proceedings, but does not clearly plead a defined accommodation request.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing plaintiff's FHA claim because his complaint was conclusory and failed to state his disability, whether he requested an accommodation from defendant, what accommodation he requested, or how defendant failed to provide a reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sinatra Young sued his landlord Ben Schwadel alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on disability discrimination, harassment, and failure to accommodate; the magistrate judge recommended dismissing the complaint for failure to state a plausible FHA claim because Young's complaint was conclusory and failed to identify his disability, describe a specific accommodation request, or allege how defendant denied a reasonable accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Linda Kay Holloway v. Delia SiFuentes
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges race-based discrimination in sale of home she sought to purchase from landlord, where defendant (real estate agent) subsequently purchased the property instead.
key_holding: The court dismissed the case for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction because there was no diversity of citizenship (both parties are Texas citizens) and no federal question jurisdiction, as plaintiff failed to plead facts showing a plausible Fair Housing Act violation even under liberal construction of her race and age discrimination claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Linda Kay Holloway sued Delia SiFuentes alleging race and age discrimination after a failed real estate transaction where SiFuentes, a real estate agent Holloway had contacted to help purchase her rented home, ultimately purchased the property herself. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the case without prejudice for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction, finding no diversity of citizenship and that even liberally construed, Holloway's complaint failed to state plausible facts supporting a Fair Housing Act violation.

case_name: Walter Tanner v. Rochdale Village Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was discriminated against based on perceived disability (HIV status and sex offender status) including refusal to perform repairs and threats of eviction.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA disparate treatment claim fails because the complaint contains only speculative and conclusory allegations about discriminatory motive without sufficient factual support to raise an inference of discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Walter Tanner sued Rochdale Village Inc., a housing cooperative in Jamaica, Queens, alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and ADA based on management's alleged belief he was HIV positive and a sex offender. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to state a plausible discrimination claim because plaintiff's conclusory allegations lacked sufficient factual detail to raise an inference of discriminatory motive.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Carey v. Torres
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks injunctive relief to access tenant storage area and restrain defendants from filing frivolous lawsuits and physical harassment, framed as FHA claims but lacking any accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The amended complaint fails to state an FHA claim because plaintiff did not allege she is a member of a protected class or that defendants discriminated against her based on protected status, warranting dismissal with leave to amend.
brief_summary: Cecilia Carey, a pro se tenant in Oakland, sued private landlord Mauricio Torres and others alleging Fourteenth Amendment violations, Title VII claims, Fair Housing Act violations, and criminal statute claims arising from alleged predatory buyout, lock changes, and unlawful detainer actions. The Court dismissed the FHA claim because plaintiff failed to allege membership in a protected class or discriminatory motivation, granting leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Zikar Holdings LLC v. Ruhland
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are developers seeking to build a residential development with a mosque (masjid) in Lino Lakes, claiming the City's development moratorium was enacted with discriminatory animus toward Muslims in violation of the FHA.
key_holding: The court denied the preliminary injunction motion because plaintiffs failed to demonstrate irreparable harm, as their representations about the purchase agreement lacked foundation and constituted mere speculation about theoretical future loss.
brief_summary: Zikar Holdings LLC and individual Muslim developers sued the City of Lino Lakes and two City Council members, alleging that a one-year development moratorium was enacted with discriminatory animus toward Muslims in violation of the FHA and Free Exercise Clause. The court denied the motion for preliminary injunction, finding that plaintiffs failed to demonstrate irreparable harm because their assertions about the purchase agreement lacked evidentiary foundation.
key_cases_cited: Church of Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah, 508 U.S. 520 (1993)
Dataphase Sys., Inc. v. C L Sys., Inc., 640 F.2d 109 (8th Cir. 1981)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Planned Parenthood Minn., N.D., S.D. v. Rounds, 530 F.3d 724 (8th Cir. 2008)
Jones v. City of Faribault, 2021 WL 1192466 (D. Minn. 2021)

case_name: United States of America v. Madison Property, L.L.C.
accommodation_description: Boucher requested an accommodation to waive the building's no-pets policy to allow her emotional support animal cat to live with her in her apartment.
key_holding: The court denied both parties' summary judgment motions because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether Boucher was financially qualified to rent, which precludes resolution of both the disparate treatment and reasonable accommodation claims at summary judgment.
brief_summary: The United States sued Madison Property and Andrew Brenner alleging they violated the FHA by refusing to rent to Beverly Boucher because of her disability (Count I - disparate treatment) and by refusing to accommodate her ESA cat under the building's no-pets policy (Count II - reasonable accommodation). The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether Boucher was financially qualified to rent, which precluded resolution of both claims.
key_cases_cited: Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997)
One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka, Minn., 93 F.4th 424 (8th Cir. 2024)
Huberty v. Wash. Cnty. Hous. & Redevelopment Auth., 374 F. Supp. 2d 768 (D. Minn. 2005)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

case_name: Hartley v. United Student Rentals
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint concerns an unrepaired broken front door but does not articulate a specific accommodation request; the complaint mentions mental health impairments but fails to connect the door repair issue to disability-related need.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal of plaintiff's FHA claim because the complaint failed to allege sufficient facts connecting defendants' failure to repair the apartment door to plaintiff's disability, and plaintiff did not adequately describe how his mental impairments limit major life activities.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Michael Hartley sued private landlords and a property attorney alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to repair his apartment front door over approximately one year, claiming disability-based discrimination. The court recommended dismissal without prejudice because plaintiff failed to allege facts connecting defendants' alleged failure to make repairs to his mental health disability, and the complaint did not state how his impairments substantially limit major life activities.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Ingraham v. Red Carpet Hous. Corp., 2017 WL 5152343 (N.D.N.Y. 2017)
Robinson v. Soc. Serv., 2013 WL 1122450 (N.D.N.Y. 2013)
Rendell-Baker v. Kohn, 457 U.S. 830 (1982)
Fabrikant v. French, 691 F.3d 193 (2d Cir. 2012)
