case_name: Angel Lopez and Kazue Koizumi v. Edward Pec, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that the landlord remediate mold and repair water leaks in their basement apartment to accommodate Angel Lopez's asthma and COPD.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs failed to adequately allege what disability entitled them to accommodation, what specific accommodation they requested, whether defendants knew of the disability, or how refusing to repair water leaks constituted discrimination based on disability.
brief_summary: Angel Lopez and Kazue Koizumi, tenants in a Palisades Park, New Jersey apartment, sued their landlord Edward Pec and The Palisades LLC, as well as a law firm, alleging Fair Housing Act claims (including reasonable accommodation), ADA violations, and state law claims arising from habitability disputes. The District of New Jersey granted both motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to adequately plead their federal claims—the FHA reasonable accommodation claim was dismissed because plaintiffs made only conclusory allegations without stating what accommodation was requested, what disability entitled them to accommodation, or how defendants' failure to repair discriminated against them—and abstained from exercising jurisdiction over related state court matters.
key_cases_cited: Spieth v. Bucks Cnty. Hous. Auth., 594 F. Supp. 2d 584 (E.D. Pa. 2009)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
El v. People's Emergency Ctr., 438 F. Supp. 3d 283 (E.D. Pa. 2020)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Butler v. Sundo Cap., LLC, 559 F. Supp. 3d 452 (W.D. Pa. 2021)

case_name: Gerald Cymbalsky v. 522 Shore Road Owners, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff did not specifically request a reasonable accommodation but appears to challenge the cooperative's commencement of a state court 'furniture eviction' action against him while he was hospitalized.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim fails because he did not allege that he requested and was denied a reasonable accommodation, and the commencement of the state court eviction action was motivated by profit-seeking rather than discriminatory animus based on his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Gerald Cymbalsky sued his cooperative housing corporation, property managers, and attorneys for Fair Housing Act violations, RICO, constitutional privacy violations, and FDCPA violations arising from a state court action seeking access to clean his apartment while he was hospitalized. The court dismissed all claims, finding that the FHA claim failed because Cymbalsky did not request any accommodation and alleged no discriminatory animus, while the other claims failed for failure to state a viable claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
Logan v. Matveevskii

source_file: 10314069_2025_(PS) Roberts v. Sacramento Housing _ Redevelopment Agency_caed
case_name: Sydney Brooke Roberts and David Tyrone Samuel v. Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency, et al.
accommodation_description: Request for an additional bedroom added to plaintiffs' Housing Choice Voucher to accommodate David Samuel's and AIMS's disabilities, including separate bedrooms for each.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment was denied because the evidence was insufficient to establish that defendants violated the FHA, ADA, or Rehabilitation Act, particularly given that the additional bedroom accommodation was ultimately granted in March 2023.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Sydney Brooke Roberts and David Tyrone Samuel, a family with disabilities (David and their minor child AIMS both have qualifying disabilities), sued the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency under the FHA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act for denying their reasonable accommodation request for an additional bedroom added to their Housing Choice Voucher. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment, finding that plaintiffs failed to meet their burden to show they were entitled to judgment as a matter of law, particularly given that the accommodation was ultimately granted in March 2023.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Zukle v. Regents of Univ. of Cal., 166 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 1999)

source_file: 10314802_2024_Xi v. BES Kessler Park Fund X11 LLC_txnd
case_name: K'in Way Xi, et al. v. BES Kessler Park Fund X11 LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege employment discrimination based on race and color, claiming they were fired for stealing Amazon packages while a white employee was given the benefit of the doubt.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a plausible disparate treatment claim because it does not allege that the adverse employment action was taken because of protected status; all employees, regardless of race, were fired for the same conduct after video evidence confirmed their participation.
brief_summary: Four plaintiffs sued their employer under Title VII, Section 1981, and the Fair Housing Act, alleging they were discriminated against based on race and color when they were fired for stealing Amazon packages while a white employee was initially given the benefit of the doubt. The court recommends granting the motion to dismiss because the complaint fails to plausibly allege that the adverse employment action was taken because of a protected status, as all employees were ultimately fired for the same conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of Afr. Am.-Owned Media, 589 U.S. 327 (2020)

case_name: Carmencita María Pedro, et al. v. Hilton Worldwide, Inc., et al.
race_if_mentioned: Pedro is Puerto Rican; Fisher is Afro-Latino
accommodation_description: Request for a $60 laundry credit as a reasonable accommodation for Fisher's schizophrenia
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because the complaint failed to allege facts supporting a plausible inference of race or disability discrimination, including dismissing the $60 laundry credit accommodation request as failing to state a nexus between Fisher's schizophrenia and the requested financial accommodation.
brief_summary: Pedro and Fisher, a mother and son with schizophrenia, sued Hilton and related entities alleging they were ejected from a hotel where Fisher was residing while awaiting permanent supportive housing, claiming discrimination based on race and disability and failure to accommodate. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state plausible claims, finding no facts supporting an inference of discrimination and that the $60 laundry credit request lacked a nexus to Fisher's disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condominium Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Lloyd v. Manbel Devco I LP., No. 23-2261 (E.D. Pa. 2024)
Fair Hous. Rts. Ctr. in Se. Pennsylvania v. Morgan Properties Mgmt. Co., LLC, No. 16-4677 (E.D. Pa. 2018)

case_name: Sydney Ellerbe v. Tuscan Highlands, LLC; Schulman Properties; and Winnie Schulman
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleges race-based disparate treatment and eviction as cover-up for racially motivated removals from a restaurant
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's constructive eviction, disparate impact, negligence, and defamation claims but allowed plaintiff's claim for wrongful eviction under Nevada law and his FHA disparate treatment claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) to proceed because plaintiff sufficiently alleged that defendants used false assault allegations as a pretext to evict him in retaliation for or to cover up racially discriminatory conduct.
brief_summary: Sydney Ellerbe, an African American tenant, sued Tuscan Highlands LLC and related defendants alleging he was discriminatorily removed from a restaurant twice due to his race and then wrongfully evicted using false assault allegations as a pretext. The court granted leave to proceed on his wrongful eviction claim under Nevada law and his Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claim, but dismissed his constructive eviction, disparate impact, negligence, and defamation claims.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10318044_2025_Fair Hous. Just. Ctr., Inc. v. Pelican Mgmt., Inc._ca2
case_name: Fair Hous. Just. Ctr., Inc. v. Pelican Mgmt., Inc.
accommodation_description: The case involves Goldfarb's minimum income policy (requiring applicants to earn 43 times monthly rent) which allegedly had a disparate impact on renters with rental subsidies, many of whom have disabilities.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed the district court's finding that Goldfarb's 2019 minimum income policy violated the Fair Housing Act and NYCHRL by having a disparate impact on applicants with partial rental subsidies, including many disabled individuals, based on Dr. Steil's statistical analysis showing only 13% of partially subsidized applicants were approved compared to 30% of unsubsidized applicants.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center sued Pelican Management and related entities (Goldfarb) alleging that their minimum income policy requiring prospective renters to earn 43 times monthly rent violated the FHA and NYCHRL by disparately impacting disabled applicants who rely on rental subsidies. The Second Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment for FHJC, agreeing that Goldfarb's 2019 Policy had a statistically significant disparate impact on partially subsidized applicants based on Dr. Steil's expert analysis.
key_cases_cited: Levin v. Yeshiva Univ., 96 N.Y.2d 484 (2001)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Mihalik v. Credit Agricole Cheuvreux N. Am., Inc., 715 F.3d 102 (2d Cir. 2013)
Clerveaux v. E. Ramapo Cent. Sch. Dist., 984 F.3d 213 (2d Cir. 2021)
Sacerdote v. N.Y. Univ., 9 F.4th 95 (2d Cir. 2021)

case_name: Latonia Black v. Royal American Management, et al.
accommodation_description: The complaint alleges failure to reasonably accommodate under § 3604(f)(3) but does not specify the accommodation requested; this procedural order does not address the substance of the accommodation claim.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff's motion for leave to file an amended complaint under Rule 15(a)(2)'s liberal standard despite finding plaintiff's delay resulted from bad faith or carelessness, because there was no undue prejudice to defendants and the amendment was filed before the scheduling order deadline.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Latonia Black, a Black tenant, sued Royal American Management and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disparate treatment and failure to reasonably accommodate under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3), along with retaliation claims. This procedural order granted Black's motion for leave to file an amended complaint under Rule 15(a)(2), finding that despite her bad faith or carelessness in delaying amendment, no undue prejudice would result to defendants and the amendment deadline had not passed.
key_cases_cited: In re Engle Cases, 767 F.3d 1082 (11th Cir. 2014)
Foman v. Davis, 371 U.S. 178 (1962)
Pinnacle Advert. & Mktg. Grp., Inc. v. Pinnacle Advert. & Mktg. Grp., LLC, 7 F.4th 989 (11th Cir. 2021)
Zenith Radio Corp. v. Hazeltine Rsch., Inc., 401 U.S. 321 (1971)
Bryant v. Dupree, 252 F.3d 1161 (11th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Shawanda V. Collins v. Team Management LLC
accommodation_description: Motion for reconsideration of sanctions denial in underlying Fair Housing Act racial discrimination case.
key_holding: The court denied Collins's motion for reconsideration under Rule 60(b), finding she failed to establish exceptional circumstances warranting relief and merely rehashes her initial arguments.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Shawanda V. Collins, proceeding pro se, filed a motion for reconsideration under Rule 60(b) seeking to overturn the court's denial of her sanctions motions against defendants in her Fair Housing Act racial discrimination case. The court denied the motion, finding Collins failed to establish any grounds for extraordinary relief under Rule 60(b) and merely reasserted her prior arguments while accusing the court of racial bias without merit.
key_cases_cited: Peacock v. Bd. of Sch. Comm'rs, 721 F.2d 210 (7th Cir. 1983)
United States ex rel. Verdone v. Cir. Ct. for Taylor Cnty., 73 F.3d 669 (7th Cir. 1995)
Omegbu v. Milwaukee County, No. 05-C-596, 2008 WL 220342 (E.D. Wis. Jan. 25, 2008)

case_name: Kiontay Smith v. Kittle Property Group, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discriminatory eviction based on race and sex following termination from employment at the property.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for Kittle Property Group on plaintiff's FHA claims because plaintiff failed to establish discriminatory intent in her eviction—she could not identify similarly situated comparators outside her protected class who engaged in comparable conduct (threatening gun violence) yet received more favorable treatment, and plaintiff presented no evidence that the decisionmakers doubted the reports of her threats or acted with discriminatory motive.
brief_summary: Kiontay Smith, an African-American bisexual woman employed as a maintenance technician at Kittle Property Group, sued the company alleging Title VII race and sex discrimination and Fair Housing Act claims after she was terminated for allegedly making threats of gun violence and subsequently evicted from her employee housing within seven days. The court granted summary judgment for Kittle on all claims, finding Smith failed to establish a prima facie case of discrimination because she identified no similarly situated comparators who engaged in comparable conduct—threatening gun violence—yet received more favorable treatment, and presented no evidence that Kittle's stated reason for her termination and eviction was pretextual.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Ortiz v. Werner Enters., Inc., 834 F.3d 760 (7th Cir. 2016)
Bloch v. Frischolz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
McDaniel v. Progress Rail Locomotive, Inc., 940 F.3d 360 (7th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Naketia Johnson v. FirstKey Homes LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves alleged race-based refusal to rent and lease termination.
key_holding: Johnson adequately stated a claim under the FHA because she alleged the defendant terminated her lease four minutes after she took possession and informed her the cancellation was based on reasons related to her race.
brief_summary: Naketia Johnson sued FirstKeyHomes LLC alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by canceling her lease four minutes after she took possession, citing reasons related to her race. The court denied the motion to dismiss the FHA claim, finding Johnson adequately alleged race-based discrimination under § 3604(a), while dismissing her § 1983 claim for failure to state that defendant acted under color of law.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A.
Stumm v. Wilkie
Lindke v. Freed

case_name: Mari Rodriguez v. City of Chicago
accommodation_description: Rodriguez requested that the City preserve the driveway apron so she could continue using it as a safe pick-up location for medical transportation due to her mobility disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed Rodriguez's FHA reasonable modification claim because the driveway apron was not within the 'premises' of her dwelling unit—it was on an adjacent commercial property—and thus did not fall within the scope of § 3604(f)(3)(A) protection.
brief_summary: Mari Rodriguez, a tenant with mobility disabilities, sued the City of Chicago alleging that the removal of a driveway apron she used for safe medical transportation pickup violated the ADA and FHA. The court granted the City's motion to dismiss, holding that the driveway was not a City service subject to ADA Title II and was not part of Rodriguez's 'premises' under the FHA's reasonable modification provision.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Moore v. W. Ill. Corr. Ctr., 89 F.4th 582 (7th Cir. 2023)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Barden v. City of Sacramento, 292 F.3d 1073 (9th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Deborah Morris v. City of Baltimore
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, an elderly disabled resident of a private apartment building, sought building-wide accessibility improvements including functioning elevators and emergency chair lifts, allegedly required to make the building accessible housing.
key_holding: The court granted the City's motion to dismiss because the City of Baltimore is not a proper legal entity subject to suit, Plaintiff lacks Article III standing as any alleged harm is traceable to her private landlord rather than the City, and Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts showing the City took any discriminatory action against her based on disability.
brief_summary: Deborah Morris, a pro se plaintiff and disabled resident of a private apartment building in Baltimore, sued the City of Baltimore alleging violations of the ADA, Section 504, and the Fair Housing Act due to the building's inaccessible conditions. The court granted the City's motion to dismiss because Plaintiff sued the wrong defendant (the City rather than her private landlord), lacked Article III standing as her alleged harm was not traceable to the City, and failed to state plausible claims showing the City acted with discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Woods v. City of Greensboro, 855 F.3d 639 (4th Cir. 2017)

source_file: 10331659_2025_Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC_caed
case_name: Pharilyn Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an exception to the lease's no-pet policy to keep her emotional support pit bull terrier, Onyx, as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The court denied the insurance broker's motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff adequately alleged the broker had knowledge of her ESA request when it communicated breed restrictions to the landlord, and that factual disputes about discriminatory intent cannot be resolved at the motion to dismiss stage.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Pharilyn Chhang sued West Coast USA Properties LLC, its landlord Sergio Madrigal, and insurance broker Dibuduo & Defendis (D&D) alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by denying her reasonable accommodation request to keep an emotional support animal. The court denied D&D's motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff adequately alleged D&D knew of her ESA request and made discriminatory statements about breed restrictions that influenced the landlord's denial.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa
Morris v. W. Hayden Ests. First Addition Homeowners Ass'n
Nat'l Fair Hous. All. v. Travelers Indem. Co.

source_file: 10333364_2025_Ana Rodriguez v. Peppertree Place Apartment SPE LLC_cacd
case_name: Ana Rodriguez v. Peppertree Place Apartment SPE LLC
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's action for failure to prosecute and failure to comply with court orders under Rule 41(b), finding that plaintiff failed to meet and confer with defendant, file required reports, and comply with the court's orders despite warnings that failure would result in dismissal.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ana Rodriguez sued Peppertree Place Apartment SPE LLC alleging failure to provide reasonable accommodations under the FHA, along with ADA, retaliation, due process, constructive eviction, and health impact claims. The court dismissed the entire action for failure to prosecute and failure to comply with the court's orders, having twice failed to meet and confer with defendant and file required reports, despite explicit warnings that non-compliance would result in dismissal.

source_file: 10335458_2025_Jean Robert Saint-Jean v. Emigrant Mortg. Co., Inc._ca2
case_name: Jean Robert Saint-Jean et al. v. Emigrant Mortgage Company, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case involves discriminatory mortgage lending practices (reverse redlining) targeting Black and Latino borrowers rather than a reasonable accommodation claim
key_holding: The Court of Appeals affirmed the jury verdict for eight Black homeowners, finding their reverse redlining claims timely under equitable tolling, rejecting challenges to jury instructions on disparate impact and disparate treatment, and upholding the district court's ruling that a loan modification release provision was unenforceable.
brief_summary: Eight Black homeowners in New York City sued Emigrant Mortgage Company and affiliated entities alleging reverse redlining—targeted predatory mortgage lending that refinanced homes at high default interest rates to Black and Latino borrowers who had high equity but poor credit and low incomes. After a jury awarded compensatory damages of $722,044 and nominal damages, the Court of Appeals affirmed the judgment, rejecting the lender's challenges to equitable tolling, jury instructions on disparate impact and disparate treatment theories, and the enforceability of a release provision in a loan modification agreement.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Rotkiske v. Klemm, 589 U.S. 8 (2019)
Regional Economic Community Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10335987_2025_Watkins v. Housing Authority of the City of Hugo, The_oked
case_name: Watkins v. Housing Authority of the City of Hugo
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED - the opinion does not identify the specific accommodation request at issue in this procedural ruling
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motion to strike the individual defendants' amended motion for summary judgment and allowed the individual defendants to constructively amend their answer to assert qualified immunity, finding plaintiffs had adequate notice and suffered no prejudice.
brief_summary: Ronesha Watkins, on behalf of herself and minor children, sued the Housing Authority of the City of Hugo and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disability discrimination and sex discrimination. The court denied plaintiffs' motion to strike the individual defendants' amended motion for summary judgment, finding the court prefers resolution on the merits and plaintiffs had sufficient notice of the qualified immunity defense through the Joint Status Report filed in December 2023.
key_cases_cited: Ahmad v. Furlong, 435 F.3d 1196 (10th Cir. 2006)
Lee v. Max Intern., LLC, 638 F.3d 1318 (10th Cir. 2011)
Ysais v. New Mexico Jud. Standard Comm'n, 616 F. Supp. 2d 1176 (D.N.M. 2009)
Creative Consumer Concepts, Inc. v. Kreisler, 563 F.3d 1070 (10th Cir. 2009)
Stapp v. Curry Co. Bd. of Comm., 672 Fed. Appx. 841 (10th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 10338763_2025_Wiggins v. Omaha Housing Authority Foundation Inc._ned
case_name: Toni Wiggins v. Omaha Housing Authority Foundation Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Wiggins, as power of attorney for tenant Henry Lee, was assisting him in obtaining a reasonable accommodation for his unspecified disabilities, but the court found no causal connection between this activity and OHA's alleged adverse actions.
key_holding: The court dismissed Wiggins' FHA retaliation claim because she failed to allege sufficient facts showing she engaged in protected activity under the FHA or that there was a causal connection between any protected activity and the OHA's false police reports and eviction action against her.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Toni Wiggins sued the Omaha Housing Authority alleging it violated her constitutional rights under § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act by making false police reports and filing a false eviction action against her after she was acting as power of attorney for her father-in-law to assist him in obtaining disability accommodations. The court dismissed Wiggins' federal claims for failure to state plausible claims, finding she failed to adequately allege protected activity under the FHA or a causal connection between any such activity and the OHA's adverse actions, though it granted her 30 days to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Neudecker v. Boisclair Corp., 351 F.3d 361 (8th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 10338845_2025_Lee v. Omaha Housing Authority Foundation Inc._ned
case_name: Lee v. Omaha Housing Authority Foundation Inc.
accommodation_description: Hasani Lee, as power of attorney for his father Henry Lee, alleges he was attempting to obtain a reasonable accommodation for Henry's disabilities when the OHA had him arrested and removed from the property.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state plausible FHA claims because he made no allegations regarding his own protected characteristics and failed to allege facts showing a causal connection between any protected activity and the OHA's adverse actions, as his allegations about reasonable accommodation were vague and conclusory.
brief_summary: Hasani Lee, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued the Omaha Housing Authority alleging it violated the Fair Housing Act by making false police reports and filing a false eviction action against him after he was acting as power of attorney for his disabled father. The court dismissed Lee's FHA claims at initial review, finding he failed to plead facts establishing a retaliation claim because his allegations were vague, failed to specify what reasonable accommodation was being sought, and failed to allege a causal connection between any protected activity and the adverse actions.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Neudecker v. Boisclair Corp.
Gallagher v. Magner
United States v. Edmunds

source_file: 10347143_2025_Lopez v. The YWCA of North East New York_nynd
case_name: Lopez v. YWCA of Northeast New York
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested service dog(s), exemption from policies relying on police intervention, and permission to use cannabis for PTSD and chronic pain; contractor-only repairs instead of landlord visits.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff's disability-based FHA claims and reasonable accommodation claims under Title III of the ADA required a response, but dismissed racial and religious discrimination FHA claims with leave to amend for failure to allege facts showing plausible discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mariah Lopez, a disabled Afro-Latina transgender woman enrolled in Defendant YWCA's housing program Rosie’s House, brought claims against the YWCA, its executives, and property owners alleging disability, race, sex, and religious discrimination under the FHA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act, including reasonable accommodation requests for service dogs, exemption from police-involvement policies, and medical cannabis use. The court granted IFP status, dismissed racial and religious FHA claims with leave to amend, but allowed disability-based FHA claims, sex discrimination claims, hostile housing environment claims, and retaliation claims to proceed, requiring Defendants to respond.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Meyer v. Holley
Francis v. Kings Park Manor
Littlejohn v. City of New York

source_file: 10347542_2025_Gurevitch v. Emerald Green Property Owners Association Inc._nysd
case_name: Gurevitch v. Emerald Green Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves claims of religious discrimination through HOA enforcement actions rather than a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to state FHA discrimination or retaliation claims because they offered no factual allegations demonstrating that Defendants were motivated by discriminatory intent, including no comparator showing non-Jewish residents were treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Alexander and Irina Gurevitch, a Hasidic Jewish couple, sued the Emerald Green Property Owners Association and its attorney alleging FHA religious discrimination and retaliation claims based on HOA fines, stop-work orders, and foreclosure actions targeting them after they purchased property in the HOA community. The court granted Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs failed to allege sufficient facts to plausibly infer discriminatory intent, as they provided no comparator showing non-Jewish residents were treated differently.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Dickerson v. BPP PCV Owners LLC, 2022 WL 4538281 (S.D.N.Y. 2022)
Favourite v. 55 Halley St., Inc., 381 F. Supp. 3d 266 (S.D.N.Y. 2019)

case_name: Susan Lloyd v. Manbel Devco I LP et al.
accommodation_description: Tenant with heart, respiratory, and mobility disabilities requested accommodation from smoke-free and pesticide policies, modifications to front door keypad timing, and bathroom modifications due to hypersensitivity conditions
key_holding: The court found that while most of Lloyd's claims fail, she adequately pleaded that defendants failed to accommodate her disabilities related to the front door accessibility and hypersensitivity to smoke and pesticides under the FHA, and allowed the reasonable accommodation claim to proceed.
brief_summary: Susan Lloyd, a tenant with heart and respiratory problems who uses a mobility scooter, sued Manbel Devco III LP (owner of Eclipse at Belmont Apartments) and its employees alleging Fair Housing Act violations, negligence, breach of contract, and other state law claims stemming from alleged failures to accommodate her disabilities regarding door accessibility, smoke-free environment, and pesticide hypersensitivity. The court granted in part and denied in part multiple motions to dismiss, dismissing most claims with prejudice but allowing the FHA reasonable accommodation claim to proceed past the motion to dismiss stage.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
Olmstead v. L.C.
Lapid-Laurel v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment

source_file: 10350993_2025_Deitch v. Medstar Medical Group Southern Maryland LLC_mdd
case_name: Ronald S. Deitch v. Medstar Medical Group, Southern Maryland LLC
subsidy_program: St. Mary's County Department of Housing affordable housing program for disabled veterans
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Habitat's property managers made a false police report that led to his arrest and eviction from his apartment, claiming this was in retaliation for his advocacy on behalf of himself and neighbors, not because of his disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state an FHA discrimination claim because his own allegations negated any plausible inference that the defendants' adverse actions (false police report and eviction) were taken because of his disability, as he alleged the motive was retaliation for his advocacy unrelated to disability.
brief_summary: Ronald Deitch, a disabled tenant with multi system atrophy, sued Habitat America LLC (his landlord) and others alleging Fair Housing Act violations after Habitat's property managers allegedly made a false police report that led to his arrest and eviction. The court granted Habitat's motion to dismiss, finding Deitch failed to state an FHA claim because his own allegations showed the alleged retaliation was motivated by his advocacy for himself and neighbors, not by his disability, and the claim was also time-barred.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army of S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Lokhova v. Halper, 995 F.3d 134 (4th Cir. 2021)
Indep. Hous. Srvs. of S.F. v. Fillmore Ctr. Assocs., 840 F. Supp. 1328 (N.D. Cal. 1993)
Want v. Shindle Props., LLC, No. PWG-18-2833 (D. Md. Oct. 29, 2018)

case_name: Rickey Banks v. Ryan Ross et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged various disabilities (arthritis, wheelchair use, anxiety) and claimed he was denied housing services and access to a donated vehicle program at a transitional housing facility, but did not articulate a specific reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims were dismissed because he failed to identify which specific disability formed the basis of discrimination and failed to establish a nexus between his alleged handicaps and Defendants' actions.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rickey Banks, a disabled veteran residing in transitional housing operated by Open Door Ministries following his release from prison, brought claims against the facility's director, executive director, and fellow residents alleging discrimination on the basis of race and disability, ADA violations, and constitutional claims. The court granted Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff failed to state viable federal claims because his pleadings were conclusory, failed to establish the required nexus between his disabilities and Defendants' actions, and sought monetary damages under Title III of the ADA which only provides for injunctive relief.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
PGA Tour, Inc. v. Martin, 532 U.S. 661 (2001)

case_name: Lakeisha C. Green v. Allied First Bank SB
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; case involves claims that bank discriminated in mortgage servicing and foreclosure prevention based on protected classes including race, sex, and marital status.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie disparate treatment claim because she admitted Servbank offered her loss mitigation options, could not show similarly situated applicants were treated differently, and offered only conclusory allegations that the bank discriminated based on her protected characteristics without evidence that race, sex, or other traits were a significant factor in its decisions.
brief_summary: LaKeisha C. Green sued Allied First Bank SB alleging the bank discriminated against her in mortgage servicing and foreclosure proceedings based on her race, sex, marital status, and other protected characteristics under the FHA and ECOA. The court granted summary judgment for the bank, finding Green failed to establish a prima facie case because she admitted the bank offered her loss mitigation options, could not show similarly situated borrowers were treated differently, and provided only conclusory allegations of discrimination without evidence that her protected traits were a significant factor in the bank's decisions.

case_name: Art Reyes v. Harris County, Christopher Ross, Amanda Fry, Alexzander French Michael, and Joseph M. Douglass
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged deputies discriminated against him based on race by arresting and jailing him while his neighbors were not arrested or charged for similar conduct, and interfered with his right to use HOA common amenities.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim because he failed to allege that Harris County discriminated in the sale or rental of housing, real estate transactions, brokerage services, or otherwise coerced, intimidated, or interfered with his fair housing rights under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604, 3605, 3606, or 3617.
brief_summary: Art Reyes, proceeding pro se, sued Harris County and individual deputies under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act, alleging that deputies discriminated against him based on race by repeatedly detaining, arresting, and charging him while declining to arrest his white neighbors for similar conduct. The court granted Harris County's motion to dismiss, dismissing all claims because Plaintiff failed to adequately plead a Monell claim against the County and because his FHA claim fell outside the statute's scope as he alleged police misconduct rather than discrimination in housing transactions.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Connick v. Thompson, 563 U.S. 51 (2011)
Piotrowski v. City of Houston, 237 F.3d 567 (5th Cir. 2001)
Monell v. Dept. of Social Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

case_name: Art Reyes v. Harris County, Christopher Ross, Amanda Fry, Alexzander French Michael, and Joseph M. Douglass
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged HOA common amenity access denial based on race, which the court found did not constitute a cognizable FHA claim
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim because none of the cited FHA sections (§ 3604, 3605, 3606, 3617) apply—Plaintiff failed to allege discrimination in the sale or rental of housing, real estate transactions, brokerage services, or coercion/interference with rights secured by those sections.
brief_summary: Art Reyes, proceeding pro se, sued Harris County and several deputy sheriffs under § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act, alleging that law enforcement falsely arrested him multiple times while ignoring similar conduct by his neighbors, based on his race. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff's FHA claim failed because none of the cited statutory provisions applied to his allegations of unequal policing, and his § 1983 claims failed for lack of personal involvement, probable cause for arrests, and plausible comparators.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Thompson v. Clark

case_name: United States of America and Fair Housing Partnership of Greater Pittsburgh, Inc. et al. v. AION Management, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Fair housing testers requested assigned parking spaces near building entrances as reasonable accommodations for prospective tenants with mobility disabilities (using walkers and crutches).
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs sufficiently alleged all three elements of a § 3604(f)(3)(B) reasonable accommodation claim (refusal, reasonable accommodation, and necessity/equal opportunity) and that Defendants' statements about refusing to assign parking spaces violated § 3604(c).
brief_summary: The United States and fair housing organizations sued AION Management and affiliated property owners for denying assigned parking spaces to prospective tenants with mobility disabilities, alleging violations of §§ 3604(c), 3604(f)(1), 3604(f)(2), and 3604(f)(3)(B) of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs established Article III standing and sufficiently stated claims that Defendants refused to make reasonable accommodations in their parking policies and made discriminatory statements regarding housing availability.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Alliance for Hippocratic Med. v. FDA, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. HUD, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Jankowski Lee & Assocs. v. Cisneros, 91 F.3d 891 (7th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 10360316_2025_Ellis v. NAICA Housing Development Fund Company, Inc._nysd
case_name: Jaezon Ellis v. NAICA Housing Development Fund Company, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discrimination based on a staff member calling him a slur, but no disability-related accommodation request is made in this case.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because one incident of offensive name-calling is not sufficiently severe or pervasive to create a hostile housing environment, and plaintiff's other federal claims fail because NAICA is a private entity not acting under color of state law.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jaezon Ellis sued NAICA Housing Development Fund Company, a nonprofit homeless prevention organization, and two employees for negligence, property loss, and discrimination after being called a slur by a staff member at a transitional housing facility. The court dismissed all federal claims, finding NAICA is a private entity not subject to Section 1983 liability, the single incident of name-calling is insufficient to state a hostile housing environment claim under the Fair Housing Act, and plaintiff has no constitutional due process claim for the property loss.

case_name: Yolanda E. Quewon v. Philadelphia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for a handicap-accessible ramp outside her home as a reasonable accommodation for hip and knee pain.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because she failed to adequately plead the elements of a reasonable accommodation claim: she did not describe her disability, did not allege whether she requested the accommodation from the defendant, and did not explain how the ramp was necessary for her to use and enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Yolanda Quewon, a public housing tenant with hip and knee pain, sued the Philadelphia Housing Authority alleging harassment about rent payments and requesting a handicap-accessible ramp as a reasonable accommodation. The court dismissed her Amended Complaint without prejudice, finding she failed to state a plausible FHA claim because she did not adequately describe her disability, did not allege whether she requested the accommodation from defendants, and did not explain how the ramp was necessary for her to use and enjoy her dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2009)
T.W. v. School District of Philadelphia, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
In re Ghada F. v. City of Philadelphia, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)

case_name: Manson v. Fresno Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that defendants maintain a functioning elevator to provide equal access to their apartments, arguing that 21 months of elevator outages and entrapments constituted a constructive denial of reasonable accommodation for their mobility disabilities.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether the 21-month delay in repairing the malfunctioning elevator constituted a constructive denial of reasonable accommodation and whether defendant Cipro had a disability known to defendants.
brief_summary: Three mobility-disabled tenants at a supportive housing project for homeless and disabled veterans in Fresno sued their housing provider for failing to reasonably accommodate their disabilities by delaying over 20 months to repair a frequently malfunctioning elevator that trapped passengers, including plaintiff Manson. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether the delay constituted a constructive denial of reasonable accommodation and whether one plaintiff had a disability known to defendants.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Hume v. Guardian Mgmt. LLC, 2022 WL 17834397 (D. Or. Dec. 21, 2022)
Elliott, 2018 WL 2933467
Fraihat v. U.S. Immigr. & Customs Enf't, 16 F.4th 613 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Anthony Jackson v. S2 Residential
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case concerns alleged racial discrimination in leasing an apartment with pest control problems.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendant because plaintiff failed to present competent evidence of discriminatory intent or identify a specific policy, procedure, or practice with a discriminatory effect on a protected class; plaintiff's conclusory assertions that defendant leased him an infested apartment because of his race did not constitute competent summary judgment evidence.
brief_summary: Anthony Jackson, a Black pro se plaintiff, sued S2 Residential, the management company for his apartment complex, alleging that defendant racially discriminated against him by renting him an apartment knowing it had a pest control problem, causing him to contract an infectious disease. The court granted defendant's motion for summary judgment and denied plaintiff's cross-motion, dismissing all claims with prejudice because plaintiff failed to present competent evidence creating a genuine issue of material fact on his race-based housing discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)
Artisan/Am. Corp. v. City of Alvin, 588 F.3d 291 (5th Cir. 2009)
Crain v. City of Selma, 952 F.3d 634 (5th Cir. 2020)

case_name: McGrath v. Dunecrest Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Request for approval to construct a balcony extension with stairs to provide additional space and a secondary exit for adult autistic children
key_holding: The court granted the Association's motion for attorney's fees because the plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims were frivolous, unreasonable, or groundless from the outset—the balcony extension accommodation failed because autism was not the but-for cause of the need, the familial status claim failed because disabled adults do not constitute protected familial status, and the retaliation claim failed because the emails did not constitute coercive conduct.
brief_summary: The McGraths sued their condominium association seeking approval for a balcony extension with stairs as a reasonable accommodation for their adult autistic children, plus familial status discrimination and retaliation claims. The court granted summary judgment for the Association in May 2024 and, in this March 2025 ruling, awarded the Association $86,562.50 in attorney's fees after finding the plaintiffs' claims were frivolous and groundless from the outset because the disability accommodation failed the causation requirement, the familial status claim did not fit the FHA's definition, and the retaliation claim lacked evidentiary support.
key_cases_cited: Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Moore v. City of New York
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request; case involves alleged racial discrimination by property management who called police on plaintiff, leading to his arrest and involuntary psychiatric evaluations.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a racial discrimination claim under § 1981, Title VI, or the FHA because it did not plead facts showing discriminatory intent, only conclusory assertions about a 'medical industrial complex' exploiting Black men.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ali Moore sued Morningside Heights Housing Corporation and FirstService Residential (Property Defendants), alleging they called police on him because of his race, leading to his arrest and involuntary psychiatric evaluations on October 1 and 5, 2021. The court granted the Property Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent, containing only conclusory assertions about a 'medical industrial complex' without factual support linking the defendants' conduct to racial animus.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Henry v. Cnty. of Nassau, 6 F.4th 324 (2d Cir. 2021)
Dolan v. Connolly, 794 F.3d 290 (2d Cir. 2015)

source_file: 10366099_2025_Resources for Human Development, Inc. v. Lower Providence Township_paed
case_name: Resources for Human Development, Inc. v. Lower Providence Township
accommodation_description: RHD requested a zoning permit to use property at Eagleville Hospital as supportive housing for individuals with disabilities, which the Township denied after initially indicating the institutional zoning designation would permit it.
key_holding: The court dismissed RHD's FHA claim without prejudice because RHD failed to identify a specific discriminatory policy or practice necessary for a disparate impact claim, did not frame its claim within the disability discrimination framework under the FHAA, failed to exhaust administrative remedies before pursuing reasonable accommodation claims, and lacked allegations of discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Resources for Human Development, Inc. (RHD), a non-profit operator, sued Lower Providence Township after being denied a zoning permit to operate supportive housing for individuals with disabilities at Eagleville Hospital. The court dismissed RHD's Fair Housing Act claim for failure to state a claim because RHD did not identify a discriminatory policy causing disparate impact, did not plead within the FHAA's disability discrimination framework, and failed to exhaust administrative remedies for its reasonable accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dep't of Hous. and Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Jennifer Dixon v. Silva-Markham Partners and Koelbel & Co.
accommodation_description: Ms. Dixon alleges Defendants created a hostile housing environment and discriminated against her based on her autoimmune disorder affecting cognition and mobility; transfer request and live-in aide discussions were referenced but not substantively analyzed as accommodation requests.
key_holding: The court adopted the Magistrate Judge's Recommendation, granting summary judgment for Defendants on the FHA discrimination and hostile housing environment claims because Plaintiff failed to show she was treated differently from similarly situated tenants or that the conduct was sufficiently severe or pervasive, and on the retaliation claim because the undisputed facts did not show Defendants interfered with Plaintiff's accommodation requests.
brief_summary: Jennifer Dixon, who has an autoimmune disorder affecting her cognition and mobility, sued her former landlord Silva-Markham Partners and Koelbel & Co., alleging housing discrimination, hostile housing environment, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act. The court adopted the Magistrate Judge's Recommendation, granting Defendants' motion for summary judgment and denying Plaintiff's cross-motion, finding no evidence of differential treatment compared to similarly situated tenants, no pretext, and insufficient severity or pervasiveness of alleged conduct to support a hostile environment or retaliation claim.

case_name: Eldridge v. City of Alton, Illinois
accommodation_description: Request for disability accommodation related to municipal waste collection services, including that the City prohibit neighbor interference and provide accessible waste collection for persons with disabilities
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because their complaint failed to connect the alleged exclusion from trash collection services to disability discrimination, instead attributing service disruptions to a neighbor rather than the City and failing to specify what reasonable accommodation was sought.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Eldridge and Scharfinski, both individuals receiving disability benefits, sued the City of Alton alleging the City violated the FHA and ADA by failing to provide reasonable accommodations for waste collection services and by citing them for ordinance violations when a neighbor interfered with trash collection. The court dismissed the complaint under § 1915(e)(2) for failing to state a claim, finding the FHA claims were vague and did not connect the alleged service exclusion to disability-based discrimination, while all other federal claims were similarly deficient.
key_cases_cited: East-Miller v. Lake Cnty. Highway Dep't, 421 F.3d 558 (7th Cir. 2005)
Southend Neighborhood Improvement Ass'n v. St. Clair Cnty., 743 F.2d 1207 (7th Cir. 1984)
McDaniel v. Bd. of Educ. of City of Chi., 956 F. Supp. 2d 887 (N.D. Ill. 2013)

case_name: Robert Ricketts v. Westwood Condo Assoc.
accommodation_description: Repair of roof leaks and carbon monoxide hazards to allow Plaintiff and his children with asthma to occupy the condo safely
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim was properly dismissed because he failed to allege sufficient facts showing that his or his children's asthma constitutes a 'handicap' as defined under the FHA, which requires that the condition substantially limit a major life activity such as breathing.
brief_summary: Robert Ricketts, a Connecticut attorney and condo owner, sued Westwood Condominium Association and related parties alleging they violated the FHA by failing to repair roof leaks and carbon monoxide hazards after learning of his and his children's asthma. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Ricketts failed to state a plausible FHA claim because his allegations did not establish that his or his children's asthma substantially limits a major life activity as required to qualify as a handicap under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Gladstone Realtors v. Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Kropelnicki v. Siegel, 290 F.3d 118 (2d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Stern v. Highland Lake Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege HOA amendments (rental ban, religious structure restrictions, Sunday viewing restrictions) and individualized harassment by HOA members were motivated by religious animus toward Hasidic Jews.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs failed to establish that the alleged discriminatory conduct—HOA by-law amendments and individualized harassment—was sufficiently severe and pervasive to create a hostile housing environment under § 3604(b), and defendants articulated legitimate non-discriminatory reasons for their policies.
brief_summary: Hasidic Jewish homeowners sued their HOA and individual defendants alleging religious discrimination based on hostile by-law amendments (rental ban, religious structure restrictions, Sunday commercial restrictions) and individualized harassment intended to create a hostile housing environment. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiffs failed to demonstrate the conduct was sufficiently severe and pervasive under § 3604(b), and defendants proffered legitimate non-discriminatory reasons for their policies that plaintiffs did not rebut with sufficient evidence of pretext.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Mitchell v. Shane, 350 F.3d 39 (2d Cir. 2003)
People of State of N.Y. by Abrams v. Merlino, 694 F. Supp. 1101 (S.D.N.Y. 1988)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

source_file: 10373175_2025_CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor and City Council Of Baltimore_mdd
case_name: CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor & City Council of Baltimore
accommodation_description: CMDS sought a use-and-occupancy permit to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility, which the City denied based on zoning requirements that CMDS claims were discriminatorily applied.
key_holding: The Court held that punitive damages are unavailable against the City under the FHA and that CMDS cannot recover lost profits if it never had a legal right to open its facility, making the outcome of parallel state-court zoning litigation critical to determining actual damages.
brief_summary: CMDS Residential, LLC sued the City of Baltimore alleging the City discriminatorily denied a use-and-occupancy permit to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility in violation of the FHA and ADA. This Court ruling addressed a motion in limine on damages, holding that punitive damages are unavailable against the municipality under the FHA and that CMDS cannot recover lost profits unless it first establishes a legal entitlement to operate the facility in pending parallel state-court zoning litigation.
key_cases_cited: City of Newport v. Fact Concerts, Inc., 453 U.S. 247 (1981)
Barnes v. Gorman, 536 U.S. 181 (2002)
Vt. Agency of Nat. Res. v. United States ex rel. Stevens, 529 U.S. 765 (2000)
Lavay Corp. v. Dominion Fed. Sav. & Loan Ass'n, 830 F.2d 522 (4th Cir. 1987)
CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor & City Council of Balt., 714 F. Supp. 3d 583 (D. Md. 2024)

case_name: Sherri Strole v. Mark Wirth, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request alleged; case involves gender discrimination, retaliation, and interference claims under the FHA.
key_holding: At IFP screening, the court found Plaintiff's FHA gender discrimination, retaliation, and interference claims plausible on their face and allowed them to proceed, while dismissing the IIED claim without prejudice for failure to allege conduct sufficiently outrageous.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Sherri Strole sued Fairgrounds RV LLC, owner Mark Wirth, and Orchard Ranch Park LLC alleging gender discrimination, retaliation, and interference under the Fair Housing Act, plus claims for economic abuse and intentional infliction of emotional distress arising from her residency at an RV park. The Court granted IFP status, dismissed the IIED claim without prejudice for failure to state outrageous conduct, but found the FHA sex discrimination, retaliation, and interference claims plausible enough to survive screening.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 122 F.4th 1097 (9th Cir. 2024)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Robert A. Johnson v. Raven Wood Homeowners Association, et al.
accommodation_description: Request that the HOA provide virtual/remote attendance option via Zoom at annual meetings as an accommodation for disability-related health risks during the COVID-19 pandemic
key_holding: Genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether Mr. Johnson is disabled within the FHA's definition, whether the accommodation was necessary and reasonable, and whether the HOA denied his request by failing to provide virtual meeting access, precluding summary judgment.
brief_summary: Disabled combat veteran Robert Johnson sued Raven Wood Homeowners Association alleging failure to provide reasonable accommodation under the FHA by denying his request to attend annual HOA meetings virtually via Zoom due to his health conditions. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact on all elements of the reasonable accommodation claim including disability status, necessity of the accommodation, reasonableness, and whether the HOA's actions constituted a denial.
key_cases_cited: DuBois v. Assoc. of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Reed v. Goertz, 598 U.S. 230 (2023)
Neithamer v. Brenneman Property Services, Inc., 81 F. Supp. 2d 1 (D.D.C. 1999)

source_file: 10373706_2025_Sara Watts v. Joggers Run Property Owners Association, Inc._ca11
case_name: Sara Watts v. Joggers Run Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: HOA's alleged discriminatory enforcement of rules targeting Watts and her family based on race, including forcing her to give up her service dog after a neighbor's complaint.
key_holding: Section 3604(b) applies to post-acquisition HOA conduct affecting housing-related contractual rights under HOA rules, and Watts plausibly stated claims for racial discrimination under the FHA and Civil Rights Act by alleging the HOA selectively enforced rules against her and denied access to community amenities because of her race.
brief_summary: Sara Watts, an African American woman, sued her HOA alleging racial discrimination in post-acquisition enforcement actions including towing her son's car without proper notice, restricting basketball court access citing concerns about people of color, and subjecting her guests to false accusations. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Section 3604(b) covers HOA conduct affecting housing-related contractual rights under HOA rules, and Watts sufficiently alleged plausible claims for racial discrimination under the FHA and Civil Rights Act.
key_cases_cited: Ga. State Conf. of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Curto v. A Country Place Condo. Ass'n, 921 F.3d 405 (3d Cir. 2019)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024)

case_name: Frazier v. Luther
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation requested; case centers on racial discrimination claim involving alleged statement 'Ain't no black man going to owe me money' and subsequent eviction
key_holding: The court found plaintiff's race-based disparate treatment claims under § 3604(a)-(c) plausible under the McDonnell Douglas framework based on his membership in a protected class, qualification to rent, eviction as comparable rejection, and inference that housing remained available to others, while dismissing the § 3617 retaliation claim for failure to plead protected activity and the trespass claim because defendant had legal right to enter under the lease.
brief_summary: Lance Frazier, a Black man, sued landlord Justin Luther and Luther Properties LLC for Fair Housing Act violations based on race discrimination, claiming he was evicted after Luther stated 'Ain't no black man going to owe me money' and changed his locks; Frazier also asserted state law claims for trespass, conversion, and breach of contract against the Kennedys. The district court partially granted the motion to dismiss, allowing the race-based discrimination claims under § 3604(a)-(c) and conversion claim to proceed while dismissing the retaliation claim under § 3617 and trespass claim, finding no protected activity was pled and the landlord had contractual right to enter the premises.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Meredith Twyman v. Recap PA Holdings, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a designated handicapped parking space closer to her apartment unit due to arthritis-related mobility limitations.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to plead sufficient facts showing (1) when, how, and to whom she made the accommodation request, and (2) why the handicapped parking space was necessary to afford her an equal opportunity to enjoy her dwelling given her arthritis.
brief_summary: An African American woman with arthritis sued her property manager under the Fair Housing Act alleging race and disability discrimination, including a claim that she requested a handicapped parking space as a reasonable accommodation. The court granted the defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts to support a reasonable accommodation claim because she did not adequately explain when her request was made or why the parking space was necessary given her disability.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Coyne v. Holy Fam. Apartments, No. 19-4583, 2020 WL 2063475 (E.D. Pa. Apr. 29, 2020)
Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Darryl West v. Marc Landau and Drinda Osborne
accommodation_description: West did not request a specific accommodation; he alleged disability discrimination in eviction proceedings based on retaliation for cooperating with law enforcement.
key_holding: The FHA claim was properly dismissed because West failed to allege that his disability was the basis for the alleged eviction—his claim was based on alleged retaliation for reporting criminal conduct to police and cooperating with law enforcement, not disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Darryl West, proceeding pro se, sued his landlord Drinda Osborne and her attorney Marc Landau alleging Fair Housing Act, ADA, and state law violations arising from an alleged lifetime housing agreement and attempted eviction. The court summarily dismissed the complaint, finding that West failed to state a plausible FHA claim because his eviction challenge was based on alleged retaliation for cooperating with law enforcement, not on disability discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Aquino v. Skyview Management Corp.
accommodation_description: Disabled plaintiffs seek emergency reinstatement of tenancy at 5900 Arlington Ave, Apt. 12H, Bronx, NY after allegedly being forced out following denial of ADA/FHA accommodation requests during 50 years of tenancy.
key_holding: The Rule 60(b) motion to vacate the prior judgment was denied as untimely under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 60(c)(1), without reaching the merits of the underlying FHA or ADA claims.
brief_summary: Pro se disabled plaintiffs Darren Dione Aquino, Sihar Aquino, and Aza Derman filed an Emergency Rule 60(b) motion seeking to vacate a prior judgment and reinstate their tenancy at Apartment 12H in the Bronx, alleging fraud upon the court, ADA violations, and Fair Housing Act violations stemming from concealed property ownership and improper disclosure of sealed medical records. The court denied the motion as untimely under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 60(c)(1), without addressing the substantive accommodation or discrimination claims.

source_file: 10384281_2025_The Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa_ca9
case_name: The Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa
accommodation_description: Ohio House requested a waiver of the City's 650-foot separation requirement between sober living homes as a reasonable accommodation for disabled persons recovering from addiction.
key_holding: The court affirmed that Ohio House's requested accommodation—granting an exception to the separation requirement—was unreasonable because a jury could find it would cause a fundamental alteration of the City's zoning scheme designed to prevent overconcentration of sober living facilities and institutionalization of disabled persons.
brief_summary: The Ohio House, LLC, operator of a sober living facility in Costa Mesa, California, appealed the district court's partial summary judgment and post-verdict rulings in its action challenging the City's zoning ordinances requiring conditional-use permits and separation requirements for group homes serving disabled persons recovering from addiction. The Ninth Circuit affirmed, holding that the differential treatment under the zoning code benefits the protected class of disabled persons, that Ohio House failed to prove disparate impact, and that Ohio House's requested accommodation (waiver of the separation requirement) was unreasonable because it would fundamentally alter the City's zoning scheme.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Community House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 10384752_2025_Crossett v. Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints, The_utd
10384826_2025_Nash v. A B C Insurance Inc_lawd
case_name: Michaela Nash v. A B C Insurance, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Nash requested transfer to another accessible apartment after rat infestation, but was denied; her original petition included Fair Housing Act discrimination claims which she voluntarily dismissed prior to this ruling.
key_holding: Following Royal Canin U.S.A., Inc. v. Wullschleger, the court lacks federal question jurisdiction because Nash voluntarily dismissed her Fair Housing Act claims in her Second Supplemental Petition, and the remaining state law tort claims do not present a substantial federal question under Grable; therefore, the court recommends granting the motion to remand.
brief_summary: Michaela Nash, a wheelchair-bound amputee residing at Cooper Road Plaza low-income housing complex, filed suit alleging rat infestation caused severe injuries including toe amputations, asserting Fair Housing Act discrimination claims along with state law tort claims. The court recommends granting Nash's motion to remand because she voluntarily dismissed her FHA claims in her Second Supplemental Petition, eliminating federal question jurisdiction under Royal Canin, and the remaining state law tort claims do not present a substantial federal question.
key_cases_cited: Royal Canin U.S.A., Inc. v. Wullschleger, 604 U.S. 22 (2025)
Gunn v. Minton, 568 U.S. 251 (2013)
Caterpillar Inc. v. Williams, 482 U.S. 386 (1987)
Franchise Tax Bd. of State of Cal. v. Constr. Laborers Vacation Trust for S. Cal., 463 U.S. 1 (1983)
Christianson v. Colt Industries Operating Corp., 486 U.S. 800 (1988)

case_name: Justin Baker v. Avenue5 Residential et al.
accommodation_description: Requests for pest vent grill modifications to prevent birds from entering the apartment, silencing of a storage room, and transfer to a quieter unit as accommodations for disabilities including depression, autism, bipolar disorder, and PTSD.
key_holding: The court allowed Baker's reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) to proceed, finding he adequately alleged that Avenue5 denied his accommodation requests in 2024-2025 for pest control modifications, storage room noise, and transfer to a quieter unit, while dismissing his reasonable modification claim under § 3604(f)(3)(A) because Avenue5 allowed him to pursue modifications.
brief_summary: Justin Baker, a disabled tenant receiving rental vouchers in Seattle, sued property manager Avenue5 Residential and others, alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to accommodate his disabilities. The court dismissed most claims under the two-dismissal rule and for failure to state a claim but allowed his reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) to proceed based on allegations that Avenue5 denied his requests for pest control modifications, noise reduction, and transfer to a quieter unit.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Rose Ct., LLC v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc., 119 F.4th 679 (9th Cir. 2024)
Slaughter v. Valley View I LLP, 2023 WL 6461058 (W.D. Wash. Oct. 4, 2023)

case_name: Meredith Twyman v. Recap PA Holdings, LLC, 963 Street Road LLC
accommodation_description: Tenant requested a private handicapped parking space with a blue ADA-compliant sign; landlord provided a reserved parking spot for her exclusive use but did not install a blue handicapped placard.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claims with prejudice because plaintiff received a private parking spot designated exclusively for her use—which is a better accommodation than a general handicapped spot—and plaintiff failed to allege how her scoliosis and anxiety necessitate brighter parking lot lighting, as brighter lighting applies to everyone regardless of disability.
brief_summary: Meredith Twyman, an African American woman with disabilities including scoliosis, chronic disc pain, and anxiety disorder, sued her property manager and landlord alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race and disability discrimination and failure to accommodate her requests for a specially marked parking space and improved parking lot lighting. The court dismissed all federal claims with prejudice after four attempts, finding plaintiff failed to plead facts showing the requested accommodations were necessary to afford her equal housing opportunity and that plaintiff received an adequate parking accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Lapid-Laurel v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Coyne v. Holy Family Apartments, No. 19-4583 (E.D. Pa. Apr. 29, 2020)

source_file: 10397848_2025_(PS) Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento_caed
case_name: Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento
accommodation_description: Request for expedited processing of Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application as a reasonable accommodation for cancer chemotherapy side effects
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint for failure to state a claim because it contained only conclusory allegations without specific facts showing defendants knew of plaintiff's disability, that discriminatory intent motivated the delays, or that a reasonable accommodation was requested and denied.
brief_summary: Mekal Faruki, an elderly individual with cancer and multiple myeloma, sued the Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento alleging the Authority denied or delayed processing his Section 8 voucher over 90 instances and failed to accommodate his disability by refusing expedited processing. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the allegations too vague and conclusory to survive screening, but granted plaintiff leave to amend; the court separately granted plaintiff's request to file electronically as a reasonable accommodation for his multiple myeloma.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10406261_2025_Raymond May, Jr. v. Martin Fein Interest Ltd._ca4
case_name: Raymond Earl May, Jr.; Angela Dolores May v. Martin Fein Interest Ltd.
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The Fourth Circuit affirmed the district court's summary judgment in favor of the defendant, finding no reversible error in the FHA violation allegations.
brief_summary: Raymond Earl May, Jr. and Angela Dolores May appealed the Eastern District of North Carolina's grant of summary judgment to their landlord, Martin Fein Interest Ltd., in their Fair Housing Act case. The Fourth Circuit affirmed the district court's decision, finding no reversible error in its rejection of the plaintiffs' FHA claims.

case_name: Yolanda E. Quewon v. Philadelphia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly suffers from hip and knee pain from walking up stairs and cannot afford assistance, but no specific accommodation was formally requested or described in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because she failed to allege any disability, any request for accommodation, or any connection between her hip and knee pain and the landlord's demands for rent.
brief_summary: Yolanda Quewon, a tenant of the Philadelphia Housing Authority, sued PHA and an individual for breach of contract, due process violations, and Fair Housing Act discrimination, alleging she was harassed to pay rent and utilities despite a lease that did not require such payments. The court dismissed her case with prejudice because her Second Amended Complaint failed to allege sufficient facts to state a viable federal claim, including failing to allege disability, any accommodation request, or a nexus between her conditions and the alleged discriminatory conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10433979_2025_Earle v. Manchester Village Owners Association Inc_wied
case_name: Dawn N. Earle v. Manchester Village Owners Association Inc, et al.
accommodation_description: Request for parking accommodation related to disability in connection with condominium housing
key_holding: The filing of an amended complaint moots any pending motion to dismiss the original complaint, so defendants' motion to dismiss was denied as moot without reaching the merits of the ADA or FHA claims.
brief_summary: Dawn Earle sued Manchester Village Owners Association Inc. and individual defendants alleging disability discrimination, failure to accommodate her disabilities in housing and parking, and retaliation in violation of the ADA and FHA. Defendants moved to dismiss for failure to state a claim. The court denied the motion as moot because Earle filed an amended complaint, which superseded the original complaint and rendered the pending motion to dismiss moot, without reaching the substantive merits of either claim.
key_cases_cited: Runnion ex rel. Runnion v. Girl Scouts of Greater Chicago & Nw. Indiana, 786 F.3d 510 (7th Cir. 2015)
Massey v. Helman, 196 F.3d 727 (7th Cir. 1999)
Aqua Finance, Inc. v. Harvest King, Inc., No. 07-C-15, 2007 WL 5404939 (W.D. Wis. Mar. 12, 2007)

case_name: Jacqueline Buckholz v. Kelso Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for informal hearing to appeal housing voucher termination, or extension of tenancy, as reasonable accommodation for mental health disabilities affecting ability to meet appeal deadlines
key_holding: Plaintiff demonstrated serious questions going to the merits of her reasonable accommodation claim, irreparable harm from imminent eviction and homelessness, and the balance of hardships and public interest favored granting the preliminary injunction to reinstate her housing voucher.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jacqueline Buckholz, an individual with mental health disabilities receiving Section 8 housing assistance, sued Kelso Housing Authority alleging the termination of her housing voucher violated the Fair Housing Act's reasonable accommodation requirement because her disability affected her ability to meet the deadline for requesting an informal hearing.
