The court granted Plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction, finding she showed serious questions going to the merits, faced irreparable harm from imminent eviction, and the balance of equities and public interest favored enjoining the defendant from refusing to make housing assistance payments while the case proceeds.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council, 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976)
Nozzi v. Hous. Auth. of City of Los Angeles, 806 F.3d 1178 (9th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Stansly T. Maponga v. Hall Park Hotel, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged the hotel removed him before the end of his extended reservation period in violation of the ADA and FHA, without specifying a particular accommodation request.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because the complaint failed to allege any facts showing discriminatory conduct, merely stating conclusorily that defendants' actions interfered with his ability to secure fair housing.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Stansly Maponga sued Hall Park Hotel and its owners alleging ADA and FHA violations after being removed from an extended hotel reservation before its end date. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the FHA allegations entirely conclusory with no factual allegations identifying discriminatory conduct or connecting any harm to a protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing & Urban Development
accommodation_description: Tenants at a deteriorating Section 8 project-based rental assistance property sought Tenant Protection Vouchers that would allow them to relocate to market-rate housing while continuing to receive HUD rental assistance.
key_holding: Although the D.C. Circuit reversed the district court's mootness holding, it affirmed dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6) because plaintiffs failed to state a plausible claim for voucher relief: the statutory condition requiring 'the owner' to have received a Notice of Default was not satisfied where the current owner Winnie Street had not received such notice.
brief_summary: Sandpiper Cove tenants and the resident association sued HUD seeking to compel issuance of Tenant Protection Vouchers to relocate from uninhabitable conditions at their Section 8 project-based rental assistance property in Galveston, Texas. The D.C. Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, holding that despite errantly characterizing the issue as moot, the complaint failed to state a viable claim because the current property owner had not received a Notice of Default as required by the Consolidated Appropriations Act for HUD to issue vouchers.
key_cases_cited: Chafin v. Chafin, 568 U.S. 165 (2013)
Marino v. NOAA, 33 F.4th 593 (D.C. Cir. 2022)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better Env't, 523 U.S. 83 (1998)

case_name: Katherine Trujillo v. Amity Plaza, LLC, Housing Authority of the City of Littleton, d/b/a South Metro Housing Options, and Frank Martinez
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; claims involve alleged sexual assault/harassment by maintenance employee and individual liability under the FHA for discriminatory housing practices.
key_holding: The court properly granted sua sponte summary judgment in favor of Frank Martinez because he is not a 'housing provider' under the FHA, had no authority over rental decisions as a maintenance employee, and no evidence established any quid pro quo relationship or discriminatory housing practice connected to his role.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Katherine Trujillo sued Amity Plaza LLC, the Housing Authority of the City of Littleton, and maintenance employee Frank Martinez for Fair Housing Act violations under §§ 3604(b) and 3617, alleging discriminatory conduct and seeking individual liability against the employee. The court denied Trujillo's motion to amend judgment and confirmed sua sponte summary judgment in favor of Martinez, finding that as a maintenance employee with no authority over housing decisions, he could not be held individually liable as a 'housing provider' under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Fielder v. Sterling Park Homeowners Ass'n, 914 F. Supp. 2d 1222 (W.D. Wash. 2012)
Lacour v. A&M Prop. Inv., LLC, No. CIV-23-370-J, 2024 WL 2988961 (W.D. Okla. May 28, 2024)
Metro. Fair Housing Council of Okla. v. Pelfrey, 292 F. Supp. 3d 1250 (W.D. Okla. 2017)

case_name: Michael-Zahir David Fleming v. Westwood Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges a new parking policy requires residents to park miles from the premises, creating severe hardship without alleging disability-related accommodation needs.
key_holding: Fleming's Fair Housing Act claim fails because he did not allege membership in a protected class (race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin) as required to state a discrimination claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604.
brief_summary: Fleming, proceeding pro se, sued Westwood Apartments and its property manager alleging constitutional violations, a § 1982 claim, and an FHA claim based on a new parking policy requiring residents to park far from the complex. The court granted Westwood's motion to dismiss, finding Fleming failed to state a federal claim because he did not allege state action for constitutional claims and did not plead protected class membership for the § 1982 and FHA claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lugar v. Edmondson Oil Co., 457 U.S. 922 (1982)
Jones v. Alfred H. Mayer Co., 392 U.S. 409 (1968)
Brentwood Academy v. Tenn. Secondary Sch. Athletic Ass'n, 531 U.S. 288 (2001)
White v. City of Annapolis ex rel. City Council, 439 F. Supp. 3d 522 (D. Md. 2020)

case_name: Tyeashia M. Blackburn; Lezlie E. Freeman v. Kelly Webb; Rent King, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs raised ADA accommodations argument on appeal regarding their capacity to meet procedural requirements, but did not raise this before the district court.
key_holding: The district court did not abuse its discretion in dismissing the case with prejudice as a sanction for plaintiffs' repeated discovery violations and procedural noncompliance, and plaintiffs forfeited their ADA-accommodation argument by raising it for the first time on appeal.
brief_summary: Blackburn and Freeman sued Kelly Webb and Rent King, LLC in the Western District of Oklahoma alleging racial discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, but the district court dismissed the case with prejudice as a sanction for plaintiffs' repeated discovery violations and procedural noncompliance. The Tenth Circuit affirmed, holding the district court properly applied the Ehrenhaus factors and did not abuse its discretion, and further held that plaintiffs forfeited their ADA-accommodation argument by raising it for the first time on appeal.
key_cases_cited: Ehrenhaus v. Reynolds, 965 F.2d 916 (10th Cir. 1992)
Richison v. Ernest Grp., Inc., 634 F.3d 1123 (10th Cir. 2011)
Garrett v. Selby Connor Maddux & Janer, 425 F.3d 836 (10th Cir. 2005)
Clark v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 590 F.3d 1134 (10th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Dawn M. Chappel v. Adams County Children's Services, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested injunctive relief preventing enforcement of a state court eviction order and allowing her to return to property at 211 Sunshine Ave., Winchester, Ohio, as relief for alleged FHA retaliation.
key_holding: The court adopted the Magistrate Judge's recommendation to deny preliminary injunction on the FHA retaliation claim because plaintiff failed to establish a substantial likelihood of success on the merits, presented no evidence of discriminatory animus beyond speculation, and the requested relief would not preserve the status quo.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Dawn Chappel, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued Adams County Children's Services, a police chief, and a housing authority official alleging FHA retaliation under § 3617, seeking preliminary injunctions to prevent enforcement of a state court eviction order and to obtain housing-related relief. The court adopted both Magistrate Judge R&Rs, denying the preliminary injunction motions because plaintiff failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on her retaliation claim, offered only speculation of discriminatory animus related to her status as a recovering drug addict, and sought relief inconsistent with the status quo.
key_cases_cited: Liberty Coins, LLC v. Goodman, 748 F.3d 682 (6th Cir. 2014)
Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condos., 833 F. App'x 623 (6th Cir. 2020)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indust. Corp., 544 U.S. 280 (2005)
Lawrence v. Welch, 531 F.3d 364 (6th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Tony Cole v. Joseph Faulkner, et al.
accommodation_description: Cole alleged retaliation under the Fair Housing Act for asserting his legal rights related to the property at issue in eviction proceedings.
key_holding: Cole's FHA retaliation claim fails because he failed to allege what specific right under §§ 3603, 3604, 3605, or 3606 he was exercising that triggered the anti-retaliation provision of § 3617, merely alleging he was asserting 'legal rights related to the property' generally.
brief_summary: Tony Cole sued Joseph Faulkner and others alleging retaliatory eviction under the Fair Housing Act and constitutional claims after being evicted from a building in Chicago through state court proceedings. The court granted reconsideration on jurisdictional grounds but ultimately maintained dismissal of Cole's claims, finding his FHA retaliation claim failed because he did not adequately allege the specific right he was exercising under the Act, and his § 1983 claim failed because private defendants did not act under color of state law.
key_cases_cited: Gilbank v. Wood Cnty. Dep't of Hum. Servs., 111 F.4th 754 (7th Cir. 2024)
Johnson v. Pushpin Holdings, LLC, 748 F.3d 769 (7th Cir. 2014)
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Industries Corp., 544 U.S. 280 (2005)
Wang v. City of Indianapolis, No. 24-2664, 2025 WL 1000158 (7th Cir. Apr. 3, 2025)
Hu v. Huey, 325 F. App'x 436 (7th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 10583019_2025_Dogbe v. The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC_nvd
case_name: John Dogbe and Alberta Dogbe v. The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case concerns alleged racial discrimination in eviction and enforcement of clubhouse policies
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiffs' racial discrimination claims under § 1981, § 1982, § 3604(b) disparate treatment, and § 3617 because plaintiffs sufficiently alleged that but-for their race, the party would not have been shut down and they would not have received an eviction notice.
brief_summary: African American tenants John and Alberta Dogbe sued their landlord The Lakes at Lemmon Valley, LLC alleging racial discrimination after their clubhouse party was shut down and they received an eviction notice while similarly-situated white residents had not faced such actions. The court granted in part and denied in part defendant's motion to dismiss, dismissing disparate impact claims but allowing disparate treatment claims under § 3604(b) and § 3617 to proceed because plaintiffs sufficiently alleged race was a but-for cause of the eviction notice.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of African Am.-Owned Media, 589 U.S. 327 (2020)

case_name: Pamela Shomo & DeJa Shomo v. Karen Cooksey, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support animal (dog named King) in the apartment as a reasonable accommodation, which was allegedly not considered before eviction.
key_holding: The FHA failure to accommodate claim was dismissed because the amended complaint failed to allege any qualifying disability, any request for reasonable accommodation, how the accommodation was reasonable and necessary, or that defendants refused any such request.
brief_summary: Pamela and DeJa Shomo sued Columbia Glade Apartments and its manager Karen Cooksey alleging that their eviction violated the Fair Housing Act on both racial discrimination and failure to accommodate an emotional support animal grounds. The court granted Cooksey's motion to dismiss, finding the amended complaint failed to state plausible FHA claims because it did not allege a qualifying disability, did not allege a request for accommodation was made, and did not allege defendants refused such a request.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, Maryland
Greengael, LLC v. Bd. of Supervisors

source_file: 10587034_2025_APPELGREN v. ISLAND HOSPITALITY MANAGEMENT VII, LLC_njd
case_name: Mark Appelgren v. Island Hospitality Management VII, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought a quiet, stress-free environment and room modifications during a hotel stay to manage his anxiety disorder, IBS, and other disabilities, but he did not inform hotel staff of his disabilities.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA and ADA reasonable accommodation claims fail because he never informed hotel staff of his disabilities, so Defendants could not have known why he made any requests and cannot be liable for refusing accommodations they were unaware were sought.
brief_summary: Mark Appelgren, a federal agent with severe anxiety, depression, and IBS, sued Island Hospitality Management and Hilton alleging discrimination during an attempted hotel stay where he experienced delays, hostile interactions, and a chaotic lobby environment that triggered his anxiety and IBS symptoms. The Court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that because Plaintiff never informed hotel staff of his disabilities, Defendants could not be held liable for failing to accommodate those disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Yates Real Est., Inc. v. Plainfield Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 404 F. Supp. 3d 889 (D.N.J. 2019)

case_name: Patsy Jay v. Grand Management Services, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made a verbal request to keep her mobility walker on her porch as a disability accommodation, which defendants did not respond to, and later cited the walker as a lease violation in the eviction notice issued after plaintiff reported McKnight's sexual assault.
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on plaintiff's hostile living environment and retaliation claims because genuine issues of material fact existed regarding whether defendants' failure to evict McKnight and issuance of an eviction notice to plaintiff constituted disability discrimination and unlawful retaliation under § 3617.
brief_summary: Patsy Jay, a mobility-disabled elderly tenant, sued Grand Management Services and related defendants alleging they failed to protect her from a neighbor who sexually assaulted and stalked her, issued a retaliatory eviction notice after she reported the assault, and discriminated against her based on disability by failing to accommodate her need to keep a walker on her porch. The court denied summary judgment on plaintiff's hostile living environment and retaliation claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding defendants' knowledge of the neighbor's dangerous behavior toward women and whether the eviction notice was retaliation for plaintiff's protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ellison v. Brady, 924 F.2d 872 (9th Cir. 1991)
San Pedro Hotel Co. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Miller v. Tabor W. Inv. Co., 223 Or. App. 700 (2008)
McAlpine v. Multnomah County, 166 Or. App. 472 (2000)

case_name: Robert L. Hatcher v. Sylvain Yang and Travis Smith
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to continue parking his vehicle in the back yard of the duplex property due to his disability-related limitations.
key_holding: The court denied Defendant Yang's motion to dismiss because he failed to demonstrate that dismissal was proper, as he submitted unsworn statements outside the pleadings and cited no legal authority addressing the FHA.
brief_summary: Robert L. Hatcher, a 74-year-old disabled African American man, sued his private landlord Sylvain Yang alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, including that Yang refused to make reasonable accommodations for his disability (allowing him to park in the back yard and be excused from lease maintenance obligations) and discriminated against him based on disability and race by showing favorable treatment to a new tenant. The court denied Yang's motion to dismiss because he failed to meet his burden, submitting only unsubstantiated facts outside the pleadings without supporting legal authority.
key_cases_cited: North Mill Street, LLC v. City of Aspen, 6 F.4th 1216 (10th Cir. 2021)
Cuervo v. Sorenson, 112 F.4th 1307 (10th Cir. 2024)

case_name: Deepa Patel et al. v. City of Stanton et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are motel business operators challenging revocation of their motel permit and business license by the City of Stanton as violating procedural due process; the case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' procedural due process claim with prejudice because plaintiffs lacked standing to challenge the permit revocation, received constitutionally adequate notice and hearing for the business license revocation, failed to adequately plead a Haas claim regarding hearing officer bias, and had no protected property interest in their business license under the Stanton Municipal Code's discretionary revocation provisions.
brief_summary: Motel operators Ahir and Megha Hospitality LLC sued the City of Stanton alleging violations of their Fourteenth Amendment procedural due process rights when the City revoked their motel permit and business license without adequate notice. The court granted the City's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs lacked standing to challenge the permit revocation, received constitutionally adequate process for the business license revocation, failed to state a Haas claim for hearing officer bias, and had no protected property interest in their business license.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife
Mullane v. Cent. Hanover Bank & Tr. Co.
Town of Castle Rock v. Gonzales

source_file: 10592491_2025_Turner v. Autumn Lake Property Owners Association, Inc._txsd
case_name: Dennis Turner and Darlene Brooks v. Autumn Lake Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case concerns HOA fee disputes, not disability accommodations; plaintiffs improperly invoked FHA for fee challenge
key_holding: Plaintiffs' federal claims citing the Fair Housing Act are wholly insubstantial and frivolous because their actual allegations concern homeowners' association fees, which have no connection to the FHA's provisions governing housing discrimination.
brief_summary: Two homeowners sued their property owners association over alleged overcharging of HOA fees for over a decade, invoking the Fair Housing Act as the sole basis for federal jurisdiction. The court recommends granting the motion to dismiss because plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims are wholly insubstantial and frivolous — their fee dispute has no connection to the FHA's provisions governing housing discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Kokkonen v. Guardian Life Ins. Co. of Am., 511 U.S. 375 (1994)
Bell v. Hood, 37 U.S. 678 (1946)
Southpark Square Ltd. v. City of Jackson, Miss., 565 F.2d 338 (5th Cir. 1977)
Badgerow v. Walters, 596 U.S. 1 (2022)
Life Partners, Inc. v. United States, 650 F.3d 1026 (5th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Mirlande Dina Michel-Wiggins v. Jes Holdings, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested two apartments under a single housing voucher to accommodate a live-in aide arrangement, because she does not wish to share living space with an aide.
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that requiring two apartments under a single housing voucher imposed an undue financial burden and did not constitute a reasonable accommodation under the FHA.
brief_summary: Mirlande Michel-Wiggins, a disabled individual using a Section 8 housing voucher, sued Jes Holdings LLC and Fairway Management alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to rent her two apartments under a single voucher to accommodate a live-in aide, and by declining to provide trash pickup and mail delivery as disability accommodations. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice, holding that requiring two apartments under one housing voucher constituted an undue financial burden and was not a reasonable accommodation, and that the ancillary service requests failed because the primary accommodation claim failed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Klossner v. IADU Table Mound MHP, LLC, 65 F.4th 349 (8th Cir. 2023)
One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka, 93 F.4th 424 (8th Cir. 2024)
Developmental Services of NE v. City of Lincoln, 504 F. Supp. 2d 714 (D. Neb. 2007)

case_name: Adrian Oree v. Parkchester Condominium Complex, PPC Residential, LLC, Parkchester Preservation Management LLC, Parkchester Preservation Company, LP
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The court issued an order directing service of process on defendants and referring plaintiff to the Pro Se Clinic, noting the complaint may be construed as asserting Fair Housing Act claims but making no substantive ruling on the merits.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Adrian Oree, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, filed this action against Parkchester Condominium Complex and related entities, which the court noted may be construed as asserting claims under the Fair Housing Act, 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b), (f), along with ADA and state/city human rights law claims. The court issued an order of service directing the Clerk to issue summonses and the U.S. Marshals Service to effect service on defendants, and referred plaintiff to the Pro Se Clinic for assistance.
key_cases_cited: Walker v. Schult
Meilleur v. Strong

case_name: Karla Hunt v. Volare Health LLC
accommodation_description: Employee terminated for advocating against discrimination of a prospective patient with a mental health diagnosis who was denied admission to a nursing facility
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim was dismissed because she did not allege that she aided or encouraged the prospective patient in exercising his FHA rights, as the patient was unaware of the dispute, but she was granted leave to amend; Plaintiff's Oregon whistleblower retaliation claim survived because the close temporal proximity between her advocacy and her termination, combined with the factual setting, was sufficient to plead causation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Karla Hunt, an employee at a nursing and rehabilitation facility operated by Volare Health LLC, sued alleging she was terminated in retaliation for advocating that the facility admit a prospective patient with a mental health diagnosis, which she believed constituted disability discrimination under the FHA. The court granted Volare's motion to dismiss the FHA retaliation claim because Hunt failed to allege she aided the patient in exercising his FHA rights, but dismissed with leave to amend; the court denied dismissal of Hunt's Oregon whistleblower retaliation claim finding sufficient temporal proximity and factual support for causation.
key_cases_cited: Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Villiarimo v. Aloha Island Air, Inc., 281 F.3d 1054 (9th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Delroy Askins v. Prestige Management Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who receives medical supplies via mail and has a home health aide, alleges he was assaulted by the building superintendent, his medical supplies were taken, and he was denied access to the community room.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because he failed to allege facts showing he has a disability as defined by the FHA or that any adverse actions taken against him were motivated by his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Delroy Askins sued Prestige Management Inc. and building superintendent Lowell Hendricks alleging disability discrimination under the FHA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act based on being assaulted, having his medical supplies taken, and being denied access to the community room. The court dismissed all claims for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts establishing he has a disability as defined by the statutes or that any adverse actions were motivated by his disability, though the court granted plaintiff 30 days' leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Rodriguez v. Vill. Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)

case_name: Nathaniel Manny Ballantyne v. Guenther Management LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in rental denial based on claim that defendant changed credit score requirement from 600 to 700 after his application.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment or disparate impact because he was not the first qualified applicant (another applicant applied two days earlier and was approved), the credit score requirement was always 700, and the property was ultimately rented to a Black individual.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Nathanial Ballantyne sued Guenther Management LLC, a property management company, alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after he was not selected as a tenant for a rental property in Spokane, Washington. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case because he was not the first qualified applicant, the credit score requirement was always 700, and evidence showed the property was rented to another Black individual.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Scoggins v. Falcon Court
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges retaliation by housing provider for engaging in protected activity, including reporting habitability issues to code enforcement, HUD, and local authorities; plaintiff mentions disabilities (cancer survivor, heart problems, depression) and her son's respiratory issues.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a retaliation claim under the Fair Housing Act because plaintiff failed to identify specific protected actions she took or any adverse actions by defendants, providing only threadbare conclusory assertions insufficient under the pleading standards.
brief_summary: Danell Scoggins, proceeding pro se, sued Falcon Court and Turning Point of Central California (a permanent supportive housing provider), alleging retaliation under the Fair Housing Act for reporting habitability issues to code enforcement and federal authorities. The magistrate judge recommended dismissal without leave to amend, finding the complaint failed to plead a prima facie retaliation claim because plaintiff did not identify specific protected actions or adverse actions, providing only conclusory allegations insufficient under Twombly and Iqbal standards.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Charlesetta Moore, SR. v. C.F.Y. Development Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; complaint alleges poor treatment and discriminatory conduct including a racial slur directed at plaintiff.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because plaintiff failed to allege membership in a protected class and failed to plead facts showing different treatment because of protected class status, as a single racial slur and generalized allegations of poor treatment are insufficient to state a disparate treatment claim.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Charlesetta Moore, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued C.F.Y. Development Inc. and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on discriminatory treatment at Galt Place Senior Apartments, including rent increases, service failures, and a racial slur. The court dismissed the first amended complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege membership in a protected class and failed to plead facts showing she was treated differently because of protected class status.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Sw. Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)
Fried v. Wynn Las Vegas, LLC, 18 F.4th 643 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Laurie Thorson v. Hawaii Public Housing Authority, Hakim Ouansafi, Ryan Akamine, and Lyle Matsuura
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested continuation of a two-bedroom voucher reasonable accommodation to permit her son to serve as her live-in aide for epilepsy and transient epileptic amnesia.
key_holding: Defendants are entitled to summary judgment because HPHA's investigation into whether Thorson's son actually resided with her as a live-in aide was motivated by legitimate compliance concerns under HUD regulations, and Thorson failed to present sufficient evidence that this explanation was pretextual for disability-based retaliation.
brief_summary: Laurie Thorson, a Section 8 voucher recipient with epilepsy, sued the Hawaii Public Housing Authority and its officials alleging disability discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, claiming HPHA improperly investigated her live-in aide arrangement and used flawed comparables in rent reasonableness determinations. The court granted summary judgment for Defendants, finding Thorson failed to establish that HPHA's legitimate, nondiscriminatory justification for investigating whether her son actually resided with her was pretext for retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Kama v. Mayorkas, 107 F.4th 1054 (9th Cir. 2024)
Nozzi v. Hous. Auth. of Los Angeles, 806 F.3d 1178 (9th Cir. 2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10601202_2025_Albert Pickett, Jr. v. City of Cleveland, OH_ca6
case_name: Albert Pickett, Jr. et al. v. City of Cleveland, Ohio
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case involves disparate impact challenge to municipal water lien policy allegedly making housing unavailable to Black homeowners
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed class certification, holding that plaintiffs' disparate-impact claim under § 3604(a) presents a common question—whether Cleveland's water lien policy violates the FHA by disproportionately affecting Black homeowners—that predominates over individualized damages issues, and that plaintiffs have Article III standing based on the FHA's congressional authorization of housing discrimination claims.
brief_summary: African American homeowners and residents in Cuyahoga County, Ohio sued the City of Cleveland alleging that its water lien policy, which places liens on properties with delinquent water bills, has a disparate impact on Black homeowners in violation of § 3604(a) of the Fair Housing Act. The Sixth Circuit affirmed class certification under Rules 23(b)(2) and 23(b)(3), finding that plaintiffs' common question—whether the disproportionate placement of water liens on Black homeowners violates the FHA—predominates over individualized damages issues and that plaintiffs have Article III standing based on the cognizable harm of housing discrimination claims.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, 594 U.S. 413 (2021)
Merck v. Walmart, Inc., 114 F.4th 762 (6th Cir. 2024)
In re Whirlpool Corp. Front-Loading Washer Prods. Liab. Litig., 722 F.3d 838 (6th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Heidi Grossman Lepp v. Conrad Mallet, Jr.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claims defendants' enforcement actions (denial of lease occupancy, parking lot closure, inspections) constituted discrimination based on religion and disability under the FHA.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims fail because she only conclusorily alleged discrimination on the basis of disability and religion without pleading facts showing defendants acted with discriminatory intent based on those protected classes.
brief_summary: Heidi Grossman Lepp, associated with Sugarleaf Church, sued Detroit officials and private parties alleging defendants conducted raids, inspections, and parking restrictions that violated her constitutional and federal statutory rights. The court denied plaintiff's motion to amend the complaint as futile, finding her FHA claims under §§ 3604 and 3617 failed because she only conclusorily alleged discrimination without factual allegations showing discriminatory intent based on disability or religion.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Brown v. Dominion Mgmt. Servs., LLC, 2023 WL 5608861 (6th Cir. 2023)

case_name: Ivaylo Dodev, et al. v. D.R. Horton Incorporated, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege discrimination in maintenance and repairs based on national origin under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a); complaint also references § 3604(f)(1) and (f)(2) for disability-based discrimination in housing terms.
key_holding: The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the FHA claim time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations and the MMWA claim legally insufficient because home fixtures constitute real property rather than tangible personal property.
brief_summary: Ivaylo and Nikolina Dodev sued D.R. Horton and Mike Douglas alleging claims under the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act and Fair Housing Act related to their new home purchase, including workmanship defects and discriminatory refusal to make repairs. The court dismissed the case without prejudice for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the FHA claim time-barred and the MMWA claim legally deficient, without reaching the merits of the discrimination allegations.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Bennett v. CMH Homes, Inc., 770 F.3d 511 (6th Cir. 2014)
Comm. Concerning Cmty. Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 10603183_2025_JONES v. FEATHERSTONE CONDOMINIUM HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION INC_gamd
case_name: Quincy Bernard Jones v. Featherstone Condominium Homeowners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that Defendants remove slippery, wet dirt from his parking spot and the sidewalk connected to his unit because he is afraid he will slip and fall.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's failure-to-accommodate claim because he failed to allege what his disability is or explain how cleaning the parking space and sidewalk would address limitations caused by his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Quincy Jones, proceeding in forma pauperis, sued Featherstone Condominium Homeowners Association alleging failure to accommodate his disability by removing slippery dirt from his parking spot and sidewalk, along with retaliation claims. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding that Jones failed to allege what his disability is or explain how the requested accommodation would address limitations caused by his disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Schaw v. Habitat for Human. of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Watts v. Joggers Run Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 133 F.4th 1032 (11th Cir. 2025)

case_name: Tasha M. Lester v. Bamboo Broadway, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges lease non-renewal was racially discriminatory; no disability accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA race discrimination claim was dismissed because she failed to allege any factual content showing she was treated differently due to her race, and she admitted she owed unpaid rent and failed to pay on time.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tasha Lester sued Bamboo Broadway, LLC alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act related to the landlord's decision not to renew her lease. The court dismissed her complaint for failure to state a claim because she provided no factual allegations demonstrating discriminatory treatment based on race, and she conceded she owed back rent and failed to pay by the required deadline.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997)
Wheatley v. Beasley, 2021 WL 1667503 (E.D. Mo. Apr. 28, 2021)
Wiles v. Capitol Indem. Corp., 280 F.3d 868 (8th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Jane Doe v. Spillman Ranch Homes, LP and The NRP Group
accommodation_description: Request to be allowed to leave trash and recycling bins outside her unit without incurring fees as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: Summary judgment for defendants was proper because plaintiff failed to demonstrate a triable issue of fact as to defendant's knowledge of her disability—she never returned the reasonable accommodation form—and failed to show she requested an accommodation in direct and specific terms as required.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jane Doe, proceeding pro se, sued Spillman Ranch Homes, LP and The NRP Group, alleging Fair Housing Act claims including failure to accommodate, discrimination based on race, gender, and disability, and retaliation. The court granted defendants' motion for summary judgment, dismissing all claims with prejudice, finding plaintiff failed to provide evidence that defendants knew of her disability or that she properly requested an accommodation in direct and specific terms.
key_cases_cited: Providence Behav. Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)
Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)
Wyndham v. Harris Cnty., 875 F.3d 229 (5th Cir. 2017)
Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
McDonnell Douglas v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Lynya Cooper v. Chicago Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request to transfer Ms. Talbert, who had a progressive neurological disease rendering her unable to walk, from a second-floor apartment to a wheelchair-accessible ground floor unit
key_holding: The court approved a $525,000 settlement and allocated 95% to Lynya Cooper individually ($498,750) and 5% to the Estate of Trebora Talbert ($26,250), finding the settlement fair and reasonable after thorough negotiation.
brief_summary: Lynya Cooper sued the Chicago Housing Authority on behalf of herself and her late daughter Trebora Talbert, alleging the CHA failed to provide reasonable alternative housing accommodations (transfer to a wheelchair-accessible unit) for 788 days despite Ms. Talbert's progressive neurological disease rendering her unable to walk. The court approved the parties' $525,000 settlement and allocated 95% to Ms. Cooper individually and 5% to the Estate.

case_name: Marcus Hinton, et al. v. County of Cochise, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request alleged; plaintiffs claim they were illegally evicted and subjected to racial epithets, alleging housing discrimination based on race under § 3604(a).
key_holding: Plaintiffs fail to state a Fair Housing Act claim because they do not plausibly allege that the County Defendants, who are not alleged to have had any authority to sell or rent the property, engaged in housing discrimination based on race; the vague allegation that racial epithets were directed at them does not support an inference of housing discrimination under § 3604(a).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Marcus, Vanessa, and Veronica Hinton sued Cochise County and numerous other defendants alleging they were illegally evicted from their rental property, subjected to racial slurs, and had their property stolen by law enforcement officials. The Court granted the County Defendants' motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Act claim, finding that plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege that county officials with no authority to rent property engaged in housing discrimination based on race under § 3604(a), though the Court granted leave to amend the complaint.
key_cases_cited: Benavidez v. County of San Diego, 993 F.3d 1134 (9th Cir. 2021)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

case_name: Joy Lucretia Clark v. PNC Bank
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case concerns denial of a business trust account application at a bank.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed with prejudice because the statute does not apply to business bank account applications—the FHA applies only to dwellings, and Clark alleged no facts connecting her claim to housing discrimination.
brief_summary: Joy Lucretia Clark sued PNC Bank for denying her business trust account application, invoking the Fair Housing Act, Consumer Financial Protection Act, and Equal Credit Opportunity Act. The court dismissed her complaint with prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B), finding the FHA inapplicable to banking transactions with no connection to dwellings, the CFP Act provides no private right of action, and the ECOA claim lacked any factual allegations of prohibited discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Deutsch v. United States, 67 F.3d 1080 (3d Cir. 1995)
Grayson v. Mayview State Hosp., 293 F.3d 103 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: United States v. AION Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Fair housing testers requested assigned parking spaces as a reasonable accommodation for mobility impairments (inability to walk long distances, use of canes, crutches, or walkers).
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion for reconsideration and motion to certify for interlocutory appeal, holding that (1) plaintiffs plausibly alleged necessity for assigned parking accommodations based on specific physical impairments, (2) testers have standing to bring § 3604(f)(3)(B) claims under fair housing law, and (3) § 3604(c) discriminatory statement claims are not duplicative of § 3604(f)(3)(B) claims.
brief_summary: The United States and Fair Housing Partnership of Greater Pittsburgh, Inc. sued AION Management, LLC and related entities for allegedly refusing to provide assigned parking spaces as reasonable accommodations for testers representing mobility-impaired individuals, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied Defendants' motion for reconsideration and motion to certify for interlocutory appeal, upholding its prior ruling that plaintiffs plausibly pleaded necessity for the accommodations and that fair housing testers have standing to assert § 3604(f)(3)(B) claims.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Association, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Smith v. Pac. Props. & Dev. Corp., 358 F.3d 1097 (9th Cir. 2004)
United States v. Perry Homes, Inc., 2022 WL 2829887 (W.D. Pa. 2022)
Access Living of Metro Chi. v. Prewitt, 111 F. Supp. 3d 890 (N.D. Ill. 2015)

case_name: Arocho v. Chicopee Housing Authority
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case concerns the housing authority's alleged failure to protect tenants from a violent neighbor based on race/national origin discrimination.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because they failed to plead facts showing they were personally subjected to discriminatory conduct, failed to identify similarly situated comparators treated differently based on race, and because the claims were barred by the FHA's two-year statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Relatives and visitors of a murder victim sued the Chicopee Housing Authority and its executive director, claiming the housing authority violated the FHA by failing to evict a violent tenant with a known criminal history while making derogatory comments about Hispanic residents, thereby discriminating based on race and national origin. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the visitor plaintiffs lacked standing under the FHA, the tenant plaintiffs failed to plead a plausible disparate treatment claim, and all FHA claims were time-barred.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
DeShaney v. Winnebago Cty. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 489 U.S. 189 (1989)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Donna Calloway v. Kenyon Morgan, as agent of Prime Time Housing, LLC, and individually, et al.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; case involves alleged housing discrimination based on sex and religion, discriminatory statements, and retaliation.
key_holding: The court rejected defendants' attempt to invoke FHA exemptions as a jurisdictional challenge, characterizing the exemptions as affirmative defenses rather than matters affecting subject matter jurisdiction, and found the court has jurisdiction over claims under §§ 3604(c) and 3617 which are not subject to the exemptions at § 3603(b).
brief_summary: Donna Calloway sued Kenyon Morgan and Prime Time Housing, LLC alleging Fair Housing Act violations including sex discrimination, religious discrimination, discriminatory statements, and retaliation in connection with a home purchase arrangement. Defendants moved to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, arguing the FHA's exemptions under § 3603(b) applied. The court denied the motion, holding that FHA exemptions are affirmative defenses rather than jurisdictional defects and that the court has jurisdiction over claims under §§ 3604(c) and 3617, which are not subject to the exemptions.
key_cases_cited: United States v. Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)
Osborne v. Belton, 131 F.4th 262 (5th Cir. 2025)
Ho v. Donovan, 569 F.3d 677 (7th Cir. 2009)
Rural Water Dist. No. 2 v. City of Glenpool, 698 F.3d 1270 (10th Cir. 2012)
Czerwinski v. Gallagher, 661 F. Supp. 3d 718 (E.D. Mich. 2023)

source_file: 10622451_2025_Gilbert v. Tucson Realty _ Trust Company Management Services LLC_azd
case_name: Shenoa Gilbert v. Tucson Realty & Trust Company Management Services LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to transfer from an upper-floor unit to a first-floor unit due to her back injury disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to adequately plead the elements of her reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because she did not sufficiently allege how her back injury and kidney disease substantially limit major life activities, that defendant knew of her disability, or how the denial deprived her of equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: Shenoa Gilbert, a Section 8 voucher holder with a back injury and Stage 3 kidney disease, sued her property management company alleging violations of the Fair Housing Amendments Act and Rehabilitation Act for denying her accommodation requests for a first-floor unit and exemption from pesticide use. The Court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, finding plaintiff failed to sufficiently plead the elements of her reasonable accommodation and retaliation claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Valentine v. Granville Realty, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Valentine, a disabled U.S. Army veteran, requested emergency injunctive relief to halt eviction proceedings, which he alleges were initiated in retaliation for his disability-related ESA and protected activity.
key_holding: The court denied the TRO motion because Plaintiffs failed to comply with procedural requirements (Rule 65(b) and Local Rule 231) by not providing notice or certifying efforts to notify Defendants, and failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, or that equities favored injunctive relief.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Valentine and Hubbard, including a disabled U.S. Army veteran with an ESA, moved for a temporary restraining order to halt eviction proceedings at their Fresno residence, alleging disability discrimination, retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, and Civil RICO violations. The court denied the TRO motion for failure to comply with procedural requirements and for failure to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, or favorable balance of equities.
key_cases_cited: Wood v. City of Hayward, 974 F.2d 1344 (9th Cir. 1992)
Salazar v. Moynihan, 2011 WL 6179262 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 12, 2011)
Gruzen v. Henry, 84 Cal. App. 3d 515 (Ct. App. 1978)
Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)

source_file: 10626360_2025_Earle v. Manchester Village Owners Association Inc_wied
case_name: Dawn N. Earle v. Manchester Village Owners Association Inc, et al.
accommodation_description: Earle, who is blind with mobility impairments, requested installation of an accessible pathway/sidewalk from her ground-floor patio door across common grassy area to the public parking lot to enable safe egress and access to public transit.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the FHA failure-to-accommodate claim, finding Earle sufficiently alleged the installed walkway was too narrow (30 inches) to accommodate her disability needs and her seeing-eye dog, and that whether an accommodation is reasonable requires a fact-specific inquiry.
brief_summary: Dawn N. Earle, a blind woman with mobility impairments, sued her condominium association alleging violations of the ADA and FHA, including claims that the association failed to reasonably accommodate her disability by installing a non-ADA-compliant walkway and retaliated against her for filing housing discrimination complaints. The court dismissed the ADA claim (which Earle conceded) but denied dismissal of the FHA claims, finding Earle pleaded sufficient facts that the installed walkway was too narrow to accommodate her needs.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Cynthia Exline-Johnson v. Rosser Properties, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to remain in her apartment as a reasonable accommodation after receiving a sixty-day notice of lease non-renewal due to smoking complaints.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish she was disabled under the FHA because her sleep impairment was not shown to substantially limit a major life activity, and she presented no evidence that defendants regarded her as disabled, so her reasonable accommodation claim failed at the first element.
brief_summary: Cynthia Exline-Johnson sued Rosser Properties LLC and its owner alleging Fair Housing Act violations after they declined to renew her lease following tenant complaints about smoking, and she requested an accommodation to remain in her apartment. The federal court granted summary judgment for defendants on all federal claims, finding Johnson failed to establish she was disabled under the FHA and therefore could not support her reasonable accommodation, discrimination, or retaliation claims, and dismissed her state-law claims without prejudice.
key_cases_cited: Metropolitan Fair Hous. Council of Oklahoma, Inc. v. Fetock
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Steele v. Thiokol Corp.
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City
Bloch v. Frischholz

case_name: John May v. Erie County, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Defendants failed to address reasonable accommodation requests for disabilities and provided substandard housing based on transgender status and disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because the complaint failed to provide sufficient factual content to support plausible FHA claims, instead relying on conclusory allegations of wrongdoing.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff John May, a homeless individual identifying as physically and mentally disabled and pansexual, sued Erie County and fourteen other defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations, Section 1983 constitutional claims, ADA violations, and state law claims related to COVID-19 fund misuse, housing discrimination, and retaliation. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim and lack of standing, finding the FHA claims conclusory and lacking sufficient factual allegations to support a plausible cause of action.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 578 U.S. 330 (2016)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Monell v. Dep't of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Roman v. Jeffes, 904 F.2d 192 (3d Cir. 1990)

case_name: Mendoza-Lebron v. Adams
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought disability-based exemptions from the City's policy of shutting off water to delinquent property owners, including adequate notice of exemption eligibility and procedures to apply.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' ADA and FHA reasonable accommodation claims fail because all plaintiffs ultimately received exemptions from water shutoffs, there was no actual deprivation of water service, and plaintiffs failed to allege they were treated differently because of their disabilities—the City's policy actually provides affirmative exemptions for disabled residents.
brief_summary: Homeowners sued New York City and its water utility officials under the ADA and FHA, alleging that the City's policy of shutting off water to delinquent property owners failed to provide adequate notice of disability exemptions and reasonable accommodations for medically vulnerable residents. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding all plaintiffs' claims moot because they all qualified for exemptions and none had their water actually shut off, and alternatively that the federal damages claims failed on the merits because plaintiffs did not allege they were treated differently because of their disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Henrietta D. v. Bloomberg, 331 F.3d 261 (2d Cir. 2003)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)

case_name: Sarah Brockway v. Arlene L. Petschke Trust No. 102
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested physical modifications to common areas including handicapped parking, handrails, and wheelchair ramps for building access due to mobility disability.
key_holding: A reasonable jury could find that the October 12 Letter gave Defendants sufficient notice that Plaintiff had a disability and sought accommodations for it (handicap parking, handrails, ramps), distinguishing this case from Watters where the requested accommodation (privacy fence) could be desired by non-disabled persons and the defendant HOA actually responded to the request.
brief_summary: Sarah Brockway sued her former landlord (Arlene L. Petschke Trust No. 102) alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate her Ehlers-Danlos syndrome mobility disability. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, holding that a reasonable jury could find the October 12 Letter provided adequate notice of her disability and accommodation needs for handicap parking, handrails, and ramps, distinguishing Watters because Plaintiff requested disability-specific modifications that a non-disabled tenant would not typically request and Defendants completely ignored the request.
key_cases_cited: Watters v. Homeowners Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Beck v. Univ. of Wisconsin Bd. of Regents, 75 F.3d 1130 (7th Cir. 1996)
E.E.O.C. v. Sears, Roebuck & Co., 417 F.3d 789 (7th Cir. 2005)
Jankowski Lee & Assocs. v. Cisneros, 91 F.3d 891 (7th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 10634417_2025_Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corporation_ca4
case_name: Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corporation
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of business-purpose covenant to operate assisted living group home for seniors with disabilities on Gibson Island
key_holding: The court held that the accommodation was 'necessary' because without the covenant waiver, disabled persons could not live on Gibson Island, but reversed summary judgment because genuine disputes existed as to whether the defendant's four conditions rendered the accommodation 'unreasonable' or were pretextual.
brief_summary: Craig Lussi, through two LLCs, sued Gibson Island Corporation (a homeowners association) alleging the HOA violated the FHA by refusing to waive its business-purpose covenant as a reasonable accommodation for an assisted living group home for seniors with disabilities, and by retaliating and discriminating against him for advocating FHA-protected housing rights. The Fourth Circuit vacated summary judgment for the Corporation, holding that Lussi's accommodation request was necessary to afford disabled persons equal housing opportunity on Gibson Island, and that genuine disputes of material fact existed as to whether the HOA's four conditions (septic monitoring, arbitration, escrow, and guarantor requirements) rendered the accommodation unreasonable or were pretextual for discrimination and retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)
Oconomowoc Residential Prog. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
A Helping Hand, LLC v. Baltimore County, 515 F.3d 356 (4th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 10634783_2025_McCready v. Unity Sober Living Homes, LLC_paed
case_name: John McCready v. Unity Sober Living Homes, LLC, RE/MAX Achievers
accommodation_description: Disabled residents receiving SSDI sought to pay rent on the same weekly schedule as non-disabled residents rather than being required to pay monthly under Unity's facially discriminatory rent policy.
key_holding: Unity's written rent policy violated the Fair Housing Act as a matter of law because it facially discriminated against disabled residents receiving SSDI by requiring them to pay rent monthly while non-disabled residents could pay weekly, and Unity failed to rebut this discriminatory intent with evidence beyond self-serving, uncorroborated testimony.
brief_summary: John McCready, a recovering drug addict with depression receiving SSDI benefits, sued Unity Sober Living Homes and its property manager Re/Max Achievers alleging Unity's rent policy that required only SSDI recipients to pay monthly while non-disabled residents paid weekly violated the Fair Housing Act. The court granted partial summary judgment to McCready, holding Unity's written policy facially discriminated against disabled individuals in violation of § 3604(f)(2), and also found Unity liable for wrongful eviction and failure to return security under Pennsylvania law, while dismissing McCready's intentional infliction of emotional distress claim.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Lakeside Resort Enters., LP v. Bd. of Supervisors, 455 F.3d 154 (3d Cir. 2006)
Scott v. Harris, 550 U.S. 372 (2007)

source_file: 10636459_2025_Derby v. Pleasant Beach Mobile Home Resort, LLC_mied
case_name: Joshua Derby, Alyssia Warren, and Z.D., a minor v. Pleasant Beach Mobile Home Resort, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs vaguely referenced a 'request for accommodation under the ADA' and alleged 'retaliatory eviction, surveillance, and interruption of therapeutic services' without specifying the disability or the nature of any accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim with prejudice because plaintiffs failed to plead sufficient factual allegations—they vaguely referenced 'protected status' and a 'request for accommodation' without identifying any specific disability, specifying what accommodation was requested, or explaining how the FHA applied to their case.
brief_summary: Joshua Derby and Alyssia Warren, proceeding pro se, sued Pleasant Beach Mobile Home Resort LLC alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on threatened eviction and interference with housing rights. The court granted reconsideration of its prior dismissal order, reviewed the amended complaint, and dismissed all claims with prejudice for failure to state a claim because plaintiffs failed to plead sufficient factual allegations to support any cause of action, including the FHA claim which contained only vague references to protected status without specifying disability or accommodation requested.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Michigan Prot. & Advoc. Serv., Inc. v. Babin, 18 F.3d 337 (6th Cir. 1994)
Jones v. Alfred H. Mayer Co., 392 U.S. 409 (1968)
Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971)

case_name: Connolly v. Lanham
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case concerns alleged racial discrimination in a home appraisal valuation.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish pretext because although the appraiser made some inconsistent adjustments, Defendants' expert testimony demonstrated the appraisal methodology complied with professional standards, and no genuine issue of material fact existed showing the valuation result was motivated by racial discrimination rather than legitimate appraisal considerations.
brief_summary: Black homeowners sued an appraiser and his company alleging racial discrimination after their home was appraised at $472,000, then re-appraised at $750,000 by a different appraiser after removing evidence of Black occupancy. The court granted summary judgment to defendants on all discrimination claims, finding plaintiffs failed to establish pretext because defendants' expert testimony showed the appraisal methodology complied with professional standards and the challenged adjustments did not demonstrate discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Alig v. Quicken Loans Inc., 990 F.3d 782 (4th Cir. 2021)
Pinchback v. Armistead Homes Corp., 907 F.2d 1447 (4th Cir. 1990)
Village of Arlington v. Northeastern Ill. Univ. Comm. Coll., 429 U.S. 252 (1976)
Causey v. Ford Motor Credit Co., 162 F.3d 794 (4th Cir. 1998)

case_name: Casey Millerborg v. Blue Bonnet Trail LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user plaintiff challenges design and construction of apartment complex in Waxahachie, Texas, alleging accessibility barriers including inaccessible routes, mailbox, doorways, and improperly positioned fixtures.
key_holding: The court dismissed the case for lack of Article III standing because the plaintiff's complaint was conclusory and lacked sufficient factual detail about whether he personally experienced (as opposed to merely observed) the accessibility barriers at the property, failing to allege a concrete injury in fact.
brief_summary: Casey Millerborg, a wheelchair user, sued Blue Bonnet Trail LLC and its managers alleging that an apartment complex in Waxahachie, Texas violated the Fair Housing Act's design and construction requirements due to accessibility barriers he observed during a visit. The court dismissed the case without prejudice for lack of Article III standing, finding Millerborg's complaint was conclusory and failed to allege sufficient facts showing he personally experienced the accessibility barriers rather than merely observing them, and failed to establish a likelihood of future injury for injunctive relief.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Laufer v. Mann Hospitality, L.L.C., 996 F.3d 269 (5th Cir. 2021)
Deutsch v. Annis Enters., Inc., 882 F.3d 169 (5th Cir. 2018)
Frame v. City of Arlington, 657 F.3d 215 (5th Cir. 2011)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

source_file: 10638097_2025_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Bank of America, National Association_mdd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance, et al. v. Bank of America, N.A., et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this is a systemic disparate impact challenge to Bank of America's maintenance and marketing of real estate owned (REO) properties in majority-minority neighborhoods.
key_holding: Organizational plaintiffs lack Article III standing under the Supreme Court's Hippocratic Medicine framework, and on the merits, defendants are entitled to summary judgment because plaintiffs failed to propose equally effective alternatives to the challenged policies under step three of the disparate impact framework.
brief_summary: A coalition of fair housing organizations and three individual homeowners sued Bank of America and its property servicer alleging that their maintenance and marketing of bank-owned foreclosure properties in minority neighborhoods violated the Fair Housing Act's disparate impact and disparate treatment provisions. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of standing and summary judgment on the merits, finding organizational plaintiffs failed to establish concrete injury after Hippocratic Medicine and that plaintiffs' proposed alternatives to defendants' policies were not equally effective.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, 594 U.S. 413 (2021)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

case_name: Henry Ortiz v. Doug V. Mull, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who has PTSD, appears to allege disability-related discrimination including denial of housing-related services and selective enforcement based on disability, though the complaint lacks sufficient factual specificity to identify a concrete accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiff's first amended complaint fails to state any claim under the Fair Housing Act because his conclusory allegations do not demonstrate how defendants knew of his PTSD disability, how defendants refused to make a necessary accommodation, or that similarly situated non-disabled persons were treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Henry Ortiz, who has PTSD, sued private landlords and property management agents at Homecoming at Creekside Apartments and Evergreen Park Apartments, alleging disability discrimination, retaliation, and constitutional violations arising from vehicle towing, eviction threats, and denial of housing services. The court granted plaintiff's motion to proceed in forma pauperis but dismissed his first amended complaint with leave to amend, finding his Fair Housing Act claims conclusory and insufficiently pled because he failed to adequately allege defendants knew of his disability or refused a specific necessary accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
San Pedro Hotel Co., Inc. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10642195_2025_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Department of Housing and Urban Development_dcd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Fair housing organizations seek HUD's completion of statutorily required FHIP grantmaking process, not an individual reasonable accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B)
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs established organizational standing and likelihood of success on APA claims challenging HUD's unreasonable delay in processing FHIP grants and ordered HUD to comply with its statutory obligations and file a detailed compliance plan.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance and Tennessee Fair Housing Council sued HUD under the APA and Constitution, alleging HUD failed to award FHIP grants by the September 30, 2025 statutory deadline and refused to implement multi-year grants. The court granted in part a temporary restraining order, finding plaintiffs established standing and likelihood of success on APA unreasonable delay claims, ordering HUD to comply with statutory obligations and file a detailed compliance plan.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Bowen v. Massachusetts, 487 U.S. 879 (1988)
Lincoln v. Vigil, 508 U.S. 182 (1993)
Md. Dep't of Hum. Res. v. Dep't of Health & Hum. Servs., 763 F.2d 1441 (D.C. Cir. 1985)
Telecommns. Rsch. & Action Ctr. v. FCC, 750 F.2d 70 (D.C. Cir. 1984)

source_file: 10643981_2025_Fussell v. Sun Vista RV Resort LLC_azd
case_name: Fussell v. Sun Vista RV Resort LLC
accommodation_description: Paul requested that Defendants not selectively enforce credit and background check registration requirements against his wife Eilleen as an accommodation for his disability.
key_holding: Paul's FHA race discrimination claim survived dismissal because he adequately pled distinct and palpable injury from Defendants' selective enforcement of registration requirements upon discovering Eilleen's Filipino national origin, while Paul's disability accommodation claim, Eilleen's race discrimination claim (for her failure to sign the complaint), and retaliation claims were dismissed with leave to amend.
brief_summary: Paul and Eilleen Fussell sued Sun Vista RV Resort LLC and related entities alleging race discrimination, failure to accommodate Paul's disability, and retaliation under the FHA, claiming Defendants selectively enforced credit and background check requirements against Eilleen after learning of her Filipino national origin while grandfathering other residents. The court denied dismissal of Paul's race discrimination claim finding he adequately pled distinct injury from discriminatory conduct, but dismissed Paul's disability accommodation claim and Eilleen's claims with leave to amend for procedural deficiencies.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Ballentine v. Bowery Residents' Committee
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges denial of reasonable accommodations for PTSD and anxiety, including transfer requests and protection from punishment due to his disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA and ADA claims because he failed to plead specific facts describing how he was punished and denied reasonable accommodations, or how those actions were due to his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Quintin Ballentine sued Bowery Residents' Committee and government officials alleging violations of the Eighth Amendment, Fair Housing Act, ADA, and 18 U.S.C. § 242 based on conditions at a men's shelter in Brooklyn, claiming he was punished due to his disability (PTSD and anxiety) and denied reasonable accommodations. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiff failed to plead specific facts describing how he was denied reasonable accommodations or how adverse actions were due to his disability, while also dismissing Eighth Amendment, criminal statute, constitutional housing, and claims against government officials.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Jallow v. Geffner, 2024 WL 37073 (S.D.N.Y. 2024)
Powell v. Nat'l Bd. of Med. Examiners, 364 F.3d 79 (2d Cir. 2004)

case_name: Jacqueline Peters v. Cafton Towers Owners Corp.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant stay her eviction until she completes medical treatment as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The court denied the TRO because plaintiff failed to show likelihood of success on her reasonable accommodation claims, as her requested accommodations (environmental screening, washer/dryer, restrictions on other residents, and eviction stay) were not shown to be reasonable without undue burden, and the two-month delay in responding did not constitute a constructive denial absent evidence of bad faith.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jacqueline Peters sued Cafton Towers Owners Corp. alleging the cooperative building violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to provide reasonable accommodations for her disability and by terminating her lease in retaliation for requesting accommodations. The court denied Peters' motion for a temporary restraining order, finding she failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits because she did not show her requested accommodations were reasonable without undue burden and did not adequately plead pretext for the eviction.
key_cases_cited: Austin v. Town of Farmington, 826 F.3d 622 (2d Cir. 2016)
Logan v. Matveevskii, 57 F. Supp. 3d 234 (S.D.N.Y. 2014)
Basank v. Decker, 449 F. Supp. 3d 205 (S.D.N.Y. 2020)
Shang Hai Zhenglang Tech. Co. v. Mengku Tech. Co., 2020 WL 6826257 (E.D.N.Y. 2020)

case_name: Allen Feingold v. Erie Insurance Exchange
accommodation_description: No housing accommodation was requested; plaintiff merely invoked the Fair Housing Act as a purported basis for federal jurisdiction in an automobile insurance dispute.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's third amended complaint for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding plaintiff failed to establish diversity jurisdiction (by not pleading Erie Insurance's citizenship) and failed to plead any valid federal claim, including a Fair Housing Act claim which the court found insufficient because plaintiff did not allege Erie is his landlord or that Erie provides housing covered by the statute.
brief_summary: Allen Feingold sued Erie Insurance Exchange asserting claims arising from an alleged 2018 assignment of his former client's rights to uninsured/underinsured motorist claims from automobile accidents; he invoked the Fair Housing Act and other federal statutes to establish federal question jurisdiction. The court dismissed for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding Feingold failed to plead Erie's citizenship for diversity jurisdiction and failed to state any valid federal claim, including an insufficient Fair Housing Act claim because he did not allege Erie is a landlord or housing provider.

case_name: Jasmine Hill v. Philadelphia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks damages and injunctive relief for alleged race discrimination (failure to maintain lead-safe housing) and retaliation for complaining about housing conditions.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a FHA or Title VI claim because plaintiff's allegations of race discrimination are conclusory and provide no factual basis to infer discriminatory intent, and plaintiff's retaliation claim fails because her complaints concerned housing conditions rather than prohibited discrimination.
brief_summary: Jasmine Hill, an African-American Section 8 voucher holder, sued the Philadelphia Housing Authority and HUD alleging she was discriminated against based on race and retaliated against for complaining about lead paint exposure in her subsidized unit. The Court dismissed her complaint for failure to state plausible claims, finding her race discrimination allegations conclusory and her retaliation claim deficient because her complaints concerned housing conditions rather than prohibited discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
White v. Barbe, 767 F. App'x 332 (3d Cir. 2019)

source_file: 10646262_2025_Chosen Consulting, LLC v. Town Council of Highland, Indiana_ca7

case_name: Dominic Son v. Oakmont Properties-Azure, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability discrimination related to his adjustment disorder but does not specify what accommodation was requested or needed.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA disability claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing his adjustment disorder substantially limited major life activities or explaining what accommodation he needed and was refused.
brief_summary: Dominic Son, a Vietnamese-American tenant at Azure Apartments in Sparks, Nevada, sued Oakmont Properties, FPI Management, and the Housing Authority of the City of Reno alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race and disability discrimination, along with state law claims.
