The court dismissed the federal claims for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations of racial discrimination without supporting facts and failed to adequately plead the disability accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs.
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr.

source_file: 10648578_2025_(PS) Holmstrand v. Mercy Housing of California_caed
case_name: Holmstrand v. Mercy Housing of California
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint is too vague to identify a specific accommodation request; plaintiff alleges housing discrimination related to an eviction and prior assault by another resident.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint was dismissed for failure to state a claim because it lacked sufficient factual allegations describing the specific actions or omissions by defendants and failed to comply with Fed. R. Civ. P. 8(a)(2) pleading requirements.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sue Ellen Holmstrand sued Mercy Housing entities alleging Fair Housing Act and VAWA violations related to her eviction from a Section 202 supportive housing property in Encinitas, California, and a prior assault by another resident. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim because the complaint lacked sufficient factual detail to identify specific causes of action or explain what each defendant did to violate her rights, granting plaintiff leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Pardo-Pena v. Kaas Props. LLC, 2024 WL 3298284 (C.D. Cal. 2024)

case_name: Jimmy Avila v. ACACIA Network, Inc. et al.
accommodation_description: Request to transfer from an apartment with mold that exacerbates tenant's chronic rhinitis and rhinosinusitis, a condition worsened by mold exposure.
key_holding: Avila's claims were properly dismissed because he failed to plead sufficient factual allegations establishing that defendants discriminated against him based on disability or that there was a causal connection between his protected activity (filing lawsuits) and the termination of his tenancy.
brief_summary: Jimmy Avila, a tenant with schizoaffective disorder living in supportive housing managed by ACACIA Network, sued ACACIA and 1212 Grant Realty for disability discrimination and retaliation under the FHA, ADA, and state laws, claiming he was denied a reasonable accommodation in the form of a transfer from a moldy apartment and was evicted in retaliation for filing lawsuits. The court granted motions to dismiss both actions, finding Avila failed to plead sufficient facts to support a causal connection between his lawsuits and tenancy termination, or to support a disability discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell A. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dodd v. City Univ. of New York, 489 F. Supp. 3d 219 (S.D.N.Y. 2020)
Wise v. JPMorgan Chase, No. 21 Civ. 3718, 2023 WL 5575889 (S.D.N.Y. Aug. 29, 2023)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)

case_name: Pamela Crawford v. Douglas M. Milner et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied rental of an apartment because of her race (African American); no accommodation request is involved.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA disparate treatment claim because she failed to plead facts supporting her belief that defendants rented the apartment to a non-African American tenant, stating only conclusory allegations about motivation without factual support.
brief_summary: African American plaintiff Pamela Crawford sued landlords Douglas and Michelle Milner alleging they refused to rent an apartment to her because of her race, claiming the unit was rented to a non-African American applicant after she submitted documentation. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) for failing to state a claim, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations without factual support for the discriminatory intent claim.
key_cases_cited: SW. Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)
Calhoun v. Stahl, 254 F.3d 845 (9th Cir. 2001)
Lopez v. Smith, 203 F.3d 1122 (9th Cir. 2000)
Watison v. Carter, 668 F.3d 1108 (9th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Manuel James Gonzalez, as legal guardian of REG, a minor v. Royse Services LLC d/b/a LaVida Vacations
accommodation_description: Request to waive pet deposit fee for minor daughter's service dog trained to prevent panic attacks and destructive tantrums associated with autism
key_holding: The court granted default judgment for plaintiff, finding that Royse Services violated both the ADA and FHA by refusing to waive a pet deposit for the minor daughter's service dog, despite knowledge of her disability and the dog's necessity to mitigate her autism symptoms.
brief_summary: Manuel James Gonzalez sued Royse Services LLC on behalf of his minor daughter R.E.G., who has autism and requires a service dog, alleging the vacation rental company violated the ADA and FHA by refusing to allow the service dog unless he paid a $150 pet deposit. The court granted default judgment for plaintiff, finding Royse Services discriminated against R.E.G. based on her disability by refusing to waive its pet fee policy despite knowing of her disability and the service animal's necessity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Surtain v. Hamlin Terrace Found., 789 F.3d 1239 (11th Cir. 2015)
Loren v. Sasser, 309 F.3d 1296 (11th Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10651719_2025_Joe v. Olive Branch Assisted Living LLC_azd
case_name: Hailei Joe v. Olive Branch Assisted Living LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff was discharged from a residential addiction treatment facility following disclosure of her HIV-positive status; no specific accommodation request was made
key_holding: Because genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether Plaintiff would not have been discharged but for her HIV-positive status and whether Defendant's affirmative defenses apply, neither party is entitled to summary judgment on the FHA discrimination claim, but Defendant is entitled to summary judgment on the retaliation, public disclosure, and punitive damages claims.
brief_summary: Hailei Joe sued Olive Branch Assisted Living LLC alleging she was unlawfully discharged from a residential addiction treatment facility because of her HIV-positive status, in violation of the Fair Housing Act, Arizona Fair Housing Act, and ADA, as well as claims for retaliation and public disclosure of private facts; the court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the discrimination claims because genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether her discharge was causally connected to her disability, but granted summary judgment to Defendant on the retaliation claims, public disclosure claim, and prayer for punitive damages.
key_cases_cited: Brown v. City of Tucson, 336 F.3d 1181 (9th Cir. 2003)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Murray v. Mayo Clinic, 934 F.3d 1101 (9th Cir. 2019)
Bragdon v. Abbott, 524 U.S. 624 (1998)
Bax v. Doctors Med. Ctr. of Modesto, Inc., 52 F.4th 858 (9th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Felicia Rosa v. Administration for Children's Services
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges ACS employee interfered with her housing and lease termination as discriminatory conduct under the FHA.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim against ACS employee Moore was dismissed for failure to state a claim because her allegations were too vague and lacked factual details about what Moore said to the landlord.
brief_summary: Felicia Rosa, proceeding pro se, sued ACS and its employees along with multiple other defendants under the Fair Housing Act, Section 1983, and Section 1985 arising from custody removal and housing voucher termination. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding Plaintiff's FHA allegations against ACS employee Moore too vague to survive dismissal, and dismissed claims against Officer Mickey as time-barred, claims against NYPD and ACS for lack of capacity to be sued, with 30 days' leave to replead.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone, Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Rodriguez v. Vill. Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
Littlejohn v. City of New York, 795 F.3d 297 (2d Cir. 2015)

case_name: J&S Group Inc., Wanaque Realty Corp., and Mountain Lakes Estates, Inc. v. Borough of Wanaque
accommodation_description: Developer-plaintiffs allege the Borough refused to assign Developer Agreements and created obstacles to development based on the national origin of the developers and their selected builder (Portuguese immigrants).
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on Plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim under an intentional discrimination theory because genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether the Borough's Administrator made discriminatory statements and took adverse actions against Portuguese immigrant developers, but granted summary judgment on the takings claim and Section 1982 claim which required race-based discrimination rather than national origin discrimination.
brief_summary: Property developer companies owned by a Portuguese immigrant sued the Borough of Wanaque, NJ alleging the municipality discriminated against them based on national origin by refusing to assign developer agreements to their Portuguese-owned builder and creating obstacles to their housing development project. The court granted summary judgment for defendant on takings and Section 1982 claims but denied summary judgment on the Fair Housing Act intentional discrimination claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding discriminatory statements made by the Borough Administrator about Portuguese people.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware County, 983 F.2d 1277 (3d Cir. 1993)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Eastampton Center L.L.C. v. Township of Eastampton, 155 F. Supp. 2d 102 (D.N.J. 2001)

case_name: Cherry-El v. Blount
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to change his status from live-in aide to primary tenant and continue residing in subsidized housing as a reasonable accommodation after his mother passed away.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint was sua sponte dismissed without prejudice for failing to comply with Rules 8 and 12(b)(6) because it was too vague to provide fair notice of the nature or substance of his claims, including his reasonable accommodation theory.
brief_summary: Pro se disabled plaintiff Dwane Cherry-El sued the District of Columbia Housing Authority seeking to remain in subsidized housing after his mother passed away, claiming he submitted documentation to change his status from live-in aide to primary tenant as a reasonable accommodation. The court sua sponte dismissed the complaint without prejudice under Rules 8 and 12(b)(6) because the pleading was too vague to put defendants on fair notice of his claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89 (2007)
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Monsanto Co. v. Geertson Seed Farms, 561 U.S. 139 (2010)
Brown v. District of Columbia, 761 F. Supp. 3d 34 (D.D.C. 2024)

source_file: 10656302_2025_Hall-Johnson v. City and County of San Francisco_cand
case_name: Carneice Kathrine Hall-Johnson v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; plaintiff alleges race discrimination in housing placement priority under a City-administered lottery system.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Amendments Act claim because the complaint contained no allegations that plaintiff had a disability, and dismissed the race discrimination claims because plaintiff failed to adequately allege the decisionmaker knew she was Black or intentionally discriminated against her.
brief_summary: Carneice Kathrine Hall-Johnson, a Black woman residing in San Francisco homeless shelters, sued the City and County of San Francisco alleging she was denied housing prioritization under the Live in/Work in San Francisco Preference program because of her race and disability. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding the FHA claim failed because plaintiff did not allege a disability, and the race discrimination claims failed because the complaint lacked factual allegations showing the decisionmaker knew plaintiff was Black or acted with discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Thornton v. City of St. Helens, 425 F.3d 1158 (9th Cir. 2005)
Pers. Adm'r of Mass. v. Feeney, 442 U.S. 256 (1979)
Robinson v. Adams, 847 F.2d 1315 (9th Cir. 1987)

case_name: Teresa Maslowski v. Brookside Condominium Association II, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff challenges HOA rule prohibiting minors from residing in the building and defendants' statements regarding that rule as familial status discrimination and coercion.
key_holding: The HOA and individual defendants did not discriminate against or coerce the plaintiff because the HOA never enforced the discriminatory rule against minors against her, the individual defendant offered to grandfather her in, and no reasonable jury could find the isolated statements constituted threats or interference.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Teresa Maslowski sued Brookside Condominium Association and individual unit owners alleging familial status discrimination under § 3604(b) and coercion under § 3617 after she learned the HOA's condominium documents prohibited minors from living in the building and voluntarily moved out. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding no evidence that the HOA enforced the discriminatory rule against her or that defendants' statements constituted actionable threats or interference.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Kormoczy v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev. ex rel. Briggs, 53 F.4d 821 (7th Cir. 1995)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Gueltzau v. Warehime
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Defendants failed to accommodate his disabilities but the specific accommodation requested is not described in the screening order.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff's amended complaint sufficient to proceed on disability discrimination, reasonable accommodation, and retaliation claims under the FHA, after Plaintiff corrected prior pleading deficiencies by connecting his disabilities to Defendants' actions and describing how Defendants failed to accommodate.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mikel Gueltzau, proceeding in forma pauperis, sued property owners Derek and Cathy Warehime alleging disability discrimination, failure to provide reasonable accommodation, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act related to his former tenancy. The court screened the amended complaint and found it sufficient to proceed to service of process after Plaintiff corrected earlier pleading deficiencies by adequately connecting his disabilities to Defendants' allegedly discriminatory conduct.
key_cases_cited: Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wright v. First Student, Inc., 710 F.3d 782 (8th Cir. 2013)
Moore v. Jackson, 123 F.3d 1082 (8th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10657915_2025_Doria v. Yavapai College District Governing Board_azd
case_name: Daniel T. Doria v. Yavapai College District Governing Board, et al.
accommodation_description: Doria requested permission to operate a pet-sitting business from his RV as an accommodation for his psychological disabilities including anxiety, depression, and minor paranoid delusions.
key_holding: The court dismissed Doria's FHA claim (Count I) as barred by res judicata because he had previously litigated disability discrimination claims arising from the same lease enforcement dispute that resulted in final adverse judgments, including a jury verdict finding just cause for lease termination.
brief_summary: Daniel Doria sued Yavapai College and its property manager alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after the college terminated his RV park lease for operating a pet-sitting business, which he claimed was a reasonable accommodation for his psychological disabilities. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Doria's FHA claim was barred by res judicata because he had previously litigated nearly identical disability discrimination claims arising from the same lease enforcement that resulted in final adverse judgments, including a jury verdict rejecting his counterclaims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Manzarek v. St. Paul Fire & Marine Ins. Co.

case_name: Brianca Michelle Sheely v. Warner Robins Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged retaliation under the Fair Housing Act based on Defendant's housing authority staff entering her residence without notice and displacing her from her unit.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim with prejudice because her Recast Complaint failed to allege any actual discrimination on a protected basis, and dismissed all remaining federal and state claims for failure to state viable claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Brianca Michelle Sheely, a former tenant in public housing operated by the Warner Robins Housing Authority, filed suit alleging violations of privacy rights, housing standards, retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, and intentional infliction of emotional distress. The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice because plaintiff failed to allege any actual discrimination on a protected basis, dismissed the § 1983 claim for failure to plead a municipal policy or custom, declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims, and dismissed the entire Recast Complaint with prejudice for failure to state a claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)

source_file: 10660660_2025_The Cottage, Inc Comfy Cottage v. Pasco County_flmd
case_name: The Comfy Cottage, Inc., Comfy Cottage v. Pasco County
accommodation_description: Request to waive the Florida Building Code's automatic fire sprinkler system requirement to operate a community residential group home for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities
key_holding: The sprinkler system requirement applies uniformly to all care facilities regardless of occupant disability status, and Comfy Cottage failed to establish that the requested waiver was necessary to afford disabled residents an equal opportunity to use and enjoy housing.
brief_summary: Comfy Cottage, a not-for-profit providing housing for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities, sued Pasco County after being denied a waiver of the fire sprinkler installation requirement needed to license its community residential group home. The court granted summary judgment for the County, finding the sprinkler requirement does not facially discriminate because it applies equally to all care facilities regardless of occupant disability status, and Comfy Cottage failed to establish the accommodation was necessary.
key_cases_cited: Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)

source_file: 10661347_2025_Clark v. Council of Unit Owners of the 100 Harborview Drive Condominium_mdd
case_name: Clark v. Council of Unit Owners of the 100 Harborview Drive Condominium Association
accommodation_description: N/A - this is a retaliation claim, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendant on the conversion of personal possessions claim because Harborview had no role in AMRestore's actions, but denied summary judgment on the demand letter and litigation updates claims because genuine disputes exist regarding whether these actions constitute materially adverse actions causally connected to plaintiffs' prior FHA complaints.
brief_summary: The Clarks sued Harborview Condominium Association alleging FHA retaliation under § 3617 for filing prior complaints about familial status discrimination; they claimed defendant retaliated through conversion of possessions during unit remediation, a demand letter for unpaid assessments with a lien threat, and publishing litigation updates to unit owners.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
Grp. Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corp., 144 F.4th 522 (4th Cir. 2025)
Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. Co. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)
Williams v. Arora Hills Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., No. 19-3370, 2021 WL 2226199 (D. Md. June 2, 2021)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

source_file: 10663021_2025_MBSC Property South LLC v. Myrtle Beach South Carolina, The City of_scd
case_name: MBSC Property South, LLC v. The City of Myrtle Beach, South Carolina
accommodation_description: This is a developer challenging a zoning moratorium and overlay zone that restricts conversion of short-term rental properties to long-term rentals; MBSC argues the ordinances prevent affordable housing serving minorities and low-income residents using housing vouchers.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims, finding MBSC plausibly alleged both disparate treatment (intentional targeting of its affordable housing conversion) and disparate impact (elimination of housing disproportionately serving minorities and voucher holders) under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604 and 3617.
brief_summary: MBSC Property South, LLC, a developer that purchased 165 condominium units to convert to long-term affordable rentals, sued the City of Myrtle Beach challenging a temporary moratorium and Short-Term Rental Conversion Overlay Zone that prohibits conversion of oceanfront properties from short-term to long-term rentals. The court granted in part and denied in part the City's motion to dismiss, dismissing MBSC's spot zoning, tax, contract rights, and vested rights claims, but allowed MBSC's Fair Housing Act disparate treatment and disparate impact claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr.
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc.
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship

source_file: 10664368_2025_Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC_caed
case_name: Pharilyn Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Underlying case involves reasonable accommodation requests for disability, though this discovery order does not specify the accommodation type.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff's motion to compel discovery responses regarding defendant's net worth and bookkeeping/accounting services, finding such information relevant to plaintiff's punitive damages claim in an FHA action.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Pharilyn Chhang sued defendants West Coast USA Properties LLC, Sergio Madrigal, and insurance broker Dibuduo & Defendis LLC for Fair Housing Act violations. The court granted plaintiff's motion to compel discovery responses to interrogatories seeking defendant's net worth and accounting personnel information, finding such information relevant to plaintiff's punitive damages claim in the underlying FHA case.
key_cases_cited: Salisbury v. Hickman, No. 1:12-CV-01098-LJO, 2013 WL 4402789 (E.D. Cal. Aug. 14, 2013)

case_name: Singletary v. City of Toledo
accommodation_description: Group home operator Treasured Homes requested a waiver of the City of Toledo's 500-foot spacing requirement between group homes to operate a second licensed adult care group home at 3442 Woodley Road.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment on the failure-to-accommodate claim because genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the zoning waiver was reasonable (as Defendants failed to show a single-property waiver would constitute a fundamental alteration), but granted Defendants' motion on the disparate treatment claim because Plaintiffs failed to show Melden's decision was motivated by Gallaher's discriminatory email rather than legitimate zoning concerns.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Treasured Homes LLC and its sole member Kimberly Cunningham sued the City of Toledo and Commissioner Ken Fischer alleging the City violated the FHA by denying their request to waive the 500-foot spacing requirement between group homes to open a second adult care facility at 3442 Woodley Road. The court denied cross-motions for summary judgment on the failure-to-accommodate claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to reasonableness, while granting summary judgment for Defendants on the disparate treatment claim for insufficient evidence of discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Smith & Lee Assocs., Inc. v. City of Taylor, Mich., 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Harding v. City of Toledo, 433 F. Supp. 2d 867 (N.D. Ohio 2006)

case_name: Sang W. Mendy v. Tracy L. Larson et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - claim involves race-based hostile housing environment under § 3604(b) and interference under § 3617, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to all defendants because Mendy presented no evidence establishing that the security guard's conduct (questioning his residency, making a false assault accusation) was based on his race rather than non-discriminatory reasons, failing to demonstrate severe or pervasive harassment based on a protected characteristic.
brief_summary: Sang Mendy, a Black tenant at Modera Jackson apartments in Seattle, sued security guard Tracy Larson, his employer Northwest Security Services, property management company Mill Creek Residential Services, and American Security Programs, alleging they created a hostile housing environment based on race under § 3604(b) and interfered with his Fair Housing Rights under § 3617. The court granted summary judgment to all defendants, finding that while Mendy was questioned about his residency and falsely accused of assault, he presented no evidence that these actions were motivated by racial animus rather than legitimate security concerns.
key_cases_cited: Morris v. W. Hayden Ests. First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Georgia St. Conf. of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
Sanzaro v. Ardiente Homeowners Ass'n, 364 F. Supp. 3d 1158 (D. Nev. 2019)
Bingham v. City of Manhattan Beach, 341 F.3d 939 (9th Cir. 2003)
Villiarimo v. Aloha Island Air, Inc., 281 F.3d 1054 (9th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Gregory R. Marlor v. The Housing Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested The Housing Company install a doorbell at the building's main entrance to enable visitors to contact residents with disabilities for accessible building entry.
key_holding: The Court found that Marlor stated one plausible reasonable accommodation claim—noting that he may proceed on his allegation that THC denied his July 2023 request for a doorbell at the main entrance—while dismissing all other claims as time-barred or failing to state a plausible claim for disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Gregory Marlor, a wheelchair user with partial paralysis, sued The Housing Company under the Fair Housing Act alleging disability discrimination, including denial of transfer to an accessible unit and denial of a request for doorbell installation at the building entrance. On initial screening, the Court found only one plausible claim—that THC denied his July 2023 request for a doorbell installation as a reasonable accommodation—while dismissing all other claims as time-barred under the FHA's two-year limitations period or for failure to state a plausible discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Nat'l R.R. Passenger Corp. v. Morgan, 536 U.S. 101 (2002)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Steven E. Hobbs, Sr. v. Igor Goncharko, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Hobbs, who suffered a spinal injury causing severe pain and mobility issues, alleged defendants failed to accommodate his disability through necessary repairs and targeted him for eviction because of his condition.
key_holding: The § 3604(b) racial discrimination claim survived against Goncharko/5120 based on allegations of using a racial slur, disparate maintenance denial, and targeted eviction, but Coldwell Banker was dismissed for failure to plead racial motivation; the § 3604(f) accommodation claim failed because requests for repairs are modifications, not accommodations under § 3604(f)(3)(B), and the § 3617 retaliation claim failed for lack of alleged protected activity.
brief_summary: Steven Hobbs, a Black commercial mortgage broker and Chicago tenant, sued property owners Igor Goncharko and 5120 South LLC, property management company Coldwell Banker, and prior owner The Stewardship Group, alleging racial discrimination, disability discrimination, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, plus a § 1981 breach of contract claim related to an alleged purchase agreement for affordable housing. The court denied Goncharko/5120's motion to dismiss the § 3604(b) racial discrimination claim based on allegations of a racial slur, disparate maintenance, and targeted eviction, but dismissed Coldwell Banker entirely for failure to plead racial motive, dismissed the § 3604(f) disability accommodation claim because repair requests are modifications not accommodations, and dismissed the § 3617 retaliation claim for failure to allege protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 10665985_2025_Heron v. Gunjan Keida CEO of US Bank Trust, N.A._nyed
case_name: Marcia Veronica Heron et al v. Gunjan Keida CEO of US Bank Trust, N.A. et al
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs request preliminary injunction enjoining defendants from commencing, continuing, or enforcing any eviction or ejectment process against plaintiffs at the property.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction is denied because plaintiffs failed to establish likelihood of success on the merits, including failing to state an FHA claim where plaintiffs do not claim they were discriminated against in housing based on a protected characteristic.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Marcia Heron and Kemar Blake filed an action and emergency motion for preliminary injunction seeking to enjoin defendants from evicting them from property at 1740 E. 54th Street, Brooklyn, NY, claiming superior equitable ownership interests and alleging FHA violations. The court denied the preliminary injunction motion and ordered plaintiffs to show cause why the action should not be dismissed for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding that plaintiffs failed to state an FHA claim because they did not allege discrimination based on any protected characteristic, and that federal courts lack jurisdiction over state foreclosure and eviction matters under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Sullivan v. Nassau Cty. Interim Fin. Auth
Dorce v. City of New York

case_name: Brian K. Legge v. Clarke County Zoning Department et al.
accommodation_description: Request for variance and exception from the County's 50-foot setback zoning requirement due to physical and financial inability to demolish or relocate the garage caused by medical conditions.
key_holding: Legge's FHA claim failed because he did not establish a direct nexus between his accommodation requests (variance) and his alleged handicap, as required to state a failure-to-accommodate claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B); his retaliation claim also failed because he did not engage in protected FHA activity before the adverse actions.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Brian Legge sued Clarke County, Virginia and its officials seeking to enjoin demolition of a garage that violated the County's 50-foot setback zoning requirement, alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on his medical conditions (cancer, gastrointestinal complications) and retaliation under § 3617. The court denied Legge's motion for preliminary injunction, finding he failed to show a likelihood of success on his FHA claims because he did not establish the required nexus between his variance requests and his alleged handicap, and because he did not engage in protected FHA activity to support his retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Vill. of Willowbrook v. Olech, 528 U.S. 562 (2000)
SAS Assocs. 1, LLC v. City Council for City of Chesapeake, 91 F.4th 715 (4th Cir. 2024)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
MLC Auto., LLC v. Town of So. Pines, 532 F.3d 269 (4th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Watkins v. Chau
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that her landlord install a shower bar in her apartment as an accommodation for her physical disabilities.
key_holding: Plaintiff adequately stated a FHAA reasonable accommodation claim against the private landlord, as the four-month delay in installing the shower bar after a doctor-recommended request may constitute a denial, and the landlord's complete failure to address other accessibility requests (repairing uneven floors and inadequate lighting) was sufficient to survive judgment on the pleadings.
brief_summary: Ida Watkins, a Section 8 voucher holder with physical disabilities, sued her private landlord Stephanie Chau and public health employees for failing to install a shower bar and make repairs, and for terminating her housing voucher after she threatened legal action. The court dismissed the ADA claims against Chau as a private landlord and dismissed all claims against the public health employees, but allowed Watkins' Fair Housing Amendments Act reasonable accommodation claim and FHA retaliation claim against Chau to proceed to discovery, finding that her complaint plausibly alleged Chau's four-month delay in installing the shower bar could constitute a denial of reasonable accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Summers v. City of Fitchburg, 940 F.3d 133 (1st Cir. 2019)
Rivera v. Mora Dev. Corp., 624 F. Supp. 3d 80 (D.P.R. 2022)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Kris v. Dusseault Fam. Revocable Tr., 594 F. Supp. 3d 333 (D.N.H. 2022)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Watkins v. Chau
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of a shower bar due to her physical disabilities, which was eventually installed in late April 2024 after being requested in January 2024.
key_holding: The court granted defendant Flacks' motion to dismiss, finding that Watkins failed to state an Eighth Amendment claim (she is not incarcerated), failed to state ADA or Rehabilitation Act claims against a federal employee, and that sovereign immunity barred her retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Ida Watkins, a pro se Section 8 tenant with physical disabilities, sued her landlord, Metro Housing Boston, and a HUD employee (Flacks) alleging Eighth Amendment deliberate indifference, disability discrimination under the ADA and Rehabilitation Act, and retaliation for complaining about housing conditions and requesting a shower bar. The court granted Flacks' motion to dismiss, finding no Eighth Amendment claim (Watkins is not incarcerated), no viable ADA claim against a federal employee, no Rehabilitation Act claim (no allegation she sought services from HUD), and sovereign immunity barred the retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents of Fed. Bureau of Narcotics, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)
Summers v. City of Fitchburg, 940 F.3d 133 (1st Cir. 2019)

case_name: Ruth Castro v. Lancaster Loft, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that landlord enforce non-smoking provisions in lease agreement to create a smoke-free living environment due to her severe asthma and smoke allergies.
key_holding: The court allowed Plaintiff's FHA discrimination and retaliation claims to survive screening under § 1915(e), finding that her allegations—construed liberally as a pro se plaintiff—were sufficient to state plausible claims that she has a disability (asthma), requested a reasonable accommodation (enforcement of non-smoking provisions), and suffered adverse actions (eviction) after engaging in protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Ruth Castro, a pro se Hispanic tenant with severe asthma, sued her landlord and property management entities under the Fair Housing Act, alleging that defendants discriminated and retaliated against her by refusing to enforce non-smoking provisions in the lease despite her disability-related need for a smoke-free environment, and ultimately evicting her after she requested accommodations and sought assistance from government agencies. The court screened the complaint under § 1915(e) and allowed Castro's FHA discrimination and retaliation claims to proceed, finding her allegations sufficient to state plausible claims, while dismissing her constitutional claims against private defendants for failure to allege state action.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Mehta v. Beaconridge Improvement Ass'n, 432 Fed. App'x 614 (7th Cir. 2011)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Denton v. Hernandez, 504 U.S. 25 (1992)
Perez v. Fenoglio, 792 F.3d 768 (7th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 10667251_2025_Watkins, Lateesha v. Fond du Lac District Attorney Office_wiwd
case_name: Watkins v. Fond du Lac District Attorney Office
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges that defendant Wallick Communities and its property manager discriminated against her regarding her emotional support animal (ESA) dog, leading to the dog's impoundment.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim because she failed to allege protected activity under the FHA, and dismissed her disability discrimination claim (concerning ESA dog) as insufficiently pled and untimely.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lateesha Watkins, proceeding without counsel, sued Wallick Communities (her landlord), Fond du Lac police and district attorney, alleging FHA retaliation, disability discrimination concerning her ESA dog, and constitutional claims. The court dismissed her complaint for failure to state a claim, finding her FHA retaliation claim failed because she did not allege protected activity and her disability discrimination claim was too vague and untimely, while allowing amendment only for certain Fourth Amendment false arrest and malicious prosecution claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Riley v. City of Kokomo, 909 F.3d 182 (7th Cir. 2018)
Lewis v. City of Chicago, 914 F.3d 472 (7th Cir. 2019)
Sykes v. Cook Cnty. Cir. Ct. Prob. Div., 837 F.3d 736 (7th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 10669050_2025_Jose R. Solano v. PHH Mortgage Company_cacd
case_name: Jose R. Solano v. PHH Mortgage Corporation
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves alleged race-based discriminatory foreclosure and failure to offer loan modification alternatives, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because his allegations that defendants' actions 'may have been' based on his race failed to adequately plead discriminatory intent under Rule 8(a)(2), and further amendment would be futile.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jose R. Solano, proceeding pro se, sued PHH Mortgage Corporation, Western Progressive, HSBC Bank, and Ocwen Loan Servicing alleging they fraudulently foreclosed on his Los Angeles condominium by entities lacking legal standing, and that they discriminated against him based on his race and national origin by failing to offer loan modification alternatives. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing all claims without leave to amend, finding that plaintiff's FHA claim failed to adequately plead discriminatory intent as he merely alleged actions 'may have been' based on his race rather than facts showing they were 'because of' his race.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Obduskey v. McCarthy & Holthus LLP, 586 U.S. 466 (2019)
Barnes v. Routh Crabtree Olsen PC, 963 F.3d 993 (9th Cir. 2020)

case_name: Cheryl Poindexter v. The Town of Haskell, Steve Arrowood, The Arrowood Family Revocable Trust, Tamera Laverne Arrowood, Jessica Parks, Unknown Occupants
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request at issue; plaintiff alleged sex and familial status discrimination as a single woman
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because her allegations that she was targeted as a single female were conclusory and speculative, failing to create a reasonable inference of discrimination; plaintiff merely pointed to the gender of officers and the timing of events after her boyfriend moved out without identifying any discriminatory statements or conduct.
brief_summary: Cheryl Poindexter sued the Town of Haskell and private defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a), (b), and (c) based on discrimination due to her sex and familial status as a single woman. The court granted the Town's motion to dismiss, finding the plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts to state a plausible discrimination claim, as her allegations consisted merely of conclusory assertions that she was targeted because she was a single female without identifying any specific discriminatory statements or conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)

case_name: Charnina Smith v. New Neighborhoods, Inc. and Downtown West Gate Apartments, L.P.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges race discrimination in commercial tenancy at 505 West Avenue, Bridgeport, CT, including disparate treatment, disparate impact, hostile housing environment, and retaliation claims under the FHA.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's federal claims with prejudice because her allegations of intentional race discrimination were wholly conclusory, failing to allege specific facts giving rise to a plausible inference of discriminatory intent, and plaintiff failed to plausibly allege state action for her § 1983 claim or federal funding for her Title VI claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Charnina Smith, an African American business owner, sued New Neighborhoods, Inc. and Downtown West Gate Apartments, L.P. alleging race discrimination in connection with her commercial license agreement at 505 West Avenue, Bridgeport, Connecticut. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss all federal claims with prejudice, finding plaintiff's allegations of intentional racial discrimination were wholly conclusory and failed to plausibly allege disparate treatment, disparate impact, hostile housing environment, or retaliation claims under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

source_file: 10672101_2025_Prendergast v. Wode Cooper Companies Atcheson Place Lofts_ohsd
case_name: Carla Prendergast v. Woda Cooper Companies Atcheson Place Lofts, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendant Triumph passed over her Section 8 housing voucher application in favor of other applicants, characterizing this as source-of-income discrimination under the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: Source of income is not a protected characteristic under the Fair Housing Act, which prohibits discrimination only because of race, color, religion, sex, handicap, familial status, or national origin, so plaintiff fails to state an FHA claim.
brief_summary: Carla Prendergast, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued her former and prospective landlords Woda Cooper Companies/Atcheson Place Lofts and Metropolitan Holdings/Triumph Apartments, along with the Legal Aid Society of Columbus and Ohio Adult Parole Authority, alleging Fair Housing Act violations, due process violations under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, and state law claims stemming from her loss of housing. The court dismissed all federal claims, including her FHA source-of-income discrimination claim, because source of income is not a protected characteristic under the FHA, and declined supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims.

case_name: Shawanda V. Collins v. Team Management LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly sought a unit with a bathtub as a disability accommodation, but this claim was raised for the first time at summary judgment and the court refused to consider it.
key_holding: Summary judgment was granted for defendants because Collins voluntarily withdrew her own rental application, and therefore she cannot establish that she was denied housing or subjected to discrimination or retaliation under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Shawanda V. Collins, a disabled African American woman, sued Team Management LLC, Eighteen87 on Water LLC, and Movin' Out Inc. alleging race-based discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act after she voluntarily withdrew her rental application for an apartment complex. The Eastern District of Wisconsin granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that because Collins withdrew her own application, she was not denied housing and cannot establish discrimination or retaliation claims.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Metropolitan Hous. Dev. Corp. v. Village of Arlington Heights, 558 F.2d 1283 (7th Cir. 1977)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n. at Preserve at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

source_file: 10674694_2025_PLACE OF GRACE, INC. v. STATE OF INDIANA_insd
case_name: Place of Grace, Inc. v. State of Indiana
accommodation_description: Place of Grace requested a variance from sprinkler system requirements as a reasonable accommodation from Class 1 building codes for its group recovery homes.
key_holding: Place of Grace lacks standing to pursue its reasonable accommodation claim against IDHS because the injury was caused by the Commission's denial of the variance, not IDHS's referral of the request, and only the Commission can redress the injury by granting the variance.
brief_summary: Place of Grace, a nonprofit operating group recovery homes for individuals with substance use disorders, sued IDHS and the Fire Prevention and Building Safety Commission alleging that IDHS's discriminatory classification of its homes as Class 1 commercial structures and denial of a variance request violated the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court granted partial dismissal, finding that Place of Grace lacks standing to pursue its reasonable accommodation claim against IDHS because IDHS merely referred the variance decision to the Commission, which actually caused the injury, but denied IDHS's motion to dismiss Place of Grace's intentional discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Church of Our Lord & Savior Jesus Christ v. City of Markham, 913 F.3d 670 (7th Cir. 2019)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Fuller v. Christine Apartments LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff challenges eviction proceedings as discriminatory retaliation and fraudulent landlord practices; no specific reasonable accommodation request identified
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint fails to state a plausible FHA claim because it does not allege facts showing plaintiff belongs to a protected class or that defendants' facially neutral practices had a discriminatory impact; the TRO motion was denied because plaintiff cannot show likelihood of success on the merits.
brief_summary: Pro se tenant Thomas Fuller sued Christine Apartments LLC and related defendants alleging fraud and unlawful eviction under the Fair Housing Act, Section 1983, and Section 1985, and sought a temporary restraining order to stop eviction proceedings. The district court denied the TRO motion and dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, holding that Fuller failed to allege sufficient facts establishing that he belongs to a protected class or that defendants' practices had a discriminatory effect, and that the court lacked jurisdiction to review the state court eviction proceedings under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine.

case_name: Gregory Austin Gadson v. Riverbay Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks to prevent eviction, renew his lease, and obtain a certified stock certificate from Riverbay, framing these as remedies for alleged FHA violations based on familial status and disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims for failure to state a claim because plaintiff's factual allegations show Riverbay's adverse actions were motivated solely by non-payment of rent arrears, not by familial status or disability, and plaintiff failed to plead protected activity for retaliation claims.
brief_summary: Gregory Austin Gadson, proceeding pro se, sued Riverbay Corporation (a Mitchell-Lama cooperative housing provider) to prevent his eviction from a Bronx apartment and obtain lease renewal and stock certificate issuance, alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on familial status and disability. The court dismissed the action because Gadson failed to state plausible claims—his allegations show Riverbay's adverse actions were motivated solely by unpaid rent arrears, not protected characteristics, and he failed to plead protected activity for retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Four Keys Leasing & Maint. v. Smithis, 849 F.2d 770 (2d Cir. 1988)
Vega v. Hempstead Union Free Sch. Dist., 801 F.3d 72 (2d Cir. 2015)
Lynn v. Vill. of Pomona, 373 F. Supp. 2d 418 (S.D.N.Y. 2005)

case_name: Luis E. Ogando v. John J. Napolitano, Esq.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff did not request a reasonable accommodation; he alleged various harms arising from a 2006 mortgage transaction, foreclosure, and eviction.
key_holding: The court dismissed the Fair Housing Act claim because Plaintiff failed to allege that Defendants discriminated against him on the basis of any protected characteristic—he merely identified as disabled, Hispanic, and of a particular religion without claiming discrimination based on those traits.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Luis E. Ogando sued private financial institutions, attorneys, and the Supreme Court of New York, challenging a 2006 mortgage transaction, foreclosure, and eviction proceedings in state court and asserting claims under the Fair Housing Act, RESPA, TILA, ECOA, and constitutional law. The court dismissed the case as frivolous and for failure to state a claim, finding that while Plaintiff identified as disabled, Hispanic, and of a particular religion, he did not allege that Defendants discriminated against him on any protected basis, and further lacked subject matter jurisdiction to review the state court proceedings under Rooker-Feldman.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus.
Mireles v. Waco
Hoblock v. Albany County Bd. of Elections

case_name: McKay Burley and Tyson Thomas v. Charles J. Chapman
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case involves neighbor harassment based on sexual orientation and sex, not a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court denied default judgment on plaintiffs' FHA discriminatory statements claim (§ 3604(c)) because defendant's alleged statements were not made 'with respect to the sale or rental of a dwelling,' and denied default judgment on the § 3617 interference claim because defendant is merely a neighbor whose conduct may not implicate FHA-protected housing rights; however, the court granted default judgment on the state IIED claim, finding plaintiffs alleged extreme and outrageous conduct sufficient to state that claim.
brief_summary: Two gay men sued their next-door neighbor for discriminatory harassment including anti-gay slurs and threats of violence, asserting claims under the Fair Housing Act for discriminatory statements (§ 3604(c)) and interference (§ 3617), as well as state law claims. The court granted default judgment on the state intentional infliction of emotional distress claim, finding extreme and outrageous conduct, but denied default judgment on the FHA claims, holding that the discriminatory statements claim failed because the neighbor's statements were not made in connection with the sale or rental of a dwelling, and that the interference claim failed because the alleged conduct did not sufficiently implicate FHA-protected housing rights.
key_cases_cited: Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)
Birch Fam. Servs., Inc. v. Wlody, No. 19-cv-03301, 2021 WL 2312852 (E.D.N.Y. June 7, 2021)
Henry v. Oluwole, 108 F.4th 45 (2d Cir. 2024)
Moore v. Booth, 122 F.4th 61 (2d Cir. 2024)
Bostock v. Clayton Cnty., 590 U.S. 644 (2020)

case_name: Larry Johnson v. Lisa Simonson and Kelly Paine
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a wheelchair accessible parking space with signage at Uptown Tower Apartments.
key_holding: Plaintiff's retaliation claims failed because he could not establish causation between protected activity and adverse actions, and his parking accommodation claim failed because he previously agreed in a settlement that the commercial parking space provided by defendants met his accessibility needs.
brief_summary: Larry Johnson, a pro se tenant at Uptown Tower Apartments (a HUD Section 8 property managed by Guardian Management), sued property managers Lisa Simonson and Kelly Paine alleging Fair Housing Act retaliation under § 3617 and reasonable accommodation claims for accessible parking and paperwork delivery procedures. The court granted defendants' motion for summary judgment on all claims, finding no evidence of causation for the retaliation claims and concluding that the parking accommodation was resolved by a prior settlement agreement.
key_cases_cited: Brown v. City of Tucson, 336 F.3d 1181 (9th Cir. 2003)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Owens v. Kaiser Found. Health Plan, Inc., 244 F.3d 708 (9th Cir. 2001)
Johnson v. Brenneke, No. 3:21-cv-00582-JR, 2022 WL 981295 (D. Or. 2022)

case_name: Amy Burnett v. Rutledge Flats, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, alleges that Defendants failed to design and construct the 174-unit apartment complex in compliance with FHA accessibility and usability requirements for covered multifamily dwellings.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff sufficiently alleged Article III standing as an individual plaintiff who was deterred from renting due to accessibility barriers, and that Plaintiff plausibly alleged FHA design-and-construction violations against all Defendants including the architect.
brief_summary: Amy Burnett, a wheelchair user, sued Rutledge Flats LLC and related development entities alleging the 174-unit Nashville apartment complex was designed and constructed in violation of FHA accessibility requirements, deterring her from renting. The court denied Defendants' motions to dismiss for lack of standing and failure to state a claim, finding Plaintiff sufficiently alleged injury-in-fact and plausibly pleaded design-and-construction violations against all Defendants including the architect.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Food & Drug Admin. v. Alliance for Hippocratic Med., 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Chapuis v. Forest Hill Grp. LLC, No. 2:24-CV-2525-SHL-TMP, 2025 WL 2382068 (W.D. Tenn. Aug. 15, 2025)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 10687804_2025_Be the Bush Recovery Ministries v. Coffee Cnty., Tenn._ca6
case_name: Be the Bush Recovery Ministries v. Coffee County, Tennessee
accommodation_description: BTB requested permission to operate its residential addiction recovery program in an RS-1 Low Density Residential zone through a zoning amendment or special exception.
key_holding: The RS-1 zoning ordinance is not facially discriminatory because it applies a neutral distinction between single-family dwellings and other residential uses rather than singling out group homes for the disabled, and BTB failed to establish standing for injunctive relief due to speculative future plans.
brief_summary: Be the Bush Recovery Ministries, a faith-based addiction recovery program, sued Coffee County, Tennessee after the County denied its requests to operate a residential rehabilitation facility in an RS-1 residential zone. The Sixth Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment for the County, holding that the zoning ordinance was not facially discriminatory because it treated all non-family residential uses equally rather than singling out disabled persons, and that BTB lacked standing for equitable relief due to speculative future plans to return to the County.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Smith & Lee Assocs., Inc. v. City of Taylor, 13 F.3d 920 (6th Cir. 1993)
MX Group, Inc. v. City of Covington, 293 F.3d 326 (6th Cir. 2002)
Larkin v. Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Livingston Christian Schs. v. Genoa Charter Township, 858 F.3d 996 (6th Cir. 2017)

case_name: Stephany Webster v. Fairway Management, Inc., Walnut Park Manor, L.P., JES Holdings, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to complete income recertification after the deadline and to have her counsel assist her with the recertification process as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to defendants on plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), finding that plaintiff failed to establish she was disabled or that defendants denied a requested accommodation, noting defendants granted several accommodation requests and ultimately resolved the recertification dispute in December 2020.
brief_summary: Stephany Webster, a resident of Walnut Park Manor senior apartment complex in Sapulpa, Oklahoma (a LIHTC/LOW-Income Housing Tax Credit property managed by Fairway Management), sued the defendants claiming disability discrimination, sex discrimination, and retaliation under the FHA, alleging defendants failed to grant reasonable accommodations, made housing unavailable based on disability and sex, and retaliated against her for filing a 2019 lawsuit. The court granted summary judgment to defendants on the disability and sex discrimination claims but denied summary judgment on the retaliation claim based on plaintiff's evidence that defendants refused to accept her rent checks shortly after she filed suit, allowing that claim to proceed.

case_name: Rebecca Armour, et al. v. Jeremy Gold, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; plaintiffs alleged neighbors harassed them, made false reports to authorities, stalked them, and defamed them.
key_holding: The court dismissed the Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiffs failed to allege they attempted to rent or purchase property from defendants or that any discrimination was based on the protected statuses enumerated in 42 U.S.C. § 3604.
brief_summary: Self-represented plaintiffs Rebecca and Stefan Armour sued their neighbors Jeremy and Danielle Gold under the Fair Housing Act, 42 U.S.C. § 1983, and state law claims alleging harassment, false reports to authorities, stalking, and defamation. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice, finding that plaintiffs failed to adequately plead a Fair Housing Act claim because they did not allege attempts to rent or purchase housing from defendants or any discrimination based on protected statuses.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Shanaghan v. Cahill
Filarsky v. Delia
West v. Atkins

source_file: 10691139_2025_Murphy v. Villages at Noah's Landing, LTD_flmd
case_name: Murphy v. Villages at Noah's Landing, Ltd.
accommodation_description: Residents with intellectual and developmental disabilities requested modification of the Villages' zero-tolerance policy for rule violations as a reasonable accommodation for their disabilities, and requested that guardians be permitted to attend meetings and visit the facility to assist residents in decision-making.
key_holding: Count IV, alleging a failure to accommodate under § 3604(f)(3)(B) by refusing to modify zero-tolerance rules and denying guardian access, survived the motion to dismiss because the complaint sufficiently alleged defendants had actual knowledge of the residents' disabilities and that accommodations were necessary, though the overall complaint was dismissed as an impermissible shotgun pleading.
brief_summary: Parents of adult children with intellectual and developmental disabilities residing at the Villages at Noah's Landing sued the property owners, operators, and management company alleging Fair Housing Act violations, including that defendants maintained a zero-tolerance rule enforcement policy and refused to grant reasonable accommodations for residents' disabilities. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss the complaint as a shotgun pleading but denied the motion to dismiss Count IV, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged a plausible reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Hawn v. Shoreline Towers Phase 1 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 347 F. App'x 464 (11th Cir. 2009)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Weiland v. Palm Beach Cty. Sheriff's Office, 792 F.3d 1313 (11th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Pamela Crawford v. Douglas M. Milner et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges disparate treatment in housing refusal based on race.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA disparate treatment claim for failing to plausibly allege discriminatory intent, noting plaintiff failed to show she was qualified for the rental unit, that someone of a different race received the unit, or that defendants continued seeking applicants after rejecting her.
brief_summary: Pamela Crawford, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Douglas and Michelle Milner alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to rent her a dwelling based on her race. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a plausible disparate treatment claim, finding plaintiff failed to allege she was qualified for the unit, identify who received it, or show discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)
SW. Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Maria Elena Ochoa, Shaina Ricks, Mary Ricks, and Fair Housing Council of South Texas v. D.R. Horton, Inc., and Continental Homes of Texas, L.P.
accommodation_description: Individual homebuyers with mobility-disabled family members requested that the homebuilder permit physical modifications during construction at the purchasers' expense, including walk-in showers, widened doorways, grab bars, ramps, and accessible fixtures.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' reasonable modification claims under § 3604(f)(3)(A) because the FHA's 'existing premises' requirement does not apply to single-family homes still under construction, but allowed reasonable accommodation claims under § 3604(f)(3)(B) to proceed, treating the builder's blanket no-modification policy as a potential accommodation failure.
brief_summary: Individual homebuyers and the Fair Housing Council of South Texas sued D.R. Horton and its affiliate, alleging the homebuilder discriminated against disabled buyers by refusing to permit physical accessibility modifications during construction of single-family homes. The district court granted in part defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing the reasonable modification claims under § 3604(f)(3)(A) on the grounds that homes under construction are not "existing premises," but denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claims under § 3604(f)(3)(B), allowing those claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
C.R. v. PLB Mgmt. LLC, No. 2:21-cv-03275-ODW(JEMx), 2023 WL 2167368 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 22, 2023)

source_file: 10691311_2025_(PS) Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento_caed
case_name: Mekal Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested expedited processing of his Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application as a reasonable accommodation for his Multiple Myeloma cancer disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because his allegations that defendants discriminated against him due to disability were conclusory, containing only speculation without sufficient factual content to plausibly infer discrimination.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mekal Faruki, an elderly individual with Multiple Myeloma cancer, sued the Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento and related entities alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and ADA, claiming his Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application was lost and he was denied reasonable accommodation through expedited processing while similarly-situated applicants received vouchers. The court dismissed the First Amended Complaint for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiff's allegations that discrimination occurred because of disability were conclusory and speculative without sufficient factual detail to state a plausible claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lovell v. Chandler, 303 F.3d 1039 (9th Cir. 2002)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 10693018_2025_Rousseaux Castellanos v. Manzano Mesa Limited Partnership_nmd
case_name: Tekisha Rousseau Castellanos v. Manzano Mesa Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint does not clearly specify the accommodation requested; court found complaint fails to allege any specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims fail because the complaint does not allege defendants treated plaintiff differently from non-disabled tenants, and ordered plaintiff to file an amended complaint within 21 days.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Tekisha Rousseau Castellanos sued Manzano Mesa Limited Partnership, Guardian Management, LLC, and Bernalillo County Housing Department alleging Fair Housing Act, ADA, Section 504, and § 1983 claims related to unsafe housing conditions. The court granted IFP status but found the complaint fails to state valid discrimination claims because it lacks allegations showing plaintiff was treated differently from non-disabled tenants, and ordered plaintiff to file an amended complaint within 21 days.

source_file: 10693710_2022_Margaret Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust et al._nhd
case_name: Margaret Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust et al.
accommodation_description: Request for assistance from MHRA to terminate Section 8 lease early and transfer to another apartment in spring 2018 to avoid pesticide exposure harmful to her COPD and emotional support animal (cat)
key_holding: A four-month delay in processing a reasonable accommodation request does not constitute constructive denial where the delay was caused by procedural missteps, failure to apply proper policies, and lack of adequate training, rather than by willful intent to delay, bad faith, or obstructionism.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Margaret Kris, a Section 8 voucher holder with COPD who uses an emotional support animal, sought to add the Manchester Housing and Redevelopment Authority as a defendant to assert reasonable accommodation claims for assistance in breaking her lease early to avoid pesticide exposure. The court denied Kris's motion to amend, holding that even assuming she stated a prima facie accommodation claim, the MHRA's four-month delay in processing her request did not constitute constructive denial because the delay was attributable to procedural missteps and inadequate training, not bad faith or willful obstruction.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, HUD, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Valle-Arce v. P.R. Ports Auth., 651 F.3d 190 (1st Cir. 2011)
Logan v. Matveevskii, 57 F. Supp. 3d 234 (S.D.N.Y. 2014)
McCray v. Wilkie, 966 F.3d 616 (7th Cir. 2020)

source_file: 10694022_2023_Brookline Opportunities, LLC et al. v. Town of Brookline et al._nhd
case_name: Brookline Opportunities, LLC et al. v. Town of Brookline et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; developers challenge municipal zoning ordinances (moratorium and growth management ordinance) as discriminatory under § 3604(a)-(c) based on familial status, race, and national origin.
key_holding: Cross-motions for summary judgment denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the Town adopted the challenged ordinances with discriminatory intent against families with children, and the ordinances are facially neutral rather than facially discriminatory.
brief_summary: Brookline Opportunities, LLC and Tamposi Brothers Holdings sued the Town of Brookline, New Hampshire alleging the Town violated the Fair Housing Act by adopting a building moratorium and growth management ordinance that discriminate against families with children, Blacks, and Latinos. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to discriminatory intent and that the ordinances are facially neutral rather than facially discriminatory.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10694556_2022_Margaret Kris v. P Dusseault Family Revocable Trust et al._nhd
case_name: Margaret Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust et al.
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support cat despite building policies, citing disability-related need for the animal and concerns that chemical extermination treatments could harm the cat and aggravate her COPD.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion for summary judgment on her FHA retaliation claims because she failed to establish that protected activity was the but-for cause of her eviction and other adverse actions, noting that the state court eviction judgment had preclusive effect and that defendants offered legitimate, non-retaliatory reasons for their conduct.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Margaret Kris, a disabled Section 8 voucher recipient in Manchester, NH, sued her landlords (the Dusseault Family Revocable Trust and individual Dusseaults) alleging FHA retaliation claims based on an alleged verbal and physical assault, failure to address maintenance requests, eviction, and retention of her security deposit. The court denied plaintiff's motion for summary judgment, finding that she failed to establish but-for causation linking her protected activity to the alleged adverse actions, and that the state court eviction judgment had preclusive effect on her eviction claim.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of Afr. Am.-Owned Media
Taylor v. Nat'l Invs., Ltd.
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
Palmquist v. Shinseki

source_file: 10696502_2017_Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Assoc., et al._nhd
case_name: Sanjeev Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association, et al.
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it is a hostile housing environment claim under § 3604(b) based on alleged assault and harassment motivated by plaintiff's sexual orientation, race, and national origin.
key_holding: Plaintiff's hostile housing environment claim under § 3604(b) was barred by the two-year statute of limitations because the alleged discriminatory confrontation occurred on June 8, 2014, but the claim was not filed until November 2016, and plaintiff failed to establish that the continuing violation doctrine, statutory tolling, or equitable tolling applied.
brief_summary: Sanjeev Lath, a unit owner, sued the Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association and its board president alleging a hostile housing environment claim under § 3604(b) based on a June 2014 altercation where he was called discriminatory slurs motivated by his sexual orientation and national origin. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding the claim time-barred by the two-year FHA statute of limitations since it was filed more than two years after the alleged incident, and plaintiff failed to establish tolling through the state human rights commission, continuing violation doctrine, or equitable tolling.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Burnett v. New York Central Railroad Co., 380 U.S. 424 (1965)
Omar v. Lynch, 814 F.3d 565 (1st Cir. 2016)
Rivera-Diaz v. Humana Ins. of P.R., Inc., 748 F.3d 387 (1st Cir. 2014)
Dickinson v. Zanesville Metro. Hous. Auth., 975 F. Supp. 2d 863 (S.D. Ohio 2013)

source_file: 10696687_2019_Scott Chandler v. Chartwell Holdings, Inc., et al._nhd
case_name: Scott Chandler v. Chartwell Holdings, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly needed a quiet apartment to protect his mental health and requested that the landlord address noisy conditions caused by a neighbor's air conditioner.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because he failed to allege that he requested a specific accommodation linked to his disability, and his general housing maintenance complaints were not plausibly connected to disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Scott Chandler sued his landlord (Chartwell Holdings/Fairways Apartments) and the Derry, New Hampshire Police Department, alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and constitutional violations. The court dismissed Chandler's FHA reasonable accommodation claim without prejudice, finding he failed to allege that he requested a specific accommodation linked to his traumatic brain injury disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Batista v. Cooperativa de Vivienda Jardines de San Ignacio, 776 F.3d 38 (1st Cir. 2015)
Colon-Jimenez v. GR Mgmt. Corp., 218 F. App'x 2 (1st Cir. 2007)
Palencar v. Rajiski, No. 15-cv-1189 (M.D. Pa. 2016)

source_file: 10697217_2017_Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners Assoc., et al._nhd
case_name: Sanjeev Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association, et al.
accommodation_description: Request for handicap-accessible parking spaces compliant with ANSI 117.1 standards and waiver of fees for carport deed transfers for residents with walking disabilities.
key_holding: Plaintiff Lath lacks standing to assert FHA claims for handicap-accessible parking because he does not allege any physical handicap of his own, and he cannot assert claims on behalf of non-party Barbara Belware; Lath's retaliation claims fail because he alleges no discriminatory animus on the part of defendants who engaged in the alleged retaliatory conduct.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sanjeev Lath sued Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association and individual board members alleging, inter alia, that the association violated the FHA by charging fees for accessible parking and failing to provide ANSI-compliant handicap parking spaces. The court dismissed Count 5 (FHA accommodation claim) for lack of standing because Lath did not allege he himself had a physical disability, and dismissed Counts 3(a)-(i) (retaliation) and Count 14 (civil conspiracy) for failure to allege discriminatory animus.
key_cases_cited: Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
SBA List v. Driehaus, 134 S. Ct. 2334 (2014)
Reddy v. Foster, 845 F.3d 493 (1st Cir. 2017)
Fahad Jafri v. 203 Sundown Lake Estates Condo. Ass'n, 970 F. Supp. 2d 856 (N.D. Ill. 2013)

source_file: 10698519_2017_Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association, et al._nhd
case_name: Sanjeev Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association, et al.
