accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep an emotional support dog as an accommodation for his mental handicap, seeking an exception to the Association's "no dog" policy.
key_holding: The court allowed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim to proceed against the Association, finding that he stated a claim under § 3604(f)(2)(A) for constructive failure to accommodate his emotional support dog request, and noted that a request may be constructively denied by unnecessary delay in responding.
brief_summary: Sanjeev Lath, a unit owner at Oak Brook Condominiums, sued the Association and individual board members asserting multiple federal and state claims including a reasonable accommodation claim under the Fair Housing Act for an emotional support dog. The court granted plaintiff's motion for leave to amend, dismissed numerous claims for failure to state a claim or lack of supplemental jurisdiction, but allowed the reasonable accommodation claim (Count 2) and several others to proceed against the Association.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, HUD, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Hagerty ex rel. U.S. v. Cyberonics, Inc., 844 F.3d 26 (1st Cir. 2016)
United States v. Town of Garner, 720 F. Supp. 2d 721 (E.D.N.C. 2010)

case_name: United States of America v. Avatar Properties, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request to use visitor parking area in front of Unit 5 instead of assigned parking behind the unit, to avoid stairs due to mobility disability
key_holding: The court held that the FHA applies to post-acquisition discrimination claims, rejecting defendants' argument that 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(2) does not protect property owners from discrimination occurring after they have acquired their dwelling.
brief_summary: The United States sued Avatar Properties and Midridge Condominium Association on behalf of Edward Tirrell, a mobility-impaired resident, alleging defendants violated the FHA by denying his request to park in visitor parking closer to his unit instead of assigned parking requiring him to climb stairs. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that the FHA applies to post-acquisition discrimination claims against property owners.
key_cases_cited: Committee Concerning Community Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)
Astralis Condominium Ass'n v. Secretary, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 10699016_2018_Sanjeev Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners’ Association, Gerard Dufresne, and Betty Mullen_nhd
case_name: Sanjeev Lath v. Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association, Gerard Dufresne, and Betty Mullen
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for disability in a no-pet condominium community.
key_holding: The court denied Lath's motions to amend his complaint because the proposed new claims (invasion of privacy, defamation, and conspiracy to violate the FHA) would be futile, as the disclosures and statements underlying those claims were absolutely privileged under the litigation privilege and failed to state valid causes of action.
brief_summary: Sanjeev Lath, proceeding pro se, filed motions for leave to amend his complaint to add claims for invasion of privacy, defamation, and conspiracy to violate the Fair Housing Act against the Oak Brook Condominium Owners' Association and individual defendants. The court denied both motions, finding the proposed claims futile because the underlying disclosures and statements were absolutely privileged as statements made in judicial proceedings and failed to state valid causes of action. The court noted that Lath's existing reasonable accommodation claim (Count 2) for an emotional support dog and discriminatory advertising claim (Count 4) remain pending in the case.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Foman v. Davis
Provencher v. Buzzell-Plourde Assocs.
McGranahan v. Dahar

case_name: Mariama Moore v. Model Cities and Safe Space Shelter
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disparate treatment and retaliation based on race, sex, sexual orientation, and other characteristics by shelter staff.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a plausible FHA claim because plaintiff offered only conclusory allegations without specific facts showing she was treated less favorably based on protected status, and the FHA does not create a cause of action against housing providers for the discriminatory conduct of other residents.
brief_summary: Mariama Moore, experiencing homelessness, sued Model Cities and Safe Space Shelter alleging Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claims based on race, sex, and sexual orientation, claiming she was harassed by other residents and retaliated against by staff. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations without specific facts showing discriminatory treatment by the defendants, and dismissed the case without prejudice.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10703960_2025_Lance Frazier v. Justin Luther, individually and d_b_a Luther Properties, LLC; Phillip Kennedy; and Angie Kennedy_msnd
case_name: Lance Frazier v. Justin Luther, individually and d/b/a Luther Properties, LLC; Phillip Kennedy; and Angie Kennedy
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in housing—defendant filed eviction and police report, changed locks, and made racially derogatory statement 'ain't no black man going to owe me money'—not a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims under §§ 3604(a)-(b) for racial discrimination survive the motion to dismiss, but his negligent infliction of emotional distress and corporate veil piercing claims are dismissed, and plaintiff's damage claims are limited to the period between September 2-13, 2022.
brief_summary: Lance Frazier, a Black tenant in Pontotoc, Mississippi, sued his landlord Justin Luther and Luther Properties LLC for Fair Housing Act violations, alleging racial discrimination based on defendant's statement 'ain't no black man going to owe me money,' filing of an eviction, and changing locks on the rental property. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss the FHA race discrimination claims and motion to strike, but dismissed the negligent infliction of emotional distress and corporate veil piercing claims, and limited plaintiff's damages to September 2-13, 2022.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Land and Bay Gauging, LLC v. Shor, 623 F. App'x 674 (5th Cir. 2015)
Bolton v. John Lee, P.A., 405 So.3d 1 (Miss. Ct. App. 2023)
U.S. v. Coney, 689 F.3d 365 (5th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 10704025_2025_Jasmine Hill v. Philadelphia Housing Authority, et al._paed
case_name: Jasmine Hill v. Philadelphia Housing Authority, et al.
accommodation_description: Hill seeks emergency housing voucher or hotel placement, safety inspections, and prompt communication regarding alternative housing due to alleged substandard conditions (lead paint, gas line failures) at her Section 8 properties, but the court finds no plausible connection to protected class discrimination or retaliation.
key_holding: The court dismissed Hill's FHA claims because her Amended Complaint failed to allege facts plausibly connecting the hazardous housing conditions she experienced to her membership in a protected class or to any protected activity, as required to state intentional discrimination or retaliation claims under the FHA.
brief_summary: Jasmine Hill, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued the Philadelphia Housing Authority and HUD alleging that hazardous housing conditions at two properties (lead paint exposure, gas line failures) violated the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed her Amended Complaint for failure to state a claim, finding she failed to allege facts showing the conditions were caused by discrimination based on a protected class or in retaliation for protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
Davis v. Rubin, 2022 WL 3037254 (3d Cir. Aug. 2, 2022)
Henry v. City of Erie, 728 F.3d 275 (3d Cir. 2013)

source_file: 10705421_2025_National Fair Housing Alliance, et al. v. Bank of America, N.A., et al._mdd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance, et al. v. Bank of America, N.A., et al.
race_if_mentioned: African American (implied by pattern of REO maintenance disparities and fair housing testing claims)
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it concerns alleged discrimination in maintenance and marketing of REO (real estate owned) properties based on race.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiffs' motion for leave to amend as both prejudicial and futile because the proposed amendments addressed only standing deficiencies but would not cure the step-three summary judgment defects in the underlying discrimination claims.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance and individual homeowners sued Bank of America and its property servicer alleging discrimination in maintenance and marketing of real estate owned properties in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied Plaintiffs' motion for leave to amend their complaint as futile because the proposed amendments only addressed organizational standing deficiencies but would not remedy the step-three summary judgment ruling already entered against the discrimination claims.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Carnegie-Mellon Univ. v. Cohill, 484 U.S. 343 (1988)

source_file: 10705497_2025_Abraham Shitrai Israel and Sarah Iris Israel v. Barbara Guinn, et al._nynd
case_name: Abraham Shitrai Israel and Sarah Iris Israel v. Barbara Guinn, et al.
accommodation_description: Pro se disabled plaintiffs allege they were denied emergency public housing assistance and funds for home repairs (electrical and water pipe) from the Onondaga County Department of Social Services.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint was devoid of allegations of discrimination based on a protected characteristic; the court also dismissed the Eighth Amendment claim with prejudice and gave Plaintiffs leave to amend the complaint within thirty days.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Abraham Shitrai Israel and Sarah Iris Israel, a pro se disabled married couple, sued state and county officials including the New York State Office of Temporary Disability Assistance and Onondaga County DSS workers, alleging violations of constitutional rights and the Fair Housing Act after being denied emergency public assistance for housing repairs and temporary shelter. The court adopted in part and rejected in part the Magistrate Judge's Report-Recommendation, dismissing the FHA claim without prejudice for failure to allege discrimination based on a protected characteristic, dismissing the Eighth Amendment claim with prejudice, and granting Plaintiffs leave to amend the complaint within thirty days.

source_file: 10705595_2025_RoSHIEKA CHARISMA JONES v. THOMPSON TOWERS APARTMENTS_mied
case_name: RoShieka Charisma Jones v. Thompson Towers Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint focuses on lack of hot water and an eviction notice but does not articulate a specific disability-related accommodation request; court found she failed to allege elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint for failure to state a claim because while she receives SSDI (suggesting disability) and references the Fair Housing Act, she failed to allege facts supporting the elements of a reasonable accommodation claim, and the court lacked jurisdiction to enjoin state court eviction proceedings.
brief_summary: RoShieka Charisma Jones, a pro se plaintiff receiving SSDI, filed suit against Thompson Towers Apartments, a Section 8 housing development, alleging habitability violations (lack of hot water, pest control issues) and seeking to stop eviction proceedings. The court dismissed her complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) for failure to state a claim because she failed to allege facts sufficient to establish the elements of a reasonable accommodation claim under the Fair Housing Act, and the court lacked jurisdiction to enjoin state court eviction proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 10706118_2025_Seyed Alireza Amidi Namin v. Kevin Jeffrey Vert_utd
case_name: Seyed Alireza Amidi Namin v. Kevin Jeffrey Vert
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; case involves alleged real estate fraud and forged property ownership documents
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604 because Plaintiff failed to allege that Defendant made the rental property unavailable to him because of a protected characteristic under § 3604(a).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Seyed Alireza Amidi Namin sued Defendant Kevin Jeffrey Vert alleging real estate fraud, constitutional violations, and Fair Housing Act claims based on allegedly forged property ownership documents used to prevent him from renting property in California. The court denied Plaintiff's Motions to Proceed IFP and for Temporary Restraining Order and dismissed the entire case for failure to state a viable legal claim, including dismissing the § 3604 claim because Plaintiff failed to allege discriminatory intent based on a protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Buchheit v. Green, 705 F.3d 1157 (10th Cir. 2012)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Kay v. Bemis, 500 F.3d 1214 (10th Cir. 2007)
United States v. Claflin, 97 U.S. 546 (1878)
Shaw v. Neece, 727 F.2d 947 (10th Cir. 1984)

source_file: 10706179_2025_Barry Glazer v. Meta Platforms, Inc., et al._mdd
10706197_2025_MARGARET MARCELUS BENS v. BRYTEN REAL ESTATE PARTNERS, LLC, and COLLIER COUNTY HOUSING AUTHORITY_flmd
case_name: Margaret Marcelus Bens v. Bryten Real Estate Partners, LLC, and Collier County Housing Authority
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request is articulated; the complaint alleges housing discrimination based on race and Section 8 status, including manipulated rent amounts, improper eviction proceedings, and falsely reported credit.
key_holding: The complaint is dismissed as a shotgun pleading for failing to give defendants adequate notice of claims, failing to separate causes of action, and failing to specify which defendant is responsible for which acts; the Housing Authority's motion is granted because the complaint failed to state any cognizable claim against it.
brief_summary: Margaret Marcelus Bens, a Black woman and Section 8 tenant, sued Bryten Real Estate Partners (property manager) and Collier County Housing Authority alleging housing discrimination, retaliation, equal protection violation, FCRA violation, and defamation related to an eviction proceeding and allegedly manipulated rent amounts. The Court granted both motions to dismiss, finding the complaint is an obvious shotgun pleading that fails to state any cognizable claim and giving plaintiff fourteen days to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Weiland v. Palm Beach Cnty. Sheriff's Off.
Efron v. Candelario
Behr v. Campbell

source_file: 10707586_2025_Daniel Maher and Colin’s Place LLC v. White Bear Township, a Minnesota Municipal Corporation_mnd
case_name: Daniel Maher and Colin's Place LLC v. White Bear Township, a Minnesota Municipal Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought a zoning ordinance amendment to increase the maximum occupancy of their sober house from five to ten unrelated individuals, framed as a reasonable accommodation for residents with substance use disorders.
key_holding: The Township's denial of the zoning amendment request did not violate the FHA or ADA because the Township engaged with the accommodation request, suggested a duplex alternative, and plaintiffs failed to demonstrate that expanding occupancy from five to ten residents was necessary for the sober house to fulfill its purpose.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Daniel Maher and Colin's Place LLC, operators of a sober living facility in White Bear Township, Minnesota, sued the Township after it denied their request to amend the zoning ordinance to allow up to ten residents instead of the permitted five. The court granted summary judgment for the Township on both the disparate treatment and reasonable accommodation claims, finding no evidence of discriminatory intent and concluding that plaintiffs failed to demonstrate the requested occupancy expansion was necessary for the facility's purpose.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
One Love Hous., LLC v. City of Anoka, 2021 WL 3774567 (D. Minn. 2021)
Meraki Recovery Hous., LLC v. City of Coon Rapids, 2021 WL 5567898 (D. Minn. 2021)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Peebles v. Potter, 354 F.3d 761 (8th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 10707647_2025_Katie M. Kononen v. City of Salem Housing Authority_ord
case_name: Katie M. Kononen v. City of Salem Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested approval for an additional service dog as a reasonable accommodation, which was delayed approximately three months before approval in January 2021.
key_holding: Summary judgment was denied on disparate treatment claims for requiring communication via third party, selective parking enforcement, and fence removal based on evidence of discriminatory animus by SHA employees; summary judgment was granted on failure to accommodate (second parking spot not disability-related), smoking policy enforcement, maintenance delays, and retaliation claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Katie Kononen, a Native American tenant with disabilities at Glen Creek Village (a public housing complex operated by the City of Salem Housing Authority), sued alleging disability and race discrimination under the FHA and ADA, claiming she was treated differently than other tenants regarding accommodation requests, communication requirements, parking enforcement, and fence removal. The court granted in part and denied in part defendant's summary judgment motion, allowing claims based on differential treatment with discriminatory animus to proceed while dismissing claims lacking evidence of connection to protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Merrick v. Farmers Ins. Grp., 892 F.2d 1434 (9th Cir. 1990)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10708624_2025_CHARLESETTA MOORE, SR. v. C.F.Y. DEVELOPMENT INC., et al._caed
case_name: Charlesetta Moore, Sr. v. C.F.Y. Development Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discriminatory treatment including use of a racial slur by site manager, repeated deactivation of key card, unauthorized entry into apartment, and failure to return full security deposit.
key_holding: The SAC fails to state an FHA claim because a single instance of a defendant using a racial slur is insufficient to establish a violation, and plaintiff's other allegations about poor housing conditions and deposit disputes do not suggest discrimination was a motivating factor.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Charlesetta Moore, proceeding pro se, sued C.F.Y. Development Inc. and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on discriminatory treatment including use of a racial slur, key card deactivation, unauthorized entry, and deposit disputes at Galt Place Senior Apartments. The magistrate judge recommended dismissal without leave to amend, finding the allegations insufficient to state a disparate treatment claim under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: SW. Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)
Scoggins v. Turning Point of Cent. California, Inc., No. 1:22-CV-01542-EPG, 2023 WL 2354820 (E.D. Cal. Mar. 3, 2023)
Fried v. Wynn Las Vegas, LLC, 18 F.4th 643 (9th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 10709344_2025_Michael Green v. Hearth Initiative Corporation, et al._wied
case_name: Michael Green v. Hearth Initiative Corporation, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was evicted from an unlicensed assisted living facility based on race and perceived identity in violation of the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because he failed to identify his own race in the complaint, failed to explain what 'perceived identity' constitutes as a protected class, and failed to allege facts showing discriminatory intent or that similarly situated individuals of a different race were treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michael Green, proceeding pro se, sued Hearth Initiative Corporation and other defendants alleging he was racially discriminated against and unlawfully evicted from an unlicensed assisted living facility in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. The court screened the complaint under 28 U.S.C. §1915A and dismissed all federal claims without prejudice, finding that the FHA claim failed because plaintiff did not identify his own race, did not explain what his 'perceived identity' protected class was, and failed to allege facts showing discriminatory intent or differential treatment of similarly situated individuals.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 10710210_2025_Johnny Moffett v. Columbia Manor et al._wawd
case_name: Johnny Moffett v. Columbia Manor et al.
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request; plaintiff alleges race-based housing discrimination and requested that a neighbor be moved to a different apartment
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to plead facts supporting an inference of race-based discrimination or any connection between alleged stalking by a neighbor and discriminatory conduct by defendants.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Johnny Moffett sued Columbia Manor/Smith Towers apartment complex and individual defendants alleging race-based discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming management hindered his work, made him fear for his safety, and engaged in racism, and that a neighbor was stalking him. The court dismissed the complaint sua sponte under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint lacked factual allegations connecting any discriminatory conduct to the defendants or supporting an inference of FHA-prohibited discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Sw. Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Watison v. Carter, 668 F.3d 1108 (9th Cir. 2012)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 10710297_2025_Randy Dewayne Pittman v. Landsphere Property Management, et al._cand
case_name: Randy Dewayne Pittman v. Landsphere Property Management, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested defendants to construct a van- and handicap-accessible parking space with striping and signage and designate it for his exclusive use near his apartment entrance.
key_holding: The court denied preliminary injunction because constructing a new accessible parking space is not a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) (which covers accommodations to rules, policies, practices, or services) but is instead a reasonable modification under § 3604(f)(3)(A), which requires modifications to be at the tenant's expense, and plaintiff did not allege defendants refused to permit such a modification at his own expense.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Randy Pittman sued his property management company seeking a preliminary injunction to compel creation of a new van-accessible parking space for his exclusive use at his apartment complex. The court denied the motion and dismissed his FHA complaint with leave to amend, holding that constructing a new parking space is not an accommodation to an existing rule or policy under § 3604(f)(3)(B) but rather a physical modification under § 3604(f)(3)(A), which would require plaintiff to bear the cost himself.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
71Five Ministries v. Williams, 2025 WL 2385151 (9th Cir. Aug. 18, 2025)
S. Cal. Hous. Rts. Ctr. v. Los Feliz Towers Homeowners Ass'n, 426 F. Supp. 2d 1061 (C.D. Cal. 2005)

source_file: 10712458_2025_Oxford House Inc v. Township of North Bergen_ca3
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House, a sober living facility operator, seeks to operate a group home for recovering substance abusers in a two-family dwelling, challenging the Township's denial of a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy based on zoning classification as a 'Community Residence.'
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal for failure to state a claim but vacated the district court's denial of leave to amend, holding that dismissal should have been without prejudice because the prior preliminary injunction denial addressed evidentiary sufficiency under a different standard than pleading requirements, and the proposed second amended complaint was merely a redline rather than a substantive revision.
brief_summary: Oxford House, a non-profit operator of sober living homes for recovering substance abusers, sued the Township of North Bergen after the Township denied its application for a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy for a two-family dwelling, claiming the zoning ordinance prohibited 'Community Residences' in such dwellings. The Third Circuit affirmed the dismissal of the complaint for failure to plead sufficient facts to infer discriminatory intent but vacated the district court's denial of leave to amend, finding the dismissal should have been without prejudice because the prior preliminary injunction denial addressed different legal standards and Oxford House had not yet been properly notified of pleading deficiencies.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. and Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 10713560_2025_Phillip Pella v. Atlantic County Department of Family and Community Development, New Jersey Department of Human Services, Helen Cheatham, Audrey Alphaeus, John_Jane Does 1-10_njd
case_name: Phillip Pella v. Atlantic County Department of Family and Community Development, New Jersey Department of Human Services, Helen Cheatham, Audrey Alphaeus, John/Jane Does 1-10
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a pro se litigant proceeding without a clear housing accommodation request, alleges wrongful denial of New Jersey Emergency Assistance and SNAP benefits as a person with disabilities.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's IFP application without prejudice for insufficient financial information and cautioned that the complaint may be dismissed for failure to state a claim because it is vague, ambiguous, and conclusory.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Phillip Pella, proceeding pro se and allegedly homeless with mental health disabilities, sued Atlantic County and New Jersey state agencies and officials alleging violations of the ADA, FHA, Rehabilitation Act, and constitutional rights based on denial of Emergency Assistance and SNAP benefits. The Court dismissed his in forma pauperis application without prejudice for lack of financial detail and warned that the vague and conclusory complaint may be dismissed for failure to state a claim.
key_cases_cited: Haines v. Kerner, 404 U.S. 519 (1972)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Sinwell v. Shapp, 536 F.2d 15 (3d Cir. 1976)

source_file: 10714396_2025_Estela Elizabeth Jeffcoat v. Brookings Housing _ Redevelopment Commission_sdd
case_name: Estela Elizabeth Jeffcoat v. Brookings Housing & Redevelopment Commission
accommodation_description: The underlying complaint alleges Fair Housing Act violations including reasonable accommodation claims, but the specific accommodation requested is not described in this order denying appointment of counsel.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion to appoint counsel, finding that plaintiff demonstrated ability to litigate her claims through her detailed complaint, successful service of process, and filing of multiple motions.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Estela Elizabeth Jeffcoat filed suit against Brookings Housing & Redevelopment Commission alleging Fair Housing Act, Section 504 Rehabilitation Act, and HUD Regulations violations, and now moves for appointment of counsel. The court denied the motion to appoint counsel, finding that plaintiff demonstrated sufficient ability to litigate through her detailed filings and successful service of process on defendant.

source_file: 10715172_2025_Wayne Alexander Howlett v. City of Susanville, et al._caed
case_name: Wayne Alexander Howlett v. City of Susanville, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendants harassed him with racial slurs, contaminated his water supply, damaged his property, and attempted to force him to sell his home because of his race.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim fails because he did not adequately allege membership in a protected class or establish the causal link between an existing policy and a disproportionate effect on a protected class necessary to state a prima facie case of disparate-impact discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Wayne Alexander Howlett sued the City of Susanville and Susanville Sanitary District alleging they harassed him with racial slurs, contaminated his water, and attempted to force him to sell his home in violation of the Fair Housing Act and Fourteenth Amendment. The court granted defendants' unopposed motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff's complaint failed to adequately plead intentional discrimination or disparate impact under the FHA because he did not sufficiently allege membership in a protected class or a causal link between a municipal policy and discriminatory effect.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 129 S. Ct. 1937 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Sw. Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)
Pac. Shores Props., Ltd. Liab. Co. v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 10716332_2025_Damia Bjurling v. Alec Johnson, et al._wawd
case_name: Damia Bjurling v. Alec Johnson, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she and her service dog were injured when a fellow tenant attacked her; the court analyzes whether defendants violated FHA provisions regarding disability discrimination rather than analyzing a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because Mr. Holder, as a fellow tenant, is not a proper defendant under the FHA which only regulates individual tenants through traditional agency principles, and plaintiff failed to plausibly allege that Mr. Holder was acting as an agent for her landlord.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Damia Bjurling sued former defendant Alec Johnson, Police Chief Shelby Parker, and fellow tenant Calvin Earl Holder III under the ADA, FHA, and constitutional provisions, alleging defendants' conduct—including recording an assault and failing to properly investigate—violated her rights as an adult with autism and injured her and her service dog. The court granted Chief Parker's motion to dismiss and dismissed all federal claims against all defendants with prejudice, finding plaintiff failed to state plausible claims including FHA claims against Mr. Holder because he is a fellow tenant not acting as an agent of a landlord.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Morris v. W. Hayden Ests. First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)

case_name: Alfey Ramdhan v. Morgan Stonehill, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks to stop an eviction as a reasonable accommodation for his medical and mental health disabilities.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a claim under the FHA because Plaintiff provides only conclusory allegations without sufficient facts to establish what accommodation he requested, how it relates to his disability, or what discriminatory conduct occurred.
brief_summary: Alfey Ramdhan, a pro se litigant with medical and mental health disabilities, sued Morgan Stonehill, LLC under the ADA and FHA alleging the defendant refused to accommodate his disability by working with him to avoid eviction. The Court granted IFP status, dismissed the ADA claim with prejudice (apartments are not places of public accommodation), and dismissed the FHA claim without prejudice for failure to state a claim because the complaint lacked sufficient factual allegations to establish a cognizable fair housing violation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Turner v. Crawford Square Apartments III, L.P., 449 F.3d 542 (3d Cir. 2006)
Independent Housing Servs. v. Fillmore Center Assocs., 840 F. Supp. 1328 (N.D. Cal. 1993)

source_file: 10716560_2025_Felice Lazae Martin, et al. v. Michael Castillo, et al._caed
case_name: Felice Lazae Martin, et al. v. Michael Castillo, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, individuals with disabilities including autism spectrum disorder, PTSD, anxiety, and depression, allege disability-based discrimination in connection with rental housing conditions and lockout, but do not articulate a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim because the complaint contained only two cursory references to the FHA with no specific facts establishing a cognizable claim, and dismissed the ADA claim because plaintiffs failed to adequately allege what reasonable accommodations they were denied.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Felice Lazae Martin and Hector Joseph Borges sued landlord Michael Castillo and attorney Michael Schneider over rental housing conditions, alleged lockout, and detained belongings at a Pine Mountain Club, California property, asserting constitutional claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, ADA Title II claims, and Fair Housing Act claims. The court granted leave to amend after dismissing all federal claims for failure to state a cognizable claim, finding the FHA claim cursory and conclusory and the ADA claim lacking any specific allegations about what accommodations were denied.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Lopez v. Smith, 203 F.3d 1122 (9th Cir. 2000)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10730094_2025_Eric Crouse v. Housing Works, Deschutes County, and the State of Oregon_ord
case_name: Eric Crouse v. Housing Works, Deschutes County, and the State of Oregon
accommodation_description: Request that payment standard be increased and utility allowance be raised to allow access to suitable housing under the Section 8 voucher program
key_holding: The State of Oregon's motion to dismiss was granted because the court lacks subject matter jurisdiction under Oregon's APA (which provides the exclusive remedy for review of final agency orders) and because Plaintiff failed to allege any affirmative acts by the State that caused injury, as opposed to mere failures to act.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Eric Crouse, a person with disabilities represented by the Deschutes County IDD program, sued Housing Works (the local housing authority), Deschutes County, and the State of Oregon alleging failures to provide reasonable housing accommodations including increased payment standards and utility allowances under Section 8. The State of Oregon moved to dismiss, and the court granted the motion on jurisdictional grounds (Oregon APA provides exclusive review mechanism) and for failure to state a claim because Plaintiff alleged no affirmative acts by the State causing injury.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wyatt B. v. Kotek, 146 F.4th 1267 (9th Cir. 2025)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 10730290_2025_Yifan Shen v. Commissioner, Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services_ca11
case_name: Yifan Shen v. Commissioner, Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege SB 264's purchase restriction, registration requirement, and affidavit requirement constitute discriminatory housing practices under the Fair Housing Act based on race and national origin, though no individualized accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the denial of preliminary injunction as to registration and affidavit requirements but reversed as to the purchase restriction because no plaintiff had standing to challenge it; the court applied rational basis review under Terrace v. Thompson, finding the purchase restriction did not violate Equal Protection as applied to noncitizens, and rejected federal preemption arguments regarding CFIUS.
brief_summary: Chinese citizens and a real estate brokerage firm sued Florida officials to enjoin enforcement of SB 264, which restricted Chinese noncitizens from purchasing Florida real property, required registration of property owned by Chinese persons, and mandated compliance affidavits. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the denial of preliminary injunction for the registration and affidavit requirements but reversed and remanded for the purchase restriction because no plaintiff had standing to challenge it, applying rational basis review and finding the statute did not facially violate Equal Protection.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Plyler v. Doe, 457 U.S. 202 (1982)
Yick Wo v. Hopkins, 118 U.S. 356 (1886)
Terrace v. Thompson, 263 U.S. 197 (1923)
Crosby v. Nat'l Foreign Trade Council, 530 U.S. 363 (2000)

source_file: 10731021_2025_Asya Weston, et al. v. WIP 800 J Lofts, et al._caed
case_name: Asya Weston, et al. v. WIP 800 J Lofts, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege the unlawful detainer action and eviction were retaliatory and discriminatory based on race, familial status, and disability, but do not specify a particular accommodation request being denied.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because plaintiffs failed to sufficiently allege they suffer from a qualifying handicap under § 3602(h), that defendants knew of their impairments, or that defendants refused to make an accommodation.
brief_summary: Two Black tenants sued WIP 800 J Lofts and associated individuals alleging the unlawful detainer action against them was fraudulent and retaliatory, targeting their race, familial status, and disabilities in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, finding plaintiffs failed to state an FHA claim because they did not adequately allege a qualifying handicap or discriminatory conduct based on protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Harris v. Itzhaki
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co.

source_file: 10731728_2025_Irish Horton v. Beacon Woods East Master Association, Inc., Ronnie Armstrong, and Christopher Skene_flmd
case_name: Irish Horton v. Beacon Woods East Master Association, Inc., Ronnie Armstrong, and Christopher Skene
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a Black man with a physical disability requiring a service dog, alleged he was expelled from the clubhouse when Defendants refused entry based on his service animal.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's race-based discrimination claims under the FHA, 42 U.S.C. §§ 1981 and 1982, finding that the Amended Complaint failed to plead sufficient facts showing that Defendants' discriminatory conduct stemmed from racial animus rather than animus toward Plaintiff's disability and use of a service dog.
brief_summary: Irish Horton, a Black/African American man with a physical disability who uses a service dog, sued Beacon Woods East Master Association, Inc. and two HOA officers alleging they expelled him from a clubhouse in May 2023 because of both his race and disability. The Court granted Defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff's factual allegations showed discriminatory animus stemmed from his disability and use of a service dog rather than his race, and that Plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts showing he was treated differently than similarly situated non-Black residents.
key_cases_cited: Watts v. Joggers Run Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 133 F.4th 1032 (11th Cir. 2025)
Ziyadat v. Diamondrock Hosp. Co., 3 F.4th 1291 (11th Cir. 2021)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Plowright v. Miami Dade Cnty., 102 F.4th 1358 (11th Cir. 2024)

source_file: 10732441_2025_Anthony George Bell v. Weinreb Management LLC; Abraham Reiss of Weinreb Management LLC; Pitor Piwowarczyk of 455 Ocean Associates LLC; 455 Ocean Associates LLC_nyed
case_name: Anthony George Bell v. Weinreb Management LLC; Abraham Reiss of Weinreb Management LLC; Pitor Piwowarczyk of 455 Ocean Associates LLC; 455 Ocean Associates LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; plaintiff alleges housing quality issues (roach infestation, inoperable vents, electrical problems) without requesting any disability-related accommodation or alleging discrimination based on his HIV and asthma
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiff does not allege any acts of discrimination—merely mentioning health conditions (HIV and asthma) without linking defendants' conduct to discriminatory treatment based on disability is insufficient to invoke FHA protections.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Anthony George Bell sued Weinreb Management LLC and 455 Ocean Associates LLC alleging housing quality issues including roach infestation, broken vents, and electrical problems at his Brooklyn apartment; Bell mentioned having HIV and asthma but did not request any disability accommodation. The court dismissed the complaint for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction, finding that merely citing the Fair Housing Act is insufficient where plaintiff fails to allege any discriminatory conduct based on his protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Rivera v. Inc. Vill. of Farmingdale, 784 F. Supp. 2d 133 (E.D.N.Y. 2011)
Gamarra v. Paul, 2024 WL 4515286 (E.D.N.Y. Oct. 17, 2024)
Scoggins v. Turning Point of Cent. Cal., Inc., 2023 WL 2354820 (E.D. Cal. Mar. 3, 2023)

source_file: 10734581_2025_N. Clark LLC, ET AL v. UNITED STATES DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING AND URBAN DEVELOPMENT, ET AL_laed
case_name: N. Clark LLC v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs (property owners accused of FHA violations) requested that HUD dismiss the discrimination complaint and charge against them and issue a corrective press release vindicating them.
key_holding: The court lacked subject matter jurisdiction because plaintiffs failed to exhaust their FTCA claims (no sum-certain monetary claim presented), their intentional tort claims were barred by the FTCA's intentional tort exception, HUD's decision to close the case without determination was committed to agency discretion, and HUD's press release was not a final agency action subject to APA review.
brief_summary: N. Clark LLC and Kathleen Cresson sued HUD and DOJ under the FTCA and APA seeking to have HUD dismiss a discrimination complaint and charge filed against them by the Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, and to compel HUD to issue a corrective press release. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding plaintiffs failed to exhaust FTCA administrative remedies, their intentional tort claims were barred, and HUD's actions were either committed to agency discretion or did not constitute final agency action subject to judicial review.
key_cases_cited: Hui v. Castaneda, 559 U.S. 799 (2010)
FDIC v. Meyer, 510 U.S. 471 (1994)
Millbrook v. U.S., 569 U.S. 50 (2012)
Bennett v. Spears, 520 U.S. 154 (1997)
Texas v. Cardona, 743 F. Supp. 3d 824 (N.D. Tex. 2024)

source_file: 10735679_2025_Delroy Askins v. Prestige Management Inc.; Lowell Hendricks_nysd
case_name: Delroy Askins v. Prestige Management Inc.; Lowell Hendricks
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was assaulted by building superintendent, denied access to community room, and had medical supplies stolen, all because of his disability (paraplegia/wheelchair use); no specific accommodation request was made.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because, even assuming he has a disability, he alleged no facts suggesting his disability motivated any adverse actions—the claim that the assault occurred 'because of his disability' was speculative without supporting facts from which a reasonable inference of discriminatory motivation could be drawn.
brief_summary: Delroy Askins, a paraplegic wheelchair user, sued Prestige Management and building superintendent Lowell Hendricks alleging assault, denial of community room access, and theft of medical supplies as discrimination because of his disability. The court dismissed the amended complaint, finding that plaintiff failed to allege facts suggesting his disability motivated any adverse action—his conclusory assertion that the assault occurred 'because of his disability' was speculative and insufficient to state a FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
Rodriguez v. Vill. Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Amato v. McGinty, 2022 WL 226798 (N.D.N.Y. 2022)

source_file: 10736151_2025_Jana Smith v. Mark Downey; Charles Olsen; Ariell Hartwell; Mark Simon; and Sherman Smith_ord
case_name: Jana Smith v. Mark Downey; Charles Olsen; Ariell Hartwell; Mark Simon; and Sherman Smith
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED - The opinion references Fair Housing Act claims under 42 U.S.C. § 3604 but does not describe the specific accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court denied defendant Downey's motion for summary judgment because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether plaintiff Smith violated housing rules, precluding judgment as a matter of law.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jana Smith sued defendants including landlord Mark Downey and others under the Fair Housing Act and civil rights conspiracy statutes. The court denied Downey's motion for summary judgment because the factual dispute over whether Smith systematically destroyed her residence and the conflicting declarations created genuine issues of material fact requiring trial resolution.
key_cases_cited: Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Clicks Billiards, Inc. v. Sixshooters, Inc., 251 F.3d 1252 (9th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 10737079_2025_Lawrence Bryant v. Jonathan Lagos, Et al._flsd
case_name: Lawrence Bryant v. Jonathan Lagos, Et al.
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed as a shotgun pleading because the counts generally alleged all defendants were liable for the same acts and multiple causes of action were improperly combined within single counts, without prejudice to filing an amended complaint.
brief_summary: Lawrence Bryant filed suit in Florida state court against Jonathan Lagos and unnamed defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations and related state law claims, including a claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604 for discriminatory housing practices. The federal court denied the plaintiff's motions to remand and granted the defendants' motions to dismiss, finding the complaint constituted an improper shotgun pleading that failed to separate causes of action and specify which defendants were responsible for which allegedly discriminatory acts.
key_cases_cited: Caterpillar, Inc. v. Williams, 482 U.S. 386 (1987)
Parker v. Scrap Metal Processors, Inc., 468 F.3d 733 (11th Cir. 2006)
Weiland v. Palm Beach Cnty. Sheriff's Office, 792 F.3d 1313 (11th Cir. 2015)
Grupo Dataflux v. Atlas Global Grp., L.P., 541 U.S. 567 (2004)
Stone v. Bank of N.Y. Mellon, N.A., 609 Fed. App'x 979 (11th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 10739282_2025_Trestle Creek Association v. Stephen R. Schooley, Jody F. Schooley_mtd
10742406_2025_David D. Crossett v. Douglas Shumway, and Carriage Crossing Homeowners Association, Inc._utd
case_name: David D. Crossett v. Douglas Shumway and Carriage Crossing Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for two additional live-in aides beyond the one already approved by the HOA, to assist with daily movement, glucose monitoring, insulin preparation, and safety needs due to legal blindness, diabetes, and bipolar disorder.
key_holding: The court denied Crossett's emergency motions for injunctive relief because he failed to show the requested accommodation of two additional live-in aides is reasonable, a required element of an FHA reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Plaintiff David Crossett, a condominium owner with legal blindness, diabetes, and bipolar disorder, sued his homeowners association seeking to compel approval of two additional live-in aides beyond the one already permitted. The court denied Crossett's emergency motions for temporary injunctive relief, finding he failed to establish a likelihood of success on the merits because he did not demonstrate the requested accommodation of two additional live-in aides is reasonable under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Carlson v. Carrington Square LLC, 2025 WL 1017536 (D. Utah Apr. 4, 2025)
Courage to Change Ranches Holding Co. v. El Paso Cnty., 73 F.4th 1175 (10th Cir. 2023)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)

source_file: 10743592_2025_JAMES B. HERRERA v. MADDOX MANAGEMENT, LLC_nmd
case_name: James B. Herrera v. Maddox Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Defendant submitted false rental verifications to prospective landlords, interfering with his Section 8 voucher use, and ignored his requests for reasonable accommodations for his disabilities.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims fail because he failed to plausibly allege a connection between his disability or protected activity and the alleged adverse action, and he did not adequately plead disability status, causation, or discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: James Herrera sued Maddox Management, LLC alleging Fair Housing Act violations, procedural due process claims, and Rehabilitation Act claims stemming from the landlord's alleged submission of false rental verifications and failure to accommodate his disabilities. The court denied Defendant's motion to dismiss but ordered Plaintiff to show cause why his case should not be dismissed for failure to state plausible claims, finding the complaint failed to adequately plead disability, causation, or discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Morgan v. Carrington Mortg. Servs., 719 F. App'x 735 (10th Cir. 2017)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n, No. 21-4035 (10th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Desiree Knight v. Macon Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for minimum rent hardship adjustment as a reasonable accommodation for anxiety and ADHD
key_holding: Knight's FHA failure-to-accommodate claim was dismissed because she failed to plausibly allege how her requested accommodations (special inspection, rent hardship adjustment) were necessary to alleviate the effects of her anxiety and ADHD, as she did not explain how these impairments limit her major life activities.
brief_summary: Desiree Knight, a Section 8 voucher holder with anxiety and ADHD, sued Macon Housing Authority alleging failures to accommodate, FHA retaliation, Section 504 discrimination, and due process violations. The court dismissed her FHA failure-to-accommodate and retaliation claims without prejudice, finding she failed to plead sufficient facts showing how her requested special inspection, rent hardship adjustment, and VAWA transfer were necessary accommodations for her disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Wright v. City of Roanoke Redevelopment & Housing Auth., 479 U.S. 418 (1987)

source_file: 10744240_2025_Irvin G. Murray v. U.S. Treasury, et al._paed
case_name: Irvin G. Murray v. U.S. Treasury, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he is being denied housing in retaliation for attempting to recover an alleged $43 million inheritance, and requests restoration of his housing rights.
key_holding: The court dismissed Murray's FHA retaliation claim with prejudice because he failed to allege discrimination based on membership in a protected class, and dismissed the entire complaint as factually frivolous and for failure to state a claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Irvin G. Murray filed a civil action against U.S. Treasury, HUD, Philadelphia Housing Authority, and numerous other defendants alleging various constitutional violations stemming from a stolen $43 million inheritance claim. The court dismissed the entire complaint as factually frivolous under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) and dismissed Murray's Fair Housing Act retaliation claim with prejudice because he failed to allege discrimination based on membership in a protected class.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Holley v. Dep't of Veteran Affairs, 165 F.3d 244 (3d Cir. 1999)
Lloyd v. Presby's Inspired Life, 251 F. Supp. 3d 891 (E.D. Pa. 2017)
Davis v. Rubin, 2020 WL 7624833 (E.D. Pa. Dec. 22, 2020)

source_file: 10745098_2025_K.D., a minor, by and through his Guardian ad Litem, LaQuantae Davis v. CALIBER CHANGEMAKERS, et al._caed

case_name: Anthony Alfieri v. Cortland Management LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges discrimination based on familial status (having a minor child) and retaliation for filing a HUD complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims under § 3604(b) and § 3617 because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient factual facts showing concrete adverse action or causal link between the alleged harms and his protected class status.
brief_summary: Anthony Alfieri sued Cortland Management LLC alleging the landlord discriminated against him based on familial status by targeting his four-year-old daughter with repeated noise complaints and threatened eviction, and retaliated against him by raising his rent after he filed a HUD complaint. The court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, finding Alfieri failed to allege sufficient facts showing concrete adverse action or a causal connection between the alleged harms and his protected class status.
key_cases_cited: Morris v. W. Hayden Ests. First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
United States v. City of Hayward, 36 F.3d 832 (9th Cir. 1994)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)

source_file: 10745833_2025_Mekal Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento, et al._caed
case_name: Mekal Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested reasonable accommodation in processing his Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application, including consideration of his certified disability, after defendants allegedly lost his application and failed to respond for years.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim was dismissed for failure to state a claim because his allegations were speculative and lacked specific supporting facts necessary to state a claim for relief, and his claim that a similarly situated friend was treated more favorably failed to suggest disability discrimination since that friend is also disabled.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mekal Faruki, an elderly individual with Multiple Myeloma, sued the Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento and related entities alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by losing his Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application and failing to accommodate his disability over years of follow-up. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the complaint without leave to amend for failure to state a plausible FHA claim, finding the allegations speculative and failing to plead facts showing disability-based discrimination since plaintiff's friend who received more favorable treatment was also disabled.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lovell v. Chandler, 303 F.3d 1039 (9th Cir. 2002)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Manal Farhan v. 2715 NMA LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to display a Palestinian flag in her apartment window to express her heritage, and was evicted when she refused to comply with defendants' neutrality policy.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Farhan's complaint failed to plausibly allege that defendants enforced a neutrality policy against her because of her national origin rather than her political expression in support of the Palestinian cause, and that her disparate impact claim failed without any statistical evidence showing the policy disproportionately affected Palestinian tenants.
brief_summary: Manal Farhan, a Palestinian American, sued her apartment building's owner and operator after they ordered her to remove a Palestinian flag from her window pursuant to a neutrality policy regarding the Israel-Palestine conflict and then terminated her tenancy when she refused. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, finding Farhan's complaint failed to plausibly allege intentional national origin discrimination or disparate impact because the eviction was motivated by her political expression rather than her protected status, and she provided no statistical evidence of disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)

source_file: 10749500_2025_Shyane Harden v. Kier Management; Michelle Tipplette; and Lindsay Borke_utd
case_name: Shyane Harden v. Kier Management; Michelle Tipplette; and Lindsay Borke
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was retaliated against for complaining about not receiving an income-based rent subsidy or reduction despite completing required certifications.
key_holding: Plaintiff fails to state an FHA retaliation claim because she did not allege she was engaged in the exercise of fair housing rights or that defendants were motivated by discriminatory intent; her complaint about not receiving a rent subsidy despite completing certifications does not constitute protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Shyane Harden, a tenant at Bramblewood Apartments in Ogden, Utah, sued Kier Management and two employees for retaliatory eviction under the Fair Housing Act, claiming they attempted to evict her in retaliation for her complaints about not receiving a rent subsidy despite completing required certifications. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a plausible FHA claim because Harden did not allege she engaged in protected fair housing activity or that defendants were motivated by discriminatory intent, and granted her leave to amend by December 29, 2025.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n

source_file: 10754452_2025_Michael S. McLain and Rosalee Janette McLain v. Board of County Commissioners of Sedgwick County, Kansas, et al._ksd
case_name: Michael S. McLain and Rosalee Janette McLain v. Board of County Commissioners of Sedgwick County, Kansas, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Michael McLain alleges he was blocked from the City of Goddard's Facebook page in retaliation for exercising rights under the ADA and FHA, including requests for PTSD accommodations during an arrest.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' FHA retaliation claim under § 3617 fails because the Goddard Defendants do not engage in any regulated activities under §§ 3603-3606 (sale/rental of housing, residential real estate transactions, or brokerage services), so blocking Plaintiffs from a Facebook page cannot constitute retaliation for exercising Fair Housing Act rights.
brief_summary: Michael and Rosalee McLain sued the City of Goddard and other defendants alleging, inter alia, that the City blocked Mr. McLain from its Facebook page in retaliation for exercising rights under the ADA and FHA. The court granted the Goddard Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs failed to plead a plausible FHA retaliation claim because the City does not engage in any regulated housing activities under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Monell v. Dep't of Social Serv. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Zhu v. Countrywide Realty, Co., 165 F. Supp. 2d 1181 (D. Kan. 2001)

source_file: 10755281_2025_U.S. Bank National Association v. Debbie Alice Thompson, et al._cand
10756235_2025_Daisy Bueno, individually and as guardian ad litem for S.B., a minor v. Bass Lake Joint Union Elementary School District_caed
10756893_2025_Felice Lazae Martin, et al. v. Michael Castillo, et al._caed
case_name: Felice Lazae Martin, et al. v. Michael Castillo, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, who have disabilities including autism, PTSD, and depression, allege disability-based housing discrimination by private landlord but make only cursory references to the Fair Housing Act without specific accommodation requests.
key_holding: The court recommends dismissal of all federal claims because plaintiffs failed to state cognizable claims: Section 1983 claims fail as private landlords and their attorney do not act under color of state law; ADA Title II claims fail because Kern County is not a named defendant and plaintiffs did not adequately plead denial of services; and FHA claims fail because plaintiffs made only cursory references without specific facts establishing a prima facie case.
brief_summary: Pro se disabled plaintiffs sued their private landlord and his attorney for housing condition defects (mold, lack of heating), illegal lockout, and constitutional violations under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, ADA, and the Fair Housing Act. The court recommends dismissal, finding that plaintiffs' § 1983 claims fail because private actors do not act under color of state law, ADA claims fail because the proper governmental defendant was not named, and FHA claims fail because plaintiffs made only cursory references without stating specific facts establishing any cognizable claim.
key_cases_cited: Kokkonen v. Guardian Life Ins. Co. of Am., 511 U.S. 375 (1994)
Caterpillar, Inc. v. Williams, 482 U.S. 386 (1987)
Monell v. Dep't of Social Servs. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 10757487_2025_Sehra Waheed v. MLMIC Insurance Company; Sonia Kim; Michael Terrani; Garden OBGYN of East 67th Street; Bethpage Medical, PLLC, d_b_a Garden OBGYN_nysd
case_name: Sehra Waheed v. MLMIC Insurance Company; Sonia Kim; Michael Terrani; Garden OBGYN of East 67th Street; Bethpage Medical, PLLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that her doctors' insurer pay her rent as a 'reasonable accommodation' for disability-related financial constraints arising from a medical procedure.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because the plaintiff sued an insurance company and medical providers rather than a housing provider, and her claims concerned monetary compensation for alleged insurance bad faith rather than any actual housing discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sehra Waheed sued her doctors' insurer (MLMIC Insurance Company) and medical providers under the ADA and FHA, seeking rent payments as a 'reasonable accommodation' for disability-related financial harm following a medical procedure. The court dismissed both federal claims, holding that ADA Title III does not apply because plaintiff was not seeking access to a public accommodation, and the FHA does not apply because her claims concerned insurance bad faith litigation rather than housing discrimination by a housing provider.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone, Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
Littlejohn v. City of New York, 795 F.3d 297 (2d Cir. 2015)

source_file: 10757573_2025_Matt Hewitt v. Jennifer Price, et al._caed
10757978_2025_Susan Suarez; Delailah Perez; Andre Rojas v. Park Lane Management Corporation, et al._nysd
case_name: Susan Suarez; Delailah Perez; Andre Rojas v. Park Lane Management Corporation, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Susan Suarez, who is blind, requested rescheduling of a housing inspection as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed Suarez's FHA claims because, assuming she is disabled, she failed to allege facts showing she suffered adverse treatment or that defendants discriminated against her because of her disability, granting 30 days' leave to amend.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs sued Park Lane Management Corporation, Kahen Properties, and individual landlords under the ADA and FHA, alleging defendants failed to accommodate Plaintiff Susan Suarez's disability (she is blind) by refusing to reschedule a housing inspection. The court dismissed ADA claims because private landlords are not subject to the ADA, dismissed FHA claims for failure to state a plausible discriminatory motive claim, and granted 30 days' leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
Blitz v. BLDG Mgmt. Co., 2023 WL 6162295 (S.D.N.Y. Sept. 21, 2023)

source_file: 10763543_2025_Tyree Moore v. Office of Economic Opportunity, Zugely Ortiz, Danielle Majted_njd
case_name: Tyree Moore v. Office of Economic Opportunity, Zugely Ortiz, Danielle Majted
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint does not clearly articulate a specific accommodation request; he alleges denial of rental arrears assistance without identifying a disability-related need.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because plaintiff failed to allege membership in any protected class (race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin) as required to state a claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604, and failed to adequately plead elements of retaliation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Tyree Moore sued the Office of Economic Opportunity and individual defendants alleging violations of Title VI, 42 U.S.C. § 1981/§ 1983, the Fair Housing Act, and New Jersey's Law Against Discrimination after OEO denied his application for rental arrears assistance. The court granted IFP status but dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege discrimination based on a protected class as required under each statute, and failed to adequately plead retaliation elements.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Conley v. Gibson, 355 U.S. 41 (1957)

source_file: 10765243_2025_Justin Baker v. The Emerald Condos et al._wawd
case_name: Justin Baker v. The Emerald Condos et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested waiver of HOA policies (recycling, trash disposal, and other physical task policies) and parking accommodations for his disability-related physical therapist visits.
key_holding: Court dismissed most claims against defendants but allowed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim under 24 C.F.R. § 100.204 and FHA discrimination claims under § 3604(b), (f) to proceed, ordering plaintiff to file an amended complaint.
brief_summary: Pro se disabled plaintiff Justin Baker sued The Emerald Condos HOA, CWD Group (property management), and individual managers alleging Fair Housing Act violations, including claims that defendants denied his requests for disability accommodations related to HOA policies and parking. The court dismissed most claims with prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) for failure to state a claim, but allowed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim under 24 C.F.R. § 100.204 and FHA discrimination claims under § 3604(b), (f) to proceed, ordering plaintiff to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Baker v. Avenue5 Residential, No. C24-01862JHC, 2025 WL 1207666 (W.D. Wash. Apr. 25, 2025)
Slaughter v. Valley View I LLP, No. C23-1360JLR, 2023 WL 6461058 (W.D. Wash. Oct. 4, 2023)

source_file: 10765288_2025_Johnny Moffett v. Columbia Manor et al._wawd
case_name: Johnny Moffett v. Columbia Manor et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; plaintiff alleges racial harassment by white supremacy group and failure of property manager to intervene.
key_holding: Plaintiff's amended complaint fails to state an FHA claim because it contains only conclusory allegations of racial bias without specific facts from which the court could infer that defendants were motivated by racial discrimination or engaged in conduct prohibited by 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a).
brief_summary: Johnny Moffett, an African American man with disabilities, sued Columbia Manor (Smith Towers) and individual defendants alleging racial harassment by a white supremacy group and claiming the property manager failed to intervene. The court dismissed the amended complaint sua sponte under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations of race-based discrimination without specific facts demonstrating discriminatory intent or conduct prohibited by the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Barren v. Harrington, 152 F.3d 1193 (9th Cir. 1998)
Missouri ex rel. Koster v. Harris, 847 F.3d 646 (9th Cir. 2017)
Watison v. Carter, 668 F.3d 1108 (9th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 10766369_2025_Justin Baker v. The Emerald Condos et al._wawd
case_name: Justin Baker v. The Emerald Condos et al.
citation: UNDETERMINED (W.D. Wash. Order, Case No. C25-1989JLR)
accommodation_description: Request for accommodation from building policy requiring residents to break down cardboard boxes before disposal, allegedly due to disability-related reasons
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim with prejudice because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts showing he is a handicapped person under the FHAA, that he made a clear accommodation request to defendants, or that the requested accommodation was necessary to afford him an equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Justin Baker, proceeding in forma pauperis, sued property management defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disability discrimination and failure to provide reasonable accommodations for cardboard box disposal policy. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding Baker's third amended complaint failed to adequately allege facts sufficient to support any FHA claim.

source_file: 10766412_2025_Randy Dewayne Pittman v. Landsphere Property Management, et al._cand
case_name: Randy Dewayne Pittman v. Landsphere Property Management, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged defendants failed to create an accessible parking space for him at his apartment building.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's federal FHA claim with prejudice because his complaint failed to state a claim for failure to provide a reasonable accommodation regarding accessible parking.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Randy Dewayne Pittman sued his landlord Landsphere Property Management and property manager Vamsi Musunuru alleging they failed to create an accessible parking space as a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the federal FHA claim with prejudice for failure to state a claim, and dismissed the remaining state law claims without prejudice after declining to exercise supplemental jurisdiction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly

source_file: 10767062_2025_Marcos Isaac Rojas v. Shelter Corporation and Kyle Didier_mnd
case_name: Marcos Isaac Rojas v. Shelter Corporation and Kyle Didier
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; claims involve race-based tenant harassment and alleged retaliation via camera installation.
key_holding: The court dismissed Rojas's FHA disparate impact and retaliation claims because he failed to allege sufficient facts to plausibly support either claim, failing to adequately plead that defendants' policies disproportionately harmed minorities or that the camera installation constituted actionable retaliation.
brief_summary: Rojas, a Mexican national origin tenant, sued his property manager and company president alleging they failed to protect him from race-based tenant harassment and retaliated by installing a camera near his parking spot. The court dismissed his FHA claims (disparate impact and retaliation) without prejudice, finding he failed to plead sufficient facts to state plausible claims under either theory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Ellis v. City of Minneapolis, 860 F.3d 1106 (8th Cir. 2017)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)

source_file: 10767966_2025_Angel Lopez and Kazue Koizumi v. Edward Pec, et al._njd
case_name: Angel Lopez and Kazue Koizumi v. Edward Pec, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Angel Lopez requested a handicapped parking space near his apartment as a reasonable accommodation for his asthma and COPD disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed Lopez's FHA reasonable accommodation claim with prejudice because his allegations were conclusory—he failed to plead specific facts showing how a handicapped parking space was necessary to afford him an equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling, as opposed to merely being something he would prefer.
brief_summary: Angel Lopez and Kazue Koizumi sued their landlord Edward Pec and The Palisades LLC, as well as attorneys Brandon Broderick and Christopher Karounos, alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to provide a reasonable accommodation (handicapped parking space) for Lopez's asthma and COPD, along with claims of harassment, invasion of privacy, and discrimination based on race and disability. The Court granted both motions to dismiss, dismissing the FHA reasonable accommodation claim with prejudice because Lopez failed to plead specific facts demonstrating how the requested parking accommodation was necessary to afford him an equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n
Sporn v. Ocean Colony Condo. Ass'n

case_name: Charles Ray Johnson v. Norman Rinehart
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was described; plaintiff's complaint was too vague and conclusory to identify any particular accommodation sought.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA and ADA claims were dismissed without prejudice because his complaint contained only conclusory statements and general legal references without specific factual allegations establishing that he is disabled, that he was discriminated against based on race, or that defendant took retaliatory action against him.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Charles Ray Johnson sued landlord Norman Rinehart alleging ADA discrimination, race-based housing discrimination, disability discrimination, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted in forma pauperis status but dismissed all claims without prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim, finding Johnson's complaint contained only conclusory legal assertions and highlighted sections from a judicial system overview without any specific factual allegations supporting his claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Martin v. Sargent, 780 F.2d 1334 (8th Cir. 1985)
Stone v. Harry, 364 F.3d 912 (8th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 10768478_2026_Ayman Adeeb v. City of Marina, et al._cand
case_name: Ayman Adeeb v. City of Marina, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discriminatory enforcement of housing code against him based on his Middle Eastern ancestry
