key_holding: Plaintiff failed to plead facts plausibly establishing that defendants discriminated against him based on his Middle Eastern ancestry; his FHA claim was dismissed along with other federal claims for failure to state a claim.
brief_summary: Dr. Ayman Adeeb, a property owner and contractor of Middle Eastern descent, sued the City of Marina and its officials alleging discriminatory and retaliatory code enforcement against his properties based on his race and in retaliation for his public records requests. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss all federal claims including the Fair Housing Act claim for failure to plead plausible facts establishing discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Sampson v. County of Los Angeles, 974 F.3d 1012 (9th Cir. 2020)
SmileDirectClub, LLC v. Tippins, 31 F.4th 1110 (9th Cir. 2022)

source_file: 10769132_2026_Judy Shaw Foundation v. City of Howard, South Dakota_sdd
case_name: Judy Shaw Foundation v. City of Howard, South Dakota
accommodation_description: The City enacted zoning ordinances requiring a special exception for group homes and residential chemical dependency treatment centers, allegedly discriminating against disabled individuals with substance use disorders.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for JSF on the issue that a residential chemical dependency treatment center was a permissible use under existing zoning ('service establishment') without needing a special exception, denied the City's motion on the FHA disparate treatment claim finding evidence of discriminatory intent in enacting the ordinances, and denied JSF's motion on the City's advice-of-counsel defense which remains for jury determination.
brief_summary: Judy Shaw Foundation (JSF), which owned property in Howard, South Dakota, sued the City of Howard after the city enacted ordinances requiring a special exception to operate a residential chemical dependency treatment center on property JSF was selling to iRecover US, Inc. The court granted summary judgment for JSF on its ADA claim (for lack of standing), denied summary judgment for both parties on JSF's FHA disparate treatment claim (finding evidence of discriminatory intent), granted JSF partial summary judgment on the interpretive question of whether the treatment center was a permissible use under existing zoning, and denied JSF's motion on the City's advice-of-counsel defense.
key_cases_cited: Gallagher v. 56 Lean Corp., 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Durand v. Fairview Health Servs., 902 F.3d 836 (8th Cir. 2018)
Hernon v. Revere Copper & Brass, Inc., 494 F.2d 705 (8th Cir. 1974)
Stockwell v. McCook Cnty. Bd. of Comm'rs, 2 N.W.3d 236 (S.D. 2024)

source_file: 10769187_2026_Judy Shaw Foundation v. City of Howard, South Dakota_sdd
case_name: Judy Shaw Foundation v. City of Howard, South Dakota
accommodation_description: JSF alleged the City enacted discriminatory zoning ordinances (745 and 746) to exclude residential chemical dependency treatment centers serving disabled individuals (those recovering from substance use disorders), requiring them to obtain a special exception permit.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City on JSF's ADA claim for lack of standing, but denied the City's motion on JSF's FHA disparate treatment claim, finding genuine issues of material fact exist regarding the City's discriminatory intent in enacting the zoning ordinances, including what advice of counsel the City received and whether the treatment center was already a permitted use under the existing ordinance.
brief_summary: Judy Shaw Foundation (JSF), which owned the Howard Hotel property in Howard, South Dakota, sued the City of Howard after iRecover backed out of purchasing the property to operate a residential chemical dependency treatment center, allegedly due to the City's enactment of zoning Ordinances 745 and 746 requiring a special exception permit for group homes and treatment facilities. The court granted summary judgment for the City on JSF's ADA claim (lack of standing), but denied summary judgment on JSF's FHA disparate treatment claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding the City's discriminatory intent, including whether the treatment center was already a permitted use under existing zoning and what advice of counsel the City received before enacting the ordinances.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Gallagher v. Catholic Health Initiatives, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Durand v. Fairview Health Servs., 902 F.3d 836 (8th Cir. 2018)
McCullum v. Orlando Reg'l Healthcare Sys., Inc., 768 F.3d 1135 (11th Cir. 2014)

source_file: 10769217_2026_Gerard Torres, + 2 Children v. Alexis Winnell, Property Manager, et al._mad
case_name: Gerard Torres, + 2 Children v. Alexis Winnell, Property Manager, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges the 7-day notice to quit was issued in retaliation for his assertion of Fair Housing Act rights as a disabled tenant, and seeks a TRO to prevent eviction while this case is pending.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint for lack of subject matter jurisdiction because he failed to identify his disability, explain how defendants discriminated based on that disability, or show how this action arises under federal law.
brief_summary: Gerard Torres, a self-represented disabled tenant in a Massachusetts condominium, sued his property manager and neighbors alleging harassment, defamation, and a retaliatory eviction notice. The court dismissed the complaint for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding Torres failed to identify his disability, explain how defendants discriminated against him, or establish a viable federal claim.

source_file: 10769390_2026_Dewanna Johnson, et al. v. Providence Homeowners Association and FirstService Residential Texas, Inc.; McKinney Housing Authority v. Providence Homeowners Association and FirstService Residential Texas, Inc
case_name: Dewanna Johnson, et al. v. Providence Homeowners Association and FirstService Residential Texas, Inc.; McKinney Housing Authority v. Providence Homeowners Association and FirstService Residential Texas, Inc.
accommodation_description: N/A - case concerns HOA's ban on renting to Section 8 voucher holders allegedly motivated by race, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: Plaintiffs plausibly alleged disparate-treatment claims under § 3604(a) because unlike rejected applicants in Lincoln Property, these tenants already lived in Providence and were forced out; plaintiffs alleged sufficient facts showing racially coded language and procedural departures supporting an inference that race motivated the Voucher Ban.
brief_summary: Individual tenants and housing authorities sued Providence Homeowners Association and its property manager alleging the HOA's Section 8 voucher ban constituted intentional race discrimination under § 3604(a). The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss the disparate-treatment claims, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged that racially coded social media posts and procedural departures during the voting process supported an inference that race motivated the Voucher Ban.
key_cases_cited: Lincoln Property Co. v. Rankin, 920 F.3d 900 (5th Cir. 2019)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Cicalese v. Univ. of Tex. Med. Branch, 924 F.3d 762 (5th Cir. 2019)
Swierkiewicz v. Sorema North America, 534 U.S. 506 (2002)
Ames v. Ohio Dep't of Youth Servs., 605 U.S. 303 (2025)

source_file: 10769863_2026_Marceline Hope Engler v. The Salvation Army_arwd
case_name: Marceline Hope Engler v. The Salvation Army
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a transgender woman, alleges she was prevented from using the women's bathroom and was evicted from The Salvation Army facility based on gender identity.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because it failed to allege sufficient facts establishing that The Salvation Army's facility qualified as a 'dwelling' under the FHA, and the damages claim of $900 fell below the jurisdictional minimum for diversity jurisdiction.
brief_summary: Marceline Hope Engler, a pro se transgender plaintiff, sued The Salvation Army alleging sex/gender-identity discrimination after being prevented from using the women's bathroom and being evicted from the facility. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice, finding the plaintiff failed to allege facts establishing the Salvation Army facility qualified as a 'dwelling' under the Fair Housing Act, and the claim fell below the jurisdictional threshold for diversity jurisdiction.

source_file: 10769944_2026_James B. Herrera v. Maddox Management, LLC_nmd
case_name: James B. Herrera v. Maddox Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff vaguely alleges Defendant failed to accommodate his disabilities and provided false/misleading rental verifications to prospective landlords, impeding his Section 8 voucher use.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state a plausible FHA claim because his allegations were conclusory, he failed to plead factual allegations describing his disability, causation between protected activity and adverse action was implausible (rental verification predated HUD complaint), and his challenge to state court damages was barred by Rooker-Feldman.
brief_summary: Plaintiff James Herrera, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued Maddox Management LLC alleging the landlord discriminated against him based on disability and retaliated against him by providing misleading rental verifications to prospective landlords and seeking inflated damages in eviction proceedings. The Court dismissed the case for failure to state a plausible claim, finding Plaintiff's allegations conclusory, lacking factual specificity about his disability, and temporally implausible since the alleged retaliatory rental verification preceded his HUD complaint.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Morgan v. Carrington Mortg. Servs., 719 F. App'x 735 (10th Cir. 2017)
Wilkerson v. Shinseki, 606 F.3d 1256 (10th Cir. 2010)
Hall v. Bellmon, 935 F.2d 1106 (10th Cir. 1991)

source_file: 10770206_2026_Telvon Taylor v. Westland Real Estate Group, LLC., et al._nvd
case_name: Telvon Taylor v. Westland Real Estate Group, LLC., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was evicted despite paying rent in violation of the Fair Housing Act because of his disabilities, requesting that the eviction be prevented as a reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: At the § 1915(e) screening stage, the court found that plaintiff adequately alleged a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim because he sufficiently alleged he is disabled, defendants knew of his disability, the accommodation to address hazardous conditions was necessary to afford equal enjoyment of the dwelling, and defendants refused to make such accommodation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Telvon Taylor, a disabled tenant, sued Westland Real Estate Group, LLC and Hacienda Hills Apartment alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and wrongful eviction after he complained about hazardous conditions including mold exposure. The court granted Taylor's in forma pauperis application and, at the § 1915(e) screening stage, found his complaint stated a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim sufficient to proceed past screening.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10770715_2026_Cincinnati Insurance Company v. Lost Run Farm Community Association, Inc., Armour Property Management, LLC, Vincent Kroon, Ben Wheat, Scott Bruns, Ashlee Wheat, Edward Kowlowitz, Rebecca Kiell, John Garcia,
case_name: Cincinnati Insurance Company v. Lost Run Farm Community Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: The Garcias requested a fence to contain their dogs as an accommodation for Mary Kay Garcia's disability, which the Board denied.
key_holding: The insurer had no duty to defend or indemnify because the CGL coverage did not apply (the intentional discriminatory acts were not an 'occurrence') and the Wrongful Acts coverage exclusions barred coverage (dishonest/malicious acts, civil rights violations, emotional distress).
brief_summary: Cincinnati Insurance Company sought declaratory judgment that it had no duty to defend or indemnify Lost Run Farm HOA, its board members, and property management company Armour against the Garcias' discrimination claims in underlying state litigation. The court granted Cincinnati's summary judgment motion, finding no coverage under the CGL policy (intentional discriminatory acts did not constitute an 'occurrence') and no coverage under the Wrongful Acts policy (exclusions for dishonest/malicious acts and civil rights violations applied).
key_cases_cited: Jim Barna Log Sys. Midwest, Inc. v. Gen. Cas. Ins. Co. of Wisconsin, 791 N.E.2d 816 (Ind. Ct. App. 2003)
Auto-Owners Ins. Co. v. Harvey, 842 N.E.2d 1279 (Ind. 2006)

source_file: 10773145_2025_Valery Kantsepolsky v. River Shores Association, Inc._flsd
case_name: Valery Kantsepolsky v. River Shores Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with chemical sensitivities requested that defendant use disability-related precautions during any entry into her unit for exploratory demolition and repairs.
key_holding: The court recommended staying the FHA claims under Colorado River abstention doctrine rather than dismissing them, finding the parallel state proceedings involve substantially similar parties and issues, while dismissing the ADA claim because residential condominiums are not places of public accommodation subject to ADA Title III.
brief_summary: Valery Kantsepolsky, a condominium owner with chemical sensitivities, sued the River Shores Association alleging the HOA violated the FHA by denying her requests for disability-related precautions during unit inspections and by refusing to engage in an interactive process. The magistrate judge recommended granting the motion to dismiss in part, dismissing the ADA claim and staying the FHA claims pending resolution of parallel state court proceedings involving the same parties and substantially similar issues.
key_cases_cited: Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Colorado River Water Conservation District v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976)
Taveras v. Bank of Am., N.A., 89 F.4th 1279 (11th Cir. 2024)
Ambrosia Coal & Constr. Co. v. Morales, 368 F.3d 1320 (11th Cir. 2004)
Moorer v. Demopolis Waterworks & Sewer Bd., 374 F.3d 994 (11th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 10774111_2026_Jacob O’Connor v. Collett’s Mountain Resorts, Inc. d_b_a Flaming Gorge Resort and Maretta Bair_utd
case_name: Jacob O'Connor v. Collett's Mountain Resorts, Inc. d/b/a Flaming Gorge Resort and Maretta Bair
accommodation_description: Request to keep a trained diabetic alert service dog in employer-provided housing as a reasonable accommodation for Type 1 diabetes
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding that (1) employer-provided housing is a 'privilege of employment' covered by the ADA, (2) the plaintiff sufficiently requested an accommodation without magic words, (3) the accommodation was plausibly necessary despite the plaintiff spending nights without the dog, and (4) the close timing between the accommodation request and termination supported a plausible inference of disability-based discrimination.
brief_summary: Jacob O'Connor, a Type 1 diabetic employee at Collett's Mountain Resorts, sued his employer after being denied the ability to keep his trained diabetic alert service dog in employer-provided housing and subsequently being terminated. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that employer-provided housing qualifies as a privilege of employment under the ADA, that the plaintiff sufficiently requested an accommodation without magic words, and that the close timing between the accommodation request and termination supported plausible claims of failure to accommodate, wrongful termination, and retaliation under both the ADA and FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Courage to Change Ranches Holding Co. v. El Paso
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City
Arnal v. Aspenview Condo. Ass'n

source_file: 10776301_2025_Rosanna Barrera v. LDG Riverstone LP, ET AL._txwd
case_name: Rosanna Barrera v. LDG Riverstone LP, ET AL.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendants provide accessible design and construction including safe, accessible routes from the parking garage to her residential unit, and requested a reserved accessible parking spot in front of her unit due to her mobility limitations.
key_holding: The magistrate recommended dismissing plaintiff's Section 1983 claims for failure to plead state action, but found she stated non-frivolous FHA claims for design and construction violations under § 3604(f)(3)(C) and for failure to accommodate under § 3604(f)(3)(B) by denying her request for an accessible parking spot.
brief_summary: Rosanna Barrera, a tenant with mobility impairments, sued LDG Riverstone LP and related entities alleging the apartment complex's design lacked accessible routes from the parking garage to her unit and that defendants denied her request for a reserved accessible parking spot in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The magistrate recommended dismissing her Section 1983 constitutional claims for failing to plead state action, but found she adequately stated claims under the FHA for design and construction violations and failure to accommodate, allowing those FHA claims to proceed past in forma pauperis screening.
key_cases_cited: Providence Behav. Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)
Bowman v. RLB Inv. Partners, LLC, 2016 WL 7326622 (E.D. Tex. 2016)
Whitaker v. W. Vill. Ltd. P'ship, 2004 WL 2008502 (N.D. Tex. 2004)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Priester v. Lowndes Cnty., 354 F.3d 414 (5th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 10776572_2026_Steven E. Hobbs, Sr. v. Igor Goncharko, et al._ilnd
case_name: Steven E. Hobbs, Sr. v. Igor Goncharko, et al.
accommodation_description: Hobbs requested expedited rodent remediation as a disability accommodation because his mobility impairment prevented him from cleaning infested areas without re-injury risk, and eviction settlement/payment plan due to disability-related financial hardship.
key_holding: The court granted Coldwell Banker's motion to dismiss the racial disparate impact claim with prejudice, but denied Landlord Defendants' motion on the failure-to-accommodate claim, finding that rodent remediation is plausibly a service accommodation rather than a modification, while eviction-related requests fail because financial hardship is not unique to disabled persons.
brief_summary: Steven Hobbs, an African American tenant with a mobility disability from spinal fractures, sued his Chicago landlords and Coldwell Banker under the Fair Housing Act alleging racial discrimination, disability discrimination, and retaliation. The court granted Coldwell Banker's motion to dismiss his disparate impact racial discrimination claim with prejudice for failure to plead causation, but denied the landlords' motion on Hobbs's failure-to-accommodate claim regarding rodent remediation, which the court found plausibly alleges a service accommodation necessary due to his disability.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10776617_2026_Daniel T. Doria v. Happy Jack Lodge LLC, et al._azd
case_name: Daniel T. Doria v. Happy Jack Lodge LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Accommodation request for disability; specific accommodation not identified in complaint.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because plaintiff failed to allege any non-conclusory facts establishing he has a disability under the FHA or describing what discriminatory housing practice occurred when defendants allegedly ignored his accommodation request.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Daniel T. Doria sued Happy Jack Lodge LLC and others alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to accommodate his disability and for retaliation after requesting accommodation. The Court granted IFP status but dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding Doria alleged only conclusory statements that he is disabled and that defendants ignored his accommodation request without providing factual details about his disability or the nature of the alleged discriminatory act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gilligan v. Jamco Dev. Corp., 108 F.3d 246 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10779646_2026_Orane M. Cornish Jr. v. Cheyanne Tripp et al._ctd
case_name: Orane M. Cornish Jr. v. Cheyanne Tripp et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges his landlord conditioned continued housing on submission to unwanted sexual advances, creating a hostile housing environment.
key_holding: The court denied Defendant Tripp's motion to dismiss Plaintiff's FHA hostile housing environment claim, finding Plaintiff sufficiently alleged that his landlord conditioned housing on submission to unwanted sexual advances on the basis of sex, but granted Defendant Paris's motion to dismiss because Paris, as a non-landlord associate, lacked sufficient connection to Plaintiff’s housing arrangement.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Orane Cornish, proceeding pro se, sued Defendant Tripp (his landlord) and Defendant Paris (Tripp's associate) under the Trafficking Victims Protection Act and Fair Housing Act, alleging he was coerced into unpaid labor and sex trafficking, and that his landlord created a hostile housing environment by conditioning housing on submission to unwanted sexual advances. The court denied both defendants' motions to dismiss the TVPA claims and denied Tripp's motion to dismiss the FHA hostile housing environment claim, but dismissed the FHA claim against Paris without leave to amend because he was not the plaintiff's landlord.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Cain v. Rambert, No. 13-CV-5807 (MKB), 2014 WL 2440596 (E.D.N.Y. May 30, 2014)

source_file: 10781103_2026_KORTNEY DONAVON v. RICHMOND-HENRICO ARMS LIMITED PARTNERSHIP_vaed
case_name: Kortney Donavon v. Richmond-Henrico Arms Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: Plaintiff asserted Fair Housing Act claims related to an eviction proceeding, but the specific accommodation requested is not described in the opinion due to the Court's jurisdictional dismissal.
key_holding: The Court lacked subject matter jurisdiction because the eviction had already been executed (mootness), the Fair Housing Act claims were improperly raised in federal court after being presented to the state court, and the Anti-Injunction Act prohibited federal courts from enjoining state court proceedings.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kortney Donavon filed an application to proceed in forma pauperis and attempted to remove a state court eviction proceeding to federal court, asserting Fair Housing Act claims under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604(f) and 3617. The Court granted IFP status but dismissed the action for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the case moot (eviction already executed), the claims improperly brought in federal court after being raised in state proceedings, and federal injunctive relief barred by the Anti-Injunction Act.
key_cases_cited: Porter v. Clarke, 852 F.3d 358 (4th Cir. 2017)
Atlantic C. L. R. Co. v. Bhd. of Locomotive Eng'rs, 398 U.S. 281 (1970)
Denny's Inc. v. Cake, 364 F.3d 521 (4th Cir. 2004)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp., 546 U.S. 500 (2006)

source_file: 10782307_2026_Jesse Lewis Walker v. Lukrom REIT LLC, et al._azd
case_name: Jesse Lewis Walker v. Lukrom REIT LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's dog was left in the apartment during eviction; the accommodation request is not clearly articulated as a reasonable accommodation but is framed as familial status discrimination and retaliation for requesting repairs.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims for failure to state a claim because the conclusory allegations of familial status discrimination and retaliation lacked sufficient factual matter to make the claims plausible under Ashcroft v. Iqbal and Bell Atlantic v. Twombly; the court granted leave to amend the FHA claims but denied leave to amend the PTFA claim as futile.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jesse Walker, who was evicted for non-payment of rent, sued his landlord Lukrom REIT LLC alleging Fair Housing Act claims for familial status discrimination and retaliation for requesting repairs. The court screened the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) and dismissed it for failure to state a claim, finding the FHA allegations were conclusory and unsupported by sufficient factual matter, but granted leave to amend the FHA claims against Lukrom while denying leave to amend the PTFA claim as futile.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ferdik v. Bonzelet, 963 F.2d 1258 (9th Cir. 1992)
Lyons v. Gene B. Glick Company, Inc., 844 F. App'x 866 (7th Cir. 2021)
Logan v. U.S. Bank Nat. Ass'n, 722 F.3d 1163 (9th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 10783818_2026_Tami Huston; Justin Huston v. Willamette Valley Rentals, LLC; Nick Sharifi; Roya Sharifi_ord
case_name: Tami Huston; Justin Huston v. Willamette Valley Rentals, LLC; Nick Sharifi; Roya Sharifi
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case involves refusal to rent to foster parents with disabled children based on familial status and disability
key_holding: Plaintiffs, who are foster parents of children with disabilities, are entitled to summary judgment on their Fair Housing Act claims because the landlord's treatment of their rental application changed adversely after learning of their familial status and children's disabilities, while a similarly situated applicant without children or disabilities was offered more favorable terms.
brief_summary: Foster parents with disabled children sued a property management company for housing discrimination after their rental application was denied following disclosure of their family composition and children's disabilities. The court granted plaintiffs' motion for partial summary judgment on federal and state Fair Housing Act claims, finding that defendants discriminated against plaintiffs based on familial status and disability by refusing to rent and offering less favorable terms compared to applicants without children or disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Pacific Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
City of Edmunds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Conlon v. United States, 474 F.3d 616 (9th Cir. 2007)

source_file: 10784337_2026_Shasta Partee, on behalf of herself and all other occupants v. Powers Properties_scd
case_name: Shasta Partee, on behalf of herself and all other occupants v. Powers Properties
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested accommodation to allow her son with mental health disabilities (ADHD, bipolar disorder, emotional dysregulation) to remain in the apartment despite behavioral incidents rather than having her lease non-renewed.
key_holding: The court held that plaintiff failed to establish the accommodation was necessary to afford an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling because the requests neither specified what accommodation was sought nor identified how it would ameliorate the son's mental impairments, and alternatively that the direct threat exception applied given documented evidence of the son posing safety risks.
brief_summary: Shasta Partee sued Powers Properties, the property manager of Briar Grove Apartments, alleging the landlord failed to accommodate her adult son's mental health disabilities and retaliated against her after she requested accommodations to prevent lease non-renewal based on the son's behavioral incidents. The court granted summary judgment for the defendant, finding plaintiff failed to establish the requested accommodation was necessary and that the direct threat exception applied given evidence of the son pointing a realistic weapon at people, jumping from a balcony, and committing arson.
key_cases_cited: Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 10784366_2026_Laura Saitta v. Greystar Real Estate Partners, LLC; et al._casd
case_name: Laura Saitta v. Greystar Real Estate Partners, LLC; et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; claim is for gender-based discrimination in housing terms and conditions
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act sex discrimination claim because the SAC contained only conclusory allegations without factual content sufficient to plausibly infer gender-based discriminatory animus, and plaintiff was currently renting a two-bedroom penthouse unit contradicting her theory that women were being forced into one-bedroom apartments.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Laura Saitta, proceeding pro se, sued Greystar Real Estate Partners (property manager), Kilroy Realty (property owner), and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Act sex discrimination and Section 1985 conspiracy claims related to her rental at One Paseo Village in San Diego, claiming she was discriminatorily subjected to unequal housing terms and pricing because she is a woman. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss without leave to amend, finding plaintiff's FHA sex discrimination claim conclusory and factually unsupported, and declined supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
In re Gilead Scis. Secs. Litig., 536 F.3d 1049 (9th Cir. 2008)
Karim-Panahi v. Los Angeles Police Dep't, 839 F.2d 621 (9th Cir. 1988)
Griffin v. Breckenridge, 403 U.S. 88 (1971)

source_file: 10784453_2026_Lori Robins v. Waterford at Aberdeen Association, Inc., et al._flsd
case_name: Lori Robins v. Waterford at Aberdeen Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Visually impaired plaintiff requested to submit HOA work orders via email as an alternative to the inaccessible website.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff summary judgment on her reasonable accommodation claim for email work order submission, finding that Waterford constructively rejected the request by ignoring it after reviewing it; however, the court denied summary judgment on her pool gate lock modification claim because she failed to offer to pay for the modification as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lori Robins, who is visually impaired, sued Waterford at Aberdeen Association, Inc. (an HOA) for discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, alleging the HOA failed to accommodate her disability by refusing to provide accessible alternatives to its inaccessible website. The court granted partial summary judgment for Robins on her claim that the HOA constructively rejected her reasonable accommodation request to submit work orders via email, while denying her modification claim for the pool gate lock because she failed to offer to pay for it as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A).
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Wood v. Briarwinds Condo. Ass'n Bd. of Directors, 2009 WL 10668944 (S.D. Fla. Mar. 20, 2009)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

source_file: 10786318_2026_Lorin Grossman v. B_J Services of Miami, LLC, et al._flsd
case_name: Lorin Grossman v. B&J Services of Miami, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, alleges landlord refused to show or lease property to her because of her physical disability.
key_holding: The court entered default judgment on Counts III–XI (Florida FHA claims and federal § 3604(c), (d), and (f)(1) claims) based on disability discrimination, but denied default judgment on Counts I and II because federal § 3604(a) and (b) do not cover disability-based discrimination.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lorin Grossman, a wheelchair user, sued B&J Services of Miami and its agent alleging they refused to show or lease a rental property to her because of her disability after representing the property was unavailable while showing it to able-bodied prospective tenants. The court adopted the magistrate judge's recommendation to enter default judgment on most claims under the Florida Fair Housing Act and federal § 3604(c), (d), and (f)(1), awarding $132,200 in damages, but denied default judgment on federal § 3604(a) and (b) claims because those provisions do not cover disability discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Banai v. Sec'y, U.S. Dept. of Hous. & Urban Dev., 102 F.3d 1203 (11th Cir. 1997)
Eagle Hosp. Physicians, LLC v. SRG Consulting, Inc., 561 F.3d 1298 (11th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 10793996_2026_Chelsie Nitschke and Cynthia George v. 326 Welch Partners; Avenue Construction, LLC; Barnett Design Studio, LLC_tnmd
case_name: Chelsie Nitschke and Cynthia George v. 326 Welch Partners; Avenue Construction, LLC; Barnett Design Studio, LLC
accommodation_description: This is a design and construction accessibility case, not an individual accommodation request case; the plaintiffs alleged the apartment complex failed to meet FHA accessibility requirements.
key_holding: The court granted judgment on the pleadings and found defendants liable for violating 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C) and the ADA by failing to design and construct the apartment complex in compliance with federal accessibility standards.
brief_summary: Two disabled Tennessee residents sued the owner, construction company, and architectural firm of a Nashville apartment complex, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act's design and construction requirements. The defendants admitted most allegations and acknowledged violations of the FHA and Fair Housing Act Accessibility Guidelines, with the court granting judgment on the pleadings and finding defendants liable for failing to design and construct accessible covered multifamily dwellings under § 3604(f)(3)(C).
key_cases_cited: Meyer Nat. Foods, LLC v. Liberty Mut. Fire Ins. Co., 218 F. Supp. 3d 1034 (D. Neb. 2016)
Grindstaff v. Green, 133 F.3d 416 (6th Cir. 1998)
Tucker v. Middleburg-Legacy Place, 539 F.3d 545 (6th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 10795232_2026_Rosalind Holmes v. Warren County Metropolitan Housing Authority_ohsd
case_name: Rosalind Holmes v. Warren County Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for a two-bedroom unit to permit plaintiff's mother, a disability-related live-in caregiver, to be included in the household
key_holding: The court recommended denying the TRO and preliminary injunction because plaintiff failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable harm, or that the requested affirmative relief fell within the proper scope of preliminary injunctive relief.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rosalind Holmes sued Warren County Metropolitan Housing Authority under the Fair Housing Act alleging the agency denied her reasonable accommodation request for a two-bedroom unit to permit her mother to live with her as a disability-related live-in caregiver. The court recommended denying plaintiff's motion for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction, finding she failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on the merits or irreparable harm warranting extraordinary relief.
key_cases_cited: Liberty Coins, LLC v. Goodman, 748 F.3d 682 (6th Cir. 2014)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Overstreet v. Lexington Fayette Urban Cnty. Gov't, 305 F.3d 566 (6th Cir. 2002)
Jones v. City of Monroe, 341 F.3d 474 (6th Cir. 2003)
Chabad of S. Ohio & Congregation Lubavitch v. City of Cincinnati, 363 F.3d 427 (6th Cir. 2004)

case_name: Connecticut Fair Housing Center and Carmen Arroyo v. CoreLogic Rental Property Solutions, LLC
accommodation_description: Arroyo requested that CoreLogic accept her conservatorship certificate as proof of authority to access her son Mikhail's consumer file, rather than requiring a power of attorney which Mikhail, as a conservatee, could not execute.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed that CoreLogic did not violate the Fair Housing Act because it did not proximately cause the denial of housing, and Arroyo failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate impact or failure to accommodate since she never provided a facially valid conservatorship certificate even after being informed of the documentation requirement.
brief_summary: Connecticut Fair Housing Center and Carmen Arroyo sued CoreLogic Rental Property Solutions, which provides tenant screening reports to housing providers, alleging that CoreLogic violated the Fair Housing Act by facilitating housing denials based on criminal history and by refusing to accept a conservatorship certificate as documentation for accessing a disabled conservatee's consumer file. The Second Circuit affirmed dismissal of the FHA claims, holding that CoreLogic did not proximately cause housing denials because landlords made independent discretionary decisions, and that Arroyo's failure-to-accommodate claim failed because she never provided a facially valid conservatorship certificate despite being informed of the requirement.
key_cases_cited: Staub v. Proctor Hospital, 562 U.S. 411 (2011)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Med., 144 S. Ct. 1540 (2024)

source_file: 10797202_2026_Leonor E Canales v. Phoenix Housing Authority, et al._azd
case_name: Leonor E Canales v. Phoenix Housing Authority, et al.
accommodation_description: Request that PHA accept alternative medical documentation instead of required physician-signed accommodation form to process her Section 8 reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's failure to accommodate claim because she failed to allege sufficient facts about what specific accommodation she requested, how it was necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, and why it was reasonable, though granting leave to amend.
brief_summary: Leonor Canales, a Section 8 participant with multiple mental health disabilities including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and bipolar disorder, sued the Phoenix Housing Authority alleging it failed to provide reasonable accommodations by refusing to accept her medical records instead of a required physician-signed form and failed to engage in an interactive process. The court dismissed her reasonable accommodation claim without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding she did not adequately allege what accommodation she requested or why it was necessary and reasonable, though it allowed her retaliation claim to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica, 998 F.3d 852 (9th Cir. 2021)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 10799433_2026_Joyce Ann Jefferson v. Landmark Apartment Associates; Ross Financial Services, Inc.; Cris Barnett_ilcd
case_name: Joyce Ann Jefferson v. Landmark Apartment Associates; Ross Financial Services, Inc.; Cris Barnett
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; plaintiff alleges racial harassment, disability-related discrimination, and constructive eviction rather than a request for accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The court granted leave to amend, finding that Jefferson's proposed complaint sufficiently alleged plausible § 3604(b) and § 3604(c) Fair Housing Act claims based on allegations that employees engaged in racial harassment, used racial slurs, and created a hostile housing environment that constructively evicted her from the property.
brief_summary: Joyce Ann Jefferson, a tenant in federally subsidized housing owned by Landmark Apartment Associates and managed by Ross Financial Services, sued alleging that employees unlawfully entered her apartment, stole and sold her belongings on Facebook Marketplace, and then engaged in a campaign of racial harassment including slurs, threats, and intimidation that constructively evicted her. The court granted Jefferson's motion for leave to amend her complaint, rejecting Landmark's futility argument and finding sufficient factual allegations to state plausible Fair Housing Act claims for racial discrimination in the terms and privileges of tenancy under § 3604(b) and disability discrimination under § 3604(c).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Community, LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Runnion ex rel. Runnion v. Girl Scouts of Greater Chi. & Nw. Ind., 786 F.3d 510 (7th Cir. 2015)
Grieveson v. Anderson, 538 F.3d 763 (7th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Brown v. Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with disability affecting executive function requested that insurance claims adjuster explain water remediation procedures in simplified terms.
key_holding: All of plaintiff's claims, including FHA reasonable accommodation claims, were dismissed with prejudice as time-barred because the alleged discriminatory conduct occurred in 2016 but plaintiff did not file suit until 2024, well beyond the applicable statutes of limitations.
brief_summary: Randy Brown sued Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company eight years after a 2016 water damage claim, alleging violations of the ADA and FHA based on the insurer's failure to provide effective communication accommodations about water remediation procedures. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice as time-barred, finding no basis for statutory tolling or equitable exception.

source_file: 10802670_2026_Sandy Phillips v. Stoddard County Circuit Court, et al._moed
case_name: Sandy Phillips v. Stoddard County Circuit Court, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled tenant, seeks telephonic court hearing participation as an ADA accommodation, and alleges housing-related accommodations regarding repairs and relocation were denied.
key_holding: The Court found multiple legal deficiencies in the complaint including improper joinder of unrelated claims against different defendants, failure to plead essential elements of FHA retaliation (no nexus between protected activity and adverse action), and insufficient facts for Title II ADA claim; Court granted leave to amend.
brief_summary: Self-represented plaintiff Sandy Phillips filed a civil action against private landlords and state court defendants alleging Fair Housing Act retaliation, ADA Title II discrimination, and § 1983 claims arising from substandard housing conditions and denial of telephonic court access. The Court conducted initial review under 28 U.S.C. § 1915 and found the complaint fails to state plausible claims due to improper joinder of unrelated claims, failure to plead FHA retaliation elements, and insufficient ADA allegations, ordering plaintiff to file a second amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Rooker v. Fidelity Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
George v. Smith, 507 F.3d 605 (7th Cir. 2007)

source_file: 10803438_2026_Tanesha A. Turner v. 3526-28 E 9TH AVE TRUST, et al._innd
case_name: Tanesha A. Turner v. 3526-28 E 9TH AVE TRUST, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges discrimination based on familial status, sex, and domestic violence history, and retaliation for reporting housing violations.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA discrimination claim for failing to allege facts connecting defendants' actions to her protected characteristics, and dismissed her retaliation claim for failing to adequately plead protected activity under Rule 8 and Twombly.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tanesha A. Turner sued her landlord, a housing assistance organization (Continuum of Care Network), and related individuals alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on familial status, sex, and domestic violence history, as well as retaliation for reporting housing violations. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to state a plausible claim because it lacked factual allegations connecting defendants' conduct to any protected characteristic and failed to adequately plead protected activity for the retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Rooker v. Fidelity Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
District of Columbia Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 10804696_2026_Karen DeMoor v. Freeman Webb Company, Realtors, et al._tnmd
case_name: Karen DeMoor v. Freeman Webb Company, Realtors, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the plaintiff alleged disability-related discrimination in housing maintenance and eviction without requesting any accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because, although she alleged she was disabled and discriminated against, she failed to allege that she requested a reasonable accommodation related to her disability, and she did not allege that defendants' actions were taken in response to any accommodation request.
brief_summary: Karen DeMoor, a tenant in Lebanon, Tennessee receiving Social Security Disability benefits, sued Freeman Webb Company (property manager) and individuals alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on delayed repairs to her window and smoke detectors and subsequent eviction. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice, finding that while plaintiff adequately alleged disability, she failed to state a plausible FHA claim because she never requested a reasonable accommodation related to her disability.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 10806862_2026_William M. Pickard, III v. American Pride Properties, LLC, et al._alnd
case_name: William M. Pickard, III v. American Pride Properties, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges APP Defendants refused to sell him property due to his disability-related criminal and litigious history (depression and mania).
key_holding: The court dismissed SPM Defendants' claims because plaintiff's FHA claims were conclusory and failed to allege SPM took prohibited discriminatory action; the court dismissed APP Defendants' claims under Colorado River abstention because parallel state court proceedings involving the same parties and substantially similar issues regarding the property sale were already pending.
brief_summary: William Pickard sued American Pride Properties (APP) and SPM LLC (his former landlord) alleging they discriminated against him due to his mental health disability by refusing to sell him property and by sharing derogatory information. The court abstained from the APP claims under Colorado River due to pending state litigation, and dismissed SPM claims for failure to state an FHA claim because plaintiff provided only conclusory allegations without facts showing SPM took prohibited discriminatory action.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Taveras v. Bank of Am., N.A., 89 F.4th 1279 (11th Cir. 2024)
Jackson-Platts v. Gen. Elec. Cap. Corp., 727 F.3d 1127 (11th Cir. 2013)
Colorado River Water Conservation Dist. v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976)

source_file: 10806920_2026_Harmony Home of Huntington, Inc. v. Executive Director, Indiana Department of Homeland Security, The Members of the Fire Prevention and Building Safety Commission, Indiana Department of Homeland Security_in
case_name: Harmony Home of Huntington, Inc. v. Executive Director, Indiana Department of Homeland Security
accommodation_description: Request for governmental exemption from Class 1 building code requirements applied to group recovery home for persons with substance use disorders
key_holding: The court found DHS engaged in intentional discrimination by classifying the Home as a Class 1 structure while treating similarly situated nuclear family and foster homes as Class 2, using a flexible yet targeted definition of 'tenant' that functions as a proxy for discrimination against persons with disabilities.
brief_summary: Harmony Home of Huntington, Inc., which operates a group recovery home for men with substance abuse disorders, sued the Indiana Department of Homeland Security and the Fire Prevention and Building Safety Commission alleging that DHS unlawfully classified the Home as a Class 1 structure under Indiana building codes while treating similarly situated nuclear family and foster homes as Class 2, violating the Fair Housing Amendments Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court granted summary judgment for Plaintiff, finding that DHS engaged in intentional discrimination by applying a facially neutral 'tenant' definition in a manner designed to impose Class 1 building requirements on group homes for persons with disabilities while exempting comparable family homes, and entered a permanent injunction requiring DHS to treat the Home as a Class 2 structure.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Next Step, 755 F. Supp. 3d 1078 (S.D. Ind. 2024)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Gresham v. Windrush Partners, Ltd., 730 F.2d 1417 (11th Cir. 1984)

source_file: 10808207_2026_Edward Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc., et al._mdd
case_name: Edward Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Homeowner with mobility disability requests exemption from HOA requirement to reinstall columns and railings (balustrade) on his flat roof, which he removed due to weather-related damage that caused him physical harm when maintaining them.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction and reconsideration, holding that plaintiff's claims regarding the balustrade fail to establish the accommodation is 'necessary' under the FHA because they do not show a direct amelioration of his disability's effects required by Bryant Woods.
brief_summary: Edward Siguel, a disabled homeowner in the King Farm HOA community, sued the HOA and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations after they denied his requests to not reinstall structural columns and railings on his roof that caused him physical harm due to his disability. The court previously dismissed most claims, and in this opinion denied plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction and reconsideration, finding that his proposed accommodation is not 'necessary' under the FHA because it does not provide direct amelioration of his disability's effects.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty, Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Grp. Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corp., 144 F.4th 522 (4th Cir. 2025)
Acosta v. A&G Mgmt. Co., Inc., 2014 WL 1236918 (D. Md. 2014)
Gavin v. Spring Ridge Conservancy, Inc., 934 F. Supp. 685 (D. Md. 1995)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 10808242_2026_Keith David Nizwantowski v. Monarch, et al._nced
case_name: Keith David Nizwantowski v. Monarch, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; complaint alleges disability discrimination, retaliation, and hostile housing environment under §§ 3604 and 3617.
key_holding: Court allowed Plaintiff to amend complaint because the original pleading contained only conclusory allegations parroting the FHA statute without identifying the nature of his disability, specific discriminatory acts, or a causal link between his disability and the alleged wrongful conduct.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Keith David Nizwantowski sued Monarch NC (property management) and The Arc of North Carolina Inc. (apartment owner), alleging disability discrimination, retaliation, and hostile housing environment under the Fair Housing Act. The court conducted frivolity review and found the complaint lacked sufficient factual allegations to state a claim, but allowed amendment to permit Plaintiff to particularize his claims by March 13, 2026.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Haines v. Kerner, 404 U.S. 519 (1972)
Estelle v. Gamble, 429 U.S. 97 (1976)
Adams v. Rice, 40 F.3d 72 (4th Cir. 1994)
Beaudett v. City of Hampton, 775 F.2d 1274 (4th Cir. 1985)

source_file: 10808264_2026_Imani Lawson v. Dice Holdings LLC, Ozarks Communities, Theresa Knoth, and Daniel_ilsd
case_name: Imani Lawson v. Dice Holdings LLC, Ozarks Communities, Theresa Knoth, and Daniel
accommodation_description: Plaintiff appears to allege retaliation for disputing Ameren utility bills, lease termination, and eviction proceedings, though no specific accommodation request is articulated in the complaint.
key_holding: The court found it lacked subject matter jurisdiction because the plaintiff failed to allege membership in any protected class and failed to allege she engaged in protected activity under the FHA, and the Rooker-Feldman doctrine barred review of the underlying state court eviction judgment.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Imani Lawson filed a complaint against her landlord/property management defendants seeking to vacate a state court eviction judgment, alleging Fair Housing Act violations including retaliation for disputing utility bills. The court found it lacked subject matter jurisdiction because the plaintiff failed to plead membership in any protected class or engagement in protected activity under the FHA, and Rooker-Feldman barred review of the state court judgment.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Nationscredit Home Equity Servs. Corp. v. City of Chicago

source_file: 10808764_2026_Terry Rockette v. Carpenter Management Company, et al._msnd
case_name: Terry Rockette v. Carpenter Management Company, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's vague reference to a request for mail-in recertification when the accommodation was allegedly provided to an out-of-state female tenant, though this was not substantively analyzed as a reasonable accommodation claim.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish the required causal connection between his protected HUD complaints and defendants' alleged adverse conduct, and his claims of harassment and discrimination rested solely on conclusory assertions insufficient to survive summary judgment.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Terry Rockette sued Carpenter Management Company and its employees alleging racial and gender discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act, retaliation for filing HUD complaints, and other federal and state claims. The court granted defendants' motion for summary judgment, dismissing all federal claims with prejudice because plaintiff failed to establish causation between his protected activity and any alleged adverse action, and his conclusory assertions were insufficient to support his discrimination and harassment claims.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Scruggs v. Marshall Hous. Auth., 232 F.3d 210 (5th Cir. 2000)
Hood v. Pope, 627 F. App'x 295 (5th Cir. 2015)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)

case_name: Dickerson v. BPP PCV Owners LLC
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request; this is an appeal from dismissal of racial discrimination and retaliation claims arising from a baseless theft accusation by a building security officer.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that the complaint failed to plead intentional racial discrimination under the FHA because the security guard's accusation was based on the plaintiff's resemblance to the person in security footage, not racial animus, and the FHA retaliation claim failed for lack of allegations connecting the incident to prior complaints.
brief_summary: Gloria D. Dickerson, a 72-year-old Black woman residing at Peter Cooper Village in Manhattan, sued BPP PCV Owners LLC alleging racial discrimination after a building safety officer accused her of theft based on security camera footage. The Second Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, finding that Dickerson failed to plead intentional discrimination because the accusation was based on physical resemblance to the person in the footage rather than racial animus, and her retaliation claim failed for lack of causal connection to any prior protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Gilead Comm. Servs., Inc. v. Town of Cromwell, 112 F.4th 93 (2d Cir. 2024)

source_file: 10814487_2026_Ronald Yocca, Rachel Estates, L.P., My Brother's House v. Allegheny County Health Department_pawd
case_name: Ronald Yocca, Rachel Estates, L.P., My Brother's House v. Allegheny County Health Department
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested exemption from ACHD Rooming House permitting and inspection requirements based on the Oxford House model, arguing the recovery house residents live as a family unit and should be treated as a single-family residence.
key_holding: The court dismissed the disparate treatment and disparate impact claims but denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged all three elements of a § 3604(f)(3)(B) claim: refusal, reasonable accommodation, and necessity for equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs My Brother's House (a drug and alcohol recovery house), its Executive Director Ronald Yocca, and property owner Rachel Estates sued Allegheny County Health Department seeking to be treated as a single-family residence rather than a Rooming House under ACHD regulations. The court granted ACHD's motion to dismiss the disparate treatment and disparate impact claims for failure to plead discriminatory animus or statistical disparity, but denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged that exemption from permitting requirements was necessary for residents to maintain their recovery in a family-like community setting.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Assoc., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 11161991_2023_Evelyn Rivera v. Jimmy Ducharme et al._nhd
case_name: Evelyn Rivera v. Jimmy Ducharme et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendant interfered with her housing rights through racially discriminatory harassment including use of racial slurs and snow plowing onto her vehicle.
key_holding: Defendant's motion for summary judgment on plaintiff's FHA claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 is denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether defendant engaged in severe or pervasive harassment based on plaintiff's race and national origin, including direct evidence of defendant's use of racial slurs.
brief_summary: Evelyn Rivera, a Puerto Rican woman, sued her neighbor Jimmy Ducharme claiming he violated the Fair Housing Act by harassing her because of her race and national origin, including using racial slurs and plowing snow onto her vehicle. The court denied Ducharme's motion for summary judgment on Rivera's § 3617 interference claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether his conduct constituted severe or pervasive harassment motivated by discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Chamberlin v. 101 Realty, Inc., 915 F.2d 777 (1st Cir. 1990)
Ortiz v. Sch. Bd. of Broward Cnty., Fla., 780 F. App'x 780 (11th Cir. 2019)

source_file: 11260497_2026_Eaton Corp. v. Angstrom Auto. Group, LLC_ca6
11267540 - Yocca v. ALLEGHENY COUNTY HEALTH DEPARTMENT
case_name: Ronald Yocca, Rachel Estates, L.P., My Brother's House v. Allegheny County Health Department
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested exemption from ACHD's Rooming House permitting and inspection requirements, arguing the recovery house should be treated as a single-family residence since residents live in a family-like setting.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim because Plaintiffs plausibly pled all three elements under § 3604(f)(3)(B): ACHD denied their request to be treated as a single-family residence, the accommodation is reasonable since ACHD could apply health and safety standards without the permitting scheme, and the accommodation is necessary because permit suspension would destabilize the recovery community essential to residents' sobriety.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs My Brother's House (a drug and alcohol recovery residence operator), its director Ronald Yocca, and property owner Rachel Estates sued Allegheny County Health Department after ACHD classified their Turtle Creek residence as an unlicensed Rooming House subject to permitting and inspection requirements. The court granted ACHD's motion to dismiss the disparate treatment and disparate impact claims for failure to plead sufficient facts, but denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, finding Plaintiffs adequately alleged that being treated as a single-family residence was a reasonable and necessary accommodation to afford recovery house residents equal opportunity to use and enjoy their housing.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Assoc., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Herbozo v. Fernandez
accommodation_description: The opinion does not identify a specific accommodation request; the complaint references Fair Housing Act claims but focuses on procedural deficiencies rather than any particular accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed the second amended complaint as an impermissible shotgun pleading and denied motions to remand, declining to reach the merits of the Fair Housing Act claims due to standing issues and failure to comply with Rule 8 pleading standards.
brief_summary: Pro se tenants Joseph Herbozo and Meghan Liesenfeld sued landlord Debbie Fernandez and others asserting Fair Housing Act claims related to an apartment complex. The court denied the plaintiffs' motions to remand the removed case to state court and dismissed their second amended complaint as an impermissible shotgun pleading that was 787 paragraphs and 200 pages long, contained claims for alleged discrimination against non-parties, and failed to separate causes of action into distinct counts.
key_cases_cited: Weiland v. Palm Beach Cnty. Sheriff's Off., 792 F.3d 1313 (11th Cir. 2015)
Pretka v. Kolter City Plaza II, Inc., 608 F.3d 744 (11th Cir. 2010)
Citizens Concerned About Our Children v. Sch. Bd. of Broward Cnty., 193 F.3d 1285 (11th Cir. 1999)
Chavez v. Sec'y Fla. Dep't of Corr., 647 F.3d 1057 (11th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 11267542 - Robinson v. BRAZOS VALLEY COUNCIL OF GOVERNMENTS
case_name: Robinson v. Brazos Valley Council of Governments
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motions for court-appointed counsel under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(1) because plaintiffs had neither sought nor been granted in forma pauperis status and had not demonstrated exceptional circumstances warranting appointment.
brief_summary: Anthony and Latoya Robinson sued the Brazos Valley Council of Governments, BVCOG employees, Heritage Apartments employees, and a former neighbor, alleging Fair Housing Act violations and that the termination of their Section 8 housing voucher was arbitrary and capricious. The court denied plaintiffs' motions for court-appointed counsel, finding the motions premature (no IFP application filed) and that plaintiffs failed to demonstrate exceptional circumstances justifying appointment.
key_cases_cited: Ulmer v. Chancellor, 691 F.2d 209 (5th Cir. 1982)
Lopez v. Reyes, 692 F.2d 15 (5th Cir. 1982)
Naranjo v. Thompson, 809 F.3d 793 (5th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 11267543 - PLACE OF GRACE, INC. v. EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR
case_name: Place of Grace, Inc. v. Executive Director
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Place of Grace operates group recovery homes for women with substance use disorders; the discriminatory treatment involves DHS classifying these group homes as Class 1 (commercial) structures rather than Class 2 (residential) structures, subjecting them to stricter building requirements like sprinkler systems that are not imposed on foster homes or nuclear family residences.
key_holding: DHS engaged in intentional discrimination under the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act by classifying group recovery homes as Class 1 structures while not applying the same classification to foster homes or nuclear family homes, resulting in disparate treatment of persons with disabilities without legitimate justification.
brief_summary: Place of Grace, Inc., a non-profit operating group recovery homes for women with substance use disorders in Huntington, Indiana, sued the Indiana Department of Homeland Security, alleging that DHS's classification of its group homes as Class 1 (commercial) structures under Indiana building codes, while not applying the same classification to foster homes or nuclear family homes, violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court granted summary judgment for Place of Grace, issued a permanent injunction requiring DHS to treat the Guilford Street Home as a Class 2 structure, and awarded $206,232.11 in monetary damages under the Rehabilitation Act, finding that DHS engaged in intentional discrimination without legitimate justification.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Next Step, Inc. v. City of Evansville, 755 F. Supp. 3d 1071 (S.D. Ind. 2024)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)

source_file: 11267544 - HARMONY HOME OF HUNTINGTON, INC. v. EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR
case_name: Harmony Home of Huntington, Inc. v. Executive Director
accommodation_description: Group recovery home operator seeking to be classified as a Class 2 residential structure rather than Class 1 commercial structure under Indiana building codes, alleging discriminatory classification based on disability
key_holding: The court found that DHS's definition of 'tenant' was designed and interpreted to impose Class 1 building requirements on the group recovery home for persons with disabilities while not applying the same requirements to nuclear family or foster homes, constituting intentional discrimination with no legitimate justification.
brief_summary: Harmony Home of Huntington, Inc., a nonprofit operating group recovery homes for men with substance abuse disorders, sued the Indiana Department of Homeland Security and Fire Prevention and Building Safety Commission alleging that classifying its group home as a Class 1 structure under Indiana building codes, rather than a Class 2 residential structure like nuclear family or foster homes, constituted disability discrimination in violation of the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court granted summary judgment for Plaintiff, finding intentional discrimination because DHS's definition of 'tenant' was designed to target group homes for persons with disabilities while excluding similarly situated family homes, with no legitimate justification.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Next Step, Inc. v. City of Ogdensburg, 176 F. Supp. 3d 112 (N.D.N.Y. 2016)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Courage to Change, Inc. v. Joaquin, 73 F.4th 1184 (9th Cir. 2024)

case_name: Ramone Taylor v. Lisa Royal
accommodation_description: Unspecified medical and disability accommodation request denied during eviction proceedings
key_holding: The court dismissed Taylor's FHAA reasonable accommodation claims because his complaint contained only conclusory allegations that he 'repeatedly requested medical and disability accommodation' without pleading sufficient factual content to state a plausible claim for relief.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ramone Taylor sued his landlord and related entities under the ADA, FHAA, and federal Constitution alleging he is blind and suffers from PTSD, was subjected to eviction, and was denied unspecified disability accommodations. The court dismissed the FHAA reasonable accommodation claims for failure to state a plausible claim because Taylor's complaint contained only conclusory allegations without factual content allowing the court to draw a reasonable inference that any defendant violated the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Baypointe W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

source_file: 11267546 - NEXT STEP RECOVERY HOME, INC. v. Indiana
case_name: Next Step Recovery Home, Inc. v. State of Indiana
accommodation_description: Plaintiff operates a substance abuse recovery home and challenges DHS's classification of the home as a Class 1 (commercial) structure rather than a Class 2 (residential) structure under Indiana building laws, which the court finds constitutes intentional discrimination against persons with disabilities.
key_holding: DHS's facially neutral definition of 'tenant,' which requires each occupant to enter an independent occupancy agreement, is applied in a discriminatory manner to impose Class 1 building requirements on group homes for persons with disabilities while the same requirements are not imposed on nuclear family or foster homes, constituting intentional discrimination under the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act.
brief_summary: Next Step Recovery Home, Inc., a substance abuse recovery home operator, sued the State of Indiana and DHS, challenging the classification of its group recovery home as a Class 1 (commercial) structure under Indiana building laws that would require sprinkler systems and other commercial requirements not imposed on family homes. The court granted summary judgment for Plaintiff, finding DHS engaged in intentional disability discrimination by applying a facially neutral definition of 'tenant' that effectively targets group homes for persons with disabilities while the same classification is not applied to nuclear family or foster homes.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Pacific Shores Properties, LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Pyke v. Cuomo, 258 F.3d 107 (2d Cir. 2001)
Courage to Change Ranches Holding Co. v. El Paso County, 73 F.4th 1175 (10th Cir. 2023)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Lisa R. Wrighten v. Old Linco LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support/service animal (Bella, a dog) as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: Both cross-motions for summary judgment are denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether Plaintiff's emotional support animal was necessary to afford her an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, as the accommodation support letter did not sufficiently connect Bella to Plaintiff's specific disability-related need.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lisa Wrighten sued her landlords, Old Linco LLC and Charlie Oliver, alleging disability discrimination under the FHA arising from their handling of her service/emotional support animal Bella and related eviction actions. The Court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the animal accommodation was necessary to afford Plaintiff equal use and enjoyment of the dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Coots v. Tankersley, 2021 WL 3617841 (E.D. Tex. June 25, 2021)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Karen DeMoor v. Freeman Webb Company, Realtors, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation was requested; plaintiff alleged she was treated worse than other tenants regarding repairs and eviction due to her disability.
key_holding: The plaintiff's FHA claims were dismissed because she failed to allege that she requested a reasonable accommodation related to her disability, and her interference claim failed because she did not allege she exercised her right to request an accommodation under the FHA.
brief_summary: Karen DeMoor, a disabled tenant on Social Security Disability, sued Freeman Webb Company and related defendants for Fair Housing Act violations after being evicted from her townhome, alleging she was discriminated against regarding repairs and eviction. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice, finding she failed to state viable FHA claims because she did not request any reasonable accommodation related to her disability and failed to allege a causal connection between any protected activity and the defendants' conduct.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 11267549 - BITTENBENDER v. FAMILY SERVICES ASSOCIATION OF BUCKS COUNTY
case_name: Cynthia Bittenbender v. Family Services Association of Bucks County
accommodation_description: Request to keep emotional support animals (cat and bearded dragon) in emergency medical housing at Extended Stay America
key_holding: The court dismissed Bittenbender's FHA claims because Extended Stay America did not constitute a 'dwelling' under the FHA under the Columbus Country Club framework, and because she failed to name a proper defendant subject to FHA liability; her ADA claims failed because emotional support animals are explicitly excluded from ADA service animal protections.
brief_summary: Cynthia Bittenbender, a pro se plaintiff with multiple disabilities including autism and PTSD, sued Family Services Association of Bucks County, Extended Stay America, and others alleging Fair Housing Act and ADA violations after being ejected from emergency medical housing for keeping emotional support animals. The court dismissed her complaint with prejudice, finding that Extended Stay America was not a 'dwelling' under the FHA because she lacked intent to remain and did not view it as a residence, and that emotional support animals are not protected as service animals under the ADA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
United States v. Columbus Country Club, 915 F.2d 877 (3d Cir. 1990)
Lakeside Resort Enters., LP v. Bd. of Supervisors of Palmyra Twp., 455 F.3d 154 (3d Cir. 2006)
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware County, 983 F.2d 1277 (3d Cir. 1993)

source_file: 11267550 - Pella v. ATLANTIC COUNTY DEPARTMENT OF FAMILY AND COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
case_name: Pella v. Atlantic County Department of Family and Community Development
subsidy_program: Work First New Jersey; Emergency Assistance; SNAP
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, found 'overhoused' and denied Emergency Assistance benefits for refusing to downsize from three bedrooms to one, challenged the benefits determination under disability discrimination theories without requesting a specific accommodation.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Act does not apply to the administration of state public benefits programs, and plaintiff failed to plausibly allege that benefit denials occurred by reason of his disability or that he requested and was denied a reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Phillip Pella, who is disabled, sued Atlantic County and New Jersey state agencies alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, ADA, Section 1983, and Rehabilitation Act based on the denial of Emergency Assistance and SNAP benefits and a determination that he was 'overhoused.' The court dismissed all federal claims, holding that the FHA does not govern the administration of state public assistance benefits and that plaintiff failed to plausibly allege disability-based discrimination or a failure to accommodate.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
Oxford House, Inc. v. Twp. of N. Bergen, 158 F.4th 486 (3d Cir. 2025)

case_name: Keith David Nizwantowski v. Monarch, et al.
