accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleging disability discrimination, retaliation, and hostile housing environment but no specific accommodation request identified in the complaint.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff's complaint failed to state a viable FHA claim because it contained only conclusory allegations parroting the statute without factual support for the nature of the disability, specific discriminatory acts, or the link between disability and alleged wrongful conduct, but allowed amendment to particularize the complaint.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Keith David Nizwantowski sued Monarch NC (property management) and The Ark of North Carolina Inc. (apartment owner) alleging disability discrimination, retaliation, and hostile housing environment under the Fair Housing Act. The court conducted frivolity review under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) and found the complaint failed to state a claim because it contained only conclusory allegations without factual support for the disability, discriminatory acts, or causal connection, but allowed plaintiff to file an amended complaint to particularize his claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Affinity Recovery Ctr., LLC v. Town Commissioners of Sudlersville, No. 23-CV-00714-LKG, 2024 WL 149835 (D. Md. Jan. 12, 2024)
Martin v. Brondum, 535 F. App'x 242 (4th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 11267552 - Phillips v. STODDARD COUNTY CIRCUIT COURT
case_name: Sandy Phillips v. Stoddard County Circuit Court, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested telephonic participation in court hearings as an ADA/disability accommodation due to documented inability to drive to court proceedings.
key_holding: The Court found multiple deficiencies in Plaintiff's amended complaint including failure to state a prima facie FHA retaliation claim, improper joinder of unrelated claims, and insufficient facts to support Title II ADA claim; Plaintiff was ordered to file a second amended complaint on the proper form.
brief_summary: Self-represented Plaintiff Sandy Phillips filed an amended complaint against private landlords and state court entities alleging disability discrimination, FHA violations, and Title II ADA violations arising from substandard housing conditions and denial of telephonic court access. The Court conducted an initial review under 28 U.S.C. § 1915 and found Plaintiff's pleading legally deficient on multiple grounds, including failure to state a prima facie FHA retaliation claim, improper joinder of unrelated claims, and insufficient facts to support Title II ADA denial of access claims; the Court ordered Plaintiff to file a second amended complaint on the proper form.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
George v. Smith, 507 F.3d 605 (7th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Nitschke v. 326 Welch Partners
accommodation_description: Design and construction of apartment complex allegedly failed to meet FHA accessibility requirements for covered multifamily dwellings.
key_holding: The court granted judgment on the pleadings finding defendants violated § 3604(f)(3)(C) by failing to design and construct the apartment complex in compliance with FHA accessibility requirements, with defendants admitting most allegations and acknowledging liability.
brief_summary: Cynthia George and Chelsie Nitschke sued 326 Welch Partners, Avenue Construction, LLC, and Barnett Design Studio alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on the inaccessibility of a 129-unit apartment complex they attempted to access. The court granted judgment on the pleadings, finding defendants violated § 3604(f)(3)(C) by failing to design and construct the dwellings in compliance with FHA accessibility requirements, with the court reserving determination of damages and injunctive relief while parties proceed to mediation.
key_cases_cited: Grindstaff v. Green, 133 F.3d 416 (6th Cir. 1998)
Tucker v. Middleburg-Legacy Place, 539 F.3d 545 (6th Cir. 2008)
Iowa Beef Processors, Inc. v. Amalgamated Meat Cutters, 627 F.2d 853 (8th Cir. 1980)
Meyer Nat. Foods, LLC v. Liberty Mut. Fire Ins. Co., 218 F. Supp. 3d 1034 (D. Neb. 2016)

source_file: 11267554 - GRACE L. HERNANDEZ TRUST v. Lozano
11267555 - Grossman v. B&J SERVICES OF MIAMI, LLC
case_name: Lorin Grossman v. B&J Services of Miami, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff who uses a wheelchair sought to tour and rent a property, but defendants refused based on the property's allegedly unsuitable stone pathway for wheelchairs, renting it to others instead.
key_holding: Because defendants defaulted, the court accepted the complaint's well-pleaded facts as true, finding plaintiff plausibly alleged disability-based discrimination under § 3604(f)(1) and violations of §§ 3604(c) and (d), warranting default judgment and $132,200 in damages.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lorin Grossman, who uses a wheelchair, sued B&J Services of Miami and its agent Carla Rodas for refusing to show or lease a rental property to her based on her physical disability, despite her offering to demonstrate the allegedly unsuitable stone pathway posed no issue. The court entered default judgment against defendants for violations of Fair Housing Act provisions prohibiting disability discrimination, discriminatory advertising, and misrepresentation, awarding $132,200 in actual and punitive damages.
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Eagle Hosp. Physicians, LLC v. SRG Consulting, Inc., 561 F.3d 1298 (11th Cir. 2009)
Nishimatsu Const. Co. v. Houston Nat'l Bank, 515 F.2d 1200 (5th Cir. 1975)
Banai v. Sec'y, U.S. Dept. of Hous. & Urban Dev. on Behalf of Times, 102 F.3d 1203 (11th Cir. 1997)
Miller v. Paradise of Port Richey, Inc., 75 F. Supp. 2d 1342 (M.D. Fla. 1999)

source_file: 11267556 - Robins v. ABERDEEN AT GOLF & COUNTRY CLUB, INC.
case_name: Lori Robins v. Waterford at Aberdeen Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Visually impaired homeowner requested to email work orders to the property manager instead of submitting them through the inaccessible HOA website, and requested large-print copies of HOA documents mailed to her.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for plaintiff on her FHA reasonable accommodation claim because Waterford constructively rejected her request to email work orders by ignoring it after review, which is legally equivalent to denial; however, summary judgment was denied on her ACH form request (fact dispute exists) and her pool gate modification request (plaintiff failed to offer to pay as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A)).
brief_summary: Lori Robins, a visually impaired homeowner, sued her HOA alleging it discriminated against her by refusing reasonable accommodations for her disability, including refusing to let her email work orders instead of using the inaccessible website. The court granted summary judgment for Robins on the work-order email accommodation claim, finding Waterford constructively rejected her request by ignoring it, while denying summary judgment on other accommodation claims due to factual disputes or failure to follow statutory requirements.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Wood v. Briarwinds Condo. Ass'n Bd. of Directors, 2009 WL 10668944 (S.D. Fla. 2009)

source_file: 11267557 - Huston v. WILLAMETTE VALLEY RENTALS, LLC
case_name: Huston v. Willamette Valley Rentals, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought to rent a property for their family including disabled foster children requiring DSP assistance, but were denied housing based on familial status and disability.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for plaintiffs because WVR admitted through failed requests for admission that it received plaintiffs' complete application first, plaintiffs met all financial criteria, WVR falsely told plaintiffs there was new activity on the property after learning about their disabled foster children, and WVR subsequently rented to an applicant with a smaller household who did not have disabled persons.
brief_summary: Tami and Justin Huston, foster parents with disabled children, sued Willamette Valley Rentals, LLC for refusing to rent them a property based on familial status and disability after learning about their children and their disabilities. The court granted plaintiffs' motion for partial summary judgment, finding that WVR admitted to discriminatory conduct including falsely representing competing interest in the property after learning about plaintiffs' family composition and subsequently renting to an applicant with a smaller household who had no disabled persons.
key_cases_cited: Pacific Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Wilson v. Guardian Management, 383 F. Supp.3d 1105 (D. Or. 2019)
Conlon v. United States, 474 F.3d 616 (9th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Doe v. 2001 ARTHUR LLC
accommodation_description: HIV-positive shelter resident's request for repairs to unit conditions (broken sink, nonfunctional stove, cleanup of hazardous needles) as reasonable accommodation for disability-related vulnerability to infection
key_holding: Plaintiff fails to state a reasonable accommodation claim because he never requested a disability-related accommodation from defendants, and his FHA claim fails because he does not 'rent' his shelter unit within the meaning of the Act.
brief_summary: John Doe, an HIV-positive individual, sued the owner and operator of an emergency temporary shelter for families with HIV/AIDS, claiming the deplorable unit conditions (including used needles that injured him) constituted denial of reasonable accommodations under the FHA and ADA. The court denied Doe's default judgment motion, finding he failed to state a claim because he never requested a disability-related accommodation—he merely complained about general repairs—and his FHA claim failed because he did not 'rent' the shelter unit within the meaning of the Act.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Levine v. Project Renewal
PGA Tour v. Martin
Bragdon v. Abbott

source_file: 11267559 - Holmes v. WARREN COUNTY METROPOLITAN HOUSING AUTHORITY
case_name: Holmes v. Warren County Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a reasonable accommodation to obtain a two-bedroom unit to allow her mother, a disability-related live-in caregiver, to live with her.
key_holding: The court recommended denying the TRO and preliminary injunction because plaintiff failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on the merits of her Fair Housing Act and ADA claims, failed to show irreparable harm, and sought affirmative relief beyond the scope of preliminary injunctive relief.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rosalind Holmes sued Warren County Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging the public housing authority denied her reasonable accommodation requests under the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act for a two-bedroom unit to accommodate her disability-related live-in caregiver (her mother). The court recommended denying plaintiff's motion for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction because she failed to demonstrate a substantial likelihood of success on the merits or irreparable harm warranting such extraordinary relief.
key_cases_cited: Liberty Coins, LLC v. Goodman, 748 F.3d 682 (6th Cir. 2014)
Overstreet v. Lexington Fayette Urban Cnty. Gov't, 305 F.3d 566 (6th Cir. 2002)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Chabad of S. Ohio & Congregation Lubavitch v. City of Cincinnati, 363 F.3d 427 (6th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 11267560 - DONAVON v. RICHMOND-HENRICO ARMS LIMITED PARTNERSHIP
case_name: Kortney Donavon v. Richmond-Henrico Arms Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff seeks to challenge a state court eviction as a Fair Housing Act violation, though no specific accommodation request is articulated in the operative filings.
key_holding: The district court dismissed the removed state court eviction action for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the case moot (eviction already executed), the removal improper, and the Anti-Injunction Act precluded federal injunctive relief against the state proceeding.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kortney Donavon attempted to remove a state court eviction proceeding to federal court, asserting Fair Housing Act claims against Richmond-Henrico Arms Limited Partnership, along with civil rights claims under 42 U.S.C. §§ 1983 and 1985(3). The district court granted in forma pauperis status but dismissed the action for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the case moot (the eviction had already been executed), the removal procedurally improper, and that the Anti-Injunction Act barred any injunctive relief against the state court proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Porter v. Clarke, 852 F.3d 358 (4th Cir. 2017)
Atlantic C. L. R. Co. v. Bhd. of Locomotive Eng'rs, 398 U.S. 281 (1970)
Denny's Inc. v. Cake, 364 F.3d 521 (4th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 11267561 - Taylor v. WESTLAND REAL ESTATE GROUP, LLC
case_name: Telvon Taylor v. Westland Real Estate Group, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges that despite paying rent, he was evicted in violation of the FHA because the landlord failed to accommodate his disability related to mold exposure and hazardous conditions at the rental property.
key_holding: At the § 1915(e) screening stage, the court found plaintiff sufficiently alleged a prima facie FHA reasonable accommodation claim by pleading he is disabled, defendants knew of his disability, he raised disability-related concerns about hazardous mold conditions requiring accommodation to afford equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, and defendants refused to make such accommodations.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Telvon Taylor, alleging disability and receipt of SSI benefits, filed suit against his landlord Westland Real Estate Group, LLC and Hacienda Hills Apartment claiming disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and wrongful eviction under Nevada state law. The court granted Taylor's in forma pauperis application and screened his complaint, finding at this preliminary stage that he stated a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim where he alleged the landlord failed to accommodate his disability by refusing to fix hazardous mold conditions and proceeding with an eviction despite his disability-related complaints.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 11267562 - ROCKERS v. HAVERFORD TOWNSHIP ZONING HEARING BOARD
case_name: Rockers v. Haverford Township Zoning Hearing Board
accommodation_description: Parents sought zoning accommodation to construct a two-story addition to their home to create ADA-accessible corridors, an enlarged bedroom, and accessible bathroom for their non-ambulatory disabled son.
key_holding: The Zoning Board capriciously disregarded uncontroverted medical evidence that the second-floor addition was therapeutically necessary for the disabled child to use and enjoy the home, and the Board lacked authority to condition approval on limiting the accommodation to a single story when the requested modification was proven necessary.
brief_summary: Lindsey and Scott Rockers appealed a zoning board's decision conditioning its grant of a reasonable accommodation on limiting construction to a first-floor addition only, despite their request for a two-story addition to accommodate their non-ambulatory disabled son with cerebral palsy. The Commonwealth Court reversed, holding the Board capriciously disregarded uncontroverted medical testimony establishing the second-floor addition was therapeutically necessary for the child to safely sleep near his parents and accommodate his medical equipment.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Association, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Board of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Evans v. Zoning Hearing Board of Borough of Spring City, 732 A.2d 686 (Pa. Cmwlth. 1999)
Carunchio v. Swarthmore Borough Council, 237 A.3d 1183 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2020)

case_name: Steven E. Hobbs, Sr. v. Igor Goncharko, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested expedited rodent remediation as a reasonable accommodation because his mobility disability prevented him from bending or squatting to clean infested areas without risking re-injury.
key_holding: The court held that requests for doorbell/locks/entry system/window guards constitute modifications requiring physical changes to the premises, not accommodations; eviction-related financial hardship accommodation fails because financial difficulty is not unique to disabled persons; but rodent remediation survives because Hobbs adequately alleged his disability left him uniquely unable to self-mitigate the infestation.
brief_summary: Steven Hobbs, an African American man with mobility disabilities from spinal fractures, sued his landlord defendants and Coldwell Banker alleging racial discrimination, disability discrimination (failure to accommodate), and retaliation after he was evicted from his Chicago apartment. The court granted Coldwell Banker's motion to dismiss the disparate impact claim with prejudice but denied the landlord defendants' motion, allowing Hobbs's reasonable accommodation claim for rodent remediation to proceed past the pleading stage while dismissing his structural modification and eviction-related accommodation requests.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)

source_file: 11267564 - Cincinnati Insurance Company v. LOST RUN FARM COMMUNITY ASSOCIATION, INC.
case_name: Cincinnati Insurance Company v. Lost Run Farm Community Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for fence to contain dogs as accommodation for Mary Kay Garcia's disability
key_holding: The court granted Cincinnati Insurance's motion for summary judgment, finding no duty to defend under the CGL coverage because the HOA's intentional enforcement of restrictive covenants did not constitute an 'occurrence' (accident), and no duty to indemnify under the Wrongful Acts coverage because the exclusion for dishonest, malicious, or fraudulent acts applied to the breach of fiduciary duty claims.
brief_summary: This is an insurance coverage declaratory judgment action where Cincinnati Insurance sought to avoid defending or indemnifying the Lost Run Farm HOA and Armour Property Management against the Garcias' Fair Housing Act claims. The Garcias alleged the HOA discriminated against them based on race, national origin, and disability by refusing accommodations including a fence for Mary Kay Garcia's disability and selectively enforcing restrictive covenants. The court granted Cincinnati summary judgment, finding no coverage under the CGL policy because the intentional enforcement of covenants was not an 'accident,' and no coverage under the Wrongful Acts policy because exclusions for malicious and dishonest acts applied.
key_cases_cited: Jim Barna Log Sys. Midwest, Inc. v. Gen. Cas. Ins. Co. of Wisconsin, 791 N.E.2d 816 (Ind. Ct. App. 2003)
Auto-Owners Ins. Co. v. Harvey, 842 N.E.2d 1279 (Ind. 2006)
Knight v. Ind. Ins. Co., 871 N.E.2d 357 (Ind. Ct. App. 2007)
Erie Ins. Exch. v. Sams, 20 N.E.3d 182 (Ind. Ct. App. 2014)

case_name: James B. Herrera v. Maddox Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff vaguely alleges he requested reasonable accommodations that were ignored, but the complaint does not articulate a specific accommodation request; the court's analysis focuses on disability discrimination and retaliation claims.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act and Rehabilitation Act claims for failure to state a plausible claim, finding plaintiff failed to plead factual allegations establishing disability, causation between protected activity and adverse action, or discriminatory intent, and the alleged retaliatory rental verification occurred before plaintiff's HUD complaint.
brief_summary: James Herrera, a Section 8 voucher holder with psychological disabilities, sued Maddox Management LLC alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act and Rehabilitation Act by providing misleading rental verification information to a prospective landlord, causing denial of housing and loss of his voucher, and retaliating by seeking inflated damages in eviction proceedings. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice, finding plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing disability, discriminatory intent, or causation between protected activity (HUD complaint) and adverse actions (rental verification predated the HUD complaint).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Morgan v. Carrington Mortg. Servs., 719 F. App'x 735 (10th Cir. 2017)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n, No. 21-4035 (10th Cir. July 6, 2022)

source_file: 11267566 - JUDY SHAW FOUNDATION v. CITY OF HOWARD
case_name: Judy Shaw Foundation v. City of Howard, South Dakota
accommodation_description: iRecover sought to operate a residential chemical dependency treatment center serving recovering addicts at the Howard Hotel property, and the City's zoning ordinances required a special exception permit for such use, allegedly motivated by discriminatory intent toward disabled individuals in recovery.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City on JSF's ADA associational standing claim because JSF lacked any association with disabled persons, but denied summary judgment on JSF's FHA disparate treatment claim because genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether the City enacted the zoning ordinances with discriminatory intent to block the operation of a residential treatment center for recovering addicts.
brief_summary: Judy Shaw Foundation sued the City of Howard, South Dakota, alleging the City violated the FHA by enacting zoning ordinances (745 and 746) requiring a special exception permit for a residential chemical dependency treatment center, which caused iRecover to terminate its $1.1 million purchase of the Howard Hotel property. The court granted summary judgment for the City on JSF's ADA claim (finding no associational standing), but denied summary judgment on the FHA disparate treatment claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Olmstead v. L.C.
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
Durand v. Fairview Health Servs.
Gallagher v. Detroit Metropolitan Airport
Loeffler v. Staten Island Univ. Hosp.
McCullum v. Orlando Reg'l Healthcare Sys.

source_file: 11267567 - Bowman v. KCAP RE FUND II, LLC
case_name: Bowman v. KCAP RE Fund II, LLC
accommodation_description: Design and construction accessibility requirements for covered multifamily dwellings under § 3604(f)(3)(C) - specifically whether ground floor units qualify as covered dwellings requiring accessible design.
key_holding: The court held as a matter of law that each of the 25 structures at the Property constitutes a single 'building' with four units, making all ground floor units 'covered multifamily dwellings' subject to FHA accessibility requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C), rejecting Triune's argument that separately platted property and firewalls created multiple buildings.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Dana Bowman, a handicapped veteran, sued Silver Leaf entities alleging the Residence at Joshua Landing Apartments violates Fair Housing Act design and construction accessibility requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C). Silver Leaf filed a third-party claim against the architects Triune and Gustin for defective design, and moved for partial summary judgment seeking a ruling that ground floor units constitute covered multifamily dwellings. The court granted the motion, holding that each of the 25 four-unit structures is a single building for FHA purposes, making all ground floor units covered dwellings subject to accessibility requirements.
key_cases_cited: United States v. Shanrie Co., 669 F. Supp. 2d 932 (S.D. Ill. 2009)
Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Vill. of Olde St. Andrews, Inc., 250 F. Supp. 2d 706 (W.D. Ky. 2003)

case_name: Justin Baker v. The Emerald Condos et al.
accommodation_description: Request for accommodation regarding the requirement to break down cardboard boxes for disposal
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim with prejudice because he failed to adequately plead that he is a handicapped person under the FHAA, that defendants knew of his disability, or that the accommodation request was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Justin Baker sued CWD Group and individuals associated with The Emerald Condos under the Fair Housing Act, alleging disability discrimination and failure to provide reasonable accommodation regarding cardboard box disposal requirements. The court dismissed his third amended complaint with prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) for failure to state a claim, finding Baker failed to adequately plead he is a handicapped person under the FHAA, that defendants knew of his disability, or that the requested accommodation was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Foman v. Davis
Ebner v. Fresh, Inc.
Becker v. Orchard Hills Apts.

source_file: 11267569 - MAPLE TREE LP, II v. Rebollo
case_name: Maple Tree LP, II v. Rebollo
accommodation_description: Tenant with arthrogryposis requested permission to install handrails in her bathtub to assist with mobility, which required an exception to the lease's prohibition on driving screws into walls.
key_holding: The appellate court affirmed the denial of summary judgment for landlords, finding genuine issues of material fact existed as to whether the tenant made a valid reasonable accommodation request and whether the landlord's failure to respond within fifty-two days constituted a constructive denial.
brief_summary: Rachel Rebollo sued her landlords alleging they failed to provide a reasonable accommodation under the FHAA by not approving her request to install handrails in her bathroom despite her arthrogryposis-related mobility limitations. The Indiana Court of Appeals affirmed the denial of summary judgment, finding genuine factual disputes about whether Rebollo's request for permission to install handrails constituted a reasonable accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B) and whether the landlord's failure to respond for fifty-two days constituted a constructive denial.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Sanders v. AHEPA 78 VI Apartments, Inc.
Furbee v. Wilson
Valencia v. City of Springfield

source_file: 11267570 - SHALER TOWNSHIP v. ZONING HEARING BOARD OF SHALER TOWNSHIP
case_name: Shaler Township v. Zoning Hearing Board of Shaler Township
accommodation_description: Scioto and ReMed sought to operate a residence for six unrelated persons with traumatic brain injuries at the Property as a reasonable accommodation under the FHAA.
key_holding: The Commonwealth Court affirmed the trial court's order, holding that Ordinance 1974's amendment allowing up to six disabled persons to constitute a 'family' constituted unlawful spot zoning because it was specifically designed to benefit the Property owner and operator without relation to the health, safety, morals, or general welfare of the Township at large.
brief_summary: Shaler Township appealed from the trial court's affirmance of the Zoning Hearing Board's decision that the Township's Ordinance 1974, which amended the zoning code to permit up to six disabled persons to live together as a 'family,' constituted unlawful spot zoning because it was targeted to benefit the property owner and ReMed Recovery Care Centers without relation to the public welfare. The Commonwealth Court affirmed, agreeing that substantial evidence supported the Board's finding that the ordinance unjustifiably singled out land for different treatment.
key_cases_cited: Atherton Dev. Co. v. Twp. of Ferguson
Penn St., L.P. v. E. Lampeter Twp. Zoning Hearing Bd.
Conshohocken Borough v. Conshohocken Borough Zoning Hearing Bd.
Laughman v. Zoning Hearing Board of Newberry Twp.
Murray v. Shaler Twp. Zoning Hearing Bd.

source_file: 11267571 - AMHERST FARMS HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION, INC. v. DM
case_name: Amherst Farms Homeowners Association, Inc. v. D.M. and L.S.
accommodation_description: Request to keep six chickens as emotional support animals (ESAs) for L.S.'s anxiety and PTSD in a homeowner association community that prohibits such animals under its Declaration.
key_holding: The appellate court affirmed that defendants failed to meet their burden of demonstrating a material issue of fact that six chickens were necessary to ameliorate L.S.'s disability and afford her an equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, as their medical documentation constituted net opinions lacking sufficient factual bases and methodology to establish a disability-related therapeutic need for the specific animals.
brief_summary: Amherst Farms Homeowners Association sued homeowners D.M. and L.S. for declaratory judgment to enforce its Declaration requiring removal of chickens, coop, and run; defendants counterclaimed under the FHA alleging the chickens were ESAs for L.S.'s anxiety and PTSD. The appellate court affirmed summary judgment for the HOA, finding defendants failed to provide sufficient documentation establishing the six chickens were necessary to ameliorate L.S.'s disability-related therapeutic needs under the second element of the reasonable accommodation analysis.
key_cases_cited: Players Place II Condo. Ass'n, Inc. v. K.P., 256 N.J. 472 (2024)
Oras v. Hous. Auth. of Bayonne, 373 N.J. Super. 302 (App. Div. 2004)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)

source_file: 11267573 - KANTSEPOLSKY v. RIVER SHORES ASSOCIATION, INC.
case_name: Valery Kantsepolsky v. River Shores Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant use disability-related precautions during exploratory demolition and repairs of her condominium unit, including protocols requested by her two treating physicians to protect against chemical, dust, mold, and VOC exposures.
key_holding: The court recommends abstaining under Colorado River doctrine and staying the FHA claims because substantially the same parties and issues are pending in parallel state court proceedings, while dismissing the ADA claim because residential condominiums are not places of public accommodation under Title III.
brief_summary: Valery Kantsepolsky, a condominium owner with environmental sensitivity conditions, sued her HOA alleging it violated the Fair Housing Act by denying her requests for disability-related precautions during exploratory demolition and repairs, and by refusing to engage in an interactive process. The Magistrate Judge recommended granting the motion to dismiss in part, dismissing the ADA claim and staying the FHA claims pending resolution of the parallel state court proceedings where the same parties litigate the same accommodation issues.
key_cases_cited: Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Colorado River Water Conservation Dist. v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Ambrosia Coal & Constr. Co. v. Morales, 368 F.3d 1320 (11th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 11267574 - READING HOUSING AUTHORITY v. Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission
case_name: Reading Housing Authority v. Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission
accommodation_description: Tenant with mobility disabilities (ruptured spinal discs, fibromyalgia, dislocated hip, knee injury, swollen feet) requested assignment of an accessible/handicapped parking space as a reasonable accommodation to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling.
key_holding: The court affirmed the PHRC's finding that the Reading Housing Authority violated the reasonable accommodation requirement by placing the mobility-disabled complainant on a standard first-come, first-served parking waitlist without priority for her disability-related need, resulting in a nearly five-year delay during which six non-disabled applicants received parking before her, and upheld the $20,000 compensatory damages award based on testimony that the lack of accessible parking exacerbated her physical pain and caused anxiety, depression, and stress.
brief_summary: Reading Housing Authority petitioned for review of a Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission order finding that the Authority violated Section 5(h)(3.2) of the Pennsylvania Human Relations Act by denying a reasonable accommodation to tenant Dorisel M. Serrano Rodriguez, who requested an accessible parking space due to her mobility-limiting disabilities. The Commonwealth Court affirmed, holding that the Authority's failure to prioritize her accommodation request on the parking waitlist for nearly five years, during which six non-disabled applicants received parking before her, constituted unlawful discrimination and warranted the $20,000 damages award for the physical pain and emotional distress she suffered.
key_cases_cited: Carunchio v. Swarthmore Borough Council, 237 A.3d 1183 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2020)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Board of Adjustment of the Township of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
1400 Main Holdings, LLC v. Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission, 326 A.3d 1040 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2024)
Kennedy House, Inc. v. Philadelphia Commission on Human Relations, 143 A.3d 476 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2016)
Neshaminy School District v. Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission, 257 A.3d 766 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2021)

source_file: 11267575 - Yocca v. TURTLE CREEK ZONING HEARING BD.
case_name: Yocca v. Turtle Creek Zoning Hearing Board
accommodation_description: Owner of a sober recovery house seeking reasonable accommodation to house up to seven individuals in a residential zone where group homes are limited to three residents by special exception.
key_holding: The Board and trial court erred as a matter of law by applying the FHA's occupancy limit exception (City of Edmonds) without first determining whether the three-person group home limit was neutrally applicable, and by failing to apply the Lapid-Laurel reasonable accommodation test requiring findings on whether the accommodation was necessary for financial viability or therapeutic benefit.
brief_summary: Ronald Yocca, who operates a sober recovery house for up to seven individuals in drug and alcohol recovery, appealed the denial of his request for a reasonable accommodation under the FHA to exceed the Borough's three-person limit for group homes in a residential zone. The Commonwealth Court affirmed the denial of a special exception but vacated and remanded the reasonable accommodation issue, holding that the Board and trial court improperly relied on the special exception as an adequate accommodation without applying the Lapid-Laurel test to determine whether Yocca's requested accommodation was necessary for financial viability or therapeutic benefit.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Board of Adjustment of Township of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Carunchio v. Swarthmore Borough Council, 237 A.3d 1183 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2020) (en banc)

case_name: Murray v. U.S. Treasury
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claims he is being denied housing and seeks restoration of his housing rights after allegedly having his inheritance stolen
key_holding: The court dismissed Murray's FHA retaliation claim because he failed to allege discrimination based on a protected class, and dismissed his remaining claims as factually frivolous and legally baseless.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Irvin G. Murray filed a rambling complaint against numerous federal, state, and local defendants alleging his $43 million inheritance was stolen and claiming housing denial, seeking restoration of housing rights. The court dismissed all claims as factually frivolous under § 1915 screening and dismissed Murray's FHA retaliation claim because he failed to allege discrimination based on a protected class, as required to state a valid FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Neitzke v. Williams
Denton v. Hernandez

case_name: Desiree Knight v. Macon Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Knight requested a special inspection to address housing defects and a minimum rent hardship adjustment as accommodations for her anxiety and ADHD.
key_holding: Knight fails to state a failure-to-accommodate claim because she does not explain how the special inspection for housing defects or minimum rent hardship adjustment would alleviate the effects of her anxiety and ADHD.
brief_summary: Knight, a Section 8 voucher holder with anxiety and ADHD, sued Macon Housing Authority alleging failure to accommodate under the FHA, FHA retaliation, Section 504 discrimination, and due process violations. The court dismissed the FHA failure-to-accommodate claim because Knight failed to allege how her requested special inspection and rent hardship adjustment would alleviate her disabilities, and dismissed her retaliation claim for failing to plausibly allege adverse action.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Schaw v. Habitat for Human. of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)

source_file: 11267579 - CHOSEN CONSULTING v. TOWN COUNCIL OF HIGHLAND

case_name: Felice Lazae Martin, et al. v. Michael Castillo, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested to be excused from rent payments during relocation while seeking alternative housing due to alleged constructive eviction, though this was not properly pled as a reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' complaint failed to state any cognizable Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiffs made only two cursory references to the FHA with no specific factual allegations establishing a prima facie case of housing discrimination based on disability.
brief_summary: Two pro se tenants sued their private landlord and his attorney alleging housing defects, lockout, constitutional violations, and ADA claims, with cursory references to the Fair Housing Act. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the complaint for failure to state cognizable federal claims, finding plaintiffs failed to adequately plead any viable FHA, § 1983, or ADA claims.
key_cases_cited: Kokkonen v. Guardian Life Ins. Co. of Am., 511 U.S. 375 (1994)
Franchise Tax Bd. v. Construction Laborers Vacation Trust, 463 U.S. 1 (1983)
Caterpillar, Inc. v. Williams, 482 U.S. 386 (1987)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 11267582 - LAWRENCE COUNTY RECOVERY v. VILLAGE OF COAL GROVE
case_name: Lawrence County Recovery, LLC v. Village of Coal Grove, OH
accommodation_description: Village enacted three ordinances restricting recovery housing: occupancy limits (three-person family definition), accreditation/inspection requirements, and a one-year moratorium on new facilities.
key_holding: The court denied most of defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that municipalities can be proper FHA defendants, that recovery housing residents are sufficiently alleged to be 'handicapped' under the FHA, that the ordinances plausibly allege intentional discrimination, and that § 3604(c) can apply to municipalities; the court dismissed only ADA claims against defendant Holt in his individual capacity and LCR's corporate ADA retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Lawrence County Recovery, LLC and individual plaintiffs sued the Village of Coal Grove, Ohio and its solicitor alleging Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Ohio law violations arising from three ordinances that allegedly target recovery housing for people with substance use disorders through occupancy limits, accreditation requirements, and a moratorium. The court denied most of defendants' motion to dismiss, allowing claims of intentional discrimination, discriminatory statements, and interference/retaliation to proceed while dismissing only ADA claims against the solicitor in his individual capacity and LCR's corporate ADA retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Larkin v. State of Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Smith & Lee Assocs., Inc. v. City of Taylor, Mich., 13 F.3d 920 (6th Cir. 1993)
MX Grp., Inc. v. City of Covington, 293 F.3d 326 (6th Cir. 2002)
Get Back Up, Inc. v. City of Detroit, 725 F. App'x 389 (6th Cir. 2018)
Michigan Prot. & Advoc. Serv., Inc. v. Babin, 18 F.3d 337 (6th Cir. 1994)

source_file: 11267583 - STASON SUTTON AND FAIR HOUSING JUSTICE CENTER, INC. v. CREF 546 WEST 44TH STREET
case_name: Stason Sutton and Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. CREF 546 West 44th Street, LLC, Patrinely Group, LLC, and CETRA/CRI Architecture, PLLC
accommodation_description: Retrofit physical accessibility features in common areas and individual dwelling units including widening doorways, modifying thresholds, adjusting bathtub controls, and installing accessible kitchen features to comply with design and construction accessibility requirements.
key_holding: The parties reached a settlement requiring defendants to pay $1,100,000 and complete accessibility retrofits to common areas and individual units at the 280-unit multifamily property within specified timeframes.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center and individual tenant Sutton sued CREF 546 West 44th Street, Patrinely Group, and CETRA/CRI Architecture alleging that the 280-unit multifamily building at 535 West 43rd Street in New York City was designed and constructed in violation of the Fair Housing Act's accessibility requirements. The parties entered a Partial Consent Decree requiring defendants to pay $1,100,000 in settlement and complete comprehensive retrofits to common areas and individual units to remove accessibility barriers.

case_name: Eric Crouse v. Housing Works, Deschutes County, and the State of Oregon
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that Housing Works increase the payment standard and utility allowance to allow access to suitable housing that does not exacerbate their disabilities.
key_holding: The court granted the State of Oregon's motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction because Oregon's APA provides the exclusive remedy for review of final agency orders, and separately dismissed claims against the State for failure to allege any affirmative acts causing injury rather than mere failures to act.
brief_summary: Eric Crouse, a disabled individual, sued Housing Works (local housing authority), Deschutes County, and the State of Oregon alleging failures to provide reasonable housing accommodations, including requests to increase payment standards and utility allowances. The court granted the State of Oregon's motion to dismiss, finding no subject matter jurisdiction under the Oregon APA and that plaintiff failed to state a claim because no affirmative acts by the State caused injury.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Wyatt B. v. Kotek, 146 F.4th 1267 (9th Cir. 2025)

case_name: Damia Bjurling v. Alec Johnson, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff references her service dog and alleges disability discrimination under FHA and ADA arising from an assault by fellow tenant; court dismisses claims for failure to state a claim against private individual defendant.
key_holding: The court granted Chief Parker's motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim and dismissed FHA/ADA claims against Mr. Holder (fellow tenant) because private individuals cannot be held liable under the FHA absent allegations of agency relationship with a housing provider.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Damia Bjurling sued former landlord Alec Johnson, Police Chief Shelby Parker, and fellow tenant Calvin Earl Holder III, alleging violations of constitutional rights, the ADA, and the FHA related to a sexual assault and subsequent police response, mentioning her status as an adult with autism and her service dog. The court granted Chief Parker's motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim and dismissed all federal claims against Mr. Holder because a private individual and fellow tenant cannot be held liable under the FHA absent an agency relationship with a housing provider.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Morris v. W. Hayden Ests. First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 104 F.4th 1128 (9th Cir. 2024)
Rizzo v. Goode, 423 U.S. 362 (1976)

source_file: 11267586 - STASON SUTTON AND FAIR HOUSING JUSTICE CENTER, INC. v. CREF 546 WEST 44TH STREET
case_name: Stason Sutton and Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. CREF 546 West 44th Street, LLC
accommodation_description: Retrofit physical accessibility features throughout a 280-unit multifamily building including widened doorways, threshold modifications, bathtub control adaptations, and kitchen work surface modifications to comply with Fair Housing Act accessibility requirements.
key_holding: Parties reached a consent decree requiring defendants to pay $1,100,000 in settlement and complete physical retrofits to the Subject Property to address inaccessibility violations under the Fair Housing Act's design and construction requirements.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center and individual tenant Stason Sutton sued CREF 546 West 44th Street, LLC and related entities alleging that the design and construction of a 280-unit multifamily building at 535 West 43rd Street in Manhattan violated the Fair Housing Act's accessibility requirements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). The parties resolved the case through a consent decree requiring defendants to pay $1.1 million in damages and implement comprehensive retrofits to common areas and individual units to bring the property into compliance with federal accessibility standards.

case_name: Howitt v. Vorvis
accommodation_description: Howitt's attempt to rent an apartment while holding a Section 8 voucher and receiving SSI disability benefits
key_holding: The court recommended denying the motion to dismiss, finding that Vorvis's email stating he could not accept government subsidies and was unsure if his apartment was suitable for disability needs supports a reasonable inference that Vorvis perceived Howitt as having a disability and that this perception was one of the reasons he refused to rent the apartment.
brief_summary: Dan Howitt, a disabled tenant using a Section 8 voucher and receiving SSI benefits, sued private landlord Athanasios Vorvis after Vorvis refused to rent him an apartment in Cambridge, citing unwillingness to accept government subsidies and uncertainty about whether the apartment met disability needs. The court recommended denying Vorvis's motion to dismiss, finding that the amended complaint plausibly stated claims under the Fair Housing Act and Americans with Disabilities Act because Vorvis's email supports an inference that he perceived Howitt as having a disability and refused to rent based on that perception.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Sonoiki v. Harvard Univ., 37 F.4th 691 (1st Cir. 2022)
Van den Heuvel v. Costello, 2023 WL 3045806 (E.D. Cal. Apr. 21, 2023)

source_file: 11267588 - Jeffcoat v. BROOKINGS HOUSING & REDEVELOPMENT COMMISSION
case_name: Jeffcoat v. Brookings Housing & Redevelopment Commission
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED - motion to appoint counsel order does not describe the underlying accommodation request
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion to appoint counsel without prejudice, finding plaintiff demonstrated ability to litigate claims through prior filings despite being pro se.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Estela Elizabeth Jeffcoat sued Brookings Housing & Redevelopment Commission alleging Fair Housing Act violations and moved to appoint counsel. The court denied the motion to appoint counsel without prejudice, finding Jeffcoat demonstrated adequate ability to litigate her claims based on her detailed complaint and other filings.

source_file: 11267589 - Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House requested permission to operate a substance abuse recovery home in a two-family dwelling, seeking accommodation from zoning requirements that limited 'Community Residences' to single-family dwellings.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of the First Amended Complaint for failure to state a plausible claim of discriminatory intent, but vacated the denial of leave to amend because the prior preliminary injunction denial did not provide adequate notice of pleading deficiencies and the proposed amendment was not clearly futile.
brief_summary: Oxford House, a nonprofit that operates substance abuse recovery homes, sued North Bergen Township after the municipality denied its Certificate of Continuing Occupancy application based on zoning restrictions. The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of the complaint, finding insufficient factual allegations to support an inference of discriminatory intent, but vacated the denial of leave to amend and remanded for further proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc.
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
Olmstead v. L.C.

case_name: Alfey Ramdhan v. Morgan Stonehill, LLC
accommodation_description: Complaint too vague to identify specific accommodation request; plaintiff references eviction dispute and defendant being unwilling to work with him but provides no factual allegations about what accommodation was requested.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim was dismissed without prejudice for failure to state a claim because the complaint provided no factual allegations beyond conclusory statements that plaintiff suffers from medical and mental health disabilities and defendant was unwilling to work with him to resolve an eviction issue.
brief_summary: An individual tenant sued a private landlord alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act in connection with an eviction dispute, but the federal district court dismissed the complaint because the plaintiff failed to provide any factual allegations beyond conclusory statements that he suffers from medical and mental health disabilities and the defendant was unwilling to work with him.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Rooker v. Fidelity Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
D.C. Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Tornheim v. Eason, 363 F. Supp. 2d 674 (S.D.N.Y. 2005)

source_file: 11267592 - Kononen v. CITY OF SALEM HOUSING AUTHORITY
case_name: Katie M. Kononen v. City of Salem Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested approval of an additional service dog as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: Summary judgment is denied on disparate treatment claims regarding communication through a third party, parking enforcement, and fence removal because evidence of discriminatory remarks by SHA employees creates genuine issues of material fact, but granted on the failure to accommodate claim because plaintiff failed to show a second parking spot was necessary to afford her an equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Katie Kononen, a long-term public housing tenant with a disability who is Native American, sued the City of Salem Housing Authority alleging disability and race-based housing discrimination under the FHA, ADA, and Oregon law, claiming defendant treated her differently than other tenants through delays in approving an additional service dog, requiring third-party communication, selective parking enforcement, and fence removal. The court granted partial summary judgment for defendant on the failure to accommodate claim (second parking spot) and retaliation claims, but denied summary judgment on disparate treatment claims regarding communication, parking, and fence removal, finding genuine disputes of material fact based on evidence of discriminatory remarks by SHA employees.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Ohio House, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa, 135 F.4th 645 (9th Cir. 2025)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Merrick v. Farmers Ins. Grp., 892 F.2d 1434 (9th Cir. 1990)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Daniel Maher and Colin's Place LLC v. White Bear Township
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to expand occupancy at his sober house from five to ten residents by amending the zoning ordinance to allow more unrelated individuals in a single-family residential zone.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish a genuine dispute of material fact on their disparate-treatment claim because they provided no evidence of discriminatory intent beyond the denial, and failed to demonstrate that the requested accommodation to expand to ten residents was necessary or that applying for an accommodation would be futile when the Township considered the request and suggested alternative paths.
brief_summary: Daniel Maher and his company Colin's Place LLC sued White Bear Township after the municipality denied their request to amend the zoning ordinance to allow a sober house to accommodate up to ten residents instead of the permitted five. The Court granted summary judgment for the Township, finding no evidence of discriminatory intent and no genuine dispute that the accommodation request was reasonable or necessary when the Township offered alternative paths for expansion.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
One Love Hous., LLC v. City of Anoka, 2021 WL 3774567 (D. Minn. Aug. 25, 2021)
Meraki Recovery Hous., LLC v. City of Coon Rapids, 2021 WL 5567898 (D. Minn. Nov. 29, 2021)
Peebles v. Potter, 354 F.3d 761 (8th Cir. 2004)

case_name: Abraham Shitrai Israel and Sarah Iris Israel v. Barbara Guinn, et al.
accommodation_description: Emergency public housing assistance, home repair funds, and healthcare services from Department of Social Services due to disability
key_holding: The court adopted the Report-Recommendation and dismissed Plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim without prejudice because the complaint was devoid of any allegations of discrimination based on protected characteristics including disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Israel sued New York State social services officials and police officers under the Fair Housing Act and constitutional claims, alleging they were wrongfully denied emergency housing assistance, home repair funds, and healthcare services. The court dismissed the FHA claim for failure to state a claim because the complaint lacked any factual allegations demonstrating discrimination based on a protected characteristic.

source_file: 11267595 - Chavous v. HOUSING VISIONS UNLIMITED, INC.
case_name: Priscilla Chavous v. Housing Visions Unlimited, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled tenant under a HUD-sponsored rent-to-own program, alleges defendant denied her the opportunity to purchase the property, failed to maintain habitable conditions, and retaliated against her because of her disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim was dismissed for failure to state a claim because she failed to allege any facts showing that her disability was at least a motivating factor in the alleged discriminatory conduct.
brief_summary: Priscilla Chavous, a pro se plaintiff with disabilities, sued Housing Visions Unlimited, Inc. alleging the defendant denied her the opportunity to purchase a property under a rent-to-own program, failed to maintain habitable conditions, and discriminated against her because of her disability in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B), finding the claims barred by res judicata from a prior action and, alternatively, that plaintiff failed to state a plausible FHA claim because she did not allege facts showing disability was a motivating factor for the alleged discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

source_file: 11267596 - Castellanos v. MANZANO MESA LIMITED PARTNERSHIP
case_name: Castellanos v. Manzano Mesa Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability-based housing discrimination due to exposure to unsafe housing conditions, but no specific accommodation request is identified in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because the complaint failed to allege facts showing defendants treated plaintiff differently from non-disabled tenants in providing housing and services, as required to state a discrimination claim under 24 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(2)(A).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tekisha Castellanos, a pro se tenant with disabilities at Manzano Mesa Apartments, sued the property owner, management company, and Bernalillo County Housing Department alleging Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act discrimination claims based on exposure to unsafe housing conditions. The court granted leave to proceed in forma pauperis but ordered plaintiff to amend her complaint, finding the FHA claims failed because she did not allege defendants treated her differently from non-disabled tenants in providing housing services.

source_file: 11267598 - BE THE BUSH RECOVERY MINISTRIES v. COFFEE COUNTY, TENNESSEE
case_name: Be the Bush Recovery Ministries v. Coffee County, Tennessee
accommodation_description: BTB sought zoning approval to operate a residential addiction recovery ministry at the Riley Creek Property in an RS-1 Low Density Residential District.
key_holding: The RS-1 zoning ordinance does not facially discriminate against the handicapped because it contains no mention of disability and treats BTB's facility the same as all other non-single-family residential uses; BTB's exclusion was not 'because of' its residents' handicap but rather due to its non-family residential character.
brief_summary: Be the Bush Recovery Ministries, a faith-based residential addiction recovery program, sued Coffee County, Tennessee after the County denied its requests to operate a recovery facility in an RS-1 Low Density Residential District, prompting BTB to relocate to Bedford County. The Sixth Circuit affirmed the district court's rulings, holding that the County's RS-1 zoning ordinance does not facially discriminate against the handicapped because it neutrally limits residential uses to single-family dwellings and duplexes without targeting disabled persons or group homes specifically.
key_cases_cited: Larkin v. Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Smith & Lee Assocs., Inc. v. City of Taylor, 13 F.3d 920 (6th Cir. 1993)
MX Group, Inc. v. City of Covington, 293 F.3d 326 (6th Cir. 2002)
Livingston Christian Schs. v. Genoa Charter Township, 858 F.3d 996 (6th Cir. 2017)
Living Water Church of God v. Charter Township of Meridian, 258 F. App'x 729 (6th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Amy Burnett v. Rutledge Flats, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, alleges that defendants failed to design and construct the property in compliance with FHA accessibility requirements, causing her to be deterred from renting.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that the plaintiff sufficiently alleged Article III standing as an individual plaintiff and that her complaint plausibly stated a claim under the FHA's design and construction requirements.
brief_summary: Amy Burnett, a wheelchair user, sued Rutledge Flats, LLC and related defendants (developers, contractors, architects, engineers) alleging the 174-unit apartment complex was not designed and constructed in compliance with the FHA's accessibility requirements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff adequately alleged Article III standing as an individual and plausibly stated a design-and-construction claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Food & Drug Admin. v. Alliance for Hippocratic Med.
Chapuis v. Forest Hill Grp. LLC
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman

case_name: Weston v. WIP 800 J Lofts
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs' complaint does not clearly articulate a specific accommodation request, though it references disability-related housing rights; the court found plaintiffs failed to sufficiently allege defendants refused to make an accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because their allegations were conclusory, they failed to sufficiently plead their disabilities (ADHD, hypertension, asthma) substantially limit major life activities, and they did not adequately allege defendants knew of their impairments or refused to make an accommodation.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Weston and Marshall sued WIP 800 J Lofts and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on race, familial status, and disability arising from an allegedly fraudulent and retaliatory unlawful detainer action. The court dismissed the first amended complaint with leave to amend, finding plaintiffs' FHA claims conclusory and insufficiently pled because they failed to adequately allege their disabilities substantially limit major life activities or that defendants refused to accommodate their impairments.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Harris v. Itzhaki
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co.
Lopez v. Smith

source_file: 11267601 - PLACE OF GRACE, INC. v. Indiana
case_name: Place of Grace, Inc. v. Indiana
accommodation_description: Place of Grace, operator of faith-based group recovery homes for individuals with substance use disorders, seeks classification of its homes as Class 2 (residential) structures rather than Class 1 (commercial), and requests variance from sprinkler requirements.
key_holding: The court found Place of Grace lacks standing to pursue failure-to-accommodate claim against IDHS because the Commission, not IDHS, denied the variance request; however, Place of Grace's intentional discrimination claim survives because IDHS's classification of the homes as Class 1 structures is fairly traceable to IDHS and redressable by prospective injunctive relief.
brief_summary: Place of Grace, a nonprofit operating faith-based group recovery homes for individuals with substance use disorders in Huntington, Indiana, sued IDHS and the Fire Prevention Commission alleging that classifying its homes as Class 1 (commercial) structures rather than Class 2 (residential) violates the ADA, FHAA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court granted partial dismissal, finding Place of Grace lacks standing against IDHS on the failure-to-accommodate claim because the Commission, not IDHS, denied the variance, but allowed the intentional discrimination claim to proceed based on IDHS's discriminatory classification of the homes.
key_cases_cited: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State, 400 F. Supp. 3d 751 (S.D. Ind. 2019)
Church of Our Lord & Savior Jesus Christ v. City of Markham, 913 F.3d 670 (7th Cir. 2019)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 11267602 - GROUP HOME ON GIBSON ISLAND v. Gibson Island Corp.
case_name: Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corp.
accommodation_description: Request for exemption from the Gibson Island business-purpose covenant to permit operation of an assisted living group home for elderly people with disabilities
key_holding: The district court erred by analyzing whether removal of the Corporation's proposed conditions was 'necessary' rather than whether Lussi's requested exemption from the business-purpose covenant was necessary to provide equal housing opportunities, and genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the Corporation's stated justifications for its conditions were pretextual, precluding summary judgment.
brief_summary: Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC and Assisted Living Well Compassionate Care 2, LLC (operated by Craig Lussi) sued Gibson Island Corporation (a private HOA) alleging violations of the FHA and Maryland fair housing law for refusing to exempt Lussi's assisted living group home from the community's business-purpose covenant, and for retaliation and discrimination based on disability. The Fourth Circuit vacated the district court's summary judgment for the Corporation, holding that the district court applied the wrong legal standard to the necessity element of the reasonable accommodation claim and that genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether the Corporation's imposed conditions on approving the accommodation were reasonable or pretextual for discriminatory and retaliatory motives.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, 124 F.3d 597
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262
Oconomowoc Residential Prog. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096
Texas Dep't. of Cmty. Affs. v. Burdine, 450 U.S. 248

case_name: Group Home on Gibson Island v. Gibson Island Corp.
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: UNDETERMINED
brief_summary: UNDETERMINED

case_name: Ruth Castro v. Lancaster Loft, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who has asthma and is allergic to smoke, requested that defendant landlord enforce the non-smoking provisions in the lease agreement to provide her a smoke-free apartment as an accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The Court granted Plaintiff's FHA claims survive screening under § 1915(e), finding her allegations sufficient to state claims for discrimination under § 3604(f)(3)(B) and retaliation under § 3617, but dismissed her constitutional claims for failure to state a claim.
brief_summary: Ruth Castro, a pro se Hispanic plaintiff with asthma, sued her landlord and property management entities alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to accommodate her disability-related need for a smoke-free apartment and by retaliating against her with adverse actions including eviction after she requested accommodations and filed complaints with government agencies. The Court screened her complaint and allowed her FHA discrimination and retaliation claims to proceed while dismissing her constitutional claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Mehta v. Beaconridge Improvement Ass'n, 432 Fed. App'x 614 (7th Cir. 2011)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Cole v. Faulkner, No. 24 C 12117, 2025 WL 1359054 (N.D. Ill. May 9, 2025)

source_file: 11267605 - LEGGE v. CLARKE COUNTY ZONING DEPARTMENT
case_name: Brian K. Legge v. Clarke County Zoning Department et al.
accommodation_description: Property owner with cancer and chronic anemia sought waiver of zoning setback requirements and continued garage use as accommodation for his physical and financial inability to dismantle the structure due to his medical conditions.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a likelihood of success on his FHA claims because he did not demonstrate any nexus between his medical conditions and his accommodation requests, and he failed to allege facts showing he engaged in protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Brian Legge sued Clarke County, Virginia and its officials seeking to enjoin demolition of his nonconforming garage and alleging violations of the Equal Protection Clause, Fair Housing Act, and procedural and substantive due process based on the County's zoning enforcement actions. The court denied Legge's motion for preliminary injunction, finding he failed to demonstrate a likelihood of success on any claim, including FHA claims where he could not establish that his accommodation requests were connected to his medical conditions or that he engaged in protected FHA activity.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Vill. of Willowbrook v. Olech, 528 U.S. 562 (2000)
SAS Assocs. 1, LLC v. City Council for City of Chesapeake, Va., 91 F.4th 715 (4th Cir. 2024)
MLC Auto., LLC v. Town of So. Pines, 532 F.3d 269 (4th Cir. 2008)
Rockville Cars, LLC v. City of Rockville, 891 F.3d 141 (4th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Gregory R. Marlor v. The Housing Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, requested that the housing provider install a doorbell at the building's main entrance to enable visitors to contact residents with mobility disabilities.
key_holding: The court found that Marlor stated one plausible reasonable accommodation claim—THC's denial of his July 2023 request to install a doorbell at the building entrance—while dismissing all other claims (including earlier denials of accessible housing, transfer requests, and rent-related disputes) as time-barred under the FHA's two-year statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Gregory Marlor, a wheelchair user receiving Social Security disability benefits, sued The Housing Company (manager of Thomas Logan Apartments, a Low-Income Housing Tax Credit property in Boise, Idaho) alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on disability. The court screened Marlor's complaint and found that while most claims were time-barred under the FHA's two-year statute of limitations, Marlor stated a plausible claim that THC denied his July 2023 request for a reasonable accommodation—specifically, installation of a doorbell at the building's main entrance to enable visitors to contact residents with mobility disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Nat'l R.R. Passenger Corp. v. Morgan, 536 U.S. 101 (2002)

source_file: 11267607 - Terry v. YWCA OF NORTHESTERN NEW YORK
case_name: Elizabeth Marie Terry v. YWCA of Northeastern New York
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff raises disability discrimination and Fourteenth Amendment claims relating to YWCA's policy prohibiting residents from operating businesses and not being eligible for full-time employment, but no specific accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B) is identified.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed without prejudice because plaintiff failed to satisfy Rule 8's pleading requirements, failed to demonstrate she obtained a right-to-sue letter or exhausted EEOC prelitigation requirements, and failed to state a prima facie claim for disability discrimination under the ADA because she did not adequately allege what her disability is or how it limits a major life activity.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Elizabeth Marie Terry, a YWCA resident since mid-2022, sued the YWCA of Northeastern New York alleging disability discrimination under the ADA and violations of her Fourteenth Amendment rights because YWCA policies prohibit residents from operating businesses and allegedly render disabled residents ineligible for full-time employment. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the complaint without prejudice because plaintiff's pleading failed to comply with Rule 8, did not demonstrate exhaustion of EEOC prelitigation requirements, and failed to adequately allege the elements of a disability discrimination claim, including failing to identify her specific disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Scalercio-Isenberg v. Morgan Stanley Servs. Grp. Inc., 2020 WL 4547317 (S.D.N.Y. Aug. 6, 2020)

source_file: 11267608 - THE COMFY COTTAGE, INC. v. Pasco County
case_name: The Comfy Cottage, Inc. v. Pasco County
accommodation_description: Comfy Cottage requested that Pasco County waive the automatic fire sprinkler system requirement under the Florida Building Code to permit operation of a community residential group home for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities.
key_holding: Pasco County's sprinkler system requirement does not facially discriminate against disabled individuals because it applies equally to care facilities and owner-occupied lodging houses regardless of whether occupants are disabled, and Comfy Cottage failed to establish that waiving the requirement was necessary to afford disabled residents an equal opportunity to use and enjoy housing.
brief_summary: The Comfy Cottage, Inc., a not-for-profit operator of a community residential group home for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities, sued Pasco County, Florida, alleging the County's requirement that care facilities install automatic fire sprinkler systems violates the Fair Housing Act and ADA. The court granted summary judgment for Pasco County, finding the sprinkler requirement does not facially discriminate because it applies equally to all care facilities regardless of disability status, and Comfy Cottage failed to establish that waiving the requirement was necessary to afford disabled residents an equal opportunity to use and enjoy housing.
key_cases_cited: Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 11267609 - Doria v. YAVAPAI COLLEGE DISTRICT GOVERNING BOARD
case_name: Daniel T Doria v. Yavapai College District Governing Board, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, citing psychological disabilities, requested accommodation of lease terms to operate a pet-sitting business from his RV site, which the lease prohibited.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim with prejudice because the same disability discrimination claim had been previously litigated to final judgment in state court proceedings, and thus was barred by res judicata; the remaining claims were dismissed under collateral estoppel.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Daniel Doria sued Yavapai College District and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act and other claims arising from lease termination and eviction for operating a pet-sitting business from his RV site, which he claimed was a reasonable accommodation for his psychological disabilities. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding the FHA claim was barred by res judicata because Doria had previously litigated identical disability discrimination claims in state court proceedings that resulted in final judgments against him.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Campbell v. SZL Props., Ltd., 204 Ariz. 221 (App. 2003)
Falcon ex rel. Sandoval v. Maricopa County, 213 Ariz. 525 (2006)
Phoenix Newspapers, Inc. v. Dep't of Corr., 188 Ariz. 237 (App. 1997)

source_file: 11267610 - HALL-JOHNSON v. City and County of San Francisco
case_name: Hall-Johnson v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation was requested; the case concerns alleged racial discrimination in housing prioritization under a city housing lottery program.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Amendments Act claim was dismissed because the complaint contained no factual allegations that Hall-Johnson has a disability or that the City discriminated against her on that basis, and the Section 1983 equal protection, Title VI, and Section 1981 claims failed for lack of sufficient allegations that the decisionmaker knew Hall-Johnson was Black or acted with discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Hall-Johnson, a Black woman experiencing homelessness in San Francisco, sued the City and County of San Francisco alleging racial discrimination after a leasing agent allegedly told her that being Black must be the only reason she was not prioritized for housing under a city housing lottery program. The district court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that the complaint failed to plausibly allege intentional discrimination because it lacked allegations that the actual decisionmaker knew Hall-Johnson was Black, and dismissed the Fair Housing Amendments Act claim for failure to allege any disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Thornton v. City of St. Helens
Pers. Adm'r of Mass. v. Feeney
Robinson v. Adams

case_name: Maple Tree LP, II, et al. v. Rachel Rebollo
accommodation_description: Rebollo requested permission to install handrails/grab bars in her bathtub to assist with mobility due to arthrogryposis, which the court characterized as requiring an exception to the lease's no-screws policy.
key_holding: The appellate court affirmed the trial court's denial of landlords' summary judgment motion, finding genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether Rebollo submitted a valid reasonable accommodation request for handrails and whether landlords' 52-day delay in responding constituted a constructive denial of the accommodation.
brief_summary: Rachel Rebollo sued her landlords alleging they violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act by failing to approve her request to install handrails in her bathroom as a reasonable accommodation for her arthrogryposis-related mobility limitations.
