The appellate court affirmed the trial court's denial of summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether Rebollo's inquiry about handrails constituted a valid accommodation request and whether the landlords' 52-day failure to respond constituted a constructive denial of the accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Sanders v. AHEPA 78 VI Apartments, Inc., 237 N.E.3d 1126 (Ind. Ct. App. 2024)
Furbee v. Wilson, 144 N.E.3d 801 (Ind. Ct. App. 2020)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, Ill., 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Wis. Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Dwane Cherry-El v. Belinda Blount, DCHA, Housing Specialist, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to change his status from live-in aide to primary tenant and continue residing in subsidized housing as a reasonable accommodation for his disability after his mother's death.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint sua sponte under Rules 8 and 12(b)(6) because his pleadings failed to provide fair notice of the nature and substance of his claims, including whether he was challenging his live-in aide designation, his housing list status, or a denied accommodation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Dwane Cherry-El sued the District of Columbia Housing Authority seeking to remain in his mother's subsidized unit as a primary tenant rather than being required to leave as a live-in aide upon her death, alleging disability discrimination and reasonable accommodation violations. The court dismissed the complaint sua sponte under Rules 8 and 12(b)(6) for failing to state a plausible claim, finding the complaint too vague and conclusory to provide defendants fair notice of the claims against them.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89 (2007)

source_file: 11267613 - Joe v. OLIVE BRANCH ASSISTED LIVING LLC
case_name: Hailei Joe v. Olive Branch Assisted Living LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff was discharged from an addiction treatment facility after disclosing her HIV-positive status; the court analyzes whether this discharge constituted intentional discrimination because of disability.
key_holding: Genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether Plaintiff's HIV-positive status was the 'but for' cause of her discharge from the facility, precluding summary judgment on her discrimination claims under the FHA, AFHA, and ADA; however, Plaintiff's retaliation claims and public disclosure claim fail as a matter of law.
brief_summary: Hailei Joe sued Olive Branch Assisted Living LLC, an addiction treatment facility, alleging she was unlawfully discharged because of her HIV-positive status in violation of the Fair Housing Act, Arizona Fair Housing Act, and Americans with Disabilities Act, along with retaliation and state law claims. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the discrimination claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact remain regarding causation, but granted summary judgment to Defendant on the retaliation and public disclosure claims.
key_cases_cited: Murray v. Mayo Clinic, 934 F.3d 1101 (9th Cir. 2019)
Pac. Shores Props., LLC v. City of Newport Beach, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Bax v. Doctors Med. Ctr. of Modesto, Inc., 52 F.4th 858 (9th Cir. 2022)
Bragdon v. Abbott, 524 U.S. 624 (1998)
Brown v. City of Tucson, 336 F.3d 1181 (9th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 11267614 - Yocca v. TURTLE CREEK ZONING HEARING BOARD
case_name: Ronald Yocca v. Turtle Creek Zoning Hearing Board
accommodation_description: Operator of a seven-resident sober recovery house seeks a reasonable accommodation under the FHA to exceed the borough's three-person occupancy limit for group homes in a residential zone.
key_holding: The court held that the Board and trial court erred by treating the special exception for group homes as an adequate accommodation under the FHA without conducting the proper Lapid-Laurel analysis, and erred by not recognizing that the three-person group home occupancy limit does not fall under the FHA's absolute exception for occupancy limits because it is not uniformly applied to all residences.
brief_summary: Ronald Yocca, operator of a seven-resident sober recovery house in Turtle Creek, Pennsylvania, appealed the denial of his request for a special exception and reasonable accommodation under the FHA to exceed the borough's three-person occupancy limit for group homes. The Commonwealth Court affirmed the special exception denial but vacated the reasonable accommodation denial, holding that the Board and trial court failed to apply the proper Lapid-Laurel legal framework for determining whether the accommodation was necessary and reasonable.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Board of Adjustment of Township of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Carunchio v. Swarthmore Borough Council, 237 A.3d 1183 (Pa. Cmwlth. 2020)
Albert v. Zoning Hearing Board of North Abington Township, 854 A.2d 401 (Pa. 2004)

source_file: 11267615 - Drews v. GOLDOLLAR REAL ESTATE INVESTMENTS
case_name: Wendy Marie Drews v. Goldollar Real Estate Investments; Lynmark Group-Orange Avenue Apartments, LLC; Carla Hines-Melcher
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a domestic violence survivor with familial status, alleged defendants discriminated against her by pursuing eviction, refusing lease renewal, denying parking, and creating a hostile housing environment based on her protected status.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims were dismissed because her amended complaint failed to allege facts giving rise to an inference that any adverse actions by defendants were motivated by her protected status as a domestic violence survivor with familial status, and plaintiff failed to adequately allege protected activity for her retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Wendy Marie Drews, proceeding pro se, sued her former landlord entities and property manager alleging Fair Housing Act claims based on discrimination as a domestic violence survivor with familial status and retaliation for engaging in protected activity. The court dismissed all federal claims with prejudice, finding that plaintiff failed to state plausible claims for FHA discrimination or retaliation because her allegations did not give rise to an inference that adverse housing actions were motivated by her protected status and she failed to adequately plead the elements of a retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 Fed. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
Lynn v. Vill. of Pomona, 373 F. Supp. 2d 418 (S.D.N.Y. 2005)

source_file: 11267616 - NATIONAL FAIR HOUSING ALLIANCE v. US DEP'T OF HOUSING AND URBAN DEVELOPMENT
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Fair housing organizations seek judicial order requiring HUD to complete FHIP grantmaking process before statutory deadline, not a traditional housing accommodation
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs established likelihood of success on APA unreasonable delay claims and constitutional claims, granted TRO ordering HUD to comply with statutory obligations and file detailed compliance plan, but reserved judgment on multi-year grant claims and declined to order funds remain available through litigation at this time.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance and Tennessee Fair Housing Council sued HUD under the APA and Constitution, challenging HUD's failure to award FHIP grants for fiscal year 2024 and to implement multi-year grants. The court granted in part a temporary restraining order, finding plaintiffs likely to succeed on claims that HUD's delay in grantmaking was unreasonable and violated statutory obligations, ordering HUD to file a compliance plan but reserving judgment on multi-year grant claims.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Lincoln v. Vigil, 508 U.S. 182 (1993)
City of Houston v. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 24 F.3d 1421 (D.C. Cir. 1994)
Nken v. Holder, 556 U.S. 418 (2009)
League of Women Voters of U.S. v. Newby, 838 F.3d 1 (D.C. Cir. 2016)

source_file: 11267617 - MILLERBORG v. BLUE BONNET TRAIL LLC
case_name: Millerborg v. Blue Bonnet Trail LLC
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user seeks removal of physical accessibility barriers (insufficient doorway widths, lack of wheelchair maneuvering space, inaccessible routes to trash dumpsters and mailbox, elevated thermostats and light switches) to access and use the dwelling and common areas.
key_holding: A plaintiff asserting claims about physical accessibility must at least have personally experienced an accessibility issue to have standing; merely observing accessibility barriers without any direct personal effect is insufficient to plead a concrete injury in fact.
brief_summary: Casey Millerborg, a wheelchair user, sued Blue Bonnet Trail LLC, owner of an apartment complex in Waxahachie, Texas, alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on physical accessibility barriers he observed at the property. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of Article III standing, finding the complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing Millerborg personally experienced any accessibility barriers rather than merely observing them, and failed to establish likelihood of future injury sufficient for injunctive relief.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Laufer v. Mann Hospitality, L.L.C.
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife
Deutsch v. Annis Enterprises, Inc.
Frame v. City of Arlington

source_file: 11267618 - McCready v. UNITY SOBER LIVING HOMES, LLC
case_name: John McCready v. Unity Sober Living Homes, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to pay rent on the same weekly schedule as non-disabled residents, rather than being required to pay monthly as an SSDI recipient
key_holding: Unity's written rent policy explicitly distinguishing SSDI recipients from other residents by requiring monthly payment from disabled tenants while allowing non-disabled tenants to pay weekly constitutes facial discrimination under the Fair Housing Amendments Act, as disability is the dispositive trait singled out for different treatment.
brief_summary: John McCready, a recovering addict suffering from depression who received SSDI benefits, sued Unity Sober Living and property manager Re/Max Achievers, alleging Unity's written rent policy requiring SSDI recipients to pay rent monthly while non-disabled residents could pay weekly constituted disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Amendments Act, and that he was wrongfully evicted without proper notice or judicial process. The court granted partial summary judgment for McCready, finding Unity's facially discriminatory policy violated the FHAA as a matter of law and that the landlord-tenant relationship triggered liability for wrongful eviction under Pennsylvania law, with damages to be assessed at trial.
key_cases_cited: 431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Assouline v. Reynolds, 219 A.3d 1131 (Pa. 2019)
Lakeside Resort Enters., LP v. Bd. of Supervisors, 455 F.3d 154 (3d Cir. 2006)
Sharpvisions, Inc. v. Borough of Plum, 475 F. Supp. 2d 514 (W.D. Pa. 2007)
Lapid-Laurel v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Mendoza-Lebron v. Adams
accommodation_description: Individual homeowners requested that the City of New York provide accurate information about disability and medical exemptions from water shutoffs and meaningful access to accommodation procedures.
key_holding: The court found no reasonable expectation of recurrence to overcome mootness for forward-looking claims because all plaintiffs qualified for exemptions and no water was actually shut off; the damages claims failed because mere threats without actual termination do not constitute a due process deprivation, and the ADA and FHA claims failed because the City's existing policies already provide exemptions for disabled residents and plaintiffs did not plausibly allege they were treated differently by reason of disability.
brief_summary: Individual homeowners sued the City of New York over its 2024 water shutoff policy targeting delinquent property owners, alleging constitutional, ADA, and FHA violations based on inadequate notice of exemptions for disabled residents. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the forward-looking claims moot since all plaintiffs qualified for exemptions and no water was actually shut off, and rejecting the damages claims on the merits because threats without actual termination do not constitute a cognizable due process deprivation and the City's policies already accommodate disabled homeowners without evidence of discrimination by reason of disability; the court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over state and local claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Pinckney v. Carroll, No. 18-cv-12198, 2019 WL 6619484 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 4, 2019)
Henrietta D. v. Bloomberg, 331 F.3d 261 (2d Cir. 2003)

case_name: Manuel James Gonzalez, as legal guardian of R.E.G. v. Royse Services LLC d/b/a LaVida Vacations
accommodation_description: Request to waive the pet deposit fee for R.E.G.'s service dog that mitigates her autism symptoms of panic attacks and destructive tantrums
key_holding: The court recommended granting default judgment because Royse Services admitted (by default) to discriminating against R.E.G. by refusing to waive its pet fee policy for her service animal despite knowing of her disability and the necessity of the accommodation, thereby denying her use and enjoyment of the vacation rental property.
brief_summary: Manuel James Gonzalez sued Royse Services LLC d/b/a LaVida Vacations on behalf of his minor daughter R.E.G. (who has autism and requires a service dog) after the vacation rental company refused to allow the service animal on the property unless he paid a $150 pet deposit, ultimately requiring his family to leave. The court granted default judgment in favor of Gonzalez, finding that Royse Services violated the ADA and FHA by discriminating against R.E.G. based on her disability and refusing to waive its pet fee policy for her necessary service animal.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Surtain v. Hamlin Terrace Found., 789 F.3d 1239 (11th Cir. 2015)
Norkunas v. Seahorse NB, LLC, 444 F. App'x 412 (11th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 11267621 - Gilbert v. TUCSON REALTY & TRUST COMPANY
case_name: Shenoa Gilbert v. Tucson Realty & Trust Company Management Services LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to transfer to a first-floor apartment due to her back injury and disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHAA reasonable accommodation claim because her complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing her disability substantially limits major life activities, that defendant knew of her disability, and that the accommodation was necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Shenoa Gilbert, a Section 8 voucher holder with a documented disability (back injury and Stage 3 kidney disease), sued her property management company alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act for denying her accommodation requests for a first-floor apartment transfer and exemption from pesticide use in her unit. The court dismissed her complaint with leave to amend, finding she failed to allege sufficient facts to state a plausible claim under the FHAA's reasonable accommodation elements.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 11267622 - United States v. FAIR HOUSING PARTNERSHIP OF GREATER PITTSBURGH, INC.
case_name: United States of America v. AION Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Assigned accessible parking space for individuals with mobility impairments who use canes, crutches, or walkers
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion for reconsideration and motion to certify for interlocutory appeal, holding that fair housing testers can assert § 3604(f)(3)(B) claims and that plaintiffs adequately pleaded the necessity element by alleging specific physical impairments from which an assigned parking space would be necessary.
brief_summary: The United States and fair housing organizations sued AION Management and affiliated property entities alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, including failure to provide assigned parking as a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B). The court denied defendants' motion for reconsideration and motion to certify for interlocutory appeal, concluding that testers have standing to bring § 3604(f)(3)(B) claims and that plaintiffs plausibly alleged the necessity element of the reasonable accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Association, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine, 602 U.S. 367 (2024)
Smith v. Pac. Props. & Dev. Corp., 358 F.3d 1097 (9th Cir. 2004)
Access Living of Metro Chi. v. Prewitt, 111 F. Supp. 3d 890 (N.D. Ill. 2015)
United States v. Perry Homes, Inc., 2022 WL 2829887 (W.D. Pa. 2022)

source_file: 11267623 - Watts v. THE IRVINE COMPANY LLC
case_name: Watts v. The Irvine Company LLC
citation: No reporter citation (Report and Recommendation, C.D. Cal. June 20, 2025)
accommodation_description: Plaintiff submitted three formal accommodation requests that were denied, but the Third Amended Complaint does not specify what accommodations were requested (prior versions mentioned carpet replacement and relocation).
key_holding: The Third Amended Complaint fails to comply with Rule 8 because it does not identify which defendant each cause of action is asserted against, and even liberally construed, fails to plausibly allege any viable federal claim, including failure to state a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Candi-Rachelle Watts, an African American woman in HUD-subsidized housing owned by The Irvine Company LLC, filed a Third Amended Complaint alleging disability discrimination, failure to accommodate, and retaliation under the FHA, along with ADA, RICO, and state law claims. The magistrate judge recommended dismissing the complaint without leave to amend for failing to comply with Rule 8's pleading requirements and for failing to state any viable federal claim, finding the allegations conclusory and insufficient to give defendants fair notice of the claims against them.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Roman v. Jefferson at Hollywood LP, 495 F. App'x 804 (9th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Cooper v. Chicago Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for transfer to wheelchair-accessible ground floor unit for disabled daughter with progressive neurological disease who could no longer navigate second-floor apartment
key_holding: The court approved a $525,000 settlement and 95%/5% allocation between the individual plaintiff and the decedent's estate, finding the settlement fair and reasonable given CHA's failure to provide accessible housing for 788 days.
brief_summary: Lynya Cooper, individually and as administrator of her deceased daughter Trebora Talbert's estate, sued the Chicago Housing Authority for failing to provide reasonable alternative housing accommodations in a wheelchair-accessible unit for 788 days despite the daughter's progressive neurological disease rendering her unable to walk. The court approved a $525,000 settlement with 95% allocated to Ms. Cooper personally and 5% to the estate.

source_file: 11267625 - Jones v. FEATHERSTONE CONDOMINIUM HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION INC.
case_name: Quincy Bernard Jones v. Featherstone Condominium Homeowners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that the HOA have a contractor remove slippery, wet dirt from his parking spot and sidewalk because he is afraid he will slip and fall due to his disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed the failure-to-accommodate claim because Plaintiff failed to allege what his disability is or how cleaning the parking space and sidewalk would address limitations caused by his impairment.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Quincy Jones, a disabled individual receiving disability payments, sued Featherstone Condominium Homeowners Association alleging failure to accommodate and retaliation claims under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted IFP status but dismissed the amended complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e) for failure to state a claim, finding that the accommodation claim failed because Plaintiff did not allege what his disability is or explain how removing dirt from his parking spot would address his impairment.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Schaw v. Habitat for Human. of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Lepp v. Mallet
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Heidi Lepp, affiliated with Sugarleaf Church, alleges that government and private defendants discriminated against her based on disability and religion in connection with church property occupancy and alleged enforcement actions.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion to amend as futile because her proposed amended complaint failed to state plausible Fair Housing Act claims; plaintiff only offered conclusory allegations of discrimination without factual allegations showing defendants acted with discriminatory animus based on religion or disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Heidi Lepp, proceeding pro se and affiliated with Sugarleaf Church, sued Conrad Mallet Jr. and other defendants alleging a coordinated campaign of government harassment based on religion and disability in connection with church property at 2627 John R. Street in Detroit. The court denied plaintiff's motion to amend her complaint as futile, finding her Fair Housing Act claims failed to state plausible allegations of discriminatory intent because she only offered conclusory assertions without factual content showing defendants acted because of her protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Fulton v. City of Philadelphia, 593 U.S. 522 (2021)
Church of Lukumi Bablu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah, 508 U.S. 520 (1993)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 11267627 - COMM'N ON HUMAN RIGHTS v. MANSIONS, LLC

case_name: Karla Hunt v. Volare Health LLC
accommodation_description: Employee retaliation claim — plaintiff was terminated after advocating for a patient with mental health diagnosis whose admission to nursing facility was denied based on disability
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state a retaliation claim under § 3617 because she did not allege she 'aided or encouraged' the patient in exercising his FHA rights — she only had a single argument with her supervisor about the patient's admission without involving or informing the patient — but dismissal was with leave to amend; her Oregon whistleblower claim survived because temporal proximity and circumstantial evidence supported causation
brief_summary: Karla Hunt, an employee at French Prairie Nursing and Rehabilitation Center operated by Volare Health LLC, sued claiming she was retaliated against under the FHA after advocating for a patient's admission despite the patient's mental health diagnosis; the court dismissed her FHA retaliation claim with leave to amend because she failed to adequately allege she aided or encouraged the patient in exercising his fair housing rights, but declined to dismiss her Oregon whistleblower retaliation claim finding sufficient temporal proximity and circumstantial evidence of causation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Macon v. Proud Ground Organization, 2021 WL 3354157 (D. Or. 2021)
University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center v. Nassar, 570 U.S. 338 (2013)

source_file: 11267632 - Watts v. JOGGERS RUN PROPERTY OWNERS ASSOCIATION
case_name: Sara Watts v. Joggers Run Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged HOA engaged in racial discrimination including selective enforcement of HOA rules, restrictions on access to community amenities like basketball courts, towing of vehicles, harassment, and unwarranted fees because of her race as an African American woman.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Section 3604(b) covers post-acquisition discriminatory conduct by an HOA against a homeowner because the mandatory HOA contract creates rights and privileges directly connected to the sale of the dwelling, and Watts plausibly alleged racially motivated discrimination in the terms, conditions, and privileges of her HOA membership.
brief_summary: Sara Watts, an African American woman, sued the Joggers Run Property Owners Association alleging that the HOA engaged in racially discriminatory conduct including selectively enforcing parking and pet rules against her while excusing violations by White residents, restricting access to basketball courts because too many Black children used them, harassing her family, and improperly towing her son's car. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Section 3604(b) of the Fair Housing Act covers post-acquisition discriminatory conduct by an HOA against homeowners because mandatory HOA membership creates contractual rights directly connected to the sale of a dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024)
Ga. State Conf. of the NAACP v. City of La-Grange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Curto v. A Country Place Condo. Ass'n, 921 F.3d 405 (3d Cir. 2019)

source_file: 11267633 - BAK REALTY, LLC v. City of Fitchburg
case_name: BAK Realty, LLC v. City of Fitchburg
accommodation_description: Crossing Over, Inc. (a nonprofit operating a 13-resident sober house in a three-family dwelling) requested reasonable accommodation under the FHA and Massachusetts zoning law to permit occupancy exceeding the four-unrelated-person limit applicable to families under Fitchburg's zoning code.
key_holding: The Massachusetts Zoning Act's anti-discrimination provision requires that groups of unrelated disabled persons be treated the same as either families OR similar-sized groups of unrelated persons, not necessarily both; because Fitchburg's zoning code treated the sober house residents the same as any other boarding house use, it did not discriminate against disabled persons.
brief_summary: BAK Realty and Crossing Over, Inc. (operator of a 13-resident sober house) challenged Fitchburg's determination that their three-family dwelling was operating as a prohibited boarding house under the zoning code. The Supreme Judicial Court of Massachusetts reversed the lower court, holding that G. L. c. 40A, § 3, fourth par., which prohibits discrimination against disabled persons in zoning, requires that unrelated disabled persons be treated the same as either families OR similar-sized groups of unrelated persons (in the disjunctive), not both simultaneously; because the sober house did not meet Fitchburg's local definition of family and was treated the same as any other similar-sized group of unrelated persons under the boarding house prohibition, there was no discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Crossing Over, Inc. v. Fitchburg, 98 Mass. App. Ct. 822 (2020)
Peabody Props., Inc. v. Sherman, 418 Mass. 603 (1994)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Pulsifer v. United States, 601 U.S. 124 (2024)

case_name: Curtis K. Jackson, Sr. v. Chicago Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a person with disabilities receiving a Project-Based Voucher, alleged denial of access to programs and services at Lake Street Studios, including an accessible parking space, and discriminatory housing conditions.
key_holding: Jackson's FHA claim failed because he provided no allegations that the Authority and its employees denied him a housing accommodation or otherwise interfered with his right to rent or enjoy property, let alone that they acted with discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Curtis Jackson sued the Chicago Housing Authority and Lake Street Studios Limited Partnership under the FHA, ADA, and § 1983, alleging that Lake Street employees engaged in discriminatory conduct against him as a disabled voucher holder, and that CHA employees failed to address his complaints. The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of the Authority defendants and the $10,000 default judgment against Lake Street, holding that Jackson failed to state an FHA claim because he did not adequately allege that defendants denied him a housing accommodation or acted with discriminatory intent based on disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Stanek v. St. Charles Cmty. Unit Sch. Dist. No. 303, 783 F.3d 634 (7th Cir. 2015)
Williams v. Shah, 927 F.3d 476 (7th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Gordon Roy Parker v. Panphil Realty, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges credit score requirements in apartment advertisements deter disabled persons from applying and seeks waiver of credit score policies as an accommodation for disability.
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss because plaintiff lacks Article III standing—he never applied for any apartment, failed to plead a specific disability, and his conclusory futility allegations and tester theory were insufficient to establish concrete, particularized injury-in-fact under controlling Supreme Court precedent.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Gordon Roy Parker sued multiple Philadelphia apartment owners and their attorney alleging their credit score requirements in rental advertisements violated the Fair Housing Act by deterring disabled persons from applying. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Parker lacks Article III standing because he never applied for any apartment, failed to plead a specific disability, and his generalized futility allegations and tester standing theory were legally insufficient under controlling Supreme Court precedent.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Carney v. Adams, 592 U.S. 53 (2020)
Ellison v. Am. Bd. of Orthopaedic Surgery, 11 F.4th 200 (3d Cir. 2021)
Int'l Bhd. of Teamsters v. United States, 431 U.S. 324 (1977)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 11267636 - APPELGREN v. ISLAND HOSPITALITY MANAGEMENT VII, LLC
case_name: Mark Appelgren v. Island Hospitality Management VII, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly requested 'a quiet, stress-free environment' and 'room modifications' during hotel check-in, but never disclosed his disabilities (severe social anxiety, panic attacks, IBS) to hotel staff.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA and ADA reasonable accommodation claims because he failed to allege that defendants knew of his disabilities, and his vague references to wanting a 'quiet, stress-free environment' and unspecified 'room modifications' did not give defendants sufficient notice of what accommodation he sought.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mark Appelgren sued Island Hospitality Management VII, LLC and Hilton alleging disability discrimination under the FHA and ADA after a difficult hotel check-in experience where he experienced delays, hostile interactions, and a chaotic lobby that allegedly triggered his anxiety and IBS symptoms. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to state a failure-to-accommodate claim because he never alleged that defendants knew of his disabilities, and his vague references to wanting a quieter environment and room modifications were insufficient to put defendants on notice of what accommodation he sought.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Yates Real Est., Inc. v. Plainfield Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 404 F. Supp. 3d 889 (D.N.J. 2019)

source_file: 11267637 - Doe v. Department of Veterans Affairs
case_name: Jane Doe v. Department of Veterans Affairs
accommodation_description: Doe alleged that Bozzuto Management Co. failed to continue an accommodation granted to her disabled husband by the prior property owner (Equity Residential), and that Friendship Place retaliated against her for reporting discrimination concerns.
key_holding: The court dismissed Doe's FHA claim because she lacked standing to sue on behalf of her disabled husband—she failed to allege that he cannot protect his own interests—and dismissed her ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims with prejudice because none of the defendants qualify as public entities.
brief_summary: Jane Doe, a combat disabled veteran, sued the Department of Veterans Affairs, Friendship Place, Equity Residential, Bozzuto Management Co., federal judges, court clerks, and others, alleging they colluded to deny accommodations for her disabled husband and obstruct her access to courts. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, holding that Doe lacked standing to sue on her husband's behalf because she did not allege he cannot protect his own interests, and that her ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims failed because the defendants are not public entities.
key_cases_cited: Thomas v. The Salvation Army of S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Wicomico Nursing Home v. Padilla, 910 F.3d 739 (4th Cir. 2018)
Freilich v. Upper Chesapeake Health, Inc., 313 F.3d 205 (4th Cir. 2002)
Gibson v. Goldston, 85 F.4th 218 (4th Cir. 2023)

case_name: Park Crossing Homeowners Association, Inc. v. Suarez
accommodation_description: The Suarezes alleged the Association's pre-suit mediation demand threatened to evict their adult son Gabriel based on his autism-related behaviors, constituting discrimination under § 3604(f)(1); the Suarezes also raised a failure-to-accommodate claim (Count II) which the jury rejected.
key_holding: The Association's pre-suit mediation letter demanding that the Suarezes arrange 'proper care and supervision' for Gabriel or face removal from the property did not constitute the kind of actual eviction threat required to support a discrimination claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(1), as the Association never filed an eviction action and the pre-suit mediation demand was not a realistic or genuine threat of housing loss.
brief_summary: Park Crossing HOA sued the Suarezes for covenant violations alleging Gabriel's autism-related noises disturbed neighbors; the Suarezes counterclaimed under the FHA, alleging the HOA's pre-suit mediation letter threatening Gabriel's removal constituted disability discrimination under § 3604(f)(1) and failure to accommodate. The appellate court reversed the new trial order on Count I, holding the mediation letter was not a realistic eviction threat sufficient to support an FHA discrimination claim, while affirming the permanent injunction requiring the Suarezes to maintain noise limits.
key_cases_cited: Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Wells v. Willow Lake Ests., Inc., 390 F. App'x 956 (11th Cir. 2010)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Watts v. Joggers Run Prop. Owners Ass'n, 133 F.4th 1032 (11th Cir. 2025)

source_file: 11267639 - Faruki v. HOUSING AUTHORITY FOR THE COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
case_name: Faruki v. Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento
accommodation_description: Request that the housing authority process plaintiff's Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application after providing notice of his Multiple Myeloma disability, alleging constructive denial of the application due to disability.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a plausible FHA claim because plaintiff's allegations are speculative and conclusory, and because plaintiff's friend who was allegedly treated more favorably was also disabled, negating an inference of disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mekal Faruki, who has Multiple Myeloma cancer, sued the Housing Authority for the County of Sacramento, Sacramento Housing Regional Authority, and the County of Sacramento, alleging they constructively denied his Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher application after he submitted disability documentation in 2017. The court recommends dismissal of plaintiff's federal claims under the ADA and FHA for failure to state a plausible claim, finding plaintiff's allegations speculative and insufficient to show disability-based discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Lovell v. Chandler, 303 F.3d 1039 (9th Cir. 2002)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 11267640 - United States v. LETTIRE CONSTRUCTION CORP.
case_name: United States of America v. Lettire Construction Corp.
accommodation_description: Retrofitting 185-unit apartment building (The Tapestry) to correct inaccessible design and construction features including door widths, thresholds, kitchen and bathroom layouts, environmental controls, mailboxes, and fitness center accessibility.
key_holding: The government and defendant East 124th Street LLC entered into a Consent Decree requiring substantial retrofits to address alleged violations of the FHA's accessible design and construction requirements at the Tapestry, including physical modifications to common areas and individual units, a $20,000 settlement fund for aggrieved persons, and compliance monitoring over two and a half years.
brief_summary: The United States brought this enforcement action against East 124th Street LLC alleging that The Tapestry, a 185-unit multifamily building completed in 2009, was designed and constructed with accessibility deficiencies in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). The parties settled via Consent Decree requiring defendant to retrofit common areas and dwelling units to meet Fair Housing Act accessibility requirements, establish a $20,000 fund for aggrieved persons, and implement compliance monitoring and training protocols.

source_file: 11267641 - LB EX REL. AB v. PREMERA BLUE CROSS
11267642 - Watts v. JOGGERS RUN PROPERTY OWNERS ASSOCIATION, INC.
case_name: Sara Watts v. Joggers Run Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff was forced to give up her service dog due to HOA enforcement of pet policies following a neighbor complaint, though disability accommodation is not the primary theory
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that HOA conduct occurring after a homeowner's purchase—including discriminatory enforcement of community rules regarding amenities, parking, and fees—falls within the scope of Section 3604(b) as discrimination in the terms, conditions, or privileges related to the sale of a dwelling.
brief_summary: Sara Watts, an African American homeowner, sued her HOA alleging racially discriminatory enforcement of community rules including restrictions on basketball court access, selective parking enforcement, and penalties for minor infractions. The district court dismissed her claims under Section 3604(b) and Section 3617 of the Fair Housing Act and Sections 1981 and 1982 of the Civil Rights Act. The Eleventh Circuit reversed, holding that discriminatory HOA conduct regarding contractual rights flowing from mandatory membership—including access to community amenities and services—constitutes discrimination in the terms, conditions, or privileges of homeownership under Section 3604(b).
key_cases_cited: Ga. State Conf. of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Curto v. A Country Place Condo. Ass'n, 921 F.3d 405 (3d Cir. 2019)
Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024)

source_file: 11267643 - CMDS RESIDENTIAL, LLC v. Mayor & City Council of Baltimore
case_name: CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor & City Council of Baltimore
accommodation_description: CMDS sought a use-and-occupancy permit from the City of Baltimore to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility, which the City denied based on zoning-related grounds.
key_holding: The court held that punitive damages are unavailable against a municipality under the Fair Housing Act, consistent with City of Newport v. Fact Concerts, and that CMDS cannot recover lost profits if it never had a legal entitlement to operate its facility under the applicable zoning law.
brief_summary: CMDS Residential, LLC, a developer seeking to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility, sued the City of Baltimore after the City denied a use-and-occupancy permit; the court ruled on the City's motion in limine that punitive damages are unavailable against a municipality under the FHA and that CMDS cannot recover lost profits if it had no legal right to operate under the zoning code, with the outcome of parallel state litigation on the zoning interpretation potentially affecting actual damages availability.
key_cases_cited: City of Newport v. Fact Concerts, Inc., 453 U.S. 247 (1981)
Barnes v. Gorman, 536 U.S. 181 (2002)
CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor & City Council of Balt., 714 F. Supp. 3d 583 (D. Md. 2024)
Gilead Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Town of Cromwell, 112 F.4th 93 (2d Cir. 2024)
Jennings v. Hous. Auth. of Balt. City, 2014 WL 346641 (D. Md. 2014)

case_name: Danny Carlson v. Carrington Square LLC
accommodation_description: Carlson requested grab bars be installed in his apartment bathroom to prevent falls due to his multiple sclerosis, which requires wheelchair use.
key_holding: Summary judgment for defendants was appropriate because Carlson failed to make his grab bar request in writing as required by the lease agreement, and his failure-to-accommodate claim regarding lease non-renewal failed because defendants demonstrated legitimate, non-discriminatory reasons for the renovation-related non-renewal that applied uniformly to all tenants.
brief_summary: Danny Carlson, a wheelchair user with multiple sclerosis, sued Carrington Square Apartments, its property manager, and its eviction attorneys claiming violations of the Fair Housing Act based on defendants' refusal to install grab bars and the non-renewal of his lease during apartment renovations. The court granted summary judgment for all defendants, finding that Carlson's grab bar request was properly denied because he failed to make the request in writing as required by the lease, and that the lease non-renewal was part of a facially neutral renovation policy applied to all tenants rather than disability-based discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Cinnamon Hills v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 897 (10th Cir. 2012)
Wilson v. Glenwood Intermountain Properties, Inc.
Smith v. Cedars, Inc., 2013 WL 5819342 (D. Utah 2013)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Robert Ricketts v. Westwood Condo Assn.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendants repair the leaking roof, ceiling, and carbon monoxide leaks in his condo unit as a reasonable accommodation for his and his children's asthma.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because he failed to allege sufficient facts showing his or his children's asthma substantially limits a major life activity as required to establish a handicap under the FHA.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Robert Ricketts, a condominium owner at Westwood Condominiums in West Hartford, Connecticut, sued the Westwood Condo Association, its members, property manager, and attorneys alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to repair a leaking roof and carbon monoxide leaks as a reasonable accommodation for his and his children's asthma. The court dismissed the FHA claim for failure to state a claim because Plaintiff did not adequately plead that his asthma constitutes a handicap substantially limiting a major life activity under the FHA definition.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Gladstone Realtors v. Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

case_name: Larry Johnson v. Lisa Simonson and Kelly Paine
accommodation_description: Plaintiff primarily alleged retaliation claims under § 3617, though underlying disputes involved requests to deliver recertification paperwork to his door as a health accommodation and prior live-in aide/spouse accommodation requests.
key_holding: Plaintiff's retaliation claims fail because his own refusal to sign required HUD recertification documents caused the adverse actions, severing any causal link between protected activity and alleged retaliation, and plaintiff's parking space claim is barred by res judicata from prior settlement.
brief_summary: Larry Johnson, a pro se plaintiff and Section 8 tenant at Uptown Tower Apartments, sued property manager Lisa Simonson and portfolio manager Kelly Paine alleging multiple FHA retaliation claims arising from disputes over HUD recertification paperwork and prior housing litigation. The court granted defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding no evidence of retaliation because plaintiff's own refusal to sign required recertification documents directly caused the eviction proceedings and subsidy termination, and plaintiff's parking accommodation claim was barred by res judicata from prior settlement.
key_cases_cited: Brown v. City of Tucson, 336 F.3d 1181 (9th Cir. 2003)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Johnson v. Guardian Mgmt., 535 F. Supp. 3d 1004 (D. Or. 2021)
Owens v. Kaiser Found. Health Plan, Inc., 244 F.3d 708 (9th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 11267648 - Resources for Human Development, Inc. v. LOWER PROVIDENCE TOWNSHIP
case_name: Resources for Human Development, Inc. v. Lower Providence Township
accommodation_description: Zoning permit to operate supportive housing for disabled individuals at Eagleville Hospital property
key_holding: The court dismissed RHD's FHA claim because the complaint failed to properly frame it as a disability discrimination claim under the FHAA and failed to allege sufficient facts showing a causal link between the Township's zoning permit denial and any discriminatory impact, though the court acknowledged that FHAA reasonable accommodation claims can be applied to local land use board decisions.
brief_summary: Resources for Human Development, Inc. sued Lower Providence Township after being denied a zoning permit to operate supportive housing for disabled individuals, alleging the denial violated the FHA through disparate impact on African American homeless persons and constituted a failure to reasonably accommodate disability under the FHAA. The court granted the Township's motion to dismiss, finding RHD failed to state an FHA claim because the complaint did not properly invoke disability discrimination law and failed to allege sufficient facts demonstrating a causal connection between the zoning denial and any discriminatory effect.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. and Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Allentown Victory Church v. City of Allentown, No. 22-2817 (3d Cir. July 17, 2024)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Gwynedd Properties, Inc. v. Lower Gwynedd Twp., 970 F.2d 1195 (3d Cir. 1992)

source_file: 11267649 - Bowman v. SWBC REAL ESTATE SERVICES, LLC
case_name: Dana Bowman v. SWBC Real Estate Services, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Bowman, a paraplegic veteran who lost the lower half of both legs, toured apartment complexes as a prospective tenant and seeks to challenge physical accessibility barriers including curbs without ramps, narrow doors, and fixtures placed too high.
key_holding: The court dismissed Bowman's § 3604(f)(2) claims for lack of standing because he failed to plead that he uses a wheelchair or was impeded by the alleged accessibility barriers during his property tour, instead merely reciting statutory requirements without connecting them to his own injury.
brief_summary: Dana Bowman, a disabled veteran who lost both legs, sued SWBC Real Estate Services and Jordan Foster Construction alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on physical accessibility barriers he encountered while touring their apartment complexes as a prospective tenant. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(1) for lack of standing, finding Bowman failed to plead that he uses a wheelchair or was personally impeded by the barriers, merely reciting statutory accessibility requirements without showing concrete injury to himself.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 578 U.S. 330 (2016)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Ronald S. Deitch v. Medstar Medical Group, Southern Maryland LLC, et al.
subsidy_program: St. Mary's County Department of Housing affordable housing program for disabled veterans
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged disability discrimination stemming from landlord's allegedly false police report that led to his arrest and eviction; accommodation request not clearly articulated.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim because Deitch's own allegations negated any plausible inference that the adverse action (false police report leading to eviction) was taken because of his disability—it was allegedly motivated by retaliation for his activism against the landlord unrelated to his disability.
brief_summary: Ronald Deitch, a tenant with multi system atrophy, sued his former landlord Habitat America, LLC and its employees, alleging they made a false police report (claiming he was building a bomb) in retaliation for his complaints about the lease and assistance to neighbors, which led to his arrest. The court granted the Habitat defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Deitch failed to state an FHA discrimination claim because his own allegations established the alleged retaliation was unrelated to his disability.
key_cases_cited: Thomas v. The Salvation Army of S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Indep. Hous. Srvs. of S.F. v. Fillmore Ctr. Assocs., 840 F. Supp. 1328 (N.D. Cal. 1993)
Want v. Shindle Props., LLC, No. PWG-18-2833 (D. Md. Oct. 29, 2018)

source_file: 11267651 - COMMISSION ON HUMAN RIGHTS & OPPORTUNITIES EX REL. PIZZOFERRATO v. THE MANSIONS,
case_name: Commission on Human Rights and Opportunities ex rel. Wendy Pizzoferrato et al. v. The Mansions, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Request for accommodation of Wendy's no-pet policy to keep two emotional support dogs for her generalized anxiety disorder
key_holding: The plaintiffs failed to prove that two emotional support dogs were necessary (indispensable) for Wendy's equal use and enjoyment of the dwelling, as evidence that both dogs alleviated her anxiety in different ways merely demonstrated that both were helpful or preferable, which is insufficient to establish necessity under the FHA.
brief_summary: The Connecticut Commission on Human Rights and Opportunities and the Pizzoferratos sued The Mansions, LLC for housing discrimination under state fair housing laws, alleging the defendants constructively denied Wendy's request to keep two emotional support dogs as a reasonable accommodation for her anxiety. The trial court found for the plaintiffs, but the Appellate Court of Connecticut reversed, holding that plaintiffs failed to prove both dogs were necessary for Wendy's equal use and enjoyment of the dwelling, as evidence that both dogs alleviated her anxiety in different ways merely showed the accommodation was preferable, not indispensable.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Assn., 903 F.3d 107 (3d Cir. 2018)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condominium Assn., Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Hollandale Apartments & Health Club, LLC v. Bonesteel, 173 App. Div. 3d 55 (N.Y. App. Div. 2019)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Davis v. Echo Valley Condominium Assn., 945 F.3d 483 (6th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Jennifer Dixon v. Silva-Markham Partners and Koelbel & Co.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that her daughter Cesca be designated as a live-in aide to enable her to transfer from a one-bedroom to a two-bedroom apartment due to her autoimmune disease causing cognitive impairment and mobility limitations.
key_holding: The court found that Ms. Dixon failed to demonstrate a genuine dispute of material fact on her FHA claims because the undisputed evidence showed SMP engaged in the accommodation process by sending paperwork to her physician and working with her on rent balance issues, while her settlement of prior state court claims and the applicable statute of limitations barred many claims.
brief_summary: Jennifer Dixon sued Silva-Markham Partners and Koelbel & Co. (owner and manager of Ash Street Apartments in Denver, Colorado) alleging Fair Housing Act violations for disability discrimination, including failure to accommodate her request for a live-in aide and transfer to a two-bedroom unit, and a hostile housing environment. The magistrate judge recommended granting defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding that Dixon's FHA claims were barred by a prior settlement agreement or the statute of limitations, and that she failed to demonstrate triable issues on her disparate treatment and hostile environment claims.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n, No. 21-4035 (10th Cir. July 6, 2022)
Peterson v. Martinez, 707 F.3d 1197 (10th Cir. 2013)
Throupe v. Univ. of Denver, 988 F.3d 1243 (10th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 2769474_2015_Batista v. Cooperativa De Vivienda Jardines De San Ignacio_ca1
case_name: Priscilla Batista v. Cooperativa de Vivienda Jardines de San Ignacio, et al.
accommodation_description: Batista requested to remain in her three-bedroom apartment rather than transfer to a two-bedroom unit, asserting that her disabilities require separate rooms for physical therapy, reading, and crafts, and that she cannot tolerate the noise levels in the proposed two-bedroom unit.
key_holding: The court affirmed summary judgment for the Cooperativa on the reasonable accommodation claim because the Section 8 subsidy determination that made Batista ineligible for the three-bedroom unit was made by the Puerto Rico Housing Finance Authority, not the private landlord, and the requested accommodation of continuing to receive a Section 8 subsidy for a unit she no longer qualified for was not a reasonable accommodation the Cooperativa could provide.
brief_summary: Priscilla Batista, a disabled tenant with osteoporosis, fibromyalgia, and other conditions, sued a San Juan housing cooperative and its board members alleging the cooperative failed to reasonably accommodate her disability by allowing her to remain in her three-bedroom apartment when Section 8 over-housing rules required her to transfer to a smaller unit. The First Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the cooperative on the accommodation claim, holding that because the Section 8 subsidy determination rested with the Puerto Rico Housing Finance Authority rather than the private landlord, the requested accommodation was not one the cooperative could reasonably provide.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Smith & Lee Assocs. v. City of Taylor, 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 2770244_2015_Eddy Jean Philippeaux v. Apartment Investment and Management Company_ca11
case_name: Eddy Jean Philippeaux v. Apartment Investment and Management Company
accommodation_description: Disabled veteran requested an assigned, handicapped parking space on the ground level of the parking garage as a reasonable accommodation for his mobility limitations following hernia surgery.
key_holding: The requested ground-level parking space was not a reasonable accommodation because it would provide preferential treatment over non-disabled residents, and imposing the obligation to break a third-party lease or pay valet fees would constitute an undue financial burden on the landlord.
brief_summary: A disabled veteran sued his apartment complex alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to assign him a ground-level handicapped parking space as a reasonable accommodation. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that the request was not reasonable because it would grant preferential treatment over all other residents and impose an undue financial burden on the landlord who had leased the ground level to a third-party valet vendor.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Loren v. Sasser, 309 F.3d 1296 (11th Cir. 2002)
Dixon v. Hallmark Cos., 627 F.3d 849 (11th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Glenn J. Hopkins v. Springfield Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Hopkins alleged the Housing Authority failed to accommodate his disability when it reduced his Section 8 subsidy, though the specific accommodation requested is not detailed in the record.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal because Hopkins failed to allege differential treatment based on disability—he conceded the subsidy reduction was for a nondiscriminatory reason—and because he did not allege that he requested any accommodation or that a requested accommodation was linked to his disability.
brief_summary: Glenn Hopkins, a Section 8 voucher recipient, appealed the dismissal of his claims against the Springfield Housing Authority alleging disability discrimination and failure to accommodate under the FHA and ADA after his housing subsidy was reduced. The Seventh Circuit affirmed, finding Hopkins failed to state a discrimination claim because he conceded the reduction was based on a change in household size rather than disability, and failed to state an accommodation claim because he did not allege he requested any accommodation linked to his disability.
key_cases_cited: Wigginton v. Bank of Am. Corp., 770 F.3d 521 (7th Cir. 2014)
Kampmier v. Emeritus Corp., 472 F.3d 930 (7th Cir. 2007)
Stone v. City of Indianapolis Pub. Utils. Div., 281 F.3d 640 (7th Cir. 2002)
Logan v. Wilkins, 644 F.3d 577 (7th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 2803230_2015_Forziano v. Indep. Grp. Home Living Program, Inc._ca2
case_name: Forziano v. Indep. Grp. Home Living Program, Inc.
accommodation_description: Developmentally disabled married couple sought to cohabitate in one of the defendants' publicly funded group homes, requesting waiver of the policy prohibiting couples from cohabitating.
key_holding: The court affirmed dismissal because plaintiffs lacked standing to seek permanent injunctive relief (they had since obtained alternative placement at East End Disability Associates) and could not recover damages for non-intentional FHA violations, and also rejected their intentional discrimination claims because defendants did not provide cohabitation benefits to anyone.
brief_summary: Paul Forziano and Hava Samuels, a developmentally disabled married couple represented by their guardians, sued Independent Group Home Living Program, Maryhaven Center of Hope, and New York State officials for denying their request to cohabitate in one of the defendants' publicly funded group homes, alleging violations of the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, FHA, and other laws. The Second Circuit affirmed dismissal of all claims, holding that plaintiffs lacked standing to seek injunctive relief (having since obtained alternative placement) and could not recover damages for non-intentional discrimination claims, and alternatively that the intentional discrimination claims failed because cohabitation was not provided to any couples.
key_cases_cited: McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Rodriguez v. City of New York, 197 F.3d 611 (2d Cir. 1999)
Doe v. Pfrommer, 148 F.3d 73 (2d Cir. 1998)
Sybalski v. Indep. Grp. Home Living Program, Inc., 546 F.3d 255 (2d Cir. 2008)

case_name: Rodriguez v. Village Green Realty, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought to remain in their rental housing and not be forced to relocate due to their minor child A.R.'s disability; no formal accommodation request was made as the dispute arose from discriminatory text messages.
key_holding: The Second Circuit held that there was sufficient evidence for a reasonable factfinder to conclude that A.R. qualifies as disabled under the FHA, and that the district court erred in granting summary judgment for defendants on claims that the landlord's agent made housing unavailable and imposed different terms because of disability.
brief_summary: Heidi and Juan Rodriguez, parents of minor child A.R. who has autism and epilepsy, sued Village Green Realty, Inc. and its agent Blanca Aponte under the FHA, alleging disability discrimination when Aponte sent text messages indicating the new property owner would not rent to them because of A.R.'s medical condition. The Second Circuit vacated summary judgment for defendants, holding that sufficient evidence existed that A.R. is disabled under the FHA and that Aponte's statements indicating a preference against renting to tenants with A.R.'s disability could support claims under §§ 3604(f)(1), (f)(2), (c), and (d).
key_cases_cited: Ragin v. New York Times Co., 923 F.2d 995 (2d Cir. 1991)
Toyota Motor Mfg., Ky., Inc. v. Williams, 534 U.S. 184 (2002)
School Bd. of Nassau Cnty., Fla. v. Arline, 480 U.S. 273 (1987)
Reeves v.升降床 Corp., 140 F.3d 147 (2d Cir. 1998)

source_file: 2809229_2015_Dick Lalowski v. City of Des Plaines_ca7
2811844_2015_Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc._scotus
case_name: Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
court: SUPREME COURT OF THE UNITED STATES
accommodation_description: No individual accommodation request; ICP, a nonprofit housing organization, brought a disparate-impact challenge to TDHCA's allocation of low-income housing tax credits allegedly perpetuating racial segregation
key_holding: The Supreme Court held that disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the Fair Housing Act, adopting a robust causality requirement and limiting liability to policies that are artificial, arbitrary, and unnecessary barriers, but remanded for the Fifth Circuit to reconsider the merits using the proper burden-shifting framework under HUD's regulation.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project sued the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs alleging that its allocation of low-income housing tax credits perpetuated racial segregation by concentrating credits in predominantly black inner-city areas rather than predominantly white suburban neighborhoods. The Supreme Court affirmed that disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the Fair Housing Act, establishing important limitations including a robust causality requirement and requiring that plaintiffs identify a specific policy causing the disparity, while remanding for proper application of HUD's burden-shifting framework.
key_cases_cited: Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U. S. 424 (1971)
Smith v. City of Jackson, 544 U. S. 228 (2005)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U. S. 557 (2009)
Huntington v. Huntington Branch, NAACP, 488 U. S. 15 (1988)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U. S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Marcia L. Jackson v. Park Place Condominiums Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: This is a race-based hostile housing environment claim, not a disability accommodation request; Ms. Jackson alleged the HOA discriminated against her because of her race through mailbox delays, noise complaints, and other incidents.
key_holding: The Tenth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the HOA because Ms. Jackson failed to present sufficient evidence that the alleged harassment (delayed mailbox name, noise complaints, decal theft, nameplate removal) was based on her race rather than legitimate property management concerns.
brief_summary: Marcia L. Jackson, an African-American woman, sued Park Place Condominiums Association alleging racial discrimination and a hostile housing environment during her tenancy, claiming the HOA harassed her because of her race through mailbox delays, noise complaints, and property incidents. The Tenth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the HOA, finding Jackson failed to present sufficient evidence that the alleged harassment was motivated by race rather than legitimate property management concerns.
key_cases_cited: Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)
Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)
Johnson v. Weld Cty., Colo., 594 F.3d 1202 (10th Cir. 2010)
Morris v. City of Colo. Springs, 666 F.3d 654 (10th Cir. 2012)
Hampton v. Dillard Department Stores, Inc., 247 F.3d 1091 (10th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 2827459_2015_Anderson Ex Rel. C.A. v. City of Blue Ash_ca6
case_name: Ingrid Anderson, individually and parent on behalf of C.A.; Housing Opportunities Made Equal, Inc. v. City of Blue Ash
accommodation_description: Request to keep a miniature horse at residence as a service animal for C.A., who has disabilities affecting her balance and mobility, enabling independent recreation and exercise in her backyard.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit reversed summary judgment for the City on the FHAA reasonable accommodation claim, finding genuine factual disputes whether a miniature horse was necessary to afford C.A. equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling and whether permitting it was reasonable, while affirming summary judgment for the City on ADA intentional discrimination and FHAA disparate treatment and impact claims.
brief_summary: Ingrid Anderson, on behalf of her disabled daughter C.A., and Housing Opportunities Made Equal sued the City of Blue Ash, Ohio, challenging the City's ordinance banning horses from residential property and seeking a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Amendments Act to keep a miniature horse as a service animal at their home. The Sixth Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment to the City on the reasonable accommodation claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding necessity and reasonableness, while affirming summary judgment for the City on ADA intentional discrimination and FHAA disparate treatment and impact claims.
key_cases_cited: Smith & Lee Assocs. v. City of Taylor, Mich., 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Nina Shahin v. Andrew Rogan
accommodation_description: Application for home equity loan allegedly denied due to plaintiffs' national origin
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of the Shahins' FHA and ECOA claims because the complaint failed to allege non-conclusory factual matter establishing a plausible inference that PNC Bank denied their loan application based on national origin discrimination.
brief_summary: Nina and Mazen Shahin sued PNC Bank and branch managers alleging that the bank discriminated against them on the basis of national origin in denying a home equity loan, in violation of the Fair Housing Act and Equal Credit Opportunity Act. The Third Circuit affirmed the District Court's dismissal of the FHA/ECOA claims, finding the complaint failed to state plausible facts supporting an inference of discrimination, though the court vacated and remanded for the plaintiffs to potentially amend their TILA claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmties. Project, Inc.
16630 Southfield Ltd. P'ship v. Flagstar Bank

source_file: 2832679_2015_City of Miami v. Bank of America Corp._ca11
case_name: City of Miami v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: This case involves discriminatory mortgage lending claims (redlining and reverse redlining), not a reasonable accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal of Miami's FHA claim, finding the City had Article III standing, fell within the FHA's zone of interests, and adequately pled proximate cause, while affirming dismissal of the state law unjust enrichment claim.
brief_summary: The City of Miami sued Wells Fargo alleging the bank engaged in discriminatory mortgage lending by targeting Black and Latino borrowers for predatory loans (redlining and reverse redlining), causing increased foreclosures that reduced property tax revenue and increased municipal costs. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding the City had Article III standing, the FHA's zone of interests extends as broadly as Article III permits, and the City adequately alleged proximate cause between Wells Fargo's discriminatory lending and the City's financial injuries.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance, 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Nasser v. City of Homewood, 671 F.2d 432 (11th Cir. 1982)

source_file: 2832680_2015_City of Miami v. Wells Fargo _ Co._ca11
case_name: City of Miami v. Citigroup Inc.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it is a discriminatory lending case where the City of Miami alleges Citigroup engaged in redlining and reverse redlining, targeting minority borrowers for predatory loans in violation of the FHA.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal of the City's FHA claims, holding that (1) the City had Article III standing based on lost tax revenue from foreclosed properties, (2) the FHA's zone of interests extends as broadly as Article III permits, and (3) the City adequately alleged proximate cause; however, the court affirmed dismissal of the state law unjust enrichment claim.
brief_summary: The City of Miami sued Citigroup Inc. and affiliated entities under the Fair Housing Act, alleging the bank engaged in discriminatory mortgage lending by targeting Black and Latino borrowers for predatory loans, resulting in higher foreclosure rates that reduced the City's property tax base. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the dismissal of the FHA claims, holding the City had standing and adequately alleged proximate cause under the FHA, but affirmed dismissal of the state law unjust enrichment claim.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone, Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance, 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Nasser v. City of Homewood, 671 F.2d 432 (11th Cir. 1982)

source_file: 2832681_2015_City of Miami v. Bank of America Corporation_ca11
case_name: City of Miami v. Bank of America Corporation
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it involves claims of discriminatory mortgage lending practices targeting minority borrowers resulting in foreclosures that harmed the City.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal of Miami's FHA claims, finding the City has standing under the FHA's zone of interests, adequately alleged proximate cause, and may invoke the continuing violation doctrine for discriminatory lending claims.
brief_summary: The City of Miami sued Bank of America alleging the bank engaged in discriminatory mortgage lending by targeting black and Latino borrowers for predatory loans, causing minority property foreclosures that deprived the City of tax revenue and increased municipal costs. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding the City has statutory standing under the FHA's broad zone of interests, adequately alleged proximate cause, and may invoke the continuing violation doctrine, while affirming dismissal of the state unjust enrichment claim.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Brenda Miles v. David Wesley
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, disabled tenants, challenge the Los Angeles Superior Court's consolidation of unlawful detainer (eviction) proceedings into centralized hub courts as having a disparate impact on disabled and minority residents who rely on public transportation.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal under O'Shea abstention, holding that federal courts must abstain from interfering with state judicial administration because the plaintiffs' challenge to the court consolidation plan seeks heavy federal interference in the state's management of its shrinking judicial resources.
brief_summary: Brenda Miles, Dane Sullivan, and several nonprofit organizations sued the State of California and Los Angeles Superior Court officials, challenging the consolidation of unlawful detainer actions into centralized hub courts as having a disparate impact on disabled and minority residents. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, holding that O'Shea abstention required federal courts to refrain from interfering with state judicial administration because the plaintiffs sought injunctive relief that would effectively place federal courts in the role of supervising state court resource allocation decisions.
key_cases_cited: O'Shea v. Littleton, 414 U.S. 488 (1974)
Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971)
Tennessee v. Lane, 541 U.S. 509 (2004)
L.A. Cty. Bar Ass'n v. Eu, 979 F.2d 697 (9th Cir. 1992)
Courthouse News Serv. v. Planet, 750 F.3d 776 (9th Cir. 2014)

source_file: 2959750_2015_Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc._scotus
case_name: Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
court: SUPREME COURT OF THE UNITED STATES
accommodation_description: Tax credit allocation policy challenged as perpetuating segregated housing patterns through disparate impact rather than individual accommodation request
key_holding: Disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the Fair Housing Act, and the Court adopted HUD's burden-shifting regulation while cautioning that liability must be properly limited with robust causality requirements to avoid constitutional concerns and prevent liability based on mere statistical disparity.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project sued the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs alleging that the Department's disproportionate allocation of low-income housing tax credits to predominantly black inner-city areas rather than white suburbs violated the Fair Housing Act's disparate-impact prohibition. The Supreme Court held that disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the FHA, affirmed the Fifth Circuit's judgment remanding the case, and established limiting principles requiring robust causality requirements and caution against racial quotas in remedial orders.
key_cases_cited: Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
Smith v. City of Jackson, 544 U.S. 228 (2005)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Huntington v. Huntington Branch, NAACP, 488 U.S. 15 (1988)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Shana Freedman v. SunTrust Banks, Inc. and SunTrust Mortgage, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff challenged SunTrust's policy requiring loan applicants with disability income (SSDI) to obtain documentation from a doctor or Social Security confirming benefits would continue, which plaintiff alleged constituted disability discrimination in lending.
key_holding: The court lacked personal jurisdiction over defendants (Georgia and Virginia corporations without sufficient contacts with D.C.) and transferred the case to the Middle District of Florida in the interest of justice to avoid time-barring plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Shana Freedman, a Florida resident receiving Social Security Disability Insurance, sued SunTrust Banks and SunTrust Mortgage alleging that their policy requiring documentation that disability benefits would continue constituted disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and ECOA. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction and transferred the case to the Middle District of Florida in the interest of justice to prevent plaintiff's FHA claims from being time-barred.
key_cases_cited: Daimler AG v. Bauman, 134 S. Ct. 746 (2014)
Goodyear Dunlop Tire Operations, S.A. v. Brown, 131 S. Ct. 2846 (2011)
Goldlawr, Inc. v. Heiman, 369 U.S. 463 (1962)
Sinclair v. Kleindienst, 711 F.2d 291 (D.C. Cir. 1983)
Gorman v. Ameritrade Holding Corp., 293 F.3d 506 (D.C. Cir. 2002)

source_file: 3004697_2015_County of Westchester v. United States Department of Housing _ Urban Development_ca2
case_name: County of Westchester v. U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
