accommodation_description: This is not an individual accommodation case; the County of Westchester challenges HUD's decision to withhold CPD grant funds because the County's Analysis of Impediments failed to adequately assess impediments to fair housing caused by local zoning ordinances.
key_holding: HUD acted within its statutory authority when it required Westchester County to analyze whether local zoning laws impeded fair housing and withheld CPD funds after the County's Analysis of Impediments was found inadequate and unsupported by the evidentiary record.
brief_summary: Westchester County appealed the District Court's grant of summary judgment upholding HUD's decision to withhold Community Planning and Development Formula Grant funds for fiscal years 2011, 2013, and 2014 because the County's required Analysis of Impediments failed to adequately assess whether local zoning ordinances impeded fair housing. The Second Circuit affirmed, holding that HUD acted within its statutory authority under the APA and applicable fair housing statutes by requiring jurisdictions receiving federal funds to analyze impediments to fair housing, including those caused by exclusionary zoning practices.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
United States v. Yonkers Bd. of Educ., 837 F.2d 1181 (2d Cir. 1987)
Rufo v. Inmates of Suffolk Cty. Jail, 502 U.S. 367 (1992)

case_name: Anthony Hood; Princess Williams v. Tessa Pope, et al.
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves alleged race-based housing discrimination, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal because plaintiffs failed to allege sufficient facts to create a plausible inference that race was a significant factor in defendants' conduct; the speculative explanations for the in-person denial did not isolate race as a motivating factor.
brief_summary: Anthony Hood and Princess Williams sued Asset Plus Corporation and its employees alleging race-based housing discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming they were denied an apartment based on their race and subsequently harassed. The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal, finding plaintiffs failed to state plausible claims because their allegations did not isolate race as a factor in the denial and subsequent conduct was unrelated to the initial refusal.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Lincoln v. Case, 340 F.3d 283 (5th Cir. 2003)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)

source_file: 3007122_2015_Mark Hibbert, Sr. v. Bellmawr Park Mutual Housing_ca3
case_name: Mark E. Hibbert, Sr. v. Bellmawr Park Mutual Housing Corporation
accommodation_description: Hibbert alleged defendants exploited his deafness to acquire his property through coercive conduct during the sale process.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants on the breach of contract claim, finding Hibbert voluntarily moved to Maine rather than being coerced, and separately waived the FHA and NJLAD claims by failing to brief them on appeal.
brief_summary: Mark Hibbert, a deaf resident of Bellmawr Park Mutual Housing Corporation, sued the housing corporation and its managers alleging breach of contract and Fair Housing Act violations, claiming defendants exploited his disability to coerce him into selling his property and moving to Maine. The Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants on appeal, finding Hibbert voluntarily moved after purchasing property in Maine, and separately held that Hibbert waived his FHA and NJLAD claims by failing to brief them on appeal.

case_name: Donavette Ely v. Mobile Housing Board
accommodation_description: Request for an extension of the term of her Section 8 housing voucher as a reasonable accommodation for her son's asthma
key_holding: The court affirmed summary judgment for the Board because Ely never explained to the Board that she needed a voucher extension as a reasonable accommodation for her son's disability, and mere knowledge of the son's asthma from a prior bedroom-size request did not obligate the Board to treat her extension request as an accommodation request.
brief_summary: Donavette Ely, a single mother, sued the Mobile Housing Board alleging the Board violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to extend her Section 8 housing voucher as a reasonable accommodation for her son's asthma. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the Board, holding that Ely failed to establish a reasonable accommodation claim because she never informed the Board that she needed a voucher extension to accommodate her son's disability, and the Board therefore could not be liable for refusing to grant an accommodation it did not know was necessary.
key_cases_cited: Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Keys Youth Servs., Inc. v. City of Olathe, 248 F.3d 1267 (10th Cir. 2001)
Bd. of Regents of State Colls. v. Roth, 408 U.S. 564 (1972)
Town of Castle Rock v. Gonzales, 545 U.S. 748 (2005)

source_file: 3132420_2015_Anderson Group, LLC v. City of Saratoga Springs_ca2
case_name: The Anderson Group, LLC v. City of Saratoga Springs
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; developer TAG challenged city's zoning rezoning as having discriminatory disparate impact on African-Americans and families with children.
key_holding: The City waived its objection to inconsistencies in the 2010 jury verdict by failing to raise it before the jury was discharged, and any inconsistencies did not constitute fundamental error; however, the $900,000 developer fee portion of the damages award was impermissibly speculative and must be remitted to $100,000.
brief_summary: The Anderson Group (TAG), a real estate developer, sued the City of Saratoga Springs challenging the city's 2005 rezoning of TAG's property as having a disparate impact on African-Americans and families with children under the Fair Housing Act. The Second Circuit reinstated the 2010 jury verdict finding the City liable on the disparate impact claim, reversed the district court's order granting a new trial, and remanded for a new trial on damages only unless TAG accepted remittitur of the $1 million award to $100,000 because the $900,000 developer fee was too speculative.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Story Parchment Co. v. Patterson Parchment Paper Co., 282 U.S. 555 (1931)
Casella v. Equifax Credit Info. Servs., 56 F.3d 469 (2d Cir. 1995)
Shcherbakovskiy v. Da Capo Al Fine, Ltd., 490 F.3d 130 (2d Cir. 2007)
Stampf v. Long Island R.R. Co., 761 F.3d 192 (2d Cir. 2014)

case_name: Parham v. CIH Properties, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made vague references to wanting a first-floor apartment and accessible parking, but the court found she failed to properly request or document any reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: Even assuming the ADA or FHA applied to private apartment complexes, plaintiff failed to demonstrate she had a qualifying disability, that defendants knew of it, or that she made a cognizable accommodation request that defendants denied.
brief_summary: Evelyn Parham sued her landlord alleging that her car with a handicap tag was improperly towed from the apartment complex parking lot and never returned; she brought claims for breach of contract, fraudulent misrepresentation, violations of the D.C. Consumer Protection Act, and reasonable accommodation under the ADA and FHA. The court granted summary judgment to defendants on all claims, finding that plaintiff failed to present competent evidence on any element of her claims, including that she made a cognizable request for reasonable accommodation under disability statutes.
key_cases_cited: Celotex Corp. v. Catrett
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc.
Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence
Flemmings v. Howard Univ.
Twin Towers Plaza Tenants Ass'n, Inc. v. Capitol Park Assocs., L.P.

source_file: 3171091_2016_Richard Wolf v. Bank of America, N.A., et a_ca5
case_name: Richard B. Wolf; Marcia A. Wolf v. Bank of America National Association; Marvin Blankenship; Kianie King; Jimmy Widdle; Phil Gonzalez
accommodation_description: Mortgage lending dispute where plaintiffs alleged discrimination but failed to specify any accommodation request related to disability.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal because the Wolfs failed to allege discriminatory intent or a significant discriminatory effect required to state a Fair Housing Act claim.
brief_summary: Richard and Marcia Wolf sued Bank of America and several employees challenging the bank's refusal to refinance their residence and related foreclosure actions, asserting fraud, breach of contract, RICO, and Fair Housing Act claims. The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal, finding the Wolfs' complaint failed to state a claim because they did not allege discriminatory intent or a significant discriminatory effect as required under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Simms v. First Gibraltar Bank, 83 F.3d 1546 (5th Cir. 1996)
Martins v. BAC Home Loans Serv., L.P., 722 F.3d 249 (5th Cir. 2013)
Warren v. Bank of America, N.A., 566 F. App'x 379 (5th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Dyan Hunt v. Aimco Properties, L.P.
accommodation_description: Dyan Hunt asked Aimco to allow the family to remain in their apartment while she arranged for offsite daytime care for her son Karl, requesting an exception to Aimco's apparent policy of non-renewing leases for tenants who make threats.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, finding that the complaint adequately pleaded Dyan requested an accommodation by explaining Karl's disability-caused misunderstandings and offering to arrange offsite daytime care, and that Aimco refused to consider this accommodation, thereby failing to reasonably accommodate Karl's disability.
brief_summary: Dyan Hunt sued Aimco Properties on behalf of herself and her son Karl, who has Down Syndrome, alleging that Aimco violated the FHA by threatening eviction and refusing to renew their lease based on Karl's disability-related behavior (perceived threats stemming from his intellectual disability). The Eleventh Circuit reversed the dismissal, holding that the complaint adequately pleaded claims for making housing unavailable, discriminatory terms and conditions, and failure to reasonably accommodate Karl's disability by disregarding Dyan's explanation of his disability and her offer to arrange offsite daytime care as an alternative to eviction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
McDonald v. Verble, 622 F.2d 1227 (6th Cir. 1980)

source_file: 3187921_2016_MHANY Mgmt., Inc. v. Cty of Nassau_ca2
case_name: MHANY Management, Inc. v. County of Nassau et al.
accommodation_description: This is a zoning discrimination case, not a reasonable accommodation case; fair housing organizations challenged Garden City's rezoning from R-M (multi-family) to R-T (single-family/townhouse) zoning that blocked affordable housing development.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed that Garden City acted with discriminatory intent in switching from R-M to R-T zoning, but vacated the disparate impact liability finding and remanded for application of the burden-shifting framework under 24 C.F.R. § 100.500(c)(3); the court also affirmed dismissal of claims against Nassau County.
brief_summary: MHANY Management and New York Communities for Change sued Garden City and Nassau County challenging the Village's rezoning of county-owned land from multi-family (R-M) to restrictive (R-T) zoning that blocked affordable housing development, alleging the rezoning decision was motivated by racial animus against African-American and Hispanic communities. The Second Circuit affirmed that Garden City acted with discriminatory intent under § 3604(a) but vacated the disparate impact finding and remanded for application of the HUD regulatory burden-shifting framework.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project
Huntington Branch, NAACP v. Town of Huntington
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Dev. Corp.
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center

source_file: 3188956_2016_Avenue 6E Investments, LLC v. City of Yuma_ca9
case_name: Avenue 6E Investments, LLC v. City of Yuma
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; this case concerns a developer's zoning application denial that allegedly discriminated against Hispanic residents through disparate impact and disparate treatment.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit reversed the dismissal of the developers' disparate-treatment claims, finding the complaint alleged sufficient facts showing the City Council's denial of rezoning was plausibly motivated by racial animus, and reversed summary judgment on the disparate-impact claim, rejecting the rule that availability of comparable housing elsewhere defeats a disparate-impact challenge.
brief_summary: Two real estate developers sued the City of Yuma challenging the denial of their rezoning application to build higher-density housing, alleging the denial violated the Equal Protection Clause and Fair Housing Act by discriminating against Hispanic residents through disparate treatment and disparate impact. The Ninth Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal of the disparate-treatment claims and summary judgment on the disparate-impact claim, holding the complaint stated plausible claims based on evidence of racially charged community opposition and that the availability of comparable housing elsewhere did not preclude a disparate-impact finding.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmties. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp. v. Vill. of Arlington Heights, 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Huntington Branch, N.A.A.C.P. v. Huntington, 844 F.2d 926 (2d Cir. 1988)
Hallmark Developers, Inc. v. Fulton County, 466 F.3d 1276 (11th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 3199679_2016_Castillo Condominium Ass'n v. United States Department of Housing _ Urban Development_ca1
case_name: Castillo Condominium Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Request for exception to condominium's 'no pets' bylaw to allow Giménez to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for his anxiety and depression
key_holding: Substantial evidence supported the Secretary's finding that the Association violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to accommodate Giménez's emotional support dog, a refusal that made his home unavailable to him as a practical matter.
brief_summary: HUD filed a charge against the Castillo Condominium Association on behalf of Carlo Giménez Bianco, alleging the Association violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to grant him a reasonable accommodation to keep an emotional support dog despite his documented anxiety and depression. The First Circuit denied the Association's petition for judicial review of the Secretary's final order and granted HUD's cross-petition for enforcement, finding substantial evidence supported the Secretary's determination that Giménez had a cognizable disability, requested a reasonable accommodation, and the Association's refusal to grant it violated the Act.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y of HUD, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Aylett v. Sec'y of HUD, 54 F.3d 1560 (10th Cir. 1995)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)

source_file: 3200963_2016_Dorothy Hartman v. Bank of New York Mellon_ca3
case_name: Dorothy Hartman v. Bank of New York Mellon
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleged defendants violated FHA by refusing to refinance based on race and disability, which court analyzed as disparate treatment claim.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal because Hartman's complaint failed to adequately plead discriminatory purpose—she alleged defendants denied refinancing based on information from the City about utility nonpayment and property valuation, not based on her protected status.
brief_summary: Pro se appellant Dorothy Hartman appealed the District Court's dismissal of her fourth amended complaint against Bank of New York Mellon, the City of Philadelphia, Prudential Fox & Roach Realtors, and Michael McCann, alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race and disability discrimination regarding mortgage refinancing denial. The Third Circuit summarily affirmed, holding that Hartman's complaint failed to plausibly allege discriminatory purpose, as defendants' stated reasons for denying refinancing (utility nonpayment and property valuation information from the City) did not give rise to an inference of discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lauren W. ex rel. Jean W. v. DeFlaminis, 480 F.3d 259 (3d Cir. 2007)

source_file: 3204035_2016_Fair Housing Rights Center v. Post Goldtex GP, LLC_ca3
case_name: Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Post Goldtex GP, LLC
accommodation_description: Alleged violations of FHA design and construction accessibility requirements in a converted building including narrow doors, high thresholds, and inaccessible kitchen counters.
key_holding: Under Chevron step two, HUD's reasonable interpretation that the FHA's design and construction requirements apply only to buildings for first occupancy as dwellings, excluding converted commercial buildings, is entitled to deference, and thus the Goldtex Building was not subject to § 3604(f)(3)(C).
brief_summary: Fair Housing Rights Center sued Goldtex and KlingStubbins alleging that a building originally constructed as a factory in 1912 but converted to 163 residential apartments in 2013 violated the FHA's design and construction accessibility requirements. The Third Circuit affirmed the dismissal, holding that under Chevron deference, HUD's reasonable interpretation that the requirements apply only to buildings with first occupancy as dwellings—excluding converted buildings that were previously occupied for other purposes—was entitled to deference.
key_cases_cited: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1983)
Barnhart v. Walton, 535 U.S. 212 (2002)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)

case_name: Tri-Corp Housing Incorporated v. Robert Bauman
subsidy_program: Wisconsin Housing and Economic Development Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Tri-Corp alleged that Alderman Bauman's public criticism and lobbying against its facilities for mentally disabled residents violated the Fair Housing Act, though no specific accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed that Tri-Corp's §1983 claims based on the ADA and Rehabilitation Act fail because those statutes contain comprehensive remedial schemes that cannot be overridden, and its FHA claims based on Bauman's speech and lobbying fail because the First Amendment and Noerr-Pennington doctrine protect public officials' political speech from civil liability.
brief_summary: Tri-Corp Housing, a nonprofit providing low-income housing to mentally disabled persons, sued Alderman Robert Bauman under §1983 and the Fair Housing Act, alleging his critical speeches and lobbying against Tri-Corp's facilities constituted discrimination and contributed to foreclosure. The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that §1983 cannot override the remedial schemes of the ADA and Rehabilitation Act, and that Bauman's political speech is protected by the First Amendment and the Noerr-Pennington doctrine regardless of its effect on housing providers.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
New West, L.P. v. Joliet, 491 F.3d 717 (7th Cir. 2007)
Blessing v. Freestone, 520 U.S. 329 (1997)
Rancho Palos Verdes v. Abrams, 544 U.S. 113 (2005)
Eastern Railroad Presidents Conference v. Noerr Motor Freight, Inc., 365 U.S. 127 (1961)

case_name: Austin v. Town of Farmington
accommodation_description: Family sought a zoning variance to install a fence, pool, and deck to accommodate their disabled son with cerebral palsy, with the disputed accommodation being the Town's requirement that the structures be removed when the child no longer resides there.
key_holding: The Second Circuit held that the Town's Restoration Provisions requiring removal of disability-related structures when the disabled child leaves are not per se unreasonable under § 3604(f)(3)(B), and the reasonableness of the accommodation must be determined on a full evidentiary record rather than as a matter of law on the pleadings.
brief_summary: The Austins sued the Town of Farmington challenging zoning variance conditions requiring removal of a fence, pool, and deck installed to accommodate their disabled son when he no longer lives there. The Second Circuit affirmed dismissal of the retaliation claim but vacated dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, holding that whether the Town's restoration requirement is a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) cannot be decided as a matter of law on the pleadings.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Olson v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Twp. of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)

source_file: 3219398_2016_Shaddonna Miles v. Housing Authority of Texarkana_ca5
case_name: Shaddonna Diane Miles v. Housing Authority of Texarkana, Texas
accommodation_description: Miles alleged HATT denied her assistance with its Affordable Homeownership Program due to her disability, though she never identified the specific disability.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal because Miles failed to allege facts showing she has a qualifying handicap under the FHA definition and failed to establish she was qualified to receive assistance under the Program, as she was denied a mortgage by an independent lender.
brief_summary: Shaddonna Miles sued the Housing Authority of Texarkana and employee Vicki Nelson alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming HATT denied her assistance in purchasing a home through its Affordable Homeownership Program. The Fifth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, finding Miles failed to state a plausible discrimination claim because she never identified her disability, alleged no facts showing it substantially limits major life activities, and failed to establish she was qualified to receive program assistance since she was denied a mortgage by an independent lender.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Petrello v. Prucka, 484 F. App'x 939 (5th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Thomas Wilson and Randy Brown v. Warren County, Illinois, Martin Edwards, Thomas Carithers, Albert Algren, Ronald Hanson, Mark Johnson, and Douglas Reiners
accommodation_description: Wilson alleged that private defendants removed his property and exploited his psychological disabilities to prevent him from objecting, constituting disability discrimination under § 3604(f)(1) and § 3617.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of Wilson's FHA claim because the complaint failed to allege facts plausibly showing the private defendants acted because of his disability, as their conduct appeared motivated by a business dispute rather than Wilson's psychological conditions.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Thomas Wilson and Randy Brown sued Warren County, Illinois, county officials, and private citizens under the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. § 1983, alleging disability discrimination when private defendants allegedly exploited Wilson's psychological conditions to remove property while law enforcement stood by. The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of Wilson's FHA claim, finding he failed to plausibly allege the defendants acted because of his disability rather than a pre-existing business dispute.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Bloch v. Frischholz
Roberts v. City of Chicago
Adickes v. S.H. Kress & Co.

case_name: Gregory Selden v. Airbnb, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged race discrimination in rental housing via Airbnb platform, claiming he was denied accommodations based on his race while similarly situated white users were accepted.
key_holding: The court granted Airbnb's motion to compel arbitration, finding that Selden agreed to Airbnb's Terms of Service—including the mandatory arbitration clause—when he signed up for the platform, and that this arbitration clause encompassed his race discrimination claims under federal civil rights laws.
brief_summary: Gregory Selden sued Airbnb, Inc. alleging race discrimination in housing under the Fair Housing Act, Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and 42 U.S.C. § 1981, claiming an Airbnb host denied him accommodations based on his race while accepting white users. The court granted Airbnb's motion to compel arbitration based on its Terms of Service agreement, finding Selden was on inquiry notice of the arbitration clause and that his discrimination claims fell within its scope.
key_cases_cited: AT&T Mobility LLC v. Concepcion, 563 U.S. 333 (2011)
Gilmer v. Interstate/Johnson Lane Corp., 500 U.S. 20 (1991)
Circuit City Stores, Inc. v. Adams, 532 U.S. 105 (2001)
Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc., 6 P.3d 669 (Cal. 2000)
Specht v. Netscape Commc'ns Corp., 306 F.3d 17 (2d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Sharon T. Thomas v. The Salvation Army Southern Territory
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a homeless woman with mental health conditions, was denied admission to and evicted from homeless shelters operated by defendants; she alleges this occurred because of her mental health disability.
key_holding: The Fourth Circuit affirmed dismissal of plaintiff's FHA claim because she failed to adequately allege a qualifying mental health disability and failed to establish a plausible nexus between her alleged disability and defendants' decision to deny her shelter, noting defendants' legitimate concerns about her behavior and their request for a mental health evaluation did not constitute discrimination based on disability.
brief_summary: Sharon Thomas, a homeless woman with mental health conditions, sued the Salvation Army and two affiliated homeless shelters alleging they denied her admission and evicted her because of her disability in violation of the FHA. The Fourth Circuit affirmed dismissal, finding Thomas failed to plausibly allege she had a qualifying handicap under the FHA or that defendants' decision to deny her shelter was because of her alleged disability rather than legitimate concerns about her behavior.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center

case_name: Adriann Borum, et al. v. Brentwood Village, LLC, et al.
subsidy_program: HUD loan collateral mentioned; otherwise NONE_MENTIONED
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; this is a disparate impact challenge to a redevelopment plan that eliminates larger family-sized apartments.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the disparate impact claim, finding plaintiffs' statistical showing that families are over three times more likely than non-families to be adversely affected by the redevelopment plan was sufficient to state a claim; the court denied plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction because the threatened displacement injuries were not sufficiently imminent.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs ONE DC and two individual tenants sued owners of Brookland Manor affordable housing complex alleging that the planned redevelopment, which eliminates most three-, four-, and five-bedroom apartments, violates the FHA's familial status protections by disparately impacting families with children. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs' statistical showing of disparate impact on families sufficient to state a claim; the court also denied plaintiffs' preliminary injunction motion, finding plaintiffs failed to demonstrate imminent irreparable harm since displacement would not occur for years.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Betsey v. Turtle Creek Associates, 736 F.2d 983 (4th Cir. 1984)
Boykin v. Gray, 986 F. Supp. 2d 14 (D.D.C. 2013)
2922 Sherman Ave. Tenants' Ass'n v. District of Columbia, 444 F.3d 673 (D.C. Cir. 2006)

source_file: 4107938_2016_Hardaway v. District of Columbia Housing Authority_cadc
case_name: Angelene Hardaway and Lena Hardaway v. District of Columbia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Angelene Hardaway requested approval for her sister Lena to serve as her live-in aide to provide round-the-clock care for her disability under the Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher Program.
key_holding: The D.C. Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal for lack of standing and mootness, holding that Angelene established injury-in-fact by demonstrating the Authority's revocation of her live-in aide approval stripped her of her legal entitlement to a two-bedroom voucher, creating concrete and particularized harm, and the Authority's subsequent 'act of administrative grace' allowing her to retain the voucher did not moot her claims because it retained authority to revoke at any time.
brief_summary: Angelene and Lena Hardaway appealed the dismissal of their claims against the District of Columbia Housing Authority, which had denied Angelene's request for a live-in aide to care for her disability, thereby threatening her legal entitlement to a two-bedroom Section 8 voucher. The D.C. Circuit reversed, holding that Angelene had Article III standing because the Authority's revocation of live-in aide approval caused concrete injury by stripping her statutory entitlement to a two-bedroom voucher, and her claims were not moot because the Authority's permissive retention of the voucher was merely discretionary and could be revoked at any time.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Friends of the Earth, Inc. v. Laidlaw Environmental Services, 528 U.S. 167 (2000)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
United States v. Hubbard, 650 F.2d 293 (D.C. Cir. 1980)
EEOC v. National Children's Center, Inc., 98 F.3d 1406 (D.C. Cir. 1996)

case_name: Jason Abdus-Sabur v. Hope Village, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a quadriplegic wheelchair user, needed the shower threshold barrier removed or modified to allow safe wheelchair transfer into the shower.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff, as a federal inmate placed at Hope Village by the BOP rather than someone who rented or purchased a dwelling, did not fall within the class of 'buyer or renter' protected under § 3604 of the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Jason Abdus-Sabur, a quadriplegic federal inmate, sued Hope Village (a halfway house) and Corrections Corporation of America alleging disability discrimination under the FHA, ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and D.C. Human Rights Act, claiming the shower in his assigned unit had an architectural barrier that caused him to fall and suffer injuries. The Court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, holding that plaintiff's status as a BOP-placed inmate, rather than a buyer or renter, placed him outside the class of persons protected by § 3604 of the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Hunter ex rel. A.H. v. District of Columbia, 64 F. Supp. 3d 158 (D.D.C. 2014)
Lee v. Corr. Corp. of Am./Corr. Treatment Facility, 61 F. Supp. 3d 139 (D.D.C. 2014)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 4154998_2017_Linkletter v. Western _ Southern Financial Group, Inc._ca6
case_name: Gayle Linkletter v. Western & Southern Financial Group, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff was not seeking a housing accommodation; the case involves retaliation for signing a petition supporting women's housing rights under the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: Signing a petition supporting a women's shelter constitutes 'encouragement' of housing rights under § 3617, and rescinding an employment offer for petition-signing constitutes unlawful 'interference' with those rights under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Gayle Linkletter sued Western & Southern Financial Group and employee Kim Chiodi after her accepted job offer was rescinded because she signed an online petition supporting the Anna Louise Inn women's shelter, which had been involved in a Fair Housing Act dispute with Western & Southern. The Sixth Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Linkletter's petition-signing constituted 'encouragement' of housing rights under § 3617, and that rescinding her employment offer was unlawful 'interference' with those rights protected by § 3604.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Mich. Prot. and Advocacy Serv., Inc. v. Babin, 18 F.3d 337 (6th Cir. 1994)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Hidden Village, LLC v. City of Lakewood, 734 F.3d 519 (6th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 4155882_2017_United States v. Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc._dcd
case_name: United States v. Mid-America Apartment Communities, Inc.
accommodation_description: Pattern or practice claim alleging failure to design and construct 50 multifamily dwellings with required accessibility features (accessible routes, environmental controls in accessible locations, grab bar reinforcements, and usable kitchens/bathrooms).
key_holding: The court ruled on evidentiary issues in advance of trial, holding that the HUD Guidelines do not set a minimum standard for FHA compliance, that features of adaptive design must be in place at construction, and that the government may only introduce evidence from 8 of the 50 properties because the remaining properties were either acquired (not designed by defendant), permitted under state/local codes that incorporated FHA requirements, or not properly inspected.
brief_summary: The United States brought a pattern-or-practice claim against Mid-America Apartment Communities (formerly Post Properties) alleging that 50 multifamily dwellings were designed and constructed without required accessibility features in violation of the FHA. The court ruled on pre-trial evidentiary issues, determining that the HUD Guidelines do not establish minimum FHA compliance standards, that adaptive design features must be in place at construction rather than added later, and limiting the government's evidence to only 8 properties because 42 properties were either acquired, properly permitted under state/local codes incorporating the FHA, or not properly inspected.
key_cases_cited: International Brotherhood of Teamsters v. United States, 431 U.S. 324 (1977)
United States v. Mead Corp., 533 U.S. 218 (2001)
Baltimore Neighborhoods, Inc. v. Rommel Builders, Inc., 40 F. Supp. 2d 700 (D. Md. 1999)
Montana Fair Housing, Inc. v. American Capital Development, Inc., 81 F. Supp. 2d 1057 (D. Mont. 1999)
FTC v. H.J. Heinz Co., 246 F.3d 708 (D.C. Cir. 2001)

source_file: 4157097_2017_Liana Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium As_ca3
case_name: Liana Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Request to keep emotional support dogs despite condominium association's no-dogs policy, as accommodations for disability-related needs
key_holding: Fair Housing Act claims survive the death of a party under federal common law, and genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether Cowpet constructively refused reasonable accommodations by declaring plaintiffs in violation of the no-dogs rule, fining them daily, and unduly delaying accommodation decisions, while neighbors' harassment on a blog constituted interference under § 3617.
brief_summary: Walters and Kromenhoek, disabled condominium owners at Cowpet Bay West, sued the association and individual neighbors under the Fair Housing Act for refusing to accommodate their emotional support animals and for harassment. The Third Circuit reversed the District Court's dismissal of Walters' claims (holding FHA claims survive death under federal common law) and reversed summary judgment for defendants on both the reasonable accommodation and interference claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether the association constructively refused accommodations and whether neighbors' blog harassment interfered with fair housing rights.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Castillo Condo. Ass'n v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 821 F.3d 92 (1st Cir. 2016)
Groome Res. Ltd. v. Par. of Jefferson, 234 F.3d 192 (5th Cir. 2000)

source_file: 4164893_2017_Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami_scotus
case_name: Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami
court: Supreme Court of the United States
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves discriminatory lending practices rather than reasonable accommodation
key_holding: The Supreme Court held that the City of Miami is an 'aggrieved person' with standing under the FHA to sue for financial injuries, but vacated and remanded on proximate cause, holding that the FHA requires a direct causal link between discriminatory conduct and injury, not merely foreseeability.
brief_summary: The City of Miami sued Bank of America and Wells Fargo alleging the banks intentionally targeted African-American and Latino borrowers with predatory lending practices, leading to concentrated foreclosures in minority neighborhoods that reduced property-tax revenue and increased municipal costs. The Supreme Court held that Miami has standing to sue as an aggrieved person under the FHA, but vacated and remanded on proximate cause, ruling that the FHA requires a direct causal connection between discriminatory conduct and injury rather than mere foreseeability.
key_cases_cited: Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Lexmark Int'l, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. ___ (2014)
Holmes v. Securities Investors Protection Corporation, 503 U.S. 258 (1992)

source_file: 4181212_2017_Andrew Ellis v. The City of Minneapolis_ca8
case_name: Andrew Ellis; Harriet A. Ellis v. The City of Minneapolis, a municipal corporation; Betsy Hodges; Nuria Rivera-Vandermyde; JoAnn Velde
accommodation_description: For-profit landlords alleging City of Minneapolis's housing code enforcement policy has a disparate impact on racial minorities seeking affordable housing
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit affirmed dismissal of disparate impact claim because plaintiffs failed to plead sufficient facts showing the City's housing code enforcement was an 'artificial, arbitrary, and unnecessary' policy causing discriminatory effects, as required by Inclusive Communities; the complaint relied on conclusory allegations and mere disagreements over code application rather than identifying a specific discriminatory policy.
brief_summary: Andrew and Harriet Ellis, for-profit low-income rental housing providers in Minneapolis, sued the City of Minneapolis and city officials alleging the City's heightened housing-code enforcement has a racially discriminatory disparate impact on protected classes seeking affordable housing under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a). The Eighth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding the Ellises failed to plead sufficient facts plausibly demonstrating that the City had adopted an 'artificial, arbitrary, and unnecessary' policy causing the alleged disparity, as required under Texas Department of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
City of Black Jack, 508 F.2d 1179 (8th Cir. 1974)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)

source_file: 4191888_2017_Goncalves Ex Rel. Goncalves v. Rady Children's Hospital San Diego_ca9
4194542_2017_Civil Rights Education _ Enforcement Center v. Hospitality Properties Trust_ca9
case_name: Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center v. Hospitality Properties Trust
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged hotels failed to provide equivalent accessible shuttle transportation services for guests with mobility disabilities, in violation of ADA Title III.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed denial of class certification, holding that plaintiffs failed to establish commonality under Rule 23(a) because HPT—a REIT that delegates hotel operations to independent contractors—did not maintain a uniform policy regarding shuttle services, and proving ADA violations at each of the 142 hotels would require individual determinations.
brief_summary: Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center (CREEC) and three mobility-disabled individuals sued Hospitality Properties Trust under ADA Title III, alleging the REIT-owned hotels failed to provide equivalent accessible transportation services. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of class certification, holding that plaintiffs failed to establish the commonality requirement under Rule 23(a) because HPT delegated hotel operations to independent contractors and maintained no uniform shuttle policy, necessitating individual determinations for each of the 142 hotels.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
Chapman v. Pier 1 Imports (U.S.) Inc., 631 F.3d 939 (9th Cir. 2011)
Pickern v. Holiday Quality Foods Inc., 293 F.3d 1133 (9th Cir. 2002)

source_file: 4197488_2017_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Travelers Indemnity Company_dcd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Travelers Indemnity Company
accommodation_description: Insurance company policy of refusing to provide habitational insurance to landlords whose tenants receive Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8), allegedly creating disparate impact on African-Americans and women.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding that NFHA had standing and sufficiently pleaded a prima facie disparate impact claim by demonstrating statistical evidence showing that Travelers' policy of denying insurance to landlords who rent to voucher recipients has a disproportionate impact on African-American residents and women-headed households in the District of Columbia.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance sued Travelers Indemnity Company alleging that the insurer's policy of refusing to provide habitational insurance to landlords who accept Housing Choice Vouchers violates the Fair Housing Act because it has a disparate impact on African-Americans and women. The court denied Travelers' motion to dismiss, finding that NFHA had organizational standing and sufficiently pleaded a causal connection between the challenged policy and disparate impact on protected classes under the heightened pleading standard from Inclusive Communities.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
NAACP v. Am. Fam. Mut. Ins. Co., 978 F.2d 287 (7th Cir. 1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Borum v. Brentwood Village, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff challenges redevelopment plan that eliminates large-family apartments, allegedly causing disparate impact based on familial status.
key_holding: The court certified a Rule 23(b)(2) class of current Brookland Manor residents in 3-5 bedroom units with minor children at risk of displacement, but denied certification for displaced residents due to failure to establish numerosity; disparate impact claims based on familial status satisfy commonality when all class members face the same injury.
brief_summary: Adriann Borum, a Brookland Manor tenant with five children, sued Brentwood Village LLC and related entities seeking to represent a class of residents facing displacement from a redevelopment plan that eliminates four- and five-bedroom apartments. The court granted class certification under Rule 23(b)(2) for current residents at risk of displacement based on familial status disparate impact, but denied certification for already-displaced residents due to lack of numerosity evidence.
key_cases_cited: Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
Amgen Inc. v. Connecticut Retirement Plans & Trust Funds, 568 U.S. 455 (2013)
Garcia v. Johanns, 444 F.3d 625 (D.C. Cir. 2006)
Amchem Products, Inc. v. Windsor, 521 U.S. 591 (1997)
Hartman v. Duffey, 19 F.3d 1459 (D.C. Cir. 1994)

case_name: Mary B. Valencia, et al. v. City of Springfield, Illinois
accommodation_description: Disabled residents of a community integrated living arrangement (CILA) sought a conditional permitted use waiver from the City's 600-foot spacing requirement between family care residences to continue occupying their home.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed the preliminary injunction, finding plaintiffs demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of success on their reasonable accommodation claim because the requested zoning waiver is reasonable and necessary to provide disabled persons with an equal opportunity to live in a residential neighborhood, and the City's financial and administrative burdens are negligible.
brief_summary: Mary B. Valencia and other disabled individuals, along with their service provider Individual Advocacy Group, sued the City of Springfield after the City threatened eviction because their family care residence was within 600 feet of an existing group home, violating the City's zoning code. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the grant of preliminary injunction, finding plaintiffs demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of success on their reasonable accommodation claim because the requested zoning waiver was reasonable and necessary to provide disabled persons with an equal opportunity to live in a residential community, and the City failed to demonstrate undue financial or administrative burdens.
key_cases_cited: Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Wisconsin Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)
Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)
Larkin v. Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)

case_name: Parviz Karim-Panahi v. 4000 Massachusetts Apartments et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlords and credit agencies conspired to deny him rental housing at multiple D.C. apartment complexes based on his race, national origin, religion, color, and age as a Section 8 voucher holder.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state plausible claims of discrimination because plaintiff alleged no facts establishing that defendants acted with discriminatory purpose beyond conclusory assertions, and landlords were permitted under federal law to screen prospective tenants based on creditworthiness.
brief_summary: An Iranian pro se plaintiff sued over twenty defendants including landlords, property management companies, and credit bureaus alleging a vast conspiracy to discriminate against him as a Section 8 voucher holder based on race, national origin, religion, and age when his rental applications were denied. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to comply with Rule 8(a) pleading standards and contained only conclusory allegations of discriminatory purpose without factual support to state plausible Fair Housing Act claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 4271090_2018_Barry Farm Tenants and Allies Association, Inc. v. District of Columbia Housing Authority_dcd
case_name: Barry Farm Tenants and Allies Association, Inc. v. District of Columbia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Residents allege defendants' redevelopment plan reduces larger family units and fails to guarantee appropriately-sized replacement housing for displaced families with children.
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding (1) the familial status disparate impact claims were not ripe because the unit mix had not been finalized at the first-stage PUD stage, (2) the constructive demolition claim under the USHA failed because Section 1437p did not create an enforceable federal right enforceable through Section 1983, and (3) the court declined supplemental jurisdiction over the DCHRA place of residence claim.
brief_summary: Barry Farm public housing tenants and advocacy organizations sued the D.C. Housing Authority and private developers alleging that the approved redevelopment plan to replace 444 public housing units with 1,400 mixed-income units would have a disparate impact on families with children by reducing larger family-sized units, and that DCHA failed to maintain the property. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss all claims, finding the familial status claims unripe because the specific unit mix remained undetermined at the first-stage PUD stage, and that the constructive demolition claim under the U.S. Housing Act failed because Section 1437p did not create enforceable federal rights.
key_cases_cited: Gonzaga University v. Doe, 536 U.S. 273 (2002)
Blessing v. Freestone, 520 U.S. 329 (1997)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Anderson v. Jackson, 556 F.3d 351 (5th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 4274256_2018_Collington v. District of Columbia Housing Authority_dcd
case_name: William Hugh Collington v. District of Columbia Housing Authority et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendants failed to comply with HUD pre-inspection guidelines resulting in leasing a substandard apartment, but does not request a specific accommodation.
key_holding: The complaint against HUD is dismissed for lack of standing since HUD does not administer Section 8 programs locally, and the FHA claim fails because the complaint does not allege discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin as required under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604, 3605, 3606, or 3617.
brief_summary: William Hugh Collington, a District of Columbia resident participating in the Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher Program, sued the District of Columbia Housing Authority and HUD alleging that defendants failed to comply with pre-inspection guidelines, resulting in his leasing of a substandard apartment. The court dismissed the claims against HUD for lack of standing and dismissed the Fair Housing Act claim for failure to state a claim because the complaint did not allege discrimination based on any protected class covered by the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Kokkonen v. Guardian Life Ins. Co. of Am., 511 U.S. 375 (1994)
Honig v. Doe, 484 U.S. 305 (1988)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Haase v. Sessions, 835 F.2d 902 (D.C. Cir. 1987)
Robbins v. United States Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 72 F. Supp. 3d 1 (D.D.C. 2014)

source_file: 4278555_2018_Holly Geraci v. Union Square Condominium Assoc_ca7
case_name: Holly B. Geraci v. Union Square Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Request to keep her dog as a reasonable accommodation for her PTSD diagnosis
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed the jury verdict for Union Square, finding no triable issue of fact as to whether the HOA's litigation updates and open forum constituted retaliatory conduct that would coerce, intimidate, threaten, or interfere with a person of reasonable fortitude, and upholding the district court's admission of expert testimony challenging whether Geraci had a qualifying handicap.
brief_summary: Holly Geraci sued Union Square Condominium Association alleging the HOA failed to accommodate her PTSD and retaliated against her for filing the lawsuit by publishing litigation updates and holding an open forum about the case. The jury returned a verdict for the HOA, and the Seventh Circuit affirmed, finding no evidence that the HOA's communications constituted retaliatory conduct and that the district court properly allowed expert testimony challenging whether Geraci had a qualifying handicap.
key_cases_cited: Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)
Stuhlmacher v. Home Depot U.S.A., Inc., 774 F.3d 405 (7th Cir. 2014)
Schneiker v. Fortis Ins. Co., 200 F.3d 1055 (7th Cir. 2000)
Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)
Good Sheperd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 4303545_2018_Traci Berardelli v. Allied Services Institute of R_ca3

case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Carson
accommodation_description: This case does not involve an individual reasonable accommodation request; it is an APA challenge to HUD's withdrawal of the AFFH Assessment Tool, which was administrative guidance for local governments conducting fair housing planning.
key_holding: The court granted HUD's motion to dismiss because plaintiffs lacked Article III standing—they failed to establish concrete injury sufficient to challenge HUD's withdrawal of the AFFH Assessment Tool and reversion to the prior Analysis of Impediments process.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance and other fair housing organizations sued HUD and Secretary Carson challenging the withdrawal of the AFFH Assessment Tool and HUD's reversion to the prior Analysis of Impediments process as arbitrary and capricious under the APA. The court granted HUD's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs lacked Article III standing because they failed to demonstrate concrete, particularized injury resulting from HUD's administrative action.
key_cases_cited: NAACP v. Sec'y of Hous. & Urban Dev., 817 F.2d 149 (1st Cir. 1987)
Shannon v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 436 F.2d 809 (3d Cir. 1970)
Heckler v. Chaney, 470 U.S. 821 (1985)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)

source_file: 4307711_2018_Marsha Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Communi_ca7
case_name: Marsha Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Community, LLC
accommodation_description: Not applicable; this case concerns landlord liability for tenant-on-tenant harassment based on sexual orientation and retaliation for complaining about such harassment, not a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: A landlord with actual notice of tenant-on-tenant harassment based on a protected characteristic who chooses not to take reasonable steps within its control to stop that harassment may be held directly liable under the Fair Housing Act for creating a hostile housing environment.
brief_summary: Marsha Wetzel, a lesbian resident of Glen St. Andrew senior living community, sued the management defendants alleging they failed to protect her from severe and pervasive verbal and physical harassment by other residents based on her sexual orientation, and retaliated against her for complaining about the harassment. The Seventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that a landlord may be held directly liable under the FHA when it has actual knowledge of harassment based on a protected characteristic and is deliberately indifferent to it.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz
Davis v. Monroe Cnty. Bd. of Educ.
DiCenso v. Cisneros
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.

case_name: Darryl O. Mason v. Adams County Recorder, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, an African-American, sought injunctive relief compelling Ohio county recorders to stop printing, publishing, or permitting public access to historical documents containing racially restrictive covenants under § 3604(c).
key_holding: Mason lacked Article III standing because his alleged injury was not particularized to him—he failed to allege any concrete, personal economic harm or that any racially restrictive covenant affected him individually—and his generalized grievance about government record-keeping practices cannot support standing.
brief_summary: Mason, an African-American Ohio resident, sued all 88 Ohio county recorders alleging they violate § 3604(c) by maintaining and providing public access to historical land records containing racially restrictive covenants, which he claims discourage Black buyers from purchasing property. The Sixth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Mason lacked Article III standing because he failed to allege any particularized injury distinct from any other Ohio resident, and his claim presented an undifferentiated generalized grievance about government conduct.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Shelley v. Kraemer, 334 U.S. 1 (1948)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

source_file: 4308092_2018_Providence Behavioral Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist._ca5
case_name: Providence Behavioral Health v. Grant Road Public Utility District
accommodation_description: Request for water, drainage, and septic utility services for a psychiatric facility serving minors with mental health disabilities, framed as a reasonable accommodation under the FHA and ADA.
key_holding: Providing water, drainage, and septic services is not a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because the need for utilities is not unique to a psychiatric facility—it exists regardless of whether disabled individuals occupy the building—and failing to provide utilities does not create unequal ability to use and enjoy a dwelling compared to non-disabled individuals.
brief_summary: Providence Behavioral Health and Texas Providence Investments sued Grant Road Public Utility District after it denied annexation for a psychiatric facility serving minors with mental health disabilities, alleging the denial of water, drainage, and septic services violated the FHA and ADA. The Fifth Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment for Grant Road, finding that Providence failed to prove intentional discrimination and that providing utilities did not constitute a reasonable accommodation because the need for utilities exists regardless of disability and does not create unequal access.
key_cases_cited: Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Doe v. Columbia-Brazoria Indep. Sch. Dist., 855 F.3d 681 (5th Cir. 2017)
Jin Choi v. Univ. of Tex. Health Sci. Ctr. of San Antonio, 633 F. App'x 214 (5th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Darryl O. Mason v. Adams County Recorder, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks to compel Ohio county recorders to stop printing and publishing historical documents containing racially restrictive covenants, to remove such records from public view, and to permit inspection and redaction of such documents.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed dismissal for lack of standing because Mason failed to allege a particularized injury—his complaint contained no allegations that he intended to buy or rent property, and his generalized grievance about government maintenance of historical records with restrictive covenants was insufficient to establish Article III standing.
brief_summary: Darryl Mason, an African-American Ohio resident, sued all 88 Ohio county recorders seeking injunctive relief to stop them from maintaining and making publicly available historical land records containing racially restrictive covenants, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act's prohibition on discriminatory statements. The Sixth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, holding that Mason lacked Article III standing because he failed to allege any particularized injury—he never claimed to have suffered actual or threatened harm from encountering these historical covenants when seeking to purchase or rent property.
key_cases_cited: Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Shelley v. Kraemer, 334 U.S. 1 (1948)

case_name: Carol Vorchheimer v. The Philadelphian Owners Association; June Idzal; Frank J. Bonom
accommodation_description: Request to leave a rolling walker in the lobby common area instead of requiring staff to retrieve it from storage upon her return
key_holding: A requested accommodation is not 'necessary' under § 3604(f)(3)(B) if the landlord offers alternative accommodations that afford the disabled person equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling; the statute requires accommodations that are essential, not merely preferable.
brief_summary: Carol Vorchheimer, a mobility-disabled condominium owner, sued The Philadelphian Owners Association and its managers for refusing to let her leave her rolling walker in the lobby, claiming a failure to accommodate under the Fair Housing Act. The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Vorchheimer did not plausibly plead necessity because the building had offered four alternative accommodations that equally satisfied her medical need for ready access to her walker without requiring her to stand unsupported.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Township of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)

case_name: Carol Vorchheimer v. The Philadelphian Owners Association; June Idzal; Frank J. Bonom
accommodation_description: Disabled tenant requested permission to store her rolling walker in the building lobby to independently retrieve it upon returning home.
key_holding: Under § 3604(f)(3)(B), an accommodation is 'necessary' only if it is required to achieve equal housing opportunity; when a landlord offers alternatives that satisfy the disabled person's disability-related needs, the tenant cannot demand her preferred accommodation.
brief_summary: Carol Vorchheimer, a disabled condominium owner at The Philadelphian, sued the Owners Association under the Fair Housing Amendments Act, claiming the building's refusal to let her store her rolling walker in the lobby violated § 3604(f)(3)(B). The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Vorchheimer failed to plausibly plead that her preferred accommodation was 'necessary' because the four alternatives offered by the building—staff retrieval, delivery to her car, doorman loading, or valet parking—all satisfied her stated disability-related needs for ready walker access without unsupported standing.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Wisconsin Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 4313320_2018_Tree of Life Christian Sch. v. City of Upper Arlington, Ohio_ca6

case_name: Lindsey Jones v. CitiMortgage, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff raised a Fair Housing Act claim (Count Three) under 42 U.S.C. § 3604, but the claim was abandoned on appeal and the court did not substantively analyze any reasonable accommodation or modification request; the complaint alleged the foreclosure-related actions were discriminatory but did not articulate a specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal of Jones's complaint in all respects except Count Five (bankruptcy discharge injunction), which was vacated and remanded because the district court lacked jurisdiction to enforce a discharge injunction issued by the bankruptcy court; the court also held that Jones's Fair Housing Act claim was abandoned on appeal and failed because Jones did not allege unequal treatment affecting housing availability.
brief_summary: Lindsey Jones, proceeding pro se, appealed the dismissal of his lawsuit challenging a second foreclosure by CitiMortgage and related defendants, raising claims under the Fair Housing Act, bankruptcy discharge injunction, and constitutional provisions. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of all claims except Count Five (bankruptcy discharge injunction), which was vacated and remanded because the district court lacked jurisdiction to enforce an injunction issued by the bankruptcy court, while noting Jones abandoned his FHA claim on appeal.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Alderwoods Group, Inc. v. Garcia
In re McLean

source_file: 4330677_2016_Hardaway v. District of Columbia Housing Authority_cadc
case_name: Hardaway v. District of Columbia Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Angelene Hardaway requested approval for her sister Lena to serve as her live-in aide to provide disability-related care under the Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher Program.
key_holding: The D.C. Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal for lack of standing and mootness, finding that Angelene Hardaway established injury-in-fact by demonstrating the Authority's revocation of her live-in aide approval stripped her of a legal entitlement to a two-bedroom voucher, and that her claims remain live because the Authority's subsequent retention of the voucher was merely administrative grace subject to revocation.
brief_summary: Angelene Hardaway, a disabled Section 8 voucher holder, sued the District of Columbia Housing Authority after it revoked approval of her sister Lena as a live-in aide, threatening her legal entitlement to a two-bedroom voucher and round-the-clock care. The D.C. Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal on standing and mootness grounds, holding that Angelene demonstrated cognizable injury by losing a statutory entitlement and that her claims remain live because the Authority's permissive retention of the voucher was an act of administrative grace rather than a binding determination.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Friends of the Earth, Inc. v. Laidlaw Environmental Services (TOC), 528 U.S. 167 (2000)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
United States v. Hubbard, 650 F.2d 293 (D.C. Cir. 1980)
EEOC v. National Children's Center, Inc., 98 F.3d 1406 (D.C. Cir. 1996)

case_name: Robyn G. Edwards; Mikki Adams v. Gene Salter Properties and Salter Construction Inc.; Brittany Pringle
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that defendants accept alternative documentation (proof of government benefits, retirement benefits, and rental income) to verify income qualification instead of requiring standard documentation (pay stubs, offer letter, or tax returns).
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit held that plaintiffs sufficiently alleged a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) by alleging defendants refused to make a reasonable exception to their income documentation policy, which may be necessary to afford plaintiffs equal opportunity to rent, regardless of whether plaintiffs' inability to provide standard documentation was caused by their disability.
brief_summary: Robyn Edwards and Mikki Adams attempted to rent an apartment from Gene Salter Properties but were denied when the landlord refused to accept alternative documentation of their income sources (government disability benefits, retirement benefits, and rental income) and insisted on standard documentation. The Eighth Circuit vacated the dismissal of plaintiffs' reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), holding that plaintiffs adequately alleged defendants failed to make a reasonable accommodation in their income-verification policy necessary to afford plaintiffs equal opportunity to rent.
key_cases_cited: Peebles v. Potter, 354 F.3d 761 (8th Cir. 2004)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
US Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)

case_name: Robyn G. Edwards; Mikki Adams v. Gene Salter Properties and Salter Construction Inc.; Brittany Pringle
accommodation_description: Request to provide alternative income documentation (government benefits, retirement benefits, rental income) in lieu of pay stubs, offer letters, or tax returns to qualify for rental housing
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit held that the district court erred in dismissing plaintiffs' reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because the inability to comply with the income documentation policy could potentially be a disability-related limitation warranting accommodation, even if not caused by the disability.
brief_summary: Robyn Edwards and Mikki Adams sued Gene Salter Properties after being told they could only qualify for an apartment by providing pay stubs, offer letters, or tax returns, despite having only disability benefits, retirement income, and rental income as sources. The Eighth Circuit affirmed dismissal of plaintiffs' disparate treatment and disparate impact claims but vacated dismissal of their reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), holding that plaintiffs sufficiently alleged defendants failed to accommodate their disability-related need to use alternative income documentation.
key_cases_cited: US Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Peebles v. Potter, 354 F.3d 761 (8th Cir. 2004)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)

source_file: 4343397_2018_Angela Riley v. City of Kokomo, Indiana, Housi_ca7
case_name: Angela Riley v. City of Kokomo, Indiana Housing Authority
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was the subject of the FHA claim; the claim involved alleged retaliation for reporting to HUD what plaintiff perceived as procedural violations by her employer.
key_holding: Riley's FHA retaliation claim failed because she presented no evidence that she called HUD to report a discriminatory housing practice—she admitted she called to report her CEO's alleged fraud—and she conceded she did not know whether anyone was treated differently because of a protected characteristic.
brief_summary: Angela Riley, a former employee of the Kokomo Housing Authority who also resided in KHA-assisted housing, sued claiming the housing authority terminated her employment in retaliation for requesting FMLA leave and ADA accommodations, and later took adverse housing actions against her as a tenant. The Seventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for KHA on all claims, finding (1) the ADA reasonable accommodation claim was outside the scope of her EEOC charge, (2) no evidence of discriminatory or retaliatory intent, and (3) no protected activity under the FHA because Riley admitted she called HUD to report alleged fraud by her CEO, not any discriminatory housing practice.
key_cases_cited: Green v. Nat'l Steel Corp., 197 F.3d 894 (7th Cir. 1999)
East-Miller v. Lake Cty. Highway Dep't, 421 F.3d 558 (7th Cir. 2005)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
White v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 475 F.3d 898 (7th Cir. 2007)
Lord v. High Voltage Software, Inc., 839 F.3d 556 (7th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the claim concerns landlord liability for failing to address tenant-on-tenant racial harassment.
key_holding: A landlord may be liable under § 3604(b) and § 3617 of the FHA for failing to take prompt action to address a racially hostile housing environment created by one tenant targeting another, where the landlord knew of the discriminatory conduct and had the power to correct it.
brief_summary: Donahue Francis, a Black tenant with a Section 8 voucher, sued Kings Park Manor and its property manager alleging they violated the FHA by failing to address repeated racial harassment by a neighboring tenant who called Francis racial slurs and made death threats over several months. The Second Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal, holding that landlords may be liable under §§ 3604(b) and 3617 for post-acquisition hostile housing environment claims when they knew of tenant-on-tenant racial harassment and had the power to address it but failed to act.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Neudecker v. Boisclair Corp., 351 F.3d 361 (8th Cir. 2003)

source_file: 4375755_2017_M.R.G. v. Marsh Harbour Maintenance Association, Inc._ca11
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenged HOA policies (Curfew Rule, Loitering Rule, Proper Attire Rule, Report Card Requirement) alleged to discriminate against families with children under familial status discrimination theory, not disability accommodation.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of plaintiffs' motion for a new trial, finding no reversible error in the jury instructions or verdict form despite plaintiffs' contention that the court improperly allowed the jury to decide liability issues that had been partially resolved at summary judgment.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center and individual residents sued condominium homeowner associations alleging that four policies (Curfew Rule, Loitering Rule, Proper Attire Rule, Report Card Requirement) discriminated against families with children in violation of 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604(b) and (c). After a seven-day jury trial resulted in a verdict for defendants, the Eleventh Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of plaintiffs' motion for a new trial, finding no prejudicial error in the jury instructions or verdict form.
key_cases_cited: City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 800 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 2015)
Gu<|reserved_200419|>|zman v. City of Chicago, 689 F.3d 740 (7th Cir. 2012)
Ragin v. N.Y. Times Co., 923 F.2d 995 (2d Cir. 1991)

case_name: Constante P. Barot et al. v. Aldon Management
citation: No official citation (unpublished order, Case No. 18-140 (TJK))
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiffs allege disparate treatment in housing based on national origin (Filipino) and age, claiming they received inferior housing compared to American counterparts.
key_holding: Plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim for disparate treatment based on national origin survived dismissal because their allegations—that Aldon provided them smaller, inferior housing units on account of their Filipino national origin while American counterparts received larger, standard two-bedroom apartments—were sufficient to create a plausible inference of discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Pro se tenants Constante and Dolores Barot sued their employer-landlord Aldon Management for housing discrimination based on national origin and age, alleging they received inferior housing (an illegally constructed second bedroom, higher rent) compared to American counterparts who received standard two-bedroom apartments. The court denied Aldon's motion to dismiss as to the Fair Housing Act national origin discrimination claim, finding plaintiffs' allegations sufficient to plausibly infer discriminatory intent, but dismissed several D.C. law claims and the age discrimination claim under the ADEA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
2922 Sherman Ave. Tenants' Ass'n v. District of Columbia, 444 F.3d 673 (D.C. Cir. 2006)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 4385539_2019_Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co._ca5
case_name: Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Property Co.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves landlord's 'no vouchers' policy challenged as discriminatory based on race rather than any disability accommodation.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal of FHA claims against landlords who refuse to participate in the Section 8 voucher program, concluding that even assuming disparate impact from the 'no vouchers' policy on Black renters, the complaint failed to adequately plead that the policy was the cause of the pre-existing racial disparity in voucher distribution.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project, a fair housing organization, sued Lincoln Property Company and apartment owners alleging their blanket 'no vouchers' policy disparately impacts Black renters in the Dallas metro area where over 80% of Section 8 voucher holders are Black. The Fifth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, holding that even accepting the statistical allegations, ICP failed to adequately plead robust causation between the 'no vouchers' policy and the pre-existing racial disparity in voucher distribution.
key_cases_cited: Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Huntington Branch, NAACP v. Town of Huntington, 844 F.2d 926 (2d Cir. 1988)
Knapp v. Eagle Prop. Mgmt. Corp., 54 F.3d 1272 (7th Cir. 1995)

case_name: Barbara Moody v. Harry Gongloff, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to transfer from a two-story house to a single-story house due to inability to climb stairs as a disability accommodation
key_holding: The district court did not abuse its discretion in refusing Moody's proposed jury instruction on unreasonable delay because Moody herself caused much of the 87-day delay by failing to respond to phone calls and letters and missing appointments, and the evidence did not support a finding that the housing authority's delay was unreasonable.
