brief_summary: Barbara Moody, a Section 8 voucher recipient, sued the Lorain Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging it failed to reasonably accommodate her disability by delaying approval of her request to transfer from a two-story house to a single-story house for 87 days. After a jury returned a verdict for the housing authority, Moody appealed the rejection of her proposed jury instruction on unreasonable delay. The Sixth Circuit affirmed, finding no abuse of discretion because Moody caused much of the delay herself through missed appointments and failure to respond to communications.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Tuttle v. Metro. Gov't of Nashville, 474 F.3d 307 (6th Cir. 2007)

source_file: 4387735_2017_Victoria Laney v. Thomas Ray Slaten, Jr._ca11
case_name: Victoria Laney v. Thomas Ray Slaten, Jr.
accommodation_description: Laney vaguely alleged the defendants perceived or regarded her as emotionally or mentally impaired, but did not specify what accommodation, if any, she requested or was denied.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal because Laney's FHA complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing how the defendants' conduct impacted her housing accessibility or how they refused reasonable modifications for handicapped persons, merely asserting she was perceived as mentally ill.
brief_summary: Victoria Laney, a homeowner in The Hammocks HOA community in Ocoee, Florida, appealed the dismissal of her Fair Housing Act claims against the HOA, property manager, and board president, alleging they discriminated against her by publicly disclosing her debt and regarding her as mentally ill. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal, finding Laney failed to plead sufficient factual content to state a plausible FHA claim because she did not allege how the conduct impacted her housing accessibility or how defendants refused reasonable accommodations for handicapped persons.

source_file: 4389389_2019_Marie Curto v. Country Place Condominium Assn_ca3
case_name: Marie Curto v. A Country Place Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenged a condominium association's sex-segregated pool schedule that allocated substantially more favorable evening swimming hours to men than women, with plaintiff Diana Lusardi needing pool therapy for stroke-related disabilities.
key_holding: The pool schedule's allocation of substantially more evening swimming hours to men (16.5 hours after 5 p.m.) compared to women (3.5 hours) constitutes facial discrimination against women in violation of the FHA's prohibition on sex discrimination in the terms, conditions, or privileges of dwelling, even though the schedule provided roughly equal aggregate swimming time to each gender.
brief_summary: Marie Curto and Steve and Diana Lusardi sued A Country Place Condominium Association over its sex-segregated pool schedule, which allocated significantly more favorable evening swimming hours to men (16.5 hours after 5 p.m.) than women (3.5 hours). The Third Circuit reversed the District Court's summary judgment for the Association, finding the schedule facially discriminated against women in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b), and remanded for entry of summary judgment for the plaintiffs.
key_cases_cited: Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515 (1996)
Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967)
Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)

source_file: 4393705_2019_City of Miami v. Wells Fargo _ Co._ca11
case_name: City of Miami v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: Discriminatory mortgage lending practices (redlining and reverse redlining) allegedly targeted Black and Latino Miami residents for predatory loans
key_holding: The City adequately pled proximate cause for its tax-base injury because discriminatory lending practices bear a direct relation to reduced property values and tax revenue, but failed to adequately plead proximate cause for increased municipal expenditures because the causal chain was too attenuated
brief_summary: The City of Miami sued Wells Fargo and Bank of America alleging the banks engaged in discriminatory mortgage lending practices (redlining and reverse redlining) that intentionally targeted Black and Latino residents, resulting in disproportionate foreclosures, diminished property values, and reduced tax revenue for the City. The Eleventh Circuit reversed the dismissal, holding that Miami adequately pled proximate cause for its tax-base injury because the connection between discriminatory lending practices and reduced property values was direct and logical when considered in the aggregate, but affirmed that the City failed to plead proximate cause for increased municipal expenditures because the causal chain was too attenuated.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Holmes v. Securities Investor Protection Corp., 503 U.S. 258 (1992)
Lexmark International, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014)

source_file: 4402335_2017_Affordable Recovery Housing v. City of Blue Island_ca7
case_name: Affordable Recovery Housing v. City of Blue Island
accommodation_description: Affordable Recovery Housing operates a faith-based residential recovery home for men with drug/alcohol addiction and sought to operate in a single-family residential zone without installing a sprinkler system.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed that Blue Island's fire safety code requiring sprinklers did not substantially burden Affordable's religious exercise under RFRA because the expulsion resulted from Affordable's own disregard of applicable regulations, not from religious discrimination.
brief_summary: Affordable Recovery Housing, a faith-based operator of a residential recovery home for men with substance use disorders, sued the City of Blue Island and its fire chief after being forced to evict residents due to the city's requirement that it install a sprinkler system. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment for the defendants, finding that Affordable's Religious Freedom Restoration Act and RLUIPA claims failed because the city acted on legitimate fire safety concerns rather than religious animus, and Affordable's own failure to research applicable regulations caused the hardship rather than government burden on religious exercise.

source_file: 4403305_2019_Paula Casillas v. Madison Avenue Associates, Inc_ca7

case_name: Borum v. Brentwood Village, LLC
accommodation_description: This is a class action challenging a redevelopment plan that would eliminate three-, four-, and five-bedroom apartments, allegedly causing disparate impact on families with children and discriminatory statements by defendants.
key_holding: The court found that proposed substitute representative Moore brings typical and adequate claims for the class's disparate impact discrimination claims but not for the discriminatory statements claims, and accordingly decertified the class as to discriminatory statements claims while granting substitution as to disparate impact claims.
brief_summary: Brookland Manor tenants and One D.C. sued Brentwood Village LLC, Mid-City Financial Corporation, and Edgewood Management Corporation alleging that the planned redevelopment reducing family-sized apartments would have a disparate impact on families with children in violation of the FHA. The court granted in part plaintiffs' motion to substitute Marita Moore as class representative, finding her claims typical and adequate for disparate impact claims, but decertified the class as to discriminatory statements claims because Moore lacked standing to bring those claims.
key_cases_cited: Borum v. Brentwood Vill., LLC (Borum I), 218 F. Supp. 3d 1
Borum v. Brentwood Vill., LLC (Borum II), 324 F.R.D. 1
Borum v. Brentwood Vill., LLC (Borum III), 329 F.R.D. 90
DL v. District of Columbia, 312 F.R.D. 1
Reynolds v. Sheet Metal Workers, Local 102, 702 F.2d 221

source_file: 4412459_2017_Oregon Bureau of Labor _ Industries Ex Rel. Fair Housing Council of Oregon v. Chandler Apartments, LLC_ca9
case_name: Oregon Bureau of Labor and Industries, ex rel Fair Housing Council of Oregon v. Chandler Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Prospective tenants requested to keep service dogs or therapy dogs as reasonable accommodations to the property's no-pet policy.
key_holding: Defendants violated the FHA by categorically denying testers' accommodation requests for service animals without engaging in the interactive process to determine whether the requested accommodations were reasonable and reasonably related to the claimed handicap.
brief_summary: BOLI and FHCO sued Chandler Apartments for housing discrimination based on denials of reasonable accommodation requests for service animals made by testers posing as prospective tenants. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for plaintiffs, holding that defendants violated the FHA by refusing to grant the requested accommodations without engaging in the interactive process to evaluate whether the requests were reasonable and necessary.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Fair Hous. Council of San Fernando Valley v. Roommate.com, LLC, 666 F.3d 1216 (9th Cir. 2012)
Consumer Fin. Prot. Bureau v. Gordon, 819 F.3d 1179 (9th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 4417289_2017_Civil Rights Education _ Enforcement Center v. Hospitality Properties Trust_ca9
case_name: Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center v. Hospitality Properties Trust
accommodation_description: Wheelchair users requested equivalent accessible shuttle transportation services at hotels owned by defendant REIT, which delegates hotel operations to independent management companies.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed denial of class certification, holding that plaintiffs had Article III standing under the ADA's deterrent effect doctrine but failed to establish commonality because HPT did not maintain a uniform policy regarding shuttle services, requiring 142 individualized determinations of whether each hotel violated the ADA.
brief_summary: The Civil Rights Education and Enforcement Center and three wheelchair-using plaintiffs sued Hospitality Properties Trust (a REIT owning hotels across the U.S.) under ADA Title III, alleging hotels failed to provide equivalent shuttle transportation services for mobility-impaired guests. The Ninth Circuit affirmed that plaintiffs established Article III standing under the deterrent effect doctrine, but upheld denial of class certification because HPT's delegation of hotel operations to independent contractors meant there was no common policy to challenge, requiring individualized determinations for each hotel.
key_cases_cited: Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
Chapman v. Pier 1 Imports (U.S.) Inc., 631 F.3d 939 (9th Cir. 2011)
Pickern v. Holiday Quality Foods Inc., 293 F.3d 1133 (9th Cir. 2002)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Kirschenbaum v. Assa Corp.
accommodation_description: Not an FHA case — this is an appeal concerning attachment and execution of Iranian corporate assets to satisfy terrorism judgments under FSIA and TRIA.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed that Assa Corporation, as Iran's alter ego and agency/instrumentality, is subject to the district court's jurisdiction and its property is subject to attachment and execution under both the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act and the Terrorism Risk Insurance Act.
brief_summary: Hundreds of terrorism victims holding default judgments against Iran sought to attach assets of Assa Corporation (a New York corporation owned by Bank Melli, ultimately owned by the Government of Iran) under the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act and Terrorism Risk Insurance Act. The Second Circuit affirmed, holding that Assa is Iran's alter ego as a matter of law and is therefore subject to the district court's jurisdiction, and that Assa's property constitutes blocked assets subject to attachment and execution under both statutes.
key_cases_cited: Kirschenbaum v. 650 Fifth Ave. & Related Props., 830 F.3d 107 (2d Cir. 2016)
Rubin v. Islamic Republic of Iran, 138 S. Ct. 816 (2018)
First National City Bank v. Banco Para El Comercio Exterior de Cuba (Bancec), 462 U.S. 611 (1983)
EM Ltd. v. Banco Central de la Republica Argentina, 800 F.3d 78 (2d Cir. 2015)

case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Carson
accommodation_description: This case does not involve an individual reasonable accommodation request; rather, fair housing organizations challenge HUD's withdrawal of the Assessment Tool for the Affirmatively Further Fair Housing (AFFH) Rule as arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' Rule 59(e) motion to alter the judgment, finding no clear error in the prior determination that plaintiffs lacked organizational standing because HUD's withdrawal of the Assessment Tool did not perceptibly impair their mission-driven activities given the AFFH Rule's remaining components.
brief_summary: Fair housing organizations sued HUD challenging the agency's withdrawal of the Assessment Tool used for the Affirmatively Further Fair Housing rule as arbitrary and capricious under the APA; the court had previously dismissed the case for lack of organizational standing and denied plaintiffs' motion to alter that judgment, finding that the withdrawal did not cause cognizable injury to plaintiffs' mission-driven activities.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Action Alliance of Senior Citizens of Greater Philadelphia v. Heckler, 789 F.2d 931 (D.C. Cir. 1986)
People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals v. U.S. Department of Agriculture, 797 F.3d 1087 (D.C. Cir. 2015)
Exxon Shipping Co. v. Baker, 554 U.S. 471 (2008)
Firestone v. Firestone, 76 F.3d 1205 (D.C. Cir. 1996)

source_file: 4439165_2019_Albert Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc._ca11
case_name: Albert Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request that Habitat count food stamps and familial support as income toward its minimum-income requirement, given that Schaw's quadriplegia prevents him from working.
key_holding: The court held that the district court improperly granted summary judgment on the reasonable accommodation claim because it applied an overly restrictive 'necessity' test that divorced Schaw's financial inability to meet the income threshold from his disability-related inability to work; the accommodation request was facially reasonable and raised genuine issues of material fact as to whether it was necessary to afford Schaw equal opportunity to obtain housing.
brief_summary: Albert Schaw, a quadriplegic, sued Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County under the Fair Housing Amendments Act after being denied a home because his Social Security Disability Insurance income ($9,336/year) fell below Habitat's minimum-income threshold ($10,170/year); Schaw requested that Habitat count his food stamps and familial support as income toward the requirement, arguing this was necessary because his disability prevents him from working. The Eleventh Circuit vacated the district court's grant of summary judgment for Habitat on the failure-to-accommodate claim, holding that the district court improperly required a direct causal link between the accommodation and the disability rather than considering whether the accommodation addresses an effect of the disability (inability to work), while affirming dismissal of Schaw's disparate-impact claim for lack of evidence.
key_cases_cited: U.S. Airways v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Giebeler v. M&B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)

case_name: Jeffrey D. Summers and Jeffrey's House Inc. v. City of Fitchburg et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested an exemption from Massachusetts's state sprinkler law (Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 148, § 26H) that requires sprinkler systems in boarding houses with six or more unrelated residents.
key_holding: The requested exemption from the sprinkler requirement was not reasonable because the plaintiffs failed to show that the financial and practical burdens of compliance outweigh the substantial public safety interests that sprinkler laws serve, and the City engaged in good-faith efforts to work with the plaintiffs toward compliance.
brief_summary: Jeffrey D. Summers and Jeffrey's House, Inc., a nonprofit operating four sober houses for recovering addicts, sued the City of Fitchburg, Massachusetts after the City enforced a state sprinkler law requiring installation of sprinkler systems in boarding houses with six or more unrelated residents. The First Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding that the requested exemption from the sprinkler requirement was not a reasonable accommodation because the plaintiffs failed to demonstrate that the financial burden of compliance was disproportionate to the substantial public safety benefits of sprinklers.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs, Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Nunes v. Mass. Dep't of Corr., 766 F.3d 136 (1st Cir. 2014)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 4445700_2019_Georgia State Conference of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, Georgia_ca11
case_name: Georgia State Conference of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, Georgia
accommodation_description: Civil rights organizations and individual plaintiffs challenge City of LaGrange's utility policies (court debt collection and photo identification requirements) as having disparate impact on Black and Hispanic residents in accessing housing-related utility services.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit held that § 3604(b) of the FHA, which prohibits discrimination in the provision of services in connection with the sale or rental of a dwelling, unambiguously reaches post-acquisition conduct and applies to municipal utility services (water, gas, electricity) that are essential to housing habitability, vacating the district court's dismissal and remanding for further proceedings on the disparate impact claim.
brief_summary: The Georgia State Conference of the NAACP, Troup County NAACP, Project South, and seven individual plaintiffs sued the City of LaGrange, Georgia, challenging two municipal utility policies (requiring payment of court debts and presenting state/federal photo ID to obtain or maintain utility services) as having discriminatory disparate impact on Black and Hispanic residents who are disproportionately affected by these policies. The Eleventh Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal, holding that § 3604(b) unambiguously applies to post-acquisition conduct and that municipal water, gas, and electricity services—essential to housing habitability—fall within the scope of services covered by the FHA, and remanded for the district court to determine whether the plaintiffs stated a viable disparate impact claim.
key_cases_cited: City of Miami v. Wells Fargo & Co., 923 F.3d 1260 (11th Cir. 2019)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Comm. Concerning Cmty. Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)
Woodard v. Fanboy, L.L.C., 298 F.3d 1261 (11th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Virginia Morgan v. Carrington Mortgage Services; Bank of America, N.A.
citation: Order and Judgment, No. 17-7014 (10th Cir. Dec. 12, 2017)
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged that Carrington discriminated and retaliated against her by failing to review her March 16, 2015 loss-mitigation application after HUD notified Bank of America of her disability discrimination complaint.
key_holding: The Tenth Circuit affirmed dismissal of Morgan's FHA claim because even assuming Bank of America notified Carrington of the HUD complaint, it was implausible that Carrington declined to review her loss-mitigation application because of her disability or protected activity, given that the loan had been foreclosed eighteen months earlier, the property had already been sold, and the foreclosure judgment had been affirmed on appeal.
brief_summary: Virginia Morgan appealed from the dismissal of her claims that Carrington Mortgage Services and Bank of America violated the FHA, RESPA, and FDCPA after foreclosing on her home. Morgan alleged the defendants discriminated and retaliated against her by failing to review her post-foreclosure loss-mitigation application after HUD notified them of her disability discrimination complaint. The Tenth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Morgan failed to plausibly allege causation because by the time she submitted the March 2015 application, the loan had been foreclosed for eighteen months, the property had been sold, and the foreclosure judgment had been affirmed on appeal.
key_cases_cited: Wilson v. Warren Cty., 830 F.3d 464 (7th Cir. 2016)
Toone v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 716 F.3d 516 (10th Cir. 2013)
Khalik v. United Air Lines, 671 F.3d 1188 (10th Cir. 2012)
Lage v. Ocwen Loan Servicing LLC, 839 F.3d 1003 (11th Cir. 2016)
Marx v. Gen. Revenue Corp., 668 F.3d 1174 (10th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 4454484_2019_Daimeon Mosley v. Kohl's Dep't Stores, Inc._ca6
case_name: Daimeon Mosley v. Kohl's Department Stores, Inc.
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user sought injunctive relief requiring Kohl's department stores to make their restroom facilities ADA-compliant (architectural barriers including inaccessible doors, improperly spaced grab bars, and sinks/mirrors/dispensers placed too high).
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Mosley established Article III standing to pursue his ADA Title III claim because he plausibly alleged intent to return to the Michigan stores based on his regular annual visits to family in the Detroit area and scheduled performances in southeast Michigan.
brief_summary: Daimeon Mosley, a wheelchair user with disabilities including paralysis from West Nile Virus, sued Kohl's Department Stores under Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act alleging inaccessibility of restroom facilities at two Michigan stores. The district court dismissed for lack of standing, finding Mosley— who lives in Arizona and visited the stores only once—failed to allege a real and immediate threat of future injury. The Sixth Circuit reversed, holding that Mosley plausibly alleged standing based on his regular annual visits to the Detroit area to see family and scheduled performances, combined with his stated intent to return to the stores if made ADA-compliant.

case_name: Donahue Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
accommodation_description: Black tenant sought landlord action to address severe and persistent racial harassment by a neighbor, including verbal slurs, death threats, and intimidation.
key_holding: The Second Circuit held that post-acquisition claims for intentional race-based discrimination are cognizable under § 3604(b) and that a landlord may be liable for tenant-on-tenant racial harassment when the landlord had actual notice and the power to take reasonable steps to address it but failed to do so.
brief_summary: Donahue Francis, a Black tenant using a Section 8 voucher, sued his landlord Kings Park Manor claiming the landlord violated the FHA by refusing to address severe racial harassment by a neighbor who repeatedly called Francis racial slurs, threatened to kill him, and photographed his apartment. The Second Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal, holding that post-acquisition race discrimination claims are cognizable under § 3604(b) and that landlords may be liable when they have actual notice of tenant-on-tenant harassment but take no reasonable action to address it.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Comm. Concerning Cmty. Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Webb v. United States Veterans Initiative
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged gender discrimination, claiming he was not offered his own apartment unit while a female client was not required to share an apartment unit.
key_holding: Plaintiff lacks Article III standing under the FHA because he is not a renter (actual or potential) as defined by the statute—U.S. VETS executed the leases directly with landlords and fully subsidized plaintiff's rent, so plaintiff has no legally protected interest in the rental relationship.
brief_summary: Stanley Webb, a pro se veteran plaintiff, sued U.S. VETS and The Community Partnership for Fair Housing Act violations, claiming gender discrimination because he was required to share an apartment while a female client was assigned single occupancy. The court dismissed the federal FHA claim for lack of Article III standing, finding that plaintiff is not a renter as defined by the FHA since U.S. VETS executed the leases directly with landlords and fully subsidized his rent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 4466048_2019_Phyllis Davis v. Echo Valley Condominium Ass'n_ca6
case_name: Phyllis Davis v. Echo Valley Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Davis requested that the Association prohibit smoking within her building as a reasonable accommodation for her asthma and multiple chemical sensitivity disorder.
key_holding: A blanket smoking ban in a condominium complex that permits smoking is not a 'reasonable accommodation' under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because it constitutes a fundamental alteration of the housing policy, not a moderate adjustment, and would intrude on the rights of third-party residents who chose to live in a complex that allows smoking.
brief_summary: Phyllis Davis sued the Echo Valley Condominium Association and its property manager, alleging that their refusal to ban smoking in her building violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act as a failure to accommodate her asthma and multiple chemical sensitivity disorder. The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the Association, holding that Davis's requested smoking ban was not a reasonable accommodation because it would fundamentally alter the Association's smoking policy rather than moderately adjusting it, and would intrude on the rights of third-party residents who chose to live in a complex permitting smoking.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Groner v. Golden Gate Apartments, 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)
Se. Cmty. Coll. v. Davis, 442 U.S. 397 (1979)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 4468236_2019_Inclusive Communities Project v. Department of Tre_ca5
case_name: Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Department of Treasury
race_if_mentioned: Black and minority families in Dallas, Texas
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case. ICP challenges Treasury and OCC's alleged failure to regulate the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program to affirmatively further fair housing under Section 3608 of the FHA.
key_holding: ICP lacks Article III standing to sue Treasury and OCC because the causal chain between the agencies' conduct and ICP's alleged injuries is too attenuated—neither agency directly controls where LIHTC projects are sited, and any remedial action would depend on independent decisions by state housing credit agencies and private project sponsors.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project, a fair housing nonprofit, sued the Department of Treasury and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, alleging that federal agencies failed to regulate the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program in a manner that affirmatively furthers fair housing, perpetuating racial segregation in Dallas. The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that ICP lacked Article III standing because the causal chain between the agencies' conduct and the location of LIHTC projects was too attenuated—the agencies do not directly control where projects are sited, and any remedial action would depend on independent decisions by state agencies and private sponsors.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Allen v. Wright, 468 U.S. 737 (1984)
Simon v. Eastern Kentucky Welfare Rights Org., 426 U.S. 26 (1976)
Bennett v. Spear, 520 U.S. 154 (1997)
Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better Environment, 523 U.S. 83 (1998)

case_name: Cynthia Madej; Robert Madej v. Jeff Maiden
accommodation_description: Request that the county engineer use non-asphalt alternatives to chip seal for road maintenance on Dutch Creek Road near plaintiffs' home due to Ms. Madej's multiple chemical sensitivity.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the county engineer because the Madejs failed to provide reliable expert testimony establishing that asphalt in chip seal would cause Ms. Madej harm, which is necessary to prove that the requested accommodation is 'necessary' under § 3604(f)(3)(B); without expert causation evidence, they could not establish that non-asphalt alternatives were needed to afford Ms. Madej an equal opportunity to enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Cynthia and Robert Madej sued Athens County Engineer Jeff Maiden, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Amendments Act and Americans with Disabilities Act because the county refused to use non-asphalt alternatives for chip seal roadwork near their home due to Ms. Madej's multiple chemical sensitivity. The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the county engineer, holding that the Madejs could not prove their reasonable accommodation claim because they failed to provide reliable expert testimony establishing that asphalt would cause Ms. Madej harm, making the accommodation unnecessary as a matter of law.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Davis v. Echo Valley Condo. Ass'n, 945 F.3d 483 (6th Cir. 2019)
Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Kumho Tire Co. v. Carmichael, 526 U.S. 137 (1999)

case_name: Alton Crain v. City of Selma
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request; Crain alleged racial discrimination in city property bidding process and ballot access
key_holding: Crain failed to establish pretext for racial discrimination because he presented no evidence that race was a significant factor in the bidding process, and procedural irregularities alone do not support an inference of discriminatory intent under the FHA.
brief_summary: Alton Crain, an African-American resident, sued the City of Selma and its employees alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act when the city rejected his bid to purchase a parcel of city-owned land and denied his ballot access for city council. The Fifth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, finding Crain failed to produce evidence that race was a significant factor in either decision, despite procedural irregularities in the bidding process.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Simms v. First Gibraltar Bank, 83 F.3d 1546 (5th Cir. 1996)
St. Mary's Honor Center v. Hicks, 509 U.S. 502 (1993)
Artisan/Am. Corp. v. City of Alvin, 588 F.3d 291 (5th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Neway Mengistu v. Housing Authority of the City of Los Angeles
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a third-bedroom Section 8 voucher to store medically necessary exercise equipment for his mobility disability.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit reversed summary judgment for HACLA, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the third-bedroom voucher accommodation was reasonable given plaintiff's unique constraints accessing external fitness facilities and whether HACLA properly evaluated his timely submitted medical documentation.
brief_summary: Neway Mengistu, a wheelchair-using disabled tenant, sued HACLA under the Fair Housing Act after the housing authority denied his request for a third-bedroom Section 8 voucher to store medically necessary exercise equipment. The Ninth Circuit reversed the district court's summary judgment for HACLA, holding that genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the accommodation was reasonable and whether HACLA improperly disregarded his medical evidence.
key_cases_cited: Vinson v. Thomas, 288 F.3d 1145 (9th Cir. 2002)
Weinreich v. Los Angeles County, 114 F.3d 976 (9th Cir. 1997)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Mustafa v. Clark Cty. Sch. Dist., 157 F.3d 1169 (9th Cir. 1998)

case_name: Borum v. Brentwood Village, LLC
accommodation_description: Familial status disparate impact challenge to redevelopment plan that would eliminate four- and five-bedroom apartments and reduce three-bedroom units, allegedly disparately impacting families with children
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs failed to establish that the redevelopment plan's elimination of larger family units would actually disproportionately affect families with children as defined under the FHA's familial status protections, and ONE DC lacked organizational standing to pursue the discriminatory statements claim because it provided no evidence connecting the alleged discriminatory statements to concrete injury to the organization.
brief_summary: A certified class of Brookland Manor residents in Washington, D.C. sued the property owner, developer, and management company alleging that the planned redevelopment violated the FHA and DCHRA by disparately impacting families with children through elimination of larger units and through discriminatory statements about large families. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiffs failed to establish disparate impact under the FHA's familial status definition, and that organizational plaintiff ONE DC lacked standing to pursue the discriminatory statements claim.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Spann v. ITB藩 Inc., 899 F.2d 21 (D.C. Cir. 1990)

source_file: 4524627_2020_Arthur v. District of Columbia Housing Authority_dcd
case_name: Evelyn Arthur, et al. v. District of Columbia Housing Authority, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to modify the visitor phone call policy to allow Ms. Arthur's deaf son to visit and approve visitors on her behalf without requiring her to answer a phone call, since she cannot hear the phone ringing.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because Ms. Arthur adequately alleged that she requested modifications to the phone call policy and hearing-impaired hardware, and defendants failed to respond or provided untimely accommodations, but granted dismissal of the live-in aide request because permitting a registered sex offender to reside in public housing would violate federal law.
brief_summary: Evelyn Arthur, a profoundly deaf tenant at Claridge Towers public housing, and her son Robert sued DCHA and CIH for failing to provide reasonable accommodations under the Fair Housing Act, including modifying the visitor phone call policy and providing hearing-impaired hardware. The court dismissed most claims as untimely but allowed the FHA reasonable accommodation claim to proceed, finding that defendants failed to respond to Ms. Arthur's accommodation requests and waited over a year to install requested hardware.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Flemmings v. Howard Univ., 198 F.3d 857 (D.C. Cir. 1999)
United States v. District of Columbia, 538 F. Supp. 2d 211 (D.D.C. 2008)

source_file: 4531039_2018_George Friedel v. Park Place Community LLC_ca11
case_name: George Friedel v. Park Place Community LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to keep his emotional support dog in the mobile home park despite community rules prohibiting dogs that display dangerous or aggressive behavior.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed that evidence of the dog's remedial training was properly excluded because it occurred after Park Place issued the notice to cure and therefore was not relevant objective evidence available at the time of the direct threat assessment.
brief_summary: George Friedel sued Park Place Community LLC, a mobile home park, under the Fair Housing Act after the park demanded he remove his emotional support dog due to alleged aggressive behavior. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the judgment for the defendant, holding that evidence of the dog's post-lawsuit remedial training was irrelevant to whether Park Place's direct threat determination was reasonable when it issued the notice to cure.

source_file: 4536067_2018_Tree of Life Christian Sch. v. City of Upper Arlington, Ohio_ca6

case_name: Keith Cohen v. Monroe County
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of deed restriction requiring that purchasers and occupants derive at least 70% of household income from gainful employment in Monroe County, because plaintiffs' disabilities prevent them from working.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for Monroe County, holding that the Cohens' requested accommodation—a waiver of the local-income deed restriction—was not reasonable because granting it would require a fundamental alteration of the County's affordable and employee housing program designed to maintain housing stock for Florida Keys employees.
brief_summary: Keith and Cheri Cohen sued Monroe County under the FHA and Florida Fair Housing Act, claiming the County failed to reasonably accommodate their disabilities by denying a request to waive a deed restriction requiring that property owners derive 70% of income from local employment. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the County, finding the requested waiver would fundamentally alter the County's employee housing program by allowing non-employees to occupy market-rate deed-restricted housing, undermining the program's core purpose of maintaining affordable housing for Florida Keys workers.
key_cases_cited: Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Loren v. Sasser, 309 F.3d 1296 (11th Cir. 2002)

source_file: 4541887_2018_Robyn Edwards v. G. Salter Prop _ Salter Const._ca8
case_name: Robyn G. Edwards; Mikki Adams v. Gene Salter Properties and Salter Construction Inc.
accommodation_description: Request that defendant property manager accept alternative income documentation (SSDI, retirement benefits, rental income) in lieu of standard pay stubs, offer letters, or tax returns to verify income for rental application.
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit reversed summary judgment for the property management company, holding that plaintiffs showed the requested accommodation (accepting alternative income documentation) was necessary and reasonable because requiring pay stubs, offer letters, or tax returns posed an additional burden on the disabled applicant who received SSDI and was not required to file federal tax returns.
brief_summary: Robyn Edwards and Mikki Adams, disabled tenants receiving SSDI, sued Gene Salter Properties after being denied an apartment because they could not provide standard income verification (pay stubs, offer letter, or tax returns). The Eighth Circuit reversed summary judgment for the property manager, holding that accepting alternative income documentation was a necessary and reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because requiring traditional documentation placed an additional burden on the disabled applicant and the co-signer/prepayment options did not level the playing field.
key_cases_cited: Peterson v. Kopp, 754 F.3d 594 (8th Cir. 2014)
US Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Wis. Cmty. Servs. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 4558819_2020_City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo _ Company_ca9
case_name: City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo & Company
accommodation_description: The City alleges Wells Fargo engaged in discriminatory lending practices (redlining and reverse redlining) by issuing predatory home loans to Black and Latino residents, causing foreclosures that reduced property-tax revenues.
key_holding: The court held that Oakland adequately pled proximate cause for its lost property-tax revenue claims based on statistical regression analyses showing Wells Fargo's discriminatory lending practices caused foreclosures that reduced property values, but failed to adequately plead proximate cause for increased municipal expenses claims lacking statistical support; the court also held that proximate cause applies to claims for injunctive and declaratory relief.
brief_summary: The City of Oakland sued Wells Fargo under the Fair Housing Act alleging the bank engaged in discriminatory lending practices by issuing predatory loans to Black and Latino residents, causing foreclosures that reduced property-tax revenues and increased municipal expenses. The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal of the increased municipal expenses claims for failure to adequately plead proximate cause without statistical support, reversed dismissal of the lost property-tax revenue claims and injunctive relief claims, holding Oakland's regression analyses sufficiently established proximate cause between the bank's discriminatory lending and the city's economic injuries.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami (Miami I), 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Lexmark Int'l, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014)
Bridge v. Phoenix Bond & Indem. Co., 553 U.S. 639 (2008)
Holmes v. Sec. Investor Prot. Corp., 503 U.S. 258 (1992)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1973)

source_file: 4569538_2018_Mississippi Rising Coalition v. City of Oce_ca5
case_name: Mississippi Rising Coalition v. City of Ocean Springs, Mississippi
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request; this is a racial steering claim based on the city displaying the Mississippi state flag with the Confederate battle emblem.
key_holding: Plaintiffs lack Article III and statutory standing because the City's resolution requiring the Mississippi state flag to be flown over public buildings does not constitute a 'discriminatory housing practice' under the FHA—it does not make housing unavailable or directly regulate or zone housing.
brief_summary: Mississippi Rising Coalition and individual residents sued the City of Ocean Springs, Mississippi under the Fair Housing Act, alleging that the City's resolution requiring the Mississippi state flag (which includes the Confederate battle flag) to be flown over municipal buildings constitutes racial steering that deters African-Americans from living in or moving to the city. The Fifth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, finding that plaintiffs lacked both Article III standing and statutory standing under the FHA because the flag display is not a 'discriminatory housing practice' as required by the statute.
key_cases_cited: Gladstone Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Moore v. Bryant, 853 F.3d 245 (5th Cir. 2017)

source_file: 4569757_2018_Mississippi Rising Coalition v. City of Oce_ca5
case_name: Mississippi Rising Coalition v. City of Ocean Springs, Mississippi
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenged the City of Ocean Springs' resolution requiring the Mississippi state flag (containing the Confederate battle flag) to be flown over municipal buildings as allegedly deterring African-Americans from housing in the city.
key_holding: Plaintiffs lacked Article III standing because exposure to a state flag does not constitute injury sufficient to confer standing, and lacked statutory standing because flying a state flag over public buildings does not constitute a discriminatory housing practice under the FHA.
brief_summary: Mississippi Rising Coalition and individual residents sued the City of Ocean Springs, Mississippi, alleging that the City's resolution requiring the Mississippi state flag (featuring the Confederate battle flag) to be flown over public buildings violated the Fair Housing Act by deterring African-Americans from housing in the city (racial steering). The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that plaintiffs lacked both Article III standing (flag exposure is not an injury) and statutory standing (flying a flag is not a discriminatory housing practice under § 3604(a) or discriminatory advertising under § 3604(c)).
key_cases_cited: Gladstone Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Moore v. Bryant, 853 F.3d 245 (5th Cir. 2017)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Tenafly Eruv Ass'n, Inc. v. Borough of Tenafly, 309 F.3d 144 (3d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 4574498_2020_431 East Palisade Avenue Real v. City of Englewood_ca3
case_name: 431 East Palisade Avenue Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood
accommodation_description: Developer sought to construct a 150-bed for-profit assisted living facility for memory care patients in a single-family residential zone (R-AAA) where assisted living facilities are not a permitted use.
key_holding: The City's zoning ordinance does not facially discriminate against individuals with disabilities because it does not explicitly classify or mention disabled persons, assisted living facilities are only explicitly referenced in a preferential context in the RIM zone, and the omission of a use from a permitted list is not equivalent to express prohibition based on protected trait.
brief_summary: A developer sued the City of Englewood challenging its zoning ordinance as facially discriminatory under the Fair Housing Amendments Act for permitting assisted living facilities only in the RIM district and implicitly excluding them from the single-family residential R-AAA district. The Third Circuit vacated the preliminary injunction granted by the district court, holding that the ordinance is not facially discriminatory because it does not explicitly classify or reference disabled persons, and the omission of a use from a permitted list does not constitute express discrimination based on a protected trait.
key_cases_cited: Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Municipal Authority, 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Board of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 4581562_2020_Dr. David S. Muransky v. Godiva Chocolatier, Inc._ca11
case_name: Dr. David S. Muransky v. Godiva Chocolatier, Inc.
accommodation_description: This is not a Fair Housing Act case. Plaintiff sued Godiva for printing too many credit card digits on receipts in violation of the Fair and Accurate Credit Transactions Act (FACTA), 15 U.S.C. § 1681c(g)(1).
key_holding: The en banc Eleventh Circuit vacated the district court's approval of a class settlement, holding that the named plaintiff Dr. Muransky lacked Article III standing because he alleged only a bare statutory violation of FACTA without demonstrating concrete injury in fact as required by Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins.
brief_summary: Dr. David Muransky filed a class action against Godiva Chocolatier alleging violations of FACTA for printing more than five digits of credit card numbers on receipts. The parties settled for $6.3 million, but objectors challenged the settlement arguing Muransky lacked Article III standing. The en banc Eleventh Circuit vacated the settlement approval, holding that Muransky failed to demonstrate concrete injury in fact beyond the bare statutory violation.
key_cases_cited: Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Thole v. U.S. Bank N.A., 140 S. Ct. 1615 (2020)

case_name: Carol Thomas v. San Francisco Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be upgraded to a larger unit or relocated during renovation as a reasonable accommodation for disability.
key_holding: The district court properly dismissed Thomas's disability claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B) because this claim was time-barred under the FHA's two-year statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Carol Thomas, a pro se plaintiff, appealed the district court's dismissal and summary judgment in her action against the San Francisco Housing Authority alleging disability and race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The Ninth Circuit affirmed, holding that Thomas's disability reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) was properly dismissed as time-barred under the FHA's two-year statute of limitations, and that her racial discrimination claim failed because she did not raise a genuine dispute of material fact regarding discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Pfaff v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 88 F.3d 739 (9th Cir. 1996)
Hoang v. Bank of Am., N.A., 910 F.3d 1096 (9th Cir. 2018)
Avenue 6E Invs., LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Mahmood Khan v. City of Minneapolis
accommodation_description: Landlord challenging city revocation of rental-dwelling licenses as having disparate impact on protected class members - not a reasonable accommodation case
key_holding: The court rejected Khan's disparate-impact claim, finding his allegations about the city's housing-code enforcement and license revocations essentially identical to those rejected in Ellis v. City of Minneapolis, and concluding that Khan failed to identify an artificial, arbitrary, and unnecessary policy that an FHA disparate-impact claim could remedy.
brief_summary: Landlord Mahmood Khan sued the City of Minneapolis after it revoked his rental-dwelling licenses under a city ordinance prohibiting license-holding for five years after two prior revocations. The Eighth Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment on the pleadings for the city, rejecting Khan's disparate-impact claim under the Fair Housing Act because the claim was essentially identical to one previously rejected in Ellis v. City of Minneapolis, and the city's policy of enforcing housing codes against landlords served legitimate health and safety objectives rather than artificial, arbitrary barriers.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Ellis v. City of Minneapolis, 860 F.3d 1106 (8th Cir. 2017)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 4621342_2019_Robert Jones, Jr. v. M_A Mgmt Corp_ca7
case_name: Robert L. Jones, Jr. v. M/A Mgmt Corp.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case involves allegations of racial discrimination in lease termination, failure to make repairs, and denial of clubhouse access.
key_holding: The district court did not abuse its discretion in dismissing the case under Rule 41(b) for failure to prosecute, as Jones demonstrated a pattern of non-compliance with discovery orders over six months despite three motions to compel, monetary sanctions, and explicit warnings that dismissal could result.
brief_summary: Robert Jones, an African American tenant, sued Runaway Bay Apartments alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming the landlord wrongfully terminated his lease, failed to make repairs, and denied him access to the clubhouse based on his race. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal for failure to prosecute because Jones repeatedly failed to comply with discovery orders over six months despite three motions to compel, monetary sanctions, and explicit warnings that continued non-compliance could result in dismissal.
key_cases_cited: Nelson v. Schultz, 878 F.3d 236 (7th Cir. 2017)
Salata v. Weyerhaeuser Co., 757 F.3d 695 (7th Cir. 2014)
Cincinnati Life Ins. Co. v. Beyrer, 722 F.3d 939 (7th Cir. 2013)
Rutledge v. United States, 230 F.3d 1041 (7th Cir. 2000)
United States v. 5443 Suffield Terrace, 607 F.3d 504 (7th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Kimberly Johnson, Deborah Brazell v. Kellie Jennings, Shelley Morris
accommodation_description: Request for chain lock on front door to prevent autistic child from eloping from the home
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed that the jury's finding that defendants did not deny the modification request was supported by the evidence, and the district court did not abuse its discretion in excluding evidence of the child's medical conditions as unfairly prejudicial under Rule 403.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs-appellants, mother and grandmother of an autistic child requiring a chain lock on the door to prevent elopement, sued the landlord and leasing agent under the Fair Housing Act claiming denial of a reasonable modification. After a jury found defendants did not deny the modification request, plaintiffs appealed evidentiary rulings excluding medical evidence. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, holding the district court did not abuse its discretion in excluding evidence of the child's gastrointestinal conditions as unfairly prejudicial, and any error in admitting code-enforcement testimony was harmless because the jury did not reach the reasonableness question.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamore Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Old Chief v. United States, 519 U.S. 172 (1997)
Shealy v. City of Albany, 89 F.3d 804 (11th Cir. 1996)

case_name: Gwendolyn Wilson v. Hillsborough Township Construction Department
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the plaintiff alleged racial discrimination in municipal construction inspections and property tax assessments of her home.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed the District Court's dismissal, holding that Wilson's FHA claim under § 3604(b) was properly dismissed because her vague, conclusory allegations of racial discrimination in housing inspection and tax assessment services were insufficient to state a claim.
brief_summary: Gwendolyn Wilson, an African-American homeowner, sued Hillsborough Township, its Construction/Building Department, and Tax Assessor under § 1983 and the FHA alleging racial discrimination in construction inspections and property tax assessments of her home. The Third Circuit summarily affirmed the District Court's dismissal, finding Wilson's FHA claims under § 3604(b) failed because her allegations were too vague and conclusory to state a claim for intentional or disparate impact discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Township of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

case_name: Glenn Howard; Gale Howard; Christine Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC; Hovik M. Khaloian
accommodation_description: Request to extend the tenancy termination date from July 15, 2017 to January 22, 2018 to accommodate Glenn Howard's brain injury and medical condition
key_holding: Because plaintiffs failed to establish a causal link between Glenn Howard's medical condition and the requested tenancy extension, defendants had no obligation under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B) to extend the tenancy-termination date, and there is no standalone liability under the FHAA for a landlord's failure to engage in an interactive process.
brief_summary: The Howards, tenants in defendants' Los Angeles property, sued under the Fair Housing Amendments Act seeking to extend their tenancy termination date as a reasonable accommodation for Glenn Howard's brain injury. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, holding that the Howards failed to establish a causal link between Howard's medical condition and the necessity of the requested accommodation, and that the FHAA does not provide standalone liability for a landlord's failure to engage in an interactive process.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co. (Mobile Home II), 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Groner v. Golden Gate Gardens Apartments, 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 4667674_2021_Metropolitan Omaha Property v. City of Omaha, Nebraska_ca8
case_name: Metropolitan Omaha Property Owners Association, Inc. v. City of Omaha, Nebraska
race_if_mentioned: Not specified - court mentions minority tenants generally without identifying specific race/ethnicity
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged the Rental Property Registration and Inspection Ordinance resulted in disparate treatment and disparate impact on minority tenants based on race, color, religion, sex, familial status, and national origin under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a).
key_holding: The appellate court affirmed dismissal because plaintiffs failed to plead facts showing discriminatory purpose for their disparate treatment claim and failed to allege facts demonstrating a causal connection between the ordinance and any alleged disparate impact on minority tenants.
brief_summary: Metropolitan Omaha Property Owners Association and individual property owners sued the City of Omaha challenging the constitutionality and legality of its Rental Property Registration and Inspection Ordinance. The Eighth Circuit affirmed the dismissal of all claims, including Fourth Amendment, Fifth Amendment, consent decree breach, and Fair Housing Act disparate treatment and disparate impact claims, finding plaintiffs failed to adequately plead facts supporting each theory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Ellis v. City of Minneapolis, 860 F.3d 1106 (8th Cir. 2017)
City of Los Angeles v. Patel, 576 U.S. 409 (2015)

case_name: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged his landlord failed to respond to race-based harassment by a fellow tenant, claiming the landlord's inaction constituted intentional racial discrimination under the FHA.
key_holding: Landlords cannot be presumed to have the degree of control over tenants necessary to impose liability under the FHA for tenant-on-tenant misconduct; landlords do not exercise the substantial control over tenants required for deliberate indifference liability under the FHA.
brief_summary: Donahue Francis, a Black tenant, sued his landlord Kings Park Manor and property manager for intentional race discrimination under the FHA, alleging they failed to respond to race-based harassment by a fellow tenant who subjected him to racist insults and death threats. The en banc Second Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that landlords cannot be presumed to exercise the substantial control over tenants required for FHA liability for tenant-on-tenant harassment.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Davis v. Monroe Cnty. Bd. of Educ., 526 U.S. 629 (1999)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Wetzel v. Lucas, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 4678020_2021_Stanley Webb v. United States Veterans Initiative_cadc
case_name: Stanley Webb v. United States Veterans Initiative (US Vets) And Community Partnership
subsidy_program: Supportive Housing Program; Shelter Plus Care
accommodation_description: This case involves a sex discrimination claim under § 3604(a), not a reasonable accommodation request; Webb alleged U.S. Vets refused to offer him a one-bedroom unit because of his sex while offering one to a less-qualified female applicant.
key_holding: Webb qualifies as an 'aggrieved person' under the Fair Housing Act even though he paid no rent, because § 3604(a) prohibits making housing 'unavailable' based on protected characteristics like sex, and the statute contains no textual limitation requiring rent payment to bring suit.
brief_summary: Pro se veteran Stanley Webb sued U.S. Vets, a nonprofit veterans' services provider, alleging sex discrimination after being denied a one-bedroom unit while a less-qualified female applicant received one. The D.C. Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that Webb qualifies as an 'aggrieved person' under the Fair Housing Act regardless of whether he paid rent because the statute's prohibition on making housing 'unavailable' based on protected characteristics contains no rent-payment requirement.
key_cases_cited: 2922 Sherman Avenue Tenants' Ass'n v. District of Columbia, 444 F.3d 673 (D.C. Cir. 2006)
Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Georgia State Conference of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
The Committee Concerning Community Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 4678095_2021_Lawrence Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica_ca9
case_name: Lawrence Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested waiver of mobile home park rules to allow parking his vehicle immediately next to his mobile home rather than in the designated parking area.
key_holding: The FHAA applies only to rentals where the landlord has received consideration in exchange for granting the right to occupy premises; because Salisbury never entered into a lease or paid rent to the City, the FHAA was inapplicable to his accommodation claim and the City had no obligation to provide, offer, or discuss an accommodation.
brief_summary: Lawrence Salisbury, who lived in his deceased father's mobile home in a City-owned mobile home park without ever signing a lease or paying rent, sued the City of Santa Monica under the FHAA after the City denied his request to park his vehicle next to his mobile home as an accommodation for his mobility disability. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding that the FHAA applies only to rentals supported by adequate consideration, and because Salisbury never provided consideration in exchange for the right to occupy the premises, the statute was inapplicable to his claim.
key_cases_cited: Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apt. Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)

source_file: 4693366_2021_Lawrence Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica_ca9
case_name: Lawrence Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica
accommodation_description: Request to waive mobile home park rules to allow parking of personal vehicle immediately next to mobile home rather than in assigned parking space, due to painful mobility limitations from spinal conditions
key_holding: The FHAA applies only to rentals where the landlord accepted consideration in exchange for granting the right to occupy the premises; because Salisbury never entered into a lease or paid rent to the City, the FHAA's reasonable accommodation duty did not apply to his claim.
brief_summary: Lawrence Salisbury, a mobility-impaired individual who lived in his deceased father's mobile home in a City-owned park but never entered a lease or paid rent, sued the City of Santa Monica under the FHAA for refusing his request to waive parking rules so he could park adjacent to his unit. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding that the FHAA applies only to rentals involving an exchange of consideration between landlord and tenant, and because Salisbury never provided such consideration, the City's duty to accommodate under the FHAA was never triggered.
key_cases_cited: Wheeler v. City of Santa Clara, 894 F.3d 1046 (9th Cir. 2018)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apt. Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)

source_file: 4698095_2021_Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development_dcd
case_name: Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs request that HUD provide tenant protection vouchers and relocation assistance to enable residents to relocate from substandard housing conditions.
key_holding: The court denied HUD's motion to transfer venue to the Southern District of Texas, finding that HUD failed to carry its burden of demonstrating that the transferee district is more convenient than the District of Columbia where HUD headquarters and relevant decision-makers are located.
brief_summary: Sandpiper Residents Association and individual residents of Sandpiper Cove Apartments in Galveston, Texas sued HUD under the APA, Fair Housing Act, and Equal Protection, alleging that HUD failed to provide tenant protection vouchers and relocation assistance despite knowing the property was substandard and the majority of residents are Black and Hispanic. The court denied HUD's motion to transfer venue to the Southern District of Texas, holding that D.C. is an appropriate venue because HUD headquarters holds final approval authority over voucher issuance and relevant decision-makers are located there.

case_name: Gregory Selden v. Airbnb, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged disparate impact from Airbnb's photo policy and true name policy on African American users, not a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The D.C. Circuit affirmed that Selden agreed to arbitrate his discrimination claims because Airbnb's sign-up screen put him on reasonable notice of the Terms of Service arbitration clause, and his discrimination claims—including those under the Fair Housing Act—were subject to arbitration.
brief_summary: Gregory Selden, an African American man, sued Airbnb alleging race-based housing discrimination after a host rejected his rental request; he claimed Airbnb's policies had a disparate impact on Black users. The D.C. Circuit affirmed that Selden agreed to arbitrate his discrimination claims because Airbnb's sign-up screen provided reasonable notice of the Terms of Service arbitration clause, and his statutory discrimination claims—including those under the Fair Housing Act—were arbitrable.
key_cases_cited: Epic Sys. Corp. v. Lewis, 138 S. Ct. 1612 (2018)
AT&T Mobility LLC v. Concepcion, 563 U.S. 333 (2011)
Mitsubishi Motors Corp. v. Soler Chrysler-Plymouth, Inc., 473 U.S. 614 (1985)
CompuCredit Corp. v. Greenwood, 565 U.S. 95 (2012)
Shearson/Am. Express, Inc. v. McMahon, 482 U.S. 220 (1987)

case_name: Rita Fox v. Lucille F. Gaines
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves sexual harassment and sex discrimination claims rather than disability accommodation requests
key_holding: Sexual harassment is actionable as sex discrimination under the FHA under a but-for causation standard where the plaintiff demonstrates she would not have been harassed but for her sex; the court vacated the dismissal and remanded for the district court to consider whether the complaint adequately stated an FHA claim.
brief_summary: Rita Fox sued property manager Dana Gaines and owner Lucille Gaines alleging sexual harassment and sex discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after being forced out of her apartment for ending a sexual relationship with the property manager who had reduced her rent in exchange for sexual favors. The Eleventh Circuit held that sexual harassment is actionable as sex discrimination under the FHA under a but-for causation standard, vacated the district court's dismissal, and remanded for reconsideration of whether the complaint adequately stated a claim.
key_cases_cited: Meritor Sav. Bank, FSB v. Vinson, 477 U.S. 57 (1986)
Henson v. City of Dundee, 682 F.2d 897 (11th Cir. 1982)
Burrage v. United States, 571 U.S. 204 (2014)
DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)

case_name: Rita Fox v. Lucille F. Gaines
accommodation_description: Sexual harassment claim - property manager allegedly conditioned rent reductions on sexual favors and subjected tenant to hostile housing environment based on sex.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit held that sexual harassment is actionable under the Fair Housing Act as a form of sex discrimination under § 3604(b) when the plaintiff demonstrates she would not have been harassed but for her sex, vacating the district court's dismissal and remanding for reconsideration of whether the complaint adequately stated a claim.
brief_summary: Rita Fox sued property owner Lucille Gaines and property manager Dana Gaines under the FHA, alleging that the property manager subjected her to quid pro quo sexual harassment and a hostile housing environment in exchange for reduced rent. The Eleventh Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal, holding for the first time in the circuit that sexual harassment constitutes actionable sex discrimination under § 3604(b) of the FHA when the harassment would not have occurred but for the plaintiff's sex, and remanded for the district court to reconsider whether Fox's complaint adequately stated a claim.
key_cases_cited: Meritor Savs. Bank, FSB v. Vinson, 477 U.S. 57 (1986)
Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)
DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
United States v. Hurt, 676 F.3d 649 (8th Cir. 2012)
Burrage v. United States, 571 U.S. 204 (2014)

source_file: 4731274_2020_Quality of Life, Corp. v. The City of Margate_ca11
case_name: Quality of Life, Corp. v. The City of Margate
accommodation_description: Quality of Life, a business entity, requested permission to operate a drug-detoxification facility in a residential zone under the FHA's reasonable accommodation framework.
key_holding: The court rejected Quality of Life's reasonable accommodation claim because it failed to follow the City's established reasonable accommodation procedures, never fully explained why the accommodation was necessary for disabled persons, and failed to demonstrate futility since City officials indicated the accommodation request process remained available.
brief_summary: Quality of Life Corp. and MMJ Financial Services sued the City of Margate, Florida, alleging that the City's refusal to permit them to operate a drug-detoxification facility in a residential zone violated the Fair Housing Act and ADA under theories of disparate treatment, disparate impact, and failure to provide reasonable accommodation. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, finding no evidence of intentional discrimination, no substantial disparate impact, and that Quality of Life failed to properly request a reasonable accommodation under the City's established procedures.
key_cases_cited: Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
MX Grp., Inc. v. City of Covington, 293 F.3d 326 (6th Cir. 2002)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Natale Mercuri v. Occidental Tower Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an additional parking pass for himself and a co-occupant as a reasonable accommodation for his disability.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the landlord because Mercuri failed to raise a genuine dispute of material fact showing the single parking pass accommodation was necessary to afford him an equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
brief_summary: Natale Mercuri, proceeding pro se, appealed the district court's summary judgment in his Fair Housing Act action against Occidental Tower Apartments, challenging the landlord's provision of only one parking pass for Mercuri and his co-occupant as a failure to provide reasonable accommodation. The Ninth Circuit affirmed, holding that Mercuri failed to establish a genuine dispute of material fact as to whether the single parking pass denied him an equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Guatay Christian Fellowship v. County of San Diego, 670 F.3d 957 (9th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Krystal Moore v. Camden Property Trust
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request at issue; claims involve race and familial status discrimination, retaliation, and interference.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal because the settlement agreement was enforceable and released claims; collateral estoppel barred relitigating the lease non-renewal; and Moore failed to state viable claims for interference, abusive litigation, or intentional infliction of emotional distress.
brief_summary: Krystal Moore, proceeding pro se, appealed the dismissal of her claims against Camden Property Trust and its law firm, alleging discrimination based on race and familial status, retaliation for filing a HUD complaint, and related state law claims including abusive litigation and intentional infliction of emotional distress. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, finding that Moore released all pre-settlement claims in an enforceable settlement agreement, was collaterally estopped from relitigating the lease non-renewal issue decided in state court, and failed to state viable claims for interference, abusive litigation, or intentional infliction of emotional distress.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 4757512_2020_Akshar Global Investments Corp v. City of Los Angeles_ca9
case_name: Akshar Global Investments Corp. v. City of Los Angeles
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it involves § 1983 constitutional claims (Fourth, Fifth, and Fourteenth Amendments) arising from revocation of a motel's conditional use permit.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit reversed dismissal of the Fourth Amendment Patel claim (related to warrantless searches of motel guest registration records) and remanded for leave to amend, while affirming dismissal of Fifth Amendment Takings, Fourteenth Amendment Equal Protection, and Due Process claims.
brief_summary: Akshar Global Investments Corp. and its owner Amitkumar Shah sued the City of Los Angeles under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act, challenging the revocation of the conditional use permit for their motel. The Ninth Circuit reversed the dismissal of the Fourth Amendment claim (regarding warrantless searches of guest registration records) for potential amendment, affirmed dismissal of takings, equal protection, and due process claims, and did not reach the FHA claim because it was not argued on appeal.
key_cases_cited: City of Los Angeles v. Patel, 576 U.S. 409 (2015)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of N.Y., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 4765131_2020_Aleksandr Selyutin v. Board of Directors of the Skok_ca7
case_name: Aleksandr Selyutin v. Board of Directors of the Skokie Gardens Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Selyutin requested that the condo association install security cameras, provide him personal access to the security system, stop vandals and burglars, and address noise from upstairs neighbors as a reasonable accommodation for his disability affecting sensory experiences.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal because Selyutin failed to state an accommodation claim—he alleged conditions that would bother anyone, not conditions to which he was particularly susceptible due to his disability—and failed to state a hostile-environment claim because he did not allege facts plausibly suggesting that the harassment was motivated by a protected characteristic.
brief_summary: Aleksandr Selyutin, a Ukrainian-born condominium owner with a sensory disability, sued the Skokie Gardens Condominium Association and its board for failing to accommodate his disability by preventing harassment, burglaries, and noise, and for creating a hostile housing environment based on disability and national origin. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of his federal claims with prejudice, finding that Selyutin failed to state an accommodation claim because he did not allege that the requested security measures would specifically help his disability, and failed to state a hostile-environment claim because he did not plausibly allege that the harassment was motivated by a protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Pension Tr. Fund for Operating Eng'rs v. Kohl's Corp., 895 F.3d 933 (7th Cir. 2018)
Healix Infusion Therapy, Inc. v. Heartland Home Infusions, Inc., 733 F.3d 700 (7th Cir. 2013)
Huon v. Denton, 841 F.3d 733 (7th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 4768065_2020_Eric Ferrier v. Cascade Falls Condominium Association, Inc._ca11
case_name: Eric Ferrier v. Cascade Falls Condominium Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Fair Housing Act discrimination claim related to foreclosure proceedings was mentioned in the complaint but not substantively analyzed.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal under Rooker-Feldman doctrine, holding that federal district courts lack subject matter jurisdiction over cases brought by state-court losers challenging state-court judgments rendered before the federal proceedings commenced.
