brief_summary: Eric Ferrier, proceeding pro se, appealed the district court's dismissal of his civil action challenging state foreclosure proceedings on his condominium; the Eleventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal, holding that the Rooker-Feldman doctrine barred federal subject matter jurisdiction because Ferrier was a state-court loser challenging injuries caused by state-court foreclosure judgments.
key_cases_cited: Lozman v. City of Riviera Beach, 713 F.3d 1066
Casale v. Tillman, 558 F.3d 1258
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus. Corp., 544 U.S. 280

case_name: Esther Collar v. Michael B. Wilson
accommodation_description: Plaintiff appears to challenge the termination of her Section 8 Housing Voucher and the alleged denial of a pre-termination hearing, though the specific accommodation request is unclear from the complaint.
key_holding: The appellate court vacated the district court's dismissal with prejudice and remanded for leave to amend because, although the FHA claim was time-barred, the pro se plaintiff's complaint was too unclear to determine whether other claims (including a potential § 1983 due process claim) might be viable.
brief_summary: Esther Collar, a pro se plaintiff with limited English proficiency, appealed the dismissal with prejudice of her complaint against Orlando Housing Authority employees challenging the termination of her Section 8 Housing Voucher. The appellate court vacated the judgment and remanded for leave to amend, holding that although the FHA claim was time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations, the district court erred in dismissing with prejudice without giving the plaintiff an opportunity to clarify whether her complaint stated viable claims—including a potential § 1983 procedural due process claim.
key_cases_cited: Jackson v. Astrue, 506 F.3d 1349 (11th Cir. 2007)
La Grasta v. First Union Sec., Inc., 358 F.3d 840 (11th Cir. 2004)
Tannenbaum v. United States, 148 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 1998)
Bank v. Pitt, 928 F.2d 1108 (11th Cir. 1991)

source_file: 4774160_2020_Inclusive Commty Project, Inc. v. Heartland Commty_ca5
case_name: The Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Heartland Community Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; case involves challenge to HOA policy prohibiting rentals to Section 8 voucher holders as racial discrimination
key_holding: Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal because ICP failed to plead adequate statistical evidence showing the HOA's 'no voucher' policy caused racial disparity rather than merely reflecting pre-existing demographic patterns, and failed to allege sufficient facts supporting an inference of discriminatory intent under the disparate treatment standard.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project sued Heartland Community Association challenging its policy prohibiting rentals to Section 8 voucher holders, arguing it constituted disparate impact and disparate treatment race discrimination under the FHA because 84% of voucher holders in the area are Black. The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that ICP failed to plead adequate facts showing the policy caused rather than merely reflected pre-existing racial disparities in voucher usage, and failed to adequately allege discriminatory intent to support its disparate treatment claim.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)

source_file: 4785274_2020_Charles Mason v. Integra Peak Management Inc., et_ca5
case_name: Charles Mason v. Integra Peak Management Incorporated
accommodation_description: Mason alleged he was treated differently from non-handicapped tenants and was evicted because of his disability, though the specific accommodation requested is not detailed.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed dismissal because Mason made only conclusory statements that he was treated differently from non-handicapped tenants but failed to allege specific facts showing any different treatment was because of his disability, failing to establish the required causal link for a disparate treatment claim.
brief_summary: Charles Mason, proceeding pro se, appealed the district court's dismissal of his Fair Housing Act claims against Integra Peak Management and individual managers arising from his 2015 eviction. The Fifth Circuit affirmed, holding that Mason failed to plausibly allege the required causal connection between his disability and any discriminatory treatment, and failed to allege any connection between his eviction and protected conduct for his retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Stratta v. Roe, 961 F.3d 340 (5th Cir. 2020)
Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Simms v. First Gibraltar Bank, 83 F.3d 1546 (5th Cir. 1996)
Williamson v. Tucker, 645 F.2d 404 (5th Cir. 1981)
Hood v. Pope, 627 F. App'x 295 (5th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Edwards v. Gene Salter Properties, Inc.
accommodation_description: Disabled applicants requested an accommodation to renting an apartment that would not require a co-signer or additional financial guarantor.
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit affirmed liability finding under law-of-the-case doctrine, rejecting GSP's argument that permitting a co-signer was a substitute for reasonable accommodation since the co-signer option placed additional burden on disabled applicants rather than leveling the playing field.
brief_summary: Robyn Edwards and Mikki Adams, disabled tenants, sued Gene Salter Properties for failure to provide reasonable accommodation in its rental application process, and both parties appealed the district court's liability and damages rulings. The Eighth Circuit affirmed the liability finding under law-of-the-case doctrine, holding that GSP could not use co-signer requirements as a substitute for accommodating the disabled applicants, while also affirming the denial of additional damages and punitive damages.
key_cases_cited: Kolstad v. Am. Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526 (1999)
Edwards v. Gene Salter Props., 739 Fed. Appx. 357 (8th Cir. 2018)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 4803706_2020_Bobbie Jo Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condominiums_ca6
case_name: Bobbie Jo Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condominiums
accommodation_description: Request to install a safety railing adjacent to an eight-inch-high outdoor porch step for Bob Romig's disability-related safety needs.
key_holding: The Association did not violate the FHA because its initial denial was not a final rejection—the Board had legitimate reasons to request medical documentation before approving the modification—and the Board lacked knowledge of the disability or that the railing was necessary until receiving the doctor's note, at which point it approved the request within 20 days.
brief_summary: Bobbie Jo Kooman, on behalf of her deceased father's estate, sued the Boulder Bluff Condominium Association and Gerow Management after the Association initially denied her request to install a safety railing on the condo's porch step. The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, holding no FHA violation occurred because the Board's preliminary denial was a legitimate request for medical documentation, the Board lacked sufficient knowledge of the resident's disability at the time of the initial denial, and the request was ultimately approved within 20 days after receiving the doctor's note.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Davis v. Echo Valley Condo. Ass'n, 945 F.3d 483 (6th Cir. 2019)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F.App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Moody v. Gongloff, 687 F.App'x 496 (6th Cir. 2017)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 4849987_2021_Christina Lyons v. Gene B. Glick Company, Incorpo_ca7
case_name: Christina G. Lyons v. Gene B. Glick Company, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of in-unit laundry machines to accommodate an unspecified disability.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of eviction-related claims under Rooker-Feldman and time-barred claims, but vacated dismissal of claims based on retaliatory acts occurring October-December 2017 (within the limitations period), remanding for further proceedings on whether Glick may be liable for independent contractors' alleged misconduct.
brief_summary: Christina Lyons sued her former landlord Gene B. Glick Company alleging Fair Housing Act violations including delay in installing in-unit laundry as a disability accommodation and subsequent retaliation. The Seventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of claims barred by Rooker-Feldman (relating to the eviction) and the statute of limitations, but vacated dismissal of timely claims based on retaliatory acts occurring October-December 2017, remanding for fact-finding on whether Glick could be held liable for independent contractors' alleged misconduct.
key_cases_cited: Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus. Corp., 544 U.S. 280 (2005)
Rooker v. Fidelity Tr. Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
D.C. Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)

source_file: 4852565_2021_Julie Wartluft v. Milton Hershey Sch and Sch Tru_ca3
case_name: Julie Ellen Wartluft, et al. v. The Milton Hershey School and School Trust
accommodation_description: Parents allege School barred student from graduation ceremony and house barbecue based on her mental illness.
key_holding: Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment because the boarding school is not a 'renter' under § 3604(f)(1) since Abbie provided no bargained-for consideration for her housing, and the graduation ceremony and barbecue are not services 'in connection with' housing under § 3604(f)(2).
brief_summary: Julie Wartluft and Frederick Bartels sued the Milton Hershey School after their daughter Abbie's suicide, claiming the School violated the Fair Housing Act by barring her from her eighth-grade graduation ceremony and a house barbecue due to her mental illness. The Third Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the School, holding that the FHA does not apply because Abbie was not a 'renter' under § 3604(f)(1) and the graduation/barbecue services were not provided 'in connection with' her housing under § 3604(f)(2).
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condominium Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Dixon v. The Hallmark Cos., 627 F.3d 849 (11th Cir. 2010)
Rep. of Sudan v. Harrison, 139 S. Ct. 1048 (2019)

source_file: 4865923_2021_Rodney Green, Sr. v. Mercy Housing, Inc._ca9
case_name: Rodney Green, Sr. v. Mercy Housing, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an accessible parking space closer to his unit due to mobility impairment.
key_holding: The court reversed summary judgment on the parking claim because genuine disputes of material fact remain as to whether a more accessible parking space was a reasonable accommodation that Mercy Housing failed to provide; the court affirmed summary judgment on the in-home care claim because Green failed to show that Elijah Green's specific care was necessary.
brief_summary: Rodney Green, Sr. appealed the district court's summary judgment for Mercy Housing on his Fair Housing Act claims alleging disability discrimination (inadequate accessible parking) and racial discrimination (racially-motivated eviction notice). The Ninth Circuit reversed summary judgment on both the parking accommodation claim and the racial discrimination claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact on each, but affirmed on Green's separate in-home care accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)
Christian v. Umpqua Bank, 984 F.3d 801 (9th Cir. 2020)
Dominguez-Curry v. Nev. Transp. Dep't, 424 F.3d 1027 (9th Cir. 2005)
Whitaker v. Tesla Motors, Inc., 985 F.3d 1173 (9th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 4868582_2021_Ricardo Pittman v. Viamonte Investments Group LLC_ca7
case_name: Ricardo Pittman, Jr. v. Viamonte Investments Group, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged discrimination based on unspecified disability and race regarding apartment conditions (flooding, mildew) and gas service termination, but did not specify a requested accommodation.
key_holding: Pittman's Fair Housing Amendments Act claim failed because his allegations did not plausibly suggest that the landlord's neglect of his unit was intentionally done to discriminate, and he failed to identify what disability he had or what accommodations he requested.
brief_summary: Ricardo Pittman, a Black disabled Section 8 voucher recipient, sued his private landlord Viamonte Investments Group, the Housing Authority of Cook County, and Nicor Gas alleging they conspired to evict him from his apartment based on his race and disability. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal of his Fair Housing Amendments Act claim, finding that his complaint failed to plausibly allege intentional discrimination and failed to identify what disability he had or what accommodations he requested from the landlord.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Wilson v. Warren Cnty., Ill., 830 F.3d 464 (7th Cir. 2016)
Iqbal v. Patel, 780 F.3d 728 (7th Cir. 2015)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Howell v. Wexford Health Sources, Inc., 987 F.3d 647 (7th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Harmony Haus Westlake, L.L.C. v. Parkstone Property Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request to operate a sober-living home with 12 unrelated residents recovering from alcoholism and drug addiction, and to park 8 vehicles, in a single-family residential subdivision subject to HOA deed restrictions limiting occupancy to single families and vehicles to garage/driveway.
key_holding: Harmony Haus failed to prove that allowing 12 unrelated residents at the Catorce house is 'necessary' under the FHA's reasonable accommodation requirement, because evidence showed that sober-living homes with six to eight residents are effective, and Harmony Haus did not establish that 12 residents is essential for therapeutic or financial viability.
brief_summary: Harmony Haus Westlake, a sober-living home operator, sued Parkstone HOA seeking to operate a 12-resident facility at a house in its Austin, Texas gated community, arguing the HOA's single-family residential use and parking restrictions violated the FHA's reasonable accommodation requirement. The Fifth Circuit vacated the district court's injunction for Harmony Haus, holding that Harmony Haus failed to prove 12 residents is a necessary accommodation because Parkstone presented evidence that effective sober-living homes operate with six to eight residents.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Brandt v. Vill. of Chebanse, 82 F.3d 172 (7th Cir. 1996)
Elderhaven, Inc. v. City of Lubbock, 98 F.3d 175 (5th Cir. 1996)
Providence Behav. Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 4874239_2021_Perricone-Bernovich v. Village of Head of the Harbor_ca2
case_name: Perricone-Bernovich v. Village of Head of the Harbor
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought a zoning variance to build a house closer to the road on a specific lot; she did not argue these requests were related to her or her brother's disabilities.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a disparate treatment claim because it contained no facts suggesting animus against persons with disabilities was a significant factor in the zoning variance denial, and failed to state a reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff did not argue her requests were related to her disabilities.
brief_summary: Barbara Perricone-Bernovich and family sued the Village of Head of the Harbor and individual officials under the Fair Housing Act, alleging that the denial of her zoning variance application constituted disability discrimination. The Second Circuit affirmed the dismissal, holding that the complaint failed to state a disparate treatment claim because it contained no facts showing animus toward disabled persons was a significant factor in the denial, and failed to state a reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff did not argue her variance requests were related to her disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Forest City Daly Hous., Inc. v. Town of North Hempstead, 175 F.3d 144 (2d Cir. 1999)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)

case_name: A.V. Avington, Jr.; Patricia L. Avington v. Bank of America, N.A.; Merscorps Holding Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs' FHA claims (including possible reasonable accommodation claims related to foreclosure/loan modification) were deemed waived on appeal and not analyzed by the court.
key_holding: The Tenth Circuit affirmed the dismissal of plaintiffs' RICO and fraud claims as time-barred, while plaintiffs' FHA and ECOA discrimination claims were deemed waived on appeal for failure to brief them.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs A.V. and Patricia Avington sued Bank of America and Merscorps alleging fraud, RICO violations, and racial discrimination under the FHA and ECOA related to foreclosure proceedings from 2008-2011. The Tenth Circuit affirmed dismissal of the RICO and fraud claims as time-barred, while declining to address the merits of plaintiffs' waived FHA/ECOA discrimination claims that were abandoned on appeal.
key_cases_cited: Dummar v. Lummis, 543 F.3d 614 (10th Cir. 2008)
Cory v. Aztec Steel Bldg., Inc., 468 F.3d 1226 (10th Cir. 2006)
Garrett v. Fleming, 362 F.3d 692 (10th Cir. 2004)
Yang v. Archuleta, 525 F.3d 925 (10th Cir. 2008)
Miller ex rel. S.M. v. Bd. of Educ. of Albuquerque Pub. Schs., 565 F.3d 1232 (10th Cir. 2009)

case_name: George Friedel v. Sun Communities, Inc.
citation: 747 F. App'x 775 (11th Cir. 2018) [Friedel I referenced]; No. 20-12275 (11th Cir. Aug. 24, 2021) [current appeal unpublished]
accommodation_description: George Friedel requested that Sun Communities and Park Place allow him to keep his golden retriever Maggie, an emotional support animal for his depression, despite the community's rule prohibiting dangerous or aggressive animals.
key_holding: The eviction notice issued October 18, 2017 was motivated by the jury verdict in Friedel I, not George Friedel's disability, and the requested accommodation of keeping Maggie was not reasonable because a prior jury found Maggie posed a direct threat to other residents.
brief_summary: George and Kathleen Friedel appealed the dismissal of their second lawsuit against Sun Communities and Park Place Community, LLC regarding their emotional support dog Maggie, after a prior jury found the dog posed a direct threat. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, finding that the eviction notice was issued the day after the first trial ended (not because of disability) and that the requested accommodation was not reasonable on its face given the jury's direct-threat finding.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Hunt v. Aimco Properties, L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Alice Hicks v. Thomas Skaar
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves claim that landlord declined to renew lease based on plaintiff's race (African American).
key_holding: Summary judgment was appropriate because plaintiff failed to raise a genuine issue of material fact that the defendants' proffered reason for nonrenewal (her repeated, unsubstantiated, racially-tinged complaints) was pretextual for race discrimination.
brief_summary: Alice Hicks, an African American tenant, sued her landlord and property management company alleging race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after they declined to renew her apartment lease. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, finding Hicks failed to raise a genuine issue that the landlord's proffered reason for nonrenewal (her repeated unsubstantiated complaints about neighbors) was pretextual for discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ave. 6E Invs., LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 5125333_2021_City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo _ Company_ca9
case_name: City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo & Company
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it is a municipal government's claim that Wells Fargo's discriminatory mortgage lending practices caused economic harm to the city through reduced property tax revenue and increased municipal expenditures.
key_holding: The en banc court held that Oakland's claimed economic injuries from Wells Fargo's discriminatory lending were too attenuated to establish proximate cause under the FHA—the 'ripples of harm' flowing from discriminatory loans to minority borrowers to higher foreclosure rates to depressed property values to reduced tax revenue and increased municipal expenditures traveled too far beyond the alleged misconduct to satisfy the direct-relation standard articulated in Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami.
brief_summary: The City of Oakland sued Wells Fargo alleging that the bank's discriminatory mortgage lending practices against African-American and Latino borrowers caused higher default and foreclosure rates, which in turn depressed property values and resulted in lost property tax revenue and increased municipal expenditures for blight remediation. The en banc Ninth Circuit reversed the district court's denial of Wells Fargo's motion to dismiss, holding that Oakland's economic injuries were too remotely caused by the discriminatory lending to satisfy the FHA's proximate cause requirement, applying the direct-relation standard from Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami.
key_cases_cited: Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Holmes v. Securities Investor Protection Corp., 503 U.S. 258 (1992)
Lexmark International, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Bridge v. Phoenix Bond & Indemnity Co., 553 U.S. 639 (2008)

case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement District
citation: SW. FAIR HOUSING V. MARICOPA DOMESTIC WATER (9th Cir. 2021)
race_if_mentioned: African American, Native American, Hispanic, White
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it challenges a water utility security deposit policy as having disparate impact on racial and familial status protected classes.
key_holding: Although the district court erred in its prima facie analysis, the Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the District because it established that the security deposit policy served legitimate business interests and the plaintiffs failed to demonstrate equally effective, less discriminatory alternatives; the disparate treatment claim also failed for lack of evidence of discriminatory animus.
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council and two public housing tenants sued Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement District challenging a policy requiring a $180 security deposit from new public housing customers (who are disproportionately African American, Native American, and Hispanic) while non-public housing customers paid only $55. The Ninth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the District, holding that although the district court erred in its prima facie analysis, the District established legitimate business justifications for the policy and plaintiffs failed to show equally effective less discriminatory alternatives.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Wards Cove Packing Co. v. Aida, 490 U.S. 642 (1989)
Watson v. Fort Worth Bank & Trust, 487 U.S. 977 (1988)
Hardie v. County of RDC, 876 F.3d 312 (9th Cir. 2017)
Ave. 6E Invs., LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Cynthia Gatling v. Jubilee Housing, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user tenant requested that defendant repair or replace an unsafe wheelchair ramp and inoperable wheelchair lift to provide safe building access.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the Rehabilitation Act failure-to-accommodate claim because factual questions remained about whether plaintiff's November 2020 request constituted a new accommodation request or a request for reconsideration, potentially restarting the statute of limitations, while dismissing plaintiff's DCHRA design-and-construction claim because the applicable provisions apply only to dwellings first occupied after 1999 and the Mozart was built in the 1930s.
brief_summary: Cynthia Gatling, a wheelchair user residing at the Mozart apartment building owned by Jubilee Housing, sued alleging the wheelchair ramp and lift were unsafe and inoperable, causing her to fall. She brought claims under the Rehabilitation Act, DCHRA, and for breach of contract. The court dismissed most claims including the DCHRA design/construction claim (building predates applicable requirements), breach of contract, and breach of implied covenant of good faith, but allowed the Rehabilitation Act reasonable accommodation claim to proceed because factual disputes existed about when the accommodation was requested.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Owens-Hart v. Howard Univ.
Floyd v. Lee
Tobin v. Liberty Mutual Ins. Co.

case_name: McNeil v. Yale University et al
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege they were denied housing opportunities at same-sex fraternities because of their sex, as membership is restricted based on sex.
key_holding: Plaintiffs lack Article III standing to bring FHA claims because they failed to allege any concrete injury related to housing—the SAC contains no allegations that any plaintiff ever sought, inquired about, or expressed interest in housing at any defendant fraternity, only that they sought membership.
brief_summary: Female Yale students sued fraternities and their housing corporations alleging sex discrimination in membership practices that prevented them from accessing fraternity housing, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The Second Circuit affirmed the dismissal, holding that plaintiffs lacked Article III standing because they failed to allege any actual or imminent injury related to housing—they sought only fraternity membership, not housing, and never inquired about or expressed interest in housing opportunities.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 578 U.S. 330 (2016)
Katz v. Donna Karan Co., 872 F.3d 114 (2d Cir. 2017)
United States v. Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)

source_file: 5299117_2021_Dayton Veterans Residences v. Dayton Metro. Housing Auth._ca6
case_name: Dayton Veterans Residences Limited Partnership v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Freedom's Path requested that GDPM amend its Administrative Plan to allow GDPM to apply for VASH project-based vouchers on Freedom's Path's behalf to enable development of supportive housing for disabled homeless veterans.
key_holding: A reasonable jury could find that GDPM's refusal to amend its Administrative Plan to enable it to seek VASH vouchers deprived disabled veterans of an equal opportunity to access housing in the Dayton community available to non-disabled persons, making the accommodation necessary under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Freedom's Path, a developer seeking to build supportive housing for homeless disabled veterans, sued Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority under the FHA and ADA for refusing to amend its Administrative Plan to apply for VASH vouchers on Freedom's Path's behalf. The Sixth Circuit reversed the district court's sua sponte summary judgment for the housing authority, holding that a reasonable jury could find the accommodation was necessary to afford disabled veterans an equal opportunity to access housing near the VA campus in Dayton.
key_cases_cited: Smith & Lee Associates, Inc. v. City of Taylor, 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs, Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 6225633_2022_Karen C. Yeh Ho v. Valencia Isles Homeowners Association, Inc._ca11
case_name: Karen C. Yeh Ho v. Valencia Isles Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Nature of accommodation request is undeterminable; complaint dismissed on procedural grounds before substantive FHA analysis.
key_holding: The district court did not abuse its discretion in dismissing the complaint without prejudice for failure to timely respond to the motion to dismiss, where the plaintiff continued to file other documents and was properly warned of potential dismissal.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Karen Yeh-Ho appealed the dismissal without prejudice of her Fair Housing Act complaint against Valencia Isles Homeowners Association and related defendants; the Eleventh Circuit affirmed, holding the district court did not abuse its discretion in dismissing the complaint for the plaintiff's failure to timely respond to the motion to dismiss despite continued filings and proper notice.

source_file: 6342877_2022_Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development_dcd
case_name: Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought Tenant Protection Vouchers to relocate from substandard housing conditions, alleging racial discrimination in HUD's withholding of this assistance.
key_holding: The court lacked jurisdiction because intervening events—the transfer of the property to a new owner who had not received a Notice of Default—rendered plaintiffs' claims moot and made it impossible to grant effective relief, as HUD no longer had statutory authority to issue Tenant Protection Vouchers based on the prior owner's Notice of Default.
brief_summary: Tenants and a tenant association at Sandpiper Cove sued HUD alleging the agency violated the Fair Housing Act and Fifth Amendment by intentionally discriminating on the basis of race in withholding Tenant Protection Vouchers that would allow tenants to relocate from substandard housing conditions. The court dismissed the case for lack of jurisdiction, holding that the transfer of the property to a new owner who had not received a Notice of Default rendered plaintiffs' claims moot and stripped the court of authority to order the relief sought.
key_cases_cited: United States v. Sanchez-Gomez, 138 S. Ct. 1532 (2018)
City of Los Angeles v. Lyons, 461 U.S. 95 (1983)
Trudeau v. Fed. Trade Comm'n, 456 F.3d 178 (D.C. Cir. 2006)
Marino v. Nat'l Oceanic and Atmospheric Admin., No. 20-5151 (D.C. Cir. May 17, 2022)
Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better Env't, 523 U.S. 83 (1998)

source_file: 6433397_2022_Secretary, United States Department of Housing v. Welch_ca10
case_name: The Secretary, United States Department of Housing and Urban Development, on behalf of Denver Metro Fair Housing Center v. David B. Welch, et al.
accommodation_description: Not a reasonable accommodation case; the complaint alleged discrimination against families with children based on familial status, with respondents marketing the property as an 'adult condominium community' and making dwellings unavailable to families with children.
key_holding: The Tenth Circuit granted HUD's Petition for Enforcement of the administrative consent order, requiring respondents to pay $800 to the Denver Metro Fair Housing Center, adopt a non-discrimination policy, undergo fair housing training, and engage in affirmative advertising indicating families with children are welcome.
brief_summary: The Secretary of HUD, on behalf of the Denver Metro Fair Housing Center, petitioned the Tenth Circuit to enforce an administrative consent order against David B. Welch, John H. Welch, and Ruth E. Welch, who allegedly discriminated against families with children at their Westwood Cove Condominiums in Gunnison, Colorado. The court granted the enforcement petition, ordering respondents to comply with all terms of the consent order including monetary payment, adoption of non-discrimination policies, fair housing training, and affirmative advertising welcoming families with children.

case_name: Jean Paraison v. Nationstar Mortgage, LLC
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves claim of race/nationality discrimination in real estate auction transaction rather than reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court affirmed dismissal of Paraison's FHA discrimination claim because his allegations were conclusory and speculative, failing to establish any factual connection between his race/nationality and the real estate agent's statements advising him to back out of the deal.
brief_summary: Jean Paraison, an African American male with a Haitian accent, sued Nationstar Mortgage alleging the company's real estate agent attempted to dissuade him from completing a real estate purchase based on his race and national origin. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal, finding Paraison's discrimination allegations were conclusory and speculative under Twombly/Iqbal standards, failing to allege facts connecting his protected characteristics to the agent's conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 6455250_2022_Eddie Hatch, Jr. v. City of Milwaukee_ca7
case_name: Eddie L. Hatch, Jr. and Michelle Davis-Hatch v. City of Milwaukee
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged defendants gave unfair preference to a white developer over Black entrepreneurs in sale of city-owned commercial building.
key_holding: The appellate court vacated the dismissal with prejudice because the district court erred in not giving pro se plaintiffs at least one opportunity to amend their complaint to cure deficiencies in pleading intentional race-based discrimination under § 1982.
brief_summary: Black plaintiffs sued the City of Milwaukee and other defendants alleging racial discrimination in the sale of a city-owned commercial building under the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. § 1982. The district court dismissed with prejudice, finding the commercial property was not a "dwelling" under the FHA and plaintiffs failed to allege intentional race-based discrimination. The Seventh Circuit vacated and remanded, holding that pro se plaintiffs should have been given leave to amend to attempt to cure the pleading deficiencies.
key_cases_cited: Jones v. Alfred H. Mayer Co., 392 U.S. 409 (1968)
Shaikh v. City of Chicago, 341 F.3d 627 (7th Cir. 2003)
Becker v. Montgomery, 532 U.S. 757 (2001)

source_file: 6472320_2022_Gene Phillips v. Acacia on the Green Condo Ass'n_ca6
case_name: Gene B. Phillips; Babette R. Krause v. Acacia on the Green Condominium Association, Inc.; Scott D. Cohen; Richard N. Dettlebach; John F. Klein; James N. Kleinfelter; Diane E. Lombardy; Nina H. Rothman; Amy W. Wachs
accommodation_description: Two disabled residents requested permission to use personal gas grills on their patios despite the condominium association's rule prohibiting grills on patios and balconies.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed that Phillips's requested accommodation was not 'necessary' because she regularly walked to the pool next to the grilling area despite her arthritis, and Acacia offered an alternative accommodation; further, neither Phillips's nor Weiss's requested accommodation was 'reasonable' because permitting individual patio grills would impose significant administrative burdens on the association, including insurance liability risks.
brief_summary: Gene Phillips and Stephen Weiss, disabled residents of the Acacia on the Green Condominium Association in Lyndhurst, Ohio, sued the association after being denied permission to use personal gas grills on their patios, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Amendments Act. The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the association, holding that Phillips's request was not necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling because she regularly walked past the grilling area to reach the pool, and both residents' requests were not reasonable because permitting individual patio grills would impose significant administrative and insurance burdens on the condominium association.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Groner v. Golden Gate Gardens Apts., 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Mercer v. N.Y.C. Hous. Auth.
accommodation_description: NYCHA tenant with asthma sought accommodation from mold conditions in his apartment, allegedly requiring remediation as a disability-related accommodation.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed the denial of leave to amend because plaintiff waived any challenge to the dismissal of his disability discrimination claim by failing to meaningfully argue it on appeal, despite having raised the issue below.
brief_summary: Robert Mercer, a pro se public housing tenant with asthma allegedly triggered by mold in his NYCHA apartment, appealed the denial of leave to amend his complaint after the district court dismissed his FHAA disability discrimination claim. The Second Circuit affirmed, holding that Mercer waived any challenge to the dismissal by failing to meaningfully argue the disability discrimination claim on appeal, instead abandoning it to focus on procedural issues about case management deadlines.
key_cases_cited: McLeod v. Jewish Guild for the Blind, 864 F.3d 154 (2d Cir. 2017)
LoSacco v. City of Middletown, 71 F.3d 88 (2d Cir. 1995)
Bogle-Assegai v. Connecticut, 470 F.3d 498 (2d Cir. 2006)

case_name: Tony Staton v. Vesta Corporation
accommodation_description: Request for an apartment suitable for plaintiff's disability, suggesting plaintiff sought transfer to an accessible unit
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim because he failed to plead any factual content regarding the nature of his disability, what accommodation he sought, when he requested it, or how his current apartment fails to accommodate his disability, though the dismissal was without prejudice allowing plaintiff to re-plead.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Tony Staton sued Vesta Corporation alleging the defendant denied him an accessible apartment suitable for his disability under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss without prejudice, finding plaintiff's complaint failed to state a plausible housing discrimination claim because he provided no factual content about his disability, the accommodation sought, or how his current unit fails to accommodate his needs.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
FTC v. Compagnie de Saint-Gobain-Pont-a-Mousson, 636 F.2d 1300 (D.C. Cir. 1980)

source_file: 7229354_2015_Montano v. Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hospital, Inc._cacd
case_name: Montano v. Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hospital, Inc.
accommodation_description: Quadriplegic wheelchair user requested physical modifications including widened bathroom doorways, lever-style door handles, accessible sink fixtures, lighter doors, and temperature control to access his room and bathroom independently.
key_holding: The nursing home operator violated the FHA by failing to provide reasonable accommodations for a quadriplegic tenant's disability, including refusing to widen bathroom doorways, install accessible door handles and fixtures, and maintain adequate room temperature, while also failing to engage in the required interactive process with the resident.
brief_summary: Hector Montano, a quadriplegic wheelchair user residing at Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hospital since 2009, sued the nursing home operator for failing to accommodate his disability by refusing to modify his bathroom (widen doorways, install lever handles, adjust sink fixtures) and provide adequate room temperature. After a bench trial, the court found the facility violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to provide reasonable accommodations and failing to engage in the required interactive process, awarding $65,000 in compensatory damages, $10,000 in punitive damages, and injunctive relief requiring ADA compliance modifications.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assoc., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir.2003)
California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir.1994)
Jankowski Lee & Assoc. v. Cisneros, 91 F.3d 891 (7th Cir.1996)
Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir.2001)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir.1994)

source_file: 7229993_2015_Alboniga v. School Board of Broward County Florida_flsd
7230169_2015_United States v. East River Housing Corp._nysd
case_name: United States v. East River Housing Corp.
accommodation_description: Three complainants (Aaron, Eisenberg, Gilbert) requested permission to keep emotional support or service dogs in their apartments as reasonable accommodations for psychiatric disabilities (depression, PTSD, anxiety).
key_holding: The court granted the government's motion to strike East River's second affirmative defense (challenging HUD procedures) and denied East River's motions for partial summary judgment on res judicata grounds and to dismiss the pattern-or-practice claim, ruling that DHR's no-probable-cause determination was unreviewed and thus cannot have preclusive effect, and that the government adequately pled a pattern-or-practice claim under § 3614.
brief_summary: The United States government sued East River Housing Corporation, a Manhattan housing cooperative, alleging that it discriminated against three disabled residents by refusing to allow them to keep emotional support/service dogs as reasonable accommodations for their psychiatric disabilities, and retaliated against one resident by threatening to impose attorneys' fees as 'additional rent.' The court denied East River's motions for partial summary judgment (based on res judicata from an unreviewed state agency determination) and to dismiss the pattern-or-practice claim, and granted the government's motion to strike East River's affirmative defense challenging HUD's investigative procedures.
key_cases_cited: Kosakow v. New Rochelle Radiology Associates, P.C., 274 F.3d 706 (2d Cir. 2001)
Ste. Marie v. Riverside Memorial Cemetery, 650 F.2d 395 (2d Cir. 1981)
De-Cintio v. Westchester County Medical Center, 821 F.2d 111 (2d Cir. 1987)
Kirkland v. City of Peekskill, 828 F.2d 108 (2d Cir. 1987)
Mitchell v. Cellone-P.R. Properties, Inc., 389 F.3d 82 (3d Cir. 2004)

case_name: Donahue Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, an African-American tenant, alleges the landlord failed to intervene in co-tenant's racial harassment, creating a hostile housing environment.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss the FHA claims because the plaintiff failed to allege specific facts showing the landlord acted with intentional racial discrimination or failed to intervene on account of the plaintiff's race, as required to state a hostile housing environment claim under the FHA against a landlord for co-tenant harassment.
brief_summary: Donahue Francis, an African-American tenant with a Section 8 voucher, sued Kings Park Manor and its property manager alleging the landlord failed to intervene in racial harassment by a co-tenant who repeatedly used racial slurs and threats. The court granted the motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Act claims, holding that the plaintiff failed to adequately plead that the landlord acted with intentional racial discrimination or failed to intervene on account of the plaintiff's race, as required to state a hostile housing environment claim against a landlord for co-tenant harassment under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Neudecker v. Boisclair Corp.
Cain v. Rambert
Haber v. ASN 50th St. LLC

case_name: DeCambre v. Brookline Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for reasonable accommodation to exclude certain trust disbursements (car purchase, phone/cable bills, veterinary costs for cats as emotional support animals) from calculation of annual income for Section 8 eligibility
key_holding: The BHA's determination to count trust disbursements as income for Section 8 purposes was a reasonable application of HUD regulations, and there was no evidence the BHA acted in a discriminatory manner based on DeCambre's disability status; the court deferred to HUD's regulatory interpretation under Chevron principles.
brief_summary: Kimberly DeCambre, a disabled Section 8 participant, sued the Brookline Housing Authority alleging it unlawfully calculated her annual income by counting distributions from her special needs trust, and that this violated the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court denied her preliminary injunction motion and held that the BHA's income calculation was a reasonable interpretation of HUD regulations, rejecting her discrimination claims but remanding for further consideration of certain expense exclusions.
key_cases_cited: Gammons v. Mass. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Dev., 523 F. Supp. 2d 76 (D. Mass. 2007)
Finley v. City of Santa Monica, No. BS127077, 2011 WL 7116184 (Cal. Super. Ct. May 25, 2011)
Lewis v. Alexander, 685 F.3d 325 (3d Cir. 2012)
Sullivan v. County of Suffolk, 174 F.3d 282 (2d Cir. 1999)
Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Res. Def. Council, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984)

case_name: Mosdos Chofetz Chaim, Inc. v. Village of Wesley Hills
accommodation_description: Religious institutions and affiliated individuals allege municipalities used environmental review laws as pretext to block development of Kiryas Radin, an Orthodox Jewish religious school and community center.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs failed to establish discriminatory intent or that they were selectively treated compared to similarly-situated development projects, as defendants were unaware of comparator sites and had legitimate environmental concerns.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs, Orthodox Jewish religious institutions and individuals, sued four New York municipalities and their officials alleging the Villages filed SEQRA-based litigation against Kiryas Radin, an adult student housing project affiliated with their community, based on discriminatory animus toward Orthodox Jews rather than legitimate environmental concerns. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiffs failed to establish discriminatory intent or that comparator development projects were treated more favorably, as defendants lacked knowledge of the comparator sites.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington v. Collet
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
LeBlanc-Sternberg v. Fletcher
LaTrieste v. Town of Oyster Bay
Chabad Lubavitch of Litchfield Cnty., Inc. v. Litchfield Historic Dist. Comm'n

case_name: Hoostein v. Mental Health Ass'n
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs (landlords) attempted to use FHA to compel MHA to continue providing Shelter Plus Care subsidy for Ms. Hoostein after her change in family size made her ineligible under the single-person eligibility requirement.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because plaintiffs are landlords seeking to use the FHA against their tenant, but the statute by its text does not provide landlords with a cause of action against tenants; the FHA provisions cited (§§ 3604(a-c), 3604(f)(1)(c), 3617) apply to potential landlords and property owners, not to the plaintiffs who own the property.
brief_summary: Michael Hoostein and Kathlyn Stein (as landlords) sued Mental Health Association Inc., Jerome Ray, the City of Northampton, and Margaret Keller alleging FHA discrimination and retaliation arising from MHA's decision to discontinue Ms. Hoostein's Shelter Plus Care subsidy after she had a child and was no longer a single individual. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that the FHA claims failed because plaintiffs, as landlords, cannot state a claim under the FHA's anti-discrimination provisions against their own tenant, and the court declined supplemental jurisdiction over remaining state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal

source_file: 7230918_2015_Holy Ghost Revival Ministries v. City of Marysville_wawd
case_name: Holy Ghost Revival Ministries v. City of Marysville
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs operate group housing for released convicts, some recovering from substance abuse; they submitted a reasonable accommodation request to permit occupancy in a commercially-zoned area.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claims for failure to state a claim because the amended complaint failed to allege discriminatory purpose—the City officials' emails attached to the complaint showed their concern was based on the population being registered sexual offenders, not on disability or religion.
brief_summary: Holy Ghost Revival Ministries and its pastors sued the City of Marysville challenging zoning enforcement actions against their group housing residences for released convicts, alleging discrimination based on religion and disability under the FHA, RLUIPA, and constitutional provisions. The court granted in part Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs failed to adequately plead discriminatory purpose since the City's emails showed its enforcement concern was based on housing for registered sexual offenders, not on Plaintiffs' religious beliefs or residents' disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Middlesex County Sewerage Authority v. National Sea Clammers Association, 453 U.S. 1 (1981)
Blessing v. Freestone, 520 U.S. 329 (1997)

source_file: 7231034_2015_Fairchild Semiconductor Corp. v. Power Integrations, Inc._ded
7231257_2015_Palm Partners, LLC v. City of Oakland Park_flsd
case_name: Palm Partners, LLC v. City of Oakland Park
accommodation_description: Request for zoning accommodation to either treat the proposed behavioral health facility as an existing non-conforming use in the Community Facilities district or grant conditional use approval as a hospital.
key_holding: The requested accommodation was not reasonable because the zoning rule prohibiting residential use of property zoned for non-residential use and designated as a non-receiving site is essential to the City's zoning scheme, and granting the accommodation would fundamentally alter that scheme; the City had no obligation to provide preferential treatment when non-disabled individuals have no greater opportunity for residential use of the Property.
brief_summary: Palm Partners, LLC, a behavioral and mental health treatment facility operator, sued the City of Oakland Park alleging the City violated the ADA and FHA by denying its conditional use application and reasonable accommodation request to develop a residential treatment facility in a Community Facilities zoning district. The court granted summary judgment for the City, finding no evidence of discriminatory animus and concluding that the accommodation request was not reasonable because granting it would fundamentally alter the City's zoning scheme by requiring the City to waive residential density requirements for a non-receiving site.
key_cases_cited: Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Caron Found. of Florida, Inc. v. City of Delray Beach, 879 F. Supp. 2d 1353 (S.D. Fla. 2012)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Germain v. M & T Bank Corp.
accommodation_description: Commercial real estate loan applications submitted by Muslim Albanian investors allegedly denied based on religion and national origin rather than creditworthiness.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claims because Plaintiffs failed to adequately plead that Defendants had knowledge of Plaintiffs' religion or that discriminatory intent was a motivating factor in denying loan discussions, as Plaintiffs' own allegations suggested legitimate, non-discriminatory reasons for Walz's conduct.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs David Germain, Selim Zherka (a Muslim of Albanian descent), and associated business entities sued M & T Bank alleging the bank discriminated against them in commercial lending based on religion and national origin, including claims that a bank officer cancelled a loan meeting upon learning of Zherka's identity and made anti-Muslim statements. The court dismissed the FHA claims without prejudice, finding the complaint failed to adequately plead that the bank had knowledge of Zherka's religion or that discriminatory animus was a but-for cause of the lending decisions, as the allegations suggested Walz's conduct was based on Zherka's notoriety rather than protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 7232003_2015_Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago v. Prewitt_ilnd
case_name: Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago v. Prewitt
accommodation_description: Fair housing test by deaf individual using IP Relay service was met with hung-up call, failure to provide apartment showing information, and email stating the apartment was not 'handicapped safe.'
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, holding that the plaintiff adequately pleaded Fair Housing Act violations by alleging the defendant made statements to a deaf tester indicating a preference against disabled tenants and misrepresented housing availability based on disability.
brief_summary: Access Living of Metropolitan Chicago, a fair housing organization, sued Bill Prewitt d/b/a Best Rents after a fair housing test revealed that a deaf tester calling via IP Relay was hung up on, told information was 'all online,' not offered an apartment showing, and sent an email stating the apartment was not 'handicapped safe,' while a non-disabled tester received full information and an apartment showing invitation. The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding the complaint adequately pleaded violations of the Fair Housing Act's prohibitions on discriminatory statements and misrepresentation of housing availability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
White v. U.S. Dept. of Housing & Urban Dev., 475 F.3d 898 (7th Cir. 2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

source_file: 7232139_2015_Viens v. America Empire Surplus Lines Ins._ctd
case_name: Viens v. America Empire Surplus Lines Insurance Co.
accommodation_description: Insurance underwriting practices that allegedly penalize landlords for renting to Section 8 voucher holders — this is not a reasonable accommodation request but rather a challenge to insurance discrimination practices.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiffs (landlords and fair housing organization) have standing to challenge insurance underwriting criteria that allegedly discriminate against Section 8 tenants based on lawful source of income and have a disparate impact on racial minorities.
brief_summary: Landlords and the Connecticut Fair Housing Center sued an insurance company alleging that its underwriting criteria charging higher premiums or denying coverage to landlords who rent to Section 8 tenants violates the Fair Housing Act (disparate impact on racial minorities) and Connecticut Fair Housing Act (lawful source of income discrimination). The court denied the motion to dismiss, holding that disparate impact claims are cognizable under the FHA, insurance qualifies as a residential real-estate-related transaction, and plaintiffs have standing to bring these claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
Olmstead v. L.C.

case_name: Austin v. Town of Farmington
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs request that the Town not require removal of the above-ground pool and protective fence when their disabled son no longer resides at the property or upon sale of the home.
key_holding: The Town's condition requiring plaintiffs to remove the pool and fence when their disabled son no longer resides there or upon sale does not violate the FHA because the restoration requirement applies equally to non-disabled homeowners, plaintiffs failed to allege discriminatory intent, and the Town properly granted the variance recognizing the FHA requirements.
brief_summary: Colleen and John Austin sued the Town of Farmington, New York seeking to enjoin enforcement of a variance condition requiring removal of an above-ground pool and fence they installed for their son with cerebral palsy upon sale of their home or when their son no longer resides there. The court granted the Town's motion to dismiss, finding that the restoration requirement does not violate the FHA because it applies equally to all homeowners, plaintiffs failed to allege discriminatory intent, and the Town properly accommodated the disability by granting the variance itself.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp.
Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of Cherry Hill
Huntington Branch, N.A.A.C.P. v. Town of Huntington
Cason v. Rochester Housing Authority

source_file: 7232353_2015_County of Cook v. Wells Fargo _ Co._ilnd
case_name: County of Cook v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it involves a county government's challenge to mortgage lending practices (reverse redlining) under the FHA's general anti-discrimination provisions.
key_holding: The district court dismissed Cook County's FHA reverse redlining claim because the county lacks statutory standing, as its alleged injuries (urban blight and reduced property tax base) are purely derivative of harm to minority borrowers and fall outside the zone of interests the FHA was designed to protect.
brief_summary: Cook County sued Wells Fargo alleging the lender engaged in reverse redlining by steering minority borrowers into subprime loans, causing concentrated foreclosures and urban blight in minority neighborhoods. The court dismissed the case, holding that Cook County lacks statutory standing under the FHA's zone-of-interests test because its injuries are purely derivative of harm to the minority borrowers, not the type of injury the FHA was designed to protect.
key_cases_cited: Thompson v. North American Stainless, LP, 562 U.S. 170 (2011)
Lexmark International, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014)
Gladstone Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

source_file: 7232396_2015_County of Westchester v. United States Department of Housing _ Urban Development_nysd
case_name: County of Westchester v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it is an APA challenge by a county government to HUD's withholding of CPD grant funds based on the County's failure to produce an adequate Analysis of Impediments to fair housing, including race-based impediments.
key_holding: HUD acted within its discretion and lawfully withheld CPD Funds from the County because the County failed to provide accurate certifications and adequate Analyses of Impediments to fair housing as required by federal law, and HUD's demands for amendments to address race-based impediments did not violate statutory limitations in 42 U.S.C. §§ 12705 and 12711.
brief_summary: The County of Westchester sued HUD under the APA, challenging HUD's withholding of Community Planning and Development Formula Grant Funds for fiscal years 2011, 2013, and 2014 because the County failed to provide adequate Analyses of Impediments to fair housing that addressed race-based impediments. The court granted HUD's motion for summary judgment, holding that HUD acted lawfully in withholding the funds and that HUD's demands for amendments to the County's Analysis of Impediments did not violate statutory limitations in the HOME program statute.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
United States ex rel. Anti-Discrimination Ctr. of Metro N.Y., Inc. v. Westchester Cnty., 668 F. Supp. 2d 548 (S.D.N.Y. 2009)

case_name: Chavez v. Aber
accommodation_description: Request to waive the duplex's no-pet policy to allow plaintiff Yvonne Chavez's minor son to keep a mixed-breed pit bull as an emotional support animal for his mental health disabilities.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs sufficiently pleaded that defendants had knowledge of the minor son's disability, that the emotional support animal accommodation request was reasonable, and that defendants directly and constructively denied the request by engaging in a campaign of harassment including eviction proceedings and Animal Control complaints.
brief_summary: Yvonne Chavez sued her landlord Dick Aber and Fairview Court, LLC alleging they discriminated against her minor son M.C. by refusing to accommodate his mental health disability with an emotional support animal (a pit bull named Chato), and retaliated against her for requesting the accommodation. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pleaded the elements of a reasonable accommodation denial claim and a retaliation claim under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc.
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House Inc.
Groome Res. v. Parish of Jefferson

source_file: 7234036_2015_County of Cook v. HSBC North America Holdings Inc._ilnd
case_name: County of Cook v. HSBC North America Holdings Inc.
accommodation_description: N/A - This is a discriminatory lending case challenging racially discriminatory mortgage lending practices, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court held that Cook County established Article III standing to bring FHA claims based on injuries from discriminatory lending practices that caused foreclosures in minority communities, and adequately stated claims for intentional discrimination and disparate impact.
brief_summary: Cook County sued HSBC and its subsidiaries alleging that Defendants intentionally targeted minority borrowers for predatory subprime mortgage loans, resulting in thousands of foreclosures that caused economic and noneconomic harm to the County. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that the County had standing to pursue its Fair Housing Act claims and adequately stated claims for intentional discrimination and disparate impact under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmmy. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman

source_file: 7234061_2015_Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n, Inc._flsd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n, Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request - case involves policies allegedly discriminating against families with children (report card requirement, attire rule, loitering rule, curfew rule)
key_holding: The court granted partial summary judgment for plaintiffs on the Loitering and Curfew Rules under § 3604(b) and (c), finding they facially discriminate against children without legitimate non-discriminatory justification, but denied summary judgment on the Report Card Requirement and Proper Attire Rule because genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether those policies discriminated based on familial status.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches and residents of Sonoma Bay and Marsh Harbour condominiums sued the HOA defendants alleging familial status discrimination based on policies requiring children's report cards, dress codes for children, no-loitering restrictions, and curfews for minors. The court granted partial summary judgment for plaintiffs on the curfew and loitering rules under § 3604(b) and (c), finding them facially discriminatory without legitimate justification, but denied summary judgment on the report card requirement and proper attire rule, finding genuine disputes of material fact requiring trial resolution.
key_cases_cited: Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Community House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Iniestra v. Cliff Warren Inv., Inc., 886 F.2d 1161 (C.D. Cal. 2012)
Pack v. Fort Washington II, 689 F.2d 1287 (E.D. Cal. 2009)
Fair Hous. Congress v. Weber, 993 F.2d 1286 (C.D. Cal. 1997)

source_file: 7234161_2015_Congregation Rabbinical College of Tartikov, Inc. v. Village of Pomona_nysd
case_name: Congregation Rabbinical College of Tartikov, Inc. v. Village of Pomona
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenge zoning laws restricting educational institutions and dormitories, alleging discriminatory effect on Orthodox Jewish community seeking to build rabbinical college with family housing
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on Plaintiffs' FHA disparate impact and equal protection claims regarding zoning laws restricting dormitories and educational institutions, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding discriminatory intent and whether the laws have a discriminatory effect on the Orthodox Jewish community.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs, an Orthodox Jewish congregation and affiliated rabbis, sued the Village of Pomona challenging zoning and wetlands laws that restrict educational institutions, dormitories, and development, alleging violations of RLUIPA, the Fair Housing Act, and equal protection. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on free speech and state common law claims, granted plaintiffs' motion for sanctions due to spoliation of evidence, but denied all parties' summary judgment motions on the FHA and equal protection claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Tenafly Eruv Ass'n v. Borough of Tenafly, 309 F.3d 144 (3d Cir. 2002)
Cornell Univ. v. Bagnardi, 68 N.Y.2d 583 (1986)
Congregation Mischkots Lavier Yakov, Inc. v. Bd. of Tr. of Vill. of Airmont

source_file: 7234475_2015_Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n, Inc._flsd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n, Inc.
accommodation_description: Policies targeting families with children including report card requirements for applicants with minors, proper attire rules for minors, no-loitering rules, and curfew rules for residents under 18
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the property management company on the report card requirement claim because Emanuel's purely ministerial role in processing applications was insufficient to establish personal liability, but denied summary judgment on the Proper Attire, Loitering, and Curfew Rules claims because genuine disputes of fact existed regarding whether Emanuel personally enforced those discriminatory policies.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center sued the HOA and its property management company alleging that policies requiring report cards for minor applicants and rules restricting children's attire, loitering, and curfew discriminated against families with children in violation of the FHA. The court granted summary judgment for the property management company on the report card requirement claim (finding insufficient personal involvement) but denied summary judgment on the other rules claims (finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding enforcement).
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Sabal Palm Condos. of Pine Island Ridge Ass'n, Inc. v. Fischer, 6 F. Supp. 3d 1272 (S.D. Fla. 2014)
Dillon v. AFBIC Dev. Corp., 597 F.2d 556 (5th Cir. 1979)

case_name: Johnson v. Macy
citation: No reporter citation provided (C.D. Cal. unpublished order)
accommodation_description: Landlord allegedly pressured disabled tenant to relocate to a first-floor unit and attempted to evict her because of her disability, making discriminatory statements about her medical conditions
key_holding: The court found plaintiff demonstrated a strong likelihood of success on her disability discrimination claims because defendant Macy's own written notes—mentioning Johnson's 'medical problems,' suggesting her life would be 'easier' in a downstairs apartment, and referencing her body 'not getting better'—constitute direct evidence of discriminatory intent to steer and evict a disabled tenant, and defendant's shifting justifications for rent increases and eviction notices raised serious questions of pretext.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Norma Johnson sued her private landlord Elaine Macy alleging disability discrimination under the FHA, claiming Macy repeatedly pressured her to relocate from her second-floor unit and issued discriminatory eviction notices because of her knee and neck injuries. The court granted Johnson's motion for preliminary injunction, finding she established a strong likelihood of success on the merits based on direct evidence in Macy's own notes showing discriminatory intent to evict a disabled tenant, and rejecting Macy's pretextual justifications.
key_cases_cited: Community Services Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Pack v. Fort Washington II, 689 F. Supp. 2d 1237 (E.D. Cal. 2009)
Fair Housing Congress v. Weber, 993 F. Supp. 1286 (C.D. Cal. 1997)
Gilligan v. Jamco Dev. Corp., 108 F.3d 246 (9th Cir. 1997)
Lowe v. City of Monrovia, 775 F.2d 998 (9th Cir. 1985)

source_file: 7235405_2016_Louis ex rel. G.A.H. v. New York City Housing Authority_nysd
case_name: Mesline Louis ex rel. G.A.H. v. New York City Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request that NYCHA provide meaningful assistance in locating suitable accessible housing and grant emergency transfer as reasonable accommodations under Section 8 tenant-based program
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' ADA Title II claims because Section 8 is a tenant-based voucher program that does not require NYCHA to provide modified housing or substantive additional benefits, and plaintiffs failed to adequately allege they were denied meaningful access to the program because of their disabilities.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Mesline Louis and her children sued NYCHA alleging it failed to provide reasonable accommodations in administering their Section 8 voucher, including modified housing and meaningful assistance in locating suitable accessible housing. The court granted NYCHA's motion to dismiss, holding that Section 8 is a tenant-based program providing vouchers for private housing that does not obligate NYCHA to supply modified housing, and plaintiffs failed to adequately allege they were denied meaningful access to the program because of their disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Alexander v. Choate, 469 U.S. 287 (1985)
Henrietta D. v. Bloomberg, 331 F.3d 261 (2d Cir. 2003)
Wright v. Giuliani, 230 F.3d 543 (2d Cir. 2000)
Taylor ex rel. Wazyluk v. Hous. Auth. of City of New Haven, 645 F.3d 152 (2d Cir. 2011)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Pelot v. Criterion 3, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged retaliation for complaining about housing practices (failure to exclude sex offenders) that he believed endangered women; no disability accommodation was requested
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaints about defendant's failure to conduct background checks on sex offenders did not constitute protected activity under the Fair Housing Act because such complaints relate to habitability rather than availability of housing, and even if the reasonable belief standard applied, plaintiff's belief that such practices violated the FHA was unreasonable under Fifth Circuit precedent.
brief_summary: Justin Bradley Pelot sued Criterion 3, LLC and Gary Conner for evicting him and terminating his employment after he wrote a letter complaining that Criterion's failure to conduct background checks on sex offenders endangered female residents at The Pointe apartments. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiff's retaliation claim under § 3617 failed because his complaints related to habitability rather than availability of housing and, under Fifth Circuit precedent, his belief that such practices violated the Fair Housing Act was not reasonable.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cox v. City of Dallas, Tex., 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)
Hidden Village, LLC v. City of Lakewood, Ohio, 734 F.3d 519 (6th Cir. 2013)
McZeal v. Ocwen Financial Corp., 252 F.3d 1355 (5th Cir. 2001)

case_name: West v. DJ Mortgage, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves sexual harassment by property manager Gene Andrews who grabbed plaintiff's genitals, demanded nude photos, and conditioned repairs on sexual favors.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding that West sufficiently alleged hostile housing environment sexual harassment, quid pro quo sexual harassment, and unlawful interference under the FHA, where the property manager grabbed her genitals, demanded nude photos in exchange for repairs, and the landlord failed to address complaints before evicting her.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Kayla West, a single mother of four, sued DJ Mortgage LLC and its property manager Gene Andrews for sexual harassment under the FHA, alleging Andrews grabbed her genitals when she signed her lease, demanded nude photos in exchange for repairs, and conditioned housing benefits on sexual favors. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding West adequately pleaded hostile housing environment sexual harassment, quid pro quo harassment, and unlawful interference claims.
key_cases_cited: Mendoza v. Borden, Inc., 195 F.3d 1238 (11th Cir. 1999)
Tagliaferri v. Winter Park Hous. Auth., 486 Fed. Appx. 771 (11th Cir. 2012)
Quigley v. Winter, 598 F.3d 938 (8th Cir. 2010)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Schwarz v. Villages Charter School, Inc.
accommodation_description: Thirty-two deaf residents of The Villages retirement community requested sign language interpreters as auxiliary aids to access recreational and educational programs offered by the Districts and Charter School.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the Districts on all claims, finding they were not 'dwellings' under the FHA and lacked public accommodation status; the court denied the Charter School's motion regarding whether its Deaf Network Services program constitutes a reasonable accommodation, finding genuine issues of material fact exist.
brief_summary: Thirty-two deaf residents of The Villages retirement community sued the Village Center and Sumter Landing Community Development Districts and The Villages Charter School, Inc. under the ADA, FHA, and Rehabilitation Act, alleging failure to provide sign language interpreters for recreational and educational programs. The court granted summary judgment for the Districts on all claims, finding they were not dwellings under the FHA and lacked public accommodation status; the court denied the Charter School's summary judgment motion on the reasonable accommodation claim regarding its Deaf Network Services program, finding genuine disputes of material fact.
key_cases_cited: Chevron U.S.A. Inc. v. Natural Res. Defense Council Inc.
Onishea v. Hopper
Edison v. Douberly

source_file: 7236712_2016_Lowman v. Platinum Property Management Services, Inc._gand
case_name: Lowman v. Platinum Property Management Services, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendant denied his rental application and imposed different qualification standards based on his race.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff sufficiently alleged his application was rejected (satisfying the third element of a prima facie FHA case) and that subsequent approval of the application does not negate a prior denial for purposes of establishing a violation.
brief_summary: Eleanor Lowman, an African American male, sued Platinum Property Management Services alleging the company denied his rental application and subjected him to different qualification standards because of his race. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff sufficiently pled a prima facie case under the Fair Housing Act despite defendant's argument that the subsequent approval of the application negated any prior denial.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev. v. Blackwell

case_name: United States v. Noble Homes, Inc.
accommodation_description: This is a design-and-construction accessibility case; no individual accommodation request is at issue. The United States alleges defendants failed to design and construct covered multifamily dwellings with accessible features including accessible entrances, accessible routes, reinforcements for grab bars, and usable kitchens and bathrooms for wheelchair users.
