brief_summary: CFHC and the Arroyos sued CoreLogic alleging its CrimSAFE tenant screening product violated the FHA by disqualifying Mr. Arroyo based on arrest records without individualized assessment and refusing to provide his screening report to his conservator without power of attorney. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs stated claims for disparate treatment, disparate impact, and failure to accommodate under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Aff. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc.

source_file: 7253536_2019_Nat'l Ass'n of the Deaf v. Harvard Univ._dcd
7253788_2019_Poursaied v. Reserve at Research Park LLC_alnd
case_name: Shahnaz Poursaied v. Reserve at Research Park LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought a quiet, soundproof apartment as an accommodation for alleged noise sensitivity, which the court found was not a recognized disability and the requested accommodation was not reasonable.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because she failed to allege any facts establishing she is disabled within the meaning of the FHA, failed to show she requested a specific reasonable accommodation, and admitted the defendant offered her options (move out or wait for another unit) without refusing accommodation.
brief_summary: Shahnaz Poursaied, proceeding pro se, sued her landlord Reserve at Research Park LLC alleging the landlord violated the FHA by failing to accommodate her alleged noise sensitivity by providing a soundproof apartment. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts establishing she is disabled under the FHA, failed to request a specific reasonable accommodation, and admitted the defendant offered her options to address her concerns.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condominium Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Noah v. Assor
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to be free from sexual harassment by her landlord; no disability accommodation request was made in this case.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiff's § 3617 interference claim, finding that plaintiff's allegations plausibly stated quid pro quo and hostile environment sexual harassment claims sufficient to survive a motion to dismiss, while dismissing plaintiff's § 3604(f)(1)-(2) claims for failure to plead she was 'ready, willing, and able' to renew her lease.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Marlen Noah sued her former landlord Isaac Assor alleging pervasive sexual harassment in violation of the federal and Florida Fair Housing Acts, including quid pro quo harassment (conditioning lease renewal on sexual relations) and hostile environment harassment (unwanted propositions, trespass, stalking, and sexually explicit communications). The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss the § 3617 interference claim, finding plaintiff's allegations sufficient to state quid pro quo and hostile environment sexual harassment claims, but granted dismissal of the § 3604(f)(1)-(2) discrimination claims for failure to plead the 'ready, willing, and able' element.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lawrence v. Courtyards at Deerwood Ass'n, Inc., 318 F. Supp. 2d 1133 (S.D. Fla. 2004)
West v. DJ Mortg., LLC, 164 F. Supp. 3d 1393 (N.D. Ga. 2016)
Quigley v. Winter, 598 F.3d 938 (8th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Rayantha Favourite v. 55 Halley Street, Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; claims centered on race-based hostile housing environment and retaliation under §§ 3604(b) and 3617
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on plaintiff's race-based hostile housing environment and retaliation claims, finding plaintiff failed to establish discriminatory animus and the evidence showed race-neutral reasons for the noise complaints and lease termination proceedings.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rayantha Favourite, a Black cooperative shareholder of Guyanese descent, sued 55 Halley Street, Inc. and its board members alleging they created a race-based hostile housing environment and retaliated against her through noise complaints and lease termination proceedings. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiff failed to establish that defendants' actions were motivated by racial animus rather than legitimate concerns about noise disturbances.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc.
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett
Cain v. Rambert
United States v. Weisz

source_file: 7253955_2019_Diamond House of SE Idaho, LLC v. City of Ammon_idd
case_name: Diamond House of SE Idaho, LLC v. City of Ammon
accommodation_description: Operator of group foster home for children challenges city ordinance restricting such homes to R2-A multi-family zones, arguing it facially discriminates against families with children under the FHA.
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs demonstrated a likelihood of success on the merits because Ordinance 598 facially discriminates against families with children by restricting group foster homes to R2-A zones while permitting any number of unrelated adults to live together in R-1 zones, violating the Fair Housing Act's familial status protections.
brief_summary: Diamond House of SE Idaho, LLC and its manager Karen Tornkvist sued the City of Ammon, Idaho, challenging Ordinance 598 which restricted group foster homes to R2-A multi-family zones, arguing it facially discriminated against families with children in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted a preliminary injunction, finding plaintiffs established a likelihood of success because the ordinance facially discriminated by burdening homes with children while allowing unrelated adults to reside together in R-1 zones, with no justification offered by the city.
key_cases_cited: Children's Alliance v. City of Bellevue, 950 F. Supp. 1491 (W.D. Wash. 1997)
Cmty. House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2006)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)

source_file: 7254034_2019_Morris v. W. Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc._idd
case_name: Morris v. West Hayden Estates First Addition Homeowners Ass'n, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought to host a large-scale Christmas program at their home in the HOA-governed community and claimed the HOA's letter objecting to the program constituted religious discrimination.
key_holding: The court granted the HOA's motion for judgment as a matter of law because the January 2015 letter, read by an ordinary reader, did not demonstrate intentional religious discrimination—the Board revised the letter to remove discriminatory language, several Board members were practicing Christians, and the HOA lacked liability for third-party homeowners' threatening conduct.
brief_summary: Jeremy and Kristy Morris sued the West Hayden Estates HOA alleging the HOA violated the Fair Housing Act by sending a letter objecting to their Christmas program and by failing to prevent threats from neighboring homeowners, claiming religious discrimination. The court granted the HOA's Rule 50(b) motion for judgment as a matter of law, finding the evidence insufficient to support a jury verdict for the Morrises on their religious discrimination claims, and also ruled in favor of the HOA on its counterclaim finding the Morrises violated multiple CC&R provisions.
key_cases_cited: Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 917 F.3d 109 (2d Cir. 2019)

case_name: Wilson v. Guardian Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claims she was discriminated against because property manager Herb wanted her to vacate after a domestic violence incident involving her ex-boyfriend, and that repairs were not made to her unit and she was not offered alternative housing after a fire.
key_holding: Although the FHA does not expressly list domestic violence victims as a protected class, evicting female DV victims can constitute sex discrimination under the FHA; however, plaintiff failed to show a genuine dispute that she was treated differently because of her status as a DV victim or her sex, as the landlord's communications and failure to repair did not give rise to an inference of unlawful discrimination.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Randi Wilson sued Guardian Management LLC and property manager Kristine Herb alleging housing discrimination under the FHA based on her status as a domestic violence victim, claiming she was pressured to vacate her apartment after a DV incident and was denied repairs and alternative housing after a fire. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, holding that while domestic violence victim status can support a sex discrimination claim under the FHA, plaintiff failed to establish she was treated differently because of her DV victim status or sex, as the landlord's communications and repair decisions did not give rise to an inference of unlawful discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Bouley v. Young-Sabourin, 394 F. Supp. 2d 675 (D. Vt. 2005)
Dickinson v. Zanesville Metro. Hous. Auth., 975 F. Supp. 2d 863 (S.D. Ohio 2013)
Creason v. Singh, 2013 WL 6185596 (N.D. Cal. 2013)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Tex. Dep't of Cmty. Affairs v. Burdine, 450 U.S. 248 (1981)

case_name: Binti O. Mohamed v. Michael McLaurin
accommodation_description: NONE
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff failed to establish that Defendant's alleged harassment and non-renewal of the lease was motivated by discriminatory animus towards Plaintiff's national origin or gender, as Defendant introduced ample evidence of legitimate business reasons for his conduct including unauthorized occupants, pets, water damage, parking violations, and property damage.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Binti O. Mohamed, a Somali immigrant and Section 8 voucher holder, sued landlord Michael McLaurin alleging discrimination based on national origin, gender, religious, and familial status under the Fair Housing Act, claiming harassment and lease non-renewal. After a bench trial, the court granted judgment for Defendant, finding that Plaintiff failed to establish discriminatory intent and that Defendant had legitimate business reasons for his conduct including unauthorized occupants, property damage, and lease violations.
key_cases_cited: Hylton v. Friends of Mansion St. George
Gallagher v. 630 Real Estate Partners
Mojica v. Gannett Co.
Inland Mediation Bd. v. City of Pomona
Baker v. Waterford Square Homeowners Ass'n
Grady v. Affiliated Cent., Inc.

case_name: Elliott v. Versa CIC, L.P.
accommodation_description: Underlying case involves disability discrimination and reasonable accommodation claims by plaintiff Natsue Elliott with Alzheimer's disease; specific accommodation request not detailed in this procedural ruling.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion in limine to preclude nonplausible claims as an untimely disguised dispositive motion filed after the scheduling order deadline and untimely as a motion for reconsideration, though noting defendants' Section 3604(c) liability arguments regarding discriminatory statements should be resolved at trial.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Elliott sued Versa CIC, L.P. and ConAm Management Corporation alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disability discrimination and reasonable accommodation claims under Section 3604(f) and discriminatory statements under Section 3604(c). The court denied defendants' motion in limine to preclude nonplausible claims, finding it was an untimely disguised dispositive motion filed after the scheduling order deadline and also untimely as a motion for reconsideration filed five months after summary judgment.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Soules v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev.

source_file: 7361172_2022_The School of the Ozarks, Inc. v. Joseph Biden, Jr._ca8
case_name: College of the Ozarks, Inc. v. Biden
accommodation_description: The College challenges HUD's memorandum interpreting the Fair Housing Act to prohibit discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity, which the College argues would require changes to its single-sex biological dormitory housing policies.
key_holding: The College of the Ozarks lacks Article III standing because its alleged injury from the HUD memorandum interpreting the Fair Housing Act to prohibit gender identity and sexual orientation discrimination is too speculative—the memorandum is an internal directive that does not directly impose restrictions or penalties, and any enforcement action would depend on a highly attenuated chain of contingencies.
brief_summary: College of the Ozarks, a private Christian college in Missouri, appealed the dismissal of its pre-enforcement challenge to a HUD memorandum interpreting the Fair Housing Act to prohibit discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity, arguing the directive would require the College to change its single-sex biological dormitory housing policies. The Eighth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that the College lacked Article III standing because its alleged injury was too speculative—the internal directive did not directly impose restrictions or penalties, and any enforcement remained a conjectural chain of contingencies.
key_cases_cited: Bostock v. Clayton County, 140 S. Ct. 1731 (2020)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Susan B. Anthony List v. Driehaus, 573 U.S. 149 (2014)
Clapper v. Amnesty Int'l USA, 568 U.S. 398 (2013)
Abbott Lab'ys v. Gardner, 387 U.S. 136 (1967)

source_file: 7804109_2022_Sailboat Bend Sober Living v. City of Fort Lauderdale, FL._ca11
case_name: Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, Florida
accommodation_description: Request to waive the automatic fire sprinkler requirement and treat the property as a single-family dwelling under the Fire Code to avoid occupancy classification requirements.
key_holding: The requested accommodation was not 'necessary' under the FHA because plaintiffs failed to demonstrate a causal connection between their residents' disabilities (addiction) and their inability to afford higher rent to cover the $30,000-$40,000 sprinkler installation costs.
brief_summary: Sailboat Bend Sober Living, a for-profit sober living home for recovering addicts, sued the City of Fort Lauderdale under the FHA and ADA, challenging the Zoning Ordinance and Fire Code enforcement, and claiming failure to grant a reasonable accommodation from the sprinkler requirement. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding the zoning ordinance facially favored disabled persons, the accommodation request was not necessary because plaintiffs failed to establish a causal link between residents' addiction and inability to afford higher rent, and no evidence supported intentional discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc., 938 F.3d 1259
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Center, Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917
Bostock v. Clayton County, 140 S. Ct. 1731
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252

case_name: Krystal Theresa Davis v. Daniel B. Rubin; Shannon Leigh McGroarty
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request is described in the opinion; the case focuses on retaliation for reporting discrimination and general disability-based discrimination claims.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal because Davis failed to adequately plead disability discrimination (her own exhibit contradicted her allegation that defendants mocked her disability) and failed to establish a causal connection between her HUD complaint and the adverse actions, which were almost exclusively prior to her HUD filing.
brief_summary: Krystal Davis, a Section 8 tenant, sued her landlords alleging disability discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act after being evicted following complaints about mistreatment and subsequent filing of a HUD complaint. The Third Circuit affirmed the dismissal, finding that Davis failed to adequately plead disability discrimination (her exhibits contradicted her claims) and failed to establish causation for her retaliation claim since the adverse actions predated her protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Phillips v. County of Allegheny, 515 F.3d 224 (3d Cir. 2008)
United States ex rel. Bookwalter v. UPMC, 946 F.3d 162 (3d Cir. 2019)

source_file: 7854286_2022_Village Green at Sayville, LLC v. Town of Islip_ca2
case_name: Village Green at Sayville, LLC v. Town of Islip et al.
race_if_mentioned: Black and Hispanic communities disproportionately concentrated in Brentwood, Bay Shore, and Central Islip, while Sayville is over 90% non-Hispanic white
accommodation_description: Developer Village Green alleged the Town of Islip's covenants and restrictions requiring owner-occupied condominiums discriminatorily excluded minorities from the predominantly white hamlet of Sayville.
key_holding: The Second Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal for lack of ripeness, holding that the Town of Islip's official resolution stating the motion to approve Village Green's application 'fails for lack of second,' combined with the town attorney's statement that no further proceedings would be held, constituted a final decision sufficient to ripen the developer's land-use discrimination claims.
brief_summary: Village Green at Sayville, LLC, a real estate developer, sued the Town of Islip alleging that the town's refusal to modify covenants and restrictions on its property constituted racial and ethnic discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and other laws. The district court dismissed for lack of ripeness under Williamson County's final-decision requirement. The Second Circuit vacated and remanded, holding that the town's December 2016 resolution stating the motion to approve 'fails for lack of second,' combined with the town attorney's representation that no further proceedings would be held, constituted a final decision sufficient to ripen the claims.
key_cases_cited: Williamson County Regional Planning Commission v. Hamilton Bank, 473 U.S. 172 (1985)
Knick v. Township of Scott, 139 S. Ct. 2162 (2019)
Sunrise Detox V, LLC v. City of White Plains, 769 F.3d 118 (2d Cir. 2014)
Murphy v. New Milford Zoning Commission, 402 F.3d 342 (2d Cir. 2005)
Mhany Management, Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)

source_file: 7861582_2022_DeAnn Graham v. UMH IN Holiday Village, LLC_ca7
case_name: DeAnn Graham v. UMH IN Holiday Village, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep a dog as a registered emotional support animal for the plaintiff's daughter who has anxiety.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed that the Rooker-Feldman doctrine bars federal courts from exercising subject-matter jurisdiction over claims that seek review of state court judgments, including federal Fair Housing Act discrimination claims challenging the outcome of eviction proceedings.
brief_summary: DeAnn Graham, a Black tenant, sued her former landlord UMH IN Holiday Village, LLC in federal court after an Indiana state court entered an eviction judgment against her based on an alleged lease violation concerning her daughter's emotional support dog. She alleged Fair Housing Act claims for race and disability discrimination. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine, holding that federal courts lack subject-matter jurisdiction to review state court judgments even when the plaintiff asserts federal statutory rights.
key_cases_cited: Rooker v. Fidelity Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
D.C. Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus. Corp., 544 U.S. 280 (2005)
Holt v. Lake Cnty. Bd. of Comm'rs, 408 F.3d 335 (7th Cir. 2005)
Andrade v. City of Hammond, Indiana, 9 F.4th 947 (7th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 7864626_2015_People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals v. United States Department of Agriculture_cadc
8174441_2015_Walters v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Ass'n_vid
case_name: Walters v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Ass'n
accommodation_description: Request to waive the no-dogs policy to keep an emotional support animal for diagnosed anxiety disorder
key_holding: Walters's federal fair housing claims do not survive her death because the Virgin Islands survival statute requires physical injury, and Walters alleged only emotional distress, embarrassment, and humiliation; accordingly, the Association and Board are entitled to judgment as a matter of law on Count One (FHA reasonable accommodation), and the court declines supplemental jurisdiction over remaining state law claims.
brief_summary: Barbara Walters, a unit owner at Cowpet Bay West Condominium, sued the Association, Board, and individual defendants alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by denying her request to keep an emotional support animal for her anxiety disorder despite a no-dogs policy. The court granted summary judgment for the defendants on all federal claims, holding that Walters's fair housing claims did not survive her death under Virgin Islands tort survival law because she alleged only emotional distress, not physical injury.
key_cases_cited: Robertson v. Wegmann, 436 U.S. 584 (1978)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Regents of Mercersburg College v. Republic Franklin Ins. Co., 458 F.3d 159 (3d Cir. 2006)
Hall v. Hall, No. CV 2011-54 (D. V.I. Aug. 9, 2013)

source_file: 8205763_2022_Tonca Watters v. Homeowners Association at the_ca7
case_name: Tonca Watters and Terence Watters v. The Homeowners' Association at the Preserve at Bridgewater, Kathryn Mamaril, and Edward Mamaril
accommodation_description: Request for a six-foot privacy fence to mitigate Terence Watters' PTSD triggers from seeing dogs in the neighborhood
key_holding: The reasonable accommodation claim fails because Terence did not mention his PTSD in his accommodation request, and the HOA lacked knowledge of his PTSD despite being aware of his separate lung condition.
brief_summary: Tonca and Terence Watters, an African-American couple, sued the HOA and the Mamarils for race discrimination and failure to accommodate Terence's PTSD with a privacy fence. The court vacated summary judgment on the race discrimination claims against the Mamarils (finding sufficient evidence of racial animus), but affirmed dismissal of the failure to accommodate claim because the HOA lacked knowledge of Terence's PTSD.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Geraci v. Union Square Condo Ass'n, 891 F.3d 274 (7th Cir. 2018)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Fam. Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)
Linkletter v. Western & Southern Financial Group, Inc., 851 F.3d 632 (6th Cir. 2017)

source_file: 8241798_2022_Jamal Akbar v. Interstate Realty Management Company_ca7
case_name: Jamal A. Akbar v. Interstate Realty Management Company
accommodation_description: Akbar alleged Interstate denied his rental application because of his disability (muscular damage to left shoulder) and low income.
key_holding: The Seventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment because Akbar presented no evidence that Interstate discriminated based on disability—the undisputed evidence showed Interstate applies its income policy consistently to both disabled and non-disabled applicants, rejecting Akbar solely due to his failure to meet the income threshold.
brief_summary: Jamal Akbar sued Interstate Realty Management Company and Legends A-2, LLC, alleging that they discriminated against him based on disability and low income when they denied his application to rent a unit at their Chicago apartment complex. The Seventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for Interstate, finding that Akbar presented no evidence of disability-based discrimination because the undisputed record showed Interstate consistently applies its income requirement policy to all applicants regardless of disability status.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Khungar v. Access Cmty. Health Network, 985 F.3d 565 (7th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 8245021_2022_Steven Unger v. Majorca at Via Verde Homeowners Association Inc._ca11
case_name: Steven Unger v. Majorca at Via Verde Homeowners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with severe ankylosing spondylitis requested that the HOA either record its board meetings or provide transcripts of meeting minutes immediately after meetings so he could access community information despite being unable to attend meetings in person.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal because it erred in evaluating Majorca's alternative accommodations (Zoom access, designating a representative) before determining whether Unger's requested accommodations were facially reasonable under the Schaw framework, which requires courts to first assess whether a plaintiff's requested accommodation seems reasonable on its face before considering a defendant's counterproposals.
brief_summary: Steven Unger, who suffers from severe ankylosing spondylitis preventing him from attending homeowners association meetings, requested that Majorca record its meetings or provide immediate transcripts of meeting minutes as a reasonable accommodation under the FHA; when Majorca refused these requests but offered alternative accommodations, the district court dismissed his complaint. The Eleventh Circuit reversed, holding the district court erred by evaluating Majorca's alternative accommodations before determining whether Unger's requested accommodations were facially reasonable under the proper legal framework.
key_cases_cited: Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
U.S. Airways v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)

case_name: Geraci v. Union Square Condo. Ass'n
accommodation_description: Geraci filed an accommodation request with Union Square related to her PTSD diagnosis and dog (likely an emotional support animal request following an elevator incident involving dogs).
key_holding: The court affirmed because the litigation updates and open forum held by Union Square's Board did not constitute retaliatory conduct that would 'coerce, intimidate, threaten, or interfere with' a person of reasonable fortitude, and Union Square did not abuse its discretion by allowing expert testimony challenging whether Geraci had a qualifying handicap.
brief_summary: Holly Geraci sued Union Square Condominium Association alleging it failed to accommodate her PTSD and retaliated against her for seeking an accommodation by publishing litigation updates and holding an open forum. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the jury verdict for Union Square, holding that the Board's communications to co-owners did not constitute actionable retaliation under § 3617, and that the district court properly allowed expert testimony challenging whether Geraci had a qualifying handicap since she bore the burden of proof on that element.
key_cases_cited: Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)
Schneiker v. Fortis Ins. Co., 200 F.3d 1055 (7th Cir. 2000)
Dadian v. Vill. of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)
Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Stuhlmacher v. Home Depot U.S.A., Inc., 774 F.3d 405 (7th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Rodriguez v. Village Green Realty, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case involves refusal to rent and discriminatory statements about the tenant's disabled child based on disability
key_holding: The Second Circuit held that there was sufficient evidence from which a reasonable jury could conclude that A.R. was disabled under the FHA's 'actually disabled' or 'regarded as' test, that the 'ordinary listener' standard does not apply to § 3604(d) misrepresentation claims, and that § 3604(c) can be violated by statements targeted at an individual even if that individual is not disabled under the FHA.
brief_summary: Heidi and Juan Rodriguez sued Village Green Realty and agent Blanca Aponte alleging disability discrimination under the FHA after Aponte sent text messages indicating the prospective landlord would not rent to them because their minor child A.R. has autism and epilepsy. The Second Circuit vacated the district court's grant of summary judgment for defendants, holding that plaintiffs presented sufficient evidence that A.R. qualifies as disabled under the FHA and that Aponte's discriminatory statements could violate § 3604(c) and (d).
key_cases_cited: Toyota Motor Manufacturing, Kentucky, Inc. v. Williams, 534 U.S. 184 (2002)
Sutton v. United Air Lines, Inc., 527 U.S. 471 (1999)
School Bd. of Nassau Cty. v. Arline, 480 U.S. 273 (1987)
Ragin v. Harry Macklowe Real Estate Co., 6 F.3d 898 (2d Cir. 1993)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 8413947_2016_Mhany Management, Inc. v. County of Nassau_ca2
case_name: MHANY Management, Inc. v. County of Nassau
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it is a zoning discrimination case challenging Garden City's rezoning decision based on racial animus.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed that Garden City's decision to abandon multi-family R-M zoning in favor of single-family R-T zoning was made with discriminatory intent in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a), but vacated the disparate impact finding and remanded for further proceedings.
brief_summary: MHANY Management and NY Communities for Change sued Garden City and Nassau County alleging that Garden City's rezoning of a county-owned parcel from multi-family to single-family zoning, after residents expressed opposition to affordable housing, violated the Fair Housing Act based on discriminatory intent. After a bench trial, the district court found Garden City liable under § 3604(a) for disparate treatment. On appeal, the Second Circuit affirmed the discriminatory intent finding but vacated the disparate impact ruling and remanded for further proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Huntington Branch, NAACP v. Town of Huntington, 844 F.2d 926 (2d Cir. 1988)
United States v. City of Yonkers, 96 F.3d 600 (2d Cir. 1996)
Comer v. Cisneros, 37 F.3d 775 (2d Cir. 1994)

source_file: 8415085_2018_Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC_ca7
case_name: Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves tenant-on-tenant harassment based on sexual orientation and landlord's deliberate indifference to that harassment
key_holding: A landlord may be held directly liable under the Fair Housing Act when it has actual notice of severe tenant-on-tenant harassment based on a protected characteristic and is deliberately indifferent to that harassment, without requiring proof that the landlord itself acted with discriminatory animus.
brief_summary: Marsha Wetzel, an openly lesbian resident of Glen St. Andrew Living Community, sued the facility and its management after suffering severe verbal and physical harassment from other residents based on her sexual orientation while management remained deliberately indifferent despite repeated complaints. The Seventh Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that landlords may be held directly liable under the Fair Housing Act for failing to take reasonable steps to stop known tenant-on-tenant harassment based on a protected characteristic, without requiring proof of discriminatory animus against the landlord itself.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
Davis v. Monroe Cnty. Bd. of Educ., 526 U.S. 629 (1999)
Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Ellerth, 524 U.S. 742 (1998)
Alamo v. Bliss, 864 F.3d 541 (7th Cir. 2017)

source_file: 8415099_2018_De Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship_ca4
case_name: De Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. Partnership
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—case involves challenge to park's documentation-of-legal-status policy allegedly having disparate impact on Latino tenants.
key_holding: The Fourth Circuit held that plaintiffs sufficiently alleged robust causality between Waples' documentation policy and disparate impact on Latinos under Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities, reversing the district court's dismissal and remand for further proceedings.
brief_summary: Four Latino couples at a mobile home park sued the owner/operator challenging a policy requiring all adult occupants to provide documentation evidencing legal U.S. residence to renew leases, alleging it disproportionately displaced Latino families. The Fourth Circuit vacated the district court's summary judgment for defendants, holding that plaintiffs presented sufficient statistical evidence to establish a prima facie disparate-impact claim under the FHA and remanded for further proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Wards Cove Packing Co. v. Atonio, 490 U.S. 642 (1989)
Watson v. Fort Worth Bank & Tr., 487 U.S. 977 (1988)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case involves a landlord's alleged failure to address tenant-on-tenant racial harassment. Francis, a Black tenant, sued after a neighbor repeatedly called him racial slurs and made threats, and the landlord took no action despite multiple reports.
key_holding: The Second Circuit held that post-acquisition claims are cognizable under § 3604(b) and that a landlord may be liable under § 3617 for failing to take prompt action to address tenant-on-tenant racial harassment of which it knew or should have known and had the power to correct, reversing dismissal of plaintiff's FHA claims and remanding for further proceedings.
brief_summary: Black tenant Francis sued Kings Park Manor and its property manager (KPM Defendants) alleging they violated the FHA and civil rights statutes by failing to address racial harassment from a neighbor who repeatedly called Francis racial slurs and made death threats over a six-month period despite multiple reports to police and the landlord. The Second Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal of Francis's post-acquisition discrimination claims under §§ 3604(b) and 3617, held that landlords may be liable for third-party tenant harassment when they knew or should have known and had power to correct it, and remanded for further proceedings while affirming dismissal of other claims.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 8415395_2019_Curto v. A COUNTRY PLACE CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION_ca3
case_name: Curto v. A Country Place Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Diana Lusardi, who suffered strokes resulting in physical disabilities, sought to use pool therapy with her husband at the condominium's communal pool.
key_holding: The pool schedule violated § 3604(b) because although it provided roughly equal aggregate swimming time for men and women, it discriminated against women in its allotment of favorable times by assigning most evening hours and Friday afternoons to men, reflecting stereotypical gender assumptions about women's roles.
brief_summary: Marie Curto and Steve and Diana Lusardi, unit owners at a 55-and-over condominium community in Lakewood, New Jersey, sued the A Country Place Condominium Association alleging the pool schedule that segregated swimming hours by sex violated the FHA. The Third Circuit reversed summary judgment for the Association, holding that the schedule violated § 3604(b) because it assigned most favorable swimming times (evening and Friday afternoon hours) to men, discriminating against women despite roughly equal aggregate time allocations.
key_cases_cited: Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Jutrowski v. Twp. of Riverdale, 904 F.3d 280 (3d Cir. 2018)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515 (1996)
Mississippi University for Women v. Hogan, 458 U.S. 718 (1982)

source_file: 8415396_2019_Curto v. A COUNTRY PLACE CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION_ca3
case_name: Curto v. A Country Place Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Sex-segregated swimming pool schedule providing different hours for men and women at a condominium
key_holding: The condominium association's sex-segregated pool schedule is per se facially discriminatory under the FHA because it allocates unequal evening hours between men and women, perpetuating stereotypes about women's likelihood of working during those hours, and the association failed to produce sufficient evidence that integrating the pool would burden Orthodox Jewish residents' religious exercise.
brief_summary: Female residents sued A Country Place Condominium Association challenging a pool schedule that provided significantly fewer evening swimming hours for women than men. The Third Circuit reversed the district court's dismissal, holding that the sex-segregated pool schedule constitutes facial discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) because it perpetuates stereotypes about women's roles, and the association's justification based on accommodating Orthodox Jewish religious observances failed for lack of evidentiary support.
key_cases_cited: Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967)
Cmty. House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Healey v. Southwood Psychiatric Hospital, 78 F.3d 128 (3d Cir. 1996)
United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515 (1996)

source_file: 8415473_2019_Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co._ca5
case_name: Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Property Company
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it is a disparate impact claim alleging that Lincoln's policy of refusing to rent to Section 8 voucher holders has a discriminatory effect on African-American households in the Dallas area.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit denied rehearing en banc, leaving intact the panel majority's decision that required plaintiffs to meet an heightened pleading standard for disparate impact claims, effectively making it nearly impossible for the Inclusive Communities Project to proceed with its claim that Lincoln's 'no vouchers' policy perpetuates segregation.
brief_summary: Inclusive Communities Project, a fair housing nonprofit, sued Lincoln Property Company alleging that its policy of refusing to rent to Section 8 voucher holders violates the Fair Housing Act's disparate impact prohibition by disproportionately harming African-American households and perpetuating residential segregation in the Dallas area. The Fifth Circuit denied rehearing en banc, leaving intact the panel majority's decision affirming the district court's Rule 12(b)(6) dismissal based on an overly stringent interpretation of the Supreme Court's 'robust causality' requirement for disparate impact claims.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Property Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park L.P., 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 8442739_2016_City of Joliet v. New West, L.P._ca7
case_name: City of Joliet v. New West, L.P.
accommodation_description: This is a condemnation case, not a reasonable accommodation case; New West raised FHA disparate impact and disparate treatment claims based on the condemnation's effect on its predominantly Black tenants.
key_holding: The court affirmed the condemnation of Evergreen Terrace, finding no disparate treatment (the City acted for legitimate reasons unrelated to race) and no unjustified disparate impact (about 97% of Joliet's Black residents live elsewhere, so closure would not adversely affect the Black population as a whole; moreover, residents would benefit from new housing and vouchers).
brief_summary: The City of Joliet condemned the Evergreen Terrace housing complex (95% Black residents), and the owner New West appealed under the Fair Housing Act, arguing the condemnation would have a disparate impact on Black residents and was motivated by discriminatory intent. The Seventh Circuit affirmed, finding no discriminatory intent and no unjustified disparate impact because the closure would not affect a substantial fraction of Joliet's Black population, and residents would benefit from new housing and vouchers.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Beacon Theatres, Inc. v. Westover, 359 U.S. 500 (1959)
Dairy Queen, Inc. v. Wood, 369 U.S. 469 (1962)
Anderson v. Bessemer City, 470 U.S. 564 (1985)
Pullman-Standard v. Swint, 456 U.S. 273 (1982)

source_file: 8443343_2017_Affordable Recovery Housing v. City of Blue Island_ca7
case_name: Affordable Recovery Housing v. City of Blue Island
accommodation_description: Faith-based recovery home for men with substance use disorders seeking to operate in a Single Family Residential zone without installing a sprinkler system
key_holding: The City of Blue Island's sprinkler requirement did not substantially burden Affordable's religious exercise under RFRA because Affordable chose to begin operations without first researching applicable regulations, and the temporary expulsion of residents resulted from Affordable's own disregard of the fire safety code rather than religious persecution.
brief_summary: Affordable Recovery Housing sued the City of Blue Island challenging the city's requirement that it install a sprinkler system before operating a faith-based residential recovery home for men with substance use disorders in a single-family residential zone. The Seventh Circuit affirmed the district court judgment, finding that the temporary expulsion of residents resulted from Affordable's own failure to comply with municipal fire safety codes rather than any substantial burden on its religious exercise under the Illinois Religious Freedom Restoration Act.

case_name: Mason v. Adams County Recorder
accommodation_description: Plaintiff seeks injunctive relief to compel Ohio county recorders to stop publishing historical documents containing racially restrictive covenants and to permit inspection and redaction of such documents.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed dismissal for lack of standing because plaintiff failed to allege a particularized injury—he made only a generalized grievance about government conduct that could be shared by all Ohio residents, and his subjective feelings of being unwelcome by reading historical covenants do not constitute a concrete, particularized injury traceable to the county recorders.
brief_summary: Darryl Mason, an African-American Ohio resident, sued all 88 Ohio county recorders alleging that maintaining and making available historical land records containing racially restrictive covenants violates the Fair Housing Act's prohibition on discriminatory statements. The Sixth Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that Mason lacked Article III standing because he failed to allege a particularized injury—he did not claim any intent to purchase property, his alleged injury was shared by all Ohio residents, and the county recorders had no statutory authority to redact historical documents.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Shelley v. Kraemer, 334 U.S. 1 (1948)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S.Ct. 1540 (2016)

source_file: 8443962_2019_Curto v. A COUNTRY PLACE CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION_ca3
case_name: Curto v. A Country Place Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought to use the communal pool without being subject to a sex-segregated schedule that restricted women's access to evening hours
key_holding: The Third Circuit held that the condominium's sex-segregated pool schedule violated the Fair Housing Act because it facially discriminated on the basis of sex through unequal allocation of favorable swimming times (disproportionately favoring men during evening hours) and perpetuated stereotypical assumptions about gender roles, rejecting the argument that the schedule was non-discriminatory merely because aggregate swimming hours were roughly equal.
brief_summary: Marie Curto and Steve and Diana Lusardi sued A Country Place Condominium Association alleging that its sex-segregated pool schedule, which allocated disproportionately more evening swimming hours to men than women, violated the Fair Housing Act; the Third Circuit reversed the District Court's grant of summary judgment to the Association, holding that the pool schedule facially discriminated against women in violation of § 3604(b) by creating sex-based classifications that reflected stereotypical assumptions about gender roles.
key_cases_cited: Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
United States v. Virginia, 518 U.S. 515 (1996)
Mississippi University for Women v. Hogan, 458 U.S. 718 (1982)
Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967)

source_file: 8509670_2022_Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development_dcd
case_name: Sandpiper Residents Association v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Tenants requested HUD issue Tenant Protection Vouchers to Sandpiper Cove residents as a remedy for substandard housing conditions.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' Rule 59(e) motion for reconsideration because the sale of the property to a new owner rendered all claims moot—the statutory precondition for issuing Tenant Protection Vouchers (a Notice of Default against the current owner) was no longer satisfied.
brief_summary: Sandpiper Cove tenants and the Sandpiper Residents Association sued HUD seeking an order requiring HUD to issue Tenant Protection Vouchers due to substandard housing conditions at the PBRA-subsidized complex. The court denied plaintiffs' motion to reconsider its prior dismissal on mootness grounds, holding that because the property was sold to a new owner who had not received a Notice of Default, the statutory prerequisite for issuing vouchers was no longer satisfied.
key_cases_cited: Ciralsky v. CIA, 355 F.3d 661 (D.C. Cir. 2004)
Firestone v. Firestone, 76 F.3d 1205 (D.C. Cir. 1996)
Patton Boggs LLP v. Chevron Corp., 683 F.3d 397 (D.C. Cir. 2012)

source_file: 8677847_2015_Forziano v. Independent Group Home Living Program, Inc._ca2
case_name: Forziano v. Independent Group Home Living Program, Inc.
accommodation_description: Developmentally disabled married couple requested permission to cohabitate together in one of the defendant group homes, which the group home policies prohibited.
key_holding: The court affirmed dismissal because plaintiffs could not show intentional discrimination (no couples were permitted to cohabitate, so denial was not disability-based) and lacked standing to pursue reasonable accommodation claims for injunctive relief, the only available remedy for non-intentional violations.
brief_summary: Paul Forziano and Hava Samuels, a developmentally disabled married couple, sued their former group home operators (Independent Group Home Living and Maryhaven) and New York State seeking to cohabitate together, alleging discrimination under the FHA, ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and Section 1983. The Second Circuit affirmed dismissal, holding that plaintiffs failed to state an intentional discrimination claim because no couples were permitted to cohabitate in the facilities, and that plaintiffs lacked standing to pursue reasonable accommodation claims since injunctive relief was the only available remedy and they had already obtained alternative housing placement.
key_cases_cited: McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Rodriguez v. City of New York, 197 F.3d 611 (2d Cir. 1999)
Doe v. Pfrommer, 148 F.3d 73 (2d Cir. 1998)
Sybalski v. Indep. Grp. Home Living Program, Inc., 546 F.3d 255 (2d Cir. 2008)

source_file: 8682302_2017_Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n_ca11
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n
accommodation_description: This case involves HOA rules restricting children's activities (curfew, loitering, proper attire, report card requirements) alleged to discriminate against families with children under familial status provisions, not a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the jury verdict for defendants because plaintiffs failed to demonstrate prejudicial error in the trial proceedings, particularly regarding jury instructions and verdict forms on enforcement and causation of the challenged HOA rules.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches and individual residents sued two condominium HOA associations alleging that rules restricting children's activities (curfew, loitering, proper attire, report card requirements) discriminated against families with children in violation of the Fair Housing Act. After a seven-day trial, the jury returned a verdict for the defendants, and the district court denied plaintiffs' motion for a new trial. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, finding no reversible error because plaintiffs failed to demonstrate prejudice from the jury instructions or verdict forms, and causation remained a properly contested factual issue for the jury.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 800 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 2016)
Ragin v. Harry Macklowe Real Estate Co., 6 F.3d 898 (2d Cir. 1993)
Fair Hous. Ctr. of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. The Shutters Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 389 Fed.Appx. 952 (11th Cir. 2010)

source_file: 8682303_2017_Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n_ca11
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n
accommodation_description: Fair Housing Center and individual condominium residents challenged HOA policies restricting children's activities (curfew, loitering, attire, report card requirements) as discriminatory based on familial status.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit majority summarily affirmed the district court's denial of plaintiffs' motion for new trial, holding that any procedural errors in submitting liability questions to the jury were harmless because plaintiffs failed to prove proximate causation of injury.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches and individual residents sued two condominium associations challenging policies that restricted children's activities (curfew, loitering, attire, and report card requirements) as familial status discrimination under the FHA. The district court granted partial summary judgment for plaintiffs on the Curfew and Loitering Rules but then submitted liability to the jury, which found no liability. The Eleventh Circuit summarily affirmed the denial of a new trial, with a dissent arguing the district court erred by not informing the jury of its prior liability determination.
key_cases_cited: City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 800 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 2015)
Ragin v. N.Y. Times Co., 923 F.2d 995 (2d Cir. 1991)
Guzman v. City of Chicago, 689 F.3d 740 (7th Cir. 2012)

source_file: 8699174_2017_Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Ass'n_ca11
case_name: Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches, Inc. v. Sonoma Bay Community Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it involves policies (Curfew Rule, Loitering Rule, Proper Attire Rule, Report Card Requirement) allegedly discriminating against families with children under familial status theory.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, holding that even assuming the district court erred by not informing the jury that it had already found the Loitering and Curfew Rules facially discriminatory under the FHA, the plaintiffs failed to demonstrate sufficient prejudice to warrant a new trial because they presented a full case on enforcement and causation and did not identify evidence they would have presented differently.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of the Greater Palm Beaches sued two condominium associations alleging that their Curfew, Loitering, Proper Attire, and Report Card policies discriminated against families with children in violation of FHA §§ 3604(b) and (c). After the district court granted partial summary judgment finding the Loitering and Curfew Rules facially discriminatory, the case proceeded to trial where the jury found no liability. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, holding that plaintiffs failed to demonstrate prejudice from any procedural errors in how liability was presented to the jury.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 800 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 2016)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Guzman v. City of Chicago, 689 F.3d 740 (7th Cir. 2012)
Ragin v. Harry Macklowe Real Estate Co., 6 F.3d 898 (2d Cir. 1993)

source_file: 8776095_2016_Civil Rights Education _ Enforcement Center v. Hospitality Properties Trust_cand
9363862_2023_Socal Recovery, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa_ca9
case_name: SoCal Recovery, LLC v. City of Costa Mesa
accommodation_description: Operators of sober living homes sought reasonable accommodation from the City's 650-foot separation requirement between sober living homes and treatment facilities to continue operating their group homes for persons recovering from substance use disorders.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit reversed summary judgment for the City, holding that sober living home operators can satisfy the 'actual disability' prong on a collective basis by demonstrating policies and procedures to serve individuals with disabilities, without providing individualized medical evidence of each resident's disability, and that the 'regarded as' prong does not require proof of the City's subjective belief that residents were disabled.
brief_summary: SoCal Recovery and RAW Recovery, operators of sober living homes for persons recovering from addiction, sued the City of Costa Mesa after the City denied their permit applications and reasonable accommodation requests from zoning ordinances requiring 650-foot separation between sober living homes. The Ninth Circuit reversed the district court's summary judgment for the City, holding that the operators need not provide individualized proof of each resident's disability but can establish actual disability collectively through policies, procedures, and testimony showing they serve persons with disabilities.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Wash. State Bldg. Code Council, 18 F.3d 802 (9th Cir. 1994)
Pac. Shores Props. v. City of San Diego Fire Hydrant, 730 F.3d 1142 (9th Cir. 2013)
Bay Area Addiction Rsch. & Treatment, Inc. v. City of Antioch, 179 F.3d 725 (9th Cir. 1999)
MX Grp., Inc. v. City of Covington, 293 F.3d 326 (6th Cir. 2002)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Ashley Christina McClendon v. Peter Bresler
accommodation_description: Request for accommodation to allow an emotional support animal despite landlord's 'no dogs' policy
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit reversed partial summary judgment for the landlord on the reasonable accommodation claim, holding that a triable issue of fact exists as to whether the landlord reasonably should have known of the tenant's disability when co-applicant's emails used terms like 'verified support animal,' 'reasonable accommodation,' and 'discrimination' that should have placed him on notice.
brief_summary: Ashley McClendon sued Peter Bresler, claiming he denied her rental application because of her emotional support animal in violation of the FHA and FEHA. The Ninth Circuit reversed partial summary judgment for Bresler on the reasonable accommodation claims, finding triable issues of fact as to whether Bresler knew or reasonably should have known of McClendon's disability, but affirmed the judgment that Bresler's 'no dogs, even if service dogs' statement did not violate § 3604(c)'s discriminatory advertising prohibition.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Or. Bureau of Lab. and Indus. ex rel. Fair Hous. Council of Or. v. Chandler Apartments, LLC, 702 Fed. Appx. 544 (9th Cir. 2017)
Iniestra v. Cliff Warren Invs., Inc., 886 F. Supp. 2d 1161 (C.D. Cal. 2012)
S. Cal. Hous. Rts. Ctr. v. Los Feliz Towers Homeowners Ass'n, 426 F. Supp. 2d 1061 (C.D. Cal. 2005)

case_name: Richard Wolf v. City of Millbrae
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought accommodation related to radiofrequency emissions from a T-Mobile cell tower, apparently due to claimed electromagnetic sensitivity disability.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal of Wolf's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because Wolf failed to allege that the requested accommodation was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Richard Wolf sued the City of Millbrae, a homeowners association, and T-Mobile seeking reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act related to radiofrequency emissions from a cell tower. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the dismissal, holding that Wolf failed to adequately plead that the requested accommodation was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Eloise K. Simmons v. Langston Lane Limited Partnership, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested transfer to a ground-floor, three-bedroom apartment as an accommodation for her osteoarthritis and obesity.
key_holding: Defendants failed to carry their summary judgment burden because they offered no evidence that plaintiff's requested accommodation (a ground-floor three-bedroom unit) was unreasonable in the run of cases, and Ross's contradictory testimony about unit availability created genuine issues of material fact.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Eloise K. Simmons sued her apartment building's owners and management company under the FHA, ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and DCHRA, alleging they discriminated against her by denying her request for a ground-floor, three-bedroom apartment as an accommodation for her osteoarthritis and obesity. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for partial summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact existed as to whether the accommodation was unreasonable and whether suitable units were unavailable.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Barth v. Gelb, 2 F.3d 1180 (D.C. Cir. 1993)
U.S. Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)

source_file: 9385862_2023_Suellen Klossner v. IADU Table Mound MHP, LLC_ca8
case_name: Klossner v. IADU Table Mound MHP, LLC
accommodation_description: Request that landlord accept government housing choice voucher to supplement rent payments as accommodation for disability that prevents full-time work
key_holding: The FHAA requires landlords to make reasonable accommodations that directly ameliorate the effects of a handicap, but does not require landlords to accept housing vouchers to accommodate a disabled tenant's economic inability to pay rent, because poverty caused by disability is not itself a handicap and addressing economic circumstances falls outside the scope of the reasonable accommodation duty.
brief_summary: Suellen Klossner, a disabled tenant in a mobile home park, sued her landlord seeking an injunction requiring acceptance of her government housing choice voucher as a reasonable accommodation under the FHAA, arguing the voucher was necessary because her disability prevents her from working full-time. The Eighth Circuit vacated the district court's injunction, holding that the FHAA's reasonable accommodation duty extends only to modifications that directly ameliorate the effects of a handicap, not to accommodating economic circumstances or poverty caused by disability-related inability to work.
key_cases_cited: Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)
Hemisphere Building Co. v. Village of Richton Park, 171 F.3d 437 (7th Cir. 1999)
U.S. Airways, Inc. v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
City of Edmonds v. Wash. State Bldg. Code Council, 18 F.3d 802 (9th Cir. 1994)

case_name: Symon Mandawala v. Struga Management
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves claim that landlord refused to allow plaintiff to remain as an occupant and withheld mail/packages due to race
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit affirmed that the plaintiff failed to establish disparate treatment under the Fair Housing Act because he did not complete application requirements for housing and failed to rebut the defendant's legitimate, non-discriminatory reason (failure to provide verifiable proof of income) for its housing decisions.
brief_summary: Black tenant Symon Mandawala appealed the district court's take-nothing judgment after a bench trial, alleging Struga Management discriminated against him by refusing to allow him to remain as an occupant in an apartment and withholding his mail and packages because of his race. The Fifth Circuit affirmed, finding Mandawala failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment under the FHA because he did not complete the required application process and failed to rebut the defendants' legitimate, non-discriminatory reason for denying housing.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Crain v. City of Selma, 952 F.3d 634 (5th Cir. 2020)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)

case_name: United States v. Louis A. Rupp, II
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested additional time to relocate after receiving eviction notice; this was not a disability-related accommodation but rather a general plea for leniency based on their difficult circumstances with a newborn.
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit affirmed the punitive damages award, concluding there was sufficient evidence that Rupp acted with reckless indifference to federal housing law given his 50 years of experience as a landlord and awareness of anti-discrimination statutes.
brief_summary: The United States sued landlord Louis Rupp under the Fair Housing Act on behalf of the Erwin-Teal family, alleging Rupp discriminated against them based on familial status by evicting them for having children in violation of the FHA. The Eighth Circuit affirmed the punitive damages award, concluding sufficient evidence supported the jury's finding that Rupp acted with reckless indifference to federal housing law.
key_cases_cited: Quigley v. Winter, 598 F.3d 938 (8th Cir. 2010)
Badami v. Flood, 214 F.3d 994 (8th Cir. 2000)
Kolstad v. Am. Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526 (1999)
State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. v. Campbell, 538 U.S. 408 (2003)
BMW of N. Am., Inc. v. Gore, 517 U.S. 559 (1996)

case_name: Theresa Thomas-Tupper v. Catholic Charities et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that Robert Kellett be permitted to serve as her live-in aide despite his disqualifying drug-related criminal conviction.
key_holding: Defendants did not deny Plaintiff a reasonable accommodation because they offered her the exact accommodation she sought—a stipulation permitting Kellett as a live-in aide—which Plaintiff refused to sign, and the eviction was based on legitimate lease violations rather than disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Theresa Thomas-Tupper sued Catholic Charities and related entities (operators of a federally funded independent living facility) alleging disability discrimination under the FHA for failing to accommodate her request to have Robert Kellett serve as a live-in aide despite his criminal history, and retaliation for engaging in protected activity. The Second Circuit affirmed summary judgment for Defendants, finding that Defendants offered Plaintiff the exact accommodation she sought via stipulation (which she refused to sign) and that the eviction proceedings were causally unrelated to any protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Sheng v. M&TBank Corp., 848 F.3d 78 (2d Cir. 2017)
Wright v. N.Y. State Dep't of Corr., 831 F.3d 64 (2d Cir. 2016)
McElwee v. Cnty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)

source_file: 9409254_2023_Carter v. Dc Department of Human Services Child Family Services_dcd
9416474_2023_Oxford House Inc v. Township of North Bergen_ca3
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House sought a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy to operate a community residence for individuals recovering from substance use disorders in a two-family dwelling.
key_holding: Oxford House failed to demonstrate a reasonable probability of success on its discrimination claims because it did not adduce sufficient evidence showing that the prospective residents' disability status played any role in the Township's denial of the Certificate of Continuing Occupancy.
brief_summary: Oxford House, a nonprofit that establishes housing for individuals recovering from substance abuse, sued the Township of North Bergen after it denied a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy for a proposed group home, alleging the denial violated the FHA and ADA. The Third Circuit affirmed the denial of preliminary injunction, finding Oxford House failed to show the Township's denial was motivated by discriminatory intent rather than legitimate zoning concerns about the property's two-family dwelling classification.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Municipal Authority, 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)

case_name: James Nathaniel Douse v. Sabrina Traeger
accommodation_description: Complaint was inadequate and incomprehensible, failing to articulate a specific accommodation request; claims related to HOA assessments and property deposit disputes rather than a discrete accommodation.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal because plaintiff's FHA complaint was wholly insubstantial and frivolous, containing no factual allegations establishing a violation of any FHA provision, and even construing the complaint generously, the claims regarding HOA fees and property deposits did not relate to discriminatory conduct on a protected basis.
brief_summary: James Douse, proceeding pro se, appealed the sua sponte dismissal of his Fair Housing Act complaint against private individuals and a homeowners association for lack of subject matter jurisdiction. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, finding Douse's complaint wholly insubstantial and frivolous because it failed to allege any factual content establishing that defendants engaged in conduct prohibited by the FHA, instead raising only contract claims regarding HOA assessments and a pool construction deposit.
key_cases_cited: Univ. of S. Ala. v. Am. Tobacco Co., 168 F.3d 405 (11th Cir. 1999)
Blue Cross & Blue Shield of Ala. v. Sanders, 138 F.3d 1347 (11th Cir. 1998)
Barnett v. Okeechobee Hosp., 283 F.3d 1232 (11th Cir. 2002)
Lawrence v. Dunbar, 919 F.2d 1525 (11th Cir. 1990)
Diaz v. Sheppard, 85 F.3d 1502 (11th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 9427143_2023_National Association of Mutual Insurance Companies v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development_dcd
case_name: National Association of Mutual Insurance Companies v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; NAMIC challenges HUD's Disparate-Impact Rule as exceeding statutory authority under the FHA.
key_holding: HUD's Disparate-Impact Rule does not conflict with the Fair Housing Act as applied to insurers' underwriting and rating practices; NAMIC's four arguments—that the Rule causes pervasive consideration of protected characteristics, is invalidated by state insurance laws, allows claims based solely on statistical disparities, and permits displacement of valid priorities—are all unpersuasive.
brief_summary: The National Association of Mutual Insurance Companies, a trade association whose members sell homeowners insurance, challenged HUD's Disparate-Impact Rule (which establishes a burden-shifting framework for disparate-impact claims under the FHA) as exceeding HUD's statutory authority under the APA and FHA as interpreted by Inclusive Communities. The court denied NAMIC's motion for summary judgment and granted HUD's cross-motion, finding the Rule does not conflict with the FHA and is a permissible construction of the statute.
key_cases_cited: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
Wards Cove Packing Co. v. Atonio, 490 U.S. 642 (1989)
Am. Ins. Ass'n v. HUD, 74 F. Supp. 3d 30 (D.D.C. 2014)

source_file: 9433324_2023_Southwest Fair Housing Council v. Wg Scottsdale LLC_ca9
case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Scottsdale LLC
accommodation_description: Request for an American Sign Language interpreter to assist a deaf applicant in understanding and signing a lease at an assisted-living facility.
key_holding: The court held that Southwest Fair Housing Council had organizational standing, sufficient evidence supported the jury's finding that an ASL interpreter was a necessary accommodation to provide a deaf applicant an equal opportunity to enjoy the housing, and the punitive damages award and permanent injunction were appropriate.
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council sued WG Scottsdale LLC (operator of Atria Sierra Pointe assisted-living facility) after testers were denied an ASL interpreter for a fictional deaf applicant, alleging Fair Housing Act violations. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the jury verdict in favor of Southwest, holding that the organization had standing, the ASL interpreter request was a necessary accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B), and the punitive damages award and permanent injunction were supported by evidence of the defendant's reckless indifference to disability accommodation requirements.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Karczewski v. DCH Mission Valley LLC, 862 F.3d 1006 (9th Cir. 2017)
Fair Hous. of Marin v. Combs, 285 F.3d 899 (9th Cir. 2002)
Valle del Sol Inc. v. Whiting, 732 F.3d 1006 (9th Cir. 2013)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Mamberto Real v. Judith Moran
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged national origin discrimination during the rental process, but no specific accommodation request is described.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of the complaint as a shotgun pleading because each of the seven counts reincorporated vague factual allegations and legal conclusions, making it impossible to determine which allegations supported which claims, and plaintiff forfeited any challenge to dismissal without leave to amend.
brief_summary: Mamberto Real, proceeding pro se, appealed the dismissal of his Fair Housing Act claims alleging national origin discrimination and retaliation against Judith Moran, Frank Diaz Gines, and Presbyterian Homes & Housing Foundation of Florida, Inc. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed, finding the complaint was properly dismissed as a shotgun pleading because each count reincorporated the same vague factual allegations and legal conclusions, failing to give defendants adequate notice of the claims against them.
key_cases_cited: Weiland v. Palm Beach Cty. Sheriff's Off., 792 F.3d 1313 (11th Cir. 2015)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Vibe Micro Inc. v. Shabanets, 878 F.3d 1291 (11th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Women's Elevated Sober Living L.L.C. v. City of Plano
accommodation_description: Request for a zoning accommodation to exceed the eight-resident capacity limit to allow 15-20 residents in a women's sober living home for individuals recovering from substance use disorders.
key_holding: The Fifth Circuit held that the district court applied the wrong legal standard by accepting that proof of greater therapeutic benefit from larger occupancy was sufficient to establish necessity under § 3604(f)(3)(B); rather, plaintiffs must prove the requested accommodation is indispensable or essential—not merely preferable—to ameliorate their disabilities.
brief_summary: Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC and its owner sued the City of Plano challenging the denial of a zoning accommodation request to allow 15-20 residents in their sober living home beyond the eight-person limit in the SF-7 zoning ordinance. The Fifth Circuit vacated the district court's finding of an FHA violation, holding that the district court improperly applied a standard accepting that therapeutically beneficial treatment constitutes necessity under the FHA, when plaintiffs must instead prove the accommodation is indispensable to recovery.
key_cases_cited: Providence Behav. Health v. Grant Rd. Pub. Util. Dist., 902 F.3d 448 (5th Cir. 2018)
Bryant Woods Inn v. Howard County, 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Harmony Haus Westlake, L.L.C. v. Parkstone Property Owners Ass'n, 851 F. App'x 461 (5th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 9443643_2023_Dayton Veterans Residences Ltd. v. Dayton Metro. Housing Authority_ca6
case_name: Dayton Veterans Residences Ltd. Partnership v. Dayton Metro. Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority to amend its Administrative Plan to permit project-based HUD-VASH vouchers for a veteran homeless housing development serving disabled veterans.
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed that the district court properly instructed the jury using the 'was necessary' standard for reasonable accommodation claims and that Freedom's Path failed to prove its accommodation claim; the court also upheld evidentiary rulings permitting testimony about whether GDPM knew or should have known its prospective tenants were disabled.
brief_summary: Dayton Veterans Residences Limited Partnership (Freedom's Path), a developer of housing for homeless veterans, sued Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging GDPM failed to reasonably accommodate disabled veterans by refusing to amend its Administrative Plan to permit project-based HUD-VASH vouchers. After a remand from the Sixth Circuit, a jury found for GDPM, and Freedom's Path appealed challenging jury instructions and evidentiary rulings. The Sixth Circuit affirmed, holding the district court properly used the 'was necessary' standard and that GDPM's late disclosure of a witness and admission of evidence about prospective tenants' disabilities did not warrant reversal.
key_cases_cited: Dayton Veterans Residences Ltd. P'ship v. Dayton Metro. Hous. Auth., No. 21-3090, 2021 WL 5411220 (6th Cir. Nov. 19, 2021)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Smith & Lee Associates, Inc. v. City of Taylor, 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 9447936_2023_Lawrence T. Newman v. Heritage Village West Condominium Association, Inc._ca11
case_name: Lawrence T. Newman v. Heritage Village West Condominium Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for advance notice of toxic chemical use so Beverly Newman can avoid exposure due to her chemical sensitivity disability.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of retaliation and discriminatory harassment claims as barred by res judicata and statute of limitations, but reversed dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim (requiring proof of different facts than retaliation) and religious discrimination claim regarding housing services, which were allowed to proceed.
brief_summary: Lawrence and Beverly Newman, tenants in a Florida condominium, sued the Heritage Village West Condominium Association and board members under the Fair Housing Act alleging religious discrimination and failure to accommodate Beverly Newman's chemical sensitivity. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed dismissal of retaliation and discriminatory harassment claims as barred by res judicata and the statute of limitations, but reversed dismissal of the reasonable accommodation and religious discrimination claims regarding housing services, remanding for further proceedings.

source_file: 9459326_2024_Vision Warriors Church, Inc. v. Harry Johnston_ca11
case_name: Vision Warriors Church, Inc. v. Harry Johnston
