accommodation_description: Vision Warriors requested zoning approval to house up to 55 men in recovery from substance abuse as a reasonable accommodation to operate its faith-based residential rehabilitation center.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the County on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because Vision Warriors failed to demonstrate necessity—specifically, it did not explain why housing 55 residents was therapeutically or economically essential rather than the eight residents legally permitted.
brief_summary: Vision Warriors Church, a faith-based residential ministry for men recovering from substance abuse, sued Cherokee County, Georgia after the County revoked its tenant occupancy permit and denied zoning approval to house up to 55 residents at its rehabilitation center. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the County on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding Vision Warriors failed to establish the requested accommodation was necessary because it offered no evidence explaining why 55 residents was therapeutically or economically essential compared to the eight residents permitted under existing zoning.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Sailboat Bend, 46 F.4th 1266 (11th Cir. 2022)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Thai Meditation Ass'n of Alabama, Inc. v. City of Mobile, 980 F.3d 821 (11th Cir. 2020)

source_file: 9468692_2024_Jose Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Limited Partnership_ca4
case_name: Jose Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: Mobile home park policy requiring all adult occupants to provide proof of legal status in the United States, challenged as disproportionately ousting Latino families
key_holding: The Fourth Circuit reversed summary judgment for the defendant, holding that the defendant's business necessity defense failed because the anti-harboring statute does not apply to landlords merely entering lease agreements with undocumented immigrants, and the record was insufficient to support the defense.
brief_summary: Latino families residing in Waples Mobile Home Park sued the park operators challenging a policy requiring all adult occupants to provide proof of legal status as having a disparate impact on Latino residents, leading to lease conversions, rent surcharges, and eventual eviction. The Fourth Circuit reversed the district court's summary judgment for the defendant, holding that the anti-harboring statute does not apply to ordinary landlord-tenant leasing relationships and that the record was insufficient to establish a business necessity defense.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
United States v. Aguilar, 477 F. App'x 1000 (4th Cir. 2012)
United States v. McClellan, 794 F.3d 743 (7th Cir. 2015)
United States v. Vargas-Cordon, 733 F.3d 366 (2d Cir. 2013)
DelRio-Mocci v. Connolly Props. Inc., 672 F.3d 241 (3d Cir. 2012)

source_file: 9470569_2024_Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Dothan, Alabama_ca11
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Dothan, Alabama
accommodation_description: Oxford House sought waiver of the City of Dothan's business license requirement for opening utility accounts at three sober living group homes for recovering addicts.
key_holding: The Eleventh Circuit affirmed that Oxford House's requested accommodation—waiving the business license requirement for utility accounts—was not necessary because plaintiffs failed to establish that the business license requirement actually affected residents' therapeutic recovery or created financial hardship directly tied to their addiction disabilities.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc. and three sober living group homes sued the City of Dothan, Alabama, alleging the City violated the Fair Housing Act by requiring them to obtain business licenses to open utility accounts. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for the City, holding that the requested accommodation was not necessary because plaintiffs failed to produce evidence that the business license requirement actually addressed the needs created by residents' substance use disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Gundy v. City of Jacksonville, 50 F.4th 60 (11th Cir. 2022)
Hornsby-Culpepper v. Ware, 906 F.3d 1302 (11th Cir. 2018)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

source_file: 9475346_2024_One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka, MN_ca8
case_name: One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka, Minnesota
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of zoning restriction limiting occupancy to four unrelated persons to allow a seven-resident sober living home for recovering alcoholics and substance abusers.
key_holding: The Eighth Circuit reversed summary judgment for plaintiffs because genuine issues of material fact existed on whether the requested zoning accommodation was reasonable and necessary, and the district court erred in considering expert evidence not presented to the City Council during its initial review of the accommodation request.
brief_summary: One Love Housing, LLC sued the City of Anoka, Minnesota, alleging the City violated the FHA and ADA by denying its request for a reasonable accommodation to waive the four-unrelated-person zoning limit to operate a seven-resident sober living home for recovering addicts. The Eighth Circuit reversed the district court's summary judgment for plaintiffs, holding that genuine issues of material fact existed on whether the accommodation was reasonable and necessary, and the district court erred in considering expert testimony not presented to the City Council during its initial review.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Keys Youth Services, Inc. v. City of Olathe, 248 F.3d 1267 (10th Cir. 2001)
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 77 F.3d 249 (8th Cir. 1996)
Klossner v. IADU Table Mound MHP, LLC, 65 F.4th 349 (8th Cir. 2023)

source_file: 9476170_2024_Jennifer Akridge v. Alfa Mutual Insurance Company_ca11
case_name: Jennifer Akridge v. Alfa Mutual Insurance Company
accommodation_description: Employment termination allegedly to avoid healthcare costs for employee's multiple sclerosis and migraines — not a housing accommodation case
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish causation under the ADA because there was no evidence that the decisionmakers who terminated her knew her individual healthcare costs, and her proffered comparators were not similarly situated since their job responsibilities were not automated to the same extent.
brief_summary: Jennifer Akridge sued her former employer Alfa Mutual Insurance Company under the Americans with Disabilities Act, alleging that Alfa terminated her to avoid paying healthcare costs for her multiple sclerosis and severe migraines. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed summary judgment for Alfa, finding that Akridge presented insufficient evidence that the decisionmakers knew her individual healthcare costs or that her disability was a but-for cause of her termination.
key_cases_cited: Gross v. FBL Financial Services, Inc., 557 U.S. 167 (2009)
Bostock v. Clayton County, 140 S. Ct. 1731 (2020)
City of Hollywood v. Hoch
Price Waterhouse v. Hopkins, 490 U.S. 228 (1989)

case_name: Amia L. Bryant v. Darrell Steinburg
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed the dismissal because Bryant failed to allege facts sufficient to show that defendants discriminated against her on the basis of a protected status under 42 U.S.C. § 3604, lacking either allegations of intentional discrimination because of a protected characteristic or a discriminatory effect on a protected class.
brief_summary: Amia L. Bryant, proceeding pro se, appealed the district court's dismissal of her Fair Housing Act claims against the Mayor of Sacramento, Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency officials, and others in connection with a government housing program. The Ninth Circuit affirmed, holding that Bryant failed to plead facts sufficient to establish either intentional discrimination because of a protected characteristic or a discriminatory effect on a protected class under 42 U.S.C. § 3604.

case_name: Frank C. Briggs v. James D. Campbell
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege Defendants failed to maintain their apartments and discriminated against them based on race and disability in the provision of housing services.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claim because they failed to allege a facially neutral policy or practice with disparate impact on a protected class, and their bare allegations of intentional discrimination were insufficient to state a disparate treatment claim.
brief_summary: Black disabled tenants sued their private landlords alleging the failure to maintain their apartments and discriminatory housing practices violated the Fair Housing Act. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Plaintiffs failed to allege a facially neutral policy with discriminatory effect or factual allegations supporting intentional discrimination based on race or disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Boykin v. Fenty, 650 F. App'x 42 (D.C. Cir. 2016)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Clifton Terrace Assocs. v. United Techs. Corp., 929 F.2d 714 (D.C. Cir. 1991)
National Fair Housing Alliance v. Travelers Indem. Co., 261 F. Supp. 3d 20 (D.D.C. 2017)

case_name: Smith v. AMH 2014-1 Borrower, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested additional time to vacate/move from his rental unit as a reasonable accommodation for his Crohn's disease.
key_holding: The court affirmed dismissal because plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts showing his Crohn's disease substantially limits a major life activity to qualify as a disability under the FHA, and because his requested accommodation (delay of eviction) did not address the needs created by his alleged disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Dwight Smith sued American Homes 4 Rent alleging the landlord failed to reasonably accommodate his Crohn's disease by refusing to grant additional time to vacate the premises, and unlawfully retaliated by continuing eviction proceedings after he requested accommodations. The Eleventh Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal, finding Smith failed to adequately plead a disability under the FHA and that his requested accommodation (delay of eviction) did not address his alleged disability needs.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schaw v. Habitat for Human. of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9493619_2024_Kalvin Schanz v. City of Otsego, Mich._ca6
case_name: Kalvin Schanz v. City of Otsego, Michigan et al.
accommodation_description: Schanz alleged that city officials' comments opposing his plan to house immigrant workers at JBS constituted racial discrimination and retaliation under the FHA
key_holding: The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants because Schanz failed to create a triable issue on the first element of his § 3617 claim—he presented no evidence that he actually aided or encouraged JBS's immigrant employees in exercising their FHA rights, as his speculative housing plan never progressed beyond idle comments to city officials.
brief_summary: Kalvin Schanz sued the City of Otsego, Michigan and city officials under the Fair Housing Act, claiming that officials' opposition to his speculative plan to house immigrant workers at JBS constituted discrimination and retaliation under § 3617. The Sixth Circuit affirmed summary judgment for defendants, holding that Schanz failed to establish that he actually aided or encouraged JBS's immigrant employees in exercising their FHA rights, as his housing plan never progressed beyond idle comments to officials who were not even decisionmakers at the time.
key_cases_cited: Linkletter v. W. & S. Fin. Group, Inc., 851 F.3d 632 (6th Cir. 2017)
Hood v. Midwest Sav. Bank, 95 F. App'x 768 (6th Cir. 2004)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Ashley Christina McClendon v. Peter Bresler
accommodation_description: Request to waive landlord's 'no dogs' policy to permit McClendon's emotional support animal to reside in the rental unit as a reasonable accommodation for disability.
key_holding: The district court did not clearly err in finding that the landlord had constructive knowledge of the prospective tenant's disability based on the rental application disclosing a 'registered support animal' and subsequent emails identifying the dog as a verified emotional support animal, and thus violated the FHAA by denying the accommodation request due to his blanket 'no dogs' policy.
brief_summary: Ashley McClendon sued landlord Peter Bresler after he denied her rental application because of his 'no dogs' policy, despite her disclosing an emotional support animal as a reasonable accommodation. The Ninth Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment that Bresler violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act, finding sufficient evidence that Bresler knew or should have known of McClendon's disability based on her rental application and follow-up emails identifying the animal as a verified emotional support animal.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Oregon Bureau of Labor & Industries ex rel. Fair Hous. Council of Or. v. Chandler Apartments, LLC, 702 F. App'x 544 (9th Cir. 2017)
McClendon v. Bresler, No. 22-55068, 2022 WL 17958633 (9th Cir. Dec. 27, 2022)

case_name: Neway Mengistu v. Forestview Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Request for landlord to submit a Request for Exception to Payment Standard to HACLA so that additional Section 8 funds could be obtained to finance accessibility-related modifications to the unit.
key_holding: A landlord is not required under the FHA to apply for an exception payment standard from a housing authority as a reasonable accommodation to finance accessibility modifications, and the landlord's refusal to do so did not constitute discriminatory refusal to rent when the landlord offered to let the tenant sign the lease.
brief_summary: Neway Mengistu, a wheelchair user with a Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher, sued Forestview Apartments and its manager alleging disability discrimination after they cancelled his rental application when he requested they finance accessibility modifications by applying for an exception payment standard from HACLA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that (1) the claims were time-barred by the two-year statute of limitations, and (2) landlords are not required under the FHA to apply for government funding to finance accessibility-related modifications as a reasonable accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalalaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Ledbetter v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co., 550 U.S. 618 (2007)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

case_name: Jason Emer v. Nicole Spearman, et al.
accommodation_description: Defendant tenant raised FHA disability discrimination counterclaims (including failure to accommodate), though the specific accommodation request is not detailed in this order.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's partial motion for summary judgment on his state law claims for unpaid rent and fraudulent inducement, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding habitability issues (gas leak) and defendant's intent that preclude summary judgment.
brief_summary: Plaintiff landlord Jason Emer sued tenant Nicole Spearman for unpaid rent and fraud arising from a lease for a single-family home at 737 North Fuller Avenue in Los Angeles; the tenant asserted Fair Housing Act counterclaims. The court denied the landlord's partial motion for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist regarding habitability defenses related to a gas leak and whether the tenant fraudulently induced the lease agreement.

case_name: C.R. v. PLB Management LLC
accommodation_description: Request that defendant assign Plaintiff a reserved parking space directly in front of his unit as an accommodation for mobility-related disability
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the window upgrade claim because Plaintiff refused to bear the expense as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A), but denied summary judgment on the parking space claim because defendants failed to properly serve the unredacted motion and failed to establish the absence of genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether the parking accommodation was necessary and reasonable.
brief_summary: Minor plaintiff C.R., through his guardian, sued PLB Management LLC alleging disability discrimination under the FHA, claiming the landlord failed to reasonably accommodate his mobility disability by refusing to upgrade his apartment windows and by refusing to assign him a preferred parking space. The district court granted summary judgment for defendants on the window upgrade claim (finding it a modification requiring plaintiff to pay) but denied summary judgment on the parking space claim due to defendants' procedural failures in their summary judgment briefing.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Fagundes v. Charter Builders, Inc., 2008 WL 268977 (N.D. Cal. 2008)
Sturm v. Davlyn Invs., Inc., 2013 WL 8604662 (C.D. Cal. 2013)

case_name: Ashley Christina McClendon v. Peter Bresler
accommodation_description: Failure to reasonably accommodate disability in rental housing (specific accommodation request not detailed in judgment).
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff proved Defendant reasonably should have known of her disability and violated the reasonable accommodation provisions of the FHAA (42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B)) by failing to accommodate her disability.
brief_summary: Ashley McClendon sued Peter Bresler, individually and as trustee of the Bresler Trust, alleging he failed to reasonably accommodate her disability in violation of the Fair Housing Amendments Act. After remand from the Ninth Circuit and a court trial on the briefs, the court found in favor of Plaintiff, awarding $14,800 in compensatory damages for out-of-pocket losses incurred in securing alternative housing.

case_name: C.R. v. PLB Management LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an assigned reserved parking space in front of his apartment unit at Park La Brea for disability-related purposes.
key_holding: The court granted JMOL on causation and damages, finding no evidence PLB's failure to assign the requested parking space caused C.R. emotional harm and no evidence to support the $250,000 damages award, while also finding PLB's reserved parking alternative was reasonable; the court conditionally granted a new trial on these issues and on whether C.R.'s request was an accommodation rather than a modification.
brief_summary: C.R., a minor with a disability, sued PLB Management LLC alleging disability discrimination for refusing to assign him an assigned parking space at Park La Brea apartments; a jury found for C.R. and awarded $250,000 in emotional distress damages. The court granted judgment as a matter of law on causation and damages, finding insufficient evidence that PLB caused C.R. emotional harm or that the damages award was supported, and conditionally ordered a new trial on these issues plus whether C.R.'s request was a reasonable accommodation rather than a modification.
key_cases_cited: Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000)
Escriba v. Foster Poultry Farms, Inc., 743 F.3d 1236 (9th Cir. 2014)
Tan Lam v. City of Los Banos, 976 F.3d 986 (9th Cir. 2020)
Lakeside-Scott v. Multnomah County, 556 F.3d 797 (9th Cir. 2009)
Silver Sage Partners, Ltd. v. City of Desert Hot Springs, 251 F.3d 814 (9th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 9519849_2024_David Roy Winters v. West Oaks Housing LLC_cacd
case_name: David Winters v. West Oaks Housing LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: The opinion does not describe the specific accommodation request alleged in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim with prejudice because plaintiff failed to file a Second Amended Complaint by the deadline and the court concluded any further leave to amend would be futile.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff David Winters filed suit against his landlord West Oaks Housing LLC and property manager David Nemanpour alleging Fair Housing Act violations. The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice after plaintiff failed to file an amended complaint following two prior dismissals for failure to state a viable claim, concluding that any further leave to amend would be futile.
key_cases_cited: Exec. Software v. U.S. Dist. Court for the Cent. Dist. of Cal., 24 F.3d 1545 (9th Cir. 1994)

source_file: 9521123_2022_Hammonds v. Montgomery Children's Specialty Center, LLC_almd
case_name: Frankie Hammonds v. Montgomery Children's Specialty Center, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a minor with paraplegia and traumatic brain injury placed in a residential facility for children with disabilities, alleges he was subjected to abuse and neglect and was evicted or forced out after complaining about mistreatment, raising a retaliation claim under the FHA.
key_holding: The FHA retaliation claim was properly dismissed because Hammonds failed to allege facts showing he engaged in protected activity under the FHA by complaining about abuse and neglect, as his complaints did not address discrimination based on disability but rather the quality of medical care received.
brief_summary: Frankie Hammonds, an individual with paraplegia and traumatic brain injury who was placed in a residential facility for children with disabilities, sued Montgomery Children's Specialty Center, LLC alleging the facility failed to protect him from abuse and neglect and evicted him after he complained about mistreatment. The court granted MCSC's partial motion to dismiss, dismissing the FHA retaliation claim because Hammonds failed to allege facts showing his complaints constituted protected activity under the statute.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Olmstead v. L.C. ex rel. Zimring, 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Silva v. Baptist S. Fla., Inc., 856 F.3d 824 (11th Cir. 2017)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 9521475_2022_Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Dothan, Alabama_almd
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Dothan, Alabama
accommodation_description: Request that Oxford House-Dothan and future Oxford Houses be exempted from the City's business license requirement to open utilities accounts in their associational names using federal employer identification numbers.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City because Oxford House failed to establish that the requested business license exemption was necessary under the FHA, as the claimed therapeutic need (honesty in recovery) lacked evidentiary support and the accommodation did not directly address the effects of residents' disabilities or address disability-created needs.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc. and three Dothan-based Oxford Houses sued the City of Dothan, Alabama alleging the City discriminated against them based on disability by requiring them to obtain business licenses to open utilities accounts in their associational names. The court granted summary judgment for the City, finding Oxford House failed to establish that exempting them from the business license requirement was necessary under the FHA because the claimed therapeutic and financial needs were either unsupported by evidence or failed to show the accommodation would directly alleviate the effects of residents' disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 77 F.3d 249 (8th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 9523061_2020_FSI Green Park South Property LLC v. Pelham, City of, The_alnd
case_name: FSI Green Park South Property, LLC v. City of Pelham, Alabama
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves challenge to municipal zoning enforcement allegedly targeting Latino residents of manufactured home park
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's takings claim and FHA interference claim but found plaintiff sufficiently alleged viable claims for discrimination under § 3604(a), equal protection, and substantive due process where plaintiff alleged the City's manufactured home park enforcement campaign was motivated by discrimination against its Latino residents.
brief_summary: FSI Green Park South Property, LLC, operator of a manufactured home park in Pelham, Alabama, sued the City of Pelham alleging that the City's zoning enforcement campaign—including denying permits, issuing citations, and pursuing condemnation—was motivated by discriminatory intent against its predominantly Latino residents in violation of the Fair Housing Act and Equal Protection Clause. The court dismissed plaintiff's regulatory takings claim and FHA interference claim but allowed plaintiff's claims for housing discrimination under § 3604(a), equal protection, and substantive due process to proceed, finding sufficient factual allegations to state plausible claims at the motion to dismiss stage.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Tex. Dept. of Hous. & Comm. Affairs v. Inclusive Comms., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Hallmark Dev., Inc. v. Fulton Cty, Ga., 466 F.3d 1276 (11th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Kaislee McCoy, et al. v. Cypress Landing, LP, et al.
accommodation_description: Mother requested multiple accommodations from April 2018 through October 2019 to ensure safety for her autistic, deaf, and blind daughter inside the home, though specific accommodation requests are not detailed in the opinion.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the retaliation claims because the close temporal proximity between Vinson's accommodation requests and the eviction, combined with allegations that other tenants were not evicted for missed rent, plausibly alleged pretext for retaliation.
brief_summary: Vinson sued on behalf of herself and her two minor daughters against their former landlord and property manager, alleging disability discrimination for denying over twenty requests to accommodate her autistic, deaf, and blind daughter Kaislee, and retaliation for evicting them after they made these requests. The court granted the motion to dismiss as to most of Vinson's individual claims as time-barred under the two-year FHA limitations period, but denied dismissal of the minor daughters' claims and the retaliation claims, finding sufficient temporal proximity and pretext allegations to survive dismissal.
key_cases_cited: Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
McCullum v. Orlando Reg'l Healthcare Sys., Inc., 768 F.3d 1135 (11th Cir. 2014)
Dixon v. The Hallmark Companies, Inc., 627 F.3d 849 (11th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Larry Arnold v. Elmington Property Management LLC
accommodation_description: Arnold requested a waiver of Elmington's policy not to accept housing choice vouchers so he could continue renting his apartment using his Section 8 voucher.
key_holding: The court denied Elmington's motion to dismiss Arnold's reasonable accommodation claim, finding Arnold's request that Elmington accept his Section 8 voucher facially reasonable and that Elmington had not demonstrated undue hardship at the pleading stage, emphasizing that the reasonableness inquiry is highly fact-specific and Elmington's motion was premature without discovery into specific hardships.
brief_summary: Larry Arnold, a disabled veteran with COPD, neuropathy, and heart disease who uses a Section 8 housing voucher, sued Elmington Property Management after it decided to no longer accept housing vouchers, effectively threatening his tenancy. The court denied Elmington's motion to dismiss Arnold's reasonable accommodation claim, finding Arnold adequately pleaded that his request for a voucher policy waiver was facially reasonable and that the undue hardship inquiry required discovery into Elmington's specific circumstances rather than a categorical rule.
key_cases_cited: Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus Cty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)
U.S. Airways v. Barnett, 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Mueller v. Sylacauga Housing Authority
race_if_mentioned: African American (mentioned in context of reported discrimination at Sylavon properties, though no evidence of actual discrimination by SHA)
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are former SHA executive officers who were fired after reporting alleged discriminatory housing practices and financial mismanagement to HUD and DOJ.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for SHA on the FHA retaliation claim because the plaintiffs failed to establish causation—they could not show the decision-maker (Royster) was aware of their protected complaints, and their cat's paw theory was unsupported by evidence showing Lozito's alleged financial motives were connected to retaliatory animus based on the plaintiffs' FHA-related reports.
brief_summary: Former executive officers of Sylacauga Housing Authority sued for retaliation under the FHA, alleging they were terminated after reporting discriminatory housing practices and financial mismanagement to HUD and DOJ. The court granted summary judgment for SHA, finding plaintiffs failed to establish causation because the decision-maker testified he was unaware of their complaints, and their cat's paw theory linking board influence to retaliatory animus was unsupported.
key_cases_cited: Gross v. FBL Fin. Servs., Inc., 557 U.S. 167 (2009)
Staub v. Proctor Hosp., 562 U.S. 411 (2011)
Martin v. Fin. Asset Mgmt. Sys., Inc., 959 F.3d 1048 (11th Cir. 2020)
Nesbitt v. Candler Cnty., 945 F.3d 1355 (11th Cir. 2020)
Chapman v. AI Transp., 229 F.3d 1012 (11th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Larry Arnold v. Elmington Property Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant make an exception to its no-subsidy policy and continue accepting his Section 8 vouchers as an accommodation for his disabilities.
key_holding: Plaintiff's request for defendant to accept his Section 8 vouchers was facially reasonable under the low bar established in Schaw, and defendant failed to produce evidence demonstrating that granting the accommodation would impose an undue burden or fundamental alteration of its program.
brief_summary: Larry Arnold, a 72-year-old disabled veteran with COPD, neuropathy, and heart disease, sued Elmington Property Management after the company adopted a no-subsidy policy and refused his request for an exception to continue accepting his Section 8 vouchers as a reasonable accommodation for his disabilities. The court denied defendant's motion for summary judgment, finding that the accommodation request was facially reasonable under Schaw because Arnold was simply asking defendant to accept the same payment from a different source, and defendant failed to produce evidence of undue burden.
key_cases_cited: Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus Cnty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Barnett v. U.S. Air, Inc., 535 U.S. 391 (2002)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Willis v. Conopco, Inc., 108 F.3d 282 (11th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 9526528_2023_Heimkes v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Ass'n_alsd
case_name: Mark Heimkes v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep his service dog Rex in the Owner's Lodge and pool area of the condominium resort to ameliorate his PTSD and anxiety symptoms.
key_holding: The court denied the preliminary injunction because Plaintiff failed to show substantial likelihood of success on the merits of his FHA reasonable accommodation claim, as Rex poses a direct threat to the health and safety of other residents and Plaintiff lacked sufficient medical documentation demonstrating the accommodation was necessary.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mark Heimkes, a disabled veteran with PTSD, sued Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association seeking a preliminary injunction to prevent enforcement of fines and restrictions preventing his service dog Rex from accessing common areas. The court denied the motion, finding Plaintiff failed to demonstrate substantial likelihood of success on the merits because the evidence showed Rex posed a direct threat to other residents and Plaintiff lacked adequate medical documentation supporting the necessity of the accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Hawn v. Shoreline Towers Phase 1 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 347 F. App'x 464 (11th Cir. 2009)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Hunt v. Amico Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Warren v. Delvista Towers Condo. Ass'n, 49 F. Supp. 3d 1082 (S.D. Fla. 2014)

source_file: 9526612_2023_Allfrey v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association, Inc._alsd
case_name: Kris Allfrey v. Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was targeted and subjected to adverse actions for assisting a neighbor (Mr. Heimkes) with his disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA discrimination and retaliation claims because his factual allegations failed to show defendants engaged in discriminatory conduct or took adverse actions causally connected to his protected activity of aiding a disabled neighbor's accommodation request.
brief_summary: Kris Allfrey, a condominium unit owner, sued the Fairhope Motorcoach Resort Condominium Owners Association alleging FHA discrimination and retaliation claims, claiming he was targeted for assisting a disabled neighbor with an accommodation request. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to plead sufficient factual allegations showing defendants engaged in discriminatory conduct or took adverse actions causally connected to his protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Philippeaux v. Apt. Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Molina v. Aurora Loan Servs., LLC, 635 F. App'x 618 (11th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Hall v. Shipley
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleges national origin discrimination (British heritage) in eviction and lease non-renewal, though no disability accommodation request is made.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint was dismissed because it failed to allege facts showing the defendant property management company's discriminatory motive in seeking eviction—the allegation that a non-British tenant harassed plaintiff due to his British heritage is a legal conclusion without supporting factual allegations.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Noel Hall, a British national, sued property management company and property manager alleging national origin discrimination in connection with eviction proceedings and lease non-renewal. The Court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing the defendants acted with discriminatory intent rather than for legitimate reasons ( alleged threat against landlord).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)

case_name: Sharon D. Harden v. CPLC Estancia LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested transfer to a first-floor apartment due to mobility impairments preventing her from climbing stairs to her second-floor unit.
key_holding: The court found most elements of the reasonable accommodation claim plausible but held the complaint failed to plausibly allege that a first-floor transfer was reasonable because it did not allege any first-floor apartments were available at the property.
brief_summary: Sharon Harden, a mobility-impaired tenant at the Residences at Camelback West, sued her landlords alleging they failed to reasonably accommodate her disability by refusing to transfer her from a second-floor apartment to a first-floor unit despite her medical need. The court dismissed her complaint for failure to plausibly allege the accommodation was reasonable, noting the FAC failed to allege that any first-floor apartments were available at the property.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua
McGary v. City of Portland
Groome Res. Ltd., L.L.C. v. Par. of Jefferson

case_name: Sharon Garnes v. Washington Manor, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to use the apartment complex elevator as a reasonable accommodation for her back and knee problems.
key_holding: The Court dismissed the case because Washington Manor and the City of Phoenix Housing Department are not jural entities that can be sued under Arizona law, but granted leave to amend and found that plaintiff sufficiently stated a reasonable accommodation claim for elevator use.
brief_summary: Sharon Garnes, a resident of Washington Manor apartment complex operated by the City of Phoenix Housing Department, filed a Fair Housing Act complaint alleging failures to accommodate her disabilities, including elevator use for mobility issues and an emotional support animal. The Court granted the motion to dismiss because the named defendants lack legal capacity to be sued, but granted leave to amend and found she adequately stated a reasonable accommodation claim for elevator use.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
AmerisourceBergen Corp. v. Dialysist West, Inc., 465 F.3d 946 (9th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 9529556_2020_Southwest Fair Housing Council Incorporated v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement District_azd
case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council Incorporated v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement District
accommodation_description: Request to waive or exempt public housing residents from a higher water security deposit policy as an accommodation for being low-income residents of public housing
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the water district because plaintiffs' statistical evidence failed to establish 'robust causality' between the district's security deposit policy and discriminatory effect—the evidence merely showed protected groups are more likely to live in public housing, not that the facially neutral policy disproportionately affected protected-class members at West Edwards Circle compared to other residents of that same property.
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council and two individual public housing tenants sued Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement District, challenging its practice of charging public housing residents a higher water security deposit ($180) than non-public housing customers ($55), alleging this policy had a discriminatory impact on protected classes under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted summary judgment for the District, finding plaintiffs' statistical evidence insufficient to establish the robust causality required under Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project because the evidence only showed protected groups are more likely to reside in public housing, not that the uniform policy caused disproportionate harm to protected-class members compared to similarly situated residents.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Ave. 6E Invs., LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)
City of Los Angeles v. Wells Fargo & Co., 691 Fed. App'x 453 (9th Cir. 2017)
City of Los Angeles v. Bank of Am. Corp., 691 Fed. App'x 464 (9th Cir. 2017)
Hardie v. NCAA, 876 F.3d 312 (9th Cir. 2017)

source_file: 9531580_2021_Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Campana del Rio SH LLC_azd
case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Campana del Rio SH LLC
accommodation_description: Fair housing testers on behalf of a deaf grandmother requested that the assisted living facility provide an American Sign Language interpreter as a reasonable accommodation for effective communication.
key_holding: Both parties' motions for summary judgment on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim were denied because genuine disputes of material fact remain as to whether an ASL interpreter was necessary and reasonable, whether defendant refused the accommodation, and whether defendant engaged in the required interactive process.
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council sued WG Campana del Rio SH LLC, an assisted living facility, alleging violations of the ADA, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, the ACA, and the Fair Housing Act based on the facility's refusal to provide an ASL interpreter for a deaf prospective resident. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the FHA claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether an ASL interpreter was a reasonable and necessary accommodation and whether defendant engaged in the required interactive process.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Vinson v. Thomas, 288 F.3d 1145 (9th Cir. 2002)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Montano v. Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hosp., Inc., 79 F. Supp. 3d 1120 (C.D. Cal. 2015)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 9531816_2021_Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Scottsdale LLC_azd
case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Scottsdale LLC
accommodation_description: Request for an ASL interpreter to facilitate communication with a deaf prospective resident for lease signing and medical-related conversations
key_holding: The court found genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether an ASL interpreter was necessary for effective communication, whether the defendant's offered whiteboard/written notes accommodation was sufficient, and whether the defendant refused the accommodation or engaged in the required interactive process, thereby denying both parties' motions for summary judgment on the FHA and ADA claims.
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council sued Sierra Pointe, a senior living apartment complex, alleging the facility violated the ADA and FHA by refusing to provide an ASL interpreter for a deaf prospective resident, instead offering only a whiteboard and written notes for communication. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist on the accommodation claims requiring trial.
key_cases_cited: McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Montano v. Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hosp., Inc., 79 F. Supp. 3d 1120 (C.D. Cal. 2015)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 9531882_2021_Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Chandler Villas SH LLC_azd
case_name: Southwest Fair Housing Council v. WG Chandler Villas SH LLC
accommodation_description: Request for a flashing doorbell to alert a deaf resident when staff conduct safety checks, which is necessary for equal access to the facility's safety check services.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendant on the ASL interpreter claim (finding no evidence defendant refused to provide one and that written communication was reasonable for basic services), but denied both parties' motions on the flashing doorbell claim (finding it is a reasonable accommodation the provider must pay for, but disputed whether alternative accommodations existed).
brief_summary: Southwest Fair Housing Council sued WG Chandler Villas SH LLC, an assisted living facility, alleging that the facility discriminated against deaf individuals by failing to provide reasonable accommodations including an ASL interpreter and a flashing doorbell to enable deaf residents to receive safety checks. The court granted summary judgment for defendant on the ASL interpreter claim (finding no refusal and that written notes were reasonable for basic services) but denied both parties' motions on the flashing doorbell claim (finding it is a required reasonable accommodation but disputed whether alternatives existed).
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Montano v. Bonnie Brae Convalescent Hosp., Inc., 79 F. Supp. 3d 1120 (C.D. Cal. 2015)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
PGA Tour, Inc. v. Martin, 532 U.S. 661 (2001)

case_name: Michael Petramala v. City of Phoenix, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that the landlord prohibit smoking in adjacent residential units to accommodate his respiratory disability.
key_holding: The FHA's reasonable accommodation requirement does not extend to prohibiting other tenants' activities in their private units, and plaintiff failed to adequately plead he is 'handicapped' under the Act.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Michael Petramala sued landlord Heinz Troska (and the City of Phoenix, later dismissed) under the FHA and ADA alleging that Troska failed to reasonably accommodate his respiratory disability by refusing to prohibit smoking in adjacent units, and refused to renew his lease because of his disability. The Court granted Troska's motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to adequately plead a qualifying disability and that the requested accommodation (prohibiting others' activities in their private units) falls outside the scope of the FHA's reasonable accommodation requirement.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Hall v. Meadowland Ltd. P'ship
Lopez v. Smith

case_name: Francine M. Arthur v. Windsor Shadows Homeowner's Association
race_if_mentioned: Hispanic, African American, and Native American
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, alleges defendant forbade her from installing a wheelchair ramp at her home and did not permit her to use her wheelchair on the street.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's 80-page, 279-paragraph complaint for violating Rule 8's prolixity and redundancy requirements but found Plaintiff sufficiently stated FHA claims in Count One to survive the facial 12(b)(1) challenge, allowing amendment; the court dismissed with prejudice Counts Four, Six, Eight through Fourteen (FDCPA and unrelated state-law claims).
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Francine Arthur, a disabled Hispanic/African American/Native American woman, sued Windsor Shadows Homeowner's Association alleging the HOA engaged in a discriminatory scheme to force her out by issuing violation notices, imposing fines, and refusing to allow her to install a wheelchair ramp. The court dismissed the prolix complaint without prejudice for Rule 8 violations but found sufficient FHA allegations to maintain subject matter jurisdiction, while dismissing several state-law claims and the FDCPA claims with prejudice.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Heralal A. Nandlal et al. v. Oregon Cascade Properties LLC et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request analyzed; complaint failed to identify discriminatory conduct by any defendant under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b)
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim was dismissed because he failed to identify any specific conduct by any named defendant that violated 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) or explain how he was injured by such conduct, and his vague references to fair housing statutes did not state a plausible claim for relief.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Heralal Nandlal, along with SHOPALX LLC, sued Oregon Cascade Properties LLC and multiple other defendants in federal court, challenging state court eviction proceedings and alleging Fair Housing Act violations under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b). The Court granted IFP status but dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the plaintiff failed to identify specific discriminatory conduct by any defendant or how he was harmed, though he was granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Noel v. Hall, 341 F.3d 1148 (9th Cir. 2003)
Rendell-Baker v. Kohn, 457 U.S. 830 (1982)
Lopez v. Smith, 203 F.3d 1122 (9th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Michael Hasenkamp v. The Shah Trust, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to keep a Belgian Malinois dog as an emotional support animal for plaintiff's daughter's mental health disability
key_holding: The court found plaintiff likely to succeed on the merits of a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because defendants refused to renew his lease after learning he had an emotional support animal for his daughter's mental health condition without engaging in any analysis of whether the accommodation was necessary or reasonable.
brief_summary: Michael Hasenkamp sued The Shah Trust and related defendants alleging fair housing violations after they refused to renew his lease when they discovered he had a third dog (Belgian Malinois) that serves as an emotional support animal for his daughter's mental health condition. The court granted plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction, finding he is likely to succeed on the merits of his reasonable accommodation claim because defendants demanded he remove the dog or pay a pet fee without engaging in any interactive process to evaluate whether the accommodation was necessary or reasonable.
key_cases_cited: United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Ass'n of Apartment Owners of Liliuokalani Gardens at Waikiki v. Taylor, 892 F. Supp. 2d 1268 (D. Haw. 2012)
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Alliance for the Wild Rockies v. Cottrell, 632 F.3d 1127 (9th Cir. 2011)
Stanley v. Univ. of S. Cal., 13 F.3d 1313 (9th Cir. 1994)

case_name: Michael Petramala v. City of Phoenix, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a closer parking spot from the defendant landlord as a reasonable accommodation for his alleged disability.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because the plaintiff failed to allege sufficient non-conclusory factual allegations establishing that he is 'handicapped' under the relevant statutes, an essential element of his ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and FHA claims; the court further found that amendment would be futile given the pattern of conclusory and ever-changing disability allegations.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Michael Petramala sued his landlord Heinz Troska, alleging disability discrimination under the FHA and failure to accommodate under the ADA and Rehabilitation Act for refusing to provide him a closer parking spot and refusing to rent him other units. The court granted Troska's motion to dismiss, finding that Petramala failed to adequately plead a disability with non-conclusory factual allegations as required, and that amendment would be futile given his repeated but unsubstantiated disability claims across multiple lawsuits.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Meadowland Ltd. P'ship, 7 F. App'x 687 (9th Cir. 2001)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Wanda Elaine Smith-Jeter v. City of Scottsdale, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to overturn her eviction and regain access to her apartment, claiming she was denied the opportunity to request a reasonable accommodation for disability before being removed.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's TRO motion because she failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits of her claims, and the record showed the eviction was procedurally proper under Arizona law.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Wanda Elaine Smith-Jeter, a Black woman with disabilities, sought a TRO overturning her eviction from Villa Montana Apartments, arguing she was denied the opportunity to request a reasonable accommodation before being removed. The court denied the TRO, finding Plaintiff failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits and that the eviction was procedurally proper under Arizona law.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc.

case_name: Francine M. Arthur v. Windsor Shadows Homeowner's Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiff referenced a ramp used for her mobility scooter, alleging harassment when a landscaper mistook it for a skateboard ramp.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the HOA because plaintiff failed to produce admissible evidence that she was treated differently than other homeowners—she merely alleged debt forgiveness for a neighbor without supporting evidence, and no adverse action was taken regarding her mobility scooter ramp.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Francine Arthur sued Windsor Shadows Homeowner's Association alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination, Arizona Fair Housing Act violation, harassment, breach of covenant of quiet enjoyment, and breach of fiduciary duty based on alleged selective enforcement of CC&Rs and differential treatment regarding her use of a mobility scooter ramp. The Court granted summary judgment for the HOA on all claims, finding no evidence of disparate treatment because plaintiff failed to provide admissible evidence that a neighbor's debt was forgiven or that the HOA took any adverse action against her for her disability-related ramp.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Budnick v. Town of Carefree
Orr v. Bank of Am.
Canady v. Prescott Canyon Ests. Homeowners Ass'n

source_file: 9535844_2023_Daniels v. BMF V AZ Saddle LLC_azd
case_name: Naomi Daniels v. BMF VAZ Saddle, LLC and Cynthia Rodriguez
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request is described; plaintiff makes general allegations of housing and racial discrimination regarding an eviction notice and lease payment dispute.
key_holding: The court recommends dismissing the FHA claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts explaining how defendant's actions (placing eviction notice on door, filing for eviction) constituted racial or housing discrimination, providing leave to amend.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Naomi Daniels sued BMF VAZ Saddle, LLC and Cynthia Rodriguez alleging Fair Housing Act violations, Section 1983 civil rights claims, and breach of contract related to an eviction proceeding and lease payment dispute. The magistrate recommends dismissing the complaint for failure to state a claim because the plaintiff failed to allege facts showing how defendant's actions constituted racial or housing discrimination under the FHA, while granting leave to amend the FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

source_file: 9546813_2019_City of Sacramento v. Wells Fargo _ Co._caed
case_name: City of Sacramento v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case concerns Wells Fargo's alleged pattern of discriminatory mortgage lending including redlining and reverse redlining of minority borrowers in Sacramento.
key_holding: The court denied Wells Fargo's motion to dismiss as to the City's claims for reduced property tax revenues and non-economic injuries, but granted the motion as to municipal services costs, finding the latter did not establish proximate cause under City of Miami.
brief_summary: The City of Sacramento sued Wells Fargo alleging the bank engaged in a pattern and practice of discriminatory mortgage lending (redlining and reverse redlining) targeting African American and Latino borrowers in Sacramento since 2004, causing foreclosures in minority neighborhoods. The court largely denied Wells Fargo's motion to dismiss, finding the City adequately pled proximate cause for property tax revenue losses and non-economic injuries, but dismissed claims for municipal services costs for failing to establish direct causation.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 923 F.3d 1268 (11th Cir. 2019)
City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 2018 WL 3008538 (N.D. Cal. 2018)
Holmes v. Securities Investor Protection Corporation, 503 U.S. 258 (1992)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 9549101_2019_The Joseph Plagenza and Rose Plagenza 1990 Living Trust v. Zavala_caed

case_name: Cupid Monique Trotter v. Con Am Group; Crisienna Vista Communities
accommodation_description: The complaint is too vague and conclusory to identify any specific accommodation request; plaintiff only generally references conditions in public housing and various statutes including the FHA without alleging specific facts.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint was dismissed because it failed to state a cognizable claim, containing only vague and conclusory allegations without factual support for breach of contract or any identifiable Fair Housing Act claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Cupid Monique Trotter filed a vague complaint against Con Am Group and Crisienna Vista Communities alleging breach of a federal government contract regarding property maintenance conditions in public housing. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding the allegations conclusory and lacking factual support for any cognizable theory, while noting plaintiff may be attempting a Fair Housing Act claim but failed to plead it properly.

case_name: Danelle Renee Scoggin v. Turning Point Central California
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's complaint is too vague to identify a specific accommodation request; plaintiff alleges disability-based housing discrimination but does not specify what accommodation was requested or denied.
key_holding: The complaint fails to comply with Rule 8(a)(2) because it contains only vague and conclusory statements alleging housing discrimination based on disability without sufficient factual details to permit the court to draw a reasonable inference that the defendant is liable; plaintiff is granted leave to amend within 30 days.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Danelle Renee Scoggin, granted in forma pauperis status, filed this action against Turning Point Central California (Falcon Court Permanent Supportive Housing) alleging disability-based housing discrimination, including failure to make reasonable accommodations. The magistrate judge screened the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) and found the complaint failed to state a cognizable claim because it lacked sufficient factual details as required by Rule 8(a)(2) and Ashcroft v. Iqbal, granting plaintiff thirty days leave to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)
Lopez v. Smith, 203 F.3d 1122 (9th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Wilkerson v. Fujinaka Properties, LP
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user who had a stroke and later leg amputation, requested installation of a pool lift to access the mobile home park's swimming pool.
key_holding: Denying defendants' motion to dismiss, the court found plaintiff sufficiently alleged a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because a pool lift is a reasonable accommodation necessary for plaintiff to have equal access to the pool, and defendants' refusal to install it over multiple requests stated a plausible FHA claim.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michael Wilkerson, a mobility-disabled resident of Castle Village Mobile Home Park, sued defendants alleging they violated the FHA by refusing to install a pool lift he requested so he could access the pool. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff sufficiently pleaded all elements of a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apt. Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Wong v. Regents of Univ. of California, 192 F.3d 807 (9th Cir. 1999)
Rodriguez v. Morgan, 2012 WL 253867 (C.D. Cal. 2012)
Elliott v. QF Circa 37, LLC, 2018 WL 2933467 (S.D. Cal. 2018)

source_file: 9556072_2020_(PS) Sharma v. Home Buyers Realty and HBR Rentals_caed
case_name: Janavi Sharma v. Home Buyers Realty and HBR Rentals
accommodation_description: Vague and conclusory allegations of wrongful eviction with no specific accommodation request identified in the complaint.
key_holding: The pro se plaintiff's complaint was dismissed for failing to state a cognizable claim because it contained only vague and conclusory allegations regarding wrongful eviction without identifying specific facts supporting any Fair Housing Act claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Janavi Sharma filed an in forma pauperis complaint against Home Buyers Realty and HBR Rentals alleging wrongful eviction and claiming Fair Housing Act violations, though the complaint contained only vague and conclusory allegations. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the plaintiff failed to identify specific facts supporting any cognizable FHA claim, but granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Decheri Hafer v. Unknown
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was discriminatorily evicted from hotels but does not clearly articulate a specific accommodation request; court analyzed claim under reasonable accommodation framework.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's housing discrimination claim with leave to amend because plaintiff failed to provide sufficient factual details to support any cognizable claim under the Fair Housing Act, including failing to identify specific defendants, alleged accommodations, or nexus between any disability and the adverse housing action.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Decheri Hafer filed a complaint against unknown defendants alleging mistreatment by law enforcement, civil rights violations, and housing discrimination based on disability, including claims she was discriminatorily evicted from hotels. The court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend for failure to state sufficient facts, finding plaintiff failed to identify named defendants, provide factual details supporting her housing discrimination claim, or establish the elements of a reasonable accommodation claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Graham v. Connor, 490 U.S. 386 (1989)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9558121_2021_Courtney v. Housing Authority of the County of Kings_caed
case_name: Colleen M. Courtney v. Housing Authority of the County of Kings
citation: No published citation (E.D. Cal. screening order)
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a Section 8 HCV participant with disabilities, requested to transfer from Santa Clara County to Kings County to live in her sister's home, with her sister serving as her caretaker.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to plead sufficient facts establishing she has a disability, that defendants knew of her disability, or that the denial of her voucher extension and refusal to approve her sister's home was because of her disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Colleen M. Courtney, proceeding pro se, sued the Housing Authority of the County of Kings, the Kings County Board of Supervisors, and the California Department of Fair Employment and Housing alleging disability discrimination under the FHA and ADA related to her Section 8 portability request, voucher extension denial, and refusal to approve her sister's property. The court granted leave to amend, finding the complaint failed to state cognizable claims because plaintiff did not adequately plead the elements of a reasonable accommodation or disparate treatment claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
McDonald v. Coldwell Banker, 543 F.3d 498 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Project Sentinel v. Komar
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves a fair housing organization's testing-based challenge to landlords' alleged racial discrimination in selecting renters.
key_holding: The magistrate judge recommends granting default judgment against Jeanette Komar for racial discrimination in violation of the FHA based on tester evidence showing different treatment of Black versus white rental inquiries, while recommending denial of the motion as to Sarah Komar due to inadequate service of process.
brief_summary: Project Sentinel, a fair housing organization, sued Jeanette and Sarah Komar alleging they engaged in racial discrimination by refusing to return calls from Black prospective tenants while responding to white applicants, supported by tester evidence from voicemail exchanges. The magistrate judge recommends granting default judgment against Jeanette Komar for FHA and FEHA violations with $33,812.67 in damages, injunctive relief, and $61,332.16 in attorneys' fees, while recommending denial of the motion as to Sarah Komar for insufficient service of process.
key_cases_cited: Eitel v. McCool, 782 F.2d 1470 (9th Cir. 1986)

case_name: Jill McGee, et al. v. Poverello House, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged that allowing a transgender woman to use shower facilities consistent with her gender identity constituted sexual harassment creating a hostile housing environment.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because Naomi's House is not a 'dwelling' under the FHA and because plaintiffs failed to present evidence that D.N.'s conduct was 'sufficiently severe or pervasive' to create a hostile housing environment.
brief_summary: Four homeless women sued Poverello House and Naomi's House alleging that the shelter's policy of allowing a transgender woman to use facilities consistent with her gender identity created a hostile housing environment in violation of the FHA and FEHA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that Naomi's House is not a 'dwelling' under the FHA and that even assuming coverage, the alleged harassment by D.N. was not 'sufficiently severe or pervasive' to constitute actionable sexual harassment.
key_cases_cited: Salisbury v. Housing Authority of the City of New York, 974 F. Supp. 2d 1264 (S.D.N.Y. 2013)
Brooks v. City of Chicago, 229 F.3d 917 (7th Cir. 2000)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9568790_2019_Edwards v. Gene Salter Properties and Salter Construction Inc_ared
case_name: Edwards v. Gene Salter Properties and Salter Construction Inc.
accommodation_description: Request that the landlord accept SSDI benefits, retirement income, and rental income documentation as proof of income for rental eligibility, as an exception to the standard income-verification policy requiring pay stubs, tax returns, or a letter of intent
key_holding: The court found that Salter Properties violated the FHA by refusing to accommodate Edwards's disability; the court's reasoning emphasized that allowing a co-signer or prepaying the full lease term were not substitutes for the requested accommodation because those options posed an additional burden on the disabled applicant rather than leveling the playing field.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Edwards and Adams sued Gene Salter Properties, alleging the property management company violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to accommodate Edwards's disability-related need to verify income using SSDI documentation instead of the standard income-verification policy. After a bench trial, the court found in favor of plaintiffs, awarding $1,380 in damages representing the difference in rent between the larger Brentwood apartment they were denied and the smaller Lake Pointe apartment they obtained.
key_cases_cited: Edwards v. Gene Salter Properties & Salter Constr. Inc., 739 F. App'x 357 (8th Cir. 2018)
Fair Hous. of the Dakotas, Inc. v. Goldmark Prop. Mgmt., Inc., 778 F. Supp. 2d 1028 (D.N.D. 2011)
Astralis Condominium Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dept. of Housing & Urban Development, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 9569750_2019_Niya Bunch v. Victor Valley Domestic Violence, Inc._cacd
case_name: Niya Bunch v. Victor Valley Domestic Violence, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges religious discrimination: staff confiscated her tarot cards based on religious beliefs and restricted phone access affecting her Section 8 eligibility.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA religion discrimination claim fails because she did not explain what religion she practices or how tarot cards relate to her religious practice, and she did not adequately plead that Ward acted with an invidious discriminatory purpose.
brief_summary: Niya Bunch, a resident at Victor Valley Domestic Violence shelter, sued the shelter alleging religious discrimination after staff confiscated her tarot cards and restricted her phone use; she also claimed retaliation under § 3617. The court dismissed the FHA claims, finding Bunch failed to adequately plead how the actions were motivated by religious discrimination or how they were causally connected to protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
City of Los Angeles v. Lyons, 461 U.S. 95 (1983)

case_name: Susan Marie Shultz v. Kern County, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges sheriff's department vacated her from rental apartment and she was charged with trespassing for trying to access shelter; no specific accommodation request identified in complaint.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA claim because plaintiff does not allege she is a member of a protected class or that she was treated unfairly because of protected class status; plaintiff granted leave to amend.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Susan Marie Shultz sued Kern County Sheriff's Department, Kern County Counsel, and Kern Community Development alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act after being vacated from her rental apartment and later charged with trespassing. The court granted IFP status but found the complaint fails to state a viable FHA claim because plaintiff did not allege membership in a protected class or that any adverse treatment occurred because of protected class status, granting plaintiff 30 days leave to amend.

case_name: Kevin Michael Butler v. Lachelle Dozier, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's vague complaint references potential loss of housing assistance and a possible transfer request, but does not clearly articulate a specific reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because it lacked sufficient factual allegations showing plaintiff was subjected to different terms, conditions, or privileges because of a protected status under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kevin Michael Butler sued landlord Rhydan Mohammad and the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency (SHRA) alleging potential loss of housing assistance, breach of contract, and violations of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing he was subjected to different terms or privileges because of a protected status, with leave to amend granted.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Cupid Monique Trotter v. Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency, et al.
accommodation_description: The complaint is too vague to identify a specific accommodation request; plaintiff alleges defendants failed to provide her with livable housing under Section 8.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims for failure to state a claim because the complaint lacked sufficient factual allegations showing defendants refused housing based on a protected class, and granted plaintiff leave to amend.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Cupid Monique Trotter, proceeding pro se and seeking $26 million in damages, sued the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency alleging Fair Housing Act violations and civil rights claims related to her Section 8 housing. The court granted her in forma pauperis status but dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the allegations too conclusory to establish actionable discrimination based on a protected class, and granted her 28 days to amend.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Community H., Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Bryant v. Steinburg
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; plaintiff alleged she was targeted for housing based on homeless status.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims with prejudice because she failed to allege facts showing she is a member of a protected class under the FHA—homelessness and economic status are not protected characteristics under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Amia L. Bryant, proceeding pro se, filed suit against eleven defendants alleging Fair Housing Act claims, claiming she and others were 'deliberately targeted' as homeless individuals for uninhabitable housing. The court screened the complaint and recommended dismissal with prejudice, finding that plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts establishing membership in a protected class under the FHA, as homelessness and economic status are not protected characteristics under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 9581094_2022_(PS) Roberts v. Sacramento Housing _ Redevelopment Agency_caed
case_name: Sydney Brooke Roberts v. Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a change in room allocation on her housing choice voucher to obtain additional bedrooms for her family members.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed without prejudice for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts about her disability, the proposed accommodations, their necessity, or defendants' refusal to make them.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sydney Brooke Roberts sued the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency alleging the agency denied her reasonable accommodation request for a change in housing choice voucher room allocation and failed to make communications accessible. The court granted her in forma pauperis status but dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint lacked any facts about her disability, the necessity of the requested accommodations, or the defendants' refusal to provide them.
key_cases_cited: United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Edward Ringgold v. Burgett, Inc., doing business as Pianodisc
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged defendant refused to make reasonable accommodations under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B), but the complaint does not specify what accommodation was requested.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because the complaint alleges an employment relationship rather than a housing relationship, failing to state a viable FHA claim under § 3604.
brief_summary: Edward Ringgold sued Burgett, Inc. (Pianodisc) for, inter alia, violating the Fair Housing Act by refusing to make reasonable accommodations for his disability. The court granted leave to amend the complaint but recommended dismissing the FHA claim because the complaint demonstrates an employment relationship rather than a housing relationship, making the claim legally insufficient.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Foman v. Davis, 371 U.S. 178 (1962)
Eminence Capital, LLC v. Aspeon, Inc., 316 F.3d 1048 (9th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Danelle Scoggins v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability-based housing discrimination and retaliation but has not clearly articulated a specific accommodation request.
