key_holding: The court found plaintiff's complaint failed to comply with Rule 8(a) because it did not adequately allege a qualifying disability, a specific reasonable accommodation that was denied, facts linking unsafe living conditions to discriminatory treatment based on disability, or a specific adverse housing consequence resulting from protected activity.
brief_summary: Danelle Scoggins, a pro se tenant in permanent supportive housing, sued Turning Point of Central California alleging disability-based housing discrimination, retaliation for filing complaints about unsafe living conditions (mold, exposed wires), and habitability violations. The court granted plaintiff leave to amend her complaint within thirty days because her pleading failed to comply with Rule 8(a)—she had not adequately alleged a qualifying disability under 42 U.S.C. § 3602(h), a specific denied reasonable accommodation, or facts connecting her injuries to discriminatory treatment based on disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Texas Dept. of Hous. and Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

case_name: Shultz v. Department of Justice
defendant_type: OTHER: Federal Department of Justice, state Department of Fair Employment and Housing, county sheriff and county administrative offices
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleges she was denied housing relocation assistance because she was not classified as a member of a 'special protected group'; no specific disability-related accommodation requested.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a plausible Fair Housing Act claim because it lacks sufficient factual allegations to give defendants fair notice of what conduct allegedly violated plaintiff's rights, and Bivens does not provide a remedy against the Department of Justice for civil rights violations; plaintiff is granted leave to file an amended complaint.
brief_summary: Susan Shultz, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued the Department of Justice, Department of Fair Employment and Housing, and Kern County officials alleging Fair Housing Act violations after being displaced by a government project and denied relocation assistance because she was not part of a protected group. The court screened the complaint and found it failed to state a viable FHA claim under Rule 8(a) due to lack of factual detail, and also found Bivens relief unavailable against DOJ, but granted plaintiff leave to amend her complaint by May 26, 2023.

source_file: 9586399_2023_(PS) Roberts v. Sacramento Housing _ Redevelopment Agency_caed
case_name: Sydney Brooke Roberts and David Tyrone Samuel, et al. v. Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency, et al.
accommodation_description: Family requested an extra bedroom be added to their Housing Choice Voucher as a reasonable accommodation for disabilities including ADHD, Autism Spectrum Disorder, and PTSD.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged they suffer from disabilities, requested an extra bedroom as a reasonable accommodation, and were denied that accommodation by defendants; the court rejected defendants' argument that an extra bedroom is not cognizable as a reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Sydney Brooke Roberts and David Tyrone Samuel, a family with disabilities including ADHD, autism, and PTSD, sued the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency alleging the agency violated the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act by denying their request for a reasonable accommodation—an extra bedroom added to their Housing Choice Voucher. The court largely denied defendants' motion to dismiss, allowing the reasonable accommodation and related claims to proceed while dismissing individual board members and a family composition discrimination claim without leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Fuentes v. Shevin, 407 U.S. 67 (1972)

case_name: LawAnna Kindle Brown, et al. v. Suri Hurley, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Brown requested to keep her service dog as an accommodation for her PTSD and psychiatric disability in the apartment she occupies with her husband.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding that plaintiffs sufficiently alleged injury-in-fact based on the property manager's statement that the apartment community was not required to accommodate assistance animals, which constitutes a potential violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(c).
brief_summary: Plaintiffs LawAnna Kindle Brown and her husband Haygood sued Suri Hurley, LLC, alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate Brown's service dog for her PTSD and by making discriminatory statements refusing to accommodate assistance animals. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding that the property manager's statement that the apartment was not required to accommodate assistance animals, made during the eviction process, was sufficient to allege a cognizable injury under § 3604(c).
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
San Pedro Hotel Co., Inc. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

case_name: Goodwin v. City Attorney's Office
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges 'Denied Fair Housing Act Intentional Discrimination' but provides no factual details about any specific accommodation request or discriminatory act.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff leave to amend because the complaint failed to comply with Fed. R. Civ. P. 8 and failed to state a cognizable claim, lacking any factual allegations identifying what happened, when it happened, or who was involved.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Duchun Lafre Goodwin filed a complaint alleging Fair Housing Act intentional discrimination against the City Attorney's Office and Laura Solano, but provided no factual allegations. The court screened the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) and found it failed to comply with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 8 and failed to state a cognizable claim, granting plaintiff leave to amend within 30 days.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Goodwin v. City Attorney's Office
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; complaint alleging Fair Housing Act intentional discrimination dismissed for failure to state any factual claims.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint was dismissed for failure to state a cognizable claim because it lacked any factual allegations identifying what happened, when it occurred, who was involved, and how any Fair Housing Act violation occurred.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Duchun Lafre Goodwin sued the City Attorney's Office under the Fair Housing Act, claiming intentional discrimination, but the case was dismissed at screening because the complaint lacked any factual allegations and failed to comply with the court's order to amend. The complaint was a barebones form that identified no specific housing transaction, no protected class basis with factual support, and no defendants' specific conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Anthony Alexander James v. Metzger Management Company
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case concerns alleged race-based housing discrimination following a fire at plaintiff's apartment.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because it failed to allege facts showing plaintiff was subjected to different terms, conditions, or privileges because of a protected status—the allegations were vague and conclusory without sufficient factual detail to state a plausible FHA claim.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Anthony James, proceeding pro se, sued Metzger Management Company alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race discrimination after a fire damaged his apartment and he was unable to secure alternative housing. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint contained only vague, conclusory allegations without sufficient factual detail to plausibly allege that plaintiff was subjected to different treatment because of his race or any other protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Crystal Lax, et al. v. Adobe Services, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Crystal Lax, who uses a wheelchair, alleges housing agencies denied her access to low-income housing because of her disability and race, with available apartments accessible only by stairs.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a claim under the FHA because plaintiffs do not allege sufficient facts showing defendants subjected them to different terms, conditions, or privileges of housing because of disability or race, and plaintiffs' suspicion of discrimination without specific factual allegations is insufficient.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Crystal and Dominique Lax filed suit alleging that two housing agencies denied Crystal access to low-income housing because of her disability (wheelchair use) and race while she held an emergency housing voucher. The court granted leave to amend after finding the complaint failed to state a claim because it contained no specific factual allegations showing defendants subjected plaintiffs to different housing terms or privileges because of protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

source_file: 9594478_2024_Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC_caed
case_name: Pharilyn Chhang v. West Coast USA Properties LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to waive the no-pets lease rule to keep an emotional support dog (ESA), a pit bull terrier named Onyx, for mental health disability.
key_holding: The Housing Authority of the City of Madera cannot be held directly liable for the private landlord's refusal to accommodate plaintiff's ESA request because HACM did not own, manage, or substantially control the property, and the form HAP contract did not create the substantial control over the private landlord necessary for liability.
brief_summary: Pharilyn Chhang, a Section 8 voucher holder, sued the Housing Authority of the City of Madera (HACM) along with private landlords for denying her request to keep an emotional support dog as a reasonable accommodation for her mental health disability. The Court granted HACM's motion to dismiss, finding that HACM could not be held directly liable for the private landlord's alleged discriminatory conduct because HACM neither owned nor managed the property, lacked substantial control over the private landlord, and did not engage in discriminatory conduct itself.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica, 998 F.3d 852 (9th Cir. 2021)
Langlois v. Abington Housing Authority, 234 F. Supp. 2d 33 (D. Mass. 2002)

case_name: Bernardo Alcaraz v. KMF Oakland LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; this is a race-based housing discrimination case where plaintiff alleges defendants refused to sell him the residence he occupies due to his Hispanic/Mexican national origin.
key_holding: The court granted the TRO because Alcaraz raised serious questions going to the merits of his Fair Housing Act race discrimination claim and the balance of hardships tipped in his favor, where eviction would cause irreparable harm to him while any harm to defendants could be compensated with money damages.
brief_summary: Bernardo Alcaraz, a pro se Hispanic plaintiff, sued KMF Oakland LLC and Altezza Condo LLC alleging defendants discriminated against him based on race, color, and national origin by refusing to sell him the residence he occupies and by initiating eviction proceedings after tensions arose. The court granted Alcaraz's application for a temporary restraining order, enjoining defendants from executing a writ of possession pending resolution of his Fair Housing Act claim.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Cmty. House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Wilson v. Liu
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; case centers on alleged race-based discrimination and retaliation for complaining about racial slurs.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding that plaintiff alleged a non-frivolous FHA claim based on race discrimination under § 3604(b) and that the § 3603(b)(1) exemption is an affirmative defense rather than a jurisdictional bar.
brief_summary: Plaintiff A Marie Wilson sued her landlord Sofie Liu alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act based on racial discrimination, claiming defendant's boyfriend made racially derogatory statements and she received eviction notices in retaliation for complaining. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding plaintiff's § 3604(b) race discrimination claim was not frivolous and the FHA exemption defendant raised constitutes an affirmative defense rather than a jurisdictional limitation.
key_cases_cited: Bell v. Hood, 327 U.S. 678 (1946)
Safe Air for Everyone v. Meyer, 373 F.3d 1035 (9th Cir. 2004)
United States v. Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)

case_name: Alcaraz v. KMF Oakland LLC
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves alleged racial discrimination in refusal to sell housing, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court found plaintiff alleged sufficient facts to state a claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a) for discriminatory refusal to sell based on race and national origin, but dismissed the Fourteenth Amendment due process claim for failure to adequately allege constitutional violations.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Alcaraz sued landlords KMF Oakland LLC and Altezza Condo LLC alleging they discriminated against him based on his Mexican national origin by refusing to sell him the unit he occupied while selling all other units in the 33-unit complex to non-Hispanic buyers. The court denied the motion to dismiss the FHA disparate treatment claim, finding the complaint stated a plausible claim under § 3604(a), but dismissed the due process claim without prejudice for failing to adequately allege constitutional violations.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Alcaraz v. KMF Oakland LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in refusal to sell his condo unit and initiation of eviction proceedings based on his Hispanic national origin
key_holding: The court found plaintiff raised serious questions going to the merits of his FHA disparate treatment claim based on defendant's alleged refusal to sell him his unit and repeated eviction actions motivated by racial discrimination, warranting preliminary injunction to prevent eviction during pendency of the case.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Bernardo Alcaraz, a Mexican-born Hispanic-American tenant, sued his landlords and property management companies alleging they refused to sell him his condominium unit and initiated repeated eviction proceedings against him because of his race, color, and national origin. The court granted plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction, preventing defendants from evicting him or selling his unit to another buyer during pendency of the case, finding plaintiff demonstrated sufficient likelihood of success on his Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claim.
key_cases_cited: Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Johnson v. Macy, 145 F. Supp. 3d 907 (C.D. Cal. 2015)

case_name: Rosemarie Vargas, et al. v. Facebook, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenge Facebook's advertising platform that allegedly allowed housing advertisers to target and exclude users based on protected characteristics including race, ethnicity, gender, and familial status.
key_holding: Plaintiffs lack Article III standing because they failed to allege specific facts about their use of Facebook to search for housing, including when and how often they searched, what parameters they used, what specific ads they saw, and whether they were ready, willing, and able to pursue the housing if shown the ads.
brief_summary: Individual plaintiffs sued Facebook, Inc. alleging that Facebook's advertising platform allowed housing advertisers to discriminatorily target and exclude users based on race, ethnicity, gender, and familial status in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted Facebook's motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiffs lacked Article III standing because they failed to allege specific facts demonstrating actual injury, including details about their housing searches and readiness to pursue advertised housing.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Tommy Galia v. Wasatch Advantage Group LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to pay rent after receiving his SSDI payment on the second Wednesday of each month rather than at the beginning of the month, to avoid late fees and eviction.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Galia adequately pleaded a prima facie case of discrimination based on failure to reasonably accommodate under § 3604(f)(3)(B) and interference under § 3617, because his requests to pay rent after receiving SSDI payments were denied and he was subsequently evicted.
brief_summary: Tommy Galia sued Wasatch Advantage Group LLC alleging the property manager violated the FHA by refusing to accommodate his disability-related need to pay rent after receiving his SSDI payment on the second Wednesday of each month, then pursuing an unlawful detainer action against him. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Galia adequately pleaded a reasonable accommodation claim and interference under § 3617, and rejecting the statute of limitations defense.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Smith v. Powdrill, No. CV 12-06388 DDP RZX, 2013 WL 5786586 (C.D. Cal. Oct. 28, 2013)
Castellano v. Access Premier Realty, Inc., 181 F. Supp. 3d 798 (E.D. Cal. 2016)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Rosemarie Vargas, et al. v. Facebook, Inc.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case. Plaintiffs challenge Facebook's advertising platform design that allegedly allowed housing advertisers to target ads in a discriminatory manner, excluding protected classes from seeing housing advertisements.
key_holding: Plaintiffs lack standing because they failed to plead facts showing they were personally injured by Facebook's ad targeting tools; they merely allege a generalized grievance that unidentified advertisers theoretically used discriminatory tools to exclude them from unidentified housing ads they assume were available.
brief_summary: Individual plaintiffs sued Facebook, Inc. alleging that Facebook's advertising platform allowed housing advertisers to use discriminatory targeting criteria to exclude protected class members from seeing housing advertisements. The court granted Facebook's motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding plaintiffs lacked Article III standing because they failed to plead facts demonstrating they were personally injured by the ad targeting tools, and alternatively that their claims were barred by Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act.
key_cases_cited: Fair Hous. Council of San Fernando Valley v. Roommates.Com, LLC
Dyroff v. Ultimate Software Group, Inc.
Carafano v. Metrosplash.com, Inc.
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla.
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman

case_name: Wolf v. City of Millbrae
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with electromagnetic hypersensitivity requested defendants to turn off, remove, or relocate a T-Mobile cell site located six feet above his condominium residence to alleviate disability-related health symptoms.
key_holding: Plaintiff's reasonable accommodation request was unreasonable per se because granting it would require defendants to violate the Telecommunications Act, and plaintiff failed to plead that the accommodation was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
brief_summary: Richard Wolf, a condominium owner suffering from electromagnetic hypersensitivity, sued the City of Millbrae, T-Mobile, and the Millbrae Heights HOA alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate his disability by removing or relocating a cell tower above his unit. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding Wolf's accommodation requests were unreasonable per se because granting them would require defendants to violate federal telecommunications law.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs.
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica

case_name: Royce McLemore v. Marin Housing Authority
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; case concerns habitability conditions and race discrimination claims
key_holding: County was properly dismissed because it had no legal ownership, management, or control of Golden Gate Village, and the MHA failed to establish exhaustion of California Tort Claims Act requirements for the habitability claim, but the federal Housing Act and disparate impact claims against the MHA may proceed.
brief_summary: A class of current and former residents of Golden Gate Village public housing sued the Marin Housing Authority and Marin County for habitability violations and race discrimination related to unsafe living conditions and demolition plans. The court dismissed all claims against the County with prejudice (for the first four claims) or with leave to amend (for the fifth and sixth claims), and dismissed only the habitability claim against the MHA with leave to amend while allowing the federal Housing Act and disparate impact claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Hous. Auth. of City of L.A. v. City of L.A., 38 Cal. 2d 853 (1952)
Martinez v. California, 444 U.S. 277 (1980)

case_name: Tate-Austin v. Miller
accommodation_description: African American homeowners allege discriminatory home appraisal based on race under § 3605; no accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims under § 3604(a) and (c) but allowed claims under § 3605 (discriminatory appraisal) and § 3617 (interference) to proceed, finding plaintiffs pleaded sufficient facts of racial discrimination in the appraisal of their Marin City home.
brief_summary: Tenisha Tate-Austin and Paul Austin, an African American couple, along with Fair Housing Advocates of Northern California, sued Janette Miller and Perotti Real Estate Appraisals, Inc. alleging racial discrimination in a home appraisal of their Marin City, California residence that valued the property nearly $500,000 below comparable appraisals. The court granted in part defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing claims under § 3604 but allowing claims under § 3605 (discriminatory appraisal) and § 3617 (interference) to proceed, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged Miller knew they were African American and may have relied on the racial demographics of Marin City in conducting a discriminatory appraisal.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Harris v. Itzhaki
Steptoe v. Sav. of Am.

source_file: 9604967_2022_West v. City and County of San Francisco_cand
case_name: West v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested reasonable accommodations to extend curfew hours at two homeless shelters (10 p.m. curfew waiver at Sanctuary shelter; arrival one hour after 10 p.m. curfew at Jazzie's Place shelter) due to mental and physical health conditions.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA reasonable accommodation claim against ECS with leave to amend, finding the pleading deficient on one or more elements of a § 3604(f)(3)(B) claim, while denying ECS's motion to dismiss the hostile environment claims under the FHA.
brief_summary: James West, a disabled homeless African American man, sued Episcopal Community Services, Dolores Street Community Services, and the City and County of San Francisco alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to provide reasonable accommodations for curfew rules at homeless shelters. The court granted in part and denied in part defendants' motions to dismiss, dismissing the reasonable accommodation claim against ECS with leave to amend while allowing hostile environment claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corporation v. Twombly
Community House, Inc. v. City of Boise
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co.
Gamble v. City of Escondido

source_file: 9605535_2022_Filho v. Chinatown Community Development Center, Inc._cand
case_name: Roberto Filho v. Chinatown Community Development Center, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request to transfer from current unit to a one-bedroom apartment with private kitchen and bathroom at Derek Silva Apartments for disability-related reasons including ability to prepare healthy meals and manage uncontrolled urination.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff adequately alleged the accommodation is necessary for use and enjoyability of the dwelling, but converted the motion to dismiss to summary judgment because CCDC submitted evidence that no contract exists between CCDC and DAH obligating CCDC to forward accommodation requests, creating a material factual dispute.
brief_summary: Roberto Filho, a pro se plaintiff with physical disabilities including traumatic brain injury and HIV, sued Chinatown Community Development Center (CCDC) alleging it violated the FHA by refusing to forward his transfer request to Direct Access to Housing (DAH) for relocation to a one-bedroom apartment with private kitchen and bathroom. The court found Filho adequately pleaded the accommodation was necessary but converted the motion to dismiss to summary judgment because CCDC submitted evidence showing no current contract exists between CCDC and DAH, creating a material factual dispute about whether CCDC had any obligation to forward such requests.
key_cases_cited: McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Wolf v. City of Millbrae, No. 21-CV-00967-PJH, 2021 WL 3727072 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 23, 2021)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Hassan Salehi v. Lakeview Terrace Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Condominium owner with mobility impairment requested assignment of a parking space closer to his unit as an accommodation for his disability, instead of his assigned space located behind the building across uneven grassy terrain.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss and granted preliminary injunction because plaintiff sufficiently alleged all elements of a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), including that the Association's failure to grant the parking reassignment for over five months constituted a constructive denial of the requested accommodation.
brief_summary: Hassan Salehi, a condominium owner with limited mobility following knee surgery, sued his homeowners association seeking a reasonable accommodation to reassign his parking space from behind his building (requiring traversal of uneven grass) to one of four spaces in front of the building. The court denied the Association's motion to dismiss and granted Salehi's motion for preliminary injunction, ordering the Association to make a front parking space available for Salehi's exclusive use pending resolution of the case.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Housing & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Salisbury v. Caritas Acquisitions V, LLC, 2018 WL 10483437 (S.D. Cal. 2018)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apt. Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Sydney Skochko v. Mercy Housing, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Request for a hospital bed (either transported from her apartment or rented from a vendor) during temporary hotel relocation while the building's wheelchair-accessible lift was being repaired.
key_holding: Plaintiff was entitled to summary judgment on her failure-to-accommodate claims because the requested hospital bed was reasonable on its face and defendants failed to demonstrate it would cause undue hardship, particularly because they never contacted any relocation facilities about renting a hospital bed.
brief_summary: Sydney Skochko sued Mercy Housing entities under the Fair Housing Act, alleging defendants failed to reasonably accommodate her mobility disabilities by refusing to provide or facilitate a hospital bed during two temporary relocations while the building's wheelchair lift was repaired. The court granted partial summary judgment to plaintiff, finding as a matter of law that her hospital bed requests were reasonable and that defendants failed to carry their burden of showing undue hardship, while denying defendants' summary judgment motion and allowing remaining claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)
Fair Hous. of Marin v. Combs, 285 F.3d 899 (9th Cir. 2002)
Kolstad v. Am. Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526 (1999)

source_file: 9606801_2022_Hall-Johnson v. City and County of San Francisco_cand
case_name: Hall-Johnson v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied housing at Five88 affordable housing complex based on her race and disability status.
key_holding: The court denied CCDC's motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Amendments Act claim, finding plaintiff's allegations that she was denied housing because she was disabled, receiving SSI, and Black — and that 'only Chinese people' qualified — sufficiently established a prima facie case of discrimination at the pleading stage.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Hall-Johnson sued CCDC, the manager of Five88 affordable housing, alleging she was denied housing because of her race and disability. The court denied CCDC's motion to dismiss the FHA claim, finding plaintiff adequately alleged she was qualified, applied, was denied, and that discriminatory reasons were given for the denial.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 9606994_2022_Christian v. Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community_cand
case_name: Emory D. Christian v. Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was analyzed; plaintiff raised general disability discrimination claim under FHA but failed to allege defendants acted because of her disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's federal claims with prejudice because she failed to plausibly allege race discrimination or disability-based discrimination, noting her FHA disability claim would fail since she does not allege defendants acted because of any handicap of hers.
brief_summary: Emory D. Christian, an African-American woman, sued Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community and related defendants alleging race discrimination, conspiracy, and Fair Housing Act violations based on disability. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss with prejudice, finding Christian failed to plausibly allege race discrimination or that defendants acted because of any disability, and denied leave to amend as futile.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9607428_2023_Rice v. City and County of San Francisco_cand
case_name: Allison Barton Rice v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: Request for permission to have a rent-paying roommate in his below-market-rate condominium as a reasonable accommodation for his service-connected depression and paranoid schizophrenia.
key_holding: Triable issues of fact exist about whether the CCSF understood the request was for a rent-paying roommate, whether a rent-paying roommate was necessary as a reasonable accommodation given the plaintiff's mental health needs, and whether it would be reasonable given the program's restrictions.
brief_summary: Allison Barton Rice, a disabled veteran with service-connected depression and paranoid schizophrenia, sued the City and County of San Francisco after the agency denied his request to have a rent-paying roommate as a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act. The court denied the CCSF's summary judgment motion, finding genuine disputes of material fact on whether the agency understood the request was for a rent-paying roommate, whether such an accommodation was necessary given the plaintiff's mental health needs, and whether it would be reasonable under the below-market-rate program.
key_cases_cited: Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)

source_file: 9608564_2023_Tidwell v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc._cand

case_name: Kai Lu v. Vivente 1, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a live-in aide as a reasonable accommodation for her disability to assist with daily living activities.
key_holding: The court denied the TRO because Defendant Vivente approved Plaintiff's reasonable accommodation requests twice (for a live-in aide and for a two-bedroom unit transfer), but Plaintiff never hired a live-in aide or provided the required documentation, and the requested injunction would constitute a collateral attack on a valid state court judgment.
brief_summary: Kai Lu sued Vivente 1, Inc. alleging Fair Housing Act violations after the landlord sought to evict her for failing to return to her original unit following a temporary relocation. The court denied Lu's motion for a temporary restraining order because Vivente approved her reasonable accommodation requests twice, but Lu never hired a live-in aide or provided the required documentation, and the TRO would improperly constitute a collateral attack on the state court eviction judgment.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Southeastern Community College v. Davis, 442 U.S. 397 (1979)
Updike v. Multnomah Cnty., 870 F.3d 939 (9th Cir. 2017)
Glasby v. Mercy Housing, Inc., 2017 WL 4808634 (N.D. Cal. 2017)
Marina Point, Ltd. v. Wolfson, 30 Cal. 3d 721 (1982)

source_file: 9608829_2023_Rice v. City and County of San Francisco_cand
case_name: Allison Barton Rice v. City and County of San Francisco
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to have a rent-paying roommate as a reasonable accommodation for his disability, recommended by his doctor.
key_holding: The court issued pretrial rulings including: (1) excluding HUD's determination as confusing and prejudicial because it was based on a different record than the CCSF's decision; (2) excluding evidence of plaintiff's HOA disputes from 2009-2012 as too attenuated from the 2016 accommodation dispute; (3) denying plaintiff's motion to exclude evidence about rent amounts as relevant to whether the accommodation was necessary and whether it would fundamentally alter the program; and (4) ruling that CCSF is not subject to punitive damages under the FHA.
brief_summary: Allison Barton Rice sued the City and County of San Francisco alleging that the city violated the Fair Housing Act by denying his request for a reasonable accommodation to have a rent-paying roommate as recommended by his doctor for his disability, and by interfering with his FHA rights. The court issued this Final Pretrial Order resolving motions in limine, ruling on the admissibility of various evidence and confirming that punitive damages are unavailable against the municipal defendant, in preparation for the scheduled jury trial.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Beachy v. Boise Cascade Corp., 191 F.3d 1010 (9th Cir. 1999)
City of Newport v. Fact Concerts, Inc., 453 U.S. 247 (1981)
Brooks v. Seattle Hous. Auth., 2013 WL 5493049 (W.D. Wash. 2013)

case_name: Sarah Birdwell v. Avalonbay Communities, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be charged rent at the one-bedroom unit rate for a two-bedroom unit needed as a reasonable accommodation to accommodate her overnight home health aide.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the rent reduction claim, finding that requiring waiver of additional rent for a two-bedroom unit as an accommodation for disability does not constitute an unreasonable burden and is necessary to afford equal housing opportunity, but dismissed the power outage assistance request for failure to allege how AvalonBay could reasonably have accommodated the request.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sarah Birdwell, a power wheelchair user with a mobility disability, sued AvalonBay Communities alleging the landlord failed to provide reasonable accommodations by charging her the two-bedroom unit rent rate when she required a two-bedroom unit to accommodate her overnight home health aide. The court denied AvalonBay's motion to dismiss the rent accommodation claim, finding the request was plausibly necessary and reasonable, but dismissed as time-barred claims regarding design and construction deficiencies and dismissed without prejudice the claim for assistance during power outages for failure to allege sufficient facts about how the accommodation could be reasonably implemented.
key_cases_cited: Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)
Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica, 998 F.3d 852 (9th Cir. 2021)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Smith v. Henry
subsidy_program: HUD (mentioned as HUD Model Lease for Subsidized Housing)
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a squatter, alleged defendants prevented him from accessing his mail, claiming Fair Housing Act violations.
key_holding: The court dismissed the Fair Housing Act claim with prejudice because the plaintiff was a squatter rather than a tenant, and thus could not have engaged in protected activity under § 3617 or have been discriminated against in the privileges of rental under § 3604(b) since there was no rental of a dwelling.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Mark Smith sued property owners and managers claiming Fair Housing Act violations arising from denial of mailbox access; the court dismissed the case with prejudice because Smith was a squatter rather than a tenant and therefore could not establish the protected activity required for an FHA retaliation claim under § 3617 or discrimination in housing services under § 3604(b).
key_cases_cited: Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Edwards v. Marin Park, Inc., 356 F.3d 1058 (9th Cir. 2004)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Eduardo Escamilla, et al. v. Echelon Communities, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested additional time to obtain required HCD permits and decal, and rescission of the Sixty-Day Notice to vacate, as accommodations for Escamilla's disability (heart condition and renal failure requiring dialysis)
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA reasonable accommodation claim for insufficiently alleging the necessity element but granted leave to amend, while rejecting defendant's arguments that plaintiffs lacked standing or failed to allege a denial of accommodation.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Eduardo Escamilla and Luisa Cordero sued Echelon Communities, LLC, operator of a mobilehome park, alleging the owner violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to grant additional time to obtain required HCD permits and decal as a reasonable accommodation for Escamilla's disability (heart condition and renal failure requiring dialysis), and by refusing to rescind an eviction notice. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss the FHA claim for failure to adequately allege the necessity element but granted leave to amend, while rejecting defendant's standing and denial-of-accommodation arguments.
key_cases_cited: Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Birdwell v. AvalonBay Communities, Inc., 2023 WL 6307894 (N.D. Cal. 2023)
Douglas v. Kriegsfeld Corp., 884 A.2d 1109 (D.C. 2005)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Escamilla v. Echelon Communities, LLC
accommodation_description: Request for rescission of sixty-day notice to vacate and additional time to comply with mobilehome park permit requirements as a reasonable accommodation for Escamilla's disability-related limitations.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged requests for accommodation (Escamilla informed defendant of his disability when requesting more time in December 2022, and counsel sent multiple letters specifically requesting rescission of the notice to vacate as a disability accommodation), necessity of the accommodation (Escamilla's dialysis schedule, hospitalizations, and caregiving burdens on his wife delayed their ability to complete permit compliance), and that rescission of the notice could be a reasonable accommodation under the FHAA.
brief_summary: Eduardo Escamilla and Luisa Cordero sued Echelon Communities, LLC, the operator of their Santa Rosa mobilehome park, alleging the park failed to accommodate Escamilla's disability by seeking to terminate their tenancy for failure to obtain permit documentation when Escamilla's heart and kidney conditions and dialysis treatment delayed their compliance. The court denied Echelon's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged disability-related accommodation requests, necessity of the requested accommodations (additional time and rescission of the eviction notice), and that such accommodations could be reasonable under the FHAA.
key_cases_cited: Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2004)
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9613394_2019_White v. San Diego Sunrise Management Company_casd
case_name: White v. San Diego Sunrise Management Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged defendants failed to install a wheelchair ramp and handicap accessible bathroom facilities for James White, who is wheelchair bound.
key_holding: Todd White's claims under the FHA and ADA were dismissed because he failed to allege he himself suffers from a disability or that any discrimination against him was causally connected to his father's disability, and he lacks standing to bring claims on behalf of his father James White.
brief_summary: Todd White sued San Diego Sunrise Management Company and others alleging they violated the FHA and ADA by failing to make reasonable accommodations (wheelchair ramp and accessible bathroom) for his wheelchair-bound father James White, raising rent, and refusing to renew the lease unless Todd vacated. The court dismissed Todd White's claims for failure to state a claim because he failed to allege his own disability or causal connection to his father's handicap, and denied his request to proceed as guardian ad litem for his father.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 129 S.Ct. 1937 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)

case_name: Williams v. Camden Old Creek
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves alleged racial discrimination through various incidents including questioning about Williams' name, a comment about 'the ghetto' when towing her car, and a repairman's statement that the manager 'doesn't like you' because 'you are black.'
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA race discrimination claim because plaintiffs failed to allege they were treated differently than any other resident of a different race, and the deterioration of the landlord-tenant relationship provided an obvious alternative legal explanation for the termination notice.
brief_summary: Shaliecia Williams, an African-American woman, and her minor daughter sued Camden USA, Inc. and Camden Old Creek apartment complex alleging racial discrimination under the Fair Housing Act based on multiple incidents including comments about her name, a 'ghetto' reference when towing her car, and a 60-day eviction notice. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent because they did not claim they were treated differently than similarly situated non-Black residents, and the deteriorating landlord-tenant relationship provided an obvious alternative explanation for the challenged actions.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)

case_name: Christina Pierson and Brandon Bolic v. Janice E. Martinelli and Janice Martinelli as Trustee of the Janice Martinelli Trust Dated June 27, 2005
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation was requested; the case involves alleged discriminatory harassment based on disability (Lupus) including calling Pierson a 'disabled loser' and stating she should 'go live in Kimball Towers with the rest of the disabled losers.'
key_holding: The court found sufficient facts to support a disability discrimination claim under the FHA where the landlord allegedly began harassing the tenant after learning she had Lupus, including cutting off water, changing locks, and filing a meritless unlawful detainer action; however, sex discrimination claims were dismissed for failing to allege discriminatory motive beyond personal dislike.
brief_summary: Christina Pierson sued her landlord Janice Martinelli for Fair Housing Act violations, alleging disability discrimination based on Lupus and sex discrimination; the landlord allegedly made derogatory comments about disabled people after learning of Pierson's Lupus and then engaged in escalating harassment including cutting off water, changing locks, and filing a meritless eviction. The court dismissed Mr. Bolic for lack of standing, dismissed sex discrimination claims for insufficient allegations of discriminatory motive, but denied dismissal of the disability discrimination claim finding sufficient factual allegations to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hou. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)

case_name: Sandy A. Eulitt v. City of San Diego
accommodation_description: Request to waive San Diego's six-month maximum stay rule for automobile trailers/RV parks as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state a reasonable accommodation claim because she did not request an accommodation from the defendant, did not specify her disability, and did not allege how extending her stay beyond six months would address her disability needs.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sandy Eulitt sued the City of San Diego alleging she was evicted from an RV park due to Ordinance No. 2584's six-month stay limit, claiming violations of the Equal Protection Clause, Fair Housing Act, and ADA. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding Plaintiff failed to adequately plead a reasonable accommodation claim because she did not request an accommodation from the defendant, did not specify her disability, and did not explain how the accommodation would address her disability needs.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., Inc.
Weinreich v. L.A. County Metro. Transp. Auth.
McGary v. City of Portland

case_name: Baker v. City of San Diego
race_if_mentioned: Minority/African American and other minority residents of affected San Diego communities
accommodation_description: No individual reasonable accommodation request; this is a systemic challenge to government defendants' alleged pattern of concentrating low-income housing in minority neighborhoods.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because they failed to establish a robust causal connection between the statistical disparity showing concentration of low-income housing in minority neighborhoods and any specific defendant policy, as required under Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project.
brief_summary: Individual residents of predominantly minority San Diego neighborhoods sued the County of San Diego, City of San Diego, and San Diego Housing Commission alleging defendants concentrated low-income housing projects in their communities, perpetuating segregation and housing discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to establish a robust causal connection between the statistical disparity in housing concentration and any specific defendant policy as required for disparate impact claims.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Committee Concerning Cmty. Improvement v. City of Modesto, 583 F.3d 690 (9th Cir. 2009)
Ave. 6E Invs., LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Christina Pierson, et al. v. Janice E. Martinelli
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege the landlord discriminated based on disability and sex by banning male visitors to Ms. Pierson's apartment, including calling police on Mr. Bolic over 50 times.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding that both Ms. Pierson and Mr. Bolic sufficiently alleged injury-in-fact; the court also denied the 12(b)(6) motion, finding that Plaintiffs plausibly stated a facially discriminatory policy against males under § 3604(b) based on the landlord's repeated statements that she does not want men at her property.
brief_summary: Christina Pierson and Brandon Bolic sued landlord Janice Martinelli alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, California FEHA, and the Unruh Civil Rights Act for banning male visitors to Pierson's apartment and calling the police on Mr. Bolic over 50 times. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding both plaintiffs sufficiently alleged injury-in-fact, and denied the 12(b)(6) motion, concluding that the landlord's repeated statements about not wanting men at her property plausibly stated a facially discriminatory policy based on sex.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Cmty. House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

case_name: Irvin Musgrove v. Angie Hanifin, Margery Pierce, Keysa Machado, Susana Sandoval-Soto, and Oceanside Housing Authority
accommodation_description: The order references Defendants' denial of Plaintiff’s reasonable accommodation requests but does not specify the nature of the requested accommodation.
key_holding: The court granted Plaintiff leave to file a Seventh Amended Complaint (adding FHA reasonable accommodation claims) while denying as moot Defendants' motion to dismiss the Sixth Amended Complaint, finding it would conserve judicial resources to allow Plaintiff to file and Defendants to respond to the new complaint.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Irvin Musgrove, a public housing tenant, sued Oceanside Housing Authority and individual employees alleging disability discrimination and denial of reasonable accommodation requests under the Fair Housing Act and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act. The district court granted Plaintiff leave to file a Seventh Amended Complaint adding new causes of action including FHA reasonable accommodation claims, while denying as moot Defendants' motion to dismiss the prior complaint.
key_cases_cited: Cato v. United States, 10 F.3d 1103 (9th Cir. 1995)
Noll v. Carlson, 803 F.2d 1446 (9th Cir. 1987)
Jones v. Community Redevelopment Agency, 733 F.2d 646 (9th Cir. 1984)
Wagh v. Metris Direct, Inc., 348 F.3d 1102 (9th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Nelson v. Sorrento Tower Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges race-based disparate treatment in rental application denial and interference with housing rights through allegedly false statements about his criminal history.
key_holding: The Court sua sponte dismissed the § 1981 and § 1985(3) claims for failure to plead sufficient facts but found the FHA disparate treatment claim and state defamation claim facially plausible and allowed those to proceed.
brief_summary: African-American plaintiff Warren F. Nelson sued Sorrento Tower Apartments alleging he was denied housing through a discriminatory process in which his rental application was allegedly altered to falsely indicate he was a sex offender, and that similarly-situated white applicants were treated more favorably. The Court granted IFP status, dismissed the § 1981 and § 1985(3) claims for insufficient factual pleading, but allowed the FHA disparate treatment claim and state defamation claim to proceed past sua sponte screening.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of African American-Owned Media, 140 S.Ct. 1009 (2020)
Lindsey v. SLT Los Angeles, LLC, 447 F.3d 1138 (9th Cir. 2005)

case_name: Stephanie Johnson v. Birks Properties, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support animal (dog named Benji) as a reasonable accommodation despite a no-pets policy in the lease
key_holding: Plaintiff sufficiently pled claims under § 3604(c) and § 3604(f)(2)(A) where defendant's statements about not renewing the lease because of the ESA request, without consideration of plaintiff's documented medical need, plausibly expressed a discriminatory preference against ESA users and constituted differential treatment based on disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Stephanie Johnson sued Birks Properties, LLC and Roger Birks alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after the landlord threatened not to renew her lease when she requested an emotional support animal as a reasonable accommodation for her mental health disability. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss on the FHA claims under § 3604(c) (discriminatory statements) and § 3604(f)(2)(A) (terms and conditions of rental), finding plaintiff adequately pled a discriminatory preference against ESA users and differential treatment compared to her non-disabled roommate, while dismissing the Unruh Act and negligence claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Giebeler v. M&B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 29 F.3d 1413 (9th Cir. 1994)
Avenue 6E Investments, LLC v. City of Yuma, 818 F.3d 493 (9th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Warren F. Nelson v. Sorrento Tower Apartments
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in denial of housing application based on falsely indicated sex offender status.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint for failure to establish Article III standing (no concrete injury-in-fact) and failure to state a cognizable claim, finding plaintiff's allegations conclusory and insufficient to plead discriminatory intent or causation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Warren Nelson, an African American applicant, sued Sorrento Tower Apartments alleging racial discrimination after his housing application was initially denied due to a falsely indicated sex offender status, later corrected. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff lacked Article III standing (no concrete injury) and failed to state plausible claims of racial discrimination, retaliation, defamation, or conspiracy, though plaintiff was granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Maya v. Centex Corp., 658 F.3d 1060 (9th Cir. 2011)
Lindsey v. SLT Los Angeles, LLC, 447 F.3d 1138 (9th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Warren F. Nelson v. Sorrento Tower Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in housing application denial and that defendants falsely labeled him a sex offender.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's second amended complaint with prejudice because plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts to establish standing or state plausible claims for racial discrimination under § 3604(b), retaliation under § 3617, or defamation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Warren Nelson sued Sorrento Tower Apartments claiming racial discrimination after his housing application was denied and later cancelled; the court dismissed the complaint with prejudice finding plaintiff failed to plead facts sufficient to establish injury-in-fact or state plausible FHA discrimination or retaliation claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Lujan v. Defs. of Wildlife

case_name: Edwards v. Rocky Galgano
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges race-based discriminatory eviction and disparate treatment of his African American guests, including repeated Three-Day Notices to Quit and fraudulent rental records
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because he failed to adequately allege a distinct and palpable injury—he did not show he was actually evicted, incurred monetary damages such as moving expenses or increased rent, or suffered compensable emotional distress as a result of defendants' alleged discriminatory conduct.
brief_summary: A veteran residing in a San Diego mobile home park sued the on-site manager and property management company under the Fair Housing Act, alleging they engaged in a fraudulent and racially discriminatory campaign to evict him and treated his African American guests disparately. The court granted leave to proceed in forma pauperis but dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiff failed to adequately plead the distinct and palpable injury required to support an FHA discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Ambruster v. Monument 3: Realty Fund VIII Ltd., 963 F. Supp. 862 (N.D. Cal. 1997)

source_file: 9621167_2023_Short Term Rental Alliance of San Diego v. City of San Diego_casd
case_name: Short Term Rental Alliance of San Diego v. City of San Diego
accommodation_description: Challenge to City ordinances regulating short-term rental licenses; not a disability reasonable accommodation case but a land use/regulatory challenge by short-term rental operators
key_holding: The court granted the City's motion to dismiss federal claims, finding the Alliance lacked associational standing to bring claims on behalf of guests and tenants, and failed to state viable claims under the Dormant Commerce Clause, Takings Clause, Equal Protection, or Substantive Due Process.
brief_summary: Short Term Rental Alliance of San Diego, an advocacy organization for short-term rental owners, sued the City of San Diego challenging Ordinances O-21305 and O-21464 that regulate whole-home short-term rentals in the Coastal Overlay Zone through a tiered licensing system and lottery. The court granted the City's motion to dismiss all federal claims, finding the Alliance lacked standing to assert racial disparate impact claims on behalf of guests and tenants, and failed to state constitutional claims under the Dormant Commerce Clause, Takings Clause, Equal Protection, or Substantive Due Process.
key_cases_cited: Rosenblatt v. City of Santa Monica, 940 F.3d 439 (9th Cir. 2019)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Colony Cove Properties, LLC v. City of Carson, 888 F.3d 445 (9th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9621223_2023_Moore v. Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC_casd
case_name: Moore v. Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was 'denied Reasonable Accommodation' but does not specify what accommodation was requested.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to allege she is handicapped or disabled, and therefore cannot state a claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) which requires a plaintiff to belong to the protected class of people with disabilities.
brief_summary: Brenda Moore, proceeding pro se, sued Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC and property managers alleging defamation, breach of covenant of quiet enjoyment, and denial of reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act. The S.D. Cal. court sua sponte dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, finding Moore failed to allege she is handicapped and therefore cannot invoke the reasonable accommodation protections of § 3604(f)(3)(B), and alternatively lacked subject matter jurisdiction due to failure to establish diversity of citizenship.
key_cases_cited: Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Karen Mary Asalone Iula v. James Voos
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves quid pro quo sexual harassment and hostile housing environment claims, not disability accommodation
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims for quid pro quo harassment, hostile housing environment, and retaliatory eviction because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient factual matter to state plausible claims, and denied her TRO motion seeking to enjoin state eviction proceedings under the Anti-Injunction Act
brief_summary: Plaintiff Karen Mary Asalone Iula sued her former sublessor James Voos alleging Fair Housing Act claims for quid pro quo sexual harassment, hostile housing environment, and retaliatory eviction based on her refusal of his sexual demands. The court dismissed all FHA claims without prejudice for failure to state plausible claims, finding plaintiff provided only conclusory allegations without supporting factual details, and denied plaintiff's motion for a temporary restraining order to enjoin ongoing state eviction proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Quigley v. Winter, 598 F.3d 938 (8th Cir. 2010)
Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)
Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Ellerth, 524 U.S. 742 (1998)

case_name: Karen Mary Asalone Iula v. James Voos
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—this case involves alleged quid pro quo sexual harassment, hostile housing environment, and retaliatory eviction under the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's amended FHA complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the allegations insufficiently detailed to support claims for quid pro quo harassment, hostile housing environment, or retaliatory eviction.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Karen Mary Asalone Iula sued landlord James Voos alleging quid pro quo sexual harassment, hostile housing environment, and retaliatory eviction under 42 U.S.C. § 3604. The court dismissed the amended complaint for failure to state plausible claims, finding the allegations conclusory and lacking detail about specific harassing conduct, and separately denied plaintiff's renewed TRO motion based on Rooker-Feldman jurisdiction bars.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Quigley v. Winter, 598 F.3d 938 (8th Cir. 2010)
Pardi v. Kaiser Found. Hosps., 389 F.3d 840 (9th Cir. 2004)

case_name: Jerome L. Grimes v. Mossy Nissan Kearny Mesa
accommodation_description: Plaintiff does not request any accommodation; this is a car repair dispute where plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in warranty coverage denial at a car dealership
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed with prejudice because none of plaintiff's allegations relate to the sale or rental of housing, and a car dealership repair dispute falls entirely outside the scope of the Fair Housing Act
brief_summary: African American and Native American plaintiff Jerome Grimes sued Mossy Nissan Kearny Mesa alleging the car dealership refused to honor his vehicle repair warranty based on race, asserting claims under Title VI, Title II, and the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice, finding it wholly inapplicable to a car repair dispute at a dealership, dismissed Title II and Title VI claims without prejudice for failure to state a claim, and granted plaintiff leave to amend the latter claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Alexander v. Sandoval, 532 U.S. 275 (2001)

source_file: 9622784_2024_Moore v. Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC_casd
case_name: Brenda Moore v. Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be transferred to a different apartment unit away from allegedly harassing upstairs neighbors as a reasonable accommodation for her emotional and sensory impairments.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim with prejudice because plaintiff failed to allege facts demonstrating a causal link between her requested transfer to another unit and her disability that impacts her ability to walk and see, as her conclusory allegation that the move was medically necessary was insufficient.
brief_summary: Brenda Moore, a pro se tenant proceeding in forma pauperis, sued Pacific View Apartments Carlsbad LLC and individual property managers and residents, alleging they discriminated against her based on disability by denying her request to transfer to a different unit away from allegedly harassing neighbors. The court dismissed plaintiff's third amended complaint with prejudice, finding she failed to state a viable FHA reasonable accommodation claim because she did not allege sufficient facts showing a causal link between the requested transfer and her disability.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Weinreich v. Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transp. Authority, 114 F.3d 976 (9th Cir. 1997)
Molski v. M.J. Cable, Inc., 481 F.3d 724 (9th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Vicki Hall v. Donna Hilderbrand
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested exemption from lawn mowing and snow removal responsibilities under the 2016 lease as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to adequately plead the necessity element of her § 3604(f)(3)(B) claim because she did not allege any connection between her unspecified disability and her request to be exempt from yard maintenance, and failed to adequately plead the refusal element because Donna Hilderbrand's rejection of the Negotiation Notes—which contained numerous demands unrelated to disability—did not constitute a refusal to accommodate her disability.
brief_summary: Vicki Hall, a disabled tenant represented pro se, sued her landlord Donna Hilderbrand and associated entities alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to reasonably accommodate her disability by modifying the 2016 lease to exempt her from lawn mowing and snow removal responsibilities that were included in the original 2010 lease. The court granted the defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Hall failed to adequately plead that her accommodation was necessary for equal use and enjoyment of the dwelling and that defendants refused to provide the requested accommodation, and also denied leave to amend the complaint as futile.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Assoc., Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9624160_2020_Courage to Change Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado_cod
case_name: John Green, Joan Green, and Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado
accommodation_description: Soaring Hope requested that the County permit its substance abuse rehabilitation facility to operate as a Group Home for Handicapped or Disabled Persons in a residential zoning district where Rehabilitation Facilities are not permitted.
key_holding: The County's denial of Soaring Hope's Special Use Permit application did not constitute a failure to provide reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because Plaintiffs were seeking a use (Rehabilitation Facility) that was not available to anyone in the residential zoning district, and the failure to create a housing opportunity that does not exist for non-disabled persons cannot be necessary to achieve equality of opportunity.
brief_summary: Soaring Hope, a substance abuse rehabilitation operator, and the Greens (property owners) sued El Paso County, Colorado, alleging the County violated the FHA by classifying their Spruce Road facility as a prohibited Rehabilitation Facility rather than a permitted Group Home for Handicapped Persons and by denying their Special Use Permit application. The court granted summary judgment for the County on the reasonable accommodation claim, holding that seeking a use unavailable to anyone under applicable zoning cannot constitute a reasonable accommodation, while allowing certain equal protection and intentional discrimination claims to proceed to trial.
key_cases_cited: Cinnamon Hills v. Eagle County, 685 F.3d 919 (10th Cir. 2012)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
McKivitz v. Township of Stowe, 769 F. Supp. 2d 803 (W.D. Pa. 2010)

source_file: 9624842_2021_Courage to Change Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado_cod
case_name: John Green, Joan Green, and Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado
accommodation_description: Soaring Hope Recovery Center sought a Special Use Permit (reasonable accommodation request) to operate a group home with more than five disabled residents despite the County's occupancy limits for group homes for handicapped persons.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motions for reconsideration and clarification, finding plaintiffs failed to demonstrate manifest error of law or fact in the prior order that denied both parties' summary judgment motions on the intentional discrimination claim regarding occupancy limits for group homes for disabled persons.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company (operating Soaring Hope Recovery Center) and individual plaintiffs sued El Paso County, Colorado, challenging the County's occupancy limit of five persons imposed on group homes for disabled persons under the Fair Housing Act. The court denied plaintiffs' motions for reconsideration of the prior summary judgment ruling that allowed the intentional discrimination claim to proceed to trial because a reasonable jury could find the different occupancy levels were based on bona fide governmental concerns.
key_cases_cited: Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)

source_file: 9624918_2021_Courage to Change Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado_cod
case_name: John Green, Joan Green, and Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado
accommodation_description: Soaring Hope Recovery Center, a sober living facility for persons in recovery from substance use disorders, challenges El Paso County's zoning regulations as discriminatory barriers to housing for disabled persons.
key_holding: The court granted in part the County's motion to exclude Dr. Farrin's testimony about residents' disability status (because the parties stipulated to this fact) but permitted his testimony about operations; the court also permitted the County's zoning experts to testify about industry standards but barred ultimate legal conclusions about whether the zoning violated federal law.
brief_summary: Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company (Soaring Hope Recovery Center) and individual residents sued El Paso County, Colorado, alleging the County's zoning regulations discriminated against disabled persons in recovery from substance use disorders by restricting operation of the Spruce Road sober living home. This order resolves cross-motions to exclude expert testimony, precluding Dr. Farrin from testifying about residents' disability status (stipulated by the parties) and barring the County's zoning experts from offering ultimate legal conclusions about whether the ordinances violated federal law.
key_cases_cited: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharm., Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Bitler v. A.O. Smith Corp., 400 F.3d 1227 (10th Cir. 2005)
Specht v. Jensen, 853 F.2d 805 (10th Cir. 1988)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 9624921_2021_Courage to Change Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado_cod
case_name: John Green, Joan Green, and Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company v. El Paso County, Colorado
accommodation_description: Group home operator Soaring Hope Recovery Center seeks to operate a residence for disabled persons under the County's zoning code without being subject to discriminatory occupancy restrictions.
key_holding: The court denied both parties' motions in limine, ruling that evidence regarding the County's definition of 'family' and state statute occupancy limits for other group homes is relevant to the County's defense of intentional discrimination, and allowing a former attorney to testify as a fact witness about recommendations he gave to the County regarding the 2014 zoning amendments.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Courage to Change Ranches Holding Company (operating Soaring Hope Recovery Center) and individual plaintiffs sued El Paso County, Colorado under the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act challenging the County's zoning occupancy limits and standards applicable to group homes for disabled persons. The court denied both parties' motions in limine regarding evidentiary issues for trial, ruling that evidence concerning the County's definition of 'family' and state statutory comparators is relevant to the intentional discrimination analysis.
key_cases_cited: Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)

case_name: Tiffany Grays v. Kittredge Co. Partners, LLC
citation: No reporter citation provided; see Civil Action No. 20-cv-0208-WJM-SKC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; claim is that defendant's misdemeanor criminal history policy had a racially disparate impact on African-American rental applicants.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA disparate impact claim failed because she relied only on a single national study and did not allege facts showing the misdemeanor policy caused a racially disparate impact in the relevant geographic area or that it reflected a pattern or practice of discrimination beyond her individual experience.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tiffany Grays sued Kittredge Co. Partners, LLC and related defendants claiming their policy of denying rental applicants with more than one misdemeanor conviction violated the Fair Housing Act by disproportionately impacting African-American applicants. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to adequately plead causation between the policy and racially disparate impact because she relied only on a single national study without statistical evidence or factual allegations specific to the relevant geographic area or property.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Mountain Side Mobile Estates P'ship v. Sec. of Hous. and Urban Dev., 56 F.3d 1243 (10th Cir. 1995)

source_file: 9626199_2022_Wolf v. Meadow Hills III Condominium Association_cod
case_name: Wolf v. Meadow Hills III Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an assigned parking spot near his condominium unit due to mobility limitations from partial foot amputations and skin graft complications.
