key_holding: The parking accommodation claim was dismissed as time-barred because the alleged injury occurred outside the limitations period and equitable tolling was inapplicable since the parking claim did not rely on untimely disclosed evidence or involve a scheme to avoid judicial sanction.
brief_summary: Richard Wolf sued Meadow Hills III Condominium Association and its attorneys alleging disability discrimination, including a foreclosure targeting scheme based on disability and a delayed parking accommodation. The court granted judgment on the pleadings for defendants on the parking accommodation claim (finding it time-barred), but denied the motion on the housing discrimination and foreclosure claims, allowing those claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Brooks v. Gaenzle, 614 F.3d 1213 (10th Cir. 2010)
DTC Energy Grp., Inc. v. Hirschfeld, 912 F.3d 1263 (10th Cir. 2018)
Lawrence v. Florida, 549 U.S. 327 (2007)
Holland v. Florida, 560 U.S. 631 (2010)

case_name: McDaniel v. Dominium Management Services, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested to burn candles, sage, incense, and other items for religious ritual purposes as an accommodation for their religious practices.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal because Plaintiffs' Amended Complaint failed to allege sufficient specific factual details identifying who engaged in the alleged discriminatory or retaliatory conduct, how those actors are connected to Defendants, or the severity, frequency, or context of such conduct to plausibly state FHA discrimination or retaliation claims.
brief_summary: Black Hebrew Jewish tenants suing property management for race and religious discrimination and retaliation at a HUD-funded Section 8 property, alleging they were subjected to harassment, false noise complaints, and threats of eviction while white neighbors received different treatment. The court recommended granting the motion to dismiss, finding the complaint lacked sufficient specific factual allegations to plausibly state discrimination or retaliation claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wilson v. Guardian Mgmt., LLC, 383 F. Supp. 3d 1105 (D. Or. 2019)
Ngiendo v. Pep-KU, LLC, 2019 WL 3430570 (D. Kan. 2019)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)

case_name: Katherine Trujillo v. Amity Plaza, LLC, Housing Authority of the City of Littleton, and Frank Martinez
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges sexual harassment and rape by a maintenance worker, claiming the housing provider failed to protect her from discriminatory conduct.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the federal FHA claims, holding that § 3604(b) applies to post-acquisition discriminatory conduct and that the housing provider may be held vicariously liable for the maintenance worker's sexual assault under the aided-in-agency theory.
brief_summary: Katherine Trujillo sued Amity Plaza LLC, the Housing Authority of the City of Littleton, and maintenance worker Frank Martinez alleging that Martinez sexually harassed and raped her, and that the housing defendants knew of his conduct. The court granted the motion to dismiss only as to the Colorado Fair Housing Act claim (due to governmental immunity), but denied dismissal of the federal FHA claims, ruling that § 3604(b) covers post-acquisition sexual harassment and that vicarious liability may attach under the aided-in-agency theory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Ellerth, 524 U.S. 742 (1998)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Trafficante v. Metro Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Gilead Community Services, Inc. v. Town of Cromwell
accommodation_description: Group home operator Gilead challenges Town of Cromwell's coordinated campaign of zoning enforcement, cease and desist orders, tax exemption denial, and petition to state health officials aimed at forcing closure of a community residence for men with mental health disabilities.
key_holding: A reasonable jury could find that Town of Cromwell officials acted with discriminatory intent toward individuals with disabilities by engaging in a coordinated campaign using municipal tools including public forums, press releases, cease and desist orders, petitions to state agencies, and tax exemption denials to force a group home for people with mental illness to close.
brief_summary: Gilead Community Services, a nonprofit providing housing and mental health services, sued the Town of Cromwell and town officials alleging the town engaged in intentional disability discrimination under the FHA by orchestrating public opposition, issuing a cease and desist order, petitioning state health officials, and denying tax exemption to force closure of a group home for men with disabilities. The court denied all cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the town's coordinated campaign to oppose and close the residence was motivated by discriminatory intent against people with disabilities.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Department, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Regional Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)

case_name: Gilead Community Services, Inc. v. Town of Cromwell
accommodation_description: Gilead, a nonprofit providing housing for people with disabilities, seeks to operate a community residence for six men with mental illness at 5 Reiman Drive without municipal interference based on their disabilities.
key_holding: The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether the Town of Cromwell and its officials acted with discriminatory intent toward individuals with disabilities by orchestrating a campaign of opposition to force a community residence to close.
brief_summary: Gilead Community Services, a nonprofit housing provider for people with mental illness, sued the Town of Cromwell, Connecticut and its officials alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by conducting a coordinated campaign of opposition including public forums, press releases, a petition to state health officials, a cease and desist order, and tax exemption denial to force closure of a community residence for six men with disabilities at 5 Reiman Drive. The court denied all cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether town officials acted with discriminatory intent based on prejudice against people with disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Department, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)
Innovative Health Sys., Inc. v. City of White Plains, 931 F. Supp. 222 (S.D.N.Y. 1996)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
S. Middlesex Opportunity Council, Inc. v. Town of Framingham, 752 F. Supp. 2d 85 (D. Mass. 2010)

case_name: Paulette Davis v. The Money Source Inc., ServiceLink Field Services, LLC, and MMC Contracting Services Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a Black homeowner, alleges discriminatory property inspections, racial slurs, and harassment by inspectors based on her race, as well as denial of CARES Act forbearance.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the § 3604(b) claim, holding that the FHA applies to post-acquisition conduct including discriminatory treatment in the provision of services, and the mortgage servicer and its agents can be liable for race-based harassment during property inspections; the § 3604(c) claim was dismissed.
brief_summary: Paulette Davis, a Black homeowner in Waterbury, Connecticut, sued her mortgage servicer (The Money Source Inc.) and its property inspection contractors (ServiceLink and MMC) alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by subjecting her to racially discriminatory property inspections, racial slurs, and harassment by inspectors based on her race, while also denying her CARES Act forbearance requests. The court denied the motion to dismiss the § 3604(b) race discrimination claim, holding that the FHA applies to post-acquisition discriminatory conduct in the provision of services, but dismissed the § 3604(c) discriminatory advertising claim.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009) (en banc)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 550 (2007)
Viens v. American Empire Surplus Lines Insurance Co., 113 F. Supp. 3d 555 (D. Conn. 2015)

case_name: Lisa Glover v. HPC-Eight, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendants failed to investigate noise complaints because of her race, created a hostile housing environment, and denied her a housing opportunity at a sister property because of her race.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because her complaints failed to allege sufficient facts supporting an inference of discriminatory intent—the factual allegations contradicted her conclusory claims of race-based discrimination.
brief_summary: Lisa Glover, an African-American woman who used a Section 8 housing choice voucher, sued her landlords HPC-Eight, LLC and Building & Land Technology Corp. alleging race-based housing discrimination, including claims that defendants failed to investigate her noise complaints because of her race, created a hostile housing environment, and denied her an apartment at a sister property. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding that plaintiff's complaint failed to allege sufficient facts to support an inference of discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Palmer v. Fannie Mae, 755 F. App'x 43 (2d Cir. 2018)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Zlotnick v. Crystal Run Vill., Inc., 2021 WL 4993712 (S.D.N.Y. 2021)

case_name: Morales v. U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to purchase a house from HACH after his mother's death, claiming discrimination and retaliation for prior complaints
key_holding: HUD's claims dismissed due to sovereign immunity and lack of waiver; HACH's claims dismissed for failure to allege sufficient facts to state plausible claims of discrimination or retaliation under Title VI and the FHA
brief_summary: Angel Morales sued HUD and Hartford Housing Authority alleging discrimination in violation of Title VI and the Fair Housing Act, claiming HACH improperly refused to let him purchase a house that his family had rented since 1999. The court granted both motions to dismiss, finding HUD immune from suit under sovereign immunity and that Morales failed to state plausible claims against HACH due to sparse factual allegations.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lane v. Pena, 518 U.S. 187 (1996)
Godwin v. Sec'y of Hous. & Urb. Dev., 356 F.3d 310 (D.C. Cir. 2004)
Turner v. Sec'y of U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev., 449 F.3d 536 (3d Cir. 2006)

case_name: Poteat v. Hartford Housing et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied ability to port her Section 8 housing voucher due to retaliation for prior fair housing complaints and race discrimination.
key_holding: The court granted Hartford Housing Authority's motion for judgment on the pleadings on Poteat's discrimination claims for failure to allege sufficient facts, but denied the motion as to her retaliation claim under § 3617 because she plausibly alleged that defendants' refusal to port her Section 8 voucher was pretextual retaliation for her prior fair housing complaints.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kennara Poteat sued Hartford Housing Authority, Enfield Housing Authority, CHRO, and HUD alleging fair housing violations including discrimination and retaliation related to her Section 8 voucher portability request. The court dismissed CHRO and HUD claims on sovereign immunity, dismissed discrimination and contract claims against Hartford Housing Authority, but allowed Poteat's retaliation claim to proceed based on plausible allegations that defendants refused to port her voucher in retaliation for her prior fair housing litigation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Glover v. HPC-Eight, LLC, 2022 WL 1004572 (D. Conn. 2022)

source_file: 9632006_2023_Kalashnikov v. Myfield Lane Homeowners' Association, Inc._ctd
case_name: Kalashnikov v. Myfield Lane Homeowners' Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B); it concerns HOA enforcement of aesthetic rules and alleged national origin/gender discrimination and retaliation.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on discrimination and harassment claims because plaintiffs failed to establish discriminatory motive, but denied summary judgment on retaliation claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether defendants' enforcement actions against plaintiffs were motivated by plaintiffs' protected activity of filing a CHRO complaint.
brief_summary: Valeriy Kalashnikov and Victoria Ledeneva, Russian immigrants, sued Myfield Lane HOA and individual board members alleging discrimination on the basis of national origin, gender, and family status, as well as retaliation for filing a CHRO complaint. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on discrimination and harassment claims (finding no evidence of discriminatory motive), but denied summary judgment on retaliation claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the HOA's enforcement of aesthetic rules was unlawfully retaliatory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Glover v. HPC-Eight, LLC
Lachira v. Sutton
Moses v. St. Vincent's Special Needs Ctr., Inc.

source_file: 9632548_2023_Connecticut Fair Housing Ctr v. CoreLogic Rental Property Solutions, LLC_ctd
case_name: Connecticut Fair Housing Ctr v. CoreLogic Rental Property Solutions, LLC
accommodation_description: Mr. Arroyo, who has a traumatic brain injury, was denied housing based on a criminal background check; the accommodation claim concerns CoreLogic's CrimSAFE screening product that allegedly has discriminatory disparate impact on minorities
key_holding: The court found for CoreLogic on the FHA claims because plaintiffs failed to prove that CrimSAFE caused housing unavailability for protected groups, and found for Mr. Arroyo only on the FCRA claim regarding CoreLogic's unreasonable requirements for consumer file disclosure.
brief_summary: Connecticut Fair Housing Center and Mikhail Arroyo sued CoreLogic Rental Property Solutions, alleging that its CrimSAFE criminal background screening product has a disparate impact on Latino and African American tenants and intentionally facilitates housing discrimination. The court ruled for CoreLogic on the FHA disparate impact and intentional discrimination claims, finding plaintiffs failed to prove housing unavailability, but found for Mr. Arroyo on his FCRA claim, awarding $1,000 statutory and $3,000 punitive damages for CoreLogic's unreasonable requirements that made it impossible for a conserved person to obtain their consumer report.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
SafeCo Ins. Co. of America v. Burr, 551 U.S. 47 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Earlando Samuel v. Robert Rizzo, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled individual, alleges disability discrimination including termination of his live-in aid and harassment by housing authority defendants in retaliation for complaining about conditions.
key_holding: The court dismissed most claims as time-barred but allowed Plaintiff's retaliation and interference claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 to proceed because he adequately alleged harassment that was sufficiently severe or pervasive to create a hostile environment.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Earlando Samuel sued New Castle County Housing Authority and related defendants alleging disability discrimination, retaliation for complaining about unsafe housing conditions, and harassment that made his living situation intolerable. The court dismissed his ADA and Fair Housing Act claims as time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations but allowed his § 3617 retaliation and interference claim to proceed, finding he adequately alleged harassment severe enough to constitute a hostile environment.
key_cases_cited: Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: David Q. Webb v. State of Delaware, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a single homeless male, alleged he was denied housing at the Hope Center because he is not a family with minor children, seeking better housing placement through the DHSS-DSCC emergency hotel voucher program.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiff failed to state a plausible familial status discrimination claim as he is a single homeless male with no minor children, and familial status under § 3602(k) only protects families with minor children.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff David Q. Webb sued the State of Delaware and various hotel defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations after being denied housing at the Hope Center emergency shelter because he was not a family. The court sua sponte screened the complaint and dismissed the FHA claim, finding that familial status under § 3602(k) only protects families with minor children, not single adults, and plaintiff failed to state a plausible claim for relief.
key_cases_cited: Alexander v. Sandoval, 532 U.S. 275 (2001)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Kirk Ernest Root v. Nilo Salazar
accommodation_description: Root requested to rent a unit in Salazar's duplex; Salazar refused and made statements indicating he would not rent to a person with a mobility disability.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for Root on the § 3604(c) discriminatory statements claim because Salazar's texts stating he would not rent to a 'handicap person with challenging mobility motor skills' indicated a disability-based preference, but denied summary judgment on the § 3604(f)(1) refusal-to-rent claim because genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether Salazar denied tenancy based on disability or financial qualifications.
brief_summary: Kirk Root, who has cerebral palsy and uses a wheelchair, sued landlord Nilo Salazar alleging disability discrimination after Salazar refused to rent a duplex unit to Root and made statements indicating he would not rent to persons with mobility disabilities. The court granted partial summary judgment for Root on his discriminatory statements claim under § 3604(c) but denied summary judgment on his refusal-to-rent claim under § 3604(f)(1), finding genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether Salazar denied tenancy based on disability or Root's financial qualifications.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
White v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 475 F.3d 898 (7th Cir. 2007)
Soules v. U.S. Dep't of Housing & Urban Dev., 967 F.2d 817 (2d Cir. 1992)

case_name: Clay v. IH4 Property Florida, L.P.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that defendants remediate toxic mold and/or provide alternative housing as a reasonable accommodation for S.C.'s severe asthma, which caused disproportionately severe reactions to mold exposure compared to non-disabled tenants.
key_holding: Mold remediation can constitute a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B) when the disabled tenant suffers disproportionately severe reactions to mold compared to non-disabled tenants, even though the accommodation addresses a condition that affects all tenants, because the disability creates heightened vulnerability requiring greater remediation efforts.
brief_summary: Grace and Stephen Clay, on behalf of their minor son S.C. who suffers from severe asthma, sued IH4 Property Florida, L.P. and Invitation Homes Realty, LLC alleging the landlords failed to reasonably accommodate S.C.'s disability by refusing to remediate toxic mold that caused life-threatening asthma exacerbations after Hurricane Irma. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the FHA claim (Count I), holding that plaintiffs plausibly alleged mold remediation as a reasonable accommodation because S.C.'s disability creates heightened vulnerability to mold exposure beyond that of non-disabled tenants.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9637225_2019_United States v. Advocate Law Groups of Florida, P.A._flmd
case_name: United States v. Advocate Law Groups of Florida, P.A.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case involves predatory loan modification and foreclosure rescue services allegedly targeted at Hispanic homeowners based on national origin.
key_holding: The court dismissed claims under §§ 3604(a), 3604(b), and 3605, finding that offering loan modification assistance does not constitute 'making a dwelling unavailable' under § 3604(a), that reverse redlining theories do not extend to foreclosure rescue services, and that Defendants did not provide 'financial assistance' within the meaning of § 3605.
brief_summary: The United States and three Hispanic homeowners sued a law firm and its principals alleging national origin discrimination in connection with predatory loan modification and foreclosure rescue services targeting Hispanic homeowners. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that offering assistance with obtaining mortgage modifications does not constitute 'making a dwelling unavailable' under § 3604(a), does not fall within § 3604(b)'s services provision, and does not constitute 'financial assistance' under § 3605.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp., 800 F.3d 1262 (11th Cir. 2015)
People ex rel. Madigan v. Wildermuth, 91 N.E.3d 865 (Ill. 2017)
Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 9639620_2020_Cobb v. Citrus Falls at Westchase Apartments, LLC_flmd
case_name: Tyrone Cobb v. Citrus Falls at Westchase Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Wheelchair user with end-stage renal failure, congestive heart failure, and mobility impairments seeks repair of noncompliant common areas and physical modifications to his apartment including pathways, ramps, door hardware, and bathroom fixtures.
key_holding: The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that § 3604(f)(3)(B) does not require landlords to undertake significant structural improvements, and requests for construction or repair are not actionable under subsection (B).
brief_summary: Tyrone Cobb, a wheelchair user with multiple disabilities, sued his apartment landlord seeking repairs and physical modifications to common areas and his apartment under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiff's demand for structural improvements and repairs does not constitute a reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B), which covers accommodations in rules, policies, practices, or services—not construction or repair requests—and dismissed the complaint without prejudice with leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Weiss v. 2100 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 941 F. Supp. 2d 1337 (S.D. Fla. 2013)
Harding v. Orlando Apartments, LLC, 748 F.3d 1128 (11th Cir. 2014)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9639806_2020_United States v. Advocate Law Groups of Florida, P.A._flmd
case_name: United States v. Advocate Law Groups of Florida, P.A.
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED - No reasonable accommodation request involved; case concerns alleged predatory loan modification services targeting Hispanic homeowners.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that allegations that defendants deliberately targeted Hispanic homeowners with predatory loan modification and foreclosure rescue services sufficiently stated a claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 for interference with housing rights, and that the § 3614 pattern-or-practice claim survived based on the § 3617 findings.
brief_summary: The United States and three Hispanic Intervenor-Plaintiffs sued a law firm and its partners alleging they deliberately targeted Hispanic homeowners with a predatory loan modification and foreclosure rescue scheme. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the § 3617 interference claims and § 3614 pattern-or-practice claim sufficiently alleged, while noting prior dismissal of the § 3604(a), § 3604(b), and § 3605 claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Sofarelli v. Pinellas Cnty., 931 F.2d 718 (11th Cir. 1991)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Ga. State Conf. of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Mamberto Real v. Judith Moran, Frank Diaz Gines, and Presbyterian Homes & Housing Foundation of Florida, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case involves alleged discriminatory refusal to rent based on national origin (Cuban) and retaliation under § 3617.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint with prejudice as a shotgun pleading that failed to comply with Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, with plaintiff having had four prior opportunities to properly plead his national origin discrimination and retaliation claims.
brief_summary: Mamberto Real, a Cuban national origin individual, sued Presbyterian Homes and its employees alleging he was denied an apartment and subjected to ongoing delays as retaliation because of his national origin. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding Real's complaint was an impermissible shotgun pleading that failed to give defendants adequate notice of the claims against them despite four prior opportunities to properly plead.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Weiland v. Palm Beach Cnty. Sheriff's Office, 792 F.3d 1313 (11th Cir. 2015)
Boatman v. Town of Oakland, 76 F.3d 341 (11th Cir. 1996)
Philippeaux v. Apartment Inv. & Mgmt. Co., 598 F. App'x 640 (11th Cir. 2015)
Anderson v. District Bd. of Trustees of Cent. Fla. Cmty. Coll., 77 F.3d 364 (11th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 9644128_2022_Rood v. Town of Ft. Myers Beach_flmd

case_name: James Nathaniel Douse v. Sabrina Traeger, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was made; plaintiff made vague references to discrimination under the Fair Housing Act without identifying a protected class or discriminatory conduct covered under the statute.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims were dismissed for lack of subject matter jurisdiction because he failed to identify his membership in any protected class (race, color, religion, sex, disability, familial status, or national origin) and failed to describe any discriminatory conduct covered under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff James Douse sued his homeowners association and an employee over a disputed pool construction deposit and assessment charges, vaguely referencing the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the case for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding the FHA claims were wholly insubstantial because plaintiff failed to identify his membership in any protected class or allege discriminatory conduct covered under the statute.
key_cases_cited: Fountain v. Metro. Atlanta Rapid Trans. Auth., 688 F.2d 1038 (11th Cir. 1982)
Bell v. Hood, 327 U.S. 678 (1946)
Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better Env't, 523 U.S. 83 (1998)

source_file: 9645190_2023_Parisi v. Sabal Springs Homeowners Association Inc._flmd
case_name: Parisi v. Sabal Springs Homeowners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Parisi requested a waiver of the two-dog rule to allow his emotional support dog (Rokco) and co-resident Clark's two pet dogs to remain in the residence.
key_holding: The court granted in part and denied in part the motion to strike affirmative defenses, striking the Third (unclean hands) and Fourth (not a reasonable accommodation) defenses as improper, while allowing the Second defense (failure to request accommodation) to proceed.
brief_summary: Joseph Parisi, a person with depression and anxiety who uses an emotional support dog, and Terry Clark sued Sabal Springs Homeowners Association alleging the HOA failed to reasonably accommodate their request to keep three dogs (the support dog plus two pet dogs) despite a two-dog rule. The court ruled on plaintiffs' motion to strike affirmative defenses, striking the Third (unclean hands) and Fourth (not a reasonable accommodation) defenses as improper while denying the motion as to the Second defense regarding whether Clark requested an accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Hawn v. Shoreline Towers Phase 1 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 347 F. App'x 464 (11th Cir. 2009)
Loren v. Sasser, 309 F.3d 1296 (11th Cir. 2002)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Weiss v. 2100 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 941 F. Supp. 2d 1337 (S.D. Fla. 2013)
Hunt v. Aimco Properties, L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)

case_name: David Wallace v. Carefree Shadowwood LLC
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support animal (ESA dog named Zena) at the manufactured home community following a dog altercation incident.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, holding that Mr. Wallace plausibly alleged Shadowwood made housing unavailable because of his disability by forcing him to choose between his home and his assistance animal, and that Shadowwood failed to conduct an individualized assessment before requiring Zena's removal.
brief_summary: David Wallace sued Carefree Shadowwood LLC, the owner of Bahia Vista Estates manufactured home community in Sarasota, Florida, alleging the housing provider violated the FHA by forcing him to choose between his home and his emotional support animal Zena after a dog altercation, and by failing to accommodate his disability-related need for the ESA. The court denied Shadowwood's motion to dismiss both claims, finding Wallace plausibly alleged that Shadowwood made housing unavailable because of his disability and improperly refused to consider his accommodation request without an individualized assessment.
key_cases_cited: Hunt v. Aimco Props., L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Smith v. Powdrill, No. CV 12-06388 DDP RZX, 2013 WL 5786586 (C.D. Cal. 2013)
Friedel v. Park Place Cmty. LLC, 747 F. App'x 775 (11th Cir. 2018)

case_name: George Friedel & Kathleen Friedel v. Sun Communities, Inc. & Park Place Community, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested to keep their dog Maggie as an emotional comfort assistance animal to assist George Friedel with depression, after receiving a notice of eviction for having an unauthorized animal.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' discrimination, reasonable accommodation, and retaliation claims as implausible because the most likely explanation for defendants' eviction notice was the prior jury verdict finding Maggie to be a dangerous animal, not discriminatory motive related to plaintiffs' accommodation requests.
brief_summary: George Friedel and Kathleen Friedel sued Sun Communities and Park Place for disability discrimination, failure to reasonably accommodate, and retaliation after receiving an eviction notice for their dog Maggie, who was found by a jury in prior litigation to be dangerous. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding that plaintiffs' claims were implausible because the most plausible explanation for the eviction was defendants' prior jury verdict, not discriminatory motive related to plaintiffs' accommodation requests.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Hunt v. Aimco Properties, L.P., 814 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2016)
Hall v. United Ins. Co., 367 F.3d 1255 (11th Cir. 2004)

case_name: Rita Fox v. Dana James Gaines et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; case involves sexual harassment (quid pro quo and hostile housing environment) by landlord against tenant.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist on plaintiff's quid pro quo harassment, hostile housing environment, retaliation, and interference claims under the FHA.
brief_summary: Rita Fox sued her landlords Dana James Gaines and Lucille Gaines for sexual harassment and discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, alleging quid pro quo harassment and a hostile housing environment created by the landlord's sexual demands. The court denied defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact on all claims regarding whether sexual favors were conditioning on continued tenancy and whether defendant's post-relationship eviction proceedings constituted unlawful discrimination and retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Fox v. Gaines, 4 F.4th 1293 (11th Cir. 2021)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Quigg v. Thomas County Sch. Dist., 814 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Chalissa Olivera Truesdale v. Venice Arms, Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request at issue; case involves allegations of racial discrimination in cooperative housing rules enforcement
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because the amended complaint was a shotgun pleading failing to identify specific statutory provisions, and even interpreting the claims charitably, section 3604 claims failed as they apply only to buying/selling/leasing transactions (not cooperative rule enforcement against existing owners) while section 3617 claims required allegations of conduct severe or pervasive enough to be threatening or violent.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Chalissa Olivera Truesdale, a Hispanic woman and member of a 55-and-over housing cooperative, sued Venice Arms, Inc. alleging the Swedish-dominated cooperative association engaged in racially discriminatory housing practices including enforcement of no-renter rules, guest policies, and association with a racist board motion to exclude minorities. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss with leave to amend, finding the complaint was a shotgun pleading that failed to state viable FHA claims because section 3604 applies only to housing transactions and the section 3617 claim lacked allegations of severe or violent discriminatory conduct required to state a claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lawrence v. Courtyards at Deerwood Ass'n, Inc., 318 F. Supp. 2d 1133 (S.D. Fla. 2004)
Gourlay v. Forest Lake Ests. Civic Ass'n of Port Richey, Inc., 276 F. Supp. 2d 1222 (M.D. Fla. 2003)
Sofarelli v. Pinellas County, 931 F.2d 718 (11th Cir. 1991)

case_name: Eli Portnoy v. MEI Condominium Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested accommodation to communicate with someone other than the Board President regarding residency business due to stress-related disability.
key_holding: The court denied the individual board member's motion to dismiss, finding that Florida state immunity statutes cannot bar federal FHA claims and that plaintiff adequately alleged individual liability against the board president for her personal involvement in discriminatory acts.
brief_summary: Eli Portnoy, a disabled condominium resident, sued MEI Condominium Association and its Board President Anna Strickstein-Zandman alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, including failure to reasonably accommodate his request to communicate with someone other than the Board President due to his stress-related disability. The court denied the individual board member's motion to dismiss, rejecting her claims of immunity under Florida state statutes and finding that Portnoy adequately pleaded individual liability under the FHA for her personal involvement in discriminatory acts.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Housing Opportunities Project for Excellence, Inc. v. Key Colony No. 4 Condo. Assoc., 510 F. Supp. 2d 1003 (S.D. Fla. 2007)
Sabal Palm Condos. of Pine Island Ridge Ass'n, Inc. v. Fischer, 2014 WL 988767 (S.D. Fla. 2014)

case_name: Hannah Gorman v. Breeze Condominium Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for an emotional support dog for Mr. Tomlinson (a person with a disability) to ameliorate the effects of his disability, which was denied based on the dog's size and breed.
key_holding: Plaintiff, as a person associated with a disabled individual under § 3604(f), has standing to bring FHA claims including failure to accommodate; the complaint adequately alleges that Mr. Tomlinson is disabled, requested an ESA accommodation, and was denied, and that plaintiff suffered adverse actions causally connected to protected activity.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Hannah Gorman sued Breeze Condominium Association, its property management company Nautica Management, and property manager Richard Muller, alleging they denied her companion's request for an emotional support animal, refused to renew her lease, and denied approval of a second rental unit in retaliation for filing a HUD complaint. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss all counts, finding that Gorman, as a person associated with a disabled individual, has standing to assert FHA claims and that she adequately pled a failure to accommodate, retaliation, and interference with housing rights.
key_cases_cited: Hawn v. Shoreline Towers Phase 1 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 347 Fed. App'x. 464 (11th Cir. 2009)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)
Wasserman v. Three Seasons Ass'n No. 1, Inc., 998 F. Supp. 1445 (S.D. Fla. 1998)
Moore v. Camden Prop. Tr., 816 F. App'x 324 (11th Cir. 2020)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Quincy Bernard Jones v. Featherstone Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Request for installation of railings on the retaining wall in front of his duplex unit to address fear of falling while playing catch with relatives
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendant because plaintiff failed to establish he had a qualifying disability under the FHA, the requested retaining wall railings were neither reasonable nor necessary, and plaintiff refused to pay for the modification as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Quincy Jones sued Featherstone Homeowners Association alleging failure to accommodate or modify under the FHA by refusing to install railings on a retaining wall, claiming his paranoia and schizophrenia made him fear falling while playing catch with relatives. The court granted summary judgment for the HOA, finding that Jones failed to establish a qualifying disability, the requested modification was not reasonable or necessary, and Jones refused to pay for the modification as required under § 3604(f)(3)(A).
key_cases_cited: Johnson v. Jennings, 772 F. App'x 822 (11th Cir. 2019)
Hawn v. Shoreline Towers Phase 1 Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 347 F. App'x 464 (11th Cir. 2009)
Alonso Cano v. 245 C & C, LLC, 2019 WL 11769102 (S.D. Fla. 2019)
Peklun v. Tierra Del Mar Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 2015 WL 8029840 (S.D. Fla. 2015)
Schwarz v. City of Treasure Island, 544 F.3d 1201 (11th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9658922_2023_WODA COOPER DEVELOPMENT INC v. CITY OF WARNER ROBINS GEORGIA_gamd
case_name: Woda Cooper Development, Inc. v. City of Warner Robins
accommodation_description: Affordable housing developers allege the City rescinded support for a LIHTC-funded affordable housing development in retaliation for exercising FHA rights.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' disparate treatment claim because affordable housing developers cannot establish they were discriminated against 'because of' their race, but found genuine issues of material fact exist on the retaliation claim because defense counsel's threats of criminal prosecution and LIHTC revocation during litigation constituted adverse action taken in response to plaintiffs' pursuit of FHA claims.
brief_summary: Affordable housing developers sued the City of Warner Robins, Georgia alleging the City violated the FHA by rescinding support for a downtown affordable housing development (disparate treatment) and by making threatening statements during litigation in retaliation for pursuing FHA claims. The court granted summary judgment for the City on the disparate treatment claim, finding plaintiffs failed to establish intentional discrimination based on race, but denied summary judgment on the retaliation claim, finding genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether defense counsel's threats of criminal prosecution and LIHTC revocation constituted unlawful retaliation.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green
Burlington N. & Santa Fe Ry. Co. v. White
Meyer v. Holley
Texas Dept. of Cmty. Affairs v. Burdine
Bone v. Vill. Club, Inc.

source_file: 9659627_2024_MILLER v. GEORGIA DEPARTMENT OF COMMUNITY SUPERVISION_gamd
case_name: Tracy A. Miller v. Georgia Department of Community Supervision, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff challenges his assignment to Bostick Nursing Center as discriminatory, arguing the Board made housing unavailable by not placing him in his preferred location.
key_holding: The Board did not discriminate against plaintiff under the FHA because it considered his disability and assigned him to Bostick specifically to accommodate his needs as an elderly, disabled parolee; the Board could not have placed him elsewhere because his parole application lacked that information.
brief_summary: Tracy A. Miller, a paraplegic parolee, sued the Georgia Board of Pardons and Paroles and Bostick Nursing Center officials under § 1983, the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and FHA, alleging disability discrimination in his placement at a nursing facility for parolees. The court found that because the Board assigned plaintiff to Bostick specifically to accommodate his disability-related needs rather than to exclude him from housing, and because his preferred housing information was not included in his parole application, plaintiff failed to state a viable disability discrimination or FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Thompson v. Davis
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale
Kirby v. Siegelman

source_file: 9660196_2022_Fulton County, Georgia v. Wells Fargo _ Co._gand
case_name: Fulton County, Georgia v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request - case involves claims of discriminatory predatory lending and equity stripping targeting minority borrowers
key_holding: The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss on statute of limitations grounds but permitted plaintiffs to amend, while finding that plaintiffs adequately pled proximate cause for property tax injuries and statistical disparities to survive disparate impact claims.
brief_summary: Three Georgia counties (Fulton, DeKalb, and Cobb) sued Wells Fargo alleging a predatory lending scheme that targeted minority borrowers through discriminatory mortgage origination, servicing, and foreclosure practices in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss on statute of limitations grounds but allowed plaintiffs to file an amended complaint, while denying dismissal of plaintiffs' disparate impact claims after finding adequate statistical allegations and proximate cause for certain property-related injuries.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Inclusive Communities Project v. Lincoln Prop. Co.
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
City of Miami v. Bank of Am. Corp.

case_name: Price v. Howard
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlord required her Black partner to undergo a background check and pay a $50 fee as a condition of leasing, based on discriminatory racial animus.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state a plausible claim for disparate treatment under the FHA because she did not allege any facts suggesting she was actually treated differently than similarly situated White prospective applicants, and her conclusory 'information and belief' allegations about Howard's treatment of White tenants lacked factual support.
brief_summary: Katie Price, a Black woman, sued her landlord Michael Howard alleging race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. § 1981, claiming Howard required her Black partner to undergo a racially-motivated background check and pay a fee as a condition of leasing. The court granted Howard's motion to dismiss, finding Price's complaint consisted entirely of conclusory allegations that failed to plausibly allege disparate treatment because she did not plead any facts showing she was treated differently than similarly situated White prospective tenants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Jackson v. Okaloosa Cnty. Fla., 21 F.3d 1531 (11th Cir. 1994)
Bonasera v. City of Norcross, 342 F. App'x 581 (11th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Katia Price v. Michael Howard
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — the case involves a landlord's requirement that the Black plaintiff's partner submit to a background check and pay a fee, allegedly not imposed on similarly situated White tenants.
key_holding: The court denied the defendant's motion to dismiss, finding that the plaintiff adequately alleged disparate treatment by pleading sufficient facts showing she was treated differently than a similarly situated White tenant regarding the landlord's visitor background check requirement.
brief_summary: Katia Price, a Black woman, sued landlord Michael Howard alleging race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. § 1981 after Howard required her partner to undergo a background check and pay a fee to lease his property. The court denied Howard's motion to dismiss, finding Price adequately alleged disparate treatment by providing sufficient facts supporting her belief that similarly situated White tenants were not subjected to the same background check requirements.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Jackson v. Okaloosa Cnty., 21 F.3d 1531 (11th Cir. 1994)
Lewis v. City of Union City, 918 F.3d 1213 (11th Cir. 2019)
Bonasera v. City of Norcross, 342 F. App'x 581 (11th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Lau v. Honolulu Park Place, AOAO
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested permission to park their wheelchair-accessible modified minivan in ground-level guest parking spaces (which have no ramps) rather than their assigned third-level parking spaces (which require using steep ramps that damage the vehicle and prevent Garrick's access).
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiffs adequately pleaded Article III standing and a valid FHA reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), rejecting arguments that the accommodation was unreasonable, that the injury was to property rather than person, and that the AOAO lacked authority to grant the requested parking accommodation.
brief_summary: Garrick Lau (a quadriplegic), along with his parents Wilson and Mabel Lau, sued Honolulu Park Place AOAO and its board members alleging the HOA violated the FHA by refusing to allow them to park their wheelchair-accessible minivan in ground-level guest parking spaces, which are the only spaces that accommodate their modified vehicle without ramp damage. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pleaded standing and a facially plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: U.S. v. California Mobile Home Park Management Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Janush v. Charities Hous. Dev. Corp., 169 F. Supp. 2d 1133 (N.D. Cal. 2000)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Garrick Lau, et al. v. Honolulu Park Place, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to park a wheelchair-accessible modified van in condominium guest parking (ground level) rather than assigned third-level parking space, where driving up ramps caused the van's undercarriage to strike the ground.
key_holding: The court held that the injunctive relief claim was moot because the HOA formally granted the parking accommodation during litigation, but the emotional distress damages claim survived summary judgment because plaintiffs' testimony was sufficient under Ninth Circuit law to support a jury award for emotional distress damages.
brief_summary: The Laus, whose adult son Garrick is a quadriplegic, sued their condominium association under the FHAA after it denied their request to park Garrick's wheelchair-accessible van in guest parking (ground level) rather than his assigned third-level parking space where the modified van's undercarriage scraped on ramps. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the injunctive relief claim as moot (because defendants granted the accommodation during litigation), but denied summary judgment on the emotional distress damages claim, finding plaintiffs' testimony sufficient to support a jury award.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Maui Family Life Center, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claims Defendant denied her rental assistance application based on her race and sexual orientation, allegedly telling her the Christian funder would not provide benefits to someone like her.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim because she provided only vague, conclusory allegations that failed to establish the defendant denied her rental assistance application because of her race or sexual orientation, as opposed to other eligibility grounds.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jason Scutt sued Maui Family Life Center, alleging the organization denied her COVID-19 rental assistance application because of her race and LGBTQIA+ status, violating the Fair Housing Act and Title VI. The court dismissed the First Amended Complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the allegations were too vague and conclusory to plausibly show discriminatory motive.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Fobbs v. Holy Cross Health Sys. Corp., 29 F.3d 1439 (9th Cir. 1994)

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Kelli Dorris, Xiayin (Gaoquiang) Lin, and Charlene Chen
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged eviction and harassment because of transgender/LGBT status and religion, rather than requesting a specific reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed the First Amended Complaint because Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts plausibly demonstrating discriminatory intent by landlords based on religion or transgender/LGBT status, and failed to establish agency relationship with co-tenant Dorris.
brief_summary: Jason Scutt, a pro se plaintiff, sued landlords Xiayin Lin and Charlene Chen, and co-tenant Kelli Dorris alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on religion and transgender/LGBT status, claiming they harassed and attempted to evict her because of these protected characteristics. The court dismissed the First Amended Complaint with leave to amend, finding Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts showing discriminatory intent or establishing agency relationship between the landlords and Dorris.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Bostock v. Clayton Cnty., 140 S. Ct. 1731 (2020)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Kelli Dorris, Xiayin (Gaoquiang) Lin, and Charlene Chen
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves claims of harassment based on transgender status and Jewish religion, and landlords' failure to prevent hostile housing environment.
key_holding: Dorris's claim dismissed because she is merely a co-tenant without agency authority over landlords; but claims against Lin and Chen may proceed for hostile housing environment under § 3617 because plaintiff adequately alleged Dorris harassed her based on sex/religion, harassment was severe or pervasive, and landlords knew of but failed to remedy the harassment.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jason Scutt sued his former landlords and co-tenant alleging they discriminated against him based on his transgender status and Jewish religion, and that landlords failed to prevent a hostile housing environment created by co-tenant Dorris. The court dismissed the claim against Dorris because she was merely a co-tenant without agency authority over the landlords, but allowed the hostile housing environment claims against the landlords to proceed because plaintiff adequately pled Dorris harassed him because of protected characteristics, the harassment was severe or pervasive, and landlords knew of but failed to remedy it.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Hicks v. Makaha Valley Plantation Homeowners Ass'n, 2015 WL 4041531 (D. Haw. 2015)

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Family Life Center, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges denial of emergency housing rental funds based on race and sexual orientation discrimination, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff sufficiently stated a claim for disparate treatment under the Fair Housing Act based on allegations that emergency housing funds were denied due to race and sexual orientation, while dismissing Plaintiff's Title VI claim to the extent it alleged sexual orientation discrimination (as Title VI only covers race, color, or national origin)
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Jason Scutt sued Family Life Center, Inc. alleging the organization denied her emergency housing rental funds based on race and sexual orientation discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act and Title VI. The court denied Defendant's motion to dismiss the FHA claim, finding Plaintiff sufficiently alleged disparate treatment; the court dismissed Plaintiff's Title VI claim only to the extent it was based on sexual orientation, as Title VI does not cover that protected class.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Holley v. Crank, 258 F.3d 1127 (9th Cir. 2001)
Fobbs v. Holy Cross Health Sys. Corp., 29 F.3d 1439 (9th Cir. 1994)

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Martin Norton, Carrie Johnson aka Norton
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlord harassment and use of slurs targeting her religion and gender during tenancy in an illegal structure.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because plaintiff's conclusory allegations failed to demonstrate that defendants caused the purported harm because of her gender or religion, and many incidents appeared to be general landlord-tenant disputes unrelated to protected grounds.
brief_summary: Jason Scutt sued landlords Martin and Carrie Norton alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on harassment and use of slurs targeting her religion and gender while she rented an illegal structure on their property. The court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, finding the allegations were conclusory and failed to adequately demonstrate the alleged harm was caused by protected characteristics rather than general landlord-tenant disputes.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Harris v. Itzhaki

case_name: Jason Scutt v. Martin Norton
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleged disparate treatment harassment based on religion, sex, and LGBTQ status
key_holding: The court granted judgment for defendants because plaintiff's allegations were conclusory, internally contradictory, and unsupported by the documentary evidence, failing to establish plausible facts showing discriminatory intent as required for an FHA disparate treatment claim.
brief_summary: Jason Scutt, a pro se plaintiff, sued her former landlords Martin Norton and Carrie Johnson alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on religion, sex, and LGBTQ status discrimination during her tenancy. The court granted defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings, finding plaintiff's allegations conclusory, internally contradictory, and implausible given her history of filing similar complaints and failure to provide evidence supporting discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Dahlia v. Rodriguez, 735 F.3d 1060 (9th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 9665527_2023_United States v. Albert C. Kobayashi, Inc._hid
case_name: United States of America v. Albert C. Kobayashi, Inc.
accommodation_description: Systemic design and construction accessibility requirements for covered multifamily dwellings (accessible routes, public use and common use areas) rather than individual tenant accommodation requests
key_holding: The court denied the defendants' motions for partial summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the designer, contractor, and developer defendants participated in the design and construction of multifamily housing with common and public use areas that are readily accessible to persons with disabilities.
brief_summary: The United States sued design and construction defendants alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to design and construct multifamily dwellings (Napili, Napilihau, Palehua, and Wailea) in Hawaii with public and common use areas that are readily accessible to persons with disabilities. The court denied the defendants' motions for partial summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist regarding the extent of each defendant's participation in the design and construction process that would render them liable under § 3604(f)(3)(C).
key_cases_cited: Mont. Fair Hous., Inc. v. Am. Capital Dev., Inc., 81 F. Supp. 2d 1057 (D. Mont. 1999)
Balt. Neighborhoods, Inc. v. Rommel Builders, Inc., 3 F. Supp. 2d 661 (D. Md. 1998)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
Salisbury v. City of Santa Monica, 998 F.3d 852 (9th Cir. 2021)
Harris v. Cnty. of Orange, 17 F.4th 849 (9th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Nathan Earl Aiwohi, et al. v. Bank of America, N.A., et al.
accommodation_description: Loan modification requests by mortgagors alleging denial based on race and national origin; underlying claim involves discriminatory lending and servicing practices targeting racial minorities.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claims because they failed to plead discriminatory intent for disparate treatment and failed to provide statistical evidence showing significantly disproportionate impact on protected classes for disparate impact.
brief_summary: Eight individual mortgagors sued Bank of America and Bank of New York Mellon under RICO and the Fair Housing Act, alleging Defendants engaged in predatory lending targeting racial minorities, falsified loan documents for fraudulent foreclosures, and denied loan modifications based on race. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the RICO claim failed because it was based on litigation activity that cannot serve as RICO predicate acts, and the FHA claims failed because Plaintiffs alleged no discriminatory intent and provided no statistical evidence of disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
United Bhd. of Carpenters & Joiners v. Bldg. & Constr. Trades Dep't, AFL-CIO, 770 F.3d 834 (9th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Lazarus v. Ouansafi
accommodation_description: A reasonable accommodation claim is noted as remaining against other defendants but was not analyzed in this order granting Ouansafi summary judgment.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish that defendant Ouansafi knew or should have known about the alleged harassment by other tenants or that he personally participated in or ratified the alleged retaliation, and mere supervisory authority is insufficient for individual liability under the FHA.
brief_summary: Tangee Renee Lazarus, a Black woman residing in public housing administered by the Hawaii Public Housing Authority, sued Executive Director Hakim Ouansafi and other HPHA employees alleging Fair Housing Act claims for hostile housing environment based on race and retaliation. The court granted Ouansafi's motion for summary judgment, finding plaintiff failed to provide evidence that Ouansafi knew of or personally participated in the alleged harassment or retaliation, and that mere supervisory authority is insufficient for individual FHA liability.
key_cases_cited: Fielder v. Sterling Park Homeowners Ass'n, 914 F. Supp. 2d 1222 (W.D. Wash. 2012)
Hicks v. Makaha Valley Plantation Homeowners Ass'n, No. CIV. 14-00254 HG-BMK, 2015 WL 4041531 (D. Haw. 2015)
San Pedro Hotel Co. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Hansen v. United States, 7 F.3d 137 (9th Cir. 1993)

case_name: Robert LaRosa, Iva LaRosa, and Intermountain Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. River Quarry Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Mr. LaRosa requested a reasonable accommodation to keep his companion dog Sid without paying the pet fee as an emotional support animal for his PTSD.
key_holding: The court dismissed the reasonable accommodation denial claim because Sid was allowed to remain with the LaRosas throughout the entire tenancy even though formal approval was delayed until October 13, 2017; however, the court allowed the interference claim under § 3617 to proceed based on allegations that defendants misrepresented the outcome of Dr. Wilper's verification.
brief_summary: Robert and Iva LaRosa and Intermountain Fair Housing Council sued River Quarry Apartments and related defendants for allegedly wrongfully denying a reasonable accommodation request for an emotional support animal to assist with Mr. LaRosa's PTSD. The court dismissed the reasonable accommodation denial claim, finding no violation because the dog was allowed to remain throughout the review process even though formal approval was delayed 45 days; however, the court allowed plaintiffs' interference claim under § 3617 to proceed based on allegations that defendants misrepresented Dr. Wilper's statements during the verification process.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Prindable v. Ass'n of Apt. Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 304 F. Supp. 2d 1245 (D. Haw. 2003)

case_name: Josephine Guion v. Bonner Homeless Transitions Board of Directors
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation was requested; plaintiff alleges race-based harassment and hostile housing environment discrimination rather than disability accommodation.
key_holding: Guion's Fair Housing Act race discrimination and retaliation claims are time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations and fail on the merits because no reasonable juror could conclude that any of Bonner Defendants' actions were discriminatory based on race.
brief_summary: Josephine Guion, an African American resident at a nonprofit transitional housing facility in Hope, Idaho, sued the facility operator and staff alleging race-based harassment, hostile housing environment, defamation, intentional infliction of emotional distress, retaliation under the FHA, and fraud. The court granted Bonner Defendants' motion for summary judgment on all claims, finding that the FHA claims were time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations and that, even viewing the evidence favorably to Guion, no reasonable juror could find that defendants' actions were discriminatory based on race.
key_cases_cited: Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)
Levald, Inc. v. City of Palm Desert, 998 F.2d 680 (9th Cir. 1993)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Orr v. Bank of Am., 285 F.3d 764 (9th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Hoffman v. Pedersen
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege defendants refused to make reasonable accommodations for Kelly Stone's disability, but the specific accommodation requested is not described in the opinion.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claim was barred by the two-year statute of limitations—no discriminatory conduct was alleged to have occurred after October 23, 2016, and plaintiffs failed to produce evidence that HUD review tolled the limitations period.
brief_summary: William Hoffman and Kelly Stone sued their former landlord Heather Pedersen and Bookpoint, LLC alleging Fair Housing Act violations, Idaho Consumer Protection Act violations, and negligence related to their eviction from a Sandpoint, Idaho apartment. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on all claims because the two-year statute of limitations had expired with no evidence of discriminatory conduct after October 23, 2016, and no evidence of HUD tolling.

case_name: Josephine Guion v. Bonner Homeless Transitions Board of Directors
accommodation_description: The accommodation request at issue in the underlying case is not described in this procedural ruling on Rule 60(b) motions.
key_holding: The court denied Guion's motion to vacate summary judgment because she failed to establish fraud on the court by clear and convincing evidence, as her allegations consisted of legal challenges to the court's prior conclusions rather than actual evidence of fraud, and a mere misstatement of a date does not constitute fraud on the court.
brief_summary: Josephine Guion moved to vacate the court's prior summary judgment ruling dismissing her Fair Housing Act claims, alleging fraud on the court, and also moved to disqualify the presiding judge and for an indicative ruling under Rule 62.1. The court denied all three motions, finding that Guion failed to establish fraud on the court by clear and convincing evidence and that her allegations were merely legal challenges to the court's prior conclusions rather than evidence of an unconscionable scheme to improperly influence the court.
key_cases_cited: Estate of Stonehill v. United States, 660 F.3d 415 (9th Cir. 2011)
Alexander v. Robertson, 882 F.2d 421 (9th Cir. 1989)
Liteky v. United States, 510 U.S. 540 (1994)
Payton v. Davis, 906 F.3d 812 (9th Cir. 2018)
Chambers v. NASCO, Inc., 501 U.S. 32 (1991)

case_name: James Alan Gerdon v. Reynold French
accommodation_description: This is not a housing accommodation case; plaintiff James Gerdon brings a civil rights action under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 against a probation officer for alleged criminal acts (breaking and entering, threats, taking property), requesting injunctive relief to stop alleged persecution for being an 'alternative lifestyle person'
key_holding: The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss without prejudice because plaintiff's complaint failed to establish federal jurisdiction, did not state a cognizable Fair Housing Act claim (42 U.S.C. § 3631 was inapplicable as no housing discrimination was alleged), and failed to state a plausible § 1983 claim for violation of constitutional rights; plaintiff was given leave to amend within 30 days.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff James Gerdon sued probation officer Reynold French under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and various criminal statutes, alleging the officer came to his home with guns, used threats and physical force, and took his property while persecuting him for being an 'alternative lifestyle person.' The court granted French's motion to dismiss without prejudice, finding Gerdon failed to establish federal jurisdiction, failed to state a viable FHA claim, and failed to allege a plausible constitutional violation under § 1983, though granting leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gonzaga Univ. v. Doe, 536 U.S. 273 (2002)
Will v. Michigan Dep't of State Police, 491 U.S. 58 (1989)
Ex parte Young, 209 U.S. 123 (1908)

source_file: 9668168_2023_Intermountain Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. Tomlinson _ Associates, Inc._idd
case_name: Intermountain Fair Housing Council, Inc. v. Tomlinson & Associates, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for reasonable accommodation for a parking permit (mentioned in factual background but not analyzed as disability claim)
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on plaintiff's FHA claims but limited recovery to conduct within the two-year statute of limitations (October 2018 steering, summer 2020 parking enforcement, summer 2021 refusal to add roommate), rejecting the continuing violations doctrine because plaintiff failed to identify a specific discriminatory practice and there were large gaps between alleged incidents.
brief_summary: Intermountain Fair Housing Council sued Tomlinson & Associates and related entities alleging racial discrimination in housing at two Boise apartment complexes. The court denied summary judgment on plaintiff's FHA claims but held that only claims arising from October 2018 steering, summer 2020 discriminatory parking enforcement, and summer 2021 refusal to add an Iraqi American tenant's roommate were timely, rejecting the continuing violations doctrine because plaintiff failed to identify a specific discriminatory practice spanning the alleged six-year period.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Lehman v. Roberts, 442 U.S. 707 (1979)
Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Valencia v. City of Springfield, Illinois
accommodation_description: Request for reasonable accommodation to City's zoning ordinance and spacing rules to allow a group home for three men with intellectual and physical disabilities to remain in a single-family residential district.
key_holding: The City of Springfield's zoning ordinance that imposed a 600-foot spacing requirement between group homes for persons with disabilities violated the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Section 504, and the Plaintiffs established they are aggrieved persons entitled to summary judgment on liability.
brief_summary: Individual Advocacy Group, individual disabled residents, and the estate of a deceased resident sued the City of Springfield, Illinois after the City denied a conditional use permit and sought to evict residents from a group home for persons with disabilities, claiming violations of the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The Court granted partial summary judgment for plaintiffs on liability, finding the City's facially discriminatory zoning ordinance violated federal law and plaintiffs established standing as aggrieved persons.
key_cases_cited: Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Bellwood v. Dwivedi, 895 F.2d 1521 (7th Cir. 1990)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Valencia v. City of Springfield, Illinois
accommodation_description: Group home residents and operator requested a Conditional Permitted Use permit and reasonable accommodation to allow the home to remain open notwithstanding the City's 600-foot spacing rule for community residences serving persons with disabilities.
key_holding: The City violated the FHA by maintaining and enforcing a discriminatory 600-foot spacing rule that treats group homes for persons with disabilities less favorably than similarly situated non-disabled families, and by denying the reasonable accommodation request when it denied the CPU permit without properly addressing the accommodation.
brief_summary: Mary B. Valencia, Individual Advocacy Group, and the United States sued the City of Springfield after the City enforced its 600-foot spacing rule against a group home for three men with intellectual and physical disabilities at 2328 S. Noble Ave., denying their permit request and refusing a reasonable accommodation. The Court granted summary judgment for the United States, finding the City violated the Fair Housing Act by treating group homes for persons with disabilities less favorably than non-disabled families and denying the reasonable accommodation request.
key_cases_cited: Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Larkin v. Mich. Dep't of Social Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
United States v. Balistrieri, 981 F.2d 916 (7th Cir. 1992)
Wis. Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 9673112_2018_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Deutsche Bank National Trust_ilnd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Deutsche Bank National Trust
accommodation_description: No individual accommodation request; fair housing organizations alleged discriminatory maintenance of REO properties based on race
key_holding: Complaint dismissed without prejudice because plaintiffs' statistical evidence was substantially time-barred under FHA's two-year statute of limitations, and plaintiffs failed to adequately plead proximate cause between defendants' alleged abdication policy and plaintiffs' claimed injuries, which were too attenuated to satisfy the direct-relation requirement.
brief_summary: Fair housing organizations sued Deutsche Bank, Ocwen, and Altisource alleging that defendants' REO property maintenance was discriminatorily disparate between white neighborhoods and communities of color, violating the Fair Housing Act. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss without prejudice, finding plaintiffs' statistical allegations substantially time-barred, proximate cause inadequately pleaded due to attenuated chain-of-injury, and discriminatory intent insufficiently alleged.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Cty. of Cook v. Wells Fargo & Co., 314 F. Supp. 3d 975 (N.D. Ill. 2018)

source_file: 9674516_2019_Finch v. Housing Authority of Cook County_ilnd
case_name: Finch v. Housing Authority of Cook County
accommodation_description: Request for three-bedroom apartment voucher as reasonable accommodation for children Ramon's autism and Shorter's bipolar II disorder and learning disabilities, which required separate bedrooms.
key_holding: Ramon's failure to accommodate claim fails because defendant provided him a two-bedroom voucher with his own room, the precise accommodation requested; however, Shorter's failure to accommodate claim survives because she sufficiently alleged disability, defendant's knowledge, need for separate room, and denial.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Adana Finch and her children Ramon and Coya Shorter sued the Housing Authority of Cook County under the Fair Housing Act and Rehabilitation Act, alleging failure to accommodate their disabilities by refusing to approve a three-bedroom voucher so the children could have separate bedrooms due to Ramon's autism and Shorter's bipolar II disorder. The court dismissed Adana's individual claims (she conceded no disability), dismissed Ramon's failure to accommodate claim (he received a two-bedroom voucher with his own room), and dismissed all plaintiffs' disparate treatment claims as conclusory, but allowed Shorter's failure to accommodate claim to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Scheidler v. Indiana, 914 F.3d 535 (7th Cir. 2019)
Geraci v. Union Square Condo. Ass'n, 2017 WL 372303 (N.D. Ill. 2017)

source_file: 9674906_2019_Connectors Realty Group Corporation, The v. State Farm Fire _ Casualty Company_ilnd
case_name: The Connectors Realty Group Corporation v. State Farm Fire & Casualty Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege State Farm discriminated in insurance claims processing based on race and neighborhood racial composition, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: Connectors' disparate treatment claim survives dismissal based on racially discriminatory comments by State Farm adjuster, but Nash's § 1981/1982 and FHA disparate impact claims fail because he lacked sufficient factual allegations connecting his denial to racial animus and submitted industry-wide rather than State Farm-specific statistical evidence
brief_summary: The Connectors Realty Group Corporation and its African-American owner sued State Farm Fire & Casualty Company alleging the insurer racially discriminated in processing property insurance claims by redlining majority-black ZIP Codes on Chicago's South and West Sides. The court denied State Farm's motion to dismiss Connectors' disparate treatment claim based on a racially charged conversation with a claims adjuster, but dismissed Nash's claims for insufficient allegations of racial animus and dismissed Nash's disparate impact claim for reliance on industry-wide rather than State Farm-specific statistical evidence.
