key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal

case_name: Demetris Hunter v. WPD Management, LLC and 7130 S. Jeffrey, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant repair the building's broken elevator to provide functional elevator service necessary for her to access her sixth-floor apartment due to mobility disability.
key_holding: The court denied WPD's motion to dismiss, holding that Hunter sufficiently alleged a reasonable accommodation claim by requesting that the elevator be repaired, and that whether the accommodation was reasonable and whether WPD provided an adequate alternative accommodation are questions of fact that cannot be resolved at the pleading stage.
brief_summary: Demetris Hunter, a tenant with a mobility disability who uses a walker and lives on the sixth floor, sued her apartment building's management company after the elevator broke and was not repaired for over ten weeks, trapping her in her apartment. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss Hunter's reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), holding that requesting elevator repair constitutes a request for accommodation, she need not speak magic words, and whether the accommodation was reasonable is a question of fact for trial.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Jankowski Lee & Assocs. v. Cisneros, 91 F.3d 891 (7th Cir. 1996)
Clifton Terrace Associates, Ltd. v. United Technologies Corp., 929 F.2d 714 (D.C. Cir. 1991)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)

source_file: 9677610_2021_Nawrocki v. Oak Brook Towers Condominium Association_ilnd
case_name: Janet Nawrocki and H.O.P.E., INC. v. Oak Brook Towers Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an assigned parking space in the row closest to the back entrance of her building as a reasonable accommodation for her mobility disability that limits her ability to walk long distances.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, holding that fair housing organization HOPE had standing to sue under Havens Realty because it adequately alleged concrete injury from diverting resources to address defendants' alleged discrimination, and that the complaint stated a plausible failure-to-accommodate claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Janet Nawrocki and HOPE Fair Housing Center sued the Oak Brook Towers Condominium Association after Nawrocki requested an assigned parking space near her unit as a reasonable accommodation for her disability that limits her ability to walk long distances, and defendants allegedly failed to respond meaningfully to the request. The district court denied both motions to dismiss, holding that HOPE had standing under Havens Realty and that plaintiffs adequately stated a failure-to-accommodate claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Access Living of Metro. Chi. v. Uber Techs., Inc., 958 F.3d 604 (7th Cir. 2020)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9677757_2021_Trinity Sober Living, LLC v. Village of Hinsdale_ilnd
case_name: Trinity Sober Living, LLC v. Village of Hinsdale
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of zoning code family definition limiting unrelated persons to three, to allow a sober living facility for up to ten persons in recovery from addiction
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim, finding that TSL adequately alleged the Village failed to engage in an interactive process by filing suit against TSL the day after receiving the accommodation request rather than assessing it or referring it to the Board of Trustees.
brief_summary: Trinity Sober Living LLC and the United States sued the Village of Hinsdale, challenging the Village's attempts to enforce its zoning code to prevent TSL from operating a sober living facility for persons in recovery from addiction in a single-family residential zone. The court denied the Village's motions to dismiss, except for TSL's retaliation claim, finding that both complaints adequately stated a claim that the Village failed to provide a reasonable accommodation by filing suit against TSL the day after receiving the accommodation request instead of engaging in the interactive process.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 554 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, Ill., 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
U.S. v. Village of Palatine, Ill., 37 F.3d 1230 (7th Cir. 1994)
Bloch v. Frischolz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Cabrini-Green Local Advisory Council v. Chicago Housing Authority
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request. This opinion addresses approval of a settlement to a motion to enforce a 21-year-old consent decree regarding governance of the CDC (affiliated development entity) and financial controls following OIG findings of mismanagement.
key_holding: The settlement between the LAC and the CHA/City was properly authorized by the non-conflicted LAC Board members following valid recusal of conflicted directors, and the settlement is fair and reasonable given the OIG findings regarding CDC financial mismanagement.
brief_summary: The Cabrini-Green Local Advisory Council sued the Chicago Housing Authority and the City of Chicago alleging that redevelopment initiatives for the Cabrini-Green area would result in discriminatory impact upon African Americans, women, and children in violation of the Fair Housing Act. This opinion approves a settlement resolving the CHA's motion to enforce the existing consent decree regarding governance reforms to the CDC following OIG findings of financial mismanagement.
key_cases_cited: Rufo v. Inmates of Suffolk Co. Jail, 502 U.S. 367 (1992)
Horne v. Flores, 557 U.S. 433 (2009)
United States v. Krilich, 303 F.3d 784 (7th Cir. 2002)
Pohl v. United Airlines, Inc., 213 F.3d 336 (7th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Sullivan v. Liberty Mutual Insurance Company
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case concerns alleged race and sexual orientation discrimination by insurer in processing property damage claim
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' claims for injunctive and declaratory relief for lack of standing because their cancellation of the insurance policy eliminated any real or immediate threat of future injury, but denied the motion to dismiss plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act disparate treatment claim based on race and sexual orientation, finding the allegations sufficient to survive dismissal.
brief_summary: Terrie Sullivan and Veronica Rodriguez sued Liberty Mutual Insurance Company alleging the insurer discriminated against them based on race and sexual orientation when processing their homeowner insurance claim for water damage, seeking damages and class certification under the Fair Housing Act and other statutes. The court granted the motion to dismiss in part, dismissing plaintiffs' claims for injunctive and declaratory relief for lack of Article III standing because their cancellation of the insurance policy before filing suit eliminated any possibility of future injury, but denied the motion to dismiss the underlying disparate treatment claims finding the allegations sufficient to proceed to discovery.
key_cases_cited: O'Shea v. Littleton, 414 U.S. 488 (1974)
City of Los Angeles v. Lyons, 461 U.S. 95 (1983)
Holmes v. Fisher, 854 F.2d 229 (7th Cir. 1988)
Laurens v. Volvo Cars of North America, LLC, 868 F.3d 622 (7th Cir. 2017)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 578 U.S. 330 (2016)

source_file: 9680683_2022_Williams Jr v. Heartland Realty Investors, Inc._ilnd
case_name: Andy Hope Williams, Jr. v. Heartland Realty Investors, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—case involves alleged selective enforcement of lease rules (grill prohibition) based on plaintiffs' race and national origin.
key_holding: Plaintiffs adequately stated FHA and § 1981 claims by alleging they were the only Hebrew Israelite and Puerto Rican family in the apartment complex and were selectively targeted with grill enforcement while other tenants were permitted to use grills.
brief_summary: Black and Puerto Rican tenants sued their private apartment complex alleging race and national origin discrimination when the landlord selectively enforced a grill prohibition against them while allowing other tenants to use grills, then refused to renew their lease. The court denied the motion to dismiss plaintiffs' FHA disparate treatment, interference, and § 1981 claims, finding sufficient allegations of selective enforcement based on protected characteristics.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A.
Wigginton v. Bank of America Corp.
Griffin v. Breckenridge

source_file: 9681431_2022_Talley v. The Housing Authority of Cook County_ilnd
case_name: Charles Talley, Jr. and Christopher J. Bonds v. The Housing Authority of Cook County, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested a voucher for a two-bedroom dwelling and moving papers to transfer to accessible housing as a reasonable accommodation for their disabilities.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' failure-to-accommodate claims because plaintiffs never formally requested repairs as an accommodation, and the Housing Authority did not have authority to compel landlords to make repairs; additionally, the Housing Authority approved the voucher request and exhibits show it issued the moving papers, defeating the claim that plaintiffs were denied the requested accommodation.
brief_summary: Two Black disabled tenants using Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers sued their landlord and the Housing Authority of Cook County for disability discrimination (failure to accommodate) and race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The district court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing all federal claims with prejudice, finding that plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege failure-to-accommodate claims because they never requested repairs as an accommodation and the Housing Authority approved their transfer request and issued moving papers, and also failed to adequately plead race discrimination claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Preserve at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)
Khan v. Bland, 630 F.3d 519 (7th Cir. 2010)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)

source_file: 9681456_2022_County of Cook, IL v. Wells Fargo _ Co._ilnd
case_name: County of Cook, Illinois v. Wells Fargo & Co.
accommodation_description: N/A - this is a third-party standing case where Cook County claims Wells Fargo's discriminatory mortgage lending and servicing practices caused minority borrowers to default, increasing the County's foreclosure-related expenditures
key_holding: The County's FHA claims failed because its key expert witnesses were excluded under Rule 702 for unreliable methodologies, leaving the County without evidence to establish that Wells Fargo intentionally discriminated against minority borrowers or that such discrimination proximately caused the County's claimed injuries.
brief_summary: Cook County sued Wells Fargo alleging that the bank's mortgage lending and servicing practices discriminated against minority borrowers, causing higher default and foreclosure rates that increased the County's expenditures on foreclosure-related services. The court granted Wells Fargo's summary judgment motion after excluding the County's expert witnesses under Rule 702 for unreliable statistical methodologies, finding the County lacked sufficient evidence of intentional discrimination or proximate cause.
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Oakland v. Wells Fargo & Co., 14 F.4th 1030 (9th Cir. 2021) (en banc)
Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Dura Auto. Sys. of Ind., Inc. v. CTS Corp., 285 F.3d 609 (7th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Azroui v. Walega
accommodation_description: Not applicable - this case involves national origin discrimination in refusal to pay for property damage, not a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The court granted the defendants' motion to stay the federal case pending resolution of the parallel state court proceedings, finding that Colorado River abstention factors weighed in favor of staying the case to avoid piecemeal litigation given the state court's significantly advanced progress.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Ghaleb Azroui (from Jordan) sued Avenue One Management Corporation and its Board directors under the Fair Housing Act alleging national origin discrimination for the Board's refusal to pay for tree damage to his car. The court granted the motion to stay the federal case pending resolution of a parallel state court case involving the same underlying property damage claim, finding that Colorado River abstention factors favored staying the case to avoid inconsistent judgments and piecemeal litigation.
key_cases_cited: Colorado River Water Conservation Dist. v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976)
Clark v. Lacy, 376 F.3d 682 (7th Cir. 2004)
Loughran v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 2 F.4th 640 (7th Cir. 2021)
DePuy Synthes Sales, Inc. v. OrthoLA, Inc., 953 F.3d 469 (7th Cir. 2020)

case_name: Emily Evans v. 210 East Pearson Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendants deliver packages directly to her apartment door as they do for other residents, as her disabilities prevent her from retrieving packages from the lobby.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim (Count I) because plaintiff sufficiently alleged she requested package delivery accommodation before defendants initiated state court proceedings, rejecting defendants' ripeness argument; however, the court dismissed the interference claims (Counts II and IV) without prejudice for failure to allege intentional discrimination.
brief_summary: Emily Evans sued her condominium association and property management company alleging they violated the FHA by refusing to provide package delivery service to her apartment as a reasonable accommodation for her disabilities. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the failure to accommodate claim, finding plaintiff's claim ripe because she requested the accommodation before defendants initiated eviction proceedings, but dismissed the interference and retaliation claims without prejudice for failure to plead intentional discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Good Shepherd Manor Found., Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Village of Palatine, Ill. v. United States, 37 F.4th 1230 (7th Cir. 1994)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Williams v. Village of Hazel Crest
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case involves alleged race discrimination in municipal sewage flooding rather than a disability accommodation request under § 3604(f)(3)(B)
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because Williams's allegations that the Village jetted sewage into her property because she is Black were conclusory and unsupported by factual allegations suggesting discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Deanna Williams, a Black property owner, sued the Village of Hazel Crest and Mayor Vernard Alsberry alleging the Village repeatedly flooded her property with sewage and discriminated against her because of her race in violation of § 3604(b). The court dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice, finding Williams failed to allege sufficient factual support for her conclusory allegation that the Village acted with discriminatory intent based on her race.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 9682801_2023_Huskey v. State Farm Fire _ Casualty Company_ilnd
case_name: Huskey v. State Farm Fire & Casualty Company
accommodation_description: Black homeowners allege State Farm's algorithmic claims-processing system creates racial disparities in homeowners insurance claim handling
key_holding: The court allowed Plaintiffs' disparate-impact claim under § 3604(b) to proceed, finding the complaint adequately alleged statistical disparities, a specific policy (algorithmic claims-processing), and a plausible causal connection; but dismissed § 3604(a) for failure to allege constructive eviction and § 3605 based on Seventh Circuit precedent that insurers are not in the business of covered real estate transactions.
brief_summary: Black homeowners Jacqueline Huskey and Riian Wynn sued State Farm Fire & Casualty Company alleging that its use of algorithmic decision-making tools in claims processing created statistically significant racial disparities in how it handled their homeowners insurance claims. The court granted in part and denied in part State Farm's motion to dismiss, dismissing the § 3604(a) claim for failure to allege constructive eviction and the § 3605 claim based on Seventh Circuit precedent, but allowed the § 3604(b) disparate impact claim to proceed because Plaintiffs adequately pleaded a statistical disparity, a specific policy, and a causal connection.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
NAACP v. Am. Fam. Mut. Ins. Co., 978 F.2d 287 (7th Cir. 1992)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Mac's Shell Serv., Inc. v. Shell Oil Prods. Co., 559 U.S. 175 (2010)
Humana, Inc. v. Forsyth, 525 U.S. 299 (1999)

case_name: Vinzella Trimuel v. Chicago Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a domestic violence survivor with disabilities affecting her communication abilities, requested reinstatement to the public housing voucher waitlist from which she was removed for failing to complete a required survey.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim because CHA approved the accommodation request but failed to implement it, and the court rejected CHA's argument that providing the accommodation would fundamentally alter its policies as inappropriate for resolution at the motion to dismiss stage.
brief_summary: Vinzella Trimuel, a domestic violence survivor with anxiety, depression, and PTSD, sued the Chicago Housing Authority for disability discrimination after CHA removed her from its housing voucher waitlist in 2013 and then failed to reinstate her despite approving her 2020 reasonable accommodation request. The court denied CHA's motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim, holding that CHA's approval of the request followed by failure to implement it stated a viable claim and that administrative burden arguments were improper at the motion to dismiss stage, while dismissing sex discrimination and promissory estoppel claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)

case_name: Manal Farhan v. 2715 NMA LLC and M. Fishman & Company
accommodation_description: UNDETERMINED
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to plausibly allege that defendants' enforcement of a neutrality policy regarding the Israeli-Palestinian conflict was motivated by national origin discrimination rather than by the political nature of the flag display, as defendants would have enforced the policy regardless of the tenant's national origin.
brief_summary: Palestinian-American tenant sued her landlords for discrimination under the Fair Housing Act based on national origin, claiming they unlawfully enforced a neutrality policy by demanding she remove a Palestinian flag from her window and serving her with an eviction notice. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the plaintiff failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent based on national origin rather than political views, as the neutrality policy would have applied regardless of the tenant's national origin.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bloch v. Frischolz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Hammad v. Dynamo Stadium, LLC, No. H-14-1938, 2015 WL 6965215 (S.D. Tex. Nov. 10, 2015)

case_name: Carl William Bass v. John Brannen and Network Property Management
accommodation_description: Bass requested that landlord control or eliminate loud noises from neighboring tenants (violin playing, TV volume, barking dog) that triggered his chronic fatigue syndrome symptoms.
key_holding: The court dismissed Bass's failure-to-accommodate claim because he failed to show his CFS was the but-for cause of his inability to enjoy his apartment, as the loud noises (screeching violin, TV at volume 70, barking dog) would be intolerable to any tenant regardless of disability; however, his disparate treatment claim survived because Brannen's statement that his building was not a 'convalescent home' and disparate treatment compared to similarly-situated tenant Berger suggested discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Carl Bass, a tenant with chronic fatigue syndrome, sued his landlord John Brannen and property management company alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act, including failure to accommodate his disability-related noise sensitivity and discriminatory lease non-renewal. The court dismissed Bass's failure-to-accommodate claim because the complained-of noises (violin, TV at high volume, barking dog) would affect any tenant regardless of disability, but allowed his disparate treatment claim to proceed based on evidence that Brannen refused to renew his lease after making comments referencing his disability and treated him differently than a similarly-situated tenant who declined the same apartment offer.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 566 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Nikolich v. Vill. of Arlington Heights, 870 F. Supp. 2d 556 (N.D. Ill. 2012)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Good Shepherd Manor Foundation, Inc. v. City of Momence, 323 F.3d 557 (7th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Bell v. Pappas
accommodation_description: No accommodation request: plaintiffs allege Cook County's property tax sale and indemnification system has disparate impact on Black and Latino homeowners and reflects discriminatory intent.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA disparate impact claim because plaintiffs failed to provide statistics connecting the indemnification fund's operation to racial disparities, and dismissed the disparate treatment claim because acknowledging the existence of disparities does not establish discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Individual homeowners and community organizations sued Cook County officials alleging the County's property tax sale system and inadequate indemnification fund have disparate impact on Black and Latino homeowners and reflect discriminatory intent. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to allege sufficient statistics connecting the indemnification fund to racial disparities or facts showing discriminatory motive.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
County of Cook v. HSBC N. Am. Holdings Inc., 314 F. Supp. 3d 975 (N.D. Ill. 2018)
TBS Grp., LLC v. City of Zion, 2017 WL 5129008 (N.D. Ill. 2017)
McCauley v. City of Chicago, 671 F.3d 611 (7th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 9684517_2019_Sunnybrook LP v. City of Alton, Illinois_ilsd
case_name: Sunnybrook LP v. City of Alton, Illinois
race_if_mentioned: African-American, Hispanic or Latino, or American Indian
accommodation_description: Developer alleges city withdrew support for affordable housing development because it would serve minorities in violation of Fair Housing Act
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding that the developer sufficiently alleged injury-in-fact, causation, and redressability, and that the state and federal actions are not parallel so abstention is not required.
brief_summary: Sunnybrook LP, a developer of affordable housing, sued the City of Alton, Illinois and Mayor Brant Walker alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act, Civil Rights Acts, and Equal Protection Clause by withdrawing support for and obstructing the Sunnybrook affordable housing development because it would serve minority renters. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding the developer adequately alleged Article III standing and that the parallel state court litigation did not require abstention.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Housing Development Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Colorado River Water Conservation District v. United States, 424 U.S. 800 (1976)

case_name: Carolina Prejean v. Jen Dischbein, Kim Schaltenbrand, and Metro Landing Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in refusal to lower rent or allow her fiancée to be added to the lease; disability claim regarding elevator access was not adequately pleaded.
key_holding: Plaintiff stated a colorable FHA claim against Metro Landing Apartments for racial discrimination in housing conditions, but her ADA Title II, Title VI, and Federal Tort Claims Act claims were dismissed for failure to adequately plead the essential elements of those claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Carolina Prejean sued Metro Landing Apartments and individual property managers alleging racial discrimination in housing after they refused to lower her rent or allow her fiancée on the lease, claiming racially motivated animus. The court granted IFP status and found plaintiff adequately stated a Fair Housing Act racial discrimination claim against the apartment complex, while dismissing ADA, Title VI, and Federal Tort Claims Act claims for failure to state viable claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)

case_name: Carl Lee Ledford v. Katrika Williams, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged denial of reasonable accommodation but failed to specify what accommodation was needed or for what reason.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to identify what accommodation he needed or for what reason, and the undisputed evidence showed the lease non-renewal was based on criminal history revealed through a background check rather than disability.
brief_summary: Carl Lee Ledford, a Section 8 tenant receiving disability income, sued Monroe Group (property manager), Community Housing Concepts East Central LLC (owner), and IHCDA, alleging race discrimination, constitutional violations, and denial of reasonable accommodation after his lease was not renewed based on criminal history and he was assessed late fees. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on all federal claims, finding no evidence of race-based discrimination and that plaintiff failed to identify any specific accommodation he needed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Mabry v. City of East Chicago
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case concerns alleged discriminatory relocation from contaminated public housing complex based on race and national origin.
key_holding: The court dismissed the Fair Housing Act intentional discrimination and disparate impact claims because plaintiffs failed to allege that similarly situated individuals outside their protected classes (race, national origin, familial status) were treated differently, and failed to plead the statistical disparity necessary to state a disparate impact claim.
brief_summary: Hispanic and African-American residents of the East Chicago Housing Authority's West Calumet Housing Complex sued the City of East Chicago and ECHA alleging intentional racial discrimination and disparate impact under the FHA based on defendants' failure to warn of lead/arsenic contamination and subsequent relocation. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to plead comparator allegations sufficient for intentional discrimination and failed to allege statistical disparity required for disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Geinosky v. City of Chicago, 675 F.3d 743 (7th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Sandra Black v. Naomi Friedrichsen, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was constructively evicted through racially discriminatory harassment including false lease violation accusations and eviction proceedings.
key_holding: A plaintiff bringing Fair Housing Act claims based on discriminatory harassment under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 need not establish constructive eviction, and entering into a joint agreement to vacate does not automatically defeat such claims; the plaintiff's allegations of a pattern of racially-motivated harassment were sufficient to survive a motion to dismiss.
brief_summary: Sandra Black, an African-American tenant, sued Hunters Run Apartments and related defendants alleging they discriminated against her on the basis of race by falsely accusing her of lease violations and pursuing eviction proceedings while treating white tenants more favorably. The district court denied the defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 for discriminatory harassment do not require proof of constructive eviction and that the joint agreement to vacate does not automatically defeat her claims.
key_cases_cited: Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Smith v. Hous. Auth. of S. Bend, 867 F.2d 1004 (7th Cir. 2012)
DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Sandra Black v. Naomi Friedrichsen, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendants discriminated against her on the basis of her race (African-American) by falsely accusing her of lease violations to force her to vacate.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's motion to compel discovery responses, finding her requests vague and overly broad, and granted Defendants' motion to compel her deposition, finding no legal basis for her refusal to attend.
brief_summary: Sandra Black, an African-American tenant with a Section 8 voucher, sued her landlord and property management company alleging race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming defendants falsely accused her of lease violations and pursued eviction to force her out. The court denied Plaintiff's motion to compel more complete discovery responses, finding her requests vague and overly broad, and granted Defendants' motion to compel Plaintiff's deposition attendance, ruling she had no legal basis to refuse her deposition based on unresolved discovery disputes.
key_cases_cited: Krieman v. Crystal Lake Apartments Ltd. P'ship, No. 05 C 0348, 2006 WL 1519320 (N.D. Ill. May 31, 2006)

case_name: Kendra Mabry, et al. v. City of East Chicago, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - case concerns eviction from contaminated housing and alleged racial discrimination in that eviction
key_holding: The court granted leave to amend discrimination claims under the FHA and § 1982 against City and ECHA defendants because plaintiffs sufficiently alleged discriminatory intent and disparate impact at the pleading stage, but denied amendment as to previously dismissed corporate defendants and § 3617 intimidation claims.
brief_summary: Public housing residents of the West Calumet Housing Complex in East Chicago, Indiana sued the City of East Chicago, East Chicago Housing Authority, and corporate defendants for Fair Housing Act violations and constitutional claims arising from their 2016 eviction due to property contamination. The court granted leave to amend the complaint to add claims of intentional discrimination and disparate impact under the FHA based on race and familial status against the City and ECHA defendants, while denying amendment as to previously dismissed corporate defendants and § 3617 intimidation claims.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Herndon v. Hous. Auth. of S. Bend, Indiana, 670 F. App'x 417 (7th Cir. 2016)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Norman v. Northwest Indiana CA Section 8
accommodation_description: Section 8 voucher holder alleging that the housing authority discriminated against her by not opposing the sale of her residence and directing rent payments to the new owner without her consent.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state an FHA claim because the plaintiff did not allege membership in a protected class nor facts showing discrimination because of such membership, and lacked standing for generalized allegations about Section 8 recipients.
brief_summary: LaSandra Norman, a Section 8 voucher holder proceeding pro se, sued Northwest Indiana Community Action and others alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on the housing authority's failure to oppose the sale of her residence and its payment of her rent assistance to the new owner. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the plaintiff did not allege membership in a protected class or facts showing discrimination because of such membership, and lacked standing to bring generalized claims about Section 8 recipients.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540 (2016)

case_name: McGrath v. Dunecrest Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Family requested permission to construct a deck extension and staircase creating a second exit from their condominium to accommodate autistic twins who struggle with emergencies and unexpected circumstances.
key_holding: The McGraths stated a plausible failure to accommodate claim because the Association's two-year refusal to issue building paperwork for the requested second exit constituted constructive denial of the accommodation, and their allegations of discriminatory animus and disparate treatment of similar construction requests were sufficient to survive futility review.
brief_summary: The McGrath family sued their condominium association alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after the association refused to issue approval paperwork for construction of a second exit to accommodate their autistic twins. The court granted leave to amend the federal FHA claims (finding them plausible) but declined jurisdiction over state condominium law claims.
key_cases_cited: Wisconsin Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condominium Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs, Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: LaSandra Norman v. Northwest Indiana CA Section 8, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges race-based retaliation for exercising fair housing rights related to rent payment and Section 8 benefits.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state a claim of race-based retaliation under § 3617 because she did not allege sufficient facts to create an inference that the defendants were motivated by discriminatory intent in seeking rent payment or threatening Section 8 termination.
brief_summary: LaSandra Norman, an African American Section 8 voucher holder, sued her landlords and Northwest Indiana CA alleging race-based retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, claiming defendants harassed her about rent and threatened Section 8 termination. The court dismissed her complaint for failing to state a retaliation claim because she alleged no facts showing defendants acted with discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Luevano v. Wal-Mart Stores, Inc., 722 F.3d 1014 (7th Cir. 2013)

case_name: LaSandra Norman v. Northwest Indiana CA Section 8, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff vaguely alleged discrimination based on disability but failed to state what accommodation, if any, was requested or needed.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims for failure to state a claim because she alleged no facts connecting the collection of unpaid rent or termination of housing assistance to her race, sex, or disability, and failed to allege she was treated differently from other tenants not in her protected classes.
brief_summary: LaSandra Norman, an African American woman using Section 8 housing assistance, sued Northwest Indiana CA and her landlord alleging race, sex, and disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and retaliation under § 3617, claiming she was targeted for complaining about paying rent that helped her landlord cover his mortgage. The court dismissed all claims with prejudice, finding the plaintiff failed to allege any facts connecting the enforcement of standard lease obligations (rent payment, lawn care) to her protected characteristics or that she was treated differently from non-protected tenants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Kendra Mabry, et al. v. City of East Chicago, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request is at issue; the case concerns eviction from contaminated housing and alleged racial discrimination in the closure of a housing complex.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing the disparate impact claim under the FHA because the one-time decision to close the housing complex does not constitute a 'policy' as required for disparate impact analysis under Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project.
brief_summary: Residents of the East Chicago Housing Authority's West Calumet Housing Complex sued the City of East Chicago, the Housing Authority, and individual officials alleging Fair Housing Act violations, constitutional claims, and civil rights violations arising from the 2016 closure of the contaminated complex and their subsequent eviction. The Magistrate Judge recommended granting the motion to dismiss, finding that the disparate impact claim failed because a one-time decision to close the complex does not constitute a 'policy' required for disparate impact analysis, while noting that the disparate treatment claim was adequately alleged.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Joliet, Ill. v. New W., L.P., 825 F.3d 827 (7th Cir. 2016)
Lafayette Linear v. Vill. of Univ. Park, Ill., 887 F.3d 842 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9695710_2022_Pinnacle Treatment Centers, Inc. v. Crown Point, Indiana City of_innd
case_name: Pinnacle Treatment Centers, Inc. v. City of Crown Point, Indiana
accommodation_description: Pinnacle operates a group home for patients recovering from substance abuse and challenges Crown Point's zoning enforcement and fines as discriminatory under the FHA.
key_holding: The court denied Crown Point's motion to dismiss for lack of standing, holding that Pinnacle suffered a concrete injury (fines) that is fairly traceable to Crown Point's conduct and redressable by favorable judgment, satisfying Article III standing requirements even though Pinnacle itself is not a member of the protected class.
brief_summary: Pinnacle Treatment Centers, operator of a group home for recovering substance abusers, sued the City of Crown Point, Indiana under the Fair Housing Act, alleging that the City's zoning enforcement and fines constitute disability discrimination. The court denied Crown Point's motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding that Pinnacle suffered a concrete injury (the fines) sufficient to establish Article III standing, even though Pinnacle is not itself disabled but rather operates on behalf of disabled patients.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Gladstone, Realtors v. Village of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Gorski v. Troy, 929 F.2d 1183 (7th Cir. 1991)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

case_name: Shedrick Northern v. Pedcor Management Corp.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged water leaking into his apartment and claimed a right to a habitable home under the FHA, but did not request a specific reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed the Fair Housing Act claim because plaintiff did not allege membership in any protected class or that discrimination based on a protected characteristic was the basis for the defendant's conduct; the FHA is a discrimination law, not a general habitability statute.
brief_summary: Shedrick Northern sued Pedcor Management Corp., his landlord, alleging water damage to his apartment and claiming violations of the Fair Housing Act based on a right to a habitable home. The court dismissed the case without prejudice, finding that the FHA does not provide a general habitability right and that Northern failed to allege membership in any protected class or that any protected characteristic motivated the defendant's conduct.
key_cases_cited: McDaniel v. VTT Management, Inc., 2018 WL 4494985 (W.D.N.C. Sept. 18, 2018)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, Illinois, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)
Walton v. Claybridge Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 191 Fed.Appx. 446 (7th Cir. 2006)
Sanghvi v. City of Claremont, 328 F.3d 532 (9th Cir. 2003)

case_name: Graham v. Herron Management Property
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request — complaint alleged harassment and discrimination based on disability and race arising from delayed water heater repairs and water bill dispute.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because her complaint contained only conclusory allegations of discrimination based on disability and race without factual content suggesting HPM was motivated by discriminatory intent regarding the water heater repair and billing dispute.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff DeAnn Graham sued Herron Property Management alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on disability and race discrimination arising from a delayed water heater repair and disputed water bill. The district court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations of discriminatory intent without plausible factual content to support a FHA claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, No. 19-3499, 2022 WL 4128529 (7th Cir. Sept. 12, 2022)
A.B. v. Hous. Auth. of South Bend, 2012 WL 1877740 (N.D. Ind. May 18, 2012)

case_name: David DeBoard v. Ventry Apartments, LLC, Domo Development Company, LLC, Sunstone Construction Company, LLC and The Ventry Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user, seeks injunctive relief to compel compliance with FHA design and construction accessibility requirements at newly constructed apartment complex.
key_holding: A professional tester who never intended to rent at the apartments and alleges only a statutory violation as injury lacks Article III standing to sue under TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, which requires concrete injury-in-fact beyond the mere fact of a statutory violation.
brief_summary: David DeBoard, a professional fair housing tester who uses a wheelchair, sued Ventry Apartments and related entities alleging the recently constructed apartment complex contained design and construction barriers that violated FHA accessibility requirements. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of Article III standing, holding that under TransUnion, a professional tester who never intended to rent and alleges only a statutory violation as injury lacks the concrete and particularized injury required for standing.
key_cases_cited: TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez, 141 S.Ct. 2190 (2021)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Laufer v. Arpan, LLC, 29 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Village of Bellwood v. Dwivedi, 895 F.2d 1521 (7th Cir. 1990)
Allen v. Wright, 468 U.S. 737 (1986)

source_file: 9698624_2024_Pinnacle Treatment Centers, Inc. v. Crown Point, Indiana City of_innd
case_name: Pinnacle Treatment Centers, Inc. v. City of Crown Point, Indiana
accommodation_description: Pinnacle requested a zoning variance as a reasonable accommodation to permit up to eight unrelated recovering substance abusers (disabled persons under the FHA) to reside in the group home in an R-1 single-family residential district.
key_holding: Both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment were denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the City's denial of a zoning variance and enforcement of the five-person occupancy limit were motivated by discriminatory animus against recovering substance abusers, and whether the requested variance was reasonable and necessary to provide disabled residents an equal opportunity to live in a residential neighborhood.
brief_summary: Pinnacle Treatment Centers sued the City of Crown Point, Indiana alleging the City violated the Fair Housing Act by denying a zoning variance that would allow a group home for recovering substance abusers to house up to eight unrelated disabled residents in an R-1 single-family district. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding triable disputes exist as to whether the City's denial was motivated by discriminatory animus reflected in community opposition and whether the variance is reasonable and necessary under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Oconomowoc Residential Programs, Inc. v. City of Oconomowoc Lake, 300 F.3d 776 (7th Cir. 2002)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Dadian v. Village of Wilmette, 269 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2001)
United States v. City of Chicago Heights, 161 F. Supp. 2d 819 (N.D. Ill. 2001)
Step By Step, Inc. v. City of Ogdensburg, 176 F. Supp. 3d 112 (N.D.N.Y. 2016)

case_name: McGrath v. Dunecrest Condominium Association
accommodation_description: Request to extend an existing second-floor balcony and add stairs leading to the ground to provide a reliable secondary exit for adult autistic children.
key_holding: The McGraths failed to establish that the balcony extension with stairs was 'necessary' under § 3604(f)(3)(B) causation standard, as they could not show Tim and Martin's autism was the but-for cause of their need for the accommodation.
brief_summary: The McGrath family sued their condominium association seeking to build a balcony extension with stairs as a reasonable accommodation for their adult autistic sons; the court granted summary judgment for the association, finding the plaintiffs failed to establish causation—that their children's autism was the but-for cause necessitating the accommodation—required under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Wis. Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. City of Milwaukee, 465 F.3d 737 (7th Cir. 2006)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)

source_file: 9698899_2019_NEW HORIZONS REHABILITATION, INC. v. STATE OF INDIANA_insd
case_name: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc. v. State of Indiana, Executive Director, Indiana Department of Homeland Security
accommodation_description: Request to be classified as a Class 2 single-family residential structure rather than a Class 1 commercial structure, including exemption from commercial fire suppression system and architect requirements, as a reasonable accommodation for intellectually and developmentally disabled residents.
key_holding: Indiana's classification of New Horizons' supported living home as a Class 1 commercial structure, requiring commercial building codes and fire suppression systems while treating similar single-family residences differently, constitutes unlawful discrimination under the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act because the disparate treatment stems solely from the residents' disabilities.
brief_summary: New Horizons Rehabilitation, Inc., a not-for-profit operator of supported living homes for adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities, sued the State of Indiana and its Department of Homeland Security after being required to construct a new Lawrenceburg home as a Class 1 commercial structure subject to commercial building codes and fire suppression requirements, while single-family residences are classified as Class 2. The court granted New Horizons' motion for summary judgment on reasonable accommodation and disparate treatment claims under the FHAA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act, permanently enjoining DHS from enforcing Class 1 requirements on the home and awarding $30,400 in damages, but denied the Equal Protection claim.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Hemisphere Bldg. v. Village of Richton Park, 171 F.3d 437 (7th Cir. 1999)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Valencia v. City of Springfield, 883 F.3d 959 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9699210_2019_WATTERS v. HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION AT THE PRESERVE AT BRIDGEWATER_insd
case_name: Watters v. Homeowners Association at the Preserve at Bridgewater
accommodation_description: Request for a six-foot vinyl privacy fence to enclose pool and yard area for therapeutic use due to disability (PTSD and pulmonary condition)
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs failed to provide sufficient admissible evidence that a privacy fence was necessary to afford equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, and the HOA offered a reasonable alternative accommodation (wrought-iron fence with landscaping).
brief_summary: Terence and Tonca Watters sued the Homeowners Association at the Preserve at Bridgewater alleging race and disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, including a failure to accommodate claim for denying their request for a privacy fence. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiffs failed to provide sufficient admissible evidence that the privacy fence was necessary to accommodate Terence Watters's disabilities, and noting the HOA offered a reasonable alternative accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Stevens v. Hollywood Towers & Condominium Ass'n, 836 F. Supp. 2d 800 (N.D. Ill. 2011)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 802 (1973)
Beck v. Univ. of Wisc. Bd. of Regents, 75 F.3d 1130 (7th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 9699611_2020_FAIR HOUSING CENTER OF CENTRAL INDIANA, INC. v. RAINBOW REALTY GROUP, INC._insd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana, Inc. et al v. Rainbow Realty Group, Inc. et al
race_if_mentioned: Racial minorities as a class; specific named plaintiffs are Mary Kamano, Norma Tejeda, Cordell Spencer, Maria Gaspar, and Franklin Paz
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request; the case involves a reverse redlining challenge to a rent-to-buy program alleged to discriminate against racial minorities
key_holding: The court certified a class of all Rainbow rent-to-buy customers since 2009 and certified common questions under Rules 23(b)(2) and (c)(4), including whether Rainbow engaged in discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act, while denying individual damages claims that would require fact-specific inquiry.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana and five minority plaintiffs sued Rainbow Realty Group challenging its rent-to-buy program as predatory and discriminatory in violation of the Fair Housing Act (reverse redlining) and other laws. The court granted partial class certification, identifying common questions suitable for class resolution under Rules 23(b)(2) and (c)(4) while denying certification of individual damages claims requiring fact-specific inquiry.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
Stevens v. Hous. Auth. of S. Bend, Indiana, 663 F.3d 300 (7th Cir. 2011)
Halprin v. Prairie Single Family Homes of Dearborn Park Ass'n, 388 F.3d 327 (7th Cir. 2004)
Estate of Davis v. Wells Fargo Bank, 633 F.3d 529 (7th Cir. 2011)

source_file: 9700858_2021_FAIR HOUSING CENTER OF CENTRAL INDIANA, INC. v. NEW_insd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana, Inc. v. New
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves racial harassment by a neighbor and HOA/property management's failure to enforce covenants to stop the discriminatory conduct
key_holding: The court denied judgment on the pleadings for the FHA claims because plaintiffs adequately alleged direct liability against the HOA and property management for failing to take prompt action to correct and end New's discriminatory harassment under 24 C.F.R. § 100.7(a)(1)(iii), despite having the authority and power to intervene.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana and Donata Banks sued the Twin Creeks HOA, Kirkpatrick Management Company, and Vicki New for racial harassment, alleging the Association Defendants knew of New's race-based harassment of minority residents but failed to enforce HOA covenants to stop it. The court granted the Association Defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the voluntarily abandoned § 1981 claim and the negligence claim, but denied the motion as to the FHA and § 1982 claims, finding plaintiffs sufficiently alleged direct liability under 24 C.F.R. § 100.7 for the Association Defendants' failure to take prompt action to correct the discriminatory conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Emergency Servs. Billing Corp. v. Allstate Ins. Co.
Pisciotta v. Old Nat'l Bancorp.

case_name: David DeBoard v. Elmwood One, LLC
accommodation_description: This is a design and construction accessibility case under § 3604(f)(3)(C), not a reasonable accommodation case under § 3604(f)(3)(B); the plaintiff's underlying complaint alleged defendants failed to design and construct Redwood Brownsburg Apartments to be accessible to persons with disabilities.
key_holding: The court granted in part plaintiff's motion to strike, striking the portion of expert Torkelson's report that relied on a prior confidential settlement agreement because it was not based on objective scientific standards; granted plaintiff's motion to preclude expert Dement from testifying due to defendants' failure to provide required Rule 26 disclosures; and granted defendants' motion to maintain the prior settlement agreement under seal.
brief_summary: David DeBoard sued Elmwood One, LLC and related defendants alleging they failed to design and construct the Redwood Brownsburg Apartments to be accessible to persons with disabilities in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). This order addresses only procedural discovery matters, granting in part plaintiff's motion to strike expert Torkelson's report (striking the portion relying on a prior settlement agreement as not based on objective scientific standards), granting plaintiff's motion to preclude expert Dement (for failure to provide Rule 26 disclosures), and granting defendants' motion to seal the prior settlement agreement.
key_cases_cited: Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Tribble v. Evangelides, 670 F.3d 753 (7th Cir. 2012)
Goesel v. Boley Int'l, 738 F.3d 831 (7th Cir. 2013)
Manpower, Inc. v. Insurance Co. of Penn., 732 F.3d 796 (7th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 9702091_2021_FAIR HOUSING CENTER OF CENTRAL INDIANA, INC. v. NEW_insd
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana, Inc. v. New
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; this case involves racial harassment by a neighbor (Vicki New) and claims that the HOA and property management company failed to take prompt action to address the discriminatory conduct.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on § 3604(a) and § 3604(c) claims but denied summary judgment on § 3604(b) hostile housing environment and § 3617 interference claims because genuine factual disputes exist regarding whether the HOA and property management company knew of racial harassment in 2016 and whether their response was sufficient under 24 C.F.R. § 100.7(a)(1)(iii).
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of Central Indiana and Donata Banks sued the Twin Creeks HOA, Kirkpatrick Management Company, and neighbor Vicki New for racial harassment and discriminatory housing practices under the FHA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on claims under § 3604(a) and § 3604(c) but denied summary judgment on § 3604(b) hostile housing environment and § 3617 interference claims because factual disputes exist about whether defendants knew of racial harassment as early as 2016 and whether their response was reasonable.
key_cases_cited: DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
Bloch v. Rim, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
City of Memphis v. Greene, 451 U.S. 100 (1981)
White v. HUD, 475 F.3d 898 (7th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Debra Dunnivan v. Camille Mitchell, Karen Mitchell, Kirkpatrick Management Co., Grandville Cooperative Board of Directors
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleges race-based discrimination, hostile housing environment, nepotism, and retaliation following her sister's FHA lawsuit.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims were dismissed for failure to state a claim because her complaint contained only conclusory allegations of discrimination without factual support regarding discriminatory intent, disparate impact, or protected activity for retaliation claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Debra Dunnivan, a Section 8 tenant at Grandville Cooperative Apartments in Indianapolis, sued the property manager, management company, and board of directors alleging race discrimination, hostile housing environment, and retaliation under the FHA after receiving a lease termination notice following a shooting incident involving her grandson. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations without sufficient factual allegations to support claims of discriminatory intent, disparate impact, or retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Tex. Dept. of Housing and Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
Bloch v. Frischholz
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC

case_name: April Royal v. Pedcor Management Corp. as M/A for Waterfront Pointe
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case involves alleged retaliation for complaining about apartment odor conditions, not a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim at screening because her complaints about apartment odors were not related to unlawful discrimination and therefore did not constitute protected activity under 42 U.S.C. § 3617.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff April Royal sued Pedcor Management Corporation alleging retaliation under the Fair Housing Act's anti-retaliation provision after she was evicted for non-payment of rent following complaints about odors in her apartment. The court dismissed her complaint without prejudice, finding that her complaints about general housing conditions did not constitute protected activity under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 because they were not related to discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC
Riley v. City of Kokomo
Kiebala v. Boris
Kummerow v. Ohawcha.org

case_name: Quinn Ngiendo v. Pep-KU, LLC, Amelia Ludlow, Madison Cline and Lucy Evans
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was constructively evicted and subjected to a hostile housing environment based on race and national origin; disability accommodation (noise sensitivity accommodation from loud music) was raised in proposed third amended complaint but not accepted.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's federal Fair Housing Act claims because the amended complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing the alleged harassment was severe or pervasive, based on a protected characteristic, or that the landlord knew or should have known about the harassment.
brief_summary: Quinn Ngiendo, a Black woman from Kenya, sued her landlord Pep-KU, LLC and three white roommates alleging they subjected her to a hostile housing environment based on race and national origin, including playing loud music with offensive lyrics, and that she was constructively evicted. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff's allegations too vague and conclusory to establish severe or pervasive harassment motivated by her protected characteristics or that the landlord was responsible for the alleged conduct.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Smith v. Mission Assocs. Ltd. P'ship, 225 F. Supp. 2d 1293 (D. Kan. 2002)
Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Community, LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Quinn Ngiendo v. University Partners, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested waiver of defendants' policies barring prospective tenants from inspecting actual rental units and barring staff from cleaning apartments, due to her allergic rhinitis triggered by mold.
key_holding: Plaintiff sufficiently stated a reasonable accommodation claim for rhinitis because she requested to inspect units for mold and requested mold removal, both of which were refused by defendants under their no-showing and no-cleaning policies, plausibly alleging a reasonable accommodation that was denied.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Quinn Ngiendo sued multiple apartment complex owners and managers alleging they failed to accommodate her rhinitis by refusing to allow unit inspections or mold remediation, placed her in mold-infested units in retaliation for seeking accommodations, and discriminated against her based on her race (Black) and national origin (Kenyan). The court dismissed her PTSD accommodation claims but allowed her rhinitis accommodation, retaliation, and disparate treatment claims to proceed against Asset and Everest.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Bekkem v. Wilkie, 915 F.3d 1258 (10th Cir. 2019)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Melissa Wren v. City of Cherryvale Kansas, The Housing Authority of the City of Cherryvale, Kansas
accommodation_description: Request to remain in a two-bedroom public housing unit rather than being transferred to a one-bedroom unit, to provide a separate bedroom for nighttime personal assistance aides who provide disability-related care.
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff was likely to succeed on her FHA failure-to-accommodate claim because CHA's occupancy policy requiring transfer to a one-bedroom unit was a rule that must yield to reasonable accommodation when Plaintiff needed a bedroom for her nighttime aides to provide disability-related care, and CHA failed to make such accommodation.
brief_summary: Melissa Wren, a mobility-impaired tenant using a wheelchair due to leg amputations, sued the Cherryvale Housing Authority after being ordered to transfer from a two-bedroom to a one-bedroom unit, claiming the transfer violated the FHA by failing to accommodate her need for a separate bedroom for her nighttime personal assistance aides. The court granted a temporary restraining order, finding Plaintiff likely would succeed on the merits because CHA's occupancy policy must yield to reasonable accommodation when Plaintiff needed the larger unit for disability-related aide care.
key_cases_cited: Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
First Baptist Church v. Kelly, 455 F. Supp. 3d 1078 (D. Kan. 2020)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)

case_name: Quinn Ngiendo v. Asset Campus USA, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested accommodations for mold allergies and breathing sensitivities, including cleaning and remediation by Everest, and for PTSD-related noise sensitivity including ground floor transfer and quiet environment from Cardinal.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to all three defendants because plaintiff failed to submit evidence supporting her reasonable accommodation claims—she never provided medical documentation to Everest for her accommodation request, and her request for accommodation from excessive noise was not shown to be necessary or reasonable.
brief_summary: Quinn Ngiendo, a pro se Black immigrant from Kenya, sued three property management companies (Asset Campus, Everest Campus West, and Cardinal Group Management) alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to accommodate her disabilities (mold allergies and PTSD-related noise sensitivity) and disparate treatment based on race and national origin. The court granted summary judgment to all defendants after plaintiff failed to respond to their motions and produce evidence supporting her claims, finding no genuine issue of material fact on her failure to accommodate, retaliation, or disparate treatment claims.
key_cases_cited: Argo v. Blue Cross Blue Shield of Kan., Inc.
Prindable v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n
Wilson v. Guardian Mgmt., LLC

source_file: 9711547_2024_Adam v. Andmark White Lakes Apartments, LLC_ksd
case_name: Garry Adam v. Andmark White Lakes Apartments, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought repairs and maintenance as a reasonable accommodation for his visual disability, along with communication in 18-point font and ability to pay rent via check instead of online portal (as specified in a prior conciliation agreement).
key_holding: The court held that repair requests do not constitute a reasonable accommodation under the FHA because the accommodation framework requires a change to an existing rule or policy, not merely a request for maintenance; the court dismissed the failure to accommodate and modification claims but allowed the retaliation claim to proceed.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Garry Adam, a visually disabled tenant at White Lakes Plaza Apartments in Topeka, Kansas, sued the property owner, management company, and subsequent owners alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to accommodate, failure to permit modifications, misrepresentation of dwelling availability, and retaliation after the parties had previously entered into a conciliation agreement regarding his disability accommodations. The court dismissed most claims but allowed the retaliation claim to proceed, holding that repair requests are not reasonable accommodations under the FHA and that post-agreement discriminatory conduct could form the basis of a retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Ngiendo v. Univ. Partners, LLC, No. 22-2393-HLT, 2021 WL 5038780 (D. Kan. Oct. 27, 2021)
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n, No. 21-4035, 2022 WL 2452379 (10th Cir. July 6, 2022)
Fleming v. Bailey, No. 8:17-CV-2346-T-33MAP, 2018 WL 904287 (M.D. Fla. Feb. 7, 2018)

source_file: 9711727_2024_Adam v. Andmark White Lakes Apartments, LLC_ksd
case_name: Garry Adam v. Andmark White Lakes Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who is blind, requested that defendants put the offer to transfer units in writing, and argued that his repair requests constituted reasonable accommodation requests.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to allege that defendants OPG and 37North knew or reasonably should have known of his disability, and because plaintiff's requests for repairs did not constitute requests for accommodation of an otherwise valid policy.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Garry Adam, who is legally blind due to a retinal dystrophy, sued his former landlords and property managers alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to accommodate, refusal to rent, and retaliation. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to state a claim because the new property managers (OPG and 37North) lacked knowledge of his disability and because plaintiff's repair requests did not constitute valid accommodation requests under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Hatfield v. Cottages on 78th Cmty. Ass'n, No. 21-4035 (10th Cir. 2022)
Ngiendo v. Univ. Partners, LLC, No. 22-2393-HLT (D. Kan. 2021)

source_file: 9714170_2023_Garvey Farm LP v. City of Elsmere, Kentucky_kyed
case_name: Garvey Farm LP v. City of Elsmere, Kentucky
accommodation_description: Mobile home park operator seeks to expand by rezoning adjacent vacant land for manufactured housing development, alleging discriminatory denial based on protected class composition of prospective residents
key_holding: The city council members have absolute legislative immunity for their zoning decisions regardless of alleged discriminatory motive; the vacant land satisfies the FHA definition of 'dwelling'; and plaintiffs failed to show the taking was pretextual since parks and public facilities constitute legitimate public use.
brief_summary: Garvey Farm LP, a mobile home park operator, sued the City of Elsmere, Kentucky after the city denied its zoning amendment to expand the park onto adjacent vacant land and passed an ordinance making such expansion more difficult. The court dismissed the claims against individual council members based on legislative immunity, found that vacant land qualifies as a dwelling under § 3604(b), and dismissed the takings and conspiracy claims because the city's stated public use (park and public works facility) was not pretextual.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Bogan v. Scott-Harris
Kelo v. City of New London

case_name: Miller v. Alco Management, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Alco Defendants discriminated against him based on gender and disabled status in the terms, conditions, and privileges of his rental and services in connection with his tenancy at Southern Oaks apartment complex.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims as time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations, finding the alleged discriminatory housing practices occurred no later than July 24, 2020 (when plaintiff moved out) but the complaint was not filed until August 11, 2022.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ron Miller sued Alco Management, Inc. and related entities (property management) and Georgetown Police Department, alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on gender and disability discrimination in the terms and conditions of his tenancy at Southern Oaks apartment complex. The court overruled plaintiff's objections and adopted the magistrate judge's recommendation, granting defendants' motions to dismiss because the FHA claims were time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Alberto James Santiago, Sr. v. Elishia Chill, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that management repair a broken door that caused him physical injury and prevented him from entering and exiting his apartment due to his mobility disability and arm condition.
key_holding: The court allowed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims alleging discrimination based on national origin and disability, retaliation, and failure to make reasonable accommodation to proceed against defendants Chill, Hext, and Key Property Management, while dismissing claims against other defendants for failure to meet pleading standards.
brief_summary: Alberto James Santiago, a 57-year-old tenant in a wheelchair with a mobility-related arm injury, sued Key Property Management and individual managers alleging discrimination, failure to accommodate (broken door not repaired for years causing physical injury and trapping him in his apartment), retaliation for filing HUD complaints, and discriminatory eviction based on his national origin (Puerto Rican) and disability. The court dismissed claims against some defendants for failing to meet pleading standards but allowed claims for discrimination, retaliation, and failure to make reasonable accommodation to proceed against the primary defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Groner v. Golden Gate Gardens Apartments, 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)
Larkin v. State of Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)

case_name: Rodney L. Nance, Sr. v. Mayfield Plaza Apartments Management et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a convicted felon released from incarceration, alleges he was threatened with eviction from his fiancée's apartment based on his criminal history.
key_holding: Plaintiff lacks standing to bring FHA claims because he is not a lessee, and even construing the complaint liberally, his status as a felon is not a protected class under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Rodney Nance sued Mayfield Plaza Apartments Management alleging he was threatened with eviction from his fiancée's apartment because he is a convicted felon. The court dismissed the case, finding plaintiff lacks standing because he is not a lessee and his status as a convicted felon is not a protected class under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife

case_name: Yusef Abdullah Bilal Ali v. Louisville Metro Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep his 120-pound pit bull-Mastiff emotional support animal off leash during surgical recovery from knee surgery.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's Emergency Motion for Injunction Pending Appeal, finding Plaintiff failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits because his requested off-leash accommodation was unreasonable given the dog's documented aggressive behavior, and his transfer request was properly denied because he failed to provide required medical documentation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Yusef Abdullah Bilal Ali, a public housing tenant with mobility impairments requiring knee surgery, sued Louisville Metro Housing Authority alleging failure to accommodate his requests to keep his emotional support animal off leash and to transfer to a two-bedroom single-family unit. The court denied Plaintiff's Emergency Motion for Injunction Pending Appeal, finding he could not demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits because the off-leash request posed unacceptable safety risks given the dog's documented aggressive history, and LMHA properly denied the transfer request after Plaintiff failed to provide required medical documentation.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Hollis v. Chesnut Bend Homeowner's Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC, 988 F.3d 1185 (9th Cir. 2021)
Kleiber v. Honda of America Mfg., Inc., 485 F.3d 862 (6th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Watkins v. Commonwealth of Kentucky
accommodation_description: Plaintiff references Fair Housing Act violations in challenging an eviction proceeding but does not specify a particular accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because her complaint was written solely as a legal conclusion unsupported by facts, failing to state a claim upon which relief may be granted.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Rita Watkins, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued the Commonwealth of Kentucky, her mobile home park landlord, an attorney, and two state court judges challenging an eviction proceeding for failure to pay rent and alleging Fair Housing Act and constitutional violations. The court dismissed the action for failure to state a claim, finding that while Plaintiff referenced the FHA, she provided only legal conclusions without factual support for any discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Ronnie Joe Forte v. Hopkinsville Housing Authority et al.
