accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges denial of housing application based on pending drug charges under a blanket criminal history ban; not framed as a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The FHA does not prohibit discrimination on the basis of criminal history, and public housing agencies may deny admission to applicants engaged in drug-related criminal activity under 42 U.S.C. § 13661(c).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ronnie Joe Forte sued the Hopkinsville Housing Authority under the Fair Housing Act, alleging he was denied Section 8 housing because of pending drug charges under a blanket criminal history ban. The court dismissed the case, holding that the FHA does not prohibit discrimination based on criminal history and that housing authorities may lawfully deny admission to applicants engaged in drug-related criminal activity.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Robertson v. Olsen 2023 WL 5284480
Smith v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev. 2022 WL 18107588
Nickell v. Metro. 2018 WL 2392849

source_file: 9718438_2019_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege discrimination based on familial status (having four children) in the rental of a condominium unit.
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on defendants' claim of exemption under § 3603(b)(2), held they cannot claim the 'Mrs. Murphy' exemption because each unit owner owns only their individual unit and common area interest, not the entire building, and found genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether defendants' imposition of fines, attempted eviction, occupancy rule enforcement, and storage shed lock changes constituted unlawful interference under § 3617.
brief_summary: The Treece family (with four children) sued the Perrier Condominium Owners Association and individual unit owners for Fair Housing Act violations based on familial status discrimination after the owners attempted to evict them and imposed fines on their landlord. The court granted summary judgment for defendants only on the § 3604(c) discriminatory advertising claim, but denied summary judgment on defendants' Mrs. Murphy exemption defense and on plaintiffs' § 3617 interference claims, finding disputed facts about whether the defendants' coordinated campaign to force the family out violated fair housing law.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Bloch v. Frischholz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Association, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Dillon v. AFBIC Dev. Corp., 597 F.2d 556 (5th Cir. 1979)

source_file: 9718630_2020_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Refusal to rent a condominium unit because the applicants had three small children, allegedly misrepresenting the unit's availability to discourage their application.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion for partial summary judgment, finding disputed material facts exist regarding whether the defendant misrepresented unit availability and refused to negotiate with tenants because of their familial status, including direct evidence that defendant stated she did not rent to them because they had three small children.
brief_summary: Dwayne and Phallon Treece sued defendant Hannah Haile, a condominium owner, alleging she violated the Fair Housing Act by misrepresenting the availability of her rental unit and refusing to rent to them because they had three children. The court denied Haile's motion for partial summary judgment, finding disputed facts and direct evidence of discriminatory intent—specifically Haile's statement that she decided the condo was not a good fit because they have three small children—precluded summary judgment on the familial status discrimination claims under § 3604(a) and (d).
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Darby v. Heather Ridge, 806 F. Supp. 170 (E.D. Mich. 1992)
Danenhower v. Dorsey, 1997 WL 469960 (E.D. La. 1997)

source_file: 9718744_2020_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; this is a disparate impact challenge to an occupancy limit on families with children.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion for reconsideration, finding no manifest error of law in using rejection rate comparisons to analyze disparate impact, and rejecting defendants' arguments that acceptance rates should be used instead.
brief_summary: The Treece family sued the Perrier Condominium Owners Association challenging an occupancy limit as having a disparate impact on families with children in violation of the Fair Housing Act's familial status protections. The court denied defendants' motion for reconsideration of a prior order, finding no manifest error of law in its methodology comparing rejection rates to analyze disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Veasey v. Abbott, 830 F.3d 216 (5th Cir. 2016)
Langlois v. Abington Housing Authority, 234 F. Supp. 2d 33 (D. Mass. 2002)
Gashi v. Grubb & Ellis Prop. Mgmt. Servs., Inc., 801 F. Supp. 2d 12 (D. Conn. 2011)

source_file: 9718781_2020_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation requested; claim concerns alleged familial status discrimination through misrepresentations about housing availability.
key_holding: The court denied Haile's motion to reconsider because she failed to demonstrate manifest error of law, presenting instead evidence that was available during the original motion and rehashing arguments that could have been raised earlier.
brief_summary: Dwayne and Phallon Treece sued landlord Hannah Haile alleging she violated the Fair Housing Act by misrepresenting the availability of her condominium unit and making housing unavailable because of their familial status. The court denied Haile's motion to reconsider its prior order denying her motion for partial summary judgment, finding Haile failed to demonstrate manifest error of law and merely attempted to present evidence and arguments that could have been raised in the original motion.
key_cases_cited: Darby v. Heather Ridge, 806 F. Supp. 170 (E.D. Mich. 1992)

case_name: Russell Andre Briley v. Ignatius Barreca Jr. et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; plaintiff generally alleges unlawful acts including discrimination related to tenants who receive government funds for disabilities.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss and granted plaintiff leave to amend because plaintiff's complaint failed to plead sufficient facts to support claims under ADA, Title VI, Title VII, or the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Russell Andre Briley sued Barreca defendants and Josephine Broadwater alleging violations of ADA, Title VI, Title VII, and state law related to his residential tenancy and eviction in New Orleans. The court denied Broadwater's motion to dismiss and granted plaintiff leave to amend because the complaint failed to plead sufficient factual allegations to state any viable federal claim, including failing to allege a qualifying disability or discriminatory animus.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2008)
Inclusive Comtys. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)

case_name: Russell Andre Briley v. Ignatius Barreca, Jr. et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; plaintiff failed to state any coherent Fair Housing Act claim.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's federal claims with prejudice because he failed to allege any facts to state a claim under the FHA, ADA, Title VI, or Title VII, and declined supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Russell Briley sued landlords Ignatius Barreca Jr., David Barreca Sr., and Josephine Broadwater alleging violations of the ADA, Title VI, Title VII, and Fair Housing Act, though the complaint contained no factual allegations supporting any claim. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to state any claim under federal law, and declined supplemental jurisdiction over state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2008)

source_file: 9719738_2021_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, a family of six with four children, sought to rent a condominium unit but were subject to occupancy limits (250/400 square feet per person) and attempted eviction based on familial status.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish robust causation required for a disparate impact claim under § 3604(a) because they offered no evidence that the occupancy limits caused families with children to be the dominant group of renters affected, as families with children already comprised larger households prior to the challenged policy.
brief_summary: The Treece family of six sued the Perrier Condominium Owners Association alleging that occupancy limits (250/400 square feet per person) violated the Fair Housing Act by having a disparate impact on families with children based on familial status. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for partial summary judgment and granted defendants' cross-motion, finding plaintiffs failed to establish the robust causation element of their disparate impact claim because families with children already had larger households before the challenged policy, and the 400 square foot rule was never actually enforced.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Property Co.
Reyes v. Azucar Bakery

case_name: Anna Monus v. Edward Riecke
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlord's sexual assault and harassment during a rent payment meeting altered the conditions of her housing and created a hostile housing environment.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiff's allegation of unwanted touching of intimate body parts—even a single incident—is sufficient to state a hostile housing environment claim under the FHA, and that interference with use and enjoyment of the property satisfies the alteration-of-housing-conditions requirement without requiring a change in economic benefits.
brief_summary: Anna Monus sued her landlord Edward Riecke alleging he sexually assaulted and harassed her during a rent payment meeting, creating a hostile housing environment in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied Riecke's motion to dismiss under Rules 12(b)(6) and 12(b)(1), holding that a single incident of unwanted touching of intimate body parts is sufficiently severe to state a hostile housing environment claim under the FHA, and that interference with use and enjoyment of the property satisfies the required alteration of housing conditions.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Greater New Orleans Fair Hous. Action Ctr. v. Kelly, 364 F. Supp. 3d 635 (E.D. La. 2019)
Harvill v. Westward Commc'ns, L.L.C., 433 F.3d 428 (5th Cir. 2005)

source_file: 9720571_2021_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No disability-related accommodation requested; case involves claims of familial status discrimination based on occupancy restrictions and attempted eviction of a family with four children.
key_holding: The court modified its February 12, 2021 order to clarify that plaintiffs' claims for intentional disparate treatment under § 3604(a), § 3617 claims, and Harlan's § 3604(a) and (b) claims remain pending; only disparate impact claims under § 3604(a) were disposed of on the merits.
brief_summary: Dwayne and Phallon Treece, a family of six, sued the Perrier Condominium Owners Association and individual unit owners for discriminating against them based on familial status by enforcing occupancy restrictions, attempting eviction, and fining their landlord. The court clarified that while disparate impact claims were dismissed, plaintiffs' claims for intentional disparate treatment under § 3604(a) and § 3617 retaliation claims remain pending.
key_cases_cited: Treece v. Perrier Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 519 F. Supp. 3d 342 (E.D. La. 2021)

source_file: 9720836_2022_Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Plantation Management Company, LLC_laed
case_name: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center Inc. v. Plantation Management Co. LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Deaf prospective residents requested video remote interpreting (VRI) services as a reasonable accommodation to enable effective communication with staff at a nursing home/assisted living facility.
key_holding: The court found that the plaintiff adequately alleged ownership interest and agency relationship, sufficient to state claims for discriminatory refusal to provide interpretive services under the FHA and ADA, rejecting defendants' arguments that conclusory ownership allegations and agency relationship were insufficient at the pleading stage.
brief_summary: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center sued nursing home owners for refusing to provide video remote interpreting services to deaf prospective residents, alleging violations of the FHA and ADA. The court denied the owners' motion to dismiss, finding the plaintiff's allegations of direct ownership and agency relationship were sufficient to put defendants on notice and survive a 12(b)(6) motion.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Rodriguez v. Baird's Bakery, Inc., 111 F.3d 893 (5th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 9720937_2022_Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Plantation Management Company, LLC_laed
case_name: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Plantation Management Company, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Fair housing organization alleges on behalf of prospective deaf residents that nursing home staff refused to provide ASL interpretation or video remote interpreting (VRI) services.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that the fair housing organization had standing based on diverted organizational resources and adequately stated FHA reasonable accommodation claims against the facility owners/operators for refusing to provide interpretive services to deaf prospective residents.
brief_summary: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center sued CommCare Corporation and CommCare Management Corporation (owners/operators of Greenbriar Community Care Center nursing facility in Slidell, Louisiana), alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to provide ASL interpretation or VRI services to prospective deaf residents. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the fair housing organization had standing to sue and adequately stated reasonable accommodation claims under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2008)
Meyer v. Holst, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)

source_file: 9721018_2022_Treece v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc._laed
case_name: Dwayne Treece, et al. v. Perrier Condominium Owners Association, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves familial status discrimination challenge to occupancy limits and eviction threats directed at family with four children.
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment on plaintiffs' § 3604(a) disparate treatment claim because material facts regarding defendants' intent to discriminate against families with children are in genuine dispute, but granted summary judgment on Harlan's claim that defendants blocked prospective buyers from purchasing his unit.
brief_summary: Dwayne and Phallon Treece (with their four children) and their landlord Clifford Harlan sued the Perrier Condominium Owners Association and individual unit owners for discriminatory actions including threatening eviction, changing occupancy rules from 250 to 400 square feet per occupant, and harassment based on the family's size. The court denied summary judgment for defendants on the familial status discrimination claim under § 3604(a) because disputed facts about intent preclude summary judgment, but granted summary judgment on Harlan's separate claim that the occupancy rules prevented him from selling his unit to families.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Texas Dept. of Housing and Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Whyte v. Alston Management, Inc., 2011 WL 12450320 (S.D. Fla. 2011)
Carlson v. Sunshine Villas HOA, Inc., 2018 WL 2317820 (M.D. Fla. 2018)

source_file: 9721118_2022_Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Azalea Garden Properties, LLC_laed
case_name: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Azalea Garden Properties, LLC
accommodation_description: Blanket criminal background screening policy allegedly having disparate impact on persons with disabilities (recovering from drug addiction) by excluding them without individualized assessment
key_holding: The disability-based disparate impact claim was dismissed because LaFHAC failed to allege any statistical data showing that individuals with drug convictions are likely to be recovering from an addiction that substantially limits major life activities, failing to establish the requisite causal connection for a disparate impact claim.
brief_summary: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center sued Azalea Garden Properties, LLC alleging that its blanket criminal background screening policy violates the FHA by having a disparate impact on African American renters and persons recovering from drug addiction. The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the race-based disparate impact claim, finding sufficient statistical allegations, but granted dismissal as to the disability claim because LaFHAC failed to allege any data connecting drug convictions to individuals with qualifying disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Property Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)

source_file: 9721185_2022_Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center, Inc. v. Plantation Management Company, LLC_laed
case_name: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center Inc. v. Plantation Management Co. LLC
accommodation_description: Prospective deaf residents requested ASL interpreter services or Video Remote Interpreting (VRI) to enable effective communication with staff at an assisted living/nursing home facility.
key_holding: The court denied the defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that the fair housing organization plaintiff had standing and adequately stated FHA and other claims based on allegations that facility employees refused to provide interpretive services (ASL/VRI) to prospective deaf residents seeking housing.
brief_summary: Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center sued St. Jude Management #2, LLC and Lawrence Stansberry (owners/operators of St. Luke's Living Center, an assisted living/nursing home), alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act and other statutes by refusing to provide ASL interpreters or VRI services to prospective deaf residents. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the organization had standing based on resource diversion and adequately stated a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
OCA-Greater Houston v. Texas, 867 F.3d 604 (5th Cir. 2017)
Neff v. Am. Dairy Queen Corp., 58 F.3d 1063 (5th Cir. 1995)

case_name: Velma Lee v. J.M. Property Holdings, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case involves refusal to rent to family with children based on policy against opposite-gender children sharing a bedroom.
key_holding: The court denied Mai's motions to dismiss and for summary judgment without prejudice under Rule 56(d), finding that genuine issues of material fact exist regarding Mai's involvement in setting and enforcing an allegedly discriminatory policy against families, and that Plaintiff needs additional discovery time to adequately oppose the motions.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Velma Lee sued J.M. Property Holdings, LLC, Thao P. Mai, and Tammy Sponberg alleging familial status discrimination after a property manager refused to rent her a unit because her two children of opposite genders were allegedly too old to share a bedroom. The court denied Mai's motions to dismiss and for summary judgment without prejudice, finding disputed facts exist regarding Mai's involvement in managing the property and setting the allegedly discriminatory policy, and that plaintiff needs additional discovery time.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)

case_name: Lisa N Carey v. St. Charles Parish
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identifiable; plaintiff appears to allege disparate treatment discrimination based on race when parish enforcement actions intensified after learning she, a Black woman, owned the property.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because she failed to plead a prima facie case of disparate treatment (no facts showing she applied for housing, was rejected, or that housing remained open to similarly situated applicants), and alternatively, all claims were barred by res judicata as they arose from the same transaction as prior state court litigation.
brief_summary: Lisa N. Carey, proceeding pro se, sued St. Charles Parish in federal court alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on the Parish's enforcement of unsafe structure ordinances against her property after learning she, a Black woman, owned it. The court granted the Parish's motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding Carey failed to state a prima facie disparate treatment claim and that res judicata barred relitigating claims arising from the same transaction as prior state court proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Crain v. City of Selma, 952 F.3d 634 (5th Cir. 2020)
Stevens v. St. Tammany Parish Government, 17 F.4th 563 (5th Cir. 2021)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
England v. La. State Board of Medical Examiners, 375 U.S. 411 (1964)

case_name: Jessica Bush v. Broad Management Group, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to keep two pit bull dogs as emotional support animals to assist with her anxiety, depression, PTSD, chemical seizures, and agoraphobia.
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment because genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether defendants had sufficient notice of plaintiff's disability and whether their April 5 denial letter, combined with the subsequent eviction notice citing unauthorized animals, constituted a refusal to provide a reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Jessica Bush sued Broad Management Group and property manager Cristina Perez after being denied permission to keep her two pit bull emotional support animals at Pinecrest Apartments, claiming violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether defendants had adequate notice of Bush's disability and whether the denial of her accommodation request, followed by an eviction citing unauthorized animals, constituted unlawful discrimination and retaliation under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Elliott v. QF Circa 37, LLC, No. 16-0288, 2018 WL 2933467 (S.D. Cal. June 12, 2018)
Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Baton Rouge, 932 F. Supp. 2d 683 (M.D. La. 2013)

case_name: Hughes v. Bouchon Properties LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly sought to defend against eviction and obtain reasonable accommodations for undisclosed disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's § 3604(f) disability discrimination claim because the complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing (1) a nexus between the eviction and plaintiff's disability, (2) that any accommodation was requested, or (3) that any accommodation was necessary to afford plaintiff equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling.
brief_summary: African-American tenant Trashawn Hughes sued landlord Lester Bouchon and Bouchon Properties LLC alleging racial discrimination, disability discrimination, and state law claims after being evicted from his rental unit. The court denied the motion to dismiss the § 1982 and § 3604(a) racial discrimination claims but granted dismissal of the § 3604(f) disability discrimination claim because plaintiff's complaint contained only conclusory allegations about disability and failed to allege that any accommodation was requested or necessary.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 420 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)
Inclusive Cmty. Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Women’s Elevated Sober Living L.L.C. v. City of Plano, Texas, 86 F.4th 1108 (5th Cir. 2023)

source_file: 9724281_2024_Military Road Revitalization Company, LLC v. St. Tammany Parish Government_laed
case_name: Military Road Revitalization Company, LLC et al. v. St. Tammany Parish Government
accommodation_description: Developers of an affordable housing project allege the parish's moratorium ordinance blocks housing for protected classes including persons with disabilities, constituting disparate impact discrimination under the FHA.
key_holding: The court granted St. Tammany's motion to dismiss BCP's claims as moot and Military Road's due process claims as unripe, but denied the motion to dismiss Military Road's Fair Housing Act claims, finding the claims are ripe and not barred by Colorado River abstention.
brief_summary: Military Road Revitalization Company and BCP (developers) sued St. Tammany Parish Government alleging that a moratorium ordinance blocking an affordable housing development violated the Fair Housing Act by having a disparate impact on African Americans, Hispanics, and persons with disabilities, and constituted intentional race-based discrimination. The court granted in part and denied in part the motions to dismiss, dismissing BCP's claims as moot and Military Road's due process claims as unripe, while allowing the FHA claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

case_name: Larry Jones v. Caliber Home Loans, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges he was steered into a sub-prime loan and subjected to a fraudulent mortgage document switch because he is African-American.
key_holding: The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss because plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts to support any theory of discrimination—his allegations were vague, conclusory, and devoid of factual enhancement required under Twombly and Iqbal, and plaintiff abandoned his FHA and ECOA claims by failing to substantively respond to defendant's arguments.
brief_summary: Larry Jones, an African-American homeowner, sued Caliber Home Loans alleging racial discrimination in mortgage refinancing, claiming he was steered into a sub-prime loan through a fraudulent bait-and-switch scheme. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff's FHA and ECOA claims failed because his allegations were conclusory, lacked factual support for disparate treatment or disparate impact theories, and plaintiff abandoned his claims by failing to substantively oppose defendant's arguments.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Tex. Dept. of House & Comm. Affairs v. Inclusive Comms. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)

case_name: Wilbert Aron and Katlin Morris v. G. Lewis-Louisiana No. 2, L.L.C. and Garry Lewis d/b/a Garry Lewis Properties
accommodation_description: Request for exception to criminal background policy as reasonable accommodation for recovering drug addiction disability
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion, finding that Plaintiffs adequately alleged Aron has a 'handicap' under the FHA because he has 'a record of such impairment'—his addiction led to drug-related arrests and convictions, and untreated, it substantially limited major life activities including work and sleep.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Wilbert Aron and Katlin Morris sued G. Lewis-Louisiana No. 2, L.L.C. and Garry Lewis d/b/a Garry Lewis Properties, alleging Defendants violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to make a reasonable accommodation for Aron's disability (recovering drug addiction) by waiving their criminal background policy, resulting in Morris and her children's eviction. The court denied Defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings, finding that Plaintiffs adequately pleaded Aron has a 'handicap' under the FHA because he has a record of impairment from his addiction.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Prop. Owners Ass'n
Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Baton Rouge, La.

source_file: 9726226_2023_Believe TGH LLC v. Pointe Coupee Parish_lamd
case_name: Believe TGH, LLC, et al. v. Pointe Coupee Parish, Louisiana
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs sought a zoning variance to expand the 'family' definition to allow ten unrelated minors with mental and emotional disabilities (PTSD, depression, developmental delays, autism) to reside together in a therapeutic group home.
key_holding: The court denied both preliminary injunction and cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist regarding whether the zoning accommodation is reasonable and necessary for the therapeutic group home housing minors with disabilities.
brief_summary: Believe TGH, LLC and related entities (operators of therapeutic group homes for minors with disabilities) sued Pointe Coupee Parish after the Planning and Zoning Commission denied their variance request to expand the zoning 'family' definition to permit ten unrelated disabled minors to reside together. The court denied Plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction and denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the accommodation is reasonable and necessary.
key_cases_cited: Oxford House, Inc. v. Browning, 266 F. Supp. 3d 896 (M.D. La. 2017)
Groome Resources, Ltd., L.L.C. v. Parish of Jefferson, 52 F. Supp. 2d 721 (E.D. La. 1999)
Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Baton Rouge, 932 F. Supp. 2d 683 (M.D. La. 2013)
Elderhaven, Inc. v. City of Lubbock, 98 F.3d 175 (5th Cir. 1996)
Harmony Haus Westlake, L.L.C. v. Parkstone Prop. Owners Ass'n, 851 F. App'x 461 (5th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Clifford Osborne et al v. Kevin Belton
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support dog at the rental property as a reasonable accommodation for developmental disabilities, anxiety, and depression
key_holding: The court found that the landlord violated the FHA by refusing to accommodate Mr. Osborne's disability with an emotional support animal, establishing all five prima facie elements including that the accommodation was necessary and reasonable, and further retaliated against him by evicting him after he requested the accommodation.
brief_summary: Clifford Osborne sued his landlord Kevin Belton alleging Fair Housing Act violations after Belton refused to allow Osborne to keep an emotional support dog at the rental property and subsequently evicted him for violating the no-pets policy. The court granted Osborne's unopposed motion for summary judgment, finding Belton violated the FHA by failing to reasonably accommodate Osborne's disability and retaliated against him for requesting the accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 666 F. Supp. 2d 850 (S.D. Ohio 2009)
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County, Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 440 F. Supp. 3d 654 (W.D. Tex. 2020)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Varrecchio v. Friends All. Hous. II, Inc., No. 18-8915 (E.D. La. Dec. 11, 2018)

source_file: 9730812_2019_Mannai Home LLC v. City of Fall River, Massachusetts_mad
case_name: Mannai Home, LLC v. City of Fall River
accommodation_description: Request that the City of Fall River allow conversion of a three-unit property into a sober recovery home for 20-22 individuals in recovery from substance abuse without requiring a special permit for group residences.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the failure-to-accommodate claim because Mannai Home never requested an accommodation—it merely asserted the right to proceed as a matter of right without a special permit—and the city's failure to respond to inquiries did not constitute a denial of reasonable accommodation where no cognizable accommodation was requested.
brief_summary: Mannai Home, LLC sued the City of Fall River and its building inspector seeking to establish a sober recovery home, alleging the City violated the Fair Housing Act and Massachusetts Zoning Act by discriminatorily requiring a special permit for group residences housing disabled individuals. The court granted defendants' motion for summary judgment on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding Mannai never actually requested an accommodation but instead demanded the right to proceed without permits, and denied all other motions as to intentional discrimination claims and the state zoning law claim.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condominium Ass'n v. Secretary, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)
Safe Haven Sober Houses, LLC v. Good, 82 Mass. App. Ct. 1112 (2012)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)

source_file: 9730987_2019_National Association of the Deaf v. Harvard University_mad

case_name: Danyela Schonton, et al. v. MPA Granada Highlands LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves Brazilian tenants alleging national origin discrimination in leasing, including allegedly discriminatory identification requirements, citizenship documentation demands, and unequal lease renewal terms.
key_holding: The court denied class certification because plaintiffs failed to establish commonality (no common discriminatory policy identified), typicality (named plaintiffs' claims varied significantly), and adequacy of representation (named plaintiffs lacked understanding of class action duties and had conflicting interests); individualized inquiries would be required for each class member's disparate treatment claim.
brief_summary: Brazilian tenants and tenant applicants sued the owner and management of Granada Highlands apartment complex alleging national origin discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming defendants selectively imposed discriminatory qualification requirements including proof of citizenship, multiple forms of identification, and pet removal policies. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for class certification, finding plaintiffs failed to establish commonality, typicality, and adequacy of representation because the proposed class lacked a common discriminatory policy and individual claims required highly individualized inquiries.
key_cases_cited: Wal-Mart v. Dukes, 564 U.S. 338 (2011)
In re Nexium Antitrust Litig., 777 F.3d 9 (1st Cir. 2015)
Comcast Corp. v. Behrend, 569 U.S. 27 (2013)
In re New Motor Vehicles Canadian Export Antitrust Litig., 522 F.3d 6 (1st Cir. 2008)
Amchem Prods., Inc. v. Windsor, 521 U.S. 591 (1997)

source_file: 9731159_2019_Pham v. Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas_mad
case_name: Kimlan T. Pham v. Deutsche Bank Trust Company Americas, Ocwen Loan Servicing, LLC and GMAC Mortgage, LLC
accommodation_description: Loan modification under HAMP for a homeowner whose husband receives disability benefits; plaintiff alleged discrimination because disability income rendered her ineligible for refinancing
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of FHA discrimination because Ocwen offered two HAMP loan modifications which plaintiff rejected by not making trial payments, and there is no evidence of discriminatory intent or disparate impact from defendants' lending practices.
brief_summary: Pham sued Deutsche Bank and Ocwen alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act based on disability discrimination in loan modification, claiming she was told her husband's disability income made her ineligible for refinancing. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that Ocwen never rejected Pham's modification request and instead offered two HAMP trial period plans which Pham herself rejected by failing to make required payments or submit documentation.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)
Nereida-Gonzalez v. Tirado-Delgado, 990 F.2d 701 (1st Cir. 1993)
Lindsay v. Yates, 578 F.3d 407 (6th Cir. 2009)
Thompson v. Coca-Cola Co., 522 F.3d 168 (1st Cir. 2008)

case_name: Danyela Schonton, et al. v. MPA Granada Highlands LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it is a national origin discrimination case under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) involving Brazilian tenants challenging rental policy requirements for foreign citizens.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish a prima facie case of national origin discrimination because they did not demonstrate they were qualified to lease units under Granada's Rental Policy (which required a visa for foreign citizens), and they produced no evidence that similarly situated non-Brazilian tenants were subject to less stringent requirements.
brief_summary: Brazilian tenants and tenant applicants sued Granada Apartments and its owner/management, alleging national origin discrimination under the Fair Housing Act based on the property's rental policies requiring foreign citizens to provide specific identification documents including visas. The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs failed to establish a prima facie case—they did not meet the rental policy requirements and could not identify similarly situated non-Brazilian tenants who were treated more favorably.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Batista v. Cooperativa de Vivienda Jardines de San Ignacio, 776 F.3d 38 (1st Cir. 2015)
Pina v. Town of Plympton, 529 F. Supp. 2d 151 (D. Mass. 2007)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)
Pham v. Deutsche Bank Trust Co. Americas, 2019 WL 2340957 (D. Mass. June 3, 2019)

case_name: Ferahnaz Kahyaoglu v. Omar Salim Sayied and Abul Maksud Sayied
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; plaintiff alleges housing discrimination based on protected characteristics (color, gender, national origin, race, ethnicity) regarding uninhabitable conditions rather than a request for reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims were dismissed because she failed to allege sufficient facts showing discriminatory intent or that defendants' actions were motivated, at least in part, by discrimination as required to maintain FHA claims under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604 and 3617.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ferahnaz Kahyaoglu sued her private landlords Omar Salim Sayied and Abul Maksud Sayied alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on her color, gender, national origin, race, and ethnicity regarding uninhabitable living conditions, along with retaliation claims. The court dismissed the amended complaint for lack of subject matter jurisdiction and, alternatively, for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to plead facts sufficient to show discriminatory intent or that defendants' conduct was motivated by protected characteristic discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Lightfoot v. Cendant Mortg. Corp.
S. Middlesex Opportunity Council Inc. v. Town of Framingham

case_name: Marie C. Shay v. Boston Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges BHA refused to comply with an order to immediately provide an apartment, but the complaint lacks sufficient factual detail to identify the specific accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff an extension of time to file an amended complaint because plaintiff's original complaint failed to allege sufficient facts to establish that defendants' conduct was motivated by discriminatory intent or had a disparate impact based on race under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Marie C. Shay sued the Boston Housing Authority and a BHA attorney alleging Fair Housing Act violations after BHA allegedly refused to comply with an order to provide an apartment. The court found the complaint deficient for failure to allege plausible facts showing discriminatory intent or disparate impact based on race, but granted plaintiff twenty-one days to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)

case_name: Clinton-Brown v. Hardick
accommodation_description: African American plaintiffs sought to purchase a home in Rhode Island but were refused sale based on their race.
key_holding: The court lacks personal jurisdiction over the Florida-resident defendants under Massachusetts's long-arm statute and venue is improper in Massachusetts because the discriminatory refusal to sell occurred in Rhode Island, so the case must be transferred to the District of Rhode Island.
brief_summary: African American plaintiffs Ebony Clinton-Brown and Todd Brown sued Florida residents Helene and John Hardick, alleging the Hardicks refused to sell them a Rhode Island property because of their race in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted the defendants' motion to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction and improper venue, finding the Massachusetts long-arm statute did not reach the defendants and Rhode Island was the proper venue, and transferred the case to the District of Rhode Island.

source_file: 9732890_2021_Vanderburgh House LLC v. City of Worcester_mad
case_name: Vanderburgh House, LLC v. City of Worcester
accommodation_description: Operators of sober recovery houses requested that the City treat their properties as single-family residences under the State Building Code to continue operating sober housing in residentially zoned areas.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss on the FHA reasonable accommodation and disparate treatment claims, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged that the City's enforcement actions were motivated by animus toward disabled persons in recovery; however, the court granted qualified immunity to individual defendants and dismissed the MZA and MCRA claims.
brief_summary: Vanderburgh House and Southbridge RE, operators of two sober living houses in Worcester, sued the City of Worcester after receiving cease-and-desist orders requiring them to reclassify their properties from single-family residences to higher-occupancy categories. The court denied the City's motion to dismiss the FHA reasonable accommodation and disparate treatment claims, finding plaintiffs adequately stated claims that enforcement was motivated by discriminatory animus toward disabled persons in recovery, while dismissing the MZA claim and granting qualified immunity to individual defendants.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec., U.S. Dept. of Hous. and Urb. Dev., 620 F.3d 62
Summers v. City of Fitchburg, 325 F. Supp. 3d 203
Crossing Over, Inc. v. City of Fitchburg, 98 Mass. App. Ct. 822
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 843 F. Supp. 1556
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662

source_file: 9733029_2021_Paulsen v. Great Bridge Attleboro Limited Partnership_mad
case_name: Paulsen v. Great Bridge Attleboro Limited Partnership
accommodation_description: The case involves sexual harassment by a maintenance worker rather than a reasonable accommodation request; plaintiff alleges sex discrimination under the FHA's post-acquisition provisions.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that post-acquisition sexual harassment is actionable under § 3604(b) and § 3617 of the FHA, and that sexual harassment may constitute sex-based discrimination under the FHA, following the weight of authority from other circuits.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sara Paulsen sued Great Bridge Attleboro Limited Partnership and others alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on sexual harassment by a maintenance worker at her Attleboro, Massachusetts apartment building. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that post-acquisition sexual harassment constitutes discriminatory conduct under the FHA and that sexual harassment claims are cognizable under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604(b) and 3617.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. King Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Webb v. United States Veterans Initiative, 993 F.3d 970 (D.C. Cir. 2021)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Rosa v. Lawrence Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Rosa requested permission to modify family composition to add her grandchildren to her household and additional time extensions on her Section 8 voucher search period as an accommodation for her multiple psychiatric and medical disabilities.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the housing authority because Rosa failed to establish a causal link between her disabilities and her inability to find housing, and indefinite requests for additional time beyond the substantial extensions already provided are not reasonable accommodations under the FHA.
brief_summary: Gricely Rosa, a Section 8 voucher holder with mental health disabilities, sued the Lawrence Housing Authority alleging failure to accommodate her requests to modify family composition to add grandchildren and to extend her voucher search time. The court granted summary judgment for the Authority, finding Rosa failed to establish the necessary causal link between her disability and her housing difficulties, and that the Authority had already provided substantial accommodations including multiple extensions totaling fifteen months beyond the standard sixty-day voucher period.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Bos. Hous. Auth. v. Bridgewaters, 452 Mass. 833 (2009)
Andover Hous. Auth. v. Shkolnik, 443 Mass. 300 (2005)
Batista v. Cooperativa de Vivienda Jardines de San Ignacio, 776 F.3d 38 (1st Cir. 2015)
Peabody Props., Inc. v. Sherman, 418 Mass. 603 (1994)

source_file: 9733179_2021_Bellus Real Estate and Management Corporation v. Brodeur_mad
case_name: Bellus Real Estate Services and Management Corporation v. Brodeur
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Bellus sought to lease property for use as sober housing for individuals in recovery from substance abuse; defendant Brodeur refused after learning the intended use.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff pled sufficient facts to plausibly allege disability discrimination under § 3604(f)(1) where defendant abruptly terminated lease negotiations after learning the property would house individuals in recovery from substance abuse.
brief_summary: Bellus Real Estate Services, a property management company that operates sober housing, sued individual landlords Brodeur alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to rent after learning the property would house individuals in recovery from substance abuse. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the allegations sufficient to state a plausible claim for relief under § 3604(f)(1) because defendant's sudden termination of negotiations upon learning the intended use permitted an inference that the disability of prospective occupants motivated the refusal.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Steele v. HAPHousing
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges race-based harassment by neighbors and failure of landlord to intervene; no accommodation request at issue.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim against Pedro is barred by res judicata from prior state small claims proceedings, and Way Finders cannot be liable because it had no legal relationship to the alleged harassers and no ability to control their conduct.
brief_summary: Tanya Steele, a Black woman, sued landlord John Pedro Jr. and Section 8 administrator Way Finders alleging they failed to protect her from racial harassment by tenants at a neighboring property in violation of the FHA. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding Steele's claims against Pedro are barred by res judicata from prior small claims proceedings, and Way Finders cannot be liable because it had no legal relationship with the alleged harassers and no power to control their behavior.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Paulsen v. Great Bridge Attleboro Ltd. P'ship, No. 21-10121-NMG (D. Mass. 2021)

case_name: Linda Valentin, Joel Valentin, and Grace Gable Manoors, LLC v. Town of Natick, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—this case involves permit denial allegedly based on race, analyzed as intentional discrimination under § 3604 rather than reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: Plaintiffs adequately alleged discriminatory intent under the FHA where the Board subjected them to 29 hearings over 16 months, reversed its interpretations of the New Bylaw after racist neighborhood opposition, and denied their permit while approving comparable projects by white applicants.
brief_summary: Black Haitian couple Linda and Joel Valentin, along with their development company, sued the Town of Natick and its Planning Board alleging racial discrimination in denying their permit to develop a historic property into condominiums, citing racist neighborhood opposition and disparate treatment compared to a similar white-sponsored church project. The court denied in part defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs stated plausible FHA disparate treatment and interference claims under § 3604 and § 3617 based on procedural irregularities, discriminatory intent, and Board acquiescence to racist private opposition.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Vill. of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Shelley v. Kraemer, 334 U.S. 1 (1948)
Macone v. Town of Wakefield, 277 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2002)

case_name: Louis v. SafeRent Solutions, LLC
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; it challenges tenant-screening algorithmic scoring that allegedly has disparate impact on Black, Hispanic, and voucher-holding applicants.
key_holding: The court denied Metropolitan's motion to dismiss and denied SafeRent's motion to dismiss on the FHA claims, finding that SafeRent's tenant-screening services are covered under the FHA and that plaintiffs adequately pleaded a disparate impact claim based on the SafeRent Score's reliance on credit history; however, the court dismissed plaintiffs' Chapter 93A state law claims.
brief_summary: Black female housing voucher holders Louis and Douglas, along with a community action agency, sued SafeRent Solutions (a tenant-screening company) and Metropolitan Management Group (a property manager), alleging that SafeRent's credit-history-based algorithmic scoring system has a disparate impact on Black, Hispanic, and voucher-holding applicants in violation of the FHA. The court denied both motions to dismiss, finding the FHA applies to SafeRent's screening services and plaintiffs adequately pleaded disparate impact, while dismissing state consumer protection claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmt'y Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
Burbank Apartments Tenant Ass'n v. Kargman

case_name: René Flores v. First Cambridge Realty Corporation
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; case concerns refusal to rent based on race and color
key_holding: The plaintiff's motion for judgment on the pleadings was denied because the defendant denied the discriminatory allegations, presenting genuine issues of material fact that must be resolved at trial or after discovery.
brief_summary: Hispanic plaintiff René Flores sued First Cambridge Realty Corporation alleging race and color discrimination when the rental management company's administrative assistant allegedly made discriminatory statements and sent a citizenship questionnaire in December 2022; the court denied the plaintiff's partial motion for judgment on the pleadings because the defendant denied the discriminatory allegations, creating genuine issues of material fact that preclude judgment at this early stage.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Langlois v. Abington Hous. Auth., 207 F.3d 43 (1st Cir. 2000)
Burbank Apartments Tenant Ass'n v. Kargman, 474 Mass. 107 (2016)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Rivera-Gomez v. de Castro, 843 F.2d 631 (1st Cir. 1988)

case_name: David Allen Ward v. Roger Paul Williams, Jr., et al.
accommodation_description: N/A - racial discrimination claim under § 3604(b); no accommodation request involved
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), finding that plaintiff adequately stated a disparate treatment claim under § 3604(b) by alleging he was treated differently from non-African American buyers who were able to complete their home purchases in a timely manner.
brief_summary: David Ward, an African American buyer, sued property developer Roger Paul Williams, Jr. and Steelehouse, LLC alleging they denied him the right to finalize the purchase of property at 3723 Fords Lane because of his race. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6), finding plaintiff adequately alleged disparate treatment under § 3604(b) by claiming he was treated differently from non-African American buyers, but quashed service of process under Rule 12(b)(5) and gave plaintiff another opportunity to properly serve defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Pinchback v. Amistead Homes Corp., 907 F.2d 1447 (4th Cir. 1990)
Want v. Shindle Props., LLC, No. 18-cv-2833-PWG (D. Md. Oct. 29, 2018)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

source_file: 9735665_2019_National Fair Housing Alliance v. Bank of America, National Association_mdd
case_name: National Fair Housing Alliance v. Bank of America, N.A.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—case involves alleged differential maintenance of bank-owned foreclosed properties based on racial composition of neighborhoods
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs sufficiently pled standing and stated claims under the Fair Housing Act for discriminatory maintenance of real estate owned properties based on race.
brief_summary: National Fair Housing Alliance and other fair housing organizations sued Bank of America and its contractor Safeguard Properties alleging that defendants disproportionately neglected routine maintenance and marketing of foreclosed bank-owned properties in communities of color while tending more closely to properties in white neighborhoods. The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pled organizational standing, personal jurisdiction, and plausible claims under Sections 804(a), 804(b), 805, and 817 of the Fair Housing Act based on both disparate treatment and disparate impact theories.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Jersey Heights Neighborhood Ass'n v. Glendening, 174 F.3d 180 (4th Cir. 1999)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Deutsche Bank Nat'l Tr. Co. v. FDIC, 2018 WL 6045216 (D.D.C. 2018)

source_file: 9735670_2019_Prince George's County, Maryland v. Wells Fargo _ Co._mdd
case_name: Prince George's County v. Wells Fargo & Co.
race_if_mentioned: African American and other minority communities
property_city: Prince George's County and Montgomery County
accommodation_description: No individual accommodation request; counties allege institutional predatory lending practices targeting minority communities.
key_holding: The court dismissed non-economic damages claims as too attenuated from alleged discriminatory conduct, deferred ruling on municipal services costs and tax base injuries pending amended complaint with more specific causation allegations, but allowed foreclosure processing costs claims and disparate impact/treatment theories to proceed.
brief_summary: Prince George's County and Montgomery County, Maryland sued Wells Fargo alleging the bank engaged in predatory mortgage lending, servicing, and foreclosure practices that disproportionately harmed FHA-protected minority communities through an equity-stripping scheme. The Court granted-in-part, deferred-in-part, and denied-in-part Wells Fargo's motion to dismiss, dismissing non-economic damages claims but allowing the counties to amend complaints regarding municipal services costs and tax base injuries while permitting foreclosure processing costs and disparate impact/treatment claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S.Ct. 1296 (2017)
City of Miami v. Wells Fargo & Co., 923 F.3d 1260 (11th Cir. 2019)
Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project, 135 S.Ct. 2507 (2015)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. Partnership, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
County of Cook v. Wells Fargo & Co., 314 F. Supp. 3d 975 (N.D. Ill. 2018)

case_name: OT, LLC, et al. v. Harford County, Maryland, et al.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request; this case concerns alleged religious discrimination in the permitting process for a residential subdivision and community center.
key_holding: The court found genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether County Defendants' permitting decisions were motivated by discriminatory intent based on anti-Muslim sentiment, warranting denial of summary judgment on the FHA disparate treatment claims, while granting summary judgment on the judicial review claim under Burford abstention.
brief_summary: OT, LLC, Gemcraft Homes, and Shades and Springs (a non-profit associated with the Ahmadiyya Movement in Islam) sued Harford County, Maryland and county officials alleging religious and racial discrimination in the county's permitting process for a residential subdivision and community center, claiming the county succumbed to community pressure based on anti-Muslim sentiment. The court denied both parties' summary judgment motions on the FHA disparate treatment and RLUIPA claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding discriminatory intent, while granting the county's motion on the judicial review claim under Burford abstention.
key_cases_cited: Sylvia Dev. Corp. v. Calvert Cty., 48 F.3d 810 (4th Cir. 1995)
Jesus Christ Is the Answer Ministries, Inc. v. Baltimore Cty., 915 F.3d 256 (4th Cir. 2019)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Hunt Valley, 2017 WL 4801542 (D.Md.)

case_name: OT, LLC v. Harford County, Maryland
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this case involves alleged religious discrimination blocking a housing development for Muslim families rather than a reasonable accommodation claim.
key_holding: Genuine questions of material fact exist about whether state legislators conspired with county officials to halt a housing project because of the religious identity of prospective Muslim homebuyers, sufficient to deny summary judgment on Fair Housing Act, constitutional, and civil rights claims.
brief_summary: OT, LLC, Gemcraft Homes, and Shades and Springs (a non-profit for the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community) sued Harford County officials and two Maryland state delegates alleging they conspired to block the Old Trails housing development because prospective buyers were Muslim, in violation of the FHA, RLUIPA, and constitutional rights. The court denied delegates' motion for summary judgment on qualified immunity grounds, finding genuine disputes of material fact about whether legislators threatened and coerced county officials to withhold permits based on religious animus.
key_cases_cited: Bethel World Outreach Ministries v. Montgomery Cty. Council, 706 F.3d 548 (4th Cir. 2013)
Smith v. Town of Clarkton, N.C., 682 F.2d 1055 (4th Cir. 1982)
Edwards v. Johnson Cty. Health Dep't, 885 F.2d 1215 (4th Cir. 1989)
Painter's Mill Grille v. Brown, 716 F.3d 342 (4th Cir. 2013)
Scott v. Greenville County, 716 F.2d 1409 (4th Cir. 1983)

case_name: Karen Rhodes v. Parklane Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to sit on a retaining wall outside her apartment while waiting for transportation due to mobility limitations from ankle surgery.
key_holding: The court denied Fairfield's motion to dismiss the retaliation claim, finding that Rhodes adequately pleaded causation between her accommodation request and the subsequent discriminatory rent increases; dismissed the hostile environment claim for failure to allege sufficiently severe or pervasive conduct; and allowed the income discrimination claim under Montgomery County law to proceed.
brief_summary: Rhodes, a tenant with mobility disabilities, sued Fairfield alleging the landlord retaliated against her for requesting to sit on a retaining wall while awaiting transportation by dramatically increasing her rent, and discriminated based on source of income through housing vouchers. The court denied Fairfield's motion to dismiss on retaliation and income discrimination claims, finding sufficient factual allegations of causation and disparate treatment of voucher holders, but dismissed the hostile environment claim for failure to plead conduct sufficiently severe or pervasive.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
Matrarese v. Archstone Pentagon City, 795 F. Supp. 2d 402 (E.D. Va. 2011)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lettieri v. Equant Inc., 478 F.3d 640 (4th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Heaven White, et al. v. The City of Annapolis by and through the City Council, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, African American public housing residents, allege the City's policy of not enforcing inspection and licensing requirements on HACA properties constitutes intentional discrimination and disparate impact based on race.
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs sufficiently pleaded a disparate impact FHA claim by demonstrating the City's policy of exempting HACA properties from inspection and licensing requirements disproportionately impacts African American residents compared to the Annapolis population as a whole, and distinguished Edwards v. Johnson County on the grounds that the policy's harm falls on Black residents as a group compared to the general population.
brief_summary: African American residents of HACA-owned public housing in Annapolis sued the City of Annapolis and HACA alleging the City's policy of exempting HACA properties from city inspection and licensing requirements, while enforcing them on private landlords, discriminates against them on the basis of race. The court granted in part the motions to dismiss, dismissing claims against individual defendants, most state law claims, and certain federal claims, but denied dismissal as to the FHA disparate impact claim which the court found sufficiently pleaded.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. and Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Edwards v. Johnson Cnty. Health Dep't, 885 F.2d 1215 (4th Cir. 1989)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Karen Lundregan v. Housing Opportunities Commission et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with PTSD and Borderline Personality Disorder requested reinstatement of her Section 8 housing voucher that was terminated due to alleged lease violations, as a reasonable accommodation for her disabilities.
key_holding: The court found that Plaintiff plausibly stated a claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because HOC's four-month delay in reinstating her housing voucher after granting the accommodation request, during which it continued requesting excessive medical documentation, may constitute a constructive denial of reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Karen Lundregan sued the Housing Opportunities Commission of Montgomery County and individual employees, alleging the HOC violated the Fair Housing Act by constructively denying her reasonable accommodation request for reinstatement of her Section 8 voucher while delaying four months to actually reinstate it despite medical documentation of her PTSD and Borderline Personality Disorder. The Court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss on the reasonable accommodation and § 1983 procedural due process claims, finding Plaintiff plausibly alleged that the HOC asked for excessive medical information and unreasonably delayed reinstating her voucher after granting the accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 554 (2007)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)

source_file: 9737209_2020_Gibson Island Corporation v. Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC_mdd
case_name: Gibson Island Corporation v. Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC
accommodation_description: Group Home sought permission to construct and operate an assisted-living group home for seniors with disabilities at 1753 Banbury Road, requesting an exception to the Gibson Island Deed Covenants.
key_holding: The business purpose covenant is facially applicable to the for-profit group home, and the Group Home's discrimination claim is unripe because it failed to follow established procedures to request a reasonable accommodation under the Deed Covenants before proceeding with construction.
brief_summary: Gibson Island Corporation sued Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC to enforce restrictive deed covenants against construction of an assisted-living group home for seniors with disabilities. The court granted summary judgment for the Corporation, holding that the covenants are facially applicable to the for-profit group home and that the Group Home's Fair Housing Act claim is unripe because it bypassed required procedures for requesting an accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Colandrea v. Wilde Lake Cmty. Ass'n, Inc., 361 Md. 371 (2000)
City of Cleburne Living Center, Inc. v. City of Cleburne, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

source_file: 9738715_2021_Williams v. Arora Hills Homeowners Association Inc._mdd
case_name: Daborah Williams v. Arora Hills Homeowners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; claims are for discriminatory treatment based on race and familial status, retaliation, and hostile environment.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the HOA because plaintiff failed to provide sufficient evidence of discriminatory animus to support her discrimination claim, failed to demonstrate objectively material adverse action for her retaliation claim, and failed to show conduct sufficiently severe or pervasive to constitute hostile environment harassment.
brief_summary: Daborah Williams, an African American mother of eight children renting a home in Clarksburg, Maryland with a Section 8 voucher, sued Arora Hills Homeowners Association alleging discrimination based on race and familial status, retaliation for protected complaints, and hostile environment harassment. The court granted summary judgment for the HOA, finding no evidence of discriminatory animus, no objectively material adverse action for the retaliation claim, and conduct insufficiently severe or pervasive for hostile environment liability.
key_cases_cited: Betsey v. Turtle Creek Assocs., 736 F.2d 983 (4th Cir. 1984)
Burlington N. & Santa Fe Rwy. Co. v. White, 548 U.S. 53 (2006)
Rhodes v. Parklane Apartments, LLC, 2019 WL 7293398 (D.Md. Dec. 27, 2019)
Hall v. Greystar Management Services, LP., 28 F.3d 490 (D.Md. 2014)

case_name: Michael Manley, et al. v. Evette L. Threeths, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation was requested; plaintiffs alleged general harassment and landlord-tenant disputes rather than a denial of reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because plaintiffs failed to allege facts establishing a causal connection between the alleged incidents and their protected characteristics, and explicitly did not claim they were denied any reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Michael and Susan Manley sued their landlord Evette Threeths and her agent Baltimore Rent Court Agents, LLC, alleging discrimination based on race, age, disability, and religion under the FHA, ADA, Section 1981, and Rehabilitation Act, along with state law abuse of process claims. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to state a plausible FHA claim because they did not allege a causal connection between the alleged incidents and their protected characteristics, and expressly did not claim they were denied a reasonable accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Want v. Shindle Properties, LLC, 2018 WL 5392521 (D. Md. 2018)
Hardaway v. Equity Residential Servs., LLC, 2015 WL 858086 (D. Md. 2015)
Campbell v. Lake Hallowell Homeowners Ass'n, 157 Md. App. 504 (2004)

case_name: Lawrence Bailey et al. v. PHH Mortgage Corporation et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, African American homeowners, allege that mortgage servicers' policies regarding forced place insurance, loan servicing, and implementation of loan modification agreements discriminated against them based on race.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' disparate treatment claim for failure to allege they were treated differently than similarly situated borrowers of a different race, and dismissed the disparate impact claim for failure to provide statistical evidence establishing a robust causal connection between defendants' policies and disparate effects on African-American homeowners.
brief_summary: Lawrence and Deborah Bailey, African American homeowners, sued PHH Mortgage Corporation, HSBC Bank USA, and Ocwen Loan Servicing alleging racial discrimination in mortgage servicing practices, including forced place insurance policies and failure to honor loan modification agreements. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss the Fair Housing Act claims, finding plaintiffs failed to plead a prima facie case of disparate treatment (no comparison to similarly situated borrowers) and disparate impact (no statistical evidence of causation).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Nat'l Fair Hous. All. v. Bank of Am., N.A., 401 F. Supp. 3d 619 (D. Md. 2019)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Adams v. Cameron
accommodation_description: Black employee alleged that defendant employer assigned him to substandard, unsanitary employee housing while non-Black employees received better housing; no disability accommodation request was made.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the FHA race discrimination claim (Count 6) because Adams adequately alleged disparate treatment theory by claiming Black employees were restricted to substandard housing while non-Black employees received better housing conditions.
brief_summary: Damon Adams, a Black man formerly employed by Rod and Reel, Inc., sued his employer and individual managers alleging race discrimination in housing, wrongful termination for complaining about unsanitary housing conditions, and violations of state housing and employment laws. The court denied dismissal of Adams's FHA race discrimination claim under § 3604(b) based on allegations that Black employees were assigned only to substandard housing while non-Black employees received better units, but granted dismissal of the hostile work environment claim and certain state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Tex. Dep't of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Corey v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev. ex rel. Walker, 719 F.3d 322 (4th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Stemmons v. Corso
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to pay rent in cash instead of electronically via Zelle, as a reasonable accommodation for his PTSD requiring schedule consistency and limited disruption.
key_holding: The preliminary injunction motion was denied as moot because the plaintiff had voluntarily vacated the premises, rendering any eviction-related injunction request non-justiciable; additionally, the plaintiff failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits of his FHA reasonable accommodation claim regarding rent payment method.
brief_summary: Adam Stemmons, a tenant with PTSD, sued his landlord Georgia Corso alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act for failing to accommodate his disability regarding rent payment methods and notice requirements. The court denied Stemmons' motion for preliminary injunction to block evictions, finding the motion moot because Stemmons had voluntarily vacated the property in September 2021, and alternatively that Stemmons failed to demonstrate likelihood of success on the merits.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Corey v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Housing and Urban Dev. ex rel. Walker, 719 F.3d 322 (4th Cir. 2013)
Pinchback v. Armistead Homes, 907 F.2d 1447 (4th Cir. 1990)
City of Erie v. Pap's A.M., 529 U.S. 277 (2000)

source_file: 9740593_2022_Tall v. Commissioner of the Howard County Housing Authority_mdd
case_name: Hesman Tall v. Commissioner of the Howard County Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an extension of the Housing Choice Voucher term beyond the standard expiration date to allow time to repair credit damaged by identity theft before securing a lease.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because plaintiff failed to allege any facts demonstrating he has a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities, which is required to state a claim under both the ADA and FHA.
brief_summary: Hesman Tall, a Black pro se plaintiff who receives Social Security disability benefits, sued the Howard County Housing Authority seeking an extension of his Section 8 voucher term as a reasonable accommodation for disability-related circumstances caused by identity theft. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that while plaintiff submitted evidence of receiving disability benefits, he failed to allege any facts showing he has a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits major life activities as required to state a claim under the ADA or FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Roberson v. Graziano, No. 09-cv-3038-WDQ, 2010 WL 2106466 (D. Md. May 21, 2010)
Summers v. Altarum Inst., Corp., 740 F.3d 325 (4th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Jerome Want v. Saundra Jones, Wayne Jones, City of Hagerstown, MD., Michael Sabol, Salvatore & Morton
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation was requested; plaintiff alleged habitability violations, personal injury from poor conditions, and contract breach under Maryland law, with FHA cited generally without identifying any rule, policy, or practice accommodation.
key_holding: The FHA claim fails because plaintiff did not allege that defendant landlord refused to make reasonable accommodations in rules or practices, and plaintiff was not denied the opportunity to lease the apartment; the ADA claim fails because the landlord is not a public entity and the apartment is not a public accommodation.
brief_summary: Self-represented plaintiff Jerome Want sued his landlord, the city of Hagerstown, a city housing inspector, and a law firm alleging habitability violations, personal injury from a foot amputation caused by apartment conditions, and federal claims under the ADA and FHA.
