The Court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the ADA inapplicable because the landlord is not a public entity and the FHA inapplicable because plaintiff did not allege any refusal to make reasonable accommodations.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89 (2007)
Hertz Corp. v. Friend, 559 U.S. 77 (2010)

source_file: 9740801_2022_CASA de Maryland, Inc. v. Arbor Realty Trust, Inc._mdd
case_name: CASA de Maryland, Inc. v. Arbor Realty Trust, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this case challenges landlord's allegedly discriminatory maintenance and repair policies affecting Hispanic tenants.
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs adequately alleged group pleading and standing to sue the corporate defendants, but dismissed certain claims including breach of management contract (no third-party beneficiary status) and discriminatory advertising; the court permitted disparate impact and perpetuation of segregation claims to proceed past the motion to dismiss stage.
brief_summary: CASA de Maryland and seven Hispanic tenant plaintiffs sued Arbor Realty Trust and related entities alleging that defendants' financialization, harvesting, divestment, and outsourcing policies result in discriminatory maintenance and repair of multifamily housing in Langley Park, Maryland, constituting disparate impact based on national origin and perpetuation of segregation in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted in part and denied in part defendants' motions to dismiss, allowing disparate impact and perpetuation of segregation claims to proceed while dismissing breach of management contract claims for failure to state a third-party beneficiary claim.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Singer Co. v. Baltimore Gas & Elec. Co., 79 Md.App. 461 (1989)
CR-RSC Tower I, LLC v. RSC Tower I, LLC, 429 Md. 387 (2012)
Amaya v. DGS Constr., LLC, No. 16-cv-3350-TDC, 2019 WL 3945933 (D.Md. Aug. 21, 2019)

case_name: Daundra Grier v. United States Dep't of Housing and Urban Development, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; plaintiff alleges general failure to provide housing services
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because she failed to plausibly allege membership in a protected class, identify specific discriminatory conduct by defendant, or state facts supporting either a disparate treatment or disparate impact theory under 42 U.S.C. § 3604.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Daundra Grier sued the Housing Authority of Elkton, Maryland and other defendants alleging civil rights violations, Fair Housing Act claims, and other causes of action stemming from alleged failures to provide housing assistance after Hurricane Katrina. The court granted EHA's motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding that plaintiff failed to state a plausible FHA claim because 'native New Orleanian' is not a protected class under the FHA and she failed to allege specific facts demonstrating discriminatory intent or disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Beaudett v. City of Hampton, 775 F.2d 1274 (4th Cir. 1985)

case_name: Cooper v. Mr. Timmins et al.
accommodation_description: This is a retaliation case; plaintiff alleges HABC employees retaliated against her for filing a 2015 sexual harassment lawsuit and a 2018 race discrimination complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's retaliation claim because she failed to allege any factual basis that the individual HABC defendants knew of her 2015 lawsuit and failed to establish causal connection between the protected activity and the allegedly retaliatory actions occurring four years later.
brief_summary: Ms. Cooper, a tenant receiving housing assistance through the Housing Authority of Baltimore City, sued ten HABC employees in their individual capacities alleging they retaliated against her for filing prior lawsuits including a 2015 sexual harassment complaint and 2018 race discrimination complaint. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Cooper failed to allege the individual defendants knew of her protected activity and failed to establish causal connection given the four-year gap between her lawsuit and the allegedly retaliatory actions.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Greystar Mgmt. Servs., L.P., 637 F. App'x 93 (4th Cir. 2016)
Clark Cnty. Sch. Dist. v. Breeden, 532 U.S. 268 (2001)
Price v. Thompson, 380 F.3d 209 (4th Cir. 2004)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Travis R. Jones v. Blue Ocean Realty LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to rent an apartment at Oaklee Village and was denied after submitting income verification including Social Security Disability Award Letter.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to plausibly allege that Defendants knew of the nature of his impairment or that they were motivated by his disability in denying his rental application, as the documented income requirements—rather than disability status—appeared to be the basis for the denial.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Travis Jones sued Blue Ocean Realty LLC and Oaklee Village Baltimore LLC alleging Fair Housing Act and ADA claims after being denied rental of an apartment when his income verification (including Social Security Disability benefits) did not satisfy the property's income requirements. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Plaintiff failed to plausibly allege disability discrimination because he did not identify his impairment, did not allege Defendants knew of the nature of his impairment, and provided no facts suggesting disability motivated the denial rather than documented income deficiencies.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center

case_name: Emmeleen Ulita Gilbert v. Pingtao Tang
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges sexual harassment and stalking by landlord as discrimination in housing under the FHA.
key_holding: The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that FHA exemptions under § 3603(b) are affirmative defenses that defendant bears the burden of pleading and proving, and plaintiff sufficiently alleged a quiet enjoyment claim based on sexual harassment and stalking by the landlord.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Emmeleen Gilbert sued her landlord Pingtao Tang alleging sexual harassment, stalking, and inappropriate communications following her signing of a lease for a townhome in Montgomery County, Maryland. The court denied defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that FHA exemptions under § 3603(b) are affirmative defenses that need not be negated in the complaint, and that plaintiff stated plausible claims under both the FHA and Maryland's covenant of quiet enjoyment based on allegations of repeated unwanted sexual advances and surveillance.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Goodman v. Praxair, Inc.
United States v. Space Hunters, Inc.
Baugher v. Wilkins

case_name: Nathan Connolly & Shani Mott v. Shane Lanham, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; this case concerns alleged racial discrimination in a home appraisal during a refinance transaction.
key_holding: The court found that plaintiffs plausibly alleged racial discrimination by defendant Lanham in the home appraisal, and by loanDepot in failing to respond to plaintiffs' discrimination complaints, but dismissed plaintiffs' Maryland Fair Housing Act claim and dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claim to the extent it relied on 42 U.S.C. § 3604.
brief_summary: Black homeowners sued their mortgage lender and an appraiser alleging the appraiser undervalued their Baltimore home at $472,000 because of race, while a subsequent appraisal after 'whitewashing' the home yielded a $750,000 valuation. The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged intentional racial discrimination under the FHA, ECOA, and Civil Rights Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Swanson v. Citibank, N.A., 614 F.3d 400 (7th Cir. 2010)
Betsey v. Turtle Creek Assocs., 736 F.2d 983 (4th Cir. 1984)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 972 (1973)

source_file: 9742558_2023_Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc._mdd
case_name: Edward Siguel v. King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to construct storage sheds closer to his gardening area to reduce movement that causes him pain and bleeding due to his physical disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed most accommodation requests (balcony columns, brick patio, trellis) as not necessary because they lacked direct nexus to disability, but allowed the storage shed claims to proceed because Plaintiff adequately pled that locating sheds closer to his garden would reduce movement-related pain and bleeding.
brief_summary: Edward Siguel, a disabled individual with mobility limitations causing pain and bleeding with movement, sued King Farm Citizens Assembly, Inc. (a homeowners' association) and others, alleging the HOA violated the Fair Housing Act by denying his requests for modifications including storage sheds, a brick patio, trellis, and modifications to a balcony structure. On motion to dismiss, the court dismissed most claims finding the requests lacked direct nexus to his disability, but allowed the storage shed claims to proceed because Plaintiff adequately alleged the sheds would reduce his painful movement when accessing gardening tools.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphi Housing Auth. Corp.
Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard County
Gavin v. Spring Ridge Conservancy, Inc.
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash

source_file: 9742748_2023_Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corporation_mdd
case_name: Group Home on Gibson Island, LLC v. Gibson Island Corporation
accommodation_description: Request to waive restrictive covenant prohibiting 'business purpose' use to permit operation of a state-licensed assisted living group home for nine seniors with disabilities on Gibson Island
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to establish that removing the four disputed conditions in the accommodation agreement (Guarantor Provision, Dispute Resolution Provision, Septic Provision, and Arbitration Escrow Provision) was 'necessary' to provide disabled residents an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the Banbury Property under the reasonable accommodation analysis.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Group Home on Gibson Island and Assisted Living Well Compassionate Care sued Gibson Island Corporation seeking a reasonable accommodation to waive restrictive covenants prohibiting business use, so they could operate an assisted living group home for disabled seniors on the private island community. The court granted GIC's motion for summary judgment, finding plaintiffs failed to demonstrate that removing four disputed conditions from the proposed accommodation agreement was 'necessary' to provide disabled residents equal housing opportunity under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn v. Howard County, 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Vorchheimer v. Penn Center North, Inc., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Property Owners' Ass'n, Inc., 851 F. App'x 461 (5th Cir. 2021)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

source_file: 9742828_2023_Bailey v. The Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners, Inc._mdd
case_name: Rosanna Bailey v. The Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for installation of video cameras and motion detecting lights (not framed as disability accommodation)
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint because her FHA claims are barred by claim preclusion from prior state court litigation, time-barred by the two-year statute of limitations, and fail to allege sufficient facts to state plausible claims of racial discrimination or retaliation.
brief_summary: Dr. Rosanna Bailey, a Black woman with disabilities, sued her condominium association alleging race, sex, and disability discrimination and retaliation under the FHA regarding unequal maintenance, delayed door repairs, and differential treatment compared to white neighbors. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding most claims precluded by a prior state court judgment, time-barred, or failing to state plausible claims without sufficient factual allegations of discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Baltimore Neighborhoods, Inc. v. Rommel Builders, Inc.
Laurel Sand & Gravel, Inc. v. Wilson

source_file: 9742964_2024_Affinity Recovery Center LLC v. Town Commissioners of Sudlersville_mdd
case_name: Affinity Recovery Center LLC v. Town Commissioners of Sudlersville
accommodation_description: Affinity Recovery Center, a substance abuse treatment facility operator, requested a special exception to the Town of Sudlersville's zoning permit to operate a 16-bed residential treatment facility, which would exceed the zoning code's 8-resident limit for group homes.
key_holding: The court dismissed Affinity's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because the complaint failed to allege facts showing that a 16-bed facility was necessary to afford disabled persons equal housing opportunity—the only proffered reason was economic non-viability, not a direct linkage to disability needs.
brief_summary: Affinity Recovery Center LLC sued the Town Commissioners of Sudlersville, Maryland, alleging that the town's denial of a special exception to operate a 16-bed residential substance abuse treatment facility violated the ADA and FHA. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that the zoning code's 8-resident limitation applied equally to all group homes and did not facially discriminate against substance abuse treatment facilities, and that Affinity failed to plausibly allege a reasonable accommodation claim because economic non-viability does not establish that a 16-bed facility is necessary to afford disabled persons equal housing opportunity.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 9742978_2024_CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor and City Council Of Baltimore_mdd
case_name: CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor and City Council of Baltimore
accommodation_description: Permit approval to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility for persons with disabilities, framed as a reasonable accommodation from zoning requirements rather than an individual tenant accommodation
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the City on the estoppel claim because CMDS failed to establish reliance on the zoning verification letter and building permit, dismissed the judicial review claim under Burford abstention, and denied both parties' motions on the discrimination claims because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the City denied the permit based on discriminatory animus toward persons with substance use disorders.
brief_summary: CMDS Residential, LLC sued Baltimore City after the City denied its permit to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility, claiming the denial violated the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, Fair Housing Act, and constitutional protections for persons with disabilities. The court granted summary judgment for the City on the estoppel claim (finding CMDS failed to prove reliance), dismissed the state-law judicial review claim under Burford abstention, but allowed the federal disability discrimination and equal protection claims to proceed because genuine factual disputes exist regarding whether community opposition was based on discriminatory animus toward the facility's future patients.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, Inc., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
New Orleans Pub. Serv., Inc. v. Council of City of New Orleans, 491 U.S. 350 (1989)
Permanent Financial Corp. v. Montgomery County, 308 Md. 239 (1986)

source_file: 9743203_2024_CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor and City Council Of Baltimore_mdd
case_name: CMDS Residential, LLC v. Mayor and City Council of Baltimore
accommodation_description: CMDS sought to operate a residential substance-abuse treatment facility at 6040 Harford Road, which was denied when the city required City Council approval after community opposition.
key_holding: The court denied the City's motion for reconsideration, adhering to its prior ruling that the 'motivating factor' causation standard applies to ADA Title II and FHA claims, and finding that Gentry did not abrogate Baird's controlling precedent.
brief_summary: CMDS Residential, LLC sued the City of Baltimore after the city denied its application for a use and occupancy permit to open a residential substance-abuse treatment facility, claiming the denial was motivated by discriminatory community opposition based on stereotypes about people with substance use disorders. The court denied the City's motion for reconsideration, adhering to its prior ruling that the 'motivating factor' causation standard governs ADA Title II and FHA claims and that Gentry v. E.W. Partners Club Management Co. did not abrogate Baird's controlling precedent establishing that standard.
key_cases_cited: Baird ex rel. Baird v. Rose, 192 F.3d 462 (4th Cir. 1999)
Gentry v. E.W. Partners Club Mgmt. Co., 816 F.3d 228 (4th Cir. 2016)
Wicomico Nursing Home v. Padilla, 910 F.3d 739 (4th Cir. 2018)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
Neal v. East Carolina University, 53 F.4th 130 (4th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Karen Lundregan v. Housing Opportunities Commission
accommodation_description: Request for reinstatement of Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher as reasonable accommodation for mental health disabilities (PTSD, Borderline Personality Disorder) following alleged improper termination.
key_holding: The court found genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether HOC violated the FHA by improperly determining, without objective evidence and based only on unverified allegations from a biased former landlord, that plaintiff posed a direct threat, and by attaching unduly invasive conditions to the accommodation approval, thereby delaying restoration of her voucher for several months.
brief_summary: Karen Lundregan, a disabled tenant with PTSD and Borderline Personality Disorder, sued the Housing Opportunities Commission of Montgomery County and employee Ethan Cohen alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by improperly delaying or denying her reasonable accommodation request to reinstate her Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher for approximately five months (April-September 2018) without sufficient evidence and without providing her a hearing. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact remain as to whether defendants relied improperly on unverified allegations from a biased landlord without conducting an individualized assessment before concluding plaintiff posed a direct threat, and whether the delay in reinstating her voucher constituted constructive denial of her accommodation request.
key_cases_cited: Corey v. Secretary, HUD ex rel. Walker, 719 F.3d 322 (4th Cir. 2013)
Department of Housing and Urban Development and the DOJ, Reasonable Accommodations Under the Fair Housing Act (2004)

case_name: Morris v. Memorial Development Partners, LP
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs, elderly/disabled tenants, alleged various discriminatory housing practices but did not request a specific reasonable accommodation; court analyzed claims under discriminatory intent framework rather than accommodation analysis.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because they failed to plausibly allege discriminatory intent or disparate impact, instead presenting only conclusory complaints about housing conditions, differential amenities, and business practices without tying them to discrimination based on a protected class.
brief_summary: Deborah Morris and Arnell Mason, pro se elderly/disabled tenants at Linden Park Apartments in Baltimore, sued their landlord and property management company alleging Fair Housing Act violations, ADA claims, and RICO claims based on various housing conditions and business practices. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss with prejudice, finding plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege any federal cause of action because their conclusory allegations did not demonstrate discriminatory intent or disparate impact based on a protected class.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 554 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
Jones v. Blue Ocean Realty LLC, 2023 WL 3074642 (D. Md. Apr. 25, 2023)
Hardaway v. Equity Residential Mgmt., LLC, 2016 WL 3957648 (D. Md. July 22, 2016)

case_name: Travis R. Jones v. Blue Ocean Realty, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's Amended Complaint references failure to provide reasonable accommodations in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(1), but no specific accommodation request is clearly identified in the opinion; the complaint mentions Plaintiff requested a walk-through inspection given his disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA housing discrimination claims were dismissed as time-barred because they were filed nearly eleven months after the two-year statute of limitations expired, and neither equitable tolling based on COVID-19 nor the continuing violation doctrine applied to excuse the late filing.
brief_summary: Travis R. Jones, a physically disabled individual, sued Blue Ocean Realty, LLC and Oaklee Village Baltimore, LLC alleging they refused to rent him an apartment and failed to provide reasonable accommodations in violation of the FHA. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the action was time-barred because it was filed eleven months after the two-year statute of limitations expired, with no basis for equitable tolling or the continuing violation doctrine.
key_cases_cited: Rouse v. Lee, 339 F.3d 238 (4th Cir. 2003)
Cruz v. Maypa, 773 F.3d 138 (4th Cir. 2014)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Bing Chen, et al. v. Commodore Management Company, Inc., et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; case involves claims of racial discrimination by Chinese immigrant tenants against their landlord.
key_holding: Plaintiffs failed to plausibly allege that Defendants discriminated against them on the basis of race or national origin under disparate-treatment or disparate-impact theories, and failed to state a plausible FHA retaliation claim due to insufficient allegations regarding protected activity, knowledge, and causal connection.
brief_summary: Chinese immigrant tenants sued their private landlord alleging racial discrimination in housing under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted the landlord's motion to dismiss, finding the plaintiffs failed to allege sufficient facts to support claims of disparate treatment, disparate impact, or retaliation under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Romane Canty-Massey v. Vantage Management, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested transfer to a different apartment unit and ultimately to a subsidized apartment due to her disabilities including cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and anxiety disorder.
key_holding: Vantage satisfied its reasonable accommodation obligation by offering comparable units at comparable prices; the FHA does not require landlords to accommodate changes in a tenant's financial status or roommate situation.
brief_summary: Romane Canty-Massey, an African American woman with disabilities, sued Vantage Management Inc. alleging race, sex, and disability discrimination under the FHA based on Vantage's failure to address neighbor harassment, denial of rental office access, and refusal to accommodate her disability-related transfer request. The court granted Vantage's motion to dismiss the disability accommodation and retaliation claims but denied it as to the race and sex discrimination claims, finding plausible allegations that Vantage treated white tenants more favorably.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: House of Providence v. Meyers
accommodation_description: Defendants allegedly interfered with plaintiffs' efforts to operate a foster care home serving African-American children with disabilities in a predominantly white neighborhood through a campaign of harassment, false complaints, and racially-motivated opposition to zoning and licensing.
key_holding: The court dismissed most claims including § 1981, § 1983, ADA, defamation, IIED, and state tort claims, but allowed to proceed the § 1982 race-based property rights claim, § 1985(3) civil conspiracy claim based on racial animus, and the FHA interference claim under § 3617.
brief_summary: African-American foster care home operators and their children sued neighboring property owners, alleging a coordinated three-year campaign of racial harassment, false complaints to government agencies, and stalking to drive them out of a predominantly white rural community. The court dismissed most claims at the pleading stage but allowed claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1982, § 1985(3), and the Fair Housing Act to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Domino's Pizza, Inc. v. McDonald, 546 U.S. 470 (2006)
Snyder v. Phelps, 562 U.S. 443 (2011)

case_name: Larry Lee v. Washtenaw County et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested placement in a shelter that would either allow separation from men or have a security system (on-site employee or camera), due to recent sexual assault and mental health conditions.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim fails because CMH is a mental health agency that assists clients in accessing housing rather than a housing provider itself, and the FHA applies only to providers of housing.
brief_summary: Larry Lee, a transgender individual with mental health conditions including PTSD and depression, sued Washtenaw County, its Community Mental Health agency (CMH), and two social workers after being directed to sleep in a parking garage and subsequently sexually assaulted. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the Fair Housing Act claim, finding that CMH is a mental health agency that assists clients in accessing housing rather than a housing provider to whom the FHA's reasonable accommodation requirements apply.

case_name: Douglas J. Johnson v. East Tawas Housing Commission
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendant adjust or replace heavy apartment and stairwell doors that exacerbated his disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state an FHA claim because he did not identify any physical or mental impairment that substantially limits major life activities, and his complaints about apartment maintenance are not cognizable federal claims.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Douglas Johnson, a Section 8 tenant at Bay Park Tower North, sued East Tawas Housing Commission alleging discrimination and failure to accommodate his disability by not fixing heavy doors and maintaining the garbage chute. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to identify any qualifying disability under the FHA, ADA, or Rehabilitation Act, and complaints about apartment maintenance are not individually enforceable federal rights.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Fulakis v. Columbus Pub. Sch., 53 F. App'x 340 (6th Cir. 2002)
Johnson v. City of Detroit, 446 F.3d 614 (6th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Geoffrey Leonard, et al. v. Nokel LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: This is a race discrimination case, not a reasonable accommodation case; plaintiffs allege defendants discriminated against Black tenants and prospective tenants by refusing to rent to them and providing unequal repair services.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state plausible claims for intentional race discrimination, disparate impact, or retaliation under the FHA because plaintiffs' allegations are conclusory and unsupported by specific factual allegations demonstrating how defendants' actions were motivated by race.
brief_summary: Non-Black tenants (Leonard, Misumi, Messer) sued their landlords (Nokel LLC and individuals) alleging race discrimination against Black tenants and prospective tenants, claiming defendants refused to rent to qualified Black applicants and provided unequal repair services to force Black tenants to move out. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to allege sufficient facts to plausibly support claims for intentional race discrimination, disparate impact, or retaliation under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Hamad v. Woodcrest Condo. Ass'n, 328 F.3d 224 (6th Cir. 2003)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Rhonda Henderson, et al. v. Vision Property Management, LLP, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege they were targeted for a predatory lease-with-option-to-purchase scheme because of their race, not a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The court denied defendants Atalaya and ACM's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pled Fair Housing Act claims under § 3604(a) and § 3605 based on allegations that defendants funded and participated in a predatory home purchase program that intentionally targeted Black homebuyers.
brief_summary: Black homebuyers sued Vision Property Management, Atalaya Capital Management, and ACM Vision V, alleging defendants funded and designed a predatory lease-with-option-to-purchase program that intentionally targeted Black communities in Southeast Michigan in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged claims under § 3604(a) and § 3605 based on allegations that defendants acted as funders and real estate investors who knowingly participated in discriminatory housing practices.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Michigan Protection and Advocacy Service, Inc. v. Babin, 18 F.3d 337 (6th Cir. 1994)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Williams v. Huron Pines Condominium Association
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; the case involves an HOA's enforcement of assessment collection, lien filing, and service maintenance against an African American unit owner.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff plausibly alleged racial discrimination under the FHA's disparate treatment theory because he adequately pleaded that he was treated differently from similarly situated white unit owners who fell behind on payments.
brief_summary: Alf Williams, the only African American resident in the Huron Pines Condominium community, sued the Association and its Board of Directors alleging they discriminated against him on the basis of race by filing a lawsuit for unpaid assessments, placing a lien on his unit, and refusing to perform storm water maintenance and septic tank cleaning while white owners who fell behind were not similarly treated. The Court denied the motion to dismiss the federal claims, finding Williams plausibly pleaded disparate treatment under the FHA, but dismissed two state breach of contract claims while allowing fiduciary duty and membership oppression claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Assoc. of Afr. Am.-Owned Media, 140 S. Ct. 1009 (2020)

case_name: Thornton v. Caramagno
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges that defendants refused to sell her a property and sold it to another buyer for $25,000 less after learning she is African American.
key_holding: The court found sufficient factual allegations to state a plausible claim of racial discrimination under the FHA, noting that defendants reversed the sale after learning plaintiff's race and sold the property for $25,000 less than plaintiff's offer, permitting a reasonable inference of racial animus.
brief_summary: Kiara Thornton, an African American prospective purchaser, sued Sam Caramagno, Susan Gottlieb, and Gottlieb Group Real Estate alleging they refused to sell her a property after learning her race and instead sold it to another buyer for $25,000 less than her offer, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the FHA and related race discrimination claims, finding Thornton's factual allegations sufficient to plausibly infer racial discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Lindsay v. Yates, 498 F.3d 434 (6th Cir. 2007)
Domino's Pizza, Inc. v. McDonald, 546 U.S. 470 (2006)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of Afr. Am.-Owned Media, 140 S. Ct. 1009 (2020)

case_name: Christopher Whiteaker v. City of Southgate
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of City Ordinance 610.13 prohibiting chickens to maintain six emotional support animals as a reasonable accommodation for Major Depressive Disorder and General Anxiety Disorder.
key_holding: Genuine issues of material fact remain as to whether Whiteaker's requested accommodation of keeping six ESA chickens is both reasonable (balancing City burden against Whiteaker's therapeutic benefit) and necessary to afford him equal opportunity in housing use and enjoyment.
brief_summary: Whiteaker sued the City of Southgate, Michigan under the FHA and PDCRA, alleging the City denied his requests for a waiver of Ordinance 610.13 (prohibiting chickens) to keep six emotional support animals for his depression and anxiety. The court denied the City's motion for summary judgment, finding triable issues of fact on both the reasonableness and necessity elements of the reasonable accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Smith & Lee Assocs., Inc. v. City of Taylor, Mich., 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)

case_name: Megan Czerwinski v. Sharon Gallagher
accommodation_description: Landlord stated she was 'looking for tenants with no children' and the property was an 'empty nesters property only,' indicating familial status discrimination in violation of § 3604(c)
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for plaintiff because Gallagher's emails stating she was 'looking for tenants with no children' and the property was an 'empty nesters property only' constitute discriminatory statements indicating familial status preference in violation of § 3604(c), and the § 3603(b)(1) exemption does not apply to § 3604(c) claims by its own terms.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Megan Czerwinski sued defendant Sharon Gallagher, a private landlord, for discriminatory advertising in violation of the Fair Housing Act after Gallagher stated she was 'looking for tenants with no children' and that her rental property was an 'empty nesters property only.' The court granted plaintiff's motion for summary judgment, holding that Gallagher's statements violated § 3604(c) and that the § 3603(b)(1) exemption for single-family home rentals does not shield landlords from liability under § 3604(c).
key_cases_cited: Campbell v. Robb, 162 F. App'x 460 (6th Cir. 2006)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Connor Grp., 725 F.3d 571 (6th Cir. 2013)
United States v. Hunter, 459 F.2d 205 (4th Cir. 1972)
Rodriguez v. Vill. Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)
Fair Hous. Advocs. Ass'n, Inc. v. City of Richmond Heights, Ohio, 209 F.3d 626 (6th Cir. 2000)

case_name: Bruce Kaatz v. Kingsbrook M.H.C., LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to continue double-parking across his two reserved parking spaces to accommodate his mobility disability, as parking in a standard space at the end of the street would require walking distances beyond his physical capability.
key_holding: The court found plaintiff demonstrated a strong likelihood of success on his reasonable accommodation claim, noting that regulations explicitly provide that refusing to reserve a parking space for a mobility-impaired tenant violates § 3604(f), and here the accommodation required only inaction from defendants at no cost while plaintiff continued paying for his reserved spaces.
brief_summary: Bruce Kaatz, a mobility-disabled mobile home park tenant, sued Kingsbrook M.H.C., LLC after the park prohibited him from double-parking across his two reserved parking spaces and threatened eviction. The court granted in part Kaatz's motion for a temporary restraining order, finding he demonstrated a strong likelihood of success on his fair housing reasonable accommodation claim, as the park's refusal to permit his parking arrangement forced him to choose between additional pain/danger from walking further or risking eviction.
key_cases_cited: Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condos., 833 F. App'x 623 (6th Cir. 2020)
Chapp v. Bowman, 750 F. Supp. 274 (W.D. Mich. 1990)

source_file: 9753901_2023_Mercer v. Edward Rose _ Sons, Inc. d_b_a The Crossings Apartments_mied
case_name: Mercer v. Edward Rose & Sons, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for exception to income qualification requirements for two tenants with cognitive impairments, including willingness to co-sign, provide larger security deposit, or pre-pay rent
key_holding: The requested accommodation was not necessary because defendants proposed an adequate alternative (leasing a less expensive unit) that would provide equal housing opportunity, and any income insufficiency was not directly linked to the disability since one tenant's temporary unemployment was not disability-related.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs, two men with cognitive impairments and their parents, sued Edward Rose & Sons alleging the apartment complex denied their lease application in violation of the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate their disability-related income limitations. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding the accommodation was not necessary because defendants offered an adequate alternative (a less expensive unit) and the income shortfall was not directly caused by disability.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Madej v. Maiden, 951 F.3d 364 (6th Cir. 2020)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Phillips v. Acacia on the Green Condo. Ass'n, No. 20-4182 (6th Cir. May 26, 2022)
Dayton Veterans Residences Ltd. P'ship v. Dayton Metro. Housing Auth., No. 21-3090 (6th Cir. Nov. 19, 2021)

source_file: 9754808_2023_Lyman v. Montclair at Partridge Creek, LLC_mied
case_name: Lamar Lyman et al. v. Montclair at Partridge Creek, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request—case involves refusal to rent based on felony conviction policy, claimed as race discrimination via disparate impact and disparate treatment theories.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs adequately pleaded a disparate-impact claim by providing local (state and county-level) statistics showing that Defendants' blanket policy excluding applicants with felony convictions disproportionately impacts African Americans, and adequately pleaded a disparate-treatment claim based on Defendants' alleged departure from normal procedures and awareness of the policy's disparate impact.
brief_summary: Lamar Lyman (an African American man) and the Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit sued Montclair at Partridge Creek, LLC and its management company, alleging that Defendants' blanket policy rejecting all applicants with felony convictions violates the FHA by having a disparate impact on African Americans and constituting intentional discrimination. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs' allegations with local statistical evidence sufficiently pleaded both disparate impact and disparate treatment claims under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Graoch Assocs. # 33 v. Louisville/Jefferson Cnty., 508 F.3d 366 (6th Cir. 2007)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Emily Scoon v. Ritchie Enterprises, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to keep two assistance animals (dogs) to assist with hearing impairment and diabetes as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's disability accommodation claim under the FHA because she failed to establish she is disabled under the Act and failed to prove she made a proper accommodation request, as Defendant Ritchie was never informed the dogs were service animals.
brief_summary: Emily Scoon sued Ritchie Enterprises, LLC and Donald Ritchie, alleging they refused to rent her an apartment because of her Native American race and national origin, called her racial slurs, and denied her reasonable accommodation to keep two assistance animals. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the disability accommodation claims (FHA Count III, PWDRA Counts VI and VII) finding she failed to establish disability and never made a proper accommodation request, but denied summary judgment on the race and national origin discrimination claims finding triable issues of fact exist.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Riverbrook v. Fabode, No. 349065, 2020 Mich. App. LEXIS 6216
Toma v. 38th District Court, 2019 WL 1897157 (E.D. Mich. 2019)
Smith v. Henderson, 376 F.3d 529 (6th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 9755167_2023_Fair Housing Center for Metropolitan Detroit v. Singh Senior Living, LLC_mied
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit v. Singh Senior Living LLC
accommodation_description: Fair Housing Center testers requested that Waltonwood senior living facilities provide American Sign Language interpreters for prospective deaf residents to ensure effective communication.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Center failed to establish a genuine issue of material fact regarding the necessity of ASL interpreters because testers made only general inquiries about interpreter availability without articulating specific circumstances requiring interpretation or explaining why alternative auxiliary aids would be insufficient.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit sued Singh Senior Living LLC and related Waltonwood senior living facilities, alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to provide ASL interpreters for prospective deaf residents. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding the testers' general inquiries about interpreter availability failed to establish that ASL interpretation was necessary rather than merely preferable, and that the facilities' offered alternatives such as communication boards, written notes, and residents who sign were never meaningfully tested or rejected.
key_cases_cited: Groner v. Golden Gate Gardens Apartments, 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

source_file: 9755170_2023_Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit v. American House Grosse Pointe, LLC_mied
case_name: Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit v. American House Senior Living, LLC
accommodation_description: Request for American Sign Language (ASL) interpreters to be provided to prospective deaf residents and their family members to ensure effective communication during housing inquiries and services.
key_holding: The Fair Housing Center failed to establish a genuine issue of material fact on its reasonable accommodation claim because testers did not develop facts showing that an ASL interpreter was necessary to afford deaf individuals an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the housing, and the plaintiff bears the burden of proving both reasonableness and necessity.
brief_summary: The Fair Housing Center of Metropolitan Detroit sued American House Senior Living, a senior living facility, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act for refusing to provide American Sign Language interpreters to prospective deaf residents and their family members. The court granted summary judgment for the defendant, finding that the plaintiff organization failed to establish that an ASL interpreter was a necessary accommodation because testers did not develop sufficient facts about why such an accommodation was required to afford deaf individuals an equal opportunity to access housing.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Groner v. Golden Gate Gardens Apartments, 250 F.3d 1039 (6th Cir. 2001)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Southwest Fair Hous. Council v. WG Chandler Villas SH LLC, 2021 WL 1087200 (D. Ariz. 2021)

case_name: David Roy Worthy v. Community Management Associates, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discrimination based on past criminal record in denial of Section 8 housing voucher application
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint because criminal history is not a protected status under the Fair Housing Act and federal regulations expressly authorize denial of Section 8 benefits based on certain criminal activity.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff David Roy Worthy sued Community Management Associates, Inc., a Section 8 housing agency, alleging the agency violated the Fair Housing Act by denying his housing choice voucher application based on his past criminal record. The court dismissed the complaint with prejudice under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim, holding that criminal history is not a protected class under the FHA and federal regulations expressly permit housing authorities to deny admission based on certain criminal activity.

source_file: 9755770_2024_Johnson Chambers v. Michigan First Credit Union_mied
case_name: Yolanda Johnson Chambers v. Michigan First Credit Union
accommodation_description: Chambers alleged defendants violated 42 U.S.C. § 3604 in connection with foreclosure of her home, but no specific accommodation request is described in the opinion.
key_holding: Chambers' FHA claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604 is time-barred because the mortgage was acquired in 2011 and the claim was brought in 2023, exceeding the FHA's two-year statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Yolanda Johnson Chambers, individually and as personal representative of her deceased husband's estate, sued Michigan First Credit Union and other defendants alleging that foreclosure of her home violated the Fair Housing Act and other federal and state laws. The court dismissed her federal claims, including the FHA claim as time-barred because the mortgage was acquired in 2011 and the 2023 filing exceeded the two-year statute of limitations, and remanded state law claims to Wayne County Circuit Court.

case_name: Brower v. Roose
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Brower requested permission to obtain a dog as an emotional support animal and to reclassify his existing cat as an ESA, both denied based on a two-animal per unit policy.
key_holding: The court found that plaintiff Brower failed to demonstrate his ESA request was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, as his physician's letter did not establish that his anxiety caused significant functional limitations or that the ESA would mitigate them.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Malcom Brower and Mariah Godfrey sued Charter Oaks Apartments and its property management company, alleging failure to accommodate requests for emotional support animals and retaliatory lease nonrenewal under the FHA and PWDCRA. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction, finding they failed to demonstrate a likelihood of success on the merits because Brower's ESA request was not necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, and he failed to establish causation for his retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir.2001)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir.2014)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir.2002)
Madej v. Maiden, 951 F.3d 364 (6th Cir.2020)
Linkletter v. W. & S. Fin. Grp., Inc., 851 F.3d 632 (6th Cir.2017)

source_file: 9756542_2020_Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condominiums, Units 73-123, 125-146, Inc._miwd
case_name: Kooman v. Boulder Bluff Condominiums, Units 73-123, 125-146, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for installation of railings on porch and steps to assist disabled resident Robert Romig with mobility/balance issues
key_holding: The two-month period between the initial request and ultimate approval was not unreasonable because the Board received no medical documentation or disability-related information in the initial request, and once provided with complete information including a doctor's note, the Board promptly approved the modification.
brief_summary: Bobbie Jo Kooman, as personal representative for her disabled father Robert Romig, sued Boulder Bluff Condominium Association and its property manager alleging the Board's two-month delay in approving railings for Mr. Romig's mobility assistance violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding the Board acted reasonably by requesting complete information and promptly approved the modification once medical documentation was provided, and that no evidence of intentional disability discrimination existed.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Moody v. Gongloff, 687 F. App'x 496 (6th Cir. 2017)
Lindsay v. Yates, 578 F.3d 407 (6th Cir. 2009)
Michigan Prot. & Advocacy Serv., Inc. v. Babin, 18 F.3d 337 (6th Cir. 1994)

source_file: 9757208_2020_Wallace v. Greystone at the Highlands Association_miwd
case_name: Wallace v. Greystone at the Highlands Association
accommodation_description: Request that the condominium association designate a disputed strip of pavement as a limited common element for the Wallaces' exclusive use so Sandra Wallace, who uses a wheelchair due to vascular dementia and mobility impairments, can safely embark and disembark vehicles.
key_holding: The Greystone Defendants' Rule 12(b)(1) motion to dismiss was denied because their arguments about whether the accommodation was 'necessary' go to the merits of the FHAA claim rather than subject matter jurisdiction, while the Parkers' motion was granted because plaintiffs failed to allege discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: William and Sandra Wallace sued their neighbors (the Parkers) and their condominium association (Greystone Defendants) alleging Fair Housing Act violations after the association denied their request to designate a disputed pavement area as a limited common element for exclusive use, claiming the accommodation was necessary for Sandra Wallace's wheelchair access. The court granted the Parkers' motion to dismiss for failure to allege discriminatory intent, but denied the Greystone Defendants' motion to dismiss the FHAA claim, holding that their arguments about the accommodation's necessity go to the merits rather than subject matter jurisdiction.
key_cases_cited: Smith & Lee Assocs. v. City of Taylor, Mich., 102 F.3d 781 (6th Cir. 1996)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9760044_2018_Crossroads Residents Organized for Stable and Secure ResiDencieS (CROSSRDS) v. MSP Crossroads Apartments LLC_mnd
case_name: Soderstrom v. MSP Crossroads Apartments LLC
accommodation_description: Class action challenging tenant screening criteria that allegedly discriminated against protected classes including persons with disabilities, though no specific individual accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The court approved a class action settlement where defendants agreed to modify tenant screening criteria and pay $650,000, finding the settlement fair, reasonable, and adequate under Rule 23(e) given the strength of plaintiffs' claims, the substantial relief provided, the minimal opposition from class members, and the complexity and expense of further litigation.
brief_summary: Individual tenants and HOME Line sued MSP Crossroads Apartments and its property management company alleging that defendants' tenant screening criteria violated the Fair Housing Act by having discriminatory effects on protected classes including non-white applicants, persons with disabilities, and families with children. The court granted final approval of a $650,000 class settlement that included modifications to screening criteria, Fair Housing Act training for leasing agents, and payments to class members.
key_cases_cited: In re Wireless Tel. Fed. Cost Recovery Fees Litig., 396 F.3d 922 (8th Cir. 2005)
Petrovic v. Amoco Oil Co., 200 F.3d 1140 (8th Cir. 1999)
Caligiuri v. Symantec Corp., 855 F.3d 860 (8th Cir. 2017)
Boeing Co. v. Van Gemert, 444 U.S. 472 (1980)

source_file: 9761270_2019_Awnuh v. Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul_mnd
case_name: Abdi Awnuh v. Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged the PHA discriminated against him because he is Somali by failing to provide adequate language interpretation services and by not providing a pre-termination hearing based on his communications during the ten-day request period
key_holding: Plaintiff is unlikely to succeed on the merits because the PHA followed its Limited English Proficiency plan, provided interpreter inserts with termination notices, and plaintiff did not request language assistance or formally request a hearing despite receiving adequate notice in English with an interpreter insert; the PHA's conduct was consistent with federal regulations and executive order requirements for language access.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Abdi Awnuh, a Somali immigrant with limited English proficiency, sued the Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul seeking to reinstate his terminated Section 8 housing voucher benefits, alleging the PHA violated the Fair Housing Act by discriminating against him based on national origin through inadequate language interpretation services and violated his Fourteenth Amendment due process rights by terminating benefits without proper notice or pre-termination hearing opportunity. The court denied his motion for preliminary injunction, finding he is unlikely to succeed on the merits because the PHA complied with its Limited English Proficiency plan and due process requirements, and plaintiff failed to request language assistance or formally request a hearing despite receiving adequate notice.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Darst-Webbe Tenant Ass'n Bd. v. St. Louis Hous. Auth., 417 F.3d 898 (8th Cir. 2005)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
Dataphase Sys., Inc. v. C L Sys., Inc., 640 F.2d 109 (8th Cir. 1981)

case_name: Brown v. Pfeiffer
accommodation_description: This case involves sexual harassment claims, not a disability accommodation request; plaintiffs allege landlord Pfeiffer engaged in quid pro quo and hostile environment sexual harassment.
key_holding: The FHA does not impose individual liability on a property owner like Fruen absent piercing the corporate veil, and Plaintiffs failed to allege facts showing Fruen had the power or legal responsibility to control Pfeiffer's discriminatory conduct to hold him directly liable under 24 C.F.R. § 100.7(a)(1)(iii).
brief_summary: Three tenants sued their landlord Reese Pfeiffer and property owners Michael Fruen and related entities alleging sexual harassment, quid pro quo demands, and retaliation under the FHA and MHRA. The Court granted Fruen's motion to dismiss the claims against him individually, holding that the FHA does not impose individual liability on property owners absent piercing the corporate veil, and that Plaintiffs failed to state a plausible claim for Fruen's direct liability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Meyer v. Holley
United States v. Hurt
Quigley v. Winter

source_file: 9761990_2020_Awnuh v. Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul_mnd
case_name: Abdi Awnuh v. Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a Somali immigrant with limited English proficiency, sought meaningful language access services including interpreter assistance and oral communication in his native language as an accommodation to the PHA's written communication policies.
key_holding: Summary judgment was granted because plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie disparate-impact claim—he provided no evidence that the PHA's language access policies had a discriminatory effect on LEP individuals beyond his own experience, and the accommodation claim was procedurally improper since plaintiff raised it for the first time at summary judgment.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Abdi Awnuh, a Somali immigrant with limited English proficiency, sued the Public Housing Agency of the City of Saint Paul seeking to challenge the termination of his Section 8 rental assistance, arguing that the PHA's language access policies (requiring LEP individuals to request interpreters and using written communications with language-block inserts) violated the FHA's disparate-impact prohibition and constitutional due process. The court granted summary judgment for the PHA because plaintiff failed to establish that the facially neutral policies caused a discriminatory impact on LEP individuals, and his reasonable accommodation claim was procedurally improper as it was not pled in the complaint.
key_cases_cited: Rodgers v. City of Des Moines, 435 F.3d 904 (8th Cir. 2006)
Oti Kaga, Inc. v. S.D. Hous. Dev. Auth., 342 F.3d 871 (8th Cir. 2003)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976)

source_file: 9762584_2021_One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka_mnd
case_name: One Love Housing, LLC v. City of Anoka
accommodation_description: Request to house seven residents in a sober house instead of the four unrelated persons permitted under City Code for single-family dwellings, to provide therapeutic support environment for recovering individuals.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for One Love because it met its burden to show the requested accommodation (increasing residents from four to seven) was both reasonable and necessary to afford recovering persons equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, and the City failed to meet its burden to demonstrate undue financial or administrative burden or fundamental alteration of its zoning scheme.
brief_summary: One Love Housing, LLC, operator of a sober house at 328 Washington Street, sued the City of Anoka after the City Council denied its reasonable accommodation request to increase occupancy from four to seven residents. The court granted summary judgment for One Love, finding the accommodation was reasonable (no cost to implement, proportional to the dwelling's capacity) and necessary (required for therapeutic support and financial viability), while the City improperly relied on property value concerns and community opposition rather than demonstrating undue burden or fundamental alteration.
key_cases_cited: Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 77 F.3d 249 (8th Cir. 1996)

case_name: Downs v. Red Brick Management, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied housing because she is a Black woman when defendants filed a police report and declined to renew her lease.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim regarding the police report as unrelated to housing terms, conditions, or privileges, but allowed the lease non-renewal claim to proceed because the plaintiff adequately alleged her race was a factor in defendants' decision.
brief_summary: Kim Downs, a Black woman, sued Red Brick Management LLC and property manager Angie Hickey alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act and her Fourth Amendment rights by filing a police report claiming she stole property and by not renewing her lease because of her race. The court granted the motion to dismiss her § 1983 claim and the FHA claim related to the police report, but denied dismissal of the FHA claim regarding lease non-renewal, finding she stated plausible facts that race may have motivated the decision.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Cavalieri-Conway v. L. Butterman & Assocs., 992 F. Supp. 995 (N.D. Ill. 1998)

case_name: Burshteyn v. Community Housing Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged disability discrimination and failure to accommodate under the FHA, though no specific accommodation request was clearly identified in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed Count IV's FHA disability discrimination claim because plaintiffs failed to adequately plead the required elements, including sufficient factual allegations regarding the nature of their disabilities, how defendants discriminated against them, and causation.
brief_summary: Yefim Burshteyn and Faina German sued Community Housing Association and its management company alleging disability discrimination, national origin discrimination, retaliation, and various state law claims arising from their tenancy at Covenant House Apartments in St. Louis. The court granted defendants' partial motion to dismiss, dismissing most claims including the FHA disability discrimination and retaliation claims for failure to state plausible claims, while allowing the habitability and punitive damages claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Althea J. Stroud v. Farr Rentals, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleges discrimination in housing services and retaliation for complaining about property conditions based on her status as a disabled African American.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because she failed to allege facts permitting the inference that she was actually treated differently due to her protected status, and failed to state a plausible retaliation claim due to lack of factual support for causal connection between her complaint and lease termination.
brief_summary: Althea J. Stroud, an African American woman claiming disability, sued Farr Rentals, LLC and others alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination and retaliation related to her tenancy at 207 North 6th Street in Canton, Missouri. The court granted her in forma pauperis motion but dismissed her original complaint for failing to state plausible claims, finding she alleged only conclusory statements without facts showing differential treatment or causal connection between her protected activity and the adverse action.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 9766529_2020_Jordan v. Sugartree Homes Association of St. Louis County_moed
case_name: Cynthia E. Jordan v. Sugartree Homes Association of St. Louis County, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request is described; the case centers on a failed real estate transaction under an operating agreement and alleged breach of contract, with general FHA claims asserted.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss the FHA claims as time-barred, finding Plaintiff's allegation of filing a HUD complaint in December 2016 sufficient to toll the statute of limitations at the pleading stage, but granted the motion to compel arbitration of the underlying contract and tort claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Cynthia Jordan sued UAH, LLC, Samuel O. Heiman, and Sugartree Homes Association alleging Fair Housing Act violations stemming from a failed real estate transaction where she could not complete purchase of property after her mother's death and her job loss. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss on statute of limitations grounds for the FHA claims but granted the motion to compel arbitration of the underlying contract and tort claims, staying the case pending arbitration.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Hooper v. Advance Am., Cash Advance Centers of Missouri, Inc., 589 F.3d 917 (8th Cir. 2009)
Humphrey v. Eureka Gardens Pub. Facility Bd., 891 F.3d 1079 (8th Cir. 2018)
ABF Freight Sys., Inc. v. Int'l Bhd. of Teamsters, 728 F.3d 853 (8th Cir. 2013)

case_name: Jeffrey S. Amick v. John Carter, et al.
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation requested; complaint concerns alleged conspiracy by neighbor and city officials obstructing property renovation.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff in forma pauperis status and ordered plaintiff to file an amended complaint that complies with Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 8(a) by providing a short and plain statement of claims, as the current 96-page complaint is long, repetitive, and fails to give defendants fair notice of the claims against them.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Jeffrey S. Amick, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued John Carter, various City of St. Louis entities and officials, and others, alleging that Carter orchestrated a conspiracy to obstruct his property renovation at 2401 Indiana Avenue through city inspections, towing, and harassment. The court found the 96-page complaint violated Rule 8(a)(2)'s short and plain statement requirement and ordered plaintiff to file an amended complaint on the court's form within 30 days, warning that failure to comply would result in dismissal without prejudice.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Barton v. Taber, 820 F.3d 958 (8th Cir. 2016)
Brown v. Green Tree Servicing LLC, 820 F.3d 371 (8th Cir. 2016)
Haines v. Kerner, 404 U.S. 519 (1972)
Madewell v. Roberts, 909 F.2d 1203 (8th Cir. 1990)

case_name: Kim Downs v. Red Brick Management, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the plaintiff alleged race-based discrimination in lease non-renewal and police report filing.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA race discrimination claim was dismissed because her amended complaint contained only conclusory allegations that failed to allege sufficient facts showing her lease non-renewal was motivated by her race rather than other reasons.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kim Downs sued her former property owner and landlord alleging race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, claiming she was treated differently because she is African American when her lease was not renewed and when police were allegedly contacted with false information. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the amended complaint contained only cryptic and conclusory allegations that failed to state plausible facts showing discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Gallagher v. City of Clayton, 699 F.3d 1013 (8th Cir. 2012)
Benton v. Merrill Lynch & Co., 524 F.3d 866 (8th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Althea J. Stroud v. Farr Rentals, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made no specific reasonable accommodation request; claims arise from alleged race-based discrimination in property maintenance and services, and retaliation for complaints about housing conditions.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because plaintiff failed to plead facts permitting the inference that she was treated differently on the basis of race, or that there was a causal connection between her complaints and the termination of her tenancy.
brief_summary: Althea J. Stroud, an African American tenant, sued Farr Rentals LLC (white property owners) and the City of Canton, Missouri alleging race-based discrimination in property maintenance and city inspection services, and retaliation for filing complaints about housing conditions. The court dismissed the case because plaintiff failed to plead facts supporting a plausible inference of discrimination or a causal link between her protected complaints and the termination of her tenancy.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Carolyn M. Jones, et al. v. Deca Realty, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with asthma sought duct cleaning and sanitization services to remediate mold spores allegedly worsening her disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim for failure to state a claim because plaintiff did not allege facts showing defendants discriminated against her on the basis of disability; the complaint read as a landlord-tenant dispute about duct cleaning rather than disability discrimination.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Carolyn Jones, who has asthma, sued her landlord Deca Realty alleging the landlord failed to properly remediate mold spores in her apartment's ductwork, causing her health problems; the court dismissed her Fair Housing Act claim for failure to state a claim because she did not adequately allege disability-based discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Estelle v. Gamble, 429 U.S. 97 (1976)

case_name: United States v. Rupp
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case concerns a landlord's enforcement of a 'NO CHILDREN' lease provision against tenants with children.
key_holding: The landlord's notice to vacate provided direct evidence of discriminatory intent based on familial status, and his deposition testimony that he included 'no children' provisions only in leases for parents of young children established different lease terms based on familial status in violation of 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a) and (b).
brief_summary: The United States sued landlord Louis Rupp alleging he violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to renew and attempting to evict tenants Laura Erwin and Martin Teal because they had children, and by including 'NO CHILDREN' provisions in his lease agreements. The court granted summary judgment for the United States, finding direct evidence that the landlord made dwellings unavailable based on familial status and offered different lease terms to tenants with children.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Hous. and Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Vivian Farris v. Bill Allen, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific reasonable accommodation was requested; plaintiff made general allegations of discriminatory treatment without identifying a disability-related accommodation need.
key_holding: The court dismissed all claims under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B) for failure to state a claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing she was blocked or impeded from housing on a protected basis, failed to exhaust MHRA administrative remedies, and failed to plead sufficient facts to support any prima facie case.
brief_summary: Vivian Farris, a Black female tenant at Heritage Place Apartments in Bowling Green, Missouri, sued property manager Bill Allen and others alleging discrimination based on race, age, and gender under the Fair Housing Act, ADA, Missouri Human Rights Act, and other statutes. The court dismissed the case under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B), finding plaintiff failed to state a plausible claim because her FHA claims lacked sufficient factual allegations showing she was denied housing on a protected basis, and her ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims failed because she never alleged she had a disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

source_file: 9772107_2023_Richardson v. Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation_moed
case_name: Richardson v. Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied housing at a Section 8 residential facility due to her schizophrenia (mental disability), though the complaint does not specify a requested accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA racial discrimination claim for failure to allege sufficient factual content showing discrimination based on race, but allowed plaintiff's disability discrimination claim under both the FHA and Title II of the ADA to proceed past initial review, finding she sufficiently alleged denial of housing due to her mental impairment (schizophrenia).
brief_summary: Self-represented plaintiff Gennena V. Richardson sued Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation, a Section 8 housing facility operator, alleging she was denied housing on May 4, 2021, because of her schizophrenia and her status as a Black American. The court dismissed her FHA racial discrimination claim for failing to allege sufficient factual content showing how she was discriminated against based on race, but allowed her disability discrimination claim under both the FHA and Title II of the ADA to proceed past initial review under 28 U.S.C. § 1915, finding she adequately alleged she was qualified for housing but denied solely due to her mental disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997)
Randolph v. Rodgers, 170 F.3d 850 (8th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Catherine Christine McKee v. Busey Bank
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, an 88-year-old Black woman, alleges Busey Bank discriminated against her in mortgage lending terms and foreclosure threats based on her race and age.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claims fail because she has not alleged facts showing she was treated differently due to her race or age; her complaints about late fees and payment increases due to escrow shortages and nonpayment are not discriminatory conduct but rather standard loan collection practices.
brief_summary: Catherine McKee, an 88-year-old Black homeowner in Missouri, sued Busey Bank alleging race and age discrimination in mortgage lending under the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed her complaint on initial review for failure to state a claim because she failed to allege facts causally connecting her race or age to any discriminatory conduct, as her complaints about late fees and payment increases stemmed from her own nonpayment and escrow deficiencies rather than discriminatory treatment.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 9773027_2023_Richardson v. Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation_moed
case_name: Gennena V. Richardson v. Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiff generally alleges she required a necessary accommodation but the complaint does not specify a particular accommodation request; the core claim is that she was denied housing at a Section 8 facility because she lacks the facility's qualifying disability categories (vision, hearing, or mobility impairment).
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because her allegations suggest she was denied housing because she lacked a qualifying disability (vision, hearing, or mobility impairment) rather than because she had a disability, failing to state a plausible claim that she was discriminated against 'because of' a handicap under § 3604(f).
brief_summary: Gennena Richardson, a pro se plaintiff with schizophrenia, sued Cardinal Ritter Institute Residential Services Corporation alleging she was denied Section 8 housing in May 2021 because she has a mental health disability. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to state a plausible FHA claim because plaintiff was informed she did not qualify for housing as she is not vision, hearing, or mobility impaired—the very allegations suggest denial because she lacked a qualifying disability rather than because she had one.
