key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Sproaps v. Brown
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged disparate treatment based on sexual orientation, including refusal to make repairs and eviction threats, rather than a request for disability accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because the only evidence of discrimination was a stray remark about plaintiff's 'lover boy' that was unrelated to any discriminatory conduct, and the evidence showed repairs were delayed due to a financial dispute rather than plaintiff's sexual orientation.
brief_summary: Self-represented plaintiff Marriell Sproaps sued his former property manager Kevin Brown alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on sexual orientation discrimination, claiming Brown refused to repair his vandalized apartment and threatened eviction because of plaintiff's same-sex partner. The court dismissed the complaint finding plaintiff failed to state a plausible discrimination claim because the only evidence was a stray remark unrelated to any discriminatory policy or action, and the evidence showed repairs were delayed due to a financial dispute over responsibility for damage.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997)

case_name: Ernesto Varela, et al. v. William Harold Hill, et al.
accommodation_description: Hispanic/Latino family alleges neighbor engaged in years-long campaign of racial harassment including slurs, threats, and false reports to authorities, forcing them to vacate their home and asserting interference with Fair Housing Act rights.
key_holding: Hill's motion to dismiss the § 1981 claim is granted because he was not in a contractual relationship with the Varelas, but his motion to dismiss the § 1982 and § 3617 claims is denied because the complaint adequately alleges race-based harassment that interfered with their property use and right to live in the neighborhood of their choice.
brief_summary: The Varelas, a Hispanic/Latino family who moved to Wildwood, Missouri so their deaf son could attend a special education school, sued their neighbor Hill alleging years of racial harassment including slurs, threats, and false reports to authorities that ultimately forced them to move. The Court dismissed their § 1981 claim against Hill (no contractual relationship) but allowed their § 1982 and § 3617 Fair Housing Act claims to proceed, finding the complaint adequately alleges race-based interference with their housing rights.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Domino's Pizza, Inc. v. McDonald, 546 U.S. 470 (2006)
Watters v. Homeowners' Ass'n at Pres. at Bridgewater, 48 F.4th 779 (7th Cir. 2022)

case_name: Robert Ray Young v. Liz Hagar-Mace and Amy Copeland
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges termination from SPC housing voucher program constituted discrimination based on race and disability, but no specific accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of discrimination because his allegations were conclusory and failed to create a reasonable inference of discriminatory intent, and even if he had made out a prima facie case, Defendants articulated legitimate non-discriminatory reasons for terminating his housing voucher.
brief_summary: Robert Ray Young, a homeless African-American man with mental and physical disabilities, sued Liz Hagar-Mace and Amy Copeland of the Missouri Department of Mental Health alleging discrimination based on race and disability in connection with his termination from the Shelter Plus Care housing voucher program. The court granted summary judgment for Defendants, finding Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of intentional discrimination because his allegations were conclusory and failed to create an inference of discriminatory intent, and Defendants had legitimate non-discriminatory reasons for terminating his benefits.
key_cases_cited: Gallagher v. Magner, 619 F.3d 823 (8th Cir. 2010)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)

case_name: Kwanza Wilson v. KLD Properties, Ken Davis, Public Administrator; and Foster House
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was not given the same opportunity to live at her residence, though no specific accommodation request is discernible from the complaint.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state an FHA claim because plaintiff did not allege differential treatment based on a protected characteristic under § 3604, and the court could not identify any cognizable federal claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Kwanza Wilson filed suit against KLD Properties and others claiming she was not given the same opportunity to live at her residence, which the court construed as a Fair Housing Act claim. The court dismissed the action for failure to state a claim because the complaint did not allege discrimination based on any protected characteristic under 42 U.S.C. § 3604, and no other cognizable federal claim was identified.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Theresa Marie Barbero v. Wilhoit Property Management, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request to allow ESA dog to go outside off leash during brutal ice and snow storm
key_holding: The FHA reasonable accommodation claim failed because Plaintiff did not allege how Defendant's refusal to allow her ESA dog off-leash during a snowstorm affected her ability to use and enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Theresa Marie Barbero sued Wilhoit Property Management, Inc. alleging the landlord forced her to move out two weeks after moving in and refused to allow her ESA dog off-leash during a snowstorm, violating the FHA among other statutes. The Court granted Defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the FHA reasonable accommodation claim failed because Plaintiff did not allege how the denial affected her ability to use and enjoy her dwelling, and Plaintiff failed to identify her race or disability in her other claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9775965_2022_Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association, Inc._mowd
case_name: Burton Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with neurological disorder requested HOA waive policy prohibiting drop-down boat covers to allow him to independently cover his boat docked at his condominium boat well.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff failed to demonstrate substantial likelihood of success on the merits of his reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) and failed to show irreparable harm, as alternative means of covering the boat may be available and money damages could compensate any harm.
brief_summary: Burton Kirsten sued his condominium homeowners association seeking to prevent removal of a drop-down boat cover he installed as a reasonable accommodation for his neurological disability, claiming the HOA's policy prohibiting such covers violates the Fair Housing Act. The court denied his motion for temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction, finding he failed to show substantial likelihood of success on the merits and failed to demonstrate irreparable harm given the availability of alternative boat-covering services.
key_cases_cited: Planned Parenthood Minnesota, N. Dakota, S. Dakota v. Rounds, 530 F.3d 724 (8th Cir. 2008)
Dataphase Sys., Inc. v. C L Syst., Inc., 640 F.2d 109 (8th Cir. 1981)
Nevels v. W. World Ins. Co., 359 F. Supp. 2d 1110 (W.D. Wash. 2004)
Novus Franchising, Inc. v. Dawson, 725 F.3d 885 (8th Cir. 2013)

source_file: 9775966_2022_Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association, Inc._mowd
case_name: Burton Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requests permission to keep a custom drop-down boat cover installed on the awning above his boat well, which his neurological disorder prevents him from covering manually.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff alleged sufficient facts that the HOA's policy prohibiting drop-down boat covers may violate the FHA by preventing Plaintiff equal opportunity to use and enjoy his boat well facility, relying on Shapiro v. Cadman Towers for the proposition that policies affecting facilities in connection with dwellings require accommodation analysis.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Burton Kirsten, a Michigan resident owning a condominium in Osage Beach, Missouri governed by the defendant HOA, sued alleging the HOA's refusal to permit his drop-down boat cover violates the FHA as a reasonable accommodation/modification for his neurological disorder. The court granted in part and denied in part Defendant's motion to dismiss, dismissing Plaintiff's IIED claim with prejudice but allowing the FHA reasonable accommodation and modification claims to proceed past the pleading stage.
key_cases_cited: Shapiro v. Cadman Towers, Inc., 51 F.3d 328 (2d Cir. 1995)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Zutz v. Nelson, 601 F.3d 842 (8th Cir. 2010)
Gibson v. Brewer, 952 S.W.2d 239 (Mo. 1997)

source_file: 9776401_2024_Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association, Inc._mowd
case_name: Burton Kirsten v. Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with neurological disorder requested HOA modify its policy prohibiting drop-down boat covers to allow his custom drop-down boat cover for his boat well.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of failure to accommodate because he did not demonstrate the policy modification was necessary to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling—plaintiff never used the boat cover, used the dock only five days total in three years, and the existing awning already provides protection, making the requested modification a mere preference rather than a necessity.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Burton Kirsten sued Cape Royale at Ski Harbor Condominium Owners Association, alleging the HOA violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to modify its policy prohibiting drop-down boat covers to accommodate his neurological disability. The court granted summary judgment for the HOA, finding Kirsten failed to establish the accommodation was necessary for equal use and enjoyment of his dwelling because he never used the cover, rarely visited the dock, and an existing awning already provided adequate protection.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
United States v. Badgett, 976 F.2d 1176 (8th Cir. 1992)
Bronk v. Ineichen, 54 F.3d 425 (7th Cir. 1995)
Shapiro v. Cadman Towers, Inc., 51 F.3d 328 (2d Cir. 1995)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Martin v. RUREDY808, L.L.C.
accommodation_description: Robert Martin requested permission to keep his service dog/ESA 'Slade' in his rented condominium to assist with his mental health disabilities including anxiety, PTSD, and bipolar disorder.
key_holding: The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the FHA claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether defendants intentionally discriminated against Martin and retaliated against him for requesting an assistance animal accommodation, precluding summary judgment for either side.
brief_summary: Robert Martin sued RUREDY808, L.L.C. and Frank Savage alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act after defendants attempted to evict him for keeping his service dog Slade despite a lease provision permitting the animal as a reasonable accommodation for his mental health disability. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether defendants' actions constituted intentional discrimination and retaliation under the FHA, such that trial is necessary.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Bloch v. Frischolz, 587 F.3d 771 (7th Cir. 2009)
Texas v. Crest Asset Management, Inc., 85 F. Supp. 2d 722

case_name: United States v. SSM Properties, LLC
accommodation_description: This case involves race-based housing discrimination and steering, not a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court found extensive evidence of race discrimination and steering against Black testers at defendants' properties, and held that the Mauldings are vicariously liable for their agent James Roe's discriminatory conduct under FHA vicarious liability principles.
brief_summary: The United States and plaintiff-intervenors (Black testers from the Louisiana Fair Housing Action Center) sued SSM Properties, LLC and its owners for race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, presenting evidence that the defendants' agent James Roe engaged in discriminatory steering by showing white testers units at Pearl Manor and 468 Place Townhomes while turning away Black testers. The court granted summary judgment for plaintiffs, finding undisputed evidence of race-based differential treatment and that the Mauldings are vicariously liable for Roe's discriminatory conduct.
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Walker v. Crigler, 976 F.2d 900 (4th Cir. 1992)
Otero v. New York City Hous. Auth., 484 F.2d 1122 (2d Cir. 1973)
United States v. Real Estate Dev. Corp., 347 F. Supp. 776 (N.D. Miss. 1972)

case_name: Gary L. Swanson v. Alex King & King Management, LLC
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request described; complaint merely alleged disability-based housing discrimination.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim for failure to state a plausible claim because plaintiff provided no factual support for his allegation that defendant discriminated against him on the basis of disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Gary Swanson sued landlord Alex King and King Management, LLC under the Fair Housing Act, alleging disability-based housing discrimination after being locked out of his home. The court granted the magistrate judge's recommendation and dismissed the FHA claim with prejudice for failure to state a plausible claim, finding plaintiff's complaint contained only conclusory allegations of discrimination without supporting factual details.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9786613_2024_Jett v. The Country Acres Association of Columbus County LTD_nced
case_name: Jett v. The Country Acres Association of Columbus County LTD
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs mention 'reasonable accommodations' in ADA context regarding fines/penalties on the disabled, but no specific accommodation request is analyzed as the ADA claim was dismissed for failure to state a claim.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act and ADA claims for failure to state a claim because plaintiffs did not identify which FHA provisions were violated or how the ADA was implicated, and legal conclusions of discrimination without supporting facts are insufficient.
brief_summary: Raymond and Tara Jett, an interracial couple, sued George and Michael Coleman, developer Mitchell Powell, attorney Martin Scott, and the Country Acres HOA alleging racial harassment under 42 U.S.C. § 1982 and Fair Housing Act violations. The court allowed the § 1982 and state tort claims against the Colemans to proceed but dismissed the FHA and ADA claims because plaintiffs failed to articulate which statutory provisions were violated and offered only conclusory allegations of discrimination without supporting facts.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Derrick Allen v. Phillip Jordan, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged housing discrimination based on race and other protected characteristics, not a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a claim because it did not allege facts showing defendants violated plaintiff's constitutional rights or engaged in discriminatory conduct, and plaintiff's own attached documents contradicted his allegations of failure to investigate.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Derrick Allen, proceeding pro se and IFP, sued four employees of the City of Durham Human Relations Department alleging they failed to investigate his complaints of housing discrimination by Lenox at Patterson Place and Feather Stone Apartments based on race. The court recommended dismissal under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations and plaintiff's own attached documents contradicted his claims by showing the investigation was conducted and no discriminatory housing practices were found.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Derrick Allen v. Annette Moore, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff claimed housing discrimination based on denial of public housing application due to allegedly inaccurate FBI criminal history, but did not request any disability-related accommodation.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a viable Fair Housing Act claim because it alleged no factual matter showing discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin; the denial stemmed from allegedly inaccurate criminal history rather than protected-class discrimination.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Derrick Allen sued Orange County Human Relations officials under Section 1983 for failing to investigate his housing discrimination claim after the Town of Chapel Hill denied his public housing application based on allegedly inaccurate FBI criminal history. The court recommended dismissal, finding the complaint failed to state a Fair Housing Act claim because it contained no factual allegations showing discrimination based on protected characteristics and merely invoked the term 'discrimination' without supporting factual matter.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Parker v. Hunting Point Apartments, LLC

source_file: 9789537_2020_Ellis-Barr v. CP_DB Housing Partners XIII, L.P._ncwd
case_name: Shalae Ellis-Barr and Brittani Thompson (Gabriel) v. CP/DB Housing Partners XIII, L.P., Tesco Properties, Inc., Mavis Williams and Shawn Lisenby
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Ellis-Barr requested an assistance/service animal to help cope with anxiety and depression caused by sexual harassment and disability-related difficulties.
key_holding: The court denied Williams' motion to dismiss, holding that individual property managers can be directly liable under the FHA if they personally participated in or ratified discriminatory conduct, and that Plaintiffs sufficiently stated claims for hostile work environment, interference, and retaliation.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Ellis-Barr and Gabriel sued the on-site property manager and others alleging sexual harassment by a maintenance worker, denial of a requested assistance animal, and retaliatory conduct including lease non-renewal. The court denied the individual property manager's motion to dismiss, holding that individual employees can be directly liable under the FHA for personally participating in discriminatory conduct, and that plaintiffs sufficiently stated claims for hostile work environment under § 3604(b) and retaliation/interference under § 3617.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Chicago v. Matchmaker Real Estate Sales Ctr., Inc., 982 F.2d 1086 (7th Cir. 1992)
Northside Realty Assocs., Inc. v. U.S., 605 F.2d 1348 (5th Cir. 1979)

source_file: 9795430_2020_Thompson v. Morgan Properties Management Company, LLC_ned
case_name: Dr. Franklin Thompson v. Morgan Properties Management Company, LLC
accommodation_description: Nathan Bird, a wheelchair-bound paraplegic, requested installation of a permanent exterior ramp at the building entrance at his own expense.
key_holding: Dr. Thompson lacks standing to bring the reasonable modification claim on behalf of Nathan Bird because Nathan was never actually denied a ramp—Defendants installed a ramp at the building entrance on May 29, 2019, at no charge to the Birds, and the temporary ramp was never removed.
brief_summary: Dr. Franklin Thompson, Director of the Omaha Human Rights and Relations Department, brought this action on behalf of Nathan Bird against Regency Lakeside Apartments and Morgan Properties Management, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act for refusing to permit installation of a permanent ramp at Nathan Bird's expense. The court granted Defendants' Motion to Dismiss on standing grounds, finding that Nathan Bird suffered no injury-in-fact because he was never actually denied a ramp—Defendants installed one at no charge in May 2019.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
Balogh v. Lombardi, 816 F.3d 536 (8th Cir. 2016)
Titus v. Sullivan, 4 F.3d 590 (8th Cir. 1993)
Iowa League of Cities v. EPA, 711 F.3d 844 (8th Cir. 2013)
Carlsen v. GameStop, Inc., 833 F.3d 903 (8th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Kandice Louise Cooke v. Randolph, Nebraska City Council and The City of Randolph, Nebraska
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support animal (dog named Rufus) despite municipal ordinance banning pit bull breeds
key_holding: The court granted preliminary injunctive relief because Cooke made a prima facie showing of success on her FHA reasonable accommodation claim—she has a qualifying mental health disability, the City knew of it, her emotional support animal was necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, and the accommodation was reasonable—while the City failed to rebut evidence of irreparable harm or demonstrate undue hardship.
brief_summary: Kandice Cooke, a Randolph, Nebraska resident with anxiety, bipolar disorder, and PTSD, sued the City of Randolph seeking to keep her emotional support animal Rufus despite the city's ordinance banning pit bull breeds. The court denied the City's motion to dismiss and granted Cooke's request for preliminary injunction, finding she demonstrated a reasonable likelihood of succeeding on her Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because the accommodation was facially reasonable and the City failed to rebut her evidence or demonstrate undue hardship.
key_cases_cited: DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Fair Hous. of the Dakotas, Inc. v. Goldmark Prop. Mgmt., Inc., 778 F. Supp. 2d 1028 (D.N.D. 2011)
Groteboer v. Eyota Econ. Dev. Auth., 724 F. Supp. 2d 1018 (D. Minn. 2010)
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 77 F.3d 249 (8th Cir. 1996)

case_name: Sheila Lynn Triplett v. Seldin Company
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested transfer to an accessible unit with additional windows to help ease her depression and anxiety.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's reasonable accommodation claim because she was ultimately permitted to transfer to an accessible unit, so she could not establish defendants refused the accommodation; however, the court allowed plaintiff's retaliation, disparate treatment, and harassment claims to proceed.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sheila Lynn Triplett sued Seldin Company and its employees for disability discrimination under the FHA and Rehabilitation Act arising from her tenancy at Forest Acres Apartments in South Sioux City, Nebraska, claiming defendants denied her accommodation request for a unit with more windows, subjected her to disparate treatment, retaliation, and harassment after she complained about the denial. The court dismissed the reasonable accommodation claim without prejudice because plaintiff was ultimately transferred to the accessible unit she requested, but allowed plaintiff's claims for retaliation, disparate treatment, and harassment to proceed to service of process.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Neudecker v. Boisclair Corp.
Gallagher v. Magner
Fair Hous. of the Dakotas, Inc. v. Goldmark Prop. Mgmt., Inc.

source_file: 9798623_2019_Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017_nhd
case_name: Margaret Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017 et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who has hearing impairment, COPD, PTSD, and anxiety, complained about inadequate notice regarding snow removal and referenced a support cat as reasonable accommodations related to her disabilities.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss Kris's FHA retaliation claim under § 3617, finding that Kris adequately pleaded (1) protected activity (her complaint to MHRA about inadequate notice due to hearing impairment), (2) adverse action (verbal and physical assault and eviction), and (3) causal connection (temporal proximity between her protected activity and the adverse actions).
brief_summary: Plaintiff Margaret Kris, a Section 8 tenant with hearing impairment, COPD, PTSD, and anxiety, sued her private landlord (Dusseault Family Revocable Trust) for retaliation under the Fair Housing Act after she complained to the Manchester Housing and Redevelopment Authority about inadequate notice related to her disability. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that Kris adequately pleaded her retaliation claim by showing protected activity (complaints to housing authority about disability discrimination in notice provision), adverse action (verbal and physical assault, followed by eviction), and causal connection (temporal proximity between protected activity and adverse actions).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Frazier v. Rominger, 27 F.3d 828 (2d Cir. 1994)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Lloyd v. Presby's Inspired Life, 251 F. Supp. 3d 891 (E.D. Pa. 2017)

source_file: 9799020_2022_Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017_nhd
case_name: Margaret Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017
accommodation_description: Kris requested assistance from the MHRA in terminating her lease early and transferring to another apartment in spring 2018 to avoid exposure to chemical pesticides that could harm her COPD and her emotional support cat.
key_holding: The court denied Kris's motion to add reasonable accommodation claims against the MHRA, finding that the four-month delay in processing her transfer request did not constitute an unreasonable delay or constructive denial where the delay was attributable to inadvertence and procedural issues rather than bad faith, and the request became moot upon her eviction.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Margaret Kris, who was evicted from her Section 8-subsidized apartment for non-payment of rent, sought to add the Manchester Housing and Redevelopment Authority and other entities as defendants and to add claims including failure to accommodate her request to transfer units due to pesticide exposure concerns. The court denied the motion as futile, finding that Kris failed to state a viable reasonable accommodation claim because the MHRA's four-month processing delay did not constitute constructive denial absent evidence of bad faith or willful obstruction.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, HUD, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Valle-Arce v. P.R. Ports Auth., 651 F.3d 190 (1st Cir. 2011)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
McCray v. Wilkie, 966 F.3d 616 (7th Cir. 2020)

source_file: 9799485_2022_Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017_nhd
case_name: Kris v. Dusseault Family Revocable Trust of 2017
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a tenant with hearing impairment, COPD, and mental health disabilities, kept a cat as an emotional support animal and submitted a reasonable accommodation request to the MHRA regarding bedbug extermination affecting her COPD and her support animal.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiff's motion for summary judgment on her FHA retaliation claims because she failed to establish that retaliation was the but-for cause of her eviction and other adverse actions, as the record showed her eviction was triggered by her failure to pay rent.
brief_summary: Margaret Kris, a Section 8 voucher holder with disabilities including hearing impairment, COPD, and mental health issues who kept an emotional support animal, sued the Dusseault Family Trust and individual landlords under the FHA, claiming retaliation for complaints to the MHRA including verbal and physical assault, failure to address maintenance, eviction, and security deposit retention. The court denied Kris's motion for summary judgment on her retaliation claims, finding she failed to establish but-for causation because her eviction resulted from nonpayment of rent rather than retaliation for protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of Afr. Am.-Owned Media, 140 S. Ct. 1009 (2020)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Taylor v. Nat'l Invs., Ltd., 2022 WL 306367 (D.R.I. Feb. 2, 2022)
Geraci v. Union Square Condo. Ass'n, 891 F.3d 274 (7th Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9800011_2019_RAMOS v. RARITAN VALLEY HABITAT FOR HUMANITY_njd
case_name: Laura Ramos and Cynthia Baker v. Raritan Valley Habitat for Humanity, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested DCA provide larger font sizes (Verdana 14pt) for state and federal forms, conduct meetings at their home or via video conference rather than requiring office visits, and provide email summaries of phone meetings.
key_holding: The court denied DCA's motion to dismiss the failure-to-accommodate claim under the FHA, ADA, and RHA, finding plaintiffs adequately pled that DCA failed to provide communication accommodations (large print documents, home/video conferences, written responses) necessary for them to participate in the Section 8 homeownership program.
brief_summary: Two disabled plaintiffs (Ramos and Baker) sued DCA and HUD alleging they were denied reasonable accommodations necessary to participate in the Section 8 homeownership program, including large print documents, home visits, and video conferencing. The court granted HUD's motion to dismiss FHA and ADA claims (sovereign immunity), but denied DCA's motion to dismiss the failure-to-accommodate claims, finding plaintiffs adequately pled DCA's refusal to provide communication accommodations denied them equal opportunity in the housing program.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lane v. Pena, 518 U.S. 187 (1996)
Tennessee v. Lane, 541 U.S. 509 (2004)
Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

source_file: 9800309_2019_431 E PALISADE AVENUE REAL ESTATE, LLC v. CITY OF ENGLEWOOD_njd
case_name: 431 E Palisade Avenue Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood
accommodation_description: Developer sought to construct a 150-bed assisted-living and memory-care facility in a residential zone, arguing the city's zoning ordinance facially discriminates against elderly and disabled individuals by only permitting such facilities in the non-residential RIM district.
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs likely to succeed on the merits of their Fair Housing Act claim because the city's zoning ordinance, which permits assisted-living facilities only in the RIM district and prohibits them in any of the city's 24 primarily residential districts, constitutes disparate treatment of elderly and disabled individuals who require congregant care.
brief_summary: Developer-plaintiffs sued the City of Englewood challenging zoning ordinances that permit assisted-living and memory-care facilities only in the non-residential RIM district, arguing this facially discriminates against elderly and disabled individuals under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted a preliminary injunction, finding plaintiffs demonstrated a likelihood of success on their disparate treatment claim because the ordinance limits congregant-care facilities to one non-residential zone while prohibiting them in all primarily residential districts, thereby limiting where disabled residents can live in the community.
key_cases_cited: Hovsons, Inc. v. Twp. of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Community Services, Inc. v. City of Moultrie, 421 F.3d 174 (3d Cir. 2005)
Resident Advisory Bd. v. Rizzo, 564 F.2d 126 (3d Cir. 1977)
Montana Fair Hous., Inc. v. City of Bozeman, 854 F. Supp. 2d 832 (D. Mont. 2012)
Assisted Living Assocs. of Moorestown, L.L.C. v. Moorestown Twp., 996 F. Supp. 409 (D.N.J. 1998)

case_name: Antonelli v. Gloucester County Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an emergency transfer and relocation to a new unit following domestic violence incidents, which the court analyzed as a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because her requests for a three-bedroom voucher and emergency transfer were motivated by her son's needs and domestic violence, respectively, not her own disability, thus failing to establish the required nexus between the accommodation and disability; however, the court declined to dismiss plaintiff's sex discrimination claim based on VAWA violations.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Danielle Lyn Antonelli sued the Gloucester County Housing Authority and its employees alleging violations of the FHA, Rehabilitation Act, ADA, and Title VI based on the downgrading and termination of her Section 8 voucher and the agency's failure to relocate her after domestic violence. The Court dismissed her disability discrimination and reasonable accommodation claims under the FHA, Rehabilitation Act, and ADA for failure to allege a nexus between her disability and the requested accommodations, but declined to dismiss her sex discrimination claim under the FHA based on VAWA violations.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. Of Adjustment of Twp. Of Scotch Plains
Comm. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth.
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware Cty.

case_name: Livingstone v. Haddon Point Manager, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested waiver or reduction of the 10% late fee and modification of rent payment due date as an accommodation for his financial circumstances arising from delayed federal student financial aid disbursements.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim because the FHA is not a vehicle for general lease reformation; Plaintiff's complaint failed to allege any facts suggesting Defendants discriminated against him based on his race or that the late fee policy had a discriminatory effect on a protected class.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michael Livingstone, a Black male graduate student, sued his private landlord Haddon Point Manager LLC and associated parties alleging violations of the FDCPA, Section 1983, and FHA related to late fees, debt collection, and eviction proceedings arising from delayed student financial aid. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss all claims except the FDCPA claim against the debt collection attorneys, finding the FHA claim failed because Plaintiff alleged no facts showing race-based discrimination or discriminatory effect from the 10% late fee policy.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Douglass v. Convergent Outsourcing, 765 F.3d 299 (3d Cir. 2014)
St. Pierre v. Retrieval-Masters Creditors Bureau, Inc., 898 F.3d 351 (3d Cir. 2018)
Mitchell v. Walters, 2010 WL 3614210 (D.N.J. Sept. 8, 2010)

source_file: 9802991_2020_THE HANSEN FOUNDATION, INC. v. CITY OF ATLANTIC CITY_njd
case_name: The Hansen Foundation, Inc. v. City of Atlantic City
accommodation_description: Permission for Serenity House, a recovery residence for women in addiction recovery, to operate in Atlantic City's R-2 Zoning District despite § 163-66B's restriction on group family households in that zone.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendant on plaintiffs' federal reasonable accommodation claims because plaintiffs failed to demonstrate that seeking a zoning variance from the City would have been demonstrably futile, a prerequisite for ripeness of FHAA reasonable accommodation claims in the Third Circuit.
brief_summary: The Hansen Foundation, Inc. and its subsidiary Hansen House, LLC sued the City of Atlantic City challenging the City's enforcement of zoning provisions against Serenity House, a recovery residence for women in substance abuse recovery, alleging violations of the FHA, ADA, RHA, and constitutional and state law provisions. The court granted summary judgment for defendant on plaintiffs' federal reasonable accommodation claims, finding plaintiffs failed to exhaust administrative remedies by not demonstrating that seeking a zoning variance would have been futile, while also granting partial injunctive relief and remanding state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Lapid-Laurel LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Assisted Living Assocs. of Moorestown, L.L.C. v. Moorestown Twp., 996 F. Supp. 409 (D.N.J. 1998)
Village of Palatine v. United States, 37 F.3d 1230 (7th Cir. 1994)
Oxford House-A v. City of Univ. City, 87 F.3d 1022 (8th Cir. 1996)
New Jersey Chinese Community Center v. Township of Warren, 712 F. App'x. 196 (3d Cir. 2017)

case_name: Michael A. D'Antonio v. Borough of Allendale, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a real estate developer, alleged defendants violated the FHA by interfering with his property development rights through zoning enforcement, but made no disability accommodation request.
key_holding: The FHA claim fails because plaintiff failed to identify which FHA provision was violated, failed to allege membership in a protected class, and the two-year statute of limitations had expired.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michael D'Antonio, a pro se real estate developer, sued the Borough of Allendale and other defendants alleging they violated the FHA by interfering with his property development through zoning enforcement, ultimately causing foreclosure and eviction. The Court dismissed the TAC with prejudice, finding plaintiff lacked standing because the property was owned by his corporation, his claims were precluded by prior state court judgments, and his FHA claim failed because he did not identify a specific protected class or cognizable FHA violation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Monell v. Department of Social Services of the City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1974)
Bostock v. Clayton County, Georgia, 140 S. Ct. 1731 (2020)

case_name: Samantha Campaniello-Cason v. Kevin O'Reilly
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this case concerns alleged racial and ethnic slurs, harassment, and lease non-renewal based on protected characteristics.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiffs leave to amend their FHA and NJLAD claims to add national origin and familial status discrimination because the proposed amendments would not cause undue prejudice and defendant did not demonstrate futility, but denied leave to add a claim under the New Jersey Anti-Eviction Act because the 'good cause' requirement does not apply to owner-occupied two-family homes.
brief_summary: Samantha Campaniello-Cason and her children sued landlord Kevin O'Reilly alleging racial and ethnic harassment including use of slurs, shutting off utilities, and non-renewal of lease; the magistrate judge granted plaintiffs leave to amend their complaint to add national origin and familial status discrimination claims to their existing Fair Housing Act and NJLAD claims, but denied their request to add a claim under the New Jersey Anti-Eviction Act because the statute's 'good cause' requirement does not apply to owner-occupied two-family dwellings.
key_cases_cited: Foman v. Davis, 371 U.S. 178 (1962)
Arthur v. Maersk, Inc., 434 F.3d 196 (3d Cir. 2006)
Cureton v. Nat'l Collegiate Athletic Ass'n, 252 F.3d 267 (3d Cir. 2001)
In re Burlington Coat Factory Sec. Litig., 114 F.3d 1410 (3d Cir. 1997)
Massarsky v. General Motors Corp., 706 F.2d 111 (3d Cir. 1983)

case_name: Michael A. D'Antonio v. Borough of Allendale, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; case concerns land development dispute and municipal interference with property development, not disability accommodation
key_holding: Motion for reconsideration denied where plaintiff failed to identify intervening change in law, new evidence, or clear error of law, and merely attempted to relitigate previously rejected arguments regarding standing and preclusion of his FHA claims.
brief_summary: Michael D'Antonio, a developer, sued the Borough of Allendale and other defendants alleging they interfered with his attempts to build affordable housing on a property and ultimately evicted him, asserting claims including under the Fair Housing Act. The Court denied D'Antonio's motion for reconsideration of the dismissal of his Third Amended Complaint, finding he lacked standing to bring claims on behalf of a corporation, his claims were precluded, and he failed to state a viable claim.
key_cases_cited: N. River Ins. Co. v. CIGNA Reinsurance Co., 52 F.3d 1194 (3d Cir. 1995)
ABS Brokerage Servs. v. Penson Fin. Servs., Inc., 2010 WL 3257992 (D.N.J. Aug. 16, 2010)
Wayne Land & Mineral Grp., LLC v. Del. River Basin Comm'n, 959 F.3d 569 (3d Cir. 2020)
Grimm v. Borough of Norristown, 226 F. Supp. 2d 606 (E.D. Pa. 2002)
Lombreglia v. Sunbeam Prod., Inc., 2021 WL 118932 (D.N.J. Jan. 13, 2021)

case_name: Selvaggi v. Point Pleasant Beach Borough
accommodation_description: Property owners challenging municipal short-term rental ordinance under FHA familial status theory - not a reasonable accommodation case
key_holding: The court granted limited injunctive relief striking down the ordinance's overbroad definition of 'Rental' under New Jersey constitutional privacy rights, but found no likelihood of success on the federal FHA claim because short-term vacation rentals are not dwellings occupied as residences under the FHA.
brief_summary: Robin and Leonardo Selvaggi, California residents owning rental properties in Point Pleasant Beach, New Jersey, sought a preliminary injunction against Ordinance No. 2021-33, a municipal short-term rental ordinance, asserting federal constitutional and Fair Housing Act violations including familial status discrimination. The court granted limited injunctive relief striking down the ordinance's overbroad definition of 'Rental' under New Jersey constitutional privacy rights, but found no likelihood of success on the federal FHA claim because short-term vacation rentals are not dwellings occupied as residences under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Weisenberg v. Town Bd. of Shelter Island, 404 F. Supp. 3d 720 (E.D.N.Y. 2019)
Slidewaters LLC v. Washington State Dep't of Lab. & Indus., 4 F.4th 747 (9th Cir. 2021)
Village of Willowbrook v. Olech, 528 U.S. 562 (2000)
Nekrilov v. City of Jersey City, 528 F. Supp. 3d 252 (D.N.J. 2021)

source_file: 9806665_2022_OXFORD HOUSE, INC. v. TOWNSHIP OF NORTH BERGEN_njd
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House, a recovery residence operator, sought a Certificate of Occupancy to operate a six-person group home for men recovering from substance abuse in a two-family dwelling zoned for single-family use.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to establish likelihood of success on the merits because Defendant's denial of the Certificate of Occupancy was based on a neutral application of the Community Residence provision requiring single-family dwelling placement for persons with disabilities, and Plaintiff presented no evidence that discriminatory purpose motivated the denial.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc. sued Township of North Bergen seeking a preliminary injunction to compel issuance of a Certificate of Occupancy for a group home for recovering addicts in a two-family dwelling, alleging violations of the FHA, ADA, and NJLAD. The Court denied the motion because Plaintiff failed to show likelihood of success—Defendant's denial was based on neutral application of a state zoning provision requiring single-family placement for disabled residents, not discriminatory intent, and Plaintiff presented no evidence of disparate impact.
key_cases_cited: Oxford House-Evergreen v. City of Plainfield, 769 F. Supp. 1329 (D.N.J. 1991)
Oxford House, Inc. v. Twp. of Cherry Hill, 799 F. Supp. 450 (D.N.J. 1992)
United States v. Borough of Audubon, 797 F. Supp. 353 (D.N.J. 1991)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

source_file: 9807546_2022_VETERAN RELOCATION PROJECT, LLC v. BOROUGH OF BRADLEY BEACH_njd
case_name: Veteran Relocation Project, LLC v. Borough of Bradley Beach
race_if_mentioned: Indian descent (intended buyer Anu Garrison); Plaintiff Marchese's race also referenced
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff-developer claims municipality discriminated against them in property development because of intended buyer's race (Indian descent).
key_holding: The Court denied the motion to dismiss Count I (FHA discrimination) finding plaintiff-developer had standing to sue because they planned to develop housing for a protected class member (Indian national) whose sale was allegedly thwarted by discriminatory municipal conduct; Count II (NJLAD) was dismissed for lack of federal jurisdiction over municipal land use discrimination claims.
brief_summary: Veteran Relocation Project, LLC and James Marchese sued the Borough of Bradley Beach and individual officials claiming the municipality discriminated against them during a construction project because the property was being sold to Anu Garrison, a woman of Indian descent. The Court denied the motion to dismiss the FHA discrimination claim (Count I), finding plaintiffs had standing as third-party beneficiaries of FHA protections, but dismissed the NJLAD claim (Count II) for lack of federal jurisdiction over municipal land use discrimination claims under state law.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Eastampton Ctr., L.L.C. v. Township of Eastampton

case_name: Adams v. DMG Park, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves intentional race-based discrimination in housing, including being lied to about available units and subjected to a campaign of harassment because Plaintiffs are African American.
key_holding: The court dismissed claims against DMG Park and DMG Investments due to impermissible group pleading and statute of limitations, but allowed Plaintiffs leave to amend within 30 days to cure these deficiencies; the court denied the motion to compel arbitration and retained supplemental jurisdiction over parking garage claims.
brief_summary: Gerald Adams and Julia Jones, an African American couple, sued DMG Investments LLC, DMG Park LLC, DMG Property Management, and the One Park Condominium Association alleging race-based housing discrimination under the FHA and New Jersey LAD, claiming Defendants lied about available units because of their race and subjected them to ongoing harassment after purchase. The court granted in part Defendants' motions to dismiss for impermissible group pleading and time-barred claims, but denied the motion to compel arbitration and allowed Plaintiffs leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
Guidotti v. Legal Helpers Debt Resol., L.L.C.

source_file: 9808330_2023_THE HANSEN FOUNDATION, INC. v. CITY OF ATLANTIC CITY_njd
case_name: The Hansen Foundation, Inc. v. City of Atlantic City
accommodation_description: The Hansen Foundation operates a group home for women recovering from drug and alcohol addiction, seeking to operate in Atlantic City's R-2 residential zone despite the city's zoning restrictions on group homes.
key_holding: The court denied the City Defendants' motion to dismiss the federal Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act claims, rejecting arguments for abstention; dismissed the NJLAD claim for lack of jurisdiction; and declined to dismiss the NJCRA claim, while also converting Defendant Oliver's motion to summary judgment to consider the settlement agreement defense.
brief_summary: The Hansen Foundation and Hansen House LLC sued the City of Atlantic City, its Zoning Board of Adjustment, and Commissioner Sheila Oliver, alleging the city's zoning enforcement banning group homes in R-2 residential zones violates the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act. The court denied the motion to dismiss the federal claims (FHA, ADA, RHA), dismissed the state NJLAD claim for lack of jurisdiction, and denied the motion to dismiss the NJCRA claim, while converting Oliver's motion to summary judgment to evaluate a settlement agreement defense.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
The Hansen Foundation, Inc. v. City of Atlantic City, 504 F. Supp. 3d 327 (D.N.J. 2020)

source_file: 9808639_2015_Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc._scotus
case_name: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
accommodation_description: ICP alleged the Department's allocation of low-income housing tax credits perpetuated segregated housing patterns by concentrating credits in predominantly black inner-city areas rather than predominantly white suburban areas.
key_holding: The Supreme Court held that disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the FHA, but simultaneously established strong safeguards limiting such claims—requiring plaintiffs to show causation between a specific policy and any statistical disparity, and preventing liability based on facially neutral policies that serve substantial legitimate interests, thereby rejecting the ICP's theory that mere statistical disparities in where low-income housing is built can establish liability.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project sued the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs alleging that its allocation of low-income housing tax credits perpetuated segregation by concentrating too many credits in predominantly black inner-city census tracts and too few in predominantly white suburban areas. The Supreme Court affirmed that disparate-impact claims are cognizable under the Fair Housing Act, but held that ICP's claims must be evaluated under the robust causation requirement and limited safeguards the Court announced, effectively reinstating dismissal of the case.
key_cases_cited: Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
Smith v. City of Jackson, 544 U.S. 228 (2005)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Huntington v. Huntington Branch, 488 U.S. 15 (1988)
Wards Cove Packing Co. v. Atonio, 490 U.S. 642 (1989)

source_file: 9808640_2015_Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc._scotus
case_name: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
accommodation_description: This case does not involve a reasonable accommodation request; it concerns whether the Fair Housing Act authorizes disparate-impact claims challenging facially neutral state policies that result in racial segregation in affordable housing site selection.
key_holding: The Court of Appeals erred in applying heightened scrutiny to the state's asserted racial benign motives, but the FHA does authorize disparate-impact claims; the case is remanded for application of proper standards.
brief_summary: Inclusive Communities Project sued the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs claiming that its method of allocating Low-Income Housing Tax Credits perpetuated racial segregation by concentrating affordable housing in predominantly Black neighborhoods. The Supreme Court affirmed that the Fair Housing Act authorizes disparate-impact claims under § 3604(a), but remanded for the Fifth Circuit to apply proper causation standards rather than heightened scrutiny.
key_cases_cited: Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
Smith v. City of Jackson, 544 U.S. 228 (2005)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Ricci v. DeStefano, 557 U.S. 557 (2009)
Parents Involved in Community Schools v. Seattle School Dist. No. 1, 551 U.S. 701 (2007)

source_file: 9810891_2023_THE HANSEN FOUNDATION, INC. v. CITY OF ATLANTIC CITY_njd
case_name: The Hansen Foundation, Inc. v. City of Atlantic City
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs seek reasonable accommodation from Atlantic City's zoning requirements to permit operation of a Cooperative Sober Living Residence for women recovering from substance addiction, including a Certificate of Land Use Compliance.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for Defendant Holmes based on claim preclusion, finding that Plaintiffs' current Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act reasonable accommodation claims arose from the same transactions as prior litigation and were barred by the August 24, 2021 settlement agreement which released all claims relating to Plaintiffs' CSLRs that were or could have been asserted.
brief_summary: The Hansen Foundation and Hansen House LLC, operators of a sober living home for women in addiction recovery, sued Atlantic City, its Zoning Board, and the New Jersey Department of Community Affairs under the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act, alleging the City refused to grant reasonable accommodations from zoning requirements. The court granted summary judgment for Defendant Holmes based on claim preclusion, finding that the reasonable accommodation claims were already resolved by a prior settlement agreement and could have been asserted in the earlier litigation.
key_cases_cited: Toscano v. Conn. Gen. Life Ins. Co., 288 F. App'x 36 (3d Cir. 2008)
Gotthelf v. Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc., 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 62045 (D.N.J. May 3, 2012)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Marino v. Indus. Crating Co., 358 F.3d 241 (3d Cir. 2004)

case_name: Jessica Berk and William Henry Kennedy v. Ritz Carlton Condominium Association, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Berk requested to keep an emotional support cat named Gemini as a reasonable accommodation for her disabilities including mobility limitations and neurological conditions causing blackouts.
key_holding: The court dismissed most claims including the standalone FHA claims (Counts VIII and IX) for failure to state a viable claim, but permitted Plaintiff Berk's failure to accommodate claim under Count XI to proceed, along with interference claims under 42 U.S.C. § 3617.
brief_summary: Jessica Berk, a disabled Jewish woman who uses a cane or wheelchair and has an emotional support animal, sued the Ritz Carlton Condominium Association and fellow resident Stanley Maragoudakis alleging disability discrimination, harassment, and failure to accommodate. The court dismissed most claims including standalone FHA claims but allowed the failure to accommodate and interference claims under § 3617 to proceed against the Association.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Powell v. Indep. Blue Cross, Inc.
Livingstone v. Hugo Boss Store, Atl. City, NJ
Butler v. Sundo Cap., LLC

source_file: 9812208_2024_1940 ROUTE 9, LLC v. TOWNSHIP OF TOMS RIVER, NEW JERSEY_njd
case_name: 1940 Route 9, LLC v. Township of Toms River, New Jersey
accommodation_description: This is a discovery dispute regarding the Township's preparation of a Rule 30(b)(6) designee to testify about the Township's Fair Housing Policy and practices; no specific reasonable accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to compel a continued deposition because the plaintiff's deposition topics were not noticed with reasonable particularity and the Township's designees were adequately prepared to answer questions within the scope of the properly noticed topics.
brief_summary: 1940 Route 9, LLC, a developer seeking to build housing for Orthodox Jewish families, sued the Township of Toms River alleging the Township improperly used eminent domain to prevent the development, in violation of the Fair Housing Act, RLUIPA, and constitutional rights. The court denied the plaintiff's motion to compel a continued Rule 30(b)(6) deposition, finding the Township's designees were adequately prepared and that many of the topics plaintiff sought to explore were not noticed with sufficient particularity.

source_file: 9812229_2024_OXFORD HOUSE, INC. v. TOWNSHIP OF NORTH BERGEN_njd
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House, a substance abuse recovery residence operator, sought a Certificate of Occupancy to operate a group home for recovering addicts at 1109 8th Street, which the Township denied based on zoning ordinance interpretation.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because Plaintiff's Amended Complaint failed to plead sufficient factual allegations from which a reasonable inference of discriminatory intent could be drawn, consisting only of conclusory allegations that the Township denied a Certificate of Occupancy for discriminatory reasons.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc., an operator of substance abuse recovery residences, sued Township of North Bergen after the Township denied its Certificate of Occupancy application to operate a six-person group home at 1109 8th Street, allegedly based on the Township's interpretation that the proposed use violated the New Jersey Community Residence statute. The court granted the Township's motion to dismiss, finding that the Amended Complaint failed to allege sufficient factual matter to support a plausible inference of discriminatory intent, consisting only of conclusory allegations without specific factual support.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Oxford House, Inc. v. Twp. of N. Bergen, No. 22-2336 (3d Cir. July 28, 2023)

case_name: Johnny Levelt Burnside v. Monarch Real Estate Corporation
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; Plaintiff alleges race discrimination in rental housing (refusal to rent after making a bona fide offer and paying a deposit).
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff's complaint deficient for failure to state a discrimination claim because it does not contain factual allegations showing that race, color, religion, sex, familial status, or national origin were a factor in Defendants' decision to deny Plaintiff the opportunity to rent the unit.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Johnny Levelt Burnside sued Monarch Real Estate Corporation, Adam Marrujo, and Bradley Berglund alleging they discriminated against him in violation of the Fair Housing Act when he attempted to rent an apartment unit. The court found the complaint deficient because it failed to allege facts showing discriminatory intent and did not explain what each defendant did, when, how it harmed him, or what specific legal right was violated.
key_cases_cited: Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)
Caddy v. J.P. Morgan Chase Bank, 237 Fed. Appx. 343 (10th Cir. 2007)
Nasious v. Two Unknown B.I.C.E. Agents, 492 F.3d 1158 (10th Cir. 2007)
Yang v. Archuleta, 525 F.3d 925 (10th Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9817149_2022_Cruz v. Board of County Commissioners of the County of Bernalillo_nmd
case_name: Antonise Cruz v. Board of County Commissioners of the County of Bernalillo and Bernalillo County Housing Department
accommodation_description: Request for 90-day reinstatement of expired Section 8 housing voucher as reasonable accommodation for organic brain syndrome and learning disorder
key_holding: The court denied preliminary injunction because Plaintiff failed to establish likelihood of success on the merits, finding no causal connection between her disability and voucher expiration—the evidence showed expiration resulted from her delay in searching for housing, not from disability-related impairment.
brief_summary: Antonise Cruz, a disabled mother of three with organic brain syndrome and learning disorder, sued the Bernalillo County Housing Department after her Section 8 voucher expired, alleging Defendants failed to reasonably accommodate her disability by denying her requests for voucher extension or reinstatement. The court denied Plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction, finding she failed to establish likelihood of success on the merits because the evidence showed her voucher expired due to her own delay in searching for housing, not due to her disability.
key_cases_cited: Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. St. George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
J.V. v. Albuquerque Pub. Sch., 813 F.3d 1289 (10th Cir. 2016)
Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Denver Homeless Out Loud v. Denver, 32 F.4th 1259 (10th Cir. 2022)
McNeill v. NYC Housing Auth., 719 F. Supp. 233 (S.D.N.Y. 1989)

case_name: Johnny Levelt Burnside v. Monarch Real Estate Corp.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged race-based discrimination in rental and eviction practices, including refusal to rent, denial of $500 payment, and retaliatory eviction.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claims because his conclusory allegation that Defendant Monarch 'doesn't like the color of my skin' was insufficient under Bell Atlantic v. Twombly and Hall v. Bellmon standards, as the complaint lacked factual allegations showing Plaintiff's race was a motivating factor in Defendants' actions.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Johnny Levelt Burnside, a Black man proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Monarch Real Estate Corp., its owner, and agents alleging race-based housing discrimination under the FHA, Section 1983, Section 1982, and state law claims arising from an alleged refusal to rent and retaliatory eviction. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state claims, finding Plaintiff's conclusory allegation that Defendants discriminated based on race was insufficient without supporting factual averments, and granted Plaintiff leave to amend within 21 days.
key_cases_cited: Hall v. Bellmon, 935 F.2d 1106 (10th Cir. 1991)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Woods-Drake v. Lundy, 667 F.2d 1198 (5th Cir. 1982)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Johnny Levelt Burnside v. Monarch Real Estate Corp.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged defendant refused to rent and evicted him because of his race.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed for failure to state a claim because the plaintiff provided no factual, non-conclusory allegations that his race, color, religion, sex, handicap, familial status, or national origin motivated the defendants' actions.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Johnny Levelt Burnside sued Monarch Real Estate Corp., its owner, and its agent alleging they refused to rent housing to him and evicted him from another property because of his race, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The Court dismissed the FHA claim under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) for failure to state a claim because plaintiff provided no factual, non-conclusory allegations establishing discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Schaffer v. Salt Lake City Corp., 814 F.3d 1151 (10th Cir. 2016)
Webb v. Caldwell, 640 Fed.Appx. 800 (10th Cir. 2016)

source_file: 9820177_2019_Hillery v. Sun City Anthem Community Association Inc_nvd
case_name: Hillery v. Sun City Anthem Community Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Mrs. Hillery, who has dementia, requested reasonable accommodation to access SCA community amenities without being required to bring her own caregiver, and the community allegedly failed to provide meaningful accommodations before banning her.
key_holding: The court struck seven of eight expert opinions as inadmissible legal conclusions or beyond the expert's qualifications, leaving only the opinion that SCA's single accommodation offer (requiring Mrs. Hillery to bring her own caregiver) was not meaningful.
brief_summary: Thomas Hillery, as Guardian Ad Litem for his mother Mary Jane Hillery who has dementia, sued Sun City Anthem Community Association alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to provide reasonable accommodation for her disability and subsequently banning her from community amenities. The court ruled on a motion to strike the plaintiffs' expert, striking seven of eight opinions as inadmissible legal conclusions or beyond the expert's qualifications, while admitting only the opinion that SCA's accommodation offer was not meaningful.
key_cases_cited: Barabin v. Asent Johnson, Inc., 740 F.3d 457 (9th Cir. 2014)
Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharm., Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Hangarter v. Provident Life & Acc. Ins. Co., 373 F.3d 998 (9th Cir. 2004)
Primiano v. Cook, 598 F.3d 558 (9th Cir. 2010)
Kumho Tire Co. v. Carmichael, 526 U.S. 137 (1999)

case_name: Fulkerson v. The Hartford
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made vague references to Fair Housing Act violations and housing discrimination against landlord Jeff Pheasant but provided no factual allegations describing any specific accommodation request.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed with leave to amend because Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts to state any viable claim, including failing to allege factual basis for Fair Housing Act discrimination or facts supporting insurance bad faith.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Heath Fulkerson filed an in forma pauperis complaint against The Hartford (insurance company) and Jeff Pheasant (landlord) alleging insurance bad faith claims and Fair Housing Act violations. The court granted IFP status but dismissed the complaint with leave to amend for failure to state claims upon which relief may be granted, finding Plaintiff's factual allegations insufficient to support either bad faith or discrimination claims.

case_name: Torres v. Rothstein
accommodation_description: N/A - case involves sexual harassment (quid pro quo and hostile environment) rather than a reasonable accommodation request
key_holding: The court denied summary judgment for plaintiff on sexual harassment claims under § 3604(b) and (c) because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the Direct Consent form was conditioned on rental, but granted summary judgment on defendants' affirmative defenses of lack of knowledge and waiver based on liability release forms.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Candy Torres sued property manager Allan Rothstein and owner Kyle Puntney alleging Rothstein sexually harassed her and required her to sign a Direct Consent for Sexual Intercourse form as a condition of renting a home subsidized by her Section 8 voucher. The court denied Torres's motion for summary judgment on her sexual harassment claims under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) and (c) because genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether the rental was conditioned on signing the form, but granted summary judgment on defendants' affirmative defenses of lack of knowledge and waiver based on liability release forms.
key_cases_cited: Burlington Indus., Inc. v. Ellerth, 524 U.S. 742 (1998)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

case_name: Aimee O'Neil v. Jovi Salas, et al.
accommodation_description: Complaint too vague to identify specific accommodation requested; plaintiff only alleges general disability discrimination without specifying what accommodation was denied.
key_holding: Complaint dismissed without prejudice for failure to state a plausible claim and noncompliance with Rule 8, as plaintiff failed to allege specific factual details about the alleged discrimination, did not identify where or when discrimination occurred, and failed to specify whether the FHA claim is based on disparate treatment, disparate impact, or reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Aimee O'Neil, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Jovi Salas and others for disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, alleging defendants engaged in a pattern of discrimination in rental dwellings based on disability. The court granted leave to amend but dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failing to state a plausible claim and failing to comply with Rule 8, as the complaint lacked specific factual allegations and did not identify the legal theory (disparate treatment, disparate impact, or reasonable accommodation) pursued.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)
Cato v. United States, 70 F.3d 1103 (9th Cir. 1995)

source_file: 9827432_2021_Carpenter v. Southern Nevada Regional Housing Authority_nvd
case_name: Phyllis J. Carpenter v. Southern Nevada Regional Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's rambling complaint references ADA compliance issues and apartment repair problems but does not articulate a specific accommodation request; the court did not identify any discrete accommodation demand.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim because Carpenter failed to allege any discriminatory housing practice under 42 U.S.C. §§ 3604, 3605, 3606, or 3617—she merely listed statutes without providing factual allegations showing how the defendant violated them.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Phyllis J. Carpenter, who both resided in and worked for Southern Nevada Regional Housing Authority, filed a rambling complaint alleging violations of the FHA, ADA, HUD regulations, Title VII, and slander without providing factual allegations supporting any claim. The court granted her in forma pauperis application but dismissed her complaint without prejudice, finding her filings failed to state plausible claims for relief under the Twombly/Iqbal pleading standard.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 9827845_2021_Seelig v. Old Vegas Manor and Estates Homeowners Association_nvd
case_name: Stephen M. Seelig and Vikki L. Seelig v. Old Vegas Manor and Estates Homeowners Association, et al.
accommodation_description: Associational discrimination claim based on denial of access to common elements in Mr. Seelig's presence due to his disability; no specific accommodation request beyond access to pedestrian gate and common areas.
key_holding: The Seeligs plausibly allege ADA public accommodation and FHA associational discrimination claims based on denial of access to common elements, but many other claims (IIED, contract, derivative claims) were dismissed for failure to state a claim or plead sufficient facts.
brief_summary: Stephen and Vikki Seelig sued Old Vegas Manor and Estates HOA and related defendants alleging 34 claims arising from neighborhood disputes, including claims that defendants obstructed access to common elements including a pedestrian gate, violating the ADA and FHA. The court granted in part motions to dismiss and for judgment on the pleadings, dismissing most claims (IIED, contract, derivative) but allowing ADA and FHA associational discrimination claims to proceed based on Mrs. Seelig's standing to sue for discrimination based on her husband's disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Kenneth O'Rourke, et al. v. Kim VanHeel, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs request to retain designated service dogs without being charged fines for failure to provide proof of service dog certification.
key_holding: The court found serious questions going to the merits of plaintiffs' FHA reasonable accommodation claim regarding service dogs and granted a TRO vacating eviction orders and prohibiting reinitiating eviction during the pendency of the order.
brief_summary: Kenneth O'Rourke, Charleen Kurr-O'Rourke, and Kassidy O'Rourke sued Palm Hills HOA and related defendants alleging violation of the Fair Housing Act for refusing to accommodate their service dogs and charging fines. The court granted a temporary restraining order finding serious questions on the merits of the reasonable accommodation claim and vacated the eviction orders pending resolution.
key_cases_cited: Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Sanzaro v. Ardiente Homeowners Ass'n, LLC, 364 F. Supp. 3d 1158 (D. Nev. 2019)
Alliance For The Wild Rockies v. Cottrell, 632 F.3d 1127 (9th Cir. 2011)
Park Vill. Apt. Tenants Ass'n v. Mortimer Howard Trust, 636 F.3d 1150 (9th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Ronald Eric Kandell v. SUR 702, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to keep a service animal in the rental property without being charged a $300 pet fee after refusing to provide medical records to verify the disability-related need for the animal.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing plaintiff's FHA disability discrimination claim because he failed to plead sufficient facts demonstrating he is disabled within the meaning of 42 U.S.C. § 3602(h) or that his use of a service animal is a necessary accommodation to afford him equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Ronald Eric Kandell, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued his apartment property manager and related entities alleging discrimination based on sexuality and disability under the Fair Housing Act, including claims that defendants charged him a $300 pet fee for his service animal after he refused to provide medical records. The court recommended dismissing the FHA claims because plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts establishing he is disabled or that his service animal is a necessary accommodation, despite referencing conditions including stress, seizures, epilepsy, and PTSD.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua
Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Ass'n
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condominium Ass'n, Inc.

case_name: Ronald A. Brown, Sr., et al. v. American Homes 4 Rent, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Ronald Brown, who suffered a stroke leaving him disabled, requested an elongated toilet and a left-sided banister in the stairway of his rental unit as reasonable accommodations for his mobility disability.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissing the FHA reasonable accommodation claim against American Homes 4 Rent as time-barred, since the accommodation denial occurred in early 2020 but the complaint was filed in April 2023—more than two years later—without sufficient allegations of a continuing violation to reset the statute of limitations.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Ronald and Tonya Brown sued American Homes 4 Rent and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations after their requests for structural accommodations—an elongated toilet and left-sided stairway banister—for Mr. Brown's stroke-related mobility disability were denied. The district court recommended dismissing the FHA claim against AHR without prejudice as time-barred because the alleged denial occurred in early 2020 but the complaint was filed in April 2023, beyond the two-year limitations period, while also dismissing individual defendant Singelyn with prejudice under principles of vicarious liability.
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Kevin Alexander & Alma Alexander v. The Croix Townhomes LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; plaintiffs alleged discrimination based on age and/or familial status when defendant made allegedly defamatory statements and took adverse housing actions.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' § 3604(b) housing discrimination claim with prejudice because their conclusory allegations failed to establish a prima facie case—they provided no factual allegations showing defendants' adverse housing actions were motivated by plaintiffs' protected status.
brief_summary: Kevin and Alma Alexander sued The Croix Townhomes LLC and employee Lizbeth Estrada in Nevada state court, alleging housing discrimination based on age and/or familial status under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) after Estrada allegedly made statements to a state services worker about the Alexanders. The federal court sua sponte dismissed the housing discrimination claim with prejudice, finding the complaint contained only conclusory allegations without factual support for a prima facie case of discrimination, then remanded the remaining state-law claims to Nevada state court.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9836005_2024_Perkins Jr. v. Panorama Towers Condo Unit Owners, Dennis Kariger_nvd
case_name: Preston D. Perkins, Jr. v. Panorama Towers Condominium Complex, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's specific accommodation request is not discernible from the complaint's general allegations regarding housing issues including mold, pest infestations, and air conditioning problems.
key_holding: The court granted motions to dismiss for lack of subject matter jurisdiction, finding plaintiff failed to establish federal question jurisdiction because his FHA claim lacked allegations that his rights were protected under the FHA, that he was discriminated against based on protected status, or that his injuries resulted from discriminatory conduct.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Preston D. Perkins, Jr. filed suit against Panorama Towers Condominium Complex, property management, and other defendants alleging various claims related to housing conditions. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss with prejudice, finding the court lacked subject matter jurisdiction because plaintiff failed to plead a viable federal claim, including failing to state a prima facie case under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

source_file: 9836268_2019_Koso v. St. Josephs Senior Housing Village_nyed
case_name: Linda Koso v. St. Joseph's Senior Housing Village and Stanan Management
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges she was denied an apartment at a senior housing facility because of her physical disability.
