key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because she failed to plausibly allege she belonged to a protected class (her unspecified physical disability was conclusory) and failed to allege facts suggesting discriminatory motivation for the denial.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Linda Koso sued St. Joseph's Senior Housing Village and Stanan Management alleging she was denied an apartment because of her physical disability in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to plausibly allege facts establishing she was a member of a protected class or that discriminatory motivation caused the denial of housing.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Boykin v. KeyCorp, 521 F.3d 202 (2d Cir. 2008)
Vega v. Hempstead Union Free Sch. Dist., 801 F.3d 72 (2d Cir. 2015)
Sybalski v. Indep. Grp. Home Living Program, Inc., 546 F.3d 255 (2d Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9836490_2019_Village Green at Sayville, LLC v. The Town of Islip_nyed
case_name: Village Green at Sayville, LLC v. The Town of Islip
accommodation_description: Developer alleges Town of Islip refused to approve rezoning and C&R amendments for a 59-unit housing development while approving a similar application for a project in a minority neighborhood, based on racial animus from residents.
key_holding: The court granted Defendants' Rule 12(b)(1) motion to dismiss Plaintiff's FHA claim for lack of standing because Plaintiff failed to establish a final decision—there was no permit denial or rezoning, only a failed motion and non-vote on Plaintiff's C&R amendment application, and a pending Article 78 proceeding in state court had not yet resolved the application.
brief_summary: Village Green at Sayville, LLC, a real estate developer, sued the Town of Islip and its officials alleging the Town discriminated against racial minorities by blocking the developer's attempts to build a 59-unit housing development in Sayville while approving a similar development in the minority neighborhood of Brentwood. The court dismissed the FHA claim for lack of standing, finding no final decision had been made on the developer's C&R amendment application since the Town Board never voted and the issue was pending in state court.
key_cases_cited: Williamson County Regional Planning Commission v. Hamilton Bank, 473 U.S. 172 (1985)
Knick v. Township of Scott, 139 S. Ct. 2162 (2019)
Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Anderson v. Town of Orwell, 693 F. Supp. 2d 182 (N.D.N.Y. 2010)
Colorado River Water Conservation Dist. v. U.S., 424 U.S. 800 (1976)

source_file: 9838610_2020_Ortega v. Samaritan Village Myrtle Ave. Men's Shelter_nyed
case_name: Ivan Fernando Ortega v. Samaritan Village Myrtle Ave. Men's Shelter
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested placement in the 'Skyway' shelter program, which he believed was better suited to manage his disabilities, rather than his current shelter placement.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state an FHA claim because he did not establish that he suffers from a disability as defined by the FHA, nor did he show that his current housing placement failed to accommodate his disabilities or denied him any services because of his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Ivan Fernando Ortega, a homeless individual claiming to be 100% disabled, sued a NYC men's shelter, its director, and city agencies seeking placement in a different shelter program allegedly better suited to manage his disabilities. The court dismissed the amended complaint for failure to state a claim under the FHA, ADA, and Section 1983, finding that plaintiff failed to establish he suffers from a qualifying disability and failed to allege facts showing he was denied benefits or accommodations because of any disability.
key_cases_cited: Jenkins v. New York City Dep't of Homeless Servs., 643 F. Supp. 2d 507 (S.D.N.Y. 2009)
Reaves v. Dep't of Veterans Affairs, 2009 WL 35074 (E.D.N.Y. Jan. 6, 2009)
DeShaney v. Winnebago County Dep't of Social Servs., 489 U.S. 189 (1989)
Salahuddin v. Cuomo, 861 F.2d 40 (2d Cir. 1988)

source_file: 9838640_2020_Brown v. Fire Department of the City of New York_nyed
case_name: Brown v. Fire Department of the City of New York
accommodation_description: African American and Afro-Costa Rican tenants allege harassment and pressure to vacate rent-regulated apartments based on race.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' hostile housing environment claim because conclusory allegations of race-based harassment failed to establish discriminatory motive, and dismissed the blockbusting claim because homeless individuals are not a protected class under the FHA.
brief_summary: Five African American and Afro-Costa Rican tenants sued their landlords and the City of New York alleging Fair Housing Act claims for hostile housing environment and blockbusting based on race-based harassment to force them from rent-regulated apartments. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to adequately plead discriminatory motive and that blockbusting claims cannot be brought against landlords inducing tenants to vacate rent-regulated apartments.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Francis v. Kings Park Manor
Cain v. Rambert
Gladstone, Realtors v. Bellwood

source_file: 9838872_2023_Reese v. Park Place Condominium Homeowners Association I_dcd
case_name: Allison Reese v. Park Place Condominium Homeowners Association I
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case involves claim that PPCA denied Reese use of its master insurance policy for foundation repairs while allowing white unit owners to make similar repairs.
key_holding: Reese's race discrimination claim survives because she adequately alleged that PPCA treated her differently from white unit owners in allowing access to the master insurance policy for foundation repairs, while her retaliation claim fails because reporting fraud and waste of association funds is not a protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Allison Reese, an African-American condominium owner, sued Park Place Condominium Homeowners Association and its property manager alleging race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 3605 and retaliation under 42 U.S.C. § 3617 for denying her access to the master insurance policy for foundation repairs while allowing white owners to use the same policy. The court granted MCM's motion to dismiss as time-barred, dismissed Reese's retaliation claim for failure to allege protected activity, but denied PPCA's motion to dismiss on the discrimination claim, finding Reese stated a plausible claim that PPCA treated her differently based on race.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Yan Ping Xu v. Suffolk County
accommodation_description: Plaintiff appears to challenge her eviction from property at 12 Mallar Avenue, Bay Shore, NY, claiming various federal rights violations including national origin discrimination
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration because Rooker-Feldman bars federal review of state court judgments; additionally, Plaintiff's federal claims fail on the merits as they are barred by qualified immunity, Monell requirements are not met, and Plaintiff's Fourth Amendment and due process claims lack merit.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Yan Ping Xu, a Chinese national, sued Suffolk County, its Sheriff's Office, and various individuals challenging her eviction from property pursuant to state court orders. The court denied her motion for reconsideration of the dismissal, holding that Rooker-Feldman bars federal review of her claims since her injuries were produced by state court judgments, and alternatively that her federal claims under Section 1983, the Fourth Amendment, and Due Process fail on the merits.
key_cases_cited: Sung Cho v. City of New York, 910 F.3d 639 (2d Cir. 2018)
Rooker-Feldman doctrine
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of N.Y., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9840258_2021_Long Island Housing Services, Inc. v. NPS Holiday Square LLC_nyed
case_name: Long Island Housing Services, Inc. v. NPS Holiday Square LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs challenge NPS's income-to-rent requirement as having an unlawful disparate impact on housing voucher users with disabilities, seeking injunctive and damages relief under the FHA.
key_holding: The court admitted expert Nancy McArdle's disparate impact methodology as reliable under Daubert, finding that controlling for income level in a disparate impact analysis would improperly convert the claim into a disparate treatment analysis; the court excluded expert Michael Salve's competing methodology as unreliable, and admitted expert John Rollins's testimony regarding NPS's business necessity defense.
brief_summary: Fair housing organizations and individuals with disabilities sued NPS Property Corp. and affiliated entities alleging that NPS's income-to-rent screening policy had an unlawful disparate impact on housing voucher users, including those with disabilities, in violation of the FHA, NYSHRL, and SCHRL. The court ruled on multiple Daubert motions, admitting plaintiffs' expert McArdle's statistical analysis showing disparate impact while excluding defendants' expert Salve's methodology as improperly converting a disparate impact claim into a disparate treatment analysis, and admitting defendants' expert Rollins on business necessity.
key_cases_cited: Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharms., Inc., 509 U.S. 579 (1993)
Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
Dothard v. Rawlinson, 433 U.S. 321 (1977)

case_name: Charles Didier Calixte v. Susan Ray Equities Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged race discrimination arising from refusal to transfer lease and lockout, with no accommodation request analyzed.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed because plaintiff failed to allege any direct evidence of race discrimination or minimal facts to support an inference of discriminatory intent under the McDonnell Douglas framework, and plaintiff's Section 1983 claim failed for lack of state action.
brief_summary: Charles Didier Calixte sued Susan Ray Equities Inc. alleging race discrimination based on the landlord's refusal to transfer his lease and subsequent lockout. The court dismissed the complaint for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction, finding that Calixte failed to state a viable Fair Housing Act claim because he offered no direct evidence of discriminatory intent and his conclusory allegations about his race and the defendant's ownership did not establish a prima facie case of discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better Env't, 523 U.S. 83 (1998)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
National Collegiate Athletic Ass'n v. Tarkanian, 488 U.S. 179 (1988)

source_file: 9841875_2022_Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. 203 Jay St. Associates, LLC_nyed
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. 203 Jay St. Associates, LLC
accommodation_description: This is a design and construction accessibility claim under § 3604(f)(3)(C), not a reasonable accommodation claim; testers and an accessibility expert observed physical inaccessibility features (heavy doors, narrow interiors, excessive slopes, beveled thresholds, inaccessible reach ranges) at a condominium building that allegedly fails to comply with FHA accessibility requirements.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, holding that (1) FHJC has organizational standing based on resource diversion from testing investigations, and (2) a plaintiff need not allege an individual disabled person was denied housing to state a design and construction violation—tester observations of inaccessible architectural features suffice to establish a plausible FHA claim under § 3604(f)(3)(C).
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc., a fair housing organization, sued property owners, a property management company, and an architectural firm alleging that the 203 Jay Street Condominium in Brooklyn was designed and constructed in violation of the FHA's accessibility requirements, as observed by testers and an accessibility expert who documented heavy doors, narrow interiors, excessive slopes, and other architectural barriers. The court denied defendants' motions to dismiss, holding that FHJC has organizational standing based on resource diversion and that design and construction violations can be established through tester observations without requiring an individual disabled person to have attempted to rent.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman
S.C. Bodner Co.
Garcia v. Brockway
Smith v. Pacific Props. and Dev. Corp.
Fair Hous. Justice Ctr., Inc. v. JDS Dev. LLC

source_file: 9842866_2023_Levy v. Lawrence Gardens Apartments Del, LLC_nyed
case_name: David Levy v. Lawrence Gardens Apartments Del, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendants provide designated handicapped parking, maintain elevators and automatic doors, and provide 24-hour access to accessible lobby entrances as accommodations for his mobility disability requiring a walker.
key_holding: The reasonable accommodation claim fails because Levy failed to plead that he made specific requests for handicapped parking, elevator/door maintenance, or 24-hour lobby access and was refused; while such policies are potentially challengeable under § 3604(f)(3)(B), a plaintiff must allege the defendant refused to accommodate a request.
brief_summary: David Levy, a tenant who uses a walker, sued Lawrence Gardens Apartments and related entities alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to accommodate his disability and creating a hostile housing environment, plus ADA and retaliation claims. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that while Levy's accessibility complaints about parking, elevators, and lobby access could potentially state a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), he failed to plead that he actually requested these accommodations and was refused them.
key_cases_cited: Reyes v. Fairfield Properties, 661 F. Supp. 2d 249 (E.D.N.Y. 2009)
Dinapoli v. DPA Wallace Ave II, LLC, 2009 WL 755354 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 23, 2009)
Favourite v. 55 Halley St., Inc., 381 F. Supp. 3d 266 (S.D.N.Y. 2019)
Cain v. Rambert, 2014 WL 2440596 (E.D.N.Y. May 30, 2014)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Thomas v. Genova
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation requested; case involves alleged race-based selective enforcement in code prosecution for using property as two-family home.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiffs' FHA claim on procedural grounds (improper amendment) and, alternatively, granted summary judgment because Plaintiffs failed to establish discriminatory motive—Plaintiffs admitted the code enforcement officer did not know Mr. Thomas's race and presented only conclusory allegations of discrimination unsupported by evidence.
brief_summary: Black and Puerto Rican homeowners sued the Town of Oyster Bay and its officials alleging race-based selective enforcement and malicious prosecution for code violations related to their two-family home use. The court dismissed the FHA claim as procedurally improper (added in violation of a prior order) and alternatively granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiffs failed to establish discriminatory motive, admitting the code enforcement officer did not know Mr. Thomas's race and presenting only conclusory allegations without evidentiary support.
key_cases_cited: Haber v. ASN 50th St. LLC, 847 F. Supp. 2d 578 (S.D.N.Y. 2012)
273 Lee Ave. Tenants Ass'n by Sanchez v. Steinmetz, 330 F. Supp. 3d 778 (E.D.N.Y. 2018)
Mitchell v. Shane, 350 F.3d 39 (2d Cir. 2003)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
McCarthy v. Dun & Bradstreet Corp., 482 F.3d 184 (2d Cir. 2007)

source_file: 9845340_2019_UMH Properties, Inc. v. Village of Coxsackie_nynd
case_name: UMH Properties, Inc. et al. v. Village of Coxsackie et al.
accommodation_description: The accommodation request is not described in this summary order.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' third cause of action under N.Y. C.P.L.R. 3001 because that statute does not apply to federal court proceedings, dismissed the fourth cause of action under Article 78 as untimely, and dismissed all claims against the Village of Coxsackie Board of Trustees for lack of capacity to be sued.
brief_summary: UMH Properties, a manufactured housing community operator, sued the Village of Coxsackie and its officials under the Fair Housing Act and state law, challenging zoning or land-use decisions affecting its property. The court granted defendants' partial motion for judgment on the pleadings, dismissing plaintiffs' state law claims and all claims against the Board of Trustees, while declining to substantively analyze the FHA claims at this procedural stage.
key_cases_cited: Buck v. Libous, 2005 WL 1460408 (N.D.N.Y. 2005)
Riano v. Town of Schroeppel, 2013 WL 5702263 (N.D.N.Y. 2013)
Willard v. Town of Hamburg, 1996 WL 607100 (W.D.N.Y. 1996)

case_name: Doe v. YWCA of Northeastern NY
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a survivor of domestic violence with documented PTSD, sought to prevent eviction and lease non-renewal as an accommodation for her disability, claiming defendants knew of her disability but ignored her requests.
key_holding: The court granted Dozer Blaze's motion to dismiss the FHA claims because Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts creating a plausible inference that discriminatory animus based on her PTSD disability motivated her eviction and harassment.
brief_summary: Jane Doe, a Black immigrant survivor of domestic violence with PTSD, sued Dozer Blaze Properties and YWCA of Northeastern NY alleging disability discrimination after she was subjected to eviction proceedings and harassment following a death threat from her spouse. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that while Plaintiff adequately alleged membership in a protected class, she failed to plead sufficient facts creating a plausible inference that discriminatory motive based on her disability motivated her eviction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Mitchell v. Shane, 350 F.3d 39 (2d Cir. 2003)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 944 F.3d 370 (2d Cir. 2019)

case_name: Timmons v. Kingsley-Johnston, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to continue using her motorized scooter on the lawn between her apartment patio and the parking lot as her primary means of egress.
key_holding: The court found genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether allowing scooter use on the lawn constituted a reasonable accommodation (not imposing undue hardship) and whether plaintiff provided adequate assurances for the sidewalk modification, while also finding triable issues on the retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Genevieve Timmons sued her property management company and landlords alleging they violated the FHA by refusing to accommodate her mobility disability (osteoarthritis, degenerative disc disease) that required use of a motorized scooter, and by refusing to permit installation of a sidewalk as a reasonable modification; she also alleged retaliation for requesting accommodations. The court denied defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether allowing scooter use on the lawn was reasonable given liability concerns, whether plaintiff's proposed sidewalk modification was adequately described, and whether the landlord's decision not to renew the lease, inspections, and code enforcement complaint were retaliatory.
key_cases_cited: Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
Austin v. Town of Farmington, 826 F.3d 622 (2d Cir. 2016)
McElwee v. Cty. of Orange, 700 F.3d 635 (2d Cir. 2012)
Warren v. Delvista Towers Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 49 F. Supp. 3d 1082 (S.D. Fla. 2014)
Tsombanidis v. City of W. Haven, 180 F. Supp. 2d 262 (D. Conn. 2001)

source_file: 9847946_2022_CNY Fair Housing, Inc. v. Clover Group Inc._nynd
case_name: CNY Fair Housing, Inc. v. Clover Group Inc.
accommodation_description: Residents with mobility disabilities requested waiver of the $15-$25 monthly rent surcharge charged for first-floor units or units near elevators, which they argued effectively penalized them for needing accessible unit features.
key_holding: The court rejected defendants' argument that the rent surcharge waiver claim was an impermissible 'economic accommodation,' finding the request directly relates to plaintiffs' disabilities because the surcharge penalizes them for needing accessible unit features, unlike cases where economic relief is sought without direct disability nexus.
brief_summary: CNY Fair Housing and individual tenants sued Welltower Inc. and related Clover Group entities for allegedly discriminating against people with mobility disabilities by charging $15-$25 monthly surcharges for first-floor and elevator-proximate units and denying requests for assigned parking spaces. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss Welltower Inc. for lack of personal jurisdiction but denied dismissal of the rent surcharge accommodation claim, holding that requesting a waiver of fees directly tied to disability-related housing needs is a cognizable accommodation under the FHA rather than an impermissible economic accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Salute v. Stratford Greens Garden Apartments, 136 F.3d 293 (2d Cir. 1998)
Bentley v. Peace & Quiet Realty 2 LLC, 367 F. Supp. 2d 341 (E.D.N.Y. 2005)
Hevner v. Village East Towers, Inc., 2011 WL 666340 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 7, 2011)
Giebeler v. M&B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Samuelson v. Mid-Atlantic Realty Co., 947 F. Supp. 756 (D. Del. 1996)

source_file: 9848408_2022_CNY Fair Housing, Inc. v. Swiss Village, LLC_nynd
case_name: CNY Fair Housing, Inc. v. Swiss Village, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request - this is a disparate treatment/disparate impact challenge to defendants' English-only language policy as discriminatory based on national origin and race.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff adequately pleaded claims for intentional discrimination, disparate impact, and discriminatory statements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a)-(c) based on allegations that defendants' English-language rental policy functioned as a proxy for national origin and race discrimination.
brief_summary: CNY Fair Housing, Inc., a fair housing organization, sued Swiss Village, LLC and related defendants alleging that their policy requiring prospective tenants to have an English-speaking occupant in the apartment violated the Fair Housing Act by discriminating based on national origin and race. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint plausibly alleged discriminatory treatment, discriminatory effects, and discriminatory statements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(a)-(c), and that the 2016 HUD Guidance on limited English proficiency was entitled to Skidmore deference.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park LP, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Mhany Mgt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Kevin V. Byng v. Bob Kelly
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a tenant with multiple disabilities including anxiety, depression, PTSD, diabetes, and COPD, sought eviction protection and overnight guest privileges as reasonable accommodations for his disabilities; the court analyzed these primarily as racial discrimination and First Amendment claims rather than disability accommodation claims.
key_holding: The FHA racial discrimination claim survives because the complaint adequately alleges defendant Kelly threatened eviction and restricted overnight guest privileges based on plaintiff's association with African American women; the disability discrimination claim under § 3604(f) was dismissed for failure to adequately plead that defendants' actions were motivated by plaintiff's disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Kevin Byng, a Black man with multiple disabilities, sued the Utica Municipal Housing Authority and its Executive Director alleging racial discrimination and disability discrimination under the FHA, as well as constitutional claims. The court screened the complaint sua sponte, dismissing most claims including disability discrimination for failure to state facts showing discrimination because of disability, but allowed his FHA racial discrimination claim and First Amendment freedom of association claim against defendant Kelly to proceed based on allegations that Kelly threatened eviction and restricted his overnight guest privileges because of his association with Black women.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Davis v. N.Y.C. Hous. Auth., 379 F. Supp. 3d 237 (S.D.N.Y. 2019)
McKenna v. Peekskill Hous. Auth., 647 F.2d 332 (2d Cir. 1981)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2005)

case_name: Weilburg v. Castellane
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged they were unlawfully evicted from their apartment because they are Jehovah's Witnesses, constituting religious discrimination under the FHA.
key_holding: Defendant is entitled to summary judgment because the property qualifies for the Mrs. Murphy exemption under 42 U.S.C. § 3603(b)(2), which excludes owner-occupied dwellings with four or fewer families from FHA coverage, regardless of whether plaintiffs' allegations of religious discrimination are true.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Daro and Maria Weilburg sued landlord Richard Castellane alleging they were unlawfully evicted because they are Jehovah's Witnesses, asserting religious discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The court granted summary judgment for defendant, finding that the property qualifies for the Mrs. Murphy exemption under 42 U.S.C. § 3603(b)(2) because it is owner-occupied with four or fewer families living independently, thereby excluding it from FHA coverage.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
United States v. Space Hunters, Inc., 429 F.3d 416 (2d Cir. 2005)
Gonzalez v. Rakkas, 1995 WL 451034 (E.D.N.Y. July 25, 1995)

case_name: Danielle Hendricks v. Krystal Curley, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges her Section 8 housing voucher was terminated, with FHA/ADA claims premised on disability discrimination.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA and ADA claims were dismissed because she failed to allege sufficient facts demonstrating that her disability (schizophrenia) was the reason for termination of her Section 8 voucher, as the complaint suggested defendants learned of her mental health status only after the voucher was already terminated.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Danielle Hendricks, proceeding pro se and alleging schizophrenia, sued Section 8 employees Krystal Curley and Francis Grant claiming her housing voucher was improperly terminated without a pre-termination hearing and in violation of the FHA and ADA. The court adopted the Report-Recommendation dismissing the FHA and ADA claims without prejudice because plaintiff failed to allege facts sufficient to establish that her disability caused the termination, while allowing her Fourteenth Amendment due process claim to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Martinez by Martinez v. Lexington Gardens Assocs., 336 F. Supp. 3d 270 (S.D.N.Y. 2018)
Perricone-Bernovich v. Tohill, 843 F. App'x 419 (2d Cir. 2021)

source_file: 9849251_2023_Clover Communities Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects P.C._nynd
case_name: Clover Communities Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects P.C.
accommodation_description: Property owners/assignees sue architects for designing multifamily senior housing complexes that allegedly fail to comply with FHA accessibility requirements (accessible routes, bathrooms lacking required clear floor space and grab bar reinforcement, non-compliant mailboxes).
key_holding: The court dismissed the professional malpractice claim as a de facto indemnification claim preempted by the FHA, but allowed the breach of contract claim to proceed as sufficiently distinct from an indemnification claim, and denied the motion regarding assigned FHA claims without prejudice pending further briefing on assignability issues.
brief_summary: Fair housing organizations and later property owners sued architects Marc Mussachio and his firm alleging they designed senior housing complexes that fail to comply with FHA accessibility requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C), leading to settlements with fair housing groups and subsequent claims for contribution, breach of contract, and professional malpractice. The court dismissed the professional malpractice claim as a de facto indemnification claim preempted by the FHA, but allowed breach of contract claims to proceed and reserved judgment on assigned FHA claims pending further briefing on assignability.
key_cases_cited: Equal Rights Center v. Niles Bolton Associates
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 9849699_2024_Clover Communities Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects P.C._nynd
case_name: Clover Communities Beavercreek, LLC v. Mussachio Architects P.C.
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; fair housing organizations assigned their design-and-construction claims under § 3604(f)(3)(C) to property owners who now sue the architects for allegedly designing senior housing complexes without required accessibility features.
key_holding: The court held that FHA claims are assignable under federal law and that the single-satisfaction rule does not bar the assigned claims at the motion to dismiss stage, as factual questions remain regarding whether the original plaintiffs were fully compensated; the court dismissed only the state fair housing claims which plaintiffs abandoned.
brief_summary: Seven fair housing organizations settled claims with property owners regarding alleged FHA design-and-construction violations at senior housing complexes; the property owners then sued the architects for those assigned claims. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that FHA claims are assignable and the single-satisfaction rule does not bar the claims at this stage, while dismissing abandoned state fair housing claims.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Equal Rts. Ctr. v. Niles Bolton Assocs., 602 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 2010)
Perma Life Mufflers, Inc. v. Int'l Parts Corp., 392 U.S. 134 (1968)
In re NYSE Specialists Sec. Litig., 405 F. Supp. 2d 281 (S.D.N.Y. 2005)
Ondimar Transportes Maritimos v. Beatty Street Properties, Inc., 555 F.3d 184 (5th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Rayntha Favourite v. 55 Halley Street, Inc.
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation request was made; the case involves alleged race-based hostile housing environment and retaliatory eviction.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to defendants because plaintiff failed to proffer evidence that the cooperative board's issuance of noise complaints, a Notice to Cure, and Notice to Terminate were motivated by racial animus rather than legitimate non-discriminatory reasons.
brief_summary: Black cooperative shareholder sued the board and neighbors alleging race-based hostile housing environment and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, claiming the board targeted her with noise complaints and eviction notices because of her race. The court granted summary judgment to defendants, finding no evidence of discriminatory animus—the complaints were supported by documented noise issues and marijuana use, and plaintiff failed to show pretext.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Products, Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000)
Cain v. Rambert, 2014 WL 2440596 (E.D.N.Y. 2014)
United States v. Weisz, 914 F. Supp. 1050 (S.D.N.Y. 1996)

source_file: 9850350_2019_Harris v. The Yonkers Department of Social Services_OTDA_OAH_nysd
case_name: Rhonda L. Harris v. The Yonkers Department of Social Services/OTDA/OAH
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff requested housing accommodation including hotel placement or five-bedroom apartment for herself and four children, stating they are disabled and need unrestricted, unsupervised housing.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claim because plaintiff failed to allege facts suggesting any defendant discriminated against her on the basis of disability, rather than merely finding that she was entitled to reasonable accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Rhonda L. Harris sued the Yonkers Department of Social Services, OTDA, and OAH alleging she and her four children (identified as disabled) were illegally evicted and denied housing and public benefits, seeking emergency shelter and housing accommodations. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding that while plaintiff identified herself and children as disabled, she did not allege facts showing defendants discriminated against them because of their disabilities, granting leave to amend within 30 days.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)

case_name: Thompson v. NYC
accommodation_description: Disabled plaintiff Thompson requested transfer to another apartment as a reasonable accommodation for her mobility impairment.
key_holding: The court granted leave to amend because Thompson failed to plausibly allege how the requested transfer or repairs would accommodate her disability of being barely able to walk.
brief_summary: Desiree Thompson (a disabled tenant who can barely walk) and Darin Poole sued New York City, NYCHA, New York State, and Kings Towers regarding substandard housing conditions in their NYCHA apartment, asserting claims under the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and Fair Housing Amendments Act. The court granted leave to amend, finding that while Thompson sufficiently alleged disability, she failed to plead facts connecting her mobility disability to the requested transfer accommodation or the housing repairs.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
McElwee v. Cty. of Orange

case_name: McKins v. Pye
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an apartment equipped with railings to accommodate his disability; the court analyzed the denial as potential disparate treatment rather than as a reasonable accommodation claim.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA claims because plaintiff failed to plead facts showing he suffered an adverse housing action because of his race, color, religion, sex, familial status, national origin, or disability, as the denial was allegedly based on his criminal record; the court granted leave to amend.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff John McKins sued United Hebrew Soundview Senior Living and its managers alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination based on race and disability after being denied an apartment despite requesting a unit with railings to accommodate his disability; the court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim because plaintiff alleged the denial was due to his prior criminal record rather than his protected characteristics, though it granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Housing and Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)

source_file: 9850835_2019_De La Fuente v. The Sherry Netherland, Inc._nysd
case_name: De La Fuente v. The Sherry Netherland, Inc.
accommodation_description: Purchase application for cooperative apartment at the Sherry-Netherland, rejected based on national origin (Mexican-American)
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiff failed to establish that the Board's articulated legitimate, nondiscriminatory reasons for denying his cooperative purchase application—financial concerns and litigious history—were pretext for anti-Mexican discrimination.
brief_summary: Roque De La Fuente, a Mexican-American businessman, sued The Sherry Netherland, Inc. (a cooperative housing corporation) and its Board of Directors, alleging they rejected his application to purchase a cooperative apartment because of his national origin in violation of the Fair Housing Act and related civil rights statutes. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding that while plaintiff established a prima facie case, he failed to demonstrate that the Board's legitimate, nondiscriminatory reasons for denial—financial concerns and extensive litigation history—were pretextual, and no reasonable jury could find discriminatory intent on this record.
key_cases_cited: Robinson v. 12 Lofts Realty, Inc., 610 F.2d 1032 (2d Cir. 1979)
Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
De La Fuente v. FDIC, 332 F.3d 1208 (9th Cir. 2003)
Haber v. ASN 50th St. LLC, 847 F. Supp. 2d 578 (S.D.N.Y. 2012)

source_file: 9850873_2019_Lopez v. New York City Dept. Homeless Services_nysd
case_name: Lopez v. City of New York
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a transgender woman with gender dysphoria and depression, requested transfer from a cisgender female shelter to a transgender-specific shelter as a reasonable accommodation.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal because the WIN Defendants lacked authority over housing placement decisions—DHS retained that authority—so plaintiff's injuries lacked traceability to the WIN Defendants; additionally, plaintiff's demand for transgender-only housing was not adequately tied to her disabilities.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Mariah Lopez, a transgender homeless woman with gender dysphoria and depression, sued WIN, Inc. and Christine Quinn (WIN Defendants) for failing to accommodate her by transferring her from a cisgender female shelter to a transgender-specific shelter. The court recommended granting the motion to dismiss because the WIN Defendants lacked Article III standing—DHS, not WIN, controlled shelter placement, so plaintiff's injuries were not traceable to the WIN Defendants—and because plaintiff's accommodation demand lacked an adequate nexus to her disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Green v. City of New York
Sinisgallo v. Islip Hous. Auth.

case_name: Vinnie Hardee v. City of New Rochelle Section 8 Housing Agency
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested an informal hearing as a reasonable accommodation for her PTSD disability under the FHA, arguing she should not be held to strict procedural requirements in requesting the hearing.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff judgment on her procedural due process claim, ordering defendants to hold an informal hearing, but dismissed the Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim as moot because plaintiff obtained the relief sought through the due process ruling.
brief_summary: Vinnie Hardee, a Section 8 voucher holder with PTSD, sued the City of New Rochelle Section 8 Housing Agency after her benefits were terminated for alleged program violations and defendants refused to provide her an informal hearing. The court granted judgment for plaintiff on her Fourteenth Amendment due process claim, finding her letters disputed the termination and constituted a timely request for a hearing under HUD regulations, and ordered defendants to provide the informal hearing; the court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act reasonable accommodation claim as moot.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
Ciambriello v. County of Nassau, 292 F.3d 307 (2d Cir. 2002)
Rios v. Town of Huntington Hous. Auth., 853 F. Supp. 2d 330 (E.D.N.Y. 2012)
Rivera v. Town of Huntington Hous. Auth., 2012 WL 1933767 (E.D.N.Y. 2012)

source_file: 9850986_2019_A.L.M. v. Board of Managers of the Vireum Schoolhouse Condominium_nysd
case_name: A.L.M., a minor, by and through her Parent and Guardian, Scott M. Moore, and Scott M. Moore, individually v. Board of Managers of the Vireum Schoolhouse Condominium
accommodation_description: No formal reasonable accommodation request was made; the case involves claims of tenant-on-tenant harassment allegedly based on A.L.M.'s race, national origin, age, and sex, with plaintiffs complaining about noise and 'intersecting behavior' directed at the Moore family.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the Board on plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims because plaintiffs failed to establish that a hostile housing environment existed, that Mulazzi or Elders acted with discriminatory animus based on A.L.M.'s protected characteristics, or that the Board could be held liable for the alleged tenant-on-tenant harassment.
brief_summary: A.L.M. (a Chinese-American minor) and her father Scott Moore sued the Board of Managers of the Vireum Schoolhouse Condominium alleging that a Board member and another resident engaged in harassment—including loud noises and physically intersecting the Moore family's movements—because of A.L.M.'s race, national origin, age, and sex, in violation of the Fair Housing Act and 42 U.S.C. §§ 1981 and 1982. The court granted summary judgment for the Board, finding no evidence that the alleged harassment created a hostile housing environment or was motivated by discriminatory animus, and no basis for holding the Board liable for the conduct of its agent or other tenants.
key_cases_cited: Pierre v. Lantern Grp. Found. Inc., 2016 WL 3461309 (S.D.N.Y. June 20, 2016)
D.K. by L.K. v. Teams, 260 F. Supp. 3d 334 (S.D.N.Y. 2017)
Cain v. Rambert, 2014 WL 2440596 (E.D.N.Y. May 30, 2014)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 91 F. Supp. 3d 420 (E.D.N.Y. 2015)
Haber v. ASN 50th St. LLC, 847 F. Supp. 2d 578 (S.D.N.Y. 2012)

case_name: Noel v. City of New York
subsidy_program: Housing New York Plan, Mandatory Inclusionary Housing, 421-a
accommodation_description: No individual accommodation request; this is a systemic challenge to NYC's Community Preference Policy as racially discriminatory in affordable housing lotteries
key_holding: The court ruled on 350 documents in the City's privilege log, applying the Rodriguez balancing test for deliberative process privilege and finding most documents required disclosure while some remained protected under attorney-client privilege, work product doctrine, or deliberative process privilege.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs commenced this action challenging New York City's Community Preference Policy, which allocates 50% of affordable housing lottery units to community district residents, alleging it violates the Fair Housing Act by perpetuating racial segregation and disparately impacting racial minorities. This opinion resolves disputes over the City's privilege log for 350 documents, with the court ordering production of many documents after applying the Rodriguez balancing test while upholding privilege claims for others.
key_cases_cited: Winfield v. City of New York, 2017 WL 5664852 (S.D.N.Y. Nov. 27, 2017)
Rodriguez v. Pataki, 280 F. Supp. 2d 89 (S.D.N.Y.)
Noel v. City of New York, 357 F. Supp. 3d 298 (S.D.N.Y. 2019)
Grand Cent. P'ship, Inc. v. Cuomo, 166 F.3d 473 (2d Cir. 1999)
In re Cty. of Erie, 473 F.3d 413 (2d Cir. 2007)

source_file: 9851239_2019_Mill Street Partners, LLC v. City of Newburgh_nysd
case_name: Mill Street Partners, LLC v. City of Newburgh
accommodation_description: This is not a reasonable accommodation case; Mill Street (a developer) alleges the City of Newburgh discriminated against its affordable housing project based on the race of intended beneficiaries.
key_holding: The FHA claim survives dismissal because Mill Street plausibly alleges the City capitulated to racially-motivated opposition and refused to support the project due to animus toward the low-income minority population the housing would serve.
brief_summary: Mill Street Partners, a private developer, sued the City of Newburgh and its city manager alleging the city discriminated against its affordable housing project based on the race of intended low-income minority beneficiaries by refusing to support removal of a deed restriction. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the FHA claim, finding Mill Street plausibly alleged both disparate treatment (racial animus in opposition) and disparate impact (disproportionate adverse effect on minority population).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Mhany Mgmt., Inc. v. County of Nassau, 819 F.3d 581 (2d Cir. 2016)
Fair Hous. in Huntington Comm. Inc. v. Town of Huntington, 316 F.3d 357 (2d Cir. 2003)
LeBlanc-Sternberg v. Fletcher, 67 F.3d 412 (2d Cir. 1995)

source_file: 9851565_2019_Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. Cuomo_nysd
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. Cuomo
accommodation_description: Waiver of categorical 'no wheelchair' policy at assisted living facilities to permit admission of individuals who use wheelchairs for mobility
key_holding: Because ACF Defendants had categorical policies against wheelchair users and explicitly stated they would not accommodate wheelchair-bound applicants, plaintiffs adequately alleged that any accommodation request would have been futile, satisfying the reasonable accommodation claim without requiring formal request.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center and individual tenants sued assisted living facility operators and New York State Department of Health, alleging that categorical policies excluding wheelchair users from admission violated the FHA. The court denied most motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged futility of requesting accommodation where facilities explicitly stated they would not admit anyone using a wheelchair under any circumstances, and that the Amended Regulations' continued exclusion of persons requiring assistance to walk did not moot the claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc.
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman

case_name: Samuel Indig, Leah Indig, Meir Kahana, Robert Klein, and Naftali Klein v. The Village of Pomona, Brett Yagel, Louis Zummo, Leon Harris, and Doris Ulman
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are Orthodox Jewish residents alleging discriminatory enforcement of Village building codes and municipal services based on religious affiliation, not a disability accommodation request.
key_holding: Plaintiffs adequately pleaded an Equal Protection claim and FHA discrimination claim based on intentional religious discrimination in enforcement of Village codes, surviving the motion to dismiss, but their Free Exercise claims and New York Civil Rights Law claim were dismissed.
brief_summary: Five Orthodox Jewish residents of the Village of Pomona sued the Village and its officials alleging religious discrimination in enforcement of building codes and municipal services, claiming violations of the Equal Protection Clause and Fair Housing Act. The court granted in part and denied in part defendants' motion to dismiss, allowing plaintiffs' Equal Protection and FHA claims to proceed while dismissing their Free Exercise claims and New York Civil Rights Law claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Church of the Lukumi Babalu Aye, Inc. v. City of Hialeah, 508 U.S. 520 (1993)
Congregation Rabbinical Coll. of Tartikov, Inc. v. Vill. of Pomona, NY, 280 F. Supp. 3d 426 (S.D.N.Y. 2017)

case_name: Mark Tunne v. Brenda S. Spears, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges racially discriminatory harassment during tenancy and seeks reversal of 2013 eviction order as a result of alleged race-based housing discrimination.
key_holding: The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim as untimely under the two-year statute of limitations, noting that Plaintiff's tenancy ended in 2013 and the complaint was filed well after the limitations period expired.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Mark Tunne sued judges, attorneys, property managers, and private parties seeking to reverse his 2013 eviction from a rent-regulated Manhattan apartment, asserting claims including race discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the action on multiple grounds, including that any FHA race discrimination claim was barred by the two-year statute of limitations, the Rooker-Feldman doctrine prohibiting federal review of state court judgments, and judicial immunity for the defendant judges.
key_cases_cited: Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus. Corp., 544 U.S. 280 (2005)
Hoblock v. Albany Cty. Bd. of Elections, 422 F.3d 77 (2d Cir. 2005)
Mireles v. Waco, 502 U.S. 9 (1991)
Havens v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Pantoja v. Scott, No. 96-CV-8593 (AJP), 2001 WL 1313358 (S.D.N.Y. Oct. 26, 2001)

case_name: Ochei v. Lapes
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleged patterns of harassment, constructive eviction, and denial of services including utilities by landlord and homeless services, but did not articulate a specific reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims for failure to state a claim because even under a liberal reading, plaintiff failed to allege facts suggesting any defendant discriminated against her on the basis of a protected characteristic.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Joan Ochei sued landlord Alan Lapes, 317 Aladdin Hotel Corporation, and NYC Department of Homeless Services alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on alleged harassment, constructive eviction, and denial of services. The court dismissed the action for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiff failed to allege facts suggesting discrimination on the basis of a protected characteristic, and alternatively barred the requested injunctive relief under the Anti-Injunction Act and Younger abstention doctrine.

source_file: 9854778_2020_Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. JDS Development LLC_nysd
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. JDS Development LLC
accommodation_description: Design and construction accessibility defects in multifamily housing (narrow doorways, heavy entrance doors, obstructed mailboxes, inaccessible kitchen and bathroom features) discovered by testers on behalf of a relative who uses a wheelchair
key_holding: The FHA limitations period for design-and-construction claims accrues when a protected person encounters the inaccessible design or construction elements, not when the building is completed or last unit sold; because plaintiff's testers discovered accessibility violations in August 2018 and filed suit in February 2019, the claims were timely.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center sued JDS Development LLC and related defendants alleging that a 51-unit Brooklyn apartment building failed to comply with FHA design-and-construction accessibility requirements. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss on statute of limitations grounds, holding that the two-year limitations period begins when a protected person encounters the inaccessible design elements, not when construction is completed or units are sold, making the August 2018 filing timely.
key_cases_cited: Garcia v. Brockway, 526 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2008)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Eastern Paralyzed Veterans Ass'n v. Lazarus-Burman Assocs., 133 F. Supp. 2d 203 (E.D.N.Y. 2001)
Curtis v. Loether, 415 U.S. 189 (1974)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Stone v. 23rd Chelsea Associates, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff is a disabled veteran proceeding pro se asserting racial discrimination claims (rent overcharging, disparate treatment) rather than disability accommodation claims.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act and related discrimination claims were dismissed as time-barred and for failing to allege sufficient facts to raise an inference of discriminatory intent based on race; the vague allegations of discriminatory conduct did not plausibly connect any adverse treatment to plaintiff's protected class status.
brief_summary: Richard E. Stone, an African American Section 8 voucher holder and disabled veteran, sued his landlord and property management company alleging they charged him higher rent than similarly situated white tenants, sided with white tenants in disputes, monitored him, and made discriminatory comments. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Stone's claims were time-barred and that his vague, conclusory allegations failed to raise a plausible inference of racial discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 944 F.3d 370 (2d Cir. 2019)

case_name: Bernard T. Anderson Bey v. ACS; NYCHA; DCSE; HRA (referenced as New York State and the City of New York)
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged NYCHA denied his request to transfer his daughter's daycare placement and refused to provide him with a copy of his lease, but these requests were not framed as formal disability accommodation requests.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim because his allegations—that NYCHA denied his daycare transfer request and failed to provide a copy of his lease—do not give rise to an inference of discrimination based on any impermissible factor under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Bernard T. Anderson Bey, appearing against NYC child welfare and housing agencies, alleged violations of the Fair Housing Act, equal protection, and due process related to family court custody proceedings, daycare transfer, and lease documentation. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state an FHA claim, finding the allegations that NYCHA denied his daycare transfer request and failed to provide his lease do not plausibly allege discrimination based on a protected characteristic under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

case_name: Matthew Hannan v. Amy Rose et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discriminatory eviction from his apartment based on his disability (HIV) and status as a gay man, claiming Fair Housing Act protections as an aggrieved person.
key_holding: The court adopted the R&R finding that plaintiff has standing under the FHA as an aggrieved person despite his unlawful occupancy arrangement with the tenant of record, rejecting defendants' argument that the FHA does not protect unauthorized occupants.
brief_summary: Matthew Hannan, a gay man with HIV, sued Rose Associates (property management) and individual employees alleging discriminatory eviction from his Henry Hall apartment after he was removed for living in a unit sublet from a friend. The court granted in part and denied in part defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing Section 1983, state constitutional, Rehabilitation Act, NYC Admin Code Section 713, and FTCA claims but allowing the FHA claim to proceed based on finding plaintiff has standing as an aggrieved person.
key_cases_cited: Garcia v. S.U.N.Y. Health Scis. Ctr. of Brooklyn
Garcia v. S.U.N.Y. Health Scis. Ctr. of Brooklyn, 280 F.3d 98 (2d Cir. 2001)

case_name: Akasha Tania Barker v. Women In Need, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Defendants failed to accommodate her gender identity by not placing her in appropriate housing at a homeless shelter, though the complaint lacks sufficient factual detail about the specific accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court dismissed ADA and RA claims because gender identity disorder is excluded from ADA disability classification and plaintiff alleged no other disability, but granted leave to amend FHA claims, noting that sex discrimination may include discrimination based on gender identity and that FHAA requires plaintiff to plead facts establishing a disability requiring accommodation.
brief_summary: Akasha Tania Barker, a transgender woman, sued Women in Need, Inc. (a private homeless shelter operator) and various city agencies alleging discrimination in housing based on her gender identity. The court dismissed her ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims because gender identity disorder is excluded from the ADA's definition of disability, dismissed her HIPAA and Section 1983 claims for failure to state a claim, but granted leave to amend her Fair Housing Act claims after noting that sex discrimination may include discrimination based on gender identity and that FHAA reasonable accommodation claims require pleading facts establishing a qualifying disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Fabian v. Hosp. of Cent. Connecticut
Logan v. Matveevskii
Kaeo-Tomaselli v. Pi'ikoi Recovery House for Women

case_name: Williams v. Urban Pathways Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; plaintiff alleges racial discrimination in termination from supportive housing
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a claim for racial discrimination under the FHA because plaintiff only alleged that a white female resident was not terminated while he was, without pleading sufficient facts showing the different treatment was motivated by his race.
brief_summary: Dwight Andre Williams, a Black former resident of Ivan Shapiro House operated by Urban Pathways Inc., sued alleging racial discrimination in housing, claiming staff mistreated him and terminated his residency because of his race. The court dismissed his Fair Housing Act claim under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(b) for failure to state a plausible claim, finding his allegations showed only that a white female resident's tenancy was not terminated while his was, without sufficient factual basis to infer the differential treatment was motivated by race.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

case_name: United States of America v. 111 East 88th Partners
accommodation_description: Gregory Reich sought permission to keep an emotional support dog in his rent-controlled apartment as a reasonable accommodation for his depression, personality disorders, and end-stage renal disease.
key_holding: The court granted the government's motion for summary judgment on the defendant's res judicata and collateral estoppel defenses, finding no privity between HUD and NYSDHR, and denied defendant's motion for summary judgment on the reasonable accommodation claim because genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether defendant's demands for excessive medical documentation and pursuit of eviction constituted constructive denial of Reich's accommodation requests.
brief_summary: The United States sued landlord 111 East 88th Partners for denying Gregory Reich's reasonable accommodation request to keep an emotional support dog and for interfering with his FHA rights through coercive demands for medical records while pursuing eviction. The court granted summary judgment for the government on the defendant's preclusion defenses and denied defendant's motion on the constructive denial issue, allowing the reasonable accommodation claim to proceed to trial.
key_cases_cited: Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Logan v. Matveevskii, 57 F. Supp. 3d 234 (S.D.N.Y. 2014)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Lance v. Dennis, 546 U.S. 459 (2006)
E. River Hous. Corp., 90 F. Supp. 3d 142 (S.D.N.Y. 2015)

source_file: 9856199_2020_Greens at Chester, LLC v. Town of Chester_nysd
case_name: Greens at Chester LLC v. Town of Chester
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this is an intentional discrimination challenge under § 3604(a) alleging Town and County blocked housing development to prevent Hasidic Jewish families from moving into the community.
key_holding: The court granted the NYAG's motion to intervene, finding the NYAG has parens patriae standing to bring FHA claims under §§ 3604(a) and 3617 alleging municipal officials engaged in intentional religious discrimination by obstructing a housing development.
brief_summary: Greens at Chester LLC, a housing developer, sued the Town and County of Chester alleging municipal officials intentionally discriminated against the Hasidic Jewish community by blocking an approved housing development through denied permits, enacted restrictive laws, and costly special requests. The New York State Attorney General moved to intervene to bring claims under §§ 3604(a) and 3617 of the FHA. The court granted the motion, finding the NYAG has parens patriae standing and authority to bring FHA claims alleging religious discrimination in housing.
key_cases_cited: Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Clearing Housing Ass'n, LLC v. Cuomo, 510 F.3d 105 (2d Cir. 2007)
Support Ministries for Persons With AIDS, Inc. v. Vill of Waterford, 799 F. Supp. 272 (N.D.N.Y. 1992)
People by Abrams v. Merlino, 694 F. Supp. 1101 (S.D.N.Y. 1988)
Trafficante v. Metro. Life Ins. Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)

case_name: Ash v. City of New York
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges landlords interfered with his religious practices and subjected him to discriminatory treatment based on race and religion during his tenancy.
key_holding: The court granted judgment on the pleadings for defendants because the FHA claim was time-barred under the two-year statute of limitations, and the § 1983 claims failed because plaintiff did not adequately allege defendants acted under color of state law.
brief_summary: Damon Ash, an African-American Jewish man proceeding pro se, sued his former landlords Betty Jacobson and Laura Porschar (employees of the United Jewish Council) for housing discrimination based on race and religion and related constitutional torts. The court granted defendants' motion for judgment on the pleadings, dismissing the FHA claim as time-barred by the two-year statute of limitations and dismissing the § 1983 claims for failure to adequately allege state action, while declining supplemental jurisdiction over state law defamation claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 944 F.3d 370 (2d Cir. 2019)
Stone v. 23rd Chelsea Assocs., No. 18-CV-3869 (VSB), 2020 WL 1503671 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 30, 2020)
Vega v. Hempstead Union Free Sch. Dist., 801 F.3d 72 (2d Cir. 2015)

case_name: Roslyn Alma Dames v. Bill De Blasio, Mayor, New York City; Dean Santa, Director of Asset Management, United States Department of Housing and Urban Development; St. Margaret's House; Trinity Church Wall Street; St. Paul's Church
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who suffers from PTSD allegedly resulting from witnessing the 9-11 attacks, seeks reinstatement of her Section 8 voucher and return to her apartment at St. Margaret's House after she vacated due to her disability.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim because, even liberally construing the pro se complaint, Plaintiff failed to allege facts suggesting that any Defendant discriminated against her on the basis of her disability.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Roslyn Alma Dames, who has PTSD from witnessing the 9-11 attacks, sued the City of New York, HUD officials, and St. Margaret's House seeking reinstatement of her Section 8 voucher and return to her apartment, alleging disability discrimination under the FHA. The Court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding Plaintiff did not allege facts showing any Defendant discriminated against her because of her disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)
Lindsey v. Normet, 405 U.S. 56 (1972)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)

case_name: Daryl J. Davis v. Port Jervis Community Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's sparse complaint alleges termination of Section 8 benefits and defamation but does not clearly articulate any specific reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The court granted plaintiff leave to amend his complaint, finding the original complaint failed to allege facts sufficient to state any federal claim, including any Fair Housing Act claim based on discrimination.
brief_summary: Daryl J. Davis, a pro se Section 8 tenant, sued Port Jervis Community Development alleging termination of his Section 8 benefits and defamation of character. The court found the complaint failed to state any viable federal claim and granted plaintiff sixty days to file an amended complaint with sufficient factual allegations, noting that no facts suggesting FHA-protected discrimination were alleged.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970)
Lindsey v. Normet, 405 U.S. 56 (1972)
Gladstone Realtors v. Vill. of Bellwood, 441 U.S. 91 (1979)

case_name: Tasfay v. Ramos
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; complaint broadly alleges discriminatory action against family at a homeless shelter based on race, including removal without notice and alleged defamatory statements.
key_holding: Court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding plaintiffs provided no factual allegations to support the inference that defendant Ramos or any other defendant acted based on racial motivation, and granted plaintiffs sixty days leave to amend.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiffs Jama Tasfay and Desmond Rolle sued Melissa Ramos (DHS employee), Department of Homeless Services, and Acacia alleging discriminatory removal from a Bronx homeless shelter based on race, First Amendment violation when a sergeant allegedly prevented recording, and defamation. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state claims, finding insufficient factual allegations to support an inference of racial motivation, and granted leave to amend within sixty days.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal
West v. Atkins
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of New York
Connick v. Thompson

case_name: Peter Thompson v. CRF-Cluster Model Program, LLC
accommodation_description: Request to transfer from fifth-floor apartment to a lower-floor apartment due to osteoarthritis of the hip
key_holding: Plaintiff stated a claim for constructive denial of reasonable accommodation where Defendant failed to address Plaintiff's transfer request for over a year, with Plaintiff asking about the request three times weekly, raising an inference of discriminatory intent rather than mere bureaucratic delay.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Peter Thompson, a Black homeless father with osteoarthritis of the hip, sued CRF-Cluster Model Program, LLC, alleging the private entity that provided temporary housing failed to accommodate his disability by refusing to transfer him from a fifth-floor apartment and discriminated against him based on race, disability, and familial status. The court denied Defendant's motion to dismiss the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding Plaintiff adequately alleged a constructive denial based on Defendant's year-long delay in responding to his transfer request, as well as his race-based disparate treatment claim.
key_cases_cited: Martinez ex rel. Martinez v. Lexington Gardens Assocs.
Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't
Austin v. Town of Farmington
Logan v. Matveevskii
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project

case_name: Keisha A. Jones v. Steven Banks, DSS Commissioner; Nonprofit Corporation 1-5; DSS Employee 1-5
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who is susceptible to COVID-19 due to unspecified preexisting conditions, requested transfer to an acceptable shelter location with a kitchenette closer to her primary care physician, medical lab, specialist, and other resources in Manhattan.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's TRO request and dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim, granting sixty days' leave to amend because Plaintiff failed to identify the nature of her disability or specify the type of reasonable accommodations she requested.
brief_summary: Pro se Plaintiff Keisha A. Jones, residing in a Manhattan homeless shelter, sued DSS Commissioner Steven Banks and unnamed nonprofit shelter contractors under § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act, alleging inadequate shelter conditions and denied reasonable accommodations for her health needs. The court denied Plaintiff's motion for a temporary restraining order and dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a viable claim, finding that Plaintiff failed to identify her disability or specify what accommodations she requested, while granting sixty days' leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Lindsey v. Normet, 405 U.S. 56 (1972)
Colon v. Coughlin, 58 F.3d 865 (2d Cir. 1995)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs. of City of New York, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

case_name: Brandon C. Jones v. Ms. Jisin H. Thomas; Mr. Jonathan Tauarez; Volunteers of America Corporation; Volunteers of America, Swartz Shelter; Volunteers of America 135 W. 50th Street, NYC, NY 10024
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made vague references to failure to provide reasonable accommodation related to medical personnel advice and medical documentation, but failed to specify the nature of the accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim because he failed to allege any facts suggesting he has a disability within the meaning of the FHA and failed to allege facts regarding the nature of any specific reasonable accommodation that defendants denied.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Brandon Jones sued Volunteers of America and its employees alleging they violated the ADA, Fair Housing Act, and his constitutional rights by failing to provide reasonable accommodation and retaliating against him at a homeless shelter. The court dismissed the § 1983 and Title VII claims, and dismissed the FHA claim with leave to amend because plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing he has a disability or identifying the specific accommodation defendants denied.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Rodriguez v. Village Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)
Fulton v. Goord, 591 F.3d 37 (2d Cir. 2009)
Roberts v. Royal Atlantic Corp., 542 F.3d 363 (2d Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9859405_2020_Shine v. New York City Housing Authority_nysd

case_name: Patricia Milhaven v. Country Village Apartment d/b/a Creekside Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with multiple sclerosis sought to keep and use her service dog Penny without restrictions, including allowing the dog to accompany her in common areas without being leashed when she uses a cane for ambulation.
key_holding: The Anti-Injunction Act bars federal court from enjoining ongoing state eviction proceedings, but the court declines to apply Younger abstention because routine landlord-tenant holdover proceedings do not fit within the narrow Sprint categories, and Plaintiff's claims for monetary damages and prospective injunctive relief unrelated to the state proceeding survive.
brief_summary: Patricia Milhaven, a disabled tenant with multiple sclerosis who uses a service dog, sued Country Village Apartments alleging the landlord violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to renew her lease and initiating eviction proceedings based on complaints about her service dog. The court granted in part and denied in part defendant's motion to dismiss, holding that the Anti-Injunction Act bars injunctive relief directly related to the ongoing state eviction proceeding, but that Younger abstention does not apply and Plaintiff's claims for damages and prospective injunctive relief unrelated to the eviction may proceed.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Sprint Commc'ns, Inc. v. Jacobs
Exxon Mobil Corp. v. Saudi Basic Indus. Corp.
Younger v. Harris

source_file: 9861510_2020_Xia v. 65 West 87th Street Housing Development Fund Corporation_nysd
case_name: Xia v. 65 West 87th Street Housing Development Fund Corporation
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; the case involves a challenge to discriminatory denial of apartment purchase application based on Ms. Xia's Asian American race.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately pleaded a prima facie case of racial discrimination by alleging they were qualified applicants who were subjected to a more burdensome application process and ultimately denied purchasing an apartment, while similarly situated non-Asian applicants were approved.
brief_summary: Eva Xia, a Chinese American woman, and her husband sued the Board of Directors of 65 West 87th Street Housing Development Fund Corporation, alleging they were denied the opportunity to purchase an apartment because of Ms. Xia's race. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged facts showing they were financially qualified, that similarly situated non-Asian applicants were approved, and that plaintiffs were subjected to a more burdensome and prolonged application process after defendants learned of Ms. Xia's race.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Hous. & Comty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 517 U.S. 519 (2015)
L.C. v. LeFrak Org., Inc., 987 F. Supp. 2d 391 (S.D.N.Y. 2013)
Boykin v. KeyCorp., 521 F.3d 202 (2d Cir. 2008)

source_file: 9862537_2021_Mercer v. New York City Housing Authority_nysd
case_name: Robert Mercer v. New York City Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request that NYCHA expedite or prioritize mold/plaster repairs to accommodate plaintiff's asthma
key_holding: Plaintiff's mild intermittent asthma without complication does not substantially limit a major life activity to qualify as a cognizable handicap under the FHA, and the complaint failed to allege that NYCHA knew or had reason to know of plaintiff's asthma.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Robert Mercer, a public housing tenant, sued NYCHA alleging failure to accommodate his asthma by not properly addressing mold in his apartment under the FHA's reasonable accommodation provision. The court granted NYCHA's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to adequately plead a cognizable disability (mild intermittent asthma does not substantially limit a major life activity) and failed to allege NYCHA knew of his condition.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Olsen v. Stark Homes, Inc., 759 F.3d 140 (2d Cir. 2014)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

case_name: Myung-Ja Melton v. Malcolm Shabazz, L.P. et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges discriminatory delay in apartment repairs (cabinets, plumbing) based on race and national origin, claiming other tenants received timelier repairs.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment; she presented no evidence identifying similarly situated tenants of different races who received timelier repairs and no evidence that defendants' actions were motivated by discriminatory intent.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Myung-Ja Melton, an Asian senior citizen, sued her landlord Malcolm Shabazz, L.P. and property manager Covington Realty Services alleging they delayed repairs to her apartment (plumbing issues, cabinets) because of her race and national origin. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of disparate treatment because she presented no evidence identifying similarly situated tenants of different races who received faster repairs and no evidence of discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 944 F.3d 370 (2d Cir. 2019)
Ash v. Jacobson, No. 16-CV-09548 (GHW), 2020 WL 2848178 (S.D.N.Y. June 1, 2020)
Khodeir v. Sayyed, No. 15-CV-08763 (DAB), 2016 WL 5817003 (S.D.N.Y. Sept. 28, 2016)
Rhodes v. Advance Prop. Mgmt., Inc., No. 10-CV-00826 (JCH), 2012 WL 12904797 (D. Conn. Aug. 30, 2012)

case_name: Sister E. Jones Bey v. Dana La Casse, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff asserted vague housing discrimination allegations against her landlord for discrimination based on nationality, family size, religion, and source of income, but no specific accommodation request or disability-related claim was alleged or analyzed.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim against her landlord because she failed to allege facts connecting Powell's conduct to any animus against a protected characteristic, relying instead on conclusory allegations insufficient to state a claim under Rule 8.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Sister E. Jones Bey, proceeding pro se, sued her landlord Vicknell Powell and numerous government defendants alleging constitutional violations under § 1983 and Fair Housing Act claims. The Court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claim against Powell for failure to state a claim, finding she failed to allege facts showing a connection between Powell's conduct and any protected characteristic, and granted leave to replead the housing discrimination claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly

source_file: 9865041_2021_Brock v. The City of New York_nysd
case_name: Mandela Brock v. City of New York
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a homeless person, alleged discrimination because he is homeless, seeking placement in a particular type of shelter.
