key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dept. of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)

case_name: Katherine Bebe Price, et al. v. Scott G. Oxley, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiffs allege defendants interfered with their FHA lawsuit by threatening sanctions if they did not dismiss related fair housing claims.
key_holding: The amended complaint failed to state an interference claim under § 3617 because plaintiffs failed to allege any facts showing they were discriminated against on the basis of a protected characteristic, and plaintiffs' conclusory allegations of coercion and intimidation were not supported by factual content allowing the court to infer discriminatory animus.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Katherine and Richard Price sued attorneys Scott G. Oxley and Gregory S. Page (and their law firms), alleging the attorneys violated the FHA by sending a letter threatening sanctions if plaintiffs did not dismiss their related FHA lawsuit. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to allege facts showing discriminatory animus or that defendants engaged in conduct constituting unlawful interference with FHA rights.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Wells v. Rhodes, 928 F. Supp. 2d 920 (S.D. Ohio 2013)
HDC, LLC v. City of Ann Arbor, 675 F.3d 608 (6th Cir. 2012)
Givens v. Wenker, 2012 WL 1680099 (S.D. Ohio 2012)

source_file: 9913564_2022_Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority_ohsd
case_name: Dayton Veterans Residences Limited Partnership v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request for project-based housing vouchers as a reasonable accommodation for homeless disabled veterans seeking housing at a VA Medical Campus development.
key_holding: The court denied DMHA's motion to withdraw its jury demand, finding that Freedom's Path is entitled to a jury trial because it seeks monetary damages under the FHA, which constitutes a legal remedy triggering Seventh Amendment rights.
brief_summary: Freedom's Path, a developer seeking to create housing for homeless veterans on a VA campus, sued DMHA for allegedly reneging on a commitment for project-based housing vouchers and denying reasonable accommodation requests for disabled veterans. The court denied DMHA's motion to withdraw its jury demand, holding that Freedom's Path has a Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial because it seeks monetary damages under the FHA, which constitutes a legal rather than equitable remedy.

source_file: 9913567_2022_Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority_ohsd
case_name: Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request by housing developer for DMHA to provide project-based vouchers and modify its administrative plan to accommodate homeless veterans with disabilities
key_holding: The court granted DMHA's motion to limit damages, finding that Freedom's Path's $21 million damage claim was speculative and remote because the proposed project was abandoned, and the claimed tax credits, VA grants, and increased construction costs would not have been retained by Freedom's Path but spent on project costs.
brief_summary: Freedom's Path at Dayton sued DMHA, alleging the housing authority discriminatorily blocked funding for 60 units of project-based housing for homeless veterans with disabilities, violating the ADA and FHA. After partial summary judgment narrowed liability to a December 2015 reasonable accommodation request, the court granted DMHA's motion in limine to exclude $21 million in claimed damages as too speculative and remote, allowing only the developer's fee to potentially be recovered.
key_cases_cited: Curtis v. Loether, 415 U.S. 189 (1974)
Anderson Grp., LLC v. City of Saratoga Springs, 805 F.3d 34 (2d Cir. 2014)
Grantham and Mann, Inc. v. American Safety Prods., 831 F.2d 596 (6th Cir. 1987)
Silver Sage Partners v. City of Desert Hot Springs, 251 F.3d 814 (9th Cir. 2001)

source_file: 9913576_2022_Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority_ohsd
case_name: Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Request that DMHA amend its Administrative Plan to allocate project-based housing vouchers and apply for VASH vouchers to support Freedom's Path's veterans housing project
key_holding: The court denied DMHA's motion to limit trial testimony to events before September 2, 2016, finding that the law of the case doctrine does not mandate such a temporal limitation and that the court should defer evidentiary rulings until trial context is available.
brief_summary: Freedom's Path at Dayton, a housing developer for homeless veterans, sued Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging it failed to provide reasonable accommodation under the FHA and ADA by refusing to amend its Administrative Plan to allocate housing vouchers and apply for VASH vouchers. The court denied DMHA's motion in limine to limit trial testimony to events before September 2, 2016, finding the law of the case doctrine does not require such a temporal cutoff and that evidentiary rulings should be deferred to the trial context.
key_cases_cited: Sperberg v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Co., 519 F.2d 708 (6th Cir. 1975)
GMAC Mortg., LLC v. McKeever, 651 F. App'x 332 (6th Cir. 2016)
Arizona v. California, 460 U.S. 605 (1983)
Scott v. Churchill, 377 F.3d 565 (6th Cir. 2004)
United States v. Love, 254 F. App'x 511 (6th Cir. 2007)

case_name: Chapman v. Franklin County Sheriff
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a pretrial detainee, requested to wear a hijab as part of her Muslim religious practice while incarcerated at the county correctional center.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because she failed to allege facts plausibly showing she qualifies for FHA protection in the jail context; her primary surviving claim is a First Amendment free exercise claim, not an FHA reasonable accommodation claim.
brief_summary: Tasha Chapman, a Muslim pretrial detainee with Bipolar Disorder II, sued the Franklin County Sheriff alleging violations including First Amendment free exercise, Fair Housing Act, ADA, and state law claims arising from being forced to remove her hijab at the county jail. The court dismissed most claims but permitted the First Amendment claim to proceed, finding plaintiff stated a plausible claim that jail policy prohibiting hijabs violated her sincerely held religious beliefs.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr.

source_file: 9914456_2022_Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority_ohsd
case_name: Freedom's Path at Dayton v. Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Developer requested that Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority amend its administrative plan to award project-based vouchers to support a housing development for disabled homeless veterans.
key_holding: The jury's verdict for the defendant was supported by sufficient evidence, and the district court did not abuse its discretion in its rulings on the motions in limine and jury instructions regarding the reasonable accommodation claim.
brief_summary: Dayton Veterans Residences Limited Partnership, a housing developer, sued Dayton Metropolitan Housing Authority alleging the housing authority's refusal to award project-based vouchers to support a veterans housing project constituted a failure to accommodate disabled veterans under the Fair Housing Act. Following a jury verdict for the defendant, the court denied the plaintiff's motion for a new trial, finding the verdict was supported by the evidence and the challenged jury instructions and evidentiary rulings did not warrant disturbing the judgment.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
Howard v. City of Beavercreek, 276 F.3d 802 (6th Cir. 2002)
Overlook Mutual Homes v. Spencer, 666 F. Supp. 2d 850 (S.D. Ohio 2009)
Schaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus County, Inc., 938 F.3d 1259 (11th Cir. 2019)
Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Ronald D. Leonard v. Amy Montgomery
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; plaintiff brought First Amendment retaliation and other claims unrelated to disability accommodation.
key_holding: The Magistrate Judge recommends dismissal because Younger abstention applies to ongoing state eviction proceedings, and even if the court could hear the case, plaintiff failed to state a valid § 3604 claim since he made no allegations of housing discrimination based on a protected class.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Ronald Leonard, a tenant of the Hocking Metropolitan Housing Authority, brought suit against Office Manager Amy Montgomery alleging First Amendment retaliation and claims under 42 U.S.C. § 3604 and state law after receiving a Notice to Leave the Premises following his refusal to permit a unit inspection and his photographing of staff. The Magistrate Judge recommends dismissal under Younger abstention doctrine since state eviction proceedings are ongoing, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to state a viable claim under § 3604 as he made no allegations of discrimination based on a protected class.

case_name: Tasha Chapman v. Columbus Metropolitan Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff's landlord requested documentation for plaintiff's emotional support animal and sought to rekey locks, which plaintiff characterized as discriminatory retaliation for disability.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal because plaintiff failed to allege that defendants' statements indicated a preference, limitation, or discrimination based on disability or family status; the landlord's requests for ESA vaccination records and lock rekeying were legitimate property management requests, not discriminatory statements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(c).
brief_summary: Tasha Chapman, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Columbus Metropolitan Housing Authority, Heels Homes Ltd., and related defendants under the Fair Housing Act alleging family status and disability discrimination based on statements regarding her ESA and alleged housing code violations. The court recommended dismissal of the entire complaint, finding that plaintiff failed to allege any statements indicating discrimination based on protected characteristics under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(c).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Connor Grp., 805 F. Supp. 2d 396 (S.D. Ohio 2011)

source_file: 9919451_2024_Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma, Inc v. Feiock_oked
case_name: Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma, Inc. v. Feiock
accommodation_description: Request for a one-year lease renewal as a reasonable accommodation for the tenant's terminal illness (stage 4 cancer) to avoid displacement during end-of-life care.
key_holding: The requested one-year lease renewal was neither necessary nor reasonable because it offered better housing terms than other tenants received (one-year vs. six-month standard leases) rather than equal opportunity, and plaintiffs provided no evidentiary support that such a lengthy renewal was required to accommodate the disability.
brief_summary: Metropolitan Fair Housing Council of Oklahoma and individual tenants James and Marta McNamee sued landlords Frank and Glenna Feiock alleging failure to accommodate under the FHA when they refused to renew the McNamees' lease for one year as an accommodation for James McNamee's terminal cancer. The court granted partial summary judgment for defendants, finding the accommodation request was neither necessary (because it provided better terms than other tenants received) nor reasonable (because indefinite lease extensions for disabled tenants are not required).
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Assoc., Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr., Inc. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Courage to Change Ranches Holding Co. v. El Paso Cnty., Colorado, 73 F.4th 1175 (10th Cir. 2023)

case_name: Della Shaw, Sherlyne Turner, Bertha Johnson, and Mary Rand v. Cherokee Meadows, LP; Carland Group, LLC; Redbud Contractors, LLC; Carland Properties, LLC; and Blackledge & Associates
accommodation_description: Tenants allege the housing development includes artificial barriers including 6-inch curbs without curb ramps and lack of connecting accessible routes that exclude persons with disabilities.
key_holding: The third-party defendant Crafton Tull's motion for summary judgment was denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether Crafton Tull assumed responsibility for accessible route design and whether the 6-inch mountable curbs complied with federal accessibility standards.
brief_summary: Tenants of Cherokee Meadows senior living apartments sued the property owner, developer, and contractors alleging design and construction violations under the Fair Housing Act, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act due to inaccessible exterior routes caused by 6-inch curbs without curb ramps. The court denied the third-party defendant engineering firm's motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of fact exist regarding whether the firm assumed responsibility for accessible route design and whether the design complied with federal accessibility standards.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574 (1986)
Pickens v. Tulsa Metro. Ministry, 951 P.2d 1079 (Okla. 1997)
Bannister v. Town of Noble, 812 F.2d 1265 (10th Cir. 1987)

case_name: Shaw v. Cherokee Meadows, LP
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged denial of reasonable accommodation requests, but the court found no evidence they directed any such request to the architect-defendant Blackledge.
key_holding: Blackledge is entitled to summary judgment on plaintiffs' reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs submitted no evidence that they requested an accommodation from Blackledge or that Blackledge denied such request; however, genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether Blackledge's architectural design complied with § 3604(f)(3)(C) accessibility requirements regarding toilet grab bars, bathing facilities, and exterior accessible routes.
brief_summary: Individual elderly tenants sued Cherokee Meadows Apartments, its developer, and architectural firm Blackledge & Associates for Fair Housing Act violations, alleging the property contained design and construction barriers and that defendants refused reasonable accommodation requests. The court granted Blackledge summary judgment on the reasonable accommodation claim (plaintiffs never directed a request to the architect) and on most design claims, but denied summary judgment on genuine disputes regarding toilet grab bars, bathing facilities, and exterior accessible route design compliance with § 3604(f)(3)(C).
key_cases_cited: Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Baltimore Neighborhoods, Inc. v. Rommel Builders, Inc., 3 F. Supp. 2d 661 (D. Md. 1998)
Barker v. Niles Bolton Assocs., Inc., 316 F. App'x 933 (11th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Della Shaw, Sherlyne Turner, Bertha Johnson, and Mary Rand v. Cherokee Meadows, LP; Carland Group, LLC; Redbud Contractors, LLC; Carland Properties, LLC; and Blackledge & Associates
accommodation_description: Elderly disabled tenants requested physical modifications to remove mountable curbs and relocate mailboxes to provide accessible pathways throughout the apartment complex.
key_holding: The court granted partial summary judgment, dismissing claims for intentional discrimination, steering, and retaliation, but denied summary judgment on the design-and-construction claim under § 3604(f)(3)(C) and the reasonable accommodation claim for curb remediation and mailbox access, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether defendants unreasonably delayed remediation.
brief_summary: Four elderly tenants with mobility disabilities sued Cherokee Meadows developers, contractors, and property managers alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failure to design and construct accessible common areas and for denying reasonable accommodation requests regarding curb remediation and mailbox access. The district court granted partial summary judgment, dismissing intentional discrimination and retaliation claims but allowing the design-and-construction claim and reasonable accommodation claims regarding physical accessibility barriers to proceed to trial.
key_cases_cited: Steele v. Title Realty Co., 478 F.2d 380 (10th Cir. 1973)
Morgan v. Sec'y of Housing & Urban Development, 985 F.2d 1451 (10th Cir. 1993)
Kolstad v. Am. Dental Ass'n, 527 U.S. 526 (1999)
Curtis v. Loether, 415 U.S. 189 (1974)
Miller v. David Grace, Inc., 212 P.3d 1223 (Okla. 2009)

case_name: Joseph Backus v. I.Q. Data International, Inc., Tulsa Greenbriar I, LLC, and Gaines Investment Trust
accommodation_description: Request to transfer to an apartment closer to the mailbox due to difficulty walking (uses a cane)
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to adequately allege he actually requested an accommodation separate from submitting a notice to vacate, and the notice to vacate did not independently serve as a request for accommodation.
brief_summary: Joseph Backus, a 74-year-old tenant with mobility limitations requiring use of a cane, sued his former landlord and collection agency alleging he was denied a reasonable accommodation when the property manager allegedly refused his request to transfer to an apartment closer to the mailbox, and that defendants retaliated by collecting alleged fraudulent debts. The district court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to state a failure to accommodate claim because his notice to vacate did not constitute a proper accommodation request and he did not adequately allege he engaged in an interactive process before vacating the apartment.
key_cases_cited: Arnal v. Aspen View Condominium Assoc., Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Center v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Sanzaro v. Ardiente Homeowners Assoc., LLC, 364 F. Supp. 3d 1158 (D. Nev. 2019)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

case_name: Joseph Backus v. Tulsa Greenbriar I, LLC and Gaines Investment Trust
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to be transferred to an apartment closer to the mailbox due to mobility limitations from a leg injury requiring use of a cane.
key_holding: Although plaintiff adequately stated a failure to accommodate claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), the claim was barred by the two-year statute of limitations because plaintiff filed suit on May 20, 2020—more than two years after the April 24, 2018 denial of his accommodation request.
brief_summary: Joseph Backus sued Tulsa Greenbriar I, LLC and Gaines Investment Trust alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by denying his request to transfer to an apartment closer to the mailbox due to his mobility limitations. The court found that while plaintiff adequately pleaded a failure to accommodate claim, the claim was dismissed as time-barred because it was filed more than two years after the April 24, 2018 denial of his accommodation request.
key_cases_cited: Arnal v. Aspen View Condominium Assoc., Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Center v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9921378_2019_Bowman v. Merrimac Real Estate Holdings II LLC_okwd
case_name: Dana Bowman v. Merrimac Real Estate Holdings II, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled veteran, alleges he was deterred from renting due to accessibility barriers in the apartment complex's design and construction.
key_holding: The court denied SRT's motion to dismiss for lack of standing, finding plaintiff is an 'aggrieved person' under the FHA with standing to bring design-and-construction claims against the property developer and builder, while noting the scope of prospective relief remains to be determined.
brief_summary: A disabled veteran plaintiff sued Merrimac Real Estate Holdings II, LLC, Legacy Housing (the developer/contractor), and SRT-Landing (current owner as Remediation Defendant) alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on accessibility barriers in an apartment complex that deterred him from renting. The court denied SRT's motion to dismiss for lack of standing and Legacy's motion to join the dismissal request, permitting the design-and-construction accessibility claims to proceed while observing that plaintiff has filed over 100 similar cases and the scope of prospective relief remains an open question.
key_cases_cited: Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Tandy v. City of Wichita, 380 F.3d 1277 (10th Cir. 2004)
Bank of America Corp. v. City of Miami, Florida, 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Judy McAdoo v. Vici Community Development Corp., Cindy Arnold, and Amanda Gresham
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that Defendants clean her oven and allow her to install a shade on her porch to avoid direct sunlight due to a medication condition.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for Defendants on the § 3604(f)(3)(B) reasonable accommodation claim because Plaintiff failed to establish she requested accommodations that Defendants knew about and denied, noting that Defendants were barred from entering her apartment to clean the oven at her request and that Plaintiff provided no evidence Defendants knew of her alleged need to avoid sunlight.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Judy McAdoo sued Vici Community Development Corporation and its property managers alleging disability and sex discrimination under the Fair Housing Act, including claims that Defendants failed to accommodate her by cleaning her oven and allowing her to install a shade on her porch. The Court granted Defendants' motion for summary judgment on all federal claims, finding that Plaintiff failed to establish a prima facie case of discrimination because she did not provide record evidence supporting her factual allegations and admitted she was not discriminated against based on disability.
key_cases_cited: Shaw v. Cherokee Meadows, LP, 2019 WL 7340138 (N.D. Okla. Dec. 30, 2019)
Cinnamon Hills Youth Crisis Ctr. v. Saint George City, 685 F.3d 917 (10th Cir. 2012)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Watson v. Vici Community Development Corp.
accommodation_description: Tenant with permanent mobility impairments due to knee disorders requested to pay rent on the third Wednesday of each month to coincide with receipt of Social Security disability benefits.
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of the reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), finding Watson plausibly alleged that defendants terminated her third Wednesday payment privilege without notice and then denied the accommodation in practice by issuing lease violation notices even after reinstating it on paper.
brief_summary: Arleta Watson, a 79-year-old tenant with permanent mobility impairments at Vici Manor Apartments in Vici, Oklahoma, sued Vici Community Development Corp. and property manager Cindy Arnold alleging Fair Housing Act violations after defendants terminated her accommodation allowing rent payment on the third Wednesday of each month and issued lease violations when she had third parties deliver rent payments. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), finding Watson plausibly alleged that terminating the accommodation without notice and issuing repeated lease violation notices after reinstatement constituted a failure to accommodate, while dismissing claims under § 3604(f)(1)(C) and § 3604(f)(2)(C) as insufficiently pled.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condominium Association, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Shaw v. Cherokee Meadows, LP, 431 F. Supp. 3d 1336 (N.D. Okla. 2019)
Dubois v. Association of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

case_name: Sarah Melton v. State of Oklahoma ex rel. University of Oklahoma
accommodation_description: Student with heightened sensitivity to toxic mold requested professional remediation of mold infestation in her university dormitory room as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to plausibly allege a refusal to accommodate; the University began remediation efforts within days of the request, which did not constitute a refusal.
brief_summary: Sarah Melton, a student at the University of Oklahoma, sued the University and individual officials alleging that her dormitory's toxic mold infestation caused her serious health problems and that the University violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to accommodate her disability (heightened susceptibility to mold) through professional remediation. The court dismissed the FHA claim, finding that the University's prompt initiation of remediation efforts within days of the request did not constitute a refusal to accommodate.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Keys Youth Servs., Inc. v. City of Olathe, 248 F.3d 1267 (10th Cir. 2001)
Levy v. Kansas Department of Social & Rehabilitation Services, 789 F.3d 1164 (10th Cir. 2015)

case_name: Watson v. Vici Community Development Corp.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to pay rent on the third week of each month when she received her income, instead of the first of the month.
key_holding: The court found that Watson raised genuine issues of material fact as to whether her requests to pay rent on the third week of the month were necessary to afford her an equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, were reasonable, and were refused by defendants.
brief_summary: Arleta Watson, a tenant at Vici Manor Apartments with mobility impairments from knee surgeries, sued the property owner and manager alleging Fair Housing Act violations including failure to accommodate her disability by allowing her to pay rent on the third week of each month. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist on Watson's reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B), her discrimination claims under § 3604(f)(1)(A) and (f)(2)(A), and her retaliation claim under § 3617.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Corey v. Secretary, U.S. Dept. of Housing & Urban Development ex rel. Walker, 719 F.3d 322 (4th Cir. 2013)
Rodriguez v. Village Green Realty, Inc., 788 F.3d 31 (2d Cir. 2015)
Toyota Motor Manufacturing, Kentucky, Inc. v. Williams, 534 U.S. 184 (2002)
Housing Rights Center v. Sterling, 404 F. Supp. 2d 1179 (C.D. Cal. 2004)

source_file: 9923186_2022_CDMR LLC v. First United Bank and Trust Company_okwd
case_name: Miller v. First United Bank and Trust Company
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves racial discrimination in lending—Black plaintiffs allege First United Bank denied their LLC's loan application to purchase a 160-unit apartment complex because of the individual plaintiffs' race.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because the loan transaction was commercial rather than residential, the discrimination was alleged against commercial applicants rather than prospective residents, and plaintiffs did not sue on behalf of any protected class members who intended to live in the apartment complex.
brief_summary: Black individual plaintiffs and their LLC sued First United Bank alleging racial discrimination in denying their loan application to purchase a 160-unit apartment complex in Oklahoma City. The court granted partial dismissal, finding the FHA claims failed because the transaction was commercial and plaintiffs did not allege discrimination against any prospective residents of the property.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Home Quest Mortg. LLC v. American Family Mut. Ins. Co., 340 F. Supp. 2d 1177 (D. Kan. 2004)
Germain v. M&T Bank Corp., 111 F. Supp. 3d 506 (S.D.N.Y. 2015)
Domino's Pizza, Inc. v. McDonald, 546 U.S. 470 (2006)
Matthiesen v. Banc One Mortg. Corp., 173 F.3d 1242 (10th Cir. 1999)

case_name: Tyeashia M. Blackburn et al. v. Kelly Webb et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs alleged racial discrimination in rental housing under § 3604(a) and (b), not disability accommodation under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss because plaintiffs failed to plead sufficient factual matter to state a plausible claim of racial discrimination under § 3604(a) or § 3604(b), as they did not allege facts showing similarly situated neighbors were treated differently or that the housing opportunity remained available.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Blackburn and Freeman, proceeding pro se, sued landlords Kelly Webb and Rent King, LLC alleging racial discrimination in violation of the Fair Housing Act based on disputes over property repairs, non-renewal of tenancy, and an FED (forcible entry and detainer) action. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to plead sufficient facts to establish a plausible claim of racial discrimination under § 3604(a) or § 3604(b) because they did not adequately allege similarly situated neighbors were treated differently or that the housing opportunity remained available.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)

case_name: Tyeashia M. Blackburn; and Lezlie E. Freeman v. Kelly Webb; Rent King, LLC
accommodation_description: No accommodation request — case involves claims of racial discrimination in housing services and retaliation for filing housing complaints
key_holding: The court found plaintiffs stated plausible claims for racial discrimination under § 3604(b) and retaliation under § 3617, but dismissed the First Amendment claim because private landlords cannot violate constitutional rights.
brief_summary: Black tenants sued their private landlord and property management company alleging racial discrimination in housing services and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act. The court denied dismissal of the § 3604(b) discrimination claim (finding plaintiffs adequately alleged disparate treatment compared to white neighbors) and the § 3617 retaliation claim, but dismissed the First Amendment claim because private actors cannot violate constitutional rights.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Arnal v. Aspen View Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 226 F. Supp. 3d 1177 (D. Colo. 2016)
Mountain Side Mobile Estates P'ship v. Sec'y of Hous. & Urban Dev., 56 F.3d 1243 (10th Cir. 1995)
Manhattan Cmty. Access Corp. v. Halleck, 139 S.Ct. 1921 (2019)

case_name: Donald Watkins v. Property Management Services, Incorporated, et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request at issue; this case concerns alleged racial discrimination in access to rent-to-own housing and retaliation for complaining about racial discrimination.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on plaintiff's racial discrimination claim regarding termination of his tenancy and FHA retaliation claim because plaintiff failed to demonstrate pretext, but denied summary judgment on plaintiff's § 3604(d) claim that defendants misrepresented the availability of rent-to-own housing.
brief_summary: Donald Watkins, a Black male, sued Property Management Services, Inc. alleging racial discrimination in denial of access to rent-to-own housing and FHA retaliation after he complained about discriminatory practices and was subsequently evicted from his month-to-month tenancy. The court granted summary judgment for defendants on the termination/eviction discrimination claim and retaliation claim, finding plaintiff failed to demonstrate pretext, but denied summary judgment on plaintiff's § 3604(d) misrepresentation claim regarding availability of rent-to-own properties.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Asbury v. Brougham, 866 F.2d 1276 (10th Cir. 1989)
Crowe v. ADT Sec. Servs., Inc., 649 F.3d 1189 (10th Cir. 2011)
Lobato v. N.M. Env't Dep't, 733 F.3d 1283 (10th Cir. 2013)
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)

source_file: 9925253_2020_Hernandez v. Golf Course Estates Home Owners Association_ord
case_name: Hernandez v. Golf Course Estates Home Owners Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested that the HOA permit school buses to continue door-to-door transportation for their child with developmental disabilities as a reasonable accommodation under the Fair Housing Act.
key_holding: Both parties' summary judgment motions were denied because genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether the accommodation was necessary and reasonable, and whether individual board members can be held personally liable for the HOA's denial of the accommodation request.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Erika Hernandez and Paulo Regalado, on behalf of their minor child A.R. with developmental disabilities, sued the Golf Course Estates HOA alleging the Board violated the Fair Housing Amendments Act by refusing to permit school buses to provide door-to-door transportation for A.R. after the HOA prohibited buses from entering the private community streets. The Court denied both plaintiffs' motion for partial summary judgment and defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the accommodation was necessary and reasonable, and whether individual board members can be held personally liable.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Kuhn v. McNary Estates Homeowners Ass'n, Inc., 228 F. Supp. 3d 1142 (D. Or. 2017)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Sanzaro v. Ardiente Homeowners Ass'n, LLC, 364 F. Supp. 3d 1158 (D. Nev. 2019)
Fielder v. Sterling Park Homeowners Ass'n, 914 F. Supp. 2d 1222 (W.D. Wash. 2012)

case_name: Kollenburn v. County of Clackamas
accommodation_description: Request to prevent euthanasia of dog Lladk, claimed as a service animal/ESA/assistance animal assisting plaintiffs with emotional disabilities, so they can retain the animal despite the County's dangerous dog designation.
key_holding: The court denied the preliminary injunction because plaintiffs failed to show likelihood of success on the merits, the state court judgment had preclusive effect, Rooker-Feldman doctrine barred constitutional challenges, and the dog's serious bite of plaintiffs' child undermined any reasonable accommodation claim under the FHA and ADA.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Lanea and Caleb Kollenburn sued Clackamas County seeking to prevent euthanasia of their dog Lladk, which the County designated as a dangerous dog after it seriously bit one of their children; plaintiffs claimed the dog was a service animal/ESA protected under the FHA and ADA. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction, finding the constitutional claims barred by res judicata and Rooker-Feldman doctrine, and the accommodation claims unlikely to succeed because allowing a dangerous dog that had bitten a child did not constitute a reasonable accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Nat. Res. Def. Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
Alliance for the Wild Rockies v. Cottrell, 632 F.3d 1127 (9th Cir. 2011)
Borenstein v. Garden, 2019 WL 2062948 (D. Nev. May 9, 2019)
Roberts v. Veterans Vill. Enters., Inc., 2017 WL 1063477 (S.D. Cal. Mar. 20, 2017)
Updike v. Multnomah Cnty., 870 F.3d 939 (9th Cir. 2017)

case_name: Larry Johnson v. Guardian Management
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that his wife be recognized as his live-in aide so her income would be excluded from Section 8 housing subsidy calculations.
key_holding: The FHEO's categorical exclusion of spouses from serving as live-in aides is plainly erroneous and inconsistent with the regulatory definition in 24 C.F.R. § 5.403, and Plaintiff presented sufficient evidence creating a genuine dispute of material fact about whether his wife lives in the unit solely to provide disability-related supportive services.
brief_summary: Larry Johnson, a disabled senior tenant with mobility issues, sued his property manager and landlord alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to consider his wife as a live-in aide and excluding her income from Section 8 calculations. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, rejecting HUD's categorical exclusion of spouses as live-in aides as inconsistent with the regulatory definition and finding sufficient evidence that Johnson's wife moved in solely to provide disability-related caregiving services.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Hardaway v. D.C. Hous. Auth., 843 F.3d 973 (D.C. Cir. 2016)
Auer v. Robbins, 519 U.S. 452 (1997)

case_name: Jenine Kristina Macon v. Proud Ground Organization, Yesika Arevalo, Tyler Koski, Ryan Parker, Katie Ullrich, Pedro Cruz
accommodation_description: Plaintiff generally alleges she received no accommodation for her disability during her house search but does not describe a specific accommodation requested or her disability.
key_holding: The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss plaintiff's Fair Housing Act discrimination and retaliation claims because plaintiff failed to allege facts establishing discriminatory intent or that she engaged in protected activity under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jenine Kristina Macon sued Proud Ground Organization and individual defendants alleging discrimination based on race and disability and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act related to her attempt to purchase a home through Proud Ground's first-time home ownership program. The court denied plaintiff's motions for default and default judgment, and granted defendants' motions to dismiss for failure to state claims because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient facts showing discriminatory intent or protected activity.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: 9927324_2021_Kononen v. City of Salem Housing Authority_ord
case_name: Katie M. Kononen v. City of Salem Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to have an additional dog in her home as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff's complaint failed to state a viable disability discrimination claim because it contained contradictory allegations (that defendant both denied and approved her accommodation request) and lacked sufficient underlying factual allegations to provide fair notice to defendant.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Katie Kononen sued the City of Salem Housing Authority alleging it denied her reasonable accommodation request to have an additional dog as a disability-related assistance animal. The magistrate judge recommended granting defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint internally contradictory and lacking sufficient factual allegations to support a prima facie case under the FHA, ADA, and Section 504.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
United States v. California Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Starr v. Baca, 652 F.3d 1202 (9th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Larry Johnson v. Guardian Management and Uptown Towers
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested his wife be recognized as his live-in aide under Section 8 regulations so her income would be excluded from household income calculations for rent purposes.
key_holding: Although Mrs. Johnson meets all three prongs of the HUD regulatory definition of a live-in aide, defendants did not refuse the requested accommodation because they neither increased Mr. Johnson's rent nor required him to vacate the unit, and thus no FHA discrimination claim can succeed under DuBois.
brief_summary: Larry Johnson, a disabled Section 8 tenant, sued his property management company and landlord seeking recognition of his wife as a live-in aide so her income would be excluded from rent calculations. After a bench trial, the court found that while Mrs. Johnson satisfies all three HUD regulatory prongs of the live-in aide definition, defendants did not unlawfully refuse the accommodation because they neither increased Johnson's rent nor removed him from the unit.
key_cases_cited: DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
U.S. v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt. Co., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Park v. Vill. Apartment Tenants Ass'n v. Mortimer Tr., 636 F.3d 1150 (9th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Larry Johnson v. Guardian Management and Uptown Towers
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that his wife be classified as a live-in aide so her income would be excluded from annual income calculations for Section 8 housing subsidy purposes.
key_holding: Although the court found Mrs. Johnson met the requirements of a live-in aide and that treating her as such was necessary to afford Plaintiff an equal opportunity under § 3604(f), no discrimination occurred because Defendants never raised Plaintiff's rent or required him to vacate—the accommodation was never actually refused.
brief_summary: Larry Johnson sued his Section 8 landlords alleging they discriminated against him by refusing to classify his wife as a live-in aide to exclude her income from rent calculations as a reasonable accommodation for his disability. The court held that while Mrs. Johnson qualifies as a live-in aide under 24 C.F.R. § 5.403 and excluding her income would be a necessary accommodation, no discrimination occurred because Defendants never raised Johnson's rent or required him to leave—meaning the accommodation was not actually refused.
key_cases_cited: Christiansburg Garment Co. v. Equal Employment Opportunity Comm'n, 434 U.S. 412 (1978)
Green v. Mercy Hous., Inc., 991 F.3d 1056 (9th Cir. 2021)
Buckhannon Bd. and Care Home, Inc. v. West Virginia Dept. of Health and Human Resources, 532 U.S. 598 (2001)
Farrar v. Hobby, 506 U.S. 103 (1992)
Northcross v. Bd. of Ed. of Memphis City Sch., 412 U.S. 427 (1973)

case_name: Hume v. Guardian Management LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a mobility-impaired resident, requested an accessible parking space and signage at her assigned parking space at the senior/disabled housing complex.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants because (1) the five-month delay in providing a parking space was not unreasonable given no spaces were available, (2) plaintiff failed to meet her burden to show why an accessible parking space is necessary rather than the regular assigned space she accepted, and (3) plaintiff provided no explanation why signage at her exclusively assigned space is required for equal use and enjoyment.
brief_summary: Michelle Hume sued Guardian Management LLC and Uptown Tower Apartments alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to provide her, a mobility-disabled resident, with an accessible parking space and signage. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, finding no unreasonable delay in assigning her a parking space, that she failed to demonstrate an accessible space was necessary rather than the regular space she accepted, and that she provided no evidence supporting her signage request.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Shapiro v. Cadman Towers, Inc., 51 F.3d 328 (2d Cir. 1995)
Elliott v. QF Circa 37, LLC, 2018 WL 2933467 (S.D. Cal. 2018)
Desch v. Wheatley, 2010 WL 11506404 (W.D. Tex. 2010)

case_name: Gibson v. Community Development Partners
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged that defendants failed to address unsafe, unclean, and dangerous housing conditions that affected her peaceful enjoyment of her dwelling due to her gender dysphoria, but she did not specify a concrete accommodation request.
key_holding: The court dismissed Gibson's reasonable accommodation claim because she failed to allege what specific accommodation she sought, how it was necessary to address her gender dysphoria, or how it would afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling.
brief_summary: Katie A. Gibson, a transgender woman with gender dysphoria, sued Community Development Partners and Guardian Management LLC (operators of Milepost 5 Studios in Portland, Oregon), alleging Fair Housing Act claims for disparate treatment, disparate impact, and failure to make reasonable accommodations based on unsafe living conditions and tenant harassment. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Gibson failed to plead sufficient facts to state any FHA claim, including failing to identify what specific accommodation she requested or how it related to her disability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Texas Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
McGary v. City of Portland, 386 F.3d 1259 (9th Cir. 2009)

case_name: Michelle Hume v. Guardian Management LLC, Uptown Tower Apartments, and Lisa Simonson
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested immediate assignment of a permanent parking space in the residential garage as a reasonable accommodation for her mobility disability requiring wheelchair use.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for defendants on claims for accessible parking space and signage, but denied summary judgment on plaintiff's claim that defendants unreasonably delayed from 2014 to 2021 in providing an assigned parking space, because genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether the delay constituted a constructive denial.
brief_summary: Michelle Hume sued Guardian Management LLC and Uptown Tower Apartments alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to provide her an accessible parking space. The court granted in part defendants' motion for summary judgment, dismissing plaintiff's claims regarding accessible parking and signage because plaintiff failed to show those accommodations were reasonable on their face, but permitted plaintiff's unreasonable delay claim to proceed because genuine disputes exist as to whether defendants' seven-year delay in assigning her a parking space constituted a constructive denial.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
DuBois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Shapiro v. Cadman Towers, Inc., 51 F.3d 328 (2d Cir. 1995)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condominium Ass'n, Inc., 756 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)

case_name: Hume v. Guardian Management LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Michelle Hume, a wheelchair user, requested an accessible parking space as a reasonable accommodation for her mobility disability.
key_holding: The court found a genuine issue of fact for jury determination as to whether the landlord unreasonably delayed accommodating the plaintiff's parking request from 2014 through July 2021, thereby constructively denying the accommodation, while upholding summary judgment on other claims.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michelle Hume sued Guardian Management LLC and Uptown Tower Apartments alleging they failed to provide her with an accessible parking space as a reasonable accommodation for her wheelchair use and unreasonably delayed in accommodating her request over several years. The court granted in part Hume's motion for reconsideration of summary judgment, finding a genuine dispute of material fact exists regarding whether the landlord constructively denied the parking accommodation through delay from 2014 to 2021, warranting trial on that limited claim.
key_cases_cited: Kona Enters., Inc. v. Estate of Bishop
Bateman v. U.S. Postal Serv.
Casey v. Albertson's Inc.

case_name: Schrader v. Pakseresht
accommodation_description: Disabled adults seek to continue receiving Medicaid-funded community living support services while living with their caregivers who are employees of Specialty Family Homes, rather than being forced to relocate to eligible settings or obtain care from different providers.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motion for preliminary injunction, finding that plaintiffs lack standing to assert disability discrimination claims on behalf of their disabled clients, and that Oregon's rule clarifying licensing requirements for community living supports does not constitute disability-based discrimination because it applies regardless of disability status.
brief_summary: Joan Schrader and Specialty Family Homes, LLC sued Oregon's ODHS Director seeking to enjoin enforcement of a rule that restricts Medicaid-funded community living supports to settings owned or controlled by the care provider, alleging it violates the FHA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act by preventing disabled adults from choosing to live with their caregivers. The court denied the preliminary injunction, finding plaintiffs lack standing because they are not disabled individuals and have not demonstrated independent injury, and alternatively that the rule does not discriminate based on disability as it applies licensing requirements uniformly regardless of disability status.
key_cases_cited: Weinreich v. L.A. Cnty. Metro. Transp. Auth.
Dubois v. Ass'n of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua
Howard v. HMK Holdings, LLC
Summers v. City of Fitchburg
Winter v. Natural Res. Def. Council Inc.

source_file: 9930022_2023_Adgerson v. Central City Concern Housing Administration_ord
case_name: Robert L. Adgerson v. Central City Concern Housing Administration
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought to enjoin Central City Concern from evicting or discharging him from its residential treatment program, alleging this eviction violated his civil rights under housing accommodation law.
key_holding: The court denied Plaintiff's TRO motion because Plaintiff failed to show a likelihood of success on the merits or serious questions going to the merits of his Fair Housing Act claim, as he did not allege that Central City Concern made any decisions because of his race, color, religion, sex, familial status, national origin, or disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Robert L. Adgerson filed a TRO motion seeking to prevent Central City Concern Housing Administration from evicting him from its residential treatment program, asserting a Fair Housing Act claim for civil rights housing accommodation. The court denied the TRO because Plaintiff failed to allege facts showing that Central City Concern made any housing decisions because of a protected characteristic under the FHA, and also failed to establish a viable § 1983 claim or sufficient grounds for state law claims.
key_cases_cited: Winter v. Nat. Res. Defense Council, Inc., 555 U.S. 7 (2008)
All. for the Wild Rockies v. Cottrell, 632 F.3d 1127 (9th Cir. 2011)
Stuhlbarg Int'l Sales Co. v. John D. Brush & Co., 240 F.3d 832 (9th Cir. 2001)
Intermountain Fair Hous. Council v. Boise Rescue Mission Ministries, 657 F.3d 988 (9th Cir. 2011)
Hebbe v. Pliler, 627 F.3d 338 (9th Cir. 2010)

case_name: Hume v. Guardian Management LLC
accommodation_description: Request for assigned/reserved parking space as a reasonable accommodation for disability
key_holding: The Court granted Plaintiff's motion to strike Defendants' second summary judgment motion as duplicative because Defendants raised arguments previously made or that could have been raised in their first motion without identifying new controlling authority or new evidence.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Michelle Hume sued Guardian Management LLC, Uptown Tower Apartments, and Lisa Simonson for Fair Housing Act violations, alleging Defendants unreasonably delayed granting her parking accommodation request from 2014 until July 2021. The Court granted Plaintiff's motion to strike Defendants' second summary judgment motion as duplicative, finding Defendants presented no new controlling authority or new evidence justifying a second motion filed over a year after the Court's Amended Opinion and one month before trial.
key_cases_cited: Hoffman v. Tonnemacher, 593 F.3d 908 (9th Cir. 2010)
Nguyen v. United States, 792 F.2d 1500 (9th Cir. 1986)
Doherty v. Portland Cmty. Coll., 2000 WL 33200560 (D. Or. Nov. 15, 2000)
Slack v. O'Fairoll, 2013 WL 1010683 (W.D. Wash. Jan. 14, 2013)
MCM Cap. Partners, LLC v. Saticoy Bay LLC Series 6684 Coronado Crest, 2018 WL 4113332 (D. Nev. Aug. 29, 2018)

case_name: Sonja Marie Boyce v. St. Vincent DePaul (Lane County)
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was clearly articulated or substantively analyzed; the complaint references staircase/railings installation and relocation but does not frame these as reasonable accommodation requests.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claim for failure to state a prima facie case because plaintiff did not allege facts showing the housing opportunity remained available to similarly situated non-protected class individuals (disparate treatment) or that any facially neutral defendant practices caused discriminatory impact (disparate impact).
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sonja Marie Boyce filed suit against St. Vincent DePaul and GBM alleging FHA violations, constitutional claims, and landlord-tenant disputes arising from an alleged illegal eviction and back rent dispute. The court dismissed the complaint under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2) for failing to establish federal jurisdiction and for failure to state viable FHA claims, finding plaintiff did not plead sufficient facts to establish prima facie cases of disparate treatment or disparate impact housing discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 554 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

case_name: Magistrate Mahdi Sufi El v. People's Emergency Center
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled veteran and Native American Moor, alleged discriminatory eviction from his apartment at 4050 Haverford Avenue and denial of access to non-residential spaces.
key_holding: The court dismissed claims against PEC with prejudice as precluded by the prior settlement in El I; dismissed other FHA eviction claims without prejudice for failure to plead discriminatory intent or effect; and dismissed all claims regarding the LAVA space because the Fair Housing Act applies only to dwellings, not commercial spaces.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff sued People's Emergency Center and other defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on discriminatory eviction from his apartment and denial of access to various spaces. The court granted motions to dismiss, finding (1) claims against PEC were precluded by a prior settlement, (2) claims against other defendants failed to plead discriminatory intent or effect, and (3) claims regarding the LAVA commercial space failed because the FHA applies only to dwellings.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Doe v. City of Butler, 892 F.2d 315 (3d Cir. 1989)
El v. People's Emergency Center, 315 F. Supp. 3d (E.D. Pa. 2018)

source_file: 9932121_2020_APONTE v. UNITED STATES DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING AND URBAN DEVELOPMENT_paed
case_name: Shanicqua Aponte v. United States Department of Housing and Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiff made only generalized, conclusory allegations that she was discriminated against because she is Black, with no specific accommodation request articulated.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because her conclusory allegation that she was discriminated against 'because of my color of being black' failed to state a plausible claim under §§ 3604(a) and (b) given that her complaint focused primarily on habitability issues and repair failures rather than discriminatory conduct.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Shanicqua Aponte sued HUD, the Montgomery County Housing Authority, a property inspection company, the Borough of Pottstown, and her landlord alleging fair housing violations, constitutional rights violations under § 1983, and state law claims related to uninhabitable housing conditions. The court dismissed her FHA claim because her conclusory allegation of racial discrimination without factual support failed to state a plausible claim, and dismissed her § 1983 claims for failure to allege municipal policies causing constitutional violations or state action by private defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Doe v. City of Butler, 892 F.2d 315 (3d Cir. 1989)
United States v. Balistrieri, 981 F.2d 916 (7th Cir. 1992)

source_file: 9932182_2020_HORIZON HOUSE, INC. v. EAST NORRITON TOWNSHIP_paed
case_name: Horizon House, Inc. v. East Norriton Township
accommodation_description: Request that defendant waive the conditions for obtaining a Special Exception for a Group Home (fire sprinkler system, fire alarm, parking, staffing, and disability disclosure requirements) so that plaintiff can establish a Community Home for up to three intellectually and developmentally disabled individuals
key_holding: The reasonable accommodation claim was dismissed because plaintiff alleged only that the requested waiver of zoning conditions would cause additional costs and delays, but failed to plausibly allege that the accommodation was essential or required to make the Community Home financially viable rather than merely preferable.
brief_summary: Horizon House, Inc., a non-profit operator of Community Homes for individuals with intellectual and developmental disabilities, sued East Norriton Township after being denied a Use and Occupancy permit and required to obtain a special exception as a "Group Home" subject to additional conditions including sprinkler systems and staffing requirements. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss the reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiff failed to plausibly allege the requested waiver of conditions was essential rather than merely causing additional costs and delays, while denying dismissal of the disparate treatment and disparate impact claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Larkin v. State of Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Sharpvisions, Inc. v. Borough of Plum, 475 F. Supp. 2d 514 (W.D. Pa. 2007)

source_file: 9932222_2020_GLYNN v. ELLSWORTH CONGREGATION OF LEHOVAH'S WITNESSES_paed
case_name: Glynn v. Ellsworth Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses
accommodation_description: The incomprehensible complaint contains no discernible accommodation request, consisting primarily of biblical scripture and demands that defendants 'do according to all that the Levitical Priest will instruct.'
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed without prejudice for failure to state a claim because it provided no factual allegations whatsoever—only biblical verses and incomprehensible demands—failing to put defendants on notice or establish any basis for federal jurisdiction.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Jermaine Glynn filed an in forma pauperis complaint against the Ellsworth Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses alleging housing discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 3604, but the complaint contained only biblical scripture and incomprehensible demands without any factual allegations. The E.D. Pa. court dismissed the complaint without prejudice for failure to state a claim under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B)(ii) and Rule 8, granting Glynn leave to file an amended complaint.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Tourscher v. McCullough, 184 F.3d 236 (3d Cir. 1999)
Grayson v. Mayview State Hosp., 293 F.3d 103 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Catherine Coyne v. Holy Family Apartments, Allentown Archdiocese, Randy Wadsworth, Karen Ohm, Sarosky Heating & Air Conditioning, and HUD
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested transfer to a first-floor accessible unit after being reassigned to a second-floor senior unit following an assault and subsequent injury (broken foot, rotator cuff injury, PTSD).
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHAA claims because she failed to plead sufficient facts showing that a first-floor unit was necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy the dwelling, as required by 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B), and because she could not establish a nexus between her alleged disabilities and her accommodation requests.
brief_summary: Catherine Coyne, a disabled resident of Holy Family Apartments in Bethlehem, PA, sued the property management company and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Amendments Act violations after they failed to move her to a first-floor accessible unit and make repairs to her unit. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts demonstrating that her requested accommodations were necessary to afford her equal housing opportunity under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Vorchheimer v. Phila. Hous. Auth., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9932569_2020_WOMACK v. HOUSING AUTHORITY OF CHESTER COUNTY_paed
case_name: Womack v. Housing Authority of Chester County
accommodation_description: Requests for voucher term extensions, increased payment standards, and exception rents under HUD regulations as reasonable accommodations for family members' disabilities
key_holding: The court denied dismissal of plaintiffs' FHA reasonable accommodation claim because plaintiffs plausibly alleged HACC denied requests for voucher extensions, payment standard increases, and exception rents under HUD regulations as disability accommodations, and whether these denials were reasonable cannot be determined at the pleadings stage.
brief_summary: Mellissa Womack and her two disabled children sued the Housing Authority of Chester County under the Fair Housing Act, alleging HACC failed to make reasonable accommodations by denying requests for voucher term extensions, increased payment standards, and exception rents under HUD regulations. The court denied HACC's motion to dismiss the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding plaintiffs plausibly alleged HACC denied disability-related accommodation requests.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware County, 983 F.2d 1277 (3d Cir. 1993)

case_name: Davis v. Rubin
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request; complaint challenges eviction and alleged harassment based on disability.
key_holding: The court dismissed Davis's FHA claims for failure to state a plausible claim, finding her allegations of disability discrimination, harassment, and retaliation were conclusory and failed to allege sufficient factual matter from which discriminatory motive or causal connection could be inferred.
brief_summary: Krystal Davis, a Black woman with a chronic illness, sued her landlords Daniel Rubin and Shannon McGroarty alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disability discrimination, harassment, and retaliation following her eviction from a Section 8 property. The court dismissed her complaint without prejudice for failure to state plausible claims, finding her allegations were conclusory and failed to adequately allege that disability was a motivating factor in her eviction or that retaliation was causally connected to her protected activity of filing a HUD complaint.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
White v. Barbe, 767 F. App'x 332 (3d Cir. 2019)

case_name: Thermuthis Lee v. HUD, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, proceeding pro se, generally alleges discrimination and retaliation related to her medical conditions (hypertension, heart issues, pending organ transplant) due to HUD's failure to intervene with the Philadelphia Housing Authority regarding her neighbors.
key_holding: The court dismissed Lee's FHA claim because she failed to allege an unlawful discriminatory housing practice by HUD or its employees as a result of her disability; her generalized, conclusory allegations of discrimination and retaliation did not state a plausible FHA claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Thermuthis Lee sued HUD and three HUD employees alleging they failed to intervene with the Philadelphia Housing Authority regarding her neighbors' alleged harassment and the PHA's failure to evict them, causing aggravation of her health conditions. The court granted leave to proceed in forma pauperis but dismissed the complaint with prejudice for failure to state a plausible Fair Housing Act claim, concluding that Lee's conclusory allegations of disability-based discrimination and retaliation did not plead an unlawful discriminatory housing practice.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Sharpvisions, Inc. v. Borough of Plum, 475 F. Supp. 2d 514 (W.D. Pa. 2007)
Lee v. Petrolichio, 697 F. App'x 112 (3d Cir. 2017)
Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents of Federal Bureau of Narcotics, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)

case_name: Alexy Dejesus v. Vicky; Arbor Village & Old Forge Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to use his Section 8 housing voucher to rent a one-bedroom apartment at defendants' property.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because private landlords are not required to participate in the Section 8 voucher program, and discrimination based on voucher status alone does not state a cognizable FHA claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Alexy Dejesus sued private landlords Vicky and Arbor Village & Old Forge Apartments, alleging Fair Housing Act discrimination after they refused to rent him a one-bedroom apartment because they do not accept Section 8 housing vouchers. The court dismissed the complaint, holding that source-of-income discrimination based on Section 8 voucher status is not prohibited under the FHA since participation in the voucher program is voluntary for private landlords.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Lincoln Prop. Co.
Graoch Assocs. #33, L.P. v. Louisville/Jefferson Cnty. Metro Human Relations Comm'n

source_file: 9934801_2021_FAIR HOUSING RIGHTS CENTER IN SOUTHEASTERN PENNSYLVANIA v. SJ LOFTS, LLC_paed
case_name: Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania v. SJ Lofts, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Tester-identified design and construction deficiencies at Michener Lofts including thermostat height, balcony door width, bathroom clearance, and electrical outlet height that allegedly render the building inaccessible to wheelchair users.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the Streamline Entities because FHRC alleged sufficient facts showing their involvement in construction and identified specific design violations, but granted dismissal without prejudice as to Michael Stillwell individually because the complaint contained only conclusory alter-ego allegations lacking sufficient factual detail.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania sued SJ Lofts, Lion Construction LLC, Streamline Entities, and Michael Stillwell alleging that Michener Lofts, a 44-unit apartment building constructed in Philadelphia in 2018, contained design and construction violations that discriminated against people with disabilities. The court denied the motion to dismiss as to the Streamline Entities, finding FHRC alleged sufficient facts to proceed, but dismissed individual defendant Stillwell without prejudice for failing to allege sufficient facts to support alter-ego liability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Fair Housing Rights Center in Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Post Goldtex GP, LLC, 823 F.3d 209 (3d Cir. 2016)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)

case_name: Reginald Carter v. Donna M. Murray
accommodation_description: Plaintiff with lung disease/COPD requested permanent relocation and implementation of a smoke-free policy as accommodation for disability-related environmental sensitivities.
key_holding: The complaint failed to state a plausible FHA reasonable accommodation claim because defendants addressed plaintiff's requests by offering alternative housing, hotel relocation during repairs, and proposing a smoke-free policy, and plaintiff rejected these accommodations.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Reginald Carter sued property management officials at Venango House in Philadelphia alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race and disability, claiming defendants failed to accommodate his lung disease by refusing to relocate him or implement a smoke-free policy. The court granted leave to proceed in forma pauperis and dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding Carter's race discrimination allegations were conclusory and his reasonable accommodation claim implausible because defendants had offered and implemented accommodations.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
White v. Barber, 767 F. App'x 332 (3d Cir. 2019)
Wilson v. Hillsborough Twp. Constr. Dep't, 779 F. App'x 969 (3d Cir. 2019)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: Justin C. Heimbach v. Ronald Hill
accommodation_description: Plaintiff did not request a reasonable accommodation; he seeks federal court review of an adverse state court eviction judgment as a FHA violation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because his complaint contained no factual allegations suggesting discrimination on any impermissible basis including race, color, religion, sex, familial status, national origin, or disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Heimbach sued landlord Hill seeking federal court review of an adverse state court eviction judgment and claiming violations of the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed claims seeking review of state court judgments under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine and dismissed FHA claims for failure to state a claim because the complaint contained no factual allegations of discrimination on any protected basis.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Great W. Mining & Mineral Co. v. Fox Rothschild LLP, 615 F.3d 159 (3d Cir. 2010)

case_name: DeJesus v. Lucey
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges defendants rented him an illegal apartment lacking heat and seeks compensation for paid rent; no disability or accommodation request is alleged.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because he failed to allege discrimination based on any protected characteristic, instead merely complaining that defendants rented him an illegal apartment lacking heat.
brief_summary: Alex DeJesus, proceeding pro se, sued private landlords Bill Lucey and Martin Garcia under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the Fair Housing Act, alleging they rented him an illegal apartment lacking heat and sought to evict him. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a claim, finding no plausible FHA claim because plaintiff did not allege discrimination based on any protected characteristic.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly

case_name: Eldon Chick, et al. v. Serrano Acquisitions, et al.
accommodation_description: Homeless plaintiffs who occupied an abandoned property without permission seek declaratory relief and injunction against eviction under the Fair Housing Act, but no formal reasonable accommodation request was made to a housing provider.
key_holding: Plaintiffs fail to state a claim under the FHA because they are neither buyers nor renters within the meaning of the Act, and they have not alleged any facts suggesting HSBC Bank made the property unavailable because of a protected characteristic under § 3604(a).
brief_summary: Homeless plaintiffs Eldon Chick and others, represented by the Poor People's Economic Human Rights Campaign, sued private property owners and government entities seeking to remain in an abandoned property they occupied without permission, asserting Fair Housing Act and constitutional claims. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, holding that plaintiffs failed to state a claim because they were neither buyers nor renters under the FHA and had not adequately alleged discrimination based on race or other protected characteristics.

source_file: 9936025_2022_HONKALA v. U.S. DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING _ URBAN DEVELOPMENT_paed
case_name: Cheri Honkala et al v. U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development et al
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are homeless individuals who occupied a vacant property owned by the Philadelphia Housing Authority without permission and sought declaratory relief to remain in the property.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' Fair Housing Act claims because plaintiffs are not 'buyers or renters' under the FHA—they occupied the property without permission from the owner, and their unilateral decision to rehabilitate the property does not constitute consideration creating a legally enforceable rental relationship.
brief_summary: Homeless individuals and the Poor People's Economic Human Rights Campaign sued the Philadelphia Housing Authority, HUD, and other defendants after occupying a vacant PHA-owned property without permission, claiming Fair Housing Act discrimination, constitutional violations, and seeking declaratory relief to remain in the property. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that plaintiffs lack standing under the FHA because they are not 'buyers or renters'—they occupied the property without any agreement with the owner—and that their constitutional claims also fail for lack of a protected property interest.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware Cty., 983 F.2d 1277 (3d Cir. 1993)
Wartluft v. Milton Hershey Sch. and Sch. Trust, 400 F. Supp. 3d 91 (M.D. Pa. 2019)
Pocono Springs Civic. Assoc., Inc. v. MacKenzie, 667 A.2d 233 (Pa. 1995)
City of Boerne v. Flores, 521 U.S. 507 (1997)

source_file: 9936337_2022_United States v. THE DORCHESTER OWNERS ASSOCIATION_paed
case_name: United States v. The Dorchester Owners Association
accommodation_description: Ms. Hamburg requested an accommodation to keep an emotional support animal (dog) in her condominium unit despite the Dorchester Owners Association's no-pet policy.
key_holding: The court denied DOA's motion for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether Ms. Hamburg is a person with a disability, whether she has a disability-related need for an ESA, and whether DOA engaged in the interactive process in good faith or constructively refused the accommodation through delay.
brief_summary: The United States and plaintiff-intervenor Louise Hamburg sued the Dorchester Owners Association, alleging it violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to grant Ms. Hamburg a reasonable accommodation for an emotional support animal under its no-pet policy. The court denied DOA's motion for summary judgment, finding genuine factual disputes exist as to Ms. Hamburg's disability status, the legitimacy of her ESA need, and whether DOA's handling of the request constituted a constructive refusal to accommodate.
key_cases_cited: Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Lapid-Laurel, LLC v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Vorchheimer v. Pennrose Properties, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Castillo Condo. Ass'n v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 821 F.3d 92 (1st Cir. 2016)

case_name: Aimee Moran and Jessica Mazzeo v. Rockwell Development Group, LLC, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs are single mothers alleging sex discrimination and harassment in the sale of new construction homes; no disability accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court held that single mothers constitute a protected class under the FHA through a sex-plus-marital-status discrimination theory, and plaintiffs adequately alleged harassment sufficient to survive dismissal, while dismissing fraud and negligent misrepresentation claims under the gist-of-the-action doctrine.
brief_summary: Two single mothers sued a housing developer alleging sex discrimination and harassment during home construction and purchase, claiming they received inferior service and were sexually harassed by employees compared to other homebuyers. The court denied the motion to dismiss on the FHA claims, finding plaintiffs adequately alleged protected-class discrimination and hostile-environment harassment, but granted dismissal on fraud and negligent misrepresentation claims as barred by the gist-of-the-action doctrine.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n
Rosencrans v. Quixote Enters.
Curto v. A Country Place Condo. Assoc.
