case_name: Edward Haywood Payne, Jr. v. The Galman Group and Sedgwick Station Apartments
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges the landlord selectively enforced its no-dog policy against white tenants, constituting racial discrimination in provision of housing services.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state a plausible race discrimination claim because plaintiff admits he never requested permission to keep a dog, does not know whether the white tenant paid a pet deposit, and provides only vague, conclusory allegations of racial motive rather than factual allegations sufficient to establish a prima facie case.
brief_summary: Black tenant Edward Haywood Payne sued his landlords alleging they discriminated against him by race in enforcing the building's no-dog policy, allowing a white tenant to keep a dog while he would not be permitted to do so. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint deficient because Payne never requested a dog accommodation, admitted he had no knowledge about whether the white tenant paid a pet deposit, and provided only speculative, conclusory allegations about racial discrimination insufficient to establish a plausible claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Community Services, Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Authority, 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Fowler v. UPMC Shadyside, 578 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2009)
Curto v. A Country Place Condominium Assoc., Inc., 921 F.3d 405 (3d Cir. 2019)

source_file: 9936771_2022_United States v. ONE BOYERTOWN PROPERTIES LP_paed
case_name: United States of America v. J. Randolph Parry Architects, P.C., et al.
accommodation_description: The United States alleges that fifteen senior living facilities across Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Virginia, and Connecticut violate design and construction accessibility requirements under the FHA and ADA.
key_holding: The court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the architect's state law indemnification and contribution crossclaims and third-party complaints, finding they would substantially predominate over the federal civil rights claims and that judicial economy, convenience, and fairness did not support accepting jurisdiction.
brief_summary: The United States brought this enforcement action under the Fair Housing Act and ADA against facility owners and an architect, alleging that fifteen senior living facilities across four states fail to meet disability accessibility requirements. The court granted motions to dismiss the architect's crossclaims and third-party complaint, declining supplemental jurisdiction over the architect's state law indemnification and contribution claims because they would substantially predominate over the federal civil rights claims and the principles of judicial economy, convenience, and fairness did not support accepting jurisdiction.
key_cases_cited: City of Chicago v. Int'l Coll. of Surgeons, 522 U.S. 156 (1997)
United Mine Workers of Am. v. Gibbs, 383 U.S. 715 (1966)
Growth Horizons, Inc. v. Delaware Cnty., Pa., 983 F.2d 1277 (3d Cir. 1993)
Borough of W. Mifflin v. Lancaster, 45 F.3d 780 (3d Cir. 1995)

source_file: 9937109_2022_HORIZON HOUSE, INC. v. EAST NORRITON TOWNSHIP_paed
case_name: Horizon House, Inc. v. East Norriton Township
accommodation_description: Horizon House requested to be allowed to use the property as a single-family dwelling for up to three individuals with intellectual disabilities without complying with the Township's group home special exception requirements.
key_holding: The Township's zoning ordinance is facially discriminatory because it imposes significant additional burdens (structural modifications, disclosure of disabilities, around-the-clock staffing) on property owners who provide housing to individuals with disabilities compared to single-family dwellings housing unrelated individuals, and the Township failed to justify this disparate treatment.
brief_summary: Horizon House, a nonprofit that provides supportive housing services to individuals with intellectual disabilities, sued East Norriton Township alleging the municipality violated the Fair Housing Act by treating its proposed single-family home use as a group home requiring a special exception with costly conditions. The court granted summary judgment for Horizon House, finding the zoning ordinance facially discriminatory because it imposes significant additional requirements on housing for disabled individuals compared to single-family dwellings.
key_cases_cited: Hovsons, Inc. v. Twp. of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Larkin v. State of Mich. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Sharpvisions, Inc. v. Borough of Plum, 475 F. Supp. 2d 514 (W.D. Pa. 2007)
Barnabei v. Chadds Ford Twp., 125 F. Supp. 3d 515 (E.D. Pa. 2015)

source_file: 9937341_2022_ALLENTOWN VICTORY CHURCH v. CITY OF ALLENTOWN, PENNSYLVANIA_paed
case_name: Allentown Victory Church v. City of Allentown, Pennsylvania
accommodation_description: AVC requested a zoning variance to operate its drug and alcohol recovery facility with 15-18 residents instead of the maximum 12 residents permitted under Allentown's zoning ordinance for group homes.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment for the city because AVC failed to produce sufficient evidence that a 15-18 resident facility was necessary to make the recovery home financially viable, presenting only conclusory statements rather than specific financial data showing why 12 residents would be inadequate.
brief_summary: Allentown Victory Church, operator of a Christian drug and alcohol recovery home for men, sued the City of Allentown after its Zoning Hearing Board denied requests to house 15-18 residents despite the zoning ordinance capping group homes at 12 residents. The court granted summary judgment for the city on all FHA claims, finding the church failed to demonstrate that operating with more than 12 residents was necessary for financial viability.
key_cases_cited: 431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
New Directions Treatment Servs. v. City of Reading, 490 F.3d 293 (3d Cir. 2007)
Dr. Gertrude A. Barber Ctr., Inc. v. Peters Twp., 273 F. Supp. 2d 643 (W.D. Pa. 2003)
Get Back Up, Inc. v. City of Detroit, 606 F. App'x 792 (6th Cir. 2015)

case_name: King Anthony Alvarez v. HUD, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; plaintiff alleged retaliation for filing complaints and lawsuits against TURN regarding housing conditions and funding mismanagement.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims because he failed to allege membership in a protected class, which is fatal to any FHA claim regardless of whether the claims are framed as discrimination, interference, or retaliation.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff King Anthony Alvarez, proceeding in forma pauperis, filed a Second Amended Complaint against HUD, the City of Philadelphia, DHCD, TURN (a nonprofit housing organization), and various individuals alleging that his subsidized housing benefits were terminated in retaliation for criticizing TURN and filing this lawsuit. The court dismissed the Second Amended Complaint, finding that plaintiff's FHA claims failed because he did not allege membership in a protected class, and dismissed state law claims for lack of diversity jurisdiction.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Mt. Holly Gardens Citizens in Action, Inc. v. Twp. of Mount Holly, 658 F.3d 375 (3d Cir. 2011)
Dew v. S. Columbia Terrace, LLC, 854 F. App'x 460 (3d Cir. 2021)

source_file: 9938100_2023_United States v. THE DORCHESTER OWNERS ASSOCIATION_paed
case_name: United States v. The Dorchester Owners Association
accommodation_description: Request by residents with mental health disabilities to keep emotional support animals in a no-pet high-rise condominium, including waiver of no-pet policies, insurance requirements, common area restrictions, and breed/weight restrictions.
key_holding: The jury found the DOA engaged in a pattern or practice of disability discrimination through unreasonable delays and conditions on ESA applications, but the court denied punitive damages finding insufficient evidence of reckless indifference to federally protected rights, awarded the Halperns $37,431 in damages, and required the DOA to maintain its revised ESA policy with prompt review requirements.
brief_summary: The United States sued the Dorchester Owners Association (a high-rise condominium association in Philadelphia) alleging that its policies and practices regarding emotional support animals—including excessive documentation requirements, insurance mandates, common area prohibitions, and unreasonable delays—constituted a pattern or practice of disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. After a jury trial returned a split verdict finding the DOA not liable to Ms. Hamburg individually but liable on the pattern-or-practice claim, the court awarded the Halperns $37,431 in damages, declined to impose civil penalties or punitive damages, and ordered the DOA to maintain its revised ESA policy with prompt review requirements.
key_cases_cited: Alexander v. Riga, 208 F.3d 419 (3d Cir. 2000)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
431 East Palisade Avenue Real Estate, LLC v. City of Englewood, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Sierra Pacific Industries, Inc., 862 F.3d 1157 (9th Cir. 2017)

case_name: Mealy v. Casablanca Investors, LLC
accommodation_description: Landlord required female tenant to sign lease addendum restricting male overnight visitors while no similar restrictions applied to male tenants.
key_holding: Plaintiff stated a plausible claim for sex-based discrimination under § 3604(b) where she alleged the landlord's guest policy imposed different restrictions on female tenants than male tenants, and she was threatened with eviction for non-compliance with the discriminatory policy.
brief_summary: Maura Mealy sued her landlord Casablanca Investors, LLC, alleging sex-based discrimination under the Fair Housing Act after being required to sign an addendum restricting male overnight visitors while male tenants faced no similar guest restrictions. The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding that plaintiff pled sufficient facts to plausibly allege the guest policy constituted intentional discrimination based on sex and that her housing rights were harmed by the discriminatory terms.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Curto v. A Country Place Condo. Ass'n., 921 F.3d 405 (3d Cir. 2019)
Edwards v. Media Borough Council, 430 F. Supp. 2d 445 (E.D. Pa. 2006)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)

source_file: 9938953_2023_SAMUEL v. THE DELAWARE COUNTY HOUSING AUTHORITY_paed
case_name: Earlando Samuel v. The Delaware County Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff sought continued approval of a live-in aide and maintenance of his two-bedroom housing voucher to accommodate his osteoporosis, degenerative joint disease, and cognitive disorder.
key_holding: The court dismissed Samuel's FHA claims because he failed to adequately plead that defendants acted 'because of' his disability, noting the only reference to his disability was a comment that he 'didn't look disabled' and instances of conduct—unannounced apartment entries and rent fluctuations—that were not expressly linked to his handicapping conditions.
brief_summary: Earlando Samuel, a 70-year-old Black man with osteoporosis, degenerative joint disease, and a cognitive disorder, sued the Delaware County Housing Authority and Ingerman Property Management alleging they engaged in a bait-and-switch scheme to downgrade his housing voucher from two bedrooms to one and failed to approve his live-in aide. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss the FHA claims, finding Samuel failed to adequately plead that defendants acted because of his disability, though his claim for violation of HUD's live-in aide regulations under 24 C.F.R. § 982.316 survived.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)

source_file: 9939435_2023_WISSMAN v. PGM REAL ESTATE LLC, PROPERTY MANAGEMENT_paed
case_name: Lauren E. Wissman v. PGM Real Estate LLC, Property Management, et al.
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request identified; plaintiff alleges disability-based harassment, eviction, and failure to make repairs, but does not request any particular accommodation.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA claims without prejudice because her vague and disjointed allegations did not plausibly link the challenged actions to discrimination on the basis of a protected class, and she failed to adequately allege a qualifying disability under the FHAA.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Lauren E. Wissman, a pro se tenant with a Section 8 voucher, sued PGM Real Estate LLC and its employees alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on disability, including claims of harassment, improper eviction, and failure to make repairs. The court dismissed her complaint without prejudice, finding her allegations too vague and conclusory to state plausible FHA claims, as she failed to adequately allege a qualifying disability under the FHAA or link her claims to her membership in a protected class.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
White v. Barbe, 767 F. App'x 332 (3d Cir. 2019)
Palencar v. Raijski, No. 15-1189, 2016 WL 6908116 (M.D. Pa. 2016)

source_file: 9939841_2023_SAMUEL v. THE DELAWARE COUNTY HOUSING AUTHORITY_paed
case_name: Earlando Samuel v. The Delaware County Housing Authority
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested approval of a live-in aide to assist with daily living needs due to osteoporosis and degenerative joint disease.
key_holding: Because plaintiff never submitted the required verifying paperwork for a live-in aide as specified in the Administrative Plan, he never properly 'requested' the accommodation and thus there was no 'refusal' by defendants to evaluate under the Fair Housing Act's reasonable accommodation standard.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Earlando Samuel sued the Delaware County Housing Authority and its employees alleging they violated Section 8 regulations and the Fair Housing Act by failing to accommodate his need for a live-in aide when his two-bedroom voucher with portability rights was converted to a one-bedroom voucher upon moving to Pennsylvania. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, holding that because Samuel never submitted the required documentation specified in the DCHA Administrative Plan for requesting a live-in aide, he never properly made the request and there was therefore no refusal to accommodate under § 3604(f)(3)(B).
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condominium Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Susan Lloyd v. Manbel Devco I LP et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of grab bars, shower seat, handheld showerhead, and patio ramp at landlord's expense, and transfer to a unit with a walk-in shower
key_holding: Section 3604(f)(3)(A) requires landlords to permit reasonable modifications but does not create an affirmative duty to fund them; plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation/modification claims fail because she was informed she could install modifications at her own expense and she failed to plead nexus between ERAP payment refusal and disability.
brief_summary: Susan Lloyd sued Manbel Devco I LP and related defendants alleging Fair Housing Act and ADA violations, claiming the landlord failed to accommodate her mobility disability by refusing to install grab bars, a shower seat, and a patio ramp at its expense, and by refusing to provide a unit with a walk-in shower. The court granted in part the motion to dismiss, dismissing ADA claims with prejudice and FHA claims without prejudice, holding that § 3604(f)(3)(A) does not require landlords to fund modifications and Lloyd failed to state a cognizable accommodation claim.
key_cases_cited: Lefkowitz v. Westlake Master Ass'n, Inc., 2019 WL 669806 (D.N.J. 2019)
Coyne v. Holy Fam. Apartments, 2020 WL 2063475 (E.D. Pa. 2020)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 540 (2007)
Rosado v. Whitcraft, 2023 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 224332 (E.D. Pa. 2023)

case_name: Darlene Robinson v. Pathway to Housing
accommodation_description: No accommodation request identified; plaintiff challenges unsafe housing conditions following a death in her apartment
key_holding: The Fair Housing Act is inapplicable because the complaint contains no allegations of discrimination; moreover, the private nonprofit housing provider is not a state actor amenable to suit under § 1983.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Darlene Robinson, proceeding pro se and in forma pauperis, sued Pathway to Housing and case managers seeking damages and safe housing after someone died in her apartment and blood was left throughout the unit. The Court dismissed the complaint with prejudice, finding no viable federal claim because the Fair Housing Act does not apply absent discrimination allegations and the private nonprofit defendants are not state actors subject to § 1983 liability.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
West v. Atkins, 487 U.S. 42 (1988)
DeShaney v. Winnebago Cnty. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 489 U.S. 189 (1989)

case_name: Karen Marie Stevens-Nunez v. Bristol Borough Municipal Administration, et al.
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleging FHA violations related to her mental health disability, lockout from apartment, and subsequent police conduct, but who failed to articulate a specific reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The FHA claim was dismissed because plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing she requested a reasonable accommodation that was refused, and failed to adequately allege membership in any protected class or that defendants knew of her disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Stevens-Nunez, a pro se litigant, sued Bristol Borough and municipal officials alleging Fair Housing Act violations arising from her lockout from her apartment after being discharged from a mental health facility. The court dismissed her FHA claim without prejudice, finding she failed to plead that she requested a reasonable accommodation or that defendants refused such an accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal

case_name: Gordon Roy Parker v. Ronald Lee, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, who resides with his brother Walt as an 'indefinite occupant/caregiver,' claims disparate treatment based on race and sex, including allegedly receiving worse lease renewal terms than nonwhite, female tenants.
key_holding: Parker failed to state plausible FHA discrimination and retaliation claims because his allegations of differential treatment lacked sufficient comparator evidence showing race or sex motivated the defendants, and his retaliation claim failed for lack of demonstrated protected activity.
brief_summary: Gordon Roy Parker, an unlisted occupant residing with his brother Walt (the tenant of record), sued the landlords, property managers, and their attorney alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race and sex discrimination and retaliation. The court granted defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Parker failed to plead sufficient facts supporting a plausible inference that race or sex motivated the allegedly discriminatory lease renewal offer or that he engaged in protected activity for the retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985)

source_file: 9941826_2020_WARTLUFT v. THE MILTON HERSHEY SCHOOL AND SCHOOL TRUST_pamd
case_name: Wartluft v. The Milton Hershey School and School Trust
accommodation_description: The school dismissed Abrielle from its residential program and barred her from attending her eighth-grade graduation and after-party following psychiatric hospitalizations for depression and suicidal ideation, allegedly under a policy that expelled students after two mental health hospitalizations.
key_holding: The court found that the school's decision to dismiss Abrielle after mental health hospitalizations reflected a clinical judgment that its program could not meet her level of care needs rather than intentional discrimination based on her mental disability, and thus did not violate the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: The Estate of Abrielle Bartels sued the Milton Hershey School and its Trust, alleging that the school violated the Fair Housing Act by dismissing Abrielle from its residential program and barring her from attending her graduation after psychiatric hospitalizations for depression and suicidal ideation under an alleged two-hospitalization expulsion policy. The court granted summary judgment to the school, finding that the school's decision reflected a clinical judgment that its program could not meet Abrielle's level of care needs rather than intentional discrimination based on her mental disability.
key_cases_cited: Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Reeves v. Sanderson Plumbing Prods., Inc., 530 U.S. 133 (2000)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)

case_name: John Doe v. University of Scranton
accommodation_description: No accommodation request was made; this case involves claims of peer-on-peer harassment based on sexual orientation in off-campus university housing.
key_holding: The court dismissed FHA claims under § 3604(b) and § 3617 with prejudice, holding that the University of Scranton could not be held liable under the Fair Housing Act for off-campus peer harassment based on sexual orientation because Plaintiff failed to plausibly allege the University provided or controlled the off-campus dwelling or permitted known discriminatory harassment.
brief_summary: John Doe sued the University of Scranton alleging peer-on-peer harassment based on sexual orientation in off-campus student housing, including claims under Title IX and the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed Plaintiff's FHA claims under §§ 3604(b) and 3617 with prejudice, finding no basis to hold the University liable as it did not provide or control the off-campus dwelling and Plaintiff failed to plausibly allege the University permitted known discriminatory harassment.
key_cases_cited: Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 944 F.3d 370 (2d Cir. 2019)
Georgia State Conference of the NAACP v. City of LaGrange, 940 F.3d 627 (11th Cir. 2019)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Davis v. Monroe County Bd. of Educ., 526 U.S. 629 (1999)

source_file: 9943863_2021_Mountainside Manor Real Estate Associates LLC v. Dallas Township_pamd
case_name: Mountainside Manor Real Estate Associates, LLC v. Dallas Township
accommodation_description: Property owners sought to expand an existing drug and alcohol addiction Step-Down treatment facility from one building to a second building on the same property.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA claims because the facts alleged did not show the Township's denial of the zoning permit application was discriminatory, as Step-Down facilities were not permitted in the B-2 Zone and the facility remained in operation.
brief_summary: Property owners and operators of a drug and alcohol addiction treatment facility sued Dallas Township after the municipality denied their application to expand the facility to an adjacent building in a commercially zoned district. The court granted the Township's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to plausibly allege that the zoning denial violated the Fair Housing Act, Americans with Disabilities Act, or other federal and state laws.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Twp. of Scotch Plains
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth.

source_file: 9947618_2024_Cori's Place v. The Zoning Hearing Board of the City of Nanticoke_pamd
case_name: Cori's Place v. The Zoning Hearing Board of the City of Nanticoke
accommodation_description: Group home operator requested zoning permit to allow four adults with intellectual disabilities to reside together in a single-family residential zone, including permission for separate leases for each resident.
key_holding: The court found that Cori's Place adequately pleaded a §3604(f)(3)(B) reasonable accommodation claim because the zoning permit denial for four intellectually disabled individuals to reside together in a residential zone could constitute unlawful discrimination, and the accommodation was plausibly reasonable and necessary to afford equal housing opportunity.
brief_summary: Cori's Place, a provider of services to adults with intellectual disabilities, sued the City of Nanticoke and its Zoning Hearing Board alleging the denial of a zoning permit to operate a group home for four intellectually disabled individuals violated the Fair Housing Act and ADA. The court granted in part the motion to dismiss, dismissing intentional discrimination claims and interference claims under the FHA, as well as all ADA claims, but held that Cori's Place adequately stated a reasonable accommodation claim under §3604(f)(3)(B) because the accommodation was plausibly reasonable and necessary to afford the disabled residents equal opportunity to housing in the residential zone.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Assoc., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay West Condominium Assoc., 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Twp. of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)

case_name: Butler v. Sundo Capital, LLC
accommodation_description: Ms. Butler requested early lease termination as a domestic violence victim facing stalking by her ex-husband; the court analyzed this as a disparate impact claim rather than a reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: Domestic violence victims can bring disparate impact claims under a sex discrimination theory because the early termination clause's application to domestic violence victims disproportionately impacts women, who comprise 85% of intimate partner violence victims.
brief_summary: Ashley Butler and Fair Housing Partnership of Greater Pittsburgh sued Sundo Capital and related parties after Defendants refused to release Butler from her lease early when she faced domestic violence and stalking by her ex-husband, then sought accelerated rent payments. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, holding that Butler adequately pled a disparate impact claim based on sex discrimination because the early termination/rent acceleration policy disproportionately impacts women who are domestic violence victims, and also adequately pled a retaliation claim under § 3617.
key_cases_cited: Tex. Dep't of Housing & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'Ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Wilson v. Guardian Mgmt., LLC, 383 F. Supp. 3d 1105 (D. Or. 2019)
Dickinson v. Zanesville Metro. Hous. Auth., 975 F. Supp. 2d 863 (S.D. Ohio 2013)

case_name: James Mims v. City of New Castle
accommodation_description: Landlord plaintiff alleges city discriminated against him through selective enforcement of rental inspections because he rents to racial minorities and people with disabilities
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claim because his amended complaint failed to allege sufficient facts to raise a reasonable expectation that discriminatory purpose was a motivating factor in the city's enforcement actions against him as a landlord.
brief_summary: James Mims, a landlord who rents to racial minorities and disabled tenants, sued the City of New Castle alleging the city violated the Fair Housing Act by selectively enforcing rental inspections and denying occupancy permits because of the protected characteristics of his tenants. The court granted the city's motion to dismiss, finding that while plaintiff alleged discriminatory intent by city officials, he failed to plead sufficient facts showing a causal connection between his tenant demographics and the adverse city actions.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
City of Englewood v. 431 E. Palisade Ave. Real Estate, LLC, 977 F.3d 277 (3d Cir. 2020)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Village of Willowbrook v. Olech, 528 U.S. 562 (2000)

case_name: United States of America v. Perry Homes, Inc., Allyson Whittington, and Robert Whittington
accommodation_description: Request to keep emotional support animals in rental housing, which defendants allegedly refused.
key_holding: The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss the 3604(f)(3)(B) failure to accommodate claim, finding that fair housing organizations can use fictional testers to advance FHA purposes, but also rejected the magistrate's recommendation to dismiss 3604(f)(1) and (f)(2) claims, effectively allowing most claims to proceed.
brief_summary: The United States brought suit on behalf of Southwestern Pennsylvania Legal Services against Perry Homes and individual defendants alleging they refused to allow emotional support animals in violation of the FHA. The court adopted the magistrate's recommendation to deny defendants' motion to dismiss the 3604(c) and 3604(f)(3)(B) claims, but rejected the recommendation to dismiss 3604(f)(1) and (f)(2) claims, allowing those claims to proceed.
key_cases_cited: Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)
Swierkiewicz v. Sorema N.A., 534 U.S. 506 (2002)
Connelly v. Lane Constr. Corp., 809 F.3d 780 (3d Cir. 2016)

case_name: United States of America v. LRG Corporation, Lewis R. Gainfort
accommodation_description: Request for an emotional support animal (dog named Onyx) for Monica Samulski's son Sabastian to accommodate his disability-related needs
key_holding: The court resolved evidentiary disputes before trial, granting the United States' motions to exclude evidence regarding school accommodation requests, the defendant's updated policies, HUD investigation challenges, other tenants' accommodation requests, and evidence of a prior civil suit, while limiting evidence of the ESA's barking to impeachment purposes.
brief_summary: The United States sued LRG Corporation and its owner alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to approve tenant Monica Samulski's request for an emotional support animal for her son Sabastian and then evicting her for having the ESA in February 2019. The court resolved pre-trial motions in limine, granting most of the United States' requests to exclude irrelevant or prejudicial evidence while denying LRG's motion to preclude emotional distress damages as premature.
key_cases_cited: Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Anderson v. City of Blue Ash, 798 F.3d 338 (6th Cir. 2015)
United States v. Greenidge, 495 F.3d 85 (3d Cir. 2007)
Regents of Mercersburg Coll. v. Republic Franklin Ins. Co., 458 F.3d 159 (3d Cir. 2006)
Bolden v. Se. Pa. Transp. Auth., 21 F.3d 29 (3d Cir. 1994)

case_name: Glagola v. MacFann
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves quid pro quo sexual harassment (conditioning housing on sex) and hostile housing environment sexual harassment.
key_holding: The Court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff adequately stated claims for quid pro quo sexual harassment, hostile housing environment sexual harassment, discriminatory statements under § 3604(c), and claims under the Trafficking Victims Protection Act.
brief_summary: Alyssia Glagola sued Walter MacFann, Tri-County Realty Associates, L.P., and AMW Consulting, LLC alleging that Mr. MacFann conditioned free rent on sex and created a hostile housing environment through sexual harassment, stalking, and physical abuse. The Court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiff adequately stated quid pro quo harassment, hostile environment claims, discriminatory statements under § 3604(c), and Trafficking Victims Protection Act claims.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Krueger v. Cuomo, 115 F.3d 487 (7th Cir. 1997)
Noah v. Assor, 379 F. Supp. 3d 1284 (S.D. Fla. 2019)
Richardson v. Nw. Univ., No. 21-522, 2023 WL 6197447 (N.D. Ill. 2023)

case_name: Euripides Del Villar-Rosario v. Victor Roberto Fernandez-Ramos, et al.
accommodation_description: The complaint is too vague to identify a specific accommodation request; it broadly alleges housing violations including unauthorized entry, property destruction, and an eviction process in violation of § 3604 and other statutes.
key_holding: The complaint was dismissed sua sponte because it failed to state a plausible claim, as it contained only scant factual allegations without sufficient detail to give defendants fair notice of the claims against them.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Euripides Del Villar-Rosario filed a vague complaint against multiple defendants alleging housing violations including unauthorized apartment entry, property destruction with a machete, and an unauthorized eviction process in violation of § 3604 and numerous other statutes and constitutional provisions. The court dismissed the complaint sua sponte under § 1915(e)(2) and Rule 12(b)(6) because the allegations were wholly insufficient to establish a plausible claim or provide fair notice to defendants.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Gonzalez-Gonzalez v. United States

case_name: Gloria Rivera de Dávila v. Mora Development Corporation
accommodation_description: Rivera requested assignment of a handicap parking space near the second-floor elevator entrance to Tower I as a reasonable accommodation for her mobility disabilities including cardiac arrhythmia, limited mobility due to leg surgery, neuropathy, herniated discs, and osteoarthritis.
key_holding: A failure to respond to a reasonable accommodation request can constitute a constructive denial for purposes of stating a prima facie case, and the plaintiff adequately pled all four elements of a failure-to-accommodate claim under the FHA.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Gloria Rivera de Dávila sued Mora Development Corporation, the Condominium Torre Cibeles Board of Directors, and the Condominium Torre Cibeles Association alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on the defendants' failure to provide her a reasonable accommodation in the form of handicap parking near her Tower I residence. The court granted the Association's motion to dismiss the ADA claim (residential condominiums are not public accommodations) but denied the motion to dismiss the FHA reasonable accommodation claim, finding the plaintiff adequately alleged all elements of a prima facie case and that failure to respond to her accommodation requests constituted constructive denial.
key_cases_cited: Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ocasio-Hernández v. Fortuño-Burset, 640 F.3d 1 (1st Cir. 2011)
Salisbury v. Caritas Acquisitions V, LLC, 2018 WL 10483437 (C.D. Cal. 2018)
Salehi v. Lakeview Terrace Homeowners Assn., 2022 WL 3018063 (N.D. Cal. 2022)

case_name: Gloria Rivera De Davila v. Mora Development Corporation
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested accessible handicap parking at the second-floor door entrance to the elevator in Tower 1 of Torre Cibeles, and restoration of designated handicap parking spaces 172, 173, and 178.
key_holding: Mora Development Corporation could not have violated the FHAA because it ceased administering the property in 2014 and thus had no knowledge of plaintiff's disability status, no ability to receive or respond to accommodation requests, and no authority to provide any accommodation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Gloria Rivera De Davila sued Mora Development Corporation, the developer of Torre Cibeles condominium, alleging Fair Housing Act violations for the sale of designated handicap parking spaces and failure to provide reasonable accommodations for her disability. On reconsideration, the Court granted Mora's motion to dismiss with prejudice because Mora ceased administering the property in 2014, making it impossible for Mora to have knowledge of plaintiff's disability, receive her accommodation requests, or provide any accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Astralis Condominium Ass'n v. Secretary, U.S. Dept. of Housing & Urban Development, 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)

case_name: United States of America v. Maria Trini Menendez, et al.
accommodation_description: Request to rent an apartment with a service dog despite the property's no-pet policy, by a legally blind tenant
key_holding: The court denied the De La Fuente defendants' motion for summary judgment because genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether defendants actually refused the accommodation request and whether their subsequent offer to rent the apartment came only after HUD conciliation proceedings rather than promptly upon learning of the service dog.
brief_summary: The United States sued property owners and their real estate agent on behalf of a legally blind woman who was denied the ability to rent an apartment because of her service dog, alleging violation of the Fair Housing Act's reasonable accommodation provision. The court denied the defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine factual disputes about whether they refused the accommodation and whether their later offer to rent came only after HUD proceedings rather than promptly upon learning of the service dog.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cnty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Castillo Condo. Ass'n v. U.S. Dep't of Housing & Urban Dev., 821 F.3d 92 (1st Cir. 2016)
Rivera v. Mora Development Corporation, 624 F. Supp.3d 80 (D.P.R. 2022)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)

case_name: Trenda Taylor and Rhode Island Commission for Human Rights v. National Investments, Ltd., et al.
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; case involves claims of racial discrimination in housing recertification and retaliation for complaints about racial discrimination.
key_holding: The court found genuine disputes of material fact exist as to whether defendant landlord's refusal to process plaintiff's Section 8 recertification and subsequent adverse actions were motivated by race rather than legitimate non-discriminatory reasons, and whether retaliation for complaints of racial discrimination played a but-for causal role.
brief_summary: Trenda Taylor, an African American tenant with Section 8 project-based housing assistance, sued her landlord alleging racial discrimination in refusing to process her income recertification paperwork and retaliation for complaining about that discrimination. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment on the discrimination and retaliation claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the landlord's stated race-neutral reasons were pretextual, but granted summary judgment for the landlord on Taylor's claim that HUD regulations created an implied private right of action.
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Reg'l Econ. Cmty. Action Program, Inc. v. City of Middletown, 294 F.3d 35 (2d Cir. 2002)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)
University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center v. Nassar, 570 U.S. 338 (2013)
Gross v. FBL Fin. Servs., Inc., 557 U.S. 167 (2009)

case_name: Scott Ellis, Jr. v. Jackie Alvarez
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a transgender woman, allegedly requested that defendant refer to her by her preferred name 'Hope' rather than her legal name 'Scott'; no specific disability-related accommodation request was clearly articulated in the complaint.
key_holding: The court recommended dismissal because plaintiff's complaint failed to comply with Rule 8 pleading standards, did not allege sufficient facts to support any Fair Housing Act theory (disparate treatment, disparate impact, or reasonable accommodation), and failed to establish a basis for the claimed $50,000 in damages.
brief_summary: Scott Ellis, Jr. sued Jackie Alvarez and JJ Property Management alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on defendant's alleged discriminatory behavior regarding her preferred name and a text message about parking. The court recommended summary dismissal under 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2), finding the complaint failed to state a cognizable FHA claim because plaintiff failed to allege sufficient factual matter to support any recognized theory of housing discrimination.
key_cases_cited: Batista v. Cooperativa De Vivienda Jardines De San Ignacio, 776 F.3d 38 (1st Cir. 2015)
Neitzke v. Williams, 490 U.S. 319 (1989)
Estelle v. Gamble, 429 U.S. 97 (1976)

case_name: Little Tom Childress, Jr. v. Rebecca Roberts, Babbie M. Jaco, and Boyd Management LTD
accommodation_description: No reasonable accommodation requested; case involves claims of racial harassment and hostile housing environment based on race under § 3604(a)
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment because a reasonable jury could find that Plaintiff, an African American tenant, was subjected to racially hostile housing environment conduct that was sufficiently severe or pervasive to alter his housing conditions under § 3604(a).
brief_summary: Little Tom Childress, Jr., an African American tenant at West End Manor apartment complex in Union, South Carolina, sued property manager Rebecca Roberts and Boyd Management LTD alleging racial harassment and a hostile housing environment in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The court denied Defendants' motion for summary judgment, finding genuine issues of material fact exist as to whether Roberts' alleged racially discriminatory statements and differential treatment were sufficiently severe or pervasive to create an abusive housing environment.
key_cases_cited: Smith v. Town of Clarkton, N.C., 682 F.2d 1055 (4th Cir. 1982)
Williams v. Poretsky Memt., Inc., 955 F. Supp. 490 (D. Md. 1996)
DiCenso v. Cisneros, 96 F.3d 1004 (7th Cir. 1996)
Honce v. Vigil, 1 F.3d 1085 (10th Cir. 1993)
Boyer-Liberto v. Fontainebleau Corp., 786 F.3d 264 (4th Cir. 2015)

source_file: 9956870_2020_United States v. Irmo South Carolina, Town of_scd
case_name: United States of America v. Town of Irmo, South Carolina
accommodation_description: Patricia Witt requested a zoning variance to build a carport on her property to accommodate her physical disability.
key_holding: The court granted in part Plaintiff's Motion to Compel, ordering Defendant Town of Irmo to produce documents in native electronic format, provide discovery spanning five years, and supply an adequate privilege log conforming to Rule 26(b)(5).
brief_summary: The United States sued the Town of Irmo, South Carolina on behalf of homeowner Patricia Witt, alleging the municipality violated the Fair Housing Act by improperly refusing to provide a reasonable accommodation or modification when denying her petition for a zoning variance to build a carport to accommodate her physical disability. This order addresses Plaintiff's Motion to Compel discovery, with the court granting most of the relief sought regarding ESI format, scope of production, and privilege log deficiencies, while denying the motion only as to Interrogatory No. 13.

source_file: 9957341_2020_United States v. Irmo South Carolina, Town of_scd
case_name: United States of America v. Town of Irmo, South Carolina
accommodation_description: Patricia Witt, a homeowner with mobility disabilities including four hip surgeries in one year, sought a zoning variance to build a carport covering her mobility ramp and a portion of her driveway for safety and accessibility.
key_holding: Both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment were denied because genuine disputes of material fact exist regarding whether the carport modification is necessary to afford Ms. Witt equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, and the court rejected Irmo's mootness argument because a violation occurred at the time of the initial denial.
brief_summary: The United States sued the Town of Irmo, South Carolina, alleging violations of the Fair Housing Act when the municipality denied homeowner Patricia Witt's zoning variance request to build a carport covering her disability-related mobility ramp. The court denied both parties' cross-motions for summary judgment, finding genuine disputes of material fact regarding whether the carport was necessary and reasonable to afford Ms. Witt equal opportunity to use and enjoy her home, and that Irmo's subsequent ordinance change did not moot the claim because a violation occurred at the time of denial.
key_cases_cited: Bryant Woods Inn, Inc. v. Howard Cty., Md., 124 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 1997)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Scoggins v. Lee's Crossing Homeowners Ass'n, 718 F.3d 262 (4th Cir. 2013)
Oxford House, Inc. v. City of Raleigh, 1999 WL 1940013 (E.D.N.C. 1999)

source_file: 9962351_2023_South Carolina State Conference of NAACP v. South Carolina Department of Juvenile Justice_scd
9963465_2023_South Carolina State Conference of the NAACP v. Georgetown County_scd
case_name: South Carolina State Conference of the NAACP v. Georgetown County
accommodation_description: This is a zoning denial case, not a reasonable accommodation case—the NAACP and developers allege Georgetown County denied rezoning for an affordable housing development (Porter's Landing) based on racial discrimination.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding that Plaintiffs sufficiently alleged standing, plausibly stated disparate treatment and disparate impact claims under the FHA, and adequately pled that Defendants' denial of the zoning application was motivated by discriminatory intent with substantial disparate impact on Black households.
brief_summary: NAACP organizations and developers FourSix Development and We Do Good Work sued Georgetown County, alleging the County's denial of a zoning application for Porter's Landing, an affordable housing development, violated the Fair Housing Act and constitutional guarantees by perpetuating racial segregation. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding Plaintiffs adequately alleged standing and stated plausible claims for disparate treatment, disparate impact, and perpetuation of segregation.
key_cases_cited: Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Tex. Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Warth v. Seldin, 422 U.S. 490 (1975)
Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Svcs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9964824_2018_Noble v. American National Property _ Casualty Insurance Co._sdd
case_name: Marty Noble and Holli Telford v. American National Property & Casualty Insurance Co.; Boardwalk Property Management Co.; Kevin West; Lehi Oasis, LLC; David L. Parker; Austin B. Cales; Jared Eldridge; Larry Deiter; Robert J. Poulsen and Poulsen & Skousen
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation request was analyzed; the court examined whether defendants discriminated against plaintiffs based on disabilities (brain injury, seizures, mobility challenges) including refusing to accommodate disability needs related to the tenancy.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiffs' FHA disability discrimination claim for failure to adequately plead the elements of a prima facie case, finding plaintiffs failed to establish protected class membership, defendant's knowledge, qualification to rent, or refusal to accommodate.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Marty Noble and Holli Telford, proceeding pro se, filed this action against multiple defendants including insurance companies, property management, and a mobile home park owner alleging federal claims including RICO, Fair Housing Act, EFTA, FCRA, civil rights conspiracy, and various state law claims arising from a mobile home park dispute, insurance coverage issues, and an eviction proceeding. The court granted all defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiffs failed to adequately plead the elements of any of their federal claims including the FHA claim, and additionally found plaintiff Telford to be a vexatious litigant subject to filing restrictions.
key_cases_cited: Radecki v. Joura, 114 F.3d 115 (8th Cir. 1997)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Titus v. Sullivan, 4 F.3d 590 (8th Cir. 1993)

case_name: Lundahl v. Gross
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that defendants accommodate her disability-related needs by completing the appraisal and loan closing in a timely manner to prevent expiration of housing assistance grants.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because plaintiff failed to allege facts showing the defendants' actions (delayed appraisal and reduced loan amount) were because of her disability.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Holli Lundahl, who is disabled and was judicially declared disabled under the Social Security Act, sued USDA officials alleging they discriminated against her by delaying her home loan appraisal and reducing her approved loan amount, causing her to lose housing assistance grants. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to state a plausible FHA claim because she did not adequately allege that defendants' actions were because of her disability, and failed to establish she was qualified for the loan given discrepancies in her application.
key_cases_cited: Rowe v. Union Planters Bank, 289 F.3d 533 (8th Cir. 2002)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

case_name: Joshua A. Debity and Leah D. Debity, individually and as next friends for G.D., a minor v. Vintage Village Homeowners Association
accommodation_description: Request for a six-foot wooden privacy fence to secure the backyard for a minor child with non-verbal autism and sensory processing disorder who elopes due to his disabilities.
key_holding: The HOA was entitled to summary judgment because plaintiffs failed to establish that a wooden fence was necessary under the FHA's reasonable modification standard when the HOA approved a six-foot wrought iron fence, an alternative that could serve the same purpose of preventing elopement.
brief_summary: Joshua and Leah Debity sued the Vintage Village Homeowners Association alleging the HOA violated the Fair Housing Act by denying their request for a six-foot wooden privacy fence to secure their backyard for their autistic son who elopes. The court granted summary judgment for the HOA, holding that plaintiffs failed to establish the wooden fence was necessary when the HOA approved a six-foot wrought iron fence as an alternative that could adequately address their son's elopement risk.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Ass'n, 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)

source_file: 9969114_2023_United States v. City of Chattanooga, Tennessee_tned
case_name: United States of America v. City of Chattanooga, Tennessee
accommodation_description: Quality Lifestyle Service, Inc. sought to operate a four-person group home for individuals with mental illness at 1104 South Greenwood Avenue in an R-1 zoning district without obtaining a special use permit.
key_holding: The City agreed to settle without admitting liability by paying monetary damages, amending its zoning ordinance to comply with state and federal disability housing laws, and providing FHA/ADA training to relevant officials.
brief_summary: The United States sued the City of Chattanooga alleging the City violated the FHA and ADA by requiring Quality Lifestyle Service, Inc., a non-profit housing provider, to obtain a special permit to operate a four-person group home for individuals with mental disabilities in a single-family zoning district, despite Tennessee state law permitting such housing by right. The parties settled via consent decree requiring the City to pay $32,600 to the provider and a $5,000 civil penalty, amend its zoning ordinance to comply with disability housing rights, and provide FHA/ADA training to relevant officials.

case_name: April Tenille Norris v. Murfreesboro Leased Housing Associates I, LP d/b/a Chariot Pointe Apartments, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested assistance from MHA in locating alternative housing, an extension of her moving voucher expiration date, and an increase in voucher size from two to three bedrooms to accommodate vocational rehabilitation employment goals.
key_holding: The FHA claim against MHA fails because MHA was not responsible for renting or managing Plaintiff's dwelling and therefore could not be liable under § 3604(f), but Plaintiff's ADA Title II reasonable accommodation claim survives screening as Plaintiff plausibly alleged that MHA failed to accommodate her disability-related requests for housing assistance.
brief_summary: Plaintiff April Tenille Norris, a mentally ill tenant participating in the Shelter Plus Care Program, sued MHA and other defendants alleging they failed to accommodate her disability by refusing to assist her in relocating from Chariot Pointe Apartments and by terminating her from the program. The court dismissed the FHA claim against MHA for failure to state a claim because MHA had no obligation under § 3604(f) as it was not responsible for renting or managing her dwelling, but allowed Plaintiff's ADA Title II reasonable accommodation claim to proceed based on her requests for housing relocation assistance and extended voucher time.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)

case_name: McGuire v. Highmark Holdings et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff was terminated from her employment as a leasing consultant after reporting alleged Fair Housing Act violations by the property management company; this is a retaliation claim rather than a direct accommodation request.
key_holding: The court found that McGuire stated plausible retaliation claims under the FHA and THRA by alleging she was terminated after reporting fair housing violations, but dismissed her Title VII retaliation claims for failure to allege sufficient facts showing she opposed employment discrimination on protected bases.
brief_summary: Cassandra McGuire, a former leasing consultant for Enfield Management (which manages properties owned by Highmark Holdings), sued her employers alleging she was terminated in retaliation for reporting fair housing violations she observed at the properties, including discriminatory treatment of disabled and minority applicants. The Magistrate Judge recommended denying the motion to dismiss on the FHA and THRA retaliation claims, finding McGuire sufficiently alleged she engaged in protected activity and was terminated thereafter, but dismissed her Title VII employment retaliation claims.
key_cases_cited: Linkletter v. W. & S. Fin. Grp., Inc., 851 F.3d 632 (6th Cir. 2017)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Clark v. Johnson, 413 F. App'x 804 (6th Cir. 2011)

case_name: Samantha McDonald v. Madison Flats and Crystal Snyder
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested permission to keep a mixed-breed service dog to assist two disabled family members, including alerting when her mother with end-stage liver disease becomes severely ill and when her three-year-old daughter with a brain tumor stops breathing.
key_holding: The court found that the complaint plausibly alleged the plaintiff sought a service dog accommodation necessary to use and enjoy her rental dwelling, that defendants arbitrarily denied the request despite allowing similar mixed-breed dogs, and that defendants' subsequent punitive eviction procedures constituted plausible interference with FHA rights.
brief_summary: Samantha McDonald sued Madison Flats and Crystal Snyder alleging defendants denied her request to keep a service dog for her disabled family members and initiated punitive eviction procedures in retaliation. At initial screening under the in forma pauperis standard, the court found the complaint stated colorable claims for reasonable accommodation denial and interference under the Fair Housing Act.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Grier v. Bryden Mgmt., LLC, 2019 WL 1046083 (S.D. Ohio 2019)

case_name: Samantha McDonald v. Madison Flats, et al.
accommodation_description: The complaint alleges Fair Housing Act discrimination but the specific accommodation request is not discussed in this procedural Report and Recommendation.
key_holding: The Magistrate Judge recommends dismissal without prejudice under Rules 4(m) and 41(b) because plaintiff failed to serve defendants with process and has had no contact with the Court since filing her lawsuit, indicating loss of interest.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Samantha McDonald filed a Fair Housing Act lawsuit against Madison Flats and Crystal Snyder alleging housing discrimination in Madison, Tennessee. The Magistrate Judge recommends dismissing the case without prejudice because plaintiff failed to serve defendants within the 90-day deadline and has had no contact with the Court since filing, constituting failure to prosecute.

case_name: Lisa Sallaj v. Calvin L. Tate and Schatten Properties
accommodation_description: Request for a dedicated disabled parking space near her apartment to accommodate mobility-related disability
key_holding: The court found plaintiff alleged sufficient facts to state plausible FHA reasonable accommodation and interference claims where she requested designated disabled parking, defendants failed to ensure the space was available for her use, and defendants subsequently initiated eviction proceedings after plaintiff hired an attorney.
brief_summary: Lisa Sallaj sued her former landlord Schatten Properties and its CEO claiming violations of the FHA, alleging she requested a designated disabled parking space as a reasonable accommodation but defendants failed to ensure the space remained available for her use, causing her to park far from her apartment where she tripped and was injured, and that defendants subsequently initiated eviction proceedings after she hired an attorney to address the accommodation denial. The court denied defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the complaint stated plausible claims for reasonable accommodation denial and FHA interference.
key_cases_cited: Hollis v. Chestnut Bend Homeowners Ass'n, 760 F.3d 531 (6th Cir. 2014)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: 9972814_2019_U.S. Department of Justice v. Ables d_b_a Pops Cove_tnwd
case_name: United States of America v. Chad David Ables d/b/a Pops Cove
accommodation_description: N/A - this case involves sex discrimination and sexual harassment claims, not a disability accommodation request
key_holding: The court denied the motion to strike defendant's affirmative defense alleging drug-related criminal activity because such evidence could show a legitimate, non-discriminatory purpose for eviction under Tennessee law.
brief_summary: The United States sued landlord Chad Ables, and individual tenants intervened, alleging that Ables discriminated on the basis of sex by demanding sexual favors in exchange for rent waivers, then retaliating by increasing rent, attempting eviction, and cutting off water when refused. The court denied intervenor-plaintiffs' motion to strike Ables's affirmative defense that plaintiffs engaged in illegal drug activity, finding the defense potentially relevant to establishing a non-discriminatory reason for eviction.

source_file: 9972949_2019_U.S. Department of Justice v. Ables d_b_a Pops Cove_tnwd
case_name: United States of America v. Chad David Ables d/b/a Pops Cove
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; this is a sex discrimination/sexual harassment case where landlord allegedly demanded sex in exchange for rent waiver
key_holding: The court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over Intervenor-Plaintiffs' state-law breach of contract and breach of quiet enjoyment claims because those claims were already pending in state court, and exercising jurisdiction would insult comity.
brief_summary: The United States sued landlord Chad Ables alleging he discriminated against tenants Jones and Yarbrough based on sex by demanding sexually explicit photographs in exchange for waiving rent, then raising rent and cutting off water when refused. The court granted Ables's motion to dismiss the intervenor-plaintiffs' state-law claims for breach of contract and breach of quiet enjoyment, declining supplemental jurisdiction due to comity concerns over the parallel state court proceedings.
key_cases_cited: Musson Theatrical, Inc. v. Fed. Express Corp., 89 F.3d 1244 (6th Cir. 1996)
Packard v. Farmers Ins. Co. of Columbus Inc., 423 F. App'x 580 (6th Cir. 2011)
Soehnlen v. Fleet Owners Ins. Fund, 844 F.3d 576 (6th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Alexander v. Lexington Associates II, L.P.
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support animal (dog) in an apartment despite the property's no-pet policy
key_holding: The court dismissed the owner defendant because the plaintiff failed to allege any facts establishing an agency or employment relationship between the owner and the property management company or its agents who allegedly harassed her and denied the reasonable accommodation.
brief_summary: Cassandra Alexander, a mentally disabled tenant, sued Lexington Associates II (the owner of Hill Crest Place Apartments) and its property management company, alleging they violated the FHA by denying her request to keep an emotional support animal and then harassing her after she obtained one. The court granted Lexington's motion to dismiss because plaintiff failed to plead facts establishing vicarious liability between the owner and the management company or its agents.
key_cases_cited: Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, Fla., 137 S. Ct. 1296 (2017)
Kanuszewski v. Mich. Dep't of Health & Human Servs., 927 F.3d 396 (6th Cir. 2019)

case_name: Messeret Terzneh v. Federal National Mortgage Association
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges Fannie Mae refused to sell her the property because of her race (African American) and sold it to a white male buyer (Tigert) for less than her offer.
key_holding: The court found the proposed amendment futile because plaintiff's race discrimination claims under §§ 1981, 1982, 2000d, 1983, and the Fair Housing Act, as well as her civil conspiracy claim under § 1985(3), contained only conclusory allegations of discriminatory intent without supporting factual allegations and failed to state plausible claims for relief.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Messeret Terzneh sued Fannie Mae and Jacob Tigert alleging race discrimination, claiming Fannie Mae refused to sell her property because she is African American and instead sold it to Tigert for less than her offer. The court denied plaintiff's motion to amend as futile and granted defendants' motions to dismiss with prejudice, finding all claims lacked sufficient factual allegations to establish discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Rubin v. Fannie Mae, 587 F. App'x 273 (6th Cir. 2014)

source_file: 9976437_2020_Eastwood v. Willow Bend Lakes Homeowners Association, Inc._txed
case_name: Eastwood v. Willow Bend Lakes Homeowners Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Request for waiver of HOA restrictive covenant requiring preapproval for fence construction to accommodate cancer-related immunocompromised condition
key_holding: The court found plaintiff failed to demonstrate substantial likelihood of success on the merits because his claimed disability of being in close proximity to strangers does not constitute a major life activity under 42 U.S.C. § 3602(h), and thus he cannot establish he is handicapped under the FHA.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Norman Eastwood sued Willow Bend Lake HOA after it denied his request for a reasonable accommodation to build a second fence in his backyard, claiming his cancer-related immunocompromised condition required protection from public contact near his property. The court denied Eastwood's motion for preliminary injunction, finding he failed to show substantial likelihood of success because his claimed limitation of 'being in close proximity to strangers' does not constitute a major life activity under the FHA's definition of handicap.
key_cases_cited: Chavez v. Aber, 122 F. Supp. 3d 581
Giebeler v. M & B Assocs., 343 F.3d 1143
Shaw v. Habitat for Humanity of Citrus Cty., Inc., 938 F.3d 1259
Kearins v. Village Creek of El Dorado Home Owners' Assoc., Inc., 2019 WL 2266635
Nichols v. Alcatel USA, Inc., 532 F.3d 364

source_file: 9976921_2020_Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC v. City of Plano, Texas_txed
case_name: Constance Swanson, Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC, and Shannon Jones v. City of Plano, Texas
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs requested a variance to allow up to fifteen unrelated disabled persons to reside in the sober living home despite the ordinance's eight-person limit for household care facilities.
key_holding: The Court granted summary judgment for defendant on the direct-evidence theory because the ordinance permits eight unrelated disabled persons in a household care facility versus only four unrelated non-disabled persons, meaning disabled persons are treated more favorably, not worse, on its face; but the Court denied summary judgment on the circumstantial-evidence theory because plaintiffs raised genuine issues of material fact regarding whether defendant's proffered legitimate justifications for the occupancy limits were a pretext for discrimination.
brief_summary: Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC and its operator sued the City of Plano challenging an ordinance that limits household care facilities to eight unrelated disabled persons while allowing up to four unrelated non-disabled adults in traditional households, alleging the occupancy limits discriminate against disabled residents in violation of the FHA. The Court granted partial summary judgment for the City, finding no direct evidence of facial discrimination because disabled persons are actually permitted in greater numbers than non-disabled persons, but denied summary judgment on the circumstantial-evidence theory because plaintiffs raised genuine factual disputes about whether the city's proffered justifications for the ordinance were pretextual.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Hous. & Cmty. Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Groome Res. Ltd., L.L.C. v. Par. of Jefferson, 234 F.3d 192 (5th Cir. 2000)
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, No. 19-60007-CIV, 2020 WL 4736211 (S.D. Fla. Aug. 14, 2020)
Oxford House-C v. City of St. Louis, 77 F.3d 249 (8th Cir. 1996)

source_file: 9977166_2021_Terkel v. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention_txed
9977919_2021_Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC v. City of Plano, Texas_txed
case_name: Swanston v. City of Plano, Texas
accommodation_description: WESL requested permission to operate a sober living home with 15 residents rather than the 8-resident maximum allowed in the SF-7 zoning district under Plano's ordinance.
key_holding: The court found that WESL's requested accommodation of 15 residents was both therapeutically necessary and reasonable under the FHA and ADA, as the evidence demonstrated that eight residents would not provide the critical mass needed for a therapeutic milieu, and the accommodation imposed no undue financial or administrative burden on Plano.
brief_summary: Constance Swanston, Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC (WESL), and Shannon Jones sued the City of Plano, Texas, alleging the city violated the FHA and ADA by denying WESL's request to accommodate 15 residents at its sober living home instead of the 8-person maximum under the city's zoning ordinance. After a bench trial, the court found for Plaintiffs on the reasonable accommodation claim, holding that 15 residents was therapeutically necessary and reasonable, while finding for the city on the disparate treatment and disparate impact claims.
key_cases_cited: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc., 514 U.S. 725 (1995)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Comms. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Olmstead v. L.C. ex rel. Zimring, 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Oconomowoc Residential Programs v. City of Milwaukee, 300 F.3d 775 (7th Cir. 2002)
Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 851 F. App'x 461 (5th Cir. 2021)

source_file: 9978404_2022_Canadian Real Estate Holdings, LP v. Karen F. Newton Revocable Trust_txed
case_name: Canadian Real Estate Holdings, LP v. Karen F. Newton Revocable Trust
accommodation_description: CREH sought to develop and operate assisted living facilities for persons with Alzheimer's disease, alleging WGE Residents' enforcement of restrictive covenants to prevent such development violated the FHA.
key_holding: The court denied the motion to dismiss, finding CREH established constitutional standing by adequately alleging the WGE Residents' actions (filing the state court action and drafting a letter to county officials) influenced Collin County's refusal to issue permits, and declined to apply judicial estoppel because CREH's prior representations about mootness of injunctive/declaratory relief claims were not plainly inconsistent with its current FHA claims.
brief_summary: CREH, a developer seeking to operate assisted living facilities for Alzheimer's patients, sued WGE Residents for opposing the development through restrictive covenant enforcement, alleging FHA violations. The court denied the WGE Residents' motion to dismiss, finding CREH established standing by adequately alleging the residents' actions contributed to permit denial and rejecting judicial estoppel because CREH's prior mootness representations did not plainly conflict with its current FHA claims.
key_cases_cited: Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
New Hampshire v. Maine, 532 U.S. 742 (2001)
Firefighters' Ret. Sys. v. EisnerAmper, L.L.P., 898 F.3d 553 (5th Cir. 2018)
Spokeo, Inc. v. Robbins, 578 U.S. 330 (2016)
AHF Comm. Dev., LLC v. City of Dallas, 633 F. Supp. 2d 287 (N.D. Tex. 2009)

source_file: 9978827_2022_Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC v. City of Plano, Texas_txed
case_name: Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC v. City of Plano, Texas
accommodation_description: Request that the City of Plano not enforce occupancy restrictions on a sober living home as a reasonable accommodation under the FHA and ADA for residents with substance use disorders in recovery.
key_holding: The court awarded plaintiffs attorneys' fees and costs as prevailing parties, finding the successful failure-to-accommodate claim was inextricably intertwined with unsuccessful claims and warranted the full lodestar amount without reduction.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs Constance Swanston, Women's Elevated Sober Living LLC, and Shannon Jones sued the City of Plano, Texas under the FHA and ADA, challenging the City's enforcement of occupancy restrictions against a sober living home as a failure to accommodate residents with substance use disorders. After prevailing on the reasonable accommodation claim at trial, plaintiffs moved for attorneys' fees, which the court granted in full, rejecting the City's argument for a downward adjustment based on limited damages recovery.
key_cases_cited: Hensley v. Eckerhart, 461 U.S. 424 (1983)
Blum v. Stenson, 465 U.S. 886 (1984)
Combs v. City of Huntington, 829 F.3d 388 (5th Cir. 2016)
Migis v. Pearle Vision, Inc., 135 F.3d 1041 (5th Cir. 1998)
Fessler v. Porcelana Corona De Mexico, S.A. DE C.V., 23 F.4th 408 (5th Cir. 2022)

source_file: 9979478_2023_Canadian Real Estate Holdings, LP v. Karen F. Newton Revocable Trust_txed
case_name: Canadian Real Estate Holdings, LP v. Karen F. Newton Revocable Trust
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges WGE Residents discriminated against the handicapped by enforcing restrictive covenants and failing to make reasonable accommodations in connection with Plaintiff's planned commercial development (assisted living facility) within the Walnut Grove neighborhood.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion for reconsideration, finding Plaintiff has standing under the FHA to seek damages for economic losses allegedly caused by Defendants' discriminatory enforcement of restrictive covenants and their influence on Collin County officials' permit decisions, and that judicial estoppel does not bar Plaintiff's claims because prior representations about mootness were not plainly inconsistent with the current FHA action.
brief_summary: Canadian Real Estate Holdings sued Walnut Grove Estate residents (WGE Residents), alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by discriminating against handicapped persons through restrictive covenant enforcement and failing to make reasonable accommodations in connection with Plaintiff's planned assisted living facility development. The court denied Defendants' motion for reconsideration of the order denying their motion to dismiss, holding that Plaintiff has standing under the FHA's broad aggrieved-person standard to seek damages for economic injuries, and that judicial estoppel does not bar the claims because Plaintiff's prior representations about mootness in related state court proceedings were not plainly inconsistent with the current FHA action.
key_cases_cited: N.A.A.C.P. v. City of Kyle, 626 F.3d 233 (5th Cir. 2010)
Bank of Am. Corp. v. City of Miami, 581 U.S. 189 (2017)
Parada v. Sandhill Shores Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 604 F. Supp. 3d 567 (S.D. Tex. 2022)
Kane v. Nat'l Union Fire Ins., 535 F.3d 380 (5th Cir. 2008)
Firefighters' Ret. Sys. v. EisnerAmper, L.L.P., 898 F.3d 553 (5th Cir. 2018)

case_name: J W Seals, Jr., Vergie Seals, and Marie L. Pace v. Itex Group LLC
accommodation_description: No specific accommodation requested; plaintiffs alleged that defendant failed to remedy habitability issues (pest infestations, mold, sewage leaks, structural defects) rendering the apartment unavailable.
key_holding: The court held that habitability issues such as pest infestations, mold, sewage leaks, and structural defects do not make a dwelling 'unavailable' within the meaning of Sections 3604(a) or 3604(f)(1), and plaintiffs failed to state a claim under the Fair Housing Act.
brief_summary: Three individual tenants sued their landlord Itex Group LLC alleging the defendant failed to remedy housing conditions (pest infestations, mold, sewage leaks, structural defects) making the apartment uninhabitable, constituting discrimination under the Fair Housing Act. The court dismissed the FHA claims, finding that uninhabitable conditions do not constitute making a dwelling 'unavailable' under Sections 3604(a) or 3604(f)(1), and declined to grant leave to amend the complaint as further amendments would be futile.
key_cases_cited: Trafficante v. Metro. Life Co., 409 U.S. 205 (1972)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Cox v. City of Dallas, 430 F.3d 734 (5th Cir. 2004)
Treece v. Perrier Condo. Owners Ass'n, 593 F. Supp. 3d 422 (E.D. La. 2022)

source_file: 9980001_2023_Chamber of Commerce of the United States of America v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau_txed
9980353_2023_Brady v. The Evangelical Lutheran Good Samaritan Society_txed
case_name: Mary Brady v. The Evangelical Lutheran Good Samaritan Society
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested priority position on waitlist, carpeting for joint condition, permission to install security system, and unit mold remediation due to allergies and autoimmune disease.
key_holding: Plaintiff failed to state an FHA claim because she did not adequately plead that her allergies, autoimmune disease, and joint condition substantially limit major life activities (establishing a handicap), nor did she allege facts showing defendant knew of her disability when refusing accommodations.
brief_summary: Mary Brady sued the Good Samaritan Society under the Fair Housing Act and Title III ADA after being denied residence at the Denton Village senior living community, claiming denial of reasonable accommodations for her disabilities (polyarthralgia, allergies, autoimmune disease). The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding Brady failed to adequately plead she is 'handicapped' under the FHA because her conclusory allegations about her impairments did not demonstrate they substantially limit major life activities compared to most people, and she failed to allege facts showing defendant knew of her disability when refusing the requested accommodations.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Mueck v. La Grange Acquisitions, L.P., 75 F.4th 469 (5th Cir. 2023)
Windham v. Harris County, 875 F.3d 229 (5th Cir. 2017)
Chavez v. Aber, 122 F Supp.3d 581 (W.D. Tex. 2015)

source_file: 9981050_2019_The Inclusive Communities Project Inc v. Heartland Community Association Inc_txnd
case_name: The Inclusive Communities Project, Inc. v. Heartland Community Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: No accommodation requested; this is a challenge to a 'no Section 8 voucher' policy alleged to discriminate based on race.
