key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's claims because its statistics failed to establish robust causation between the 'no Section 8 voucher' policy and racial disparities, and plaintiff failed to plead sufficient facts to support a reasonable inference of intentional race discrimination.
brief_summary: The Inclusive Communities Project sued Heartland Community Association challenging a policy prohibiting Section 8 voucher holders from renting in a single-family development, alleging disparate impact and disparate treatment race discrimination under the FHA. The court granted HCA's motion to dismiss, finding ICP failed to plead facts showing the policy caused racial disparities (robust causation) and failed to adequately allege discriminatory intent.
key_cases_cited: Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 135 S. Ct. 2507 (2015)
Inclusive Communities Project v. Lincoln Prop. Co., 920 F.3d 890 (5th Cir. 2019)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: 9981922_2020_Jackson v. City of Grand Prairie Texas_txnd
case_name: Cleveland Jackson, Jr. v. City of Grand Prairie Texas
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a second extension of the 60-day housing voucher search period due to disability-related difficulties in searching for and visiting apartments.
key_holding: The court dismissed the reasonable accommodation claim because the complaint failed to allege with particularity the nature of the equal opportunity denied, the impact of an additional extension, and how that extension would directly ameliorate the effect of the disability to provide equal opportunity to obtain housing.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Cleveland Jackson, Jr., a mobility-impaired individual, sued the City of Grand Prairie, Texas, alleging the city violated the FHA, ADA, and Rehabilitation Act by refusing to grant a second extension of his Section 8 voucher search period beyond 120 days due to his disability-related difficulties in finding accessible housing. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to allege with sufficient particularity how the requested accommodation would directly provide equal opportunity to obtain housing, though the dismissal was without prejudice and plaintiff was granted leave to amend.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harmony Haus Westlake, LLC v. Parkstone Prop. Owners Ass'n, Inc., 2020 WL 1241724 (W.D. Tex. 2020)
Oxford House, Inc. v. Browning, 266 F. Supp. 3d 896 (M.D. La. 2017)
Melton v. Dallas Area Rapid Transit, 391 F.3d 669 (5th Cir. 2004)

source_file: 9999651_2024_Emory Christian v. Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community_ca9
case_name: Emory D. Christian v. Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request; Christian alleged racial discrimination in housing based on her being Black, differential treatment compared to white residents, and a reference in an email to her race.
key_holding: The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal of Christian's racial discrimination claims under 42 U.S.C. §§ 1981 and 1982 because she failed to plead sufficient facts to raise a plausible inference that racial animus was a but-for cause of any defendant's actions, and properly denied leave to add FHA race discrimination and retaliation claims that were inadequately pled.
brief_summary: Emory Christian, a Black woman residing in the Rancho Grande Manufactured Home Community in Rohnert Park, California, sued the mobile home park, its managers, and a neighbor alleging racial discrimination and constitutional violations arising from a property dispute. The Ninth Circuit affirmed dismissal of her federal claims, finding that she failed to plead sufficient facts to support a plausible inference that racial animus motivated any defendant's conduct, and properly denied leave to add Fair Housing Act claims for race discrimination and retaliation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Comcast Corp. v. Nat'l Ass'n of Afr. Am.-Owned Media, 589 U.S. 327 (2020)
General Bldg. Contractors Ass'n, Inc. v. Pennsylvania, 458 U.S. 375 (1982)
Griffin v. Breckenridge, 403 U.S. 88 (1971)
Southwest Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Maricopa Domestic Water Improvement Dist., 17 F.4th 950 (9th Cir. 2021)

source_file: ABDELRASOUL v. Trustees of Boston University
case_name: Abdelrasoul v. Trustees of Boston University
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to transfer from a studio apartment to a one-bedroom apartment as a reasonable accommodation for his mental health disability
key_holding: Counts 11-13 alleging disability discrimination under the FHA are dismissed because plaintiff failed to adequately plead a qualifying disability or explain why the one-bedroom accommodation was necessary for equal opportunity to use and enjoy his dwelling, while Count 14 alleging retaliation survives because plaintiff adequately pleaded a causal connection between his accommodation requests and BU's decision to decline his housing renewal.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Islam Abdelrasoul, a Ph.D. student at Boston University, sued BU alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act for denying his request to transfer from a studio to a one-bedroom apartment and refusing to renew his housing agreement. The Court granted BU's motion to dismiss Counts 11-13 for failure to adequately plead a qualifying disability and explain why the accommodation was necessary, but denied the motion as to Count 14 (retaliation), finding plaintiff adequately pleaded that BU's decision to block his housing renewal was motivated by his accommodation requests.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Sec'y, U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Dixon v. Hallmark Cos., 627 F.3d 849 (11th Cir. 2010)
Valle-Arce v. P.R. Ports Auth., 651 F.3d 190 (1st Cir. 2011)

case_name: King Anthony Alvarez v. City of Philadelphia, et al.
accommodation_description: Pro se plaintiff alleges retaliation for filing fair housing complaints; no reasonable accommodation request is at issue.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA retaliation claim as barred by res judicata because the same claim was raised and dismissed with prejudice in a prior lawsuit, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to allege membership in a protected class as required to state an FHA retaliation claim.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff King Anthony Alvarez sued the City of Philadelphia, HUD, and nonprofit organizations, alleging that his termination from a subsidized housing program in November 2022 constituted FHA retaliation for filing fair housing complaints. The court dismissed the complaint as barred by res judicata because the same retaliation claim had been litigated and dismissed with prejudice in a prior case, and alternatively because plaintiff failed to adequately allege membership in a protected class required for an FHA retaliation claim.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Beasley v. Howard, 14 F.4th 226 (3d Cir. 2021)
Gimenez v. Morgan Stanley DW, Inc., 202 F. App'x 583 (3d Cir. 2006)
Haefner v. N. Cornwall Twp., 40 F. App'x 656 (3d Cir. 2002)

source_file: Amber Reineck House v City of Howell Michigan
Avenue 6E Investments LLC v City of Yuma Arizona
BOARD OF COUNTY COMMISSIONERS OF JOHNSON COUNTY v. Jorgensen
case_name: BOARD OF COUNTY COMMISSIONERS OF JOHNSON COUNTY v. Jorgensen
accommodation_description: Johnson County requested that group homes for developmentally impaired adults be exempted from state fire code requirements as a reasonable accommodation under the FHA.
key_holding: A blanket exclusion from an exhaustive regulatory scheme does not constitute a reasonable accommodation under the FHA; an appropriate accommodation requires a more tailored modification of specific requirements rather than a wholesale exemption that amounts to a fundamental alteration of the regulatory scheme.
brief_summary: Johnson County appealed a district court decision upholding the State Fire Marshal's denial of an exemption from state fire code requirements for group homes housing developmentally impaired adults, arguing the exemption constituted a reasonable accommodation under the FHA. The Kansas Court of Appeals affirmed, holding that a blanket exclusion from an exhaustive regulatory scheme does not constitute a reasonable accommodation under the FHA because it amounts to a fundamental alteration of the regulatory framework rather than a tailored modification of specific requirements.
key_cases_cited: Summers v. City of Fitchburg, 940 F.3d 133 (1st Cir. 2019)
Davis v. Echo Valley Condominium Association, 945 F.3d 483 (6th Cir. 2019)
Giebler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Alexander v. Choate, 469 U.S. 287 (1985)
Texas Dept. of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)

source_file: Bailey v. THE QUEEN'S LANDING COUNCIL OF UNIT OWNERS, INC.
case_name: Bailey v. The Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a disabled unit owner with severe allergies triggered by mold, alleges the Council failed to remediate mold in her condominium unit, rendering it uninhabitable, which the court treats as disparate treatment discrimination rather than analyzing as a standalone reasonable accommodation request.
key_holding: The Court dismissed plaintiff's sex discrimination claim for failure to state facts supporting disparate treatment because of sex, but denied motion to dismiss as to race and disability discrimination claims (finding sufficient allegations of differential treatment in repair services based on plaintiff's race and disability) and retaliation claim (finding sufficient causal connection between protected activity and adverse action).
brief_summary: Dr. Rosanna Bailey, a Black disabled condominium owner, sued the Queen's Landing Council of Unit Owners (her HOA) alleging race discrimination, disability discrimination, and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act, claiming the Council treated her inequitably regarding facility repairs and maintenance compared to white and non-disabled neighbors. The Court dismissed her sex discrimination claim for failure to state a claim but allowed her race and disability discrimination and retaliation claims to proceed past the motion to dismiss stage.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Reyes v. Waples Mobile Home Park Ltd. P'ship, 903 F.3d 415 (4th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Rickey Banks v. Ryan Ross et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly requested accommodations including transportation services, a donated vehicle, and continued housing at a transitional shelter, but the accommodation requests are vaguely pleaded.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims were dismissed because his complaint failed to allege sufficient facts connecting his alleged disabilities (arthritis, wheelchair/cane/walker use, anxiety) to Defendants' actions, and his Title III ADA claims fail because Title III does not provide for monetary damages.
brief_summary: Rickey Banks, a pro se plaintiff who uses a wheelchair and alleges various disabilities, sued Ryan Ross (Executive Director of Open Door Ministries) and other employees of the Arthur Cassell Memorial Transitional House, a VA-funded transitional housing program, alleging discrimination and failure to accommodate under the ADA and FHA. The court recommended dismissing the federal claims without prejudice because the complaint failed to state plausible claims—Plaintiff's ADA Title III claims cannot seek monetary damages, his FHA claims lack sufficient factual nexus between his disabilities and Defendants' actions, and his constitutional § 1983 claims fail because Defendants are private actors not acting under color of state law.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)
PGA Tour, Inc. v. Martin, 532 U.S. 661 (2001)

case_name: Banks v. Ross
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a wheelchair user with arthritis and anxiety, alleges he was denied receipt of a donated vehicle and transportation services from a transitional housing program as an accommodation for his disability.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA and ADA claims fail because he has not alleged sufficient facts showing a nexus between his alleged disabilities and the defendants' actions; additionally, his Title III ADA claim fails because he seeks monetary damages rather than injunctive relief.
brief_summary: Rickey Banks, a veteran in transitional housing who uses a wheelchair and has anxiety, sued program directors and staff at Open Door Ministries alleging they denied him transportation services and a donated vehicle in violation of the ADA and FHA, and discriminated against him based on race and disability. The court granted defendants' motions to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to state plausible federal claims because plaintiff did not adequately allege how his disabilities connected to the alleged discriminatory conduct, and his ADA Title III damages claim was improper.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)

case_name: Latonia Black v. Royal American Management, et al.
accommodation_description: Procedural order granting leave to amend complaint; underlying complaint alleged reasonable accommodation denial and race discrimination in rental housing.
key_holding: The court granted Black leave to file an amended complaint under Rule 15(a)(2)'s liberal standard, finding no undue prejudice to defendants despite Black's delay and carelessness in the amendment process, as the amendment was filed before the scheduling order deadline.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Latonia Black sued Royal American Management and others alleging Fair Housing Act violations including reasonable accommodation denial and race discrimination in connection with her rental property. After the Magistrate Judge recommended dismissing two retaliation claims and Black missed the deadline to amend as a matter of course, the court granted Black leave to file an amended complaint under Rule 15(a)(2)'s liberal standard because no undue prejudice would result and the amendment was filed before the scheduling order deadline.
key_cases_cited: In re Engle Cases, 767 F.3d 1082 (11th Cir. 2014)
Foman v. Davis, 371 U.S. 178 (1962)
Bryant v. Dupree, 252 F.3d 1161 (11th Cir. 2001)
Zenith Radio Corp. v. Hazeltine Rsch., Inc., 401 U.S. 321 (1971)
Pinnacle Advert. & Mktg. Grp., Inc. v. Pinnacle Advert. & Mktg. Grp., LLC, 7 F.4th 989 (11th Cir. 2021)

case_name: Sonja Marie Boyce v. St. Vincent DePaul (Lane County), GBM Garder Beckley Meadows
accommodation_description: Plaintiff filed a vague complaint alleging housing discrimination under the FHA but failed to articulate a specific accommodation request; court analyzed disparate treatment and disparate impact theories rather than reasonable accommodation denial.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's complaint because she failed to allege sufficient facts to establish federal jurisdiction, constitutional violations, or viable FHA claims under either disparate treatment or disparate impact theories.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Sonja Marie Boyce sued St. Vincent DePaul and GBM alleging Fair Housing Act violations related to an eviction and back rent dispute in Eugene, Oregon. The court dismissed the complaint without service of process for failure to state claims, finding plaintiff's allegations too vague to establish federal jurisdiction, constitutional violations, or viable disparate treatment or disparate impact claims under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 554 (2007)
Gamble v. City of Escondido, 104 F.3d 300 (9th Cir. 1997)

source_file: Brandt v Village of Chebanse Ill
CORI'S PLACE v. THE ZONING HEARING BOARD OF THE CITY OF NANTICOKE
case_name: Cori's Place v. The Zoning Hearing Board of the City of Nanticoke
accommodation_description: Zoning permit to allow four adults with intellectual disabilities to reside in a single-family residential zone with staff supervision, including the ability to have separate leases.
key_holding: The court found Plaintiff adequately pleaded a reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) because allowing four individuals with intellectual disabilities to live together in an R-2 residential zone with separate leases is a plausibly reasonable accommodation necessary to afford them equal opportunity to housing.
brief_summary: Cori's Place, an operator providing residential services for adults with intellectual disabilities, sued the City of Nanticoke and its Zoning Hearing Board after being denied a zoning permit to operate a group residence for four disabled individuals in an R-2 residential zone. The court granted in part and denied in part Defendants' motion to dismiss, dismissing intentional discrimination, disparate impact, interference, and ADA claims but allowing the reasonable accommodation claim under § 3604(f)(3)(B) to proceed because Plaintiff adequately pleaded that separate leases for four disabled residents constitute a plausibly reasonable accommodation necessary to afford them equal housing opportunity.
key_cases_cited: Vorchheimer v. Philadelphian Owners Assoc., 903 F.3d 100 (3d Cir. 2018)
Hovsons, Inc. v. Twp. of Brick, 89 F.3d 1096 (3d Cir. 1996)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)

source_file: CYMBALSKY v. 522 SHORE ROAD OWNERS, INC.
case_name: Gerald Cymbalsky v. 522 Shore Road Owners, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleged he was subjected to adverse housing actions due to his disabilities (osteoarthritis, anxiety, morbid obesity) but did not request any specific accommodation.
key_holding: Plaintiff's FHA claim fails because he continues to reside in his apartment, prevailed in the underlying state court action, and critically, he never requested any accommodation for his disabilities; the court dismissed the FHA claim finding no adverse action and no causal connection to disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Gerald Cymbalsky, a disabled tenant with osteoarthritis and anxiety disorders, sued his cooperative apartment building, property management companies, and attorneys for alleged Fair Housing Act violations, civil RICO violations, constitutional rights violations, and FDCPA violations stemming from a state court 'furniture eviction' proceeding. The court granted all motions to dismiss, finding that Cymbalsky's FHA claim failed because he continues to reside in his apartment, he 'won' the state court action, and he never requested any accommodation for his disabilities.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Logan v. Matveevskii, 175 F. Supp. 3d 209 (S.D.N.Y. 2016)

source_file: Candlehouse Inc v Town of Vestal NY
Caron Foundation of Florida Inc v City of Delray Beach

case_name: Chapman v. Davis
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to keep her dog Nemo as an emotional support animal as a reasonable accommodation for her disability.
key_holding: The housing provider's request for the ESA's vaccination records was not a refusal to make a reasonable accommodation but merely a request for information, and plaintiff failed to allege defendants refused to make the accommodation after learning of her disability and ESA status.
brief_summary: Tasha Chapman, an African American woman with minor children participating in the Section 8 HCV program, sued her private landlords and property managers alleging they discriminated against her based on race, familial status, and disability by refusing to allow her emotional support animal and initiating eviction proceedings. The court dismissed all claims on initial screen, finding the ESA accommodation request failed because requesting vaccination records was not a refusal to accommodate, and plaintiff failed to allege any discriminatory statements supporting her race and familial status claims.
key_cases_cited: Overlook Mut. Homes, Inc. v. Spencer, 415 F. App'x 617 (6th Cir. 2011)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Connor Grp., 805 F. Supp. 2d 396 (S.D. Ohio 2011)

source_file: City of Grants Pass Oregon v Johnson
DELAWARE HUMAN AND CIVIL RIGHTS COMMISSION EX REL. MILLAR v. GREENBRIER CONDOS, LLC
case_name: Delaware Human and Civil Rights Commission ex rel. Millar v. Greenbrier Condos, L.L.C.
accommodation_description: Ms. Millar preferred to lease a first-floor apartment because she has difficulty walking.
key_holding: The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss Counts I and III, holding that the Commission could pursue claims beyond those specifically found to have reasonable cause in the Division's Written Findings, as long as the claims arise from the same operative facts in the Charge.
brief_summary: The Delaware Human and Civil Rights Commission (on behalf of Relator Gabrielle Millar, a disabled person with an emotional support animal) sued Greenbrier Condos and its property manager alleging they refused to rent to Ms. Millar and failed to accommodate her ESA request in violation of the Delaware Fair Housing Act. The court denied Defendants' motion to dismiss Counts I and III, holding that under Delaware law, the Commission could pursue additional claims arising from the same operative facts as the Division's Charge, even though the Division did not find reasonable cause on all claims.
key_cases_cited: Central Mortg. Co. v. Morgan Stanley Mortg. Capital Holdings LLC, 227 A.3d 531 (Del. 2011)
Doe v. Cedars Academy, 2010 WL 5825353 (Del. Super. Oct. 27, 2010)
Ramunno v. Crawley, 705 A.2d 1029 (Del. 1998)

source_file: Dews v Town of Sunnyvale Tex

case_name: Dunn v. Manuel
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested a temporary substitute apartment to live in while her apartment was being repaired and she dealt with personal items related to her hoarding disorder.
key_holding: The court dismissed all FHA claims because plaintiff failed to establish that hoarding disorder qualifies as a 'handicap' under the FHA, as she did not plead facts showing it substantially limits major life activities, and separately failed to state a plausible failure-to-accommodate claim.
brief_summary: Nora Dunn sued her landlord Sharon Hydinger alleging the landlord discriminated against her based on her hoarding disorder by refusing to repair her apartment, refusing to provide a substitute apartment as an accommodation, and attempting to evict her. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding that hoarding disorder is not a protected disability under the FHA and that even if it were, Dunn failed to plead sufficient facts to support her claims for intentional discrimination, failure to accommodate, retaliation, or hostile environment.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale, 46 F.4th 1268 (11th Cir. 2022)
Bonasera v. City of Norcross, 342 F. App'x 581 (11th Cir. 2009)

source_file: EIGHT IS ENOUGH, LLC v. OHLIG
case_name: Eight Is Enough, LLC v. Ohlig
accommodation_description: Tenant requested permission to keep an emotional support dog, have a live-in caretaker (her adult grandson), and receive help with cleaning and maintenance as reasonable accommodations for anxiety, depression, and PTSD.
key_holding: The appellate court held that the trial court erred by failing to consider Ohlig's disability discrimination defense (including retaliation for requesting reasonable accommodations) at the show cause hearing, and remanded for the court to expressly consider the defense and determine if genuine issues of material fact require trial; the court otherwise affirmed the judgment for unpaid rent and rejected the notice-of-proposed-judgment argument.
brief_summary: Tenant Cynthia Ohlig appealed an unlawful detainer judgment seeking possession of her rental home, arguing the trial court erred by failing to consider her disability discrimination defense (that the eviction was retaliatory for her reasonable accommodation request for an emotional support dog and live-in caretaker) at the show cause hearing. The Washington Court of Appeals held that under Josephinium and the Washington Law Against Discrimination, discrimination defenses that affect the right to possession must be considered at show cause hearings, and remanded for the trial court to expressly evaluate Ohlig's discrimination defense and determine whether genuine issues of material fact require trial.
key_cases_cited: Josephinium Assocs. v. Kahli, 111 Wn. App. 617
Faciszewski v. Brown, 187 Wn.2d 308
Hansen v. Boeing Co., 903 F. Supp. 2d 1215

source_file: EWIDEH v. HOMESITE INS. CO. OF THE MIDWEST
case_name: EWIDEH v. HOMESITE INS. CO. OF THE MIDWEST
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs vaguely alleged discrimination based on unidentified health conditions and sexuality by an insurance claims adjuster, but this claim was not a reasonable accommodation request and was dismissed for failure to state a claim.
key_holding: The plaintiffs' FHA claim was dismissed without prejudice because the complaint failed to adequately plead any cognizable federal claim, failing to identify what disability was at issue or articulate a viable theory of housing discrimination.
brief_summary: Omar Ewideh and Nivertiti Geaith, proceeding pro se, sued Homesite Insurance Company and other defendants asserting breach of contract, bad faith insurance denial, and a cursory Fair Housing Act claim alleging discrimination based on unspecified health conditions and sexuality. The court recommended granting the motion to dismiss because the plaintiffs failed to oppose the motion, violated numerous court orders demonstrating failure to prosecute, and failed to adequately plead any cognizable federal claim, dismissing the FHA claim without prejudice.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly
Ashcroft v. Iqbal

source_file: FAIR HOUS. JUST. CTR., INC. v. PELICAN MGMT., INC.
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. Pelican Management, Inc.
accommodation_description: Facially neutral minimum income policy (40x rent requirement for partially subsidized applicants) challenged as having disparate impact on renters with disabilities who receive rental subsidies.
key_holding: The Second Circuit affirmed that the district court properly credited FHJC's expert methodology in finding that Goldfarb's 2019 Policy—requiring partially subsidized applicants to earn 40 times their share of monthly rent while fully subsidized applicants faced no income requirements—had a disparate impact on applicants with disabilities who receive rental subsidies, in violation of the NYCHRL.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center sued Goldfarb Properties (a large New York managing agent) alleging that Goldfarb's minimum income policies violated the Fair Housing Act and New York City Human Rights Law by disparately impacting applicants with disabilities who receive rental subsidies. The Second Circuit affirmed the district court's judgment, holding that the 2019 Policy's requirement that partially subsidized applicants earn 40 times their share of monthly rent had a statistically significant disparate impact on protected applicants.
key_cases_cited: Tsombanidis v. W. Haven Fire Dep't, 352 F.3d 565 (2d Cir. 2003)
Levin v. Yeshiva Univ., 96 N.Y.2d 484 (2001)
Mihalik v. Credit Agricole Cheuvreux N. Am., Inc., 715 F.3d 102 (2d Cir. 2013)
Clerveaux v. E. Ramapo Cent. Sch. Dist., 984 F.3d 213 (2d Cir. 2021)
Sacerdote v. N.Y. Univ., 9 F.4th 95 (2d Cir. 2021)

source_file: FAIR HOUSING JUSTICE CENTER, INC. v. BRUCKNER TOWER LLC
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. Bruckner Tower LLC
accommodation_description: Fair housing organization alleges design and construction accessibility violations in a residential complex, seeking to present evidence of statutory violations at trial.
key_holding: A certificate of occupancy does not constitute conclusive evidence of compliance with design and construction requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C), and § 3604(f)(6)(B) expressly provides that state/local government determinations shall not be conclusive in enforcement proceedings.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center sued Bruckner Tower LLC and related entities alleging Federal Fair Housing Amendments Act violations regarding design and construction accessibility requirements for a residential complex in the Bronx. The court denied the defendants' motion in limine to preclude evidence of statutory violations, holding that a certificate of occupancy does not provide conclusive evidence of substantial compliance with § 3604(f)(3)(C) design and construction requirements.

source_file: FAIR HOUSING JUSTICE CENTER, INC. v. BRUCKNER TOWER, LLC
case_name: Fair Housing Justice Center, Inc. v. Bruckner Tower, LLC
accommodation_description: Fair housing organization alleged that the 130-unit multi-family building known as the Crescendo was designed and constructed with accessibility violations requiring physical modifications to common areas and individual dwelling units to comply with FHAA design and construction requirements.
key_holding: The parties settled via consent judgment requiring the owner defendants to perform remediation items to address alleged design and construction accessibility violations and pay $200,000 in damages, fees, and costs, while defendants denied any admission of liability.
brief_summary: Fair Housing Justice Center sued Bruckner Tower LLC, Carnegie Management, and related architect entities alleging that the 130-unit multi-family residential building at 25 Bruckner Boulevard in the Bronx was designed and constructed in violation of the Fair Housing Act's accessibility requirements under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). The parties settled via consent judgment requiring owner defendants to perform physical remediation modifications to the building's common areas and individual units within specified deadlines, hire a neutral inspector to verify compliance, adopt fair housing policies and training programs, and pay $200,000 in damages, attorneys' fees, and costs.

source_file: George v. OVERALL CREEK APARTMENTS, LLC (1)
case_name: George v. Overall Creek Apartments, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff who uses a wheelchair encountered physical accessibility barriers at the apartment complex that would interfere with her ability to access and use the facilities.
key_holding: The FHA preempts state law claims for equitable indemnification (which shift the entire loss to another party) as an obstacle to the FHA's remedial purposes, but does not preempt state law claims for equitable contribution (which apportion only a proportionate share of liability) because such claims do not frustrate congressional objectives.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Cynthia George sued property owners and developers alleging Fair Housing Act violations for failing to design and construct accessible apartment units; the property owners filed a third-party complaint against the architect seeking indemnification, contribution, and breach of contract damages. The court granted the architect's motion to dismiss the indemnification claim as preempted by the FHA but denied dismissal of the contribution claim, holding that proportional fault-sharing claims do not obstruct FHA enforcement objectives.
key_cases_cited: Equal Rights Ctr. v. Niles Bolton Assocs., 602 F.3d 597 (4th Cir. 2010)
City of L.A. v. AECOM Servs., Inc., 854 F.3d 1149 (9th Cir. 2017)
United States v. Murphy Dev., LLC, No. 3:08-0960, 2009 WL 3614829 (M.D. Tenn. Oct. 27, 2009)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Campus Vill. Wright State, LLC, 2012 WL 4473236 (S.D. Ohio Sept. 26, 2012)
County of Livingston v. PSA-Dewberry, Inc., No. 19-CV-01334, 2023 WL 5962079 (C.D. Ill. Sept. 13, 2023)

source_file: George v. OVERALL CREEK APARTMENTS, LLC
case_name: Cynthia George v. Overall Creek Apartments, LLC et al.
accommodation_description: Design and construction accessibility claim under § 3604(f)(3)(C) — plaintiff, a wheelchair user, alleged the defendant failed to design and construct the property in compliance with Fair Housing Act accessibility guidelines
key_holding: The plaintiff's design and construction claim under § 3604(f)(3)(C) is time-barred because the statute of limitations begins running when the last available unit is initially rented, and here the final unit was rented on March 19, 2021, more than two years before the lawsuit was filed on March 31, 2023; the model unit (No. 1107) did not toll the limitations period because it was never intended to be made available for rent.
brief_summary: Cynthia George sued Overall Creek Apartments, LLC and related defendants alleging the multifamily housing development in Murfreesboro, Tennessee was designed and constructed in violation of the Fair Housing Act's accessibility requirements for persons with disabilities. The court granted summary judgment for defendants, holding that George's FHA design-and-construction claim was time-barred because the two-year statute of limitations began running when the last available rental unit was initially occupied on March 19, 2021, more than two years before she filed suit, and the model unit she identified did not toll the limitations period since it was never offered for rent.
key_cases_cited: Fair Hous. Council, Inc. v. Vill. of Olde St. Andrews, Inc., 210 F. App'x 469 (6th Cir. 2006)
Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477 U.S. 242 (1986)
Miami Valley Fair Hous. Ctr., Inc. v. Preferred Living Real Est. Invs., LLC, 2018 WL 4690790 (S.D. Ohio 2018)
Hous. Rsch. & Advoc. Ctr. v. WXZ Residential Grp./Circle Lofts 118 LLC, 2017 WL 1078956 (N.D. Ohio 2017)
Hous. Advocs., Inc. v. Berardi & Partners, Inc., 2010 WL 4905547 (N.D. Ohio 2010)

source_file: Greater New Orleans Fair Housing Action Center v St Bernard Parish

case_name: Michael S. Hartley v. United Student Rentals, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges disability-based discrimination arising from landlord's failure to repair apartment front door over approximately one year period.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA claim because plaintiff does not describe how his mental impairments limit major life activities, nor does he allege facts connecting defendants' failure to repair the door to his disability.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Michael Hartley sued private landlords and their attorney alleging the defendants violated the Fair Housing Act by failing to repair his apartment's front door over approximately one year despite his disability-related mental health conditions. The N.D.N.Y. recommended dismissing the complaint because plaintiff failed to allege facts connecting any failure to repair the door to his disability or showing defendants were responsible for the repair.
key_cases_cited: Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)
Ingraham v. Red Carpet Hous. Corp., 2017 WL 5152343 (N.D.N.Y. 2017)
Robinson v. Soc. Serv., 2013 WL 1122450 (N.D.N.Y. 2013)
Fabrikant v. French, 691 F.3d 193 (2d Cir. 2012)
Triestman v. Fed. Bureau of Prisons, 470 F.3d 471 (2d Cir. 2006)

source_file: Hispanics United of DuPage County v Village of Addison Ill
Humphries v. US DEPARTMENT OF HOUSING & URBAN DEVELOPMENT
case_name: Humphries v. U.S. Department of Housing & Urban Development
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested to respond in writing or by electronic communication instead of telephone conversations during HUD's investigation of her housing discrimination complaints, as an accommodation for her disabilities (anxiety, depression, memory loss, brain injuries).
key_holding: The court dismissed all claims because HUD is immune from suit under the ADA and Rehabilitation Act due to sovereign immunity, the FHA provides no private right of action against HUD, and the individual HUD employees cannot be held liable under the ADA, RA, or Bivens.
brief_summary: Jacqueline Humphries, proceeding pro se, sued HUD and HUD employees alleging they denied her reasonable accommodations (requesting written/electronic communication instead of phone calls) during HUD's investigation of her housing discrimination complaints against housing authorities. The court dismissed all claims, finding HUD immune from ADA and Rehabilitation Act claims, no private right of action under the FHA against HUD, and no individual liability under any asserted theory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2009)
Biven v. Six Unknown Named Agents of Fed. Bureau of Narcotics, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)
Turner v. Sec'y of U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urban Dev., 449 F.3d 536 (3d Cir. 2006)
Venter v. Potter, 435 F. App'x 92 (3d Cir. 2011)

source_file: Huntington Branch NAACP v Town of Huntington
Jackson v City of Auburn Ala

case_name: Anthony Alexander James v. Metzger Management Company
accommodation_description: No disability accommodation request was made; plaintiff alleged race-based housing discrimination under § 3604(b).
key_holding: The complaint failed to allege sufficient facts showing plaintiff was subjected to different terms, conditions, or privileges because of his race or any other protected characteristic, warranting dismissal for failure to state an FHA claim.
brief_summary: African-American tenant sued property management company alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on race discrimination after the defendant required him to vacate his apartment following a fire and sought back rent. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding the complaint failed to allege facts sufficient to show plaintiff was subjected to different terms or conditions because of his protected status.

source_file: Jones v. BLUE OCEAN REALTY, LLC
case_name: Travis R. Jones v. Blue Ocean Realty, LLC
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a physically disabled person injured in a bus crash, alleges Defendants refused to rent him an apartment and failed to provide reasonable accommodations.
key_holding: Plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims were dismissed as time-barred because the complaint was filed nearly eleven months after the two-year statute of limitations had expired, and neither equitable tolling based on COVID-19 nor the continuing violation doctrine applied to excuse the late filing.
brief_summary: Travis R. Jones, a physically disabled plaintiff, sued Blue Ocean Realty, LLC and Oaklee Village Baltimore, LLC alleging they violated the Fair Housing Act by refusing to rent him an apartment and failing to provide reasonable accommodations. The court granted Defendants' motion to dismiss, finding the action was barred by the two-year statute of limitations because Plaintiff filed his complaint nearly eleven months after the limitations period expired, and neither equitable tolling nor the continuing violation doctrine applied.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cruz v. Maypa, 773 F.3d 138 (4th Cir. 2014)
Rouse v. Lee, 339 F.3d 238 (4th Cir. 2003)

source_file: KRASZYNSKI v. ROB ROY COUNTRY CLUB VILLAGE ASSOCIATION
LABOR COMMISSION v. FCS COMMUNITY MGMT.
case_name: Labor Commission v. FCS Community Management
accommodation_description: Request to keep eight chickens as emotional support animals for the daughter with PTSD, anxiety, and sensory processing disorder, and for Natalie's reactive hypoglycemia.
key_holding: The Court of Appeals reversed the district court's determination of constructive denial, holding that no constructive denial occurred because the HOA allowed the Sackses to keep all eight chickens during the investigation period, did not punish them during that time, and ultimately granted a reasonable partial accommodation (two chickens) that UALD found reasonable.
brief_summary: The Sackses, whose daughter has PTSD and sensory processing disorder, requested that their HOA allow them to keep eight chickens as emotional support animals despite CC&Rs prohibiting poultry. The HOA initially denied a variance request, then conducted an investigation after the UFHA was invoked, ultimately offering to allow two chickens. The district court found constructive denial based on delay, but the Court of Appeals reversed, holding no constructive denial occurred because the HOA allowed all chickens during review, did not punish the Sackses during investigation, and granted a reasonable partial accommodation.
key_cases_cited: LaRosa v. River Quarry Apartments, LLC, 2019 WL 3538951 (D. Idaho 2019)
Dubois v. Association of Apartment Owners of 2987 Kalakaua, 453 F.3d 1175 (9th Cir. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Astralis Condo. Ass'n v. Secretary, U.S. Dep't of Housing & Urban Dev., 620 F.3d 62 (1st Cir. 2010)
Haws v. Norman, 2017 WL 4221064 (D. Utah 2017)

case_name: Lax v. Adobe Services
accommodation_description: Plaintiff Crystal Lax, who uses a wheelchair, alleges housing agencies refused to find her permanent housing because of her disability and race while she held an emergency housing voucher.
key_holding: The complaint fails to state an FHA claim because it contains no specific factual allegations that defendants subjected plaintiffs to different terms, conditions, or privileges because of disability or race—the complaint only states plaintiffs 'suspect' discrimination without supporting facts.
brief_summary: Crystal and Dominique Lax sued housing agencies alleging they were denied access to low-income housing because of Crystal's race and disabilities while she held an emergency housing voucher. The court dismissed the complaint with leave to amend, finding the allegations too conclusory to state a Fair Housing Act claim because there were no specific facts showing defendants discriminated based on disability or race rather than simply failing to locate suitable housing.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)

source_file: MALAVE-SYKES v. GEODIS SCO USA LLC
MHANY Management Inc v Incorporated Village of Garden City
May v. MARTIN FEIN INTEREST LTD.
case_name: May v. Martin Fein Interest Ltd.
accommodation_description: Plaintiffs allege defendant discriminated against their adult daughter Alexis (who has multiple sclerosis and uses a wheelchair) by misrepresenting Unit 237 as handicap-accessible and refusing to accept deliveries of her medical supplies.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to defendant because plaintiffs failed to establish prima facie cases of disability or race discrimination—the Mays cannot assert claims on behalf of their adult daughter Alexis, and the evidence failed to show defendant's actions were motivated by discriminatory intent or had a discriminatory impact based on either Alexis's mobility disability or plaintiffs' race.
brief_summary: Raymond and Angela May, proceeding pro se, sued Martin Fein Interest Ltd. (the former property manager of Creekside at Crabtree Apartments) alleging disability discrimination regarding their adult daughter Alexis who has multiple sclerosis and uses a wheelchair, and race discrimination. The court granted summary judgment for defendant, finding that plaintiffs cannot assert claims on behalf of their non-party daughter, and failed to produce evidence that defendant's employees discriminated against them based on Alexis's disability (by allegedly misrepresenting unit accessibility and refusing to sign for medical deliveries) or based on race (through various alleged mistreatment).
key_cases_cited: McDonnell Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792 (1973)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Martin v. Brondum, 535 F. App'x 242 (4th Cir. 2013)
Havens Realty Corp. v. Coleman, 455 U.S. 363 (1982)
Reeves v. Hampton Forest Apts, 689 F. App'x 768 (4th Cir. 2017)

source_file: McFARLAND FARM PROPERTY OWNERS'ASSOCIATION v. Ryan

case_name: McGinn v. Broadmead, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested gluten-free food preparation and dining services to accommodate her celiac disease at a continuing care retirement community.
key_holding: The court granted summary judgment to Broadmead on the FHA reasonable accommodation claim because many claims were time-barred, discrete incidents of alleged gluten exposure did not establish a continuing violation, and plaintiff failed to demonstrate that Broadmead denied her a 'like experience' or failed to accommodate her disability under federal law.
brief_summary: Eleanor McGinn, an 83-year-old resident with celiac disease at Broadmead, a continuing care retirement community in Hunt Valley, Maryland, sued Broadmead under the Fair Housing Act alleging the community failed to accommodate her disability by not providing adequate gluten-free food options. The court granted summary judgment to Broadmead, finding most claims time-barred, the continuing violation doctrine inapplicable to discrete accommodation failures, and insufficient evidence that Broadmead denied McGinn a like experience or failed to accommodate her celiac disease.
key_cases_cited: Ott v. Maryland Dep't of Pub. Safety and Corr. Servs., 909 F.3d 655 (4th Cir. 2018)
A Soc'y Without A Name v. Virginia, 655 F.3d 342 (4th Cir. 2011)
Semenova v. Maryland Transit Admin., 845 F.3d 564 (4th Cir. 2017)
Thomas v. The Salvation Army S. Territory, 841 F.3d 632 (4th Cir. 2016)

source_file: Miller v. GEORGIA DEPARTMENT OF COMMUNITY SUPERVISION
case_name: Tracy A. Miller v. Georgia Department of Community Supervision, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, a paraplegic parolee, alleges the Board discriminated against him by placing him at a nursing facility (Bostick) for elderly and disabled parolees rather than allowing him to reside elsewhere.
key_holding: The court found that Bostick was designed to accommodate elderly and disabled parolees and represents a legitimate effort to accommodate plaintiff's paraplegia, not discrimination; plaintiff failed to state an FHA claim because he was not a buyer or renter and the Board did not 'make unavailable' any dwelling.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Tracy A. Miller, a paraplegic parolee, sued the Georgia State Board of Pardons and Paroles and others, claiming disability discrimination under the ADA, Rehabilitation Act, and Fair Housing Act for being paroled to a nursing facility (Bostick) designed for elderly and disabled parolees rather than being allowed to reside elsewhere. The court recommended dismissing the FHA claim because plaintiff failed to show he was a buyer or renter and the Board did not make housing unavailable but rather attempted to accommodate his disability.
key_cases_cited: Kirby v. Siegelman
Thompson v. Davis
Jenkins v. N.Y. City Dep't of Homeless Svcs.
Abdus-Sabur v. Hope Village, Inc.
Sailboat Bend Sober Living, LLC v. City of Fort Lauderdale

case_name: Deborah Morris v. City of Baltimore
accommodation_description: Plaintiff, an elderly/disabled resident, allegedly requested that the City transform her private apartment building into accessible housing through elevator repairs, ventilation improvements, and installation of chair lifts for emergency evacuation.
key_holding: The Court dismissed Plaintiff's claims because she sued the wrong party (City instead of her private landlord), lacked Article III standing as any alleged harm was not traceable to City actions, and failed to allege sufficient facts that the City took any discriminatory action against her because of disability.
brief_summary: Deborah Morris, a disabled/elderly resident of a privately-owned apartment building in Baltimore, sued the City of Baltimore alleging the City violated the ADA, Section 504, and Fair Housing Act by failing to ensure her building provided accessible housing. The Court granted the City's motion to dismiss without prejudice, finding Morris sued the wrong party (the City rather than her private landlord), lacked Article III standing because her alleged harms were not traceable to City actions, and failed to allege facts plausibly showing the City took discriminatory action against her because of her disability.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555 (1992)
City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center, 473 U.S. 432 (1985)
Woods v. City of Greensboro, 855 F.3d 639 (4th Cir. 2017)

source_file: Nelson v. HOUSING AUTHORITY OF SOUTH BEND
case_name: Nelson v. Housing Authority of South Bend
accommodation_description: Tenant facing eviction for being over-housed requested to add an adult family member to her lease or be permitted to remain in a two-bedroom unit as an accommodation for disability.
key_holding: Nelson waived arguments about HASB's Occupancy Plan by not raising them below, explicitly disavowed a failure-to-accommodate claim at trial, and the trial court's factual findings that she violated her lease by failing to transfer or add an adult were not clearly erroneous; HASB provided due process.
brief_summary: Nelson, a public housing tenant, appealed an eviction order after refusing HASB's transfer demand from a two-bedroom to a one-bedroom unit, arguing she was entitled to reasonable accommodation for disability. The Indiana Court of Appeals affirmed, finding Nelson explicitly disavowed the failure-to-accommodate claim at trial, her other arguments were waived for not being raised below, and the trial court's factual findings were not clearly erroneous.
key_cases_cited: Trinity Homes, LLC v. Fang, 848 N.E.2d 1065 (Ind. 2006)
Berg v. Berg, 170 N.E.3d 224 (Ind. 2021)
Horner v. Carter, 981 N.E.2d 1210 (Ind. 2013)

case_name: Matter of Oceanview Home for Adults, Inc. v. Zucker
accommodation_description: Adult home challenged state regulations capping admissions of persons with serious mental illness at facilities with 80+ beds if the SMI population exceeds 25%, arguing the restriction violates the FHA
key_holding: The Court of Appeals held that the challenged regulations do not facially discriminate against persons with disabilities because the admissions cap reflects a professional clinical judgment about appropriate therapeutic settings for persons with serious mental illness, furthers the integration mandate of Olmstead, and gives SMI residents greater ability to interact with non-disabled persons.
brief_summary: Oceanview Home for Adults, an adult home licensed by the New York Department of Health, brought a facial challenge to state regulations restricting facilities with 80+ beds from admitting additional residents with serious mental illness if their SMI population exceeds 25%, claiming the admission cap violates the Fair Housing Act. The Court of Appeals affirmed that the regulations do not make housing unavailable on the basis of disability because they reflect professional clinical judgment about therapeutically appropriate settings and further the integration mandate by giving SMI residents greater opportunity to interact with non-disabled persons.
key_cases_cited: Olmstead v. L.C., 527 U.S. 581 (1999)
Community House, Inc. v. City of Boise, 490 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 2007)
Larkin v. State of Mich. Dept. of Social Servs., 89 F.3d 285 (6th Cir. 1996)
Bangerter v. Orem City Corp., 46 F.3d 1491 (10th Cir. 1995)
Disability Advocates, Inc. v. Paterson, 653 F. Supp. 2d 184 (E.D.N.Y. 2009)

source_file: Oakwood at Madison Inc v Madison Tp
Oconomowoc Residential Programs Inc v City of Greenfield
Open Homes Fellowship Inc v Orange County Fla

case_name: Henry Ortiz v. Doug V. Mull, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges generally that defendants denied housing-related services routinely granted to other tenants without disabilities and failed to accommodate his PTSD, though no specific accommodation request is clearly identified in the complaint.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's Fair Housing Act claims because his conclusory allegations failed to plausibly allege that defendants knew of his PTSD, that a specific accommodation was requested and necessary, or that defendants refused to make such accommodation.
brief_summary: Plaintiff Henry Ortiz, proceeding pro se, sued individuals associated with Homecoming at Creekside Apartments and Evergreen Park Apartments alleging Fair Housing Act, ADA, § 1983, and state law claims stemming from disability discrimination related to his PTSD and alleged retaliation for protected activity. The court dismissed the first amended complaint with leave to amend, finding that plaintiff's FHA claims were conclusory and failed to plausibly allege defendants knew of his disability, that a reasonable accommodation was requested and necessary, or that defendants refused to provide such accommodation.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
San Pedro Hotel Co., Inc. v. City of Los Angeles, 159 F.3d 470 (9th Cir. 1998)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Budnick v. Town of Carefree, 518 F.3d 1109 (9th Cir. 2008)

case_name: Henry Ortiz v. Doug V. Mull, et al.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff allegedly requested ground-floor unit, garage storage, and Jacuzzi access for therapy due to mobility limitations, but these requests were denied without stated reason.
key_holding: The second amended complaint was dismissed without leave to amend because plaintiff failed to allege specific facts showing defendants knew of his disability or that any discriminatory conduct was undertaken 'because of' his handicap, and failed to plausibly allege causation between protected activity and alleged retaliatory conduct.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Henry Ortiz sued property manager Karissa Hailer, regional manager Doug Mull, and Lewis Management Corporation alleging disability discrimination under § 3604(f)(2), FHA retaliation under § 3617, and First Amendment retaliation under § 1983 based on denied accommodation requests for a ground-floor unit and therapy amenities, surveillance, and alleged retaliatory eviction. The magistrate judge recommended dismissal without leave to amend, finding the complaint lacked specific factual allegations showing defendants' knowledge of plaintiff's disability or that any adverse actions were motivated by his handicap or protected complaints.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
United States v. Cal. Mobile Home Park Mgmt., 107 F.3d 1374 (9th Cir. 1997)
Harris v. Itzhaki, 183 F.3d 1043 (9th Cir. 1999)
Walker v. City of Lakewood, 272 F.3d 1114 (9th Cir. 2001)

case_name: Ortiz v. Valasek
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested installation of a handicap ramp for her mother who could not use stairs due to disability.
key_holding: The federal district court lacked subject matter jurisdiction under the Rooker-Feldman doctrine because plaintiff's claims arose from and sought to reverse the state court eviction judgment, and alternatively abstained under Younger doctrine since ongoing state eviction proceedings afforded adequate opportunity to raise federal claims.
brief_summary: Andrea Ortiz, proceeding pro se, sued Douglas County Housing Authority and related defendants alleging disability discrimination and retaliation under the Fair Housing Act arising from her eviction from public housing for unpaid rent. The court dismissed the complaint without prejudice, finding it lacked subject matter jurisdiction under Rooker-Feldman because the claims sought to reverse the state court eviction judgment, and alternatively abstained under Younger since the state eviction proceedings remained ongoing and provided adequate opportunity to raise federal claims.
key_cases_cited: Mosby v. Ligon, 418 F.3d 927 (8th Cir. 2005)
D.C. Court of Appeals v. Feldman, 460 U.S. 462 (1983)
Rooker v. Fid. Trust Co., 263 U.S. 413 (1923)
Younger v. Harris, 401 U.S. 37 (1971)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

source_file: Oxford House, Inc. v. TP. OF NORTH BERGEN
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House sought to operate a recovery home for individuals with substance use disorders, and the Township denied a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy based on zoning classification.
key_holding: The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of the First Amended Complaint for failure to adequately plead discriminatory intent but vacated the denial of leave to amend, holding the District Court incorrectly concluded amendment would be futile because the prior preliminary injunction rulings addressed evidentiary sufficiency, not pleading standards.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc., a recovery housing operator, sued North Bergen Township after the municipality denied a Certificate of Continuing Occupancy for a two-family dwelling based on zoning classification, alleging discrimination under the FHA, ADA, and NJLAD. The Third Circuit affirmed dismissal of the First Amended Complaint for failure to plead discriminatory intent but vacated the denial of leave to amend and remanded, finding the District Court incorrectly used prior preliminary injunction rulings—which addressed evidentiary sufficiency—to preclude amendment when the plaintiff had never been given proper notice of the pleading deficiencies.
key_cases_cited: Village of Arlington Heights v. Metropolitan Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Tex. Dep't of Hous. & Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Cmtys. Project, Inc., 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Fowler v. UPMC Shadyside, 578 F.3d 203 (3d Cir. 2009)

source_file: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
case_name: Oxford House, Inc. v. Township of North Bergen
accommodation_description: Oxford House, a substance abuse recovery residence operator, requested a Certificate of Occupancy to operate a group home for six men recovering from alcoholism/substance abuse in a two-family dwelling, which the Township denied based on the Community Residence provision.
key_holding: The court granted the motion to dismiss because Plaintiff failed to allege sufficient factual matter from which the court could reasonably infer discriminatory intent—the Amended Complaint contained only conclusory allegations of discrimination without factual support for the Township's denial of the Certificate of Occupancy.
brief_summary: Oxford House, Inc. sued the Township of North Bergen alleging the Township violated the Fair Housing Act and ADA by denying a Certificate of Occupancy for a substance abuse recovery residence, arguing the denial was motivated by discriminatory intent toward disabled prospective residents. The court granted the motion to dismiss, finding the Amended Complaint failed to plead sufficient factual allegations supporting an inference of discriminatory intent, containing only conclusory allegations that the Township's application of the Community Residence zoning provision was discriminatory.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Cmty. Servs., Inc. v. Wind Gap Mun. Auth., 421 F.3d 170 (3d Cir. 2005)
Village of Arlington Heights v. Metro. Hous. Dev. Corp., 429 U.S. 252 (1977)
Oxford House, Inc. v. Twp. of N. Bergen, No. 22-2336, 2023 WL 4837835 (3d Cir. July 28, 2023)

source_file: PLAYERS PLACE II CONDO. v. KP
case_name: Players Place II Condominium Association, Inc. v. K.P. and B.F.
accommodation_description: Request to keep an emotional support dog (Luna, weighing approximately 63 pounds) in the condominium unit despite a 30-pound pet weight restriction
key_holding: B.F. has a disability under the LAD and presented sufficient evidence that her emotional support animal may be necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling; the burden therefore shifts to the Association to prove the requested accommodation is unreasonable, and the dismissal of defendants' counterclaims under the LAD and FHA was improperly granted.
brief_summary: The Players Place II Condominium Association sued K.P. and B.F. to enforce its 30-pound pet weight limit after they obtained an emotional support dog (Luna, 63 pounds) as an accommodation for B.F.'s mental health conditions including bipolar II disorder, PTSD, and anxiety. The Supreme Court of New Jersey reversed the lower courts' dismissal of defendants' counterclaims under the New Jersey Law Against Discrimination and the Fair Housing Act, held that B.F. established she has a disability and presented sufficient evidence that her ESA may be necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, and remanded for further proceedings on whether the accommodation was unreasonable.
key_cases_cited: Oras v. Hous. Auth. of Bayonne, 373 N.J. Super. 302, 861 A.2d 194 (App. Div. 2004)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)

source_file: PLAYERS PLACE II CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION, INC. v. KP
case_name: Players Place II Condominium Association, Inc. v. K.P.
accommodation_description: B.F., a person with mental health disabilities, requested an accommodation to keep a 63-pound emotional support dog (Luna) despite the condominium association's 30-pound pet weight limit.
key_holding: The New Jersey Supreme Court held that B.F. established she has a disability under the LAD, presented sufficient evidence that the ESA accommodation may be necessary to afford her equal opportunity to use and enjoy her dwelling, and that the burden shifts to the housing provider to prove the accommodation is unreasonable; because the trial court and Appellate Division failed to apply this framework, the dismissal of defendants' discrimination claims was vacated and remanded.
brief_summary: Players Place II Condominium Association sued unit owner K.P. for violating the Association's pet weight limit by keeping a 63-pound emotional support dog for his spouse B.F., who has mental health disabilities; defendants counterclaimed under the New Jersey LAD and federal FHA, alleging disability discrimination. The New Jersey Supreme Court reversed the dismissal of defendants' discrimination claims, holding that B.F. established a disability and presented sufficient evidence that the ESA accommodation may be necessary for her equal use and enjoyment of the dwelling, and that the matter must be remanded for the housing provider to prove the accommodation is unreasonable after engaging in a good-faith interactive process.
key_cases_cited: Oras v. Bayonne Housing Authority, 373 N.J. Super. 306 (App. Div. 2006)
Bhogaita v. Altamonte Heights Condo. Ass'n, Inc., 765 F.3d 1277 (11th Cir. 2014)
Castillo Condo. Ass'n v. U.S. Dep't of Hous. & Urb. Dev., 821 F.3d 92 (1st Cir. 2016)
Revock v. Cowpet Bay W. Condo. Ass'n, 853 F.3d 96 (3d Cir. 2017)
Lapid-Laurel, L.L.C. v. Zoning Bd. of Adjustment of Scotch Plains, 284 F.3d 442 (3d Cir. 2002)

case_name: PRJ Capital, LLC v. Moran
accommodation_description: Defendant requested that plaintiff provide documentation to support her application for rent assistance from the Salvation Army, which she claimed was necessary due to a disability that diminished her earning capacity.
key_holding: The trial court properly dismissed defendant's disability discrimination claims because she failed to plead sufficient facts establishing she had a disability within the meaning of the PWDCRA or FHAA, and she did not inform the landlords of any disability until after they decided to terminate her tenancy.
brief_summary: Valerie Moran sued PRJ Capital and David James, her former landlords, claiming disability discrimination and retaliation under the PWDCRA and FHAA after they terminated her month-to-month tenancy while she sought rent assistance. The Michigan Court of Appeals affirmed the dismissal of her counterclaims, holding that Moran failed to plead sufficient facts to establish she had a disability within the meaning of the statutes, and she did not inform the landlords of any disability until after they had already decided to terminate her tenancy.
key_cases_cited: Riverbrook v. Fabode, 333 Mich App 645 (2020)
Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Bachman v. Swan Harbour Ass'n, 252 Mich App 400 (2002)
Jankowski Lee & Assoc. v. Cisneros, 91 F.3d 891 (7th Cir. 1996)

source_file: Pinnacle Treatment Centers Inc v City of Crown Point Indiana
Portnoy v. MEI CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION, INC.
case_name: Eli Portnoy v. MEI Condominium Association, Inc.
accommodation_description: Portnoy requested accommodation that someone other than Anna Strickstein-Zandman interact with him about any business regarding his residency, due to disability-related stress from their antagonistic relationship.
key_holding: The court denied the individual board director's motion to dismiss, holding that Florida state immunity statutes cannot shield individuals from Fair Housing Act liability and that Portnoy sufficiently alleged willful discrimination to overcome statutory immunity.
brief_summary: Eli Portnoy sued MEI Condominium Association and its Board President Anna Strickstein-Zandman under the Fair Housing Act, alleging they denied his reasonable accommodation request to communicate with someone other than the Board President due to disability-related stress, and then discriminated against him by restricting gym access and refusing to renew his lease. The court denied Strickstein-Zandman's motion to dismiss, rejecting her immunity defense under Florida state statutes and finding that Portnoy adequately alleged willful individual discrimination to survive dismissal.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Meyer v. Holley, 537 U.S. 280 (2003)
Housing Opportunities Project for Excellence, Inc. v. Key Colony No. 4 Condo. Assoc., 510 F. Supp. 2d 1003 (S.D. Fla. 2007)
Sabal Palm Condos. of Pine Island Ridge Ass'n, Inc. v. Fischer, 2014 WL 988767 (S.D. Fla. 2014)

source_file: Rehabilitation Support Services Inc v City of Albany New York
Roberts v. SACRAMENTO HOUSING AND REDEVELOPMENT AGENCY
case_name: Sydney Brooke Roberts and David Tyrone Samuel v. Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency
accommodation_description: Request for an additional bedroom on their Housing Choice Voucher to accommodate David Samuel's and minor daughter AIMS's disabilities (Autism, ADHD, PTSD, depression) so each can have a separate bedroom.
key_holding: The motion for summary judgment was denied because plaintiffs failed to meet their burden: the housing authority granted the extra bedroom accommodation in March 2023, making it unclear whether a benefit was denied, and plaintiffs' motion was vague about what accommodations remained at issue.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs David Samuel (disabled adult with ADHD, Autism, PTSD, and depression) and Sydney Roberts, holders of a HUD Housing Choice Voucher, sued the Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency alleging disability discrimination for denying their reasonable accommodation request for an additional bedroom to accommodate disability-related needs. The court denied plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment because the housing authority had granted the extra bedroom accommodation in March 2023, and plaintiffs failed to demonstrate they were entitled to additional benefits or to identify what accommodations remained in dispute.
key_cases_cited: Giebeler v. M & B Associates, 343 F.3d 1143 (9th Cir. 2003)
Duvall v. County of Kitsap, 260 F.3d 1124 (9th Cir. 2001)
Weinreich v. Los Angeles County Metro. Transp. Auth., 114 F.3d 976 (9th Cir. 1997)
Zukle v. Regents of Univ. of Cal., 166 F.3d 1041 (9th Cir. 1999)
Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317 (1986)

case_name: Rodriguez v. City of Chicago
accommodation_description: Plaintiff requested that the City preserve an existing driveway apron she used as a safe location to access medical transportation, which was necessary due to her mobility disability preventing her from being picked up on busy streets.
key_holding: The FHA reasonable modification claim fails because the driveway apron was not within the property line of Rodriguez's residence but rather on an adjacent commercial lot, so it did not fall within the 'premises' of her dwelling that she is entitled to modify under § 3604(f)(3)(A).
brief_summary: Mari Rodriguez, a tenant with mobility disabilities, sued the City of Chicago after the city planned to remove a driveway apron on an adjacent commercial lot that she used to safely access medical transportation. The court granted the City's motion to dismiss, holding that the FHA reasonable modification claim failed because the driveway was not part of Rodriguez's premises but rather on an adjacent property, and the ADA claim failed because the driveway was not a City service.
key_cases_cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Moore v. W. Illinois Corr. Ctr., 89 F.4th 582 (7th Cir. 2023)
Barden v. City of Sacramento, 292 F.3d 1073 (9th Cir. 2002)
Wetzel v. Glen St. Andrew Living Cmty., LLC, 901 F.3d 856 (7th Cir. 2018)

case_name: Sproaps v. Brown
accommodation_description: No accommodation request; plaintiff alleges intentional discrimination based on sexual orientation through hostile treatment and failure to make repairs.
key_holding: The court found that plaintiff's allegation of a stray discriminatory remark about his 'lover boy' and delayed repairs did not state a plausible claim of sex discrimination under the FHA because there was no evidence linking Brown's discriminatory remark to any discriminatory conduct, repairs were delayed due to financial disputes rather than sexual orientation, and plaintiff provided no examples of other tenants treated more favorably.
brief_summary: Marriell Sproaps, proceeding in forma pauperis, sued his former property manager Kevin Brown alleging Fair Housing Act violations based on sexual orientation discrimination, claiming Brown treated him with hostility and refused to make repairs because of his same-sex partner. The court dismissed the complaint for failure to state a plausible claim, finding that Brown's single discriminatory remark about plaintiff's 'lover boy' was an isolated stray comment unconnected to any discriminatory policy, and the delayed repairs were motivated by a financial dispute over responsibility for vandalism damage rather than sexual orientation bias.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal
Gallagher v. Magner
Ellis v. City of Minneapolis
Tex. Dep't of Hous. Cmty. Affs. v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.

case_name: Walter Tanner v. Rochdale Village, Inc.
accommodation_description: Plaintiff alleges a management staff member directed personnel not to repair his unit because of a false perception that plaintiff was a sexual offender and HIV positive.
key_holding: The court dismissed plaintiff's FHA disparate treatment claim because his allegations were too vague and speculative to support an inference of discriminatory animus, and the claim based on retaliation for litigation against the MTA failed because litigants are not a protected class under the FHA.
brief_summary: Pro se plaintiff Walter Tanner sued Rochdale Village Inc., a housing cooperative in Jamaica, Queens, alleging disability discrimination under the Fair Housing Act and Americans with Disabilities Act, claiming management refused to repair his unit based on a perceived disability. The court granted defendant's motion to dismiss, finding plaintiff failed to state a plausible discrimination claim because his allegations were speculative and unsupported by factual details, and because being a litigant is not a protected class under the FHA.
key_cases_cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009)
Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)
Francis v. Kings Park Manor, Inc., 992 F.3d 67 (2d Cir. 2021)

source_file: United States v. RUTHERFORD TENANTS CORP.
case_name: United States of America v. Rutherford Tenants Corp. and James Ramadei
accommodation_description: Request to keep emotional support animals in a cooperative apartment unit
key_holding: Parties settled by consent decree requiring Rutherford to pay $165,000 in damages, offer to purchase complainant's shares for $585,000, adopt a reasonable accommodation policy, and provide staff training on disability accommodations under the FHA.
brief_summary: The United States sued Rutherford Tenants Corp. and its Board President James Ramadei for discriminating against a disabled tenant (Meril Lesser) by refusing to allow her to keep emotional support animals and attempting to evict her, in violation of the Fair Housing Act. The parties resolved through a consent decree requiring Rutherford to pay $165,000 in damages, offer to purchase the complainant's cooperative shares for $585,000, adopt a reasonable accommodation policy, and provide staff training.

source_file: United States v. TOLL BROTHERS, INC. (1)
case_name: United States of America v. Toll Brothers, Inc.
accommodation_description: Systemic design and construction deficiencies at North 8th Condominium including insufficiently wide doors, inaccessible public/common use areas, and failure to include required accessible design features
key_holding: Lendlease LMB agreed to a Consent Decree requiring FHA compliance training, retention of an FHA compliance adviser for future projects, and payment of a $10,000 civil penalty, without admitting liability, to resolve claims that North 8th Condominium was not designed and constructed in compliance with the FHA's accessible design requirements.
brief_summary: The United States brought this action against Toll Brothers, Inc. and related entities including Lendlease LMB alleging that North 8th Condominium in Brooklyn was designed and constructed in violation of the FHA's accessibility requirements under § 3604(f)(3)(C). Lendlease LMB, the construction manager, entered into a Consent Decree requiring compliance training, an FHA compliance adviser for future projects, record-keeping, and a $10,000 civil penalty to resolve the claims without admission of liability.

source_file: United States v. TOLL BROTHERS, INC.
case_name: United States of America v. Toll Brothers, Inc.
accommodation_description: Design and construction accessibility requirements for covered multifamily dwellings including accessible common areas, door widths, accessible routes, environmental controls, grab bar reinforcements, and usable kitchens and bathrooms
key_holding: GreenbergFarrow Architecture consents to a three-year consent decree requiring FHA compliance training, record-keeping, and a $30,000 civil penalty for failing to design and construct accessible covered multifamily dwellings as required by 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C)
brief_summary: The United States sued Toll Brothers and its affiliates, including architecture firm GreenbergFarrow, alleging that the North 8th Street condominium in Brooklyn was not designed and constructed with required accessible features as mandated by 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(C). The court approved a consent decree resolving the claims against GreenbergFarrow, requiring the firm to pay a $30,000 civil penalty, implement FHA compliance training programs, and maintain compliance records for three years.

source_file: WILLOW HAVEN ON 106th STREET, LLC v. NAGIREDDY
Walker v US Dept of Housing and Urban Development
Watkins v. HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY OF HUGO
case_name: Watkins v. Housing Authority of the City of Hugo
accommodation_description: The complaint alleges disability discrimination including reasonable accommodation denial under § 3604(f)(3)(b), but this procedural order does not describe the specific accommodation requested.
key_holding: The court denied plaintiffs' motion to strike the individual defendants' summary judgment motions, finding the original motion moot due to an amended filing, and permitted the individual defendants to constructively amend their answer to raise qualified immunity since plaintiffs received adequate notice through the Joint Status Report and suffered no prejudice.
brief_summary: Plaintiffs sued the Housing Authority of the City of Hugo and individual defendants alleging Fair Housing Act violations including disability discrimination and failure to accommodate, as well as sex discrimination. This procedural order denies plaintiffs' motion to strike the individual defendants' summary judgment filings and allows the defendants to raise qualified immunity as a defense, without addressing the merits of the underlying FHA claims.
